topics.title: Introduction to Rheology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Rheology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Rheology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RHEOLOGY — The Science of Flow and Deformation**
> *Rheology* is the branch of physics and engineering that deals with the **deformation and flow of matter** under the influence of applied forces (stresses). The term was formally coined by **Eugene C. Bingham** in **1920** (proposed by Professor Markus Reiner), derived from the Greek word *rheo* meaning *"to flow"* and *logos* meaning *"study of."* It encompasses the study of both **solids (elastic deformation)** and **liquids (viscous flow)**, as well as materials that exhibit both behaviors simultaneously.

In the context of **Food Science and Technology**, rheology is the study of how food materials deform, flow, or behave when subjected to forces during processing, handling, storage, and consumption. It is a **fundamental discipline** that links the **microstructure** of food to its **macroscopic mechanical behavior**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ketchup Bottle" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you have three substances in identical bottles:
> 1. **Water** — Tilts and pours out instantly, with no resistance. This is a classic **Newtonian liquid**.
> 2. **Honey** — Requires a sustained squeeze and flows slowly, showing resistance. Also a Newtonian liquid, but with **higher viscosity**.
> 3. **Ketchup / Tomato Paste** — Stays put in the bottle until you **shake it first** (yield stress), then suddenly becomes easy to pour. This is **non-Newtonian** and shows **time-dependent, structure-dependent behavior**.

Rheology is essentially the science that **mathematically describes why these three substances behave so differently** when the same force is applied to them. The answer lies in their **internal microstructure** — the arrangement of molecules, droplets, fibers, and particles that make up each food.

## 1.3 Why Rheology is Critical in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Food Engineers MUST Study Rheology**
> Rheological properties govern nearly every step of food manufacturing and consumption:
> - **Process Design**: Sizing of pumps, pipelines, mixers, extruders, and heat exchangers.
> - **Quality Control**: Ensuring consistent texture, mouthfeel, and appearance of products.
> - **Product Development**: Creating new textures (e.g., pourable yogurts, spreadable cheeses, squeezable sauces).
> - **Sensory Science**: Correlating instrumental measurements with human perception of texture.
> - **Shelf Life**: Predicting changes in flow behavior during storage (syneresis, crystallization, etc.).
> - **Instrumentation**: Designing texture analyzers, viscometers, and rheometers.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities in Rheology (Standard SI Units)

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|---------|
| Force | F | Push or pull acting on a body | **Newton (N)** |
| Area | A | Cross-section over which force acts | **m²** |
| Stress | σ or τ | Force per unit area | **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²** |
| Strain | γ or ε | Relative deformation | **dimensionless (—)** |
| Strain Rate | γ̇ | Rate of change of strain | **s⁻¹ (reciprocal seconds)** |
| Viscosity | η (eta) | Resistance to flow | **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** |
| Elastic Modulus | G | Stiffness of an elastic solid | **Pa (Pascal)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **SI vs CGS Units in Rheology**
> The CGS unit of viscosity is the **Poise (P)** = 0.1 Pa·s. The commonly used sub-unit is the **centipoise (cP)** = 0.001 Pa·s. Water at 20°C has η ≈ **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**.
> Conversion: **1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Rheology: Stress and Strain

### 2.1.1 Stress (σ or τ) — The "Cause"

When a force is applied to a material, it is not the absolute magnitude of the force that determines deformation, but the **force per unit area** — this is the **stress**.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = stress applied to the material (Pa)
- $F$ = applied force (N)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area over which the force acts (m²)

**Three Types of Stress relevant to foods**:
1. **Tensile Stress (Normal Stress)**: Pulling the material apart (e.g., stretching cheese strings, pasta dough).
2. **Compressive Stress**: Squeezing the material (e.g., biting a biscuit).
3. **Shear Stress (τ)**: Forces acting parallel to the surface (e.g., stirring coffee, pumping milk through a pipe). **This is the dominant stress in rheological measurements.**

### 2.1.2 Strain (γ) — The "Effect"

Strain is the **dimensionless measure of deformation** relative to the original size of the material. For **simple shear**, strain is defined as the ratio of the displacement of a layer to its distance from the fixed reference plane.

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Strain is Dimensionless**
> In the formula above, $\Delta x$ is the horizontal displacement (in meters) and $\Delta y$ is the vertical distance between layers (in meters). The ratio gives a pure number — like 0.05, meaning a 5% deformation. No units appear in the final answer.

## 2.2 Shear Rate (γ̇) — The Bridge Between Force and Flow

When a fluid is sheared, different layers move at different velocities. The **shear rate** measures how rapidly the velocity changes with position:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $v$ = velocity of the fluid layer (m/s)
- $y$ = distance from the stationary surface (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Shear Rate Ranges in Food Processing**
> - Sedimentation of particles: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ s⁻¹
> - Pouring from a bottle: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{1}$ to $10^{2}$ s⁻¹
> - Mixing in tanks: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{1}$ to $10^{3}$ s⁻¹
> - Pumping in pipelines: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{2}$ to $10^{3}$ s⁻¹
> - High-pressure homogenization: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{6}$ s⁻¹

## 2.3 The Two Ideal Material Behaviors

### 2.3.1 The Ideal Elastic Solid — Hooke's Law (1678)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOOKE'S LAW OF ELASTICITY**
> For an **ideal Hookean solid**, stress is **directly proportional to strain** and is **independent of time**. When the force is removed, the material returns to its original shape (energy is stored elastically).

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

- $G$ = Shear Modulus / Elastic Modulus (Pa)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = Shear strain (dimensionless)

### 2.3.2 The Ideal Viscous Liquid — Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEWTON'S LAW OF VISCOSITY**
> For a **Newtonian fluid**, stress is **directly proportional to shear rate** and is **independent of the previous deformation history**. The proportionality constant is the **viscosity (η)**.

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| Formula | Name | Variables | Units | Application |
|---------|------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| $\sigma = F/A$ | Stress Definition | F, A | Pa | Universal |
| $\gamma = \Delta x / \Delta y$ | Shear Strain | displacement, distance | dimensionless | Shear deformation |
| $\dot{\gamma} = dv / dy$ | Shear Rate | velocity, position | s⁻¹ | Flow field analysis |
| $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Hooke's Law | G, τ, γ | Pa | Elastic solid behavior |
| $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | η, τ, γ̇ | Pa·s | Newtonian fluid flow |
| $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Constitutive Equation (Newtonian) | η, γ̇ | Pa | Pipe flow, mixing |
| $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | Kinematic Viscosity | η, ρ | m²/s | Dimensionless analysis |
| $De = \lambda / t$ | Deborah Number | λ, t | dimensionless | Viscoelasticity |
| $Re = \rho v L / \eta$ | Reynolds Number | ρ, v, L, η | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall**
> Students frequently confuse **G (shear modulus)** with **E (Young's modulus)**. For an incompressible material, $E = 2G(1+\nu) \approx 3G$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. For foods (often nearly incompressible), $E \approx 3G$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these equations are used in industry**:
> - **Newton's law** is used to size pumps for milk, fruit juice, and refined oils (all Newtonian).
> - **Hooke's law** is used to model the initial bite of firm foods like carrots or cheese.
> - **Shear rate calculations** inform the design of scraped-surface heat exchangers for ice cream.
> - **Reynolds number** dictates whether a sauce flows as smooth laminar layers or turbulent swirls in a pipe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Definition of Viscosity from Newton's Law

We start from the **fundamental postulate** that in a Newtonian fluid, shear stress and shear rate are linearly related. We now derive the full constitutive form step by step.

**Step 1 — State the proportionality relationship.**

> We assert that shear stress is proportional to the shear rate. This is the empirical definition of a Newtonian fluid, first observed by Isaac Newton in his *Principia* (1687).

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constant.**

> The constant of proportionality is given a physical name: **dynamic viscosity (η)**. The sign and units reflect the fact that the fluid resists deformation.

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the kinematic definitions of τ and γ̇.**

> We expand both sides. The left side remains as stress. The right side is replaced by the velocity gradient from Section 2.2.

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for viscosity.**

> The viscosity is therefore a measurable ratio of stress to velocity gradient, independent of the magnitude of the deformation.

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{dv/dy} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify dimensional consistency.**

> Now we check the units. Stress has units of Pa = N/m² = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻². The shear rate has units of s⁻¹. Therefore, viscosity must have units of:

$$\left[\eta\right] = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

> This confirms our SI unit. The CGS unit, the **Poise (P)**, is 0.1 Pa·s, named after **Jean Louis Marie Poiseuille**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity

Kinematic viscosity is the **ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density**. The derivation is purely definitional but carries physical meaning.

**Step 1 — State the definition.**

> Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass density. It appears naturally in dimensionless numbers like the Reynolds number.

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Express the units.**

> $\eta$ has units of Pa·s = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹. Density $\rho$ has units of kg·m⁻³. The ratio yields:

$$\left[\nu\right] = \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}}{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}} = \text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

> This is the unit of **diffusivity**, confirming that kinematic viscosity is fundamentally a measure of how fast momentum diffuses through the fluid.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Three Mechanical Behaviors of Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rheological Trinity — How materials respond to stress**
> The table below classifies materials based on their response to an applied step strain. The KTU examiner loves to test the differences between these three behaviors.

| Material Type | Constitutive Equation | Response to Step Strain | Response to Step Stress | Examples in Food |
|---------------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Hookean Solid (Elastic)** | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | Instantaneous, finite strain that remains constant over time | Strain increases instantaneously and remains constant | Ideal jelly, dry spaghetti strand |
| **Newtonian Fluid (Viscous)** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Strain increases linearly with time (continuous flow) | Strain increases instantaneously and continues to grow (creep) | Water, refined oil, milk, honey |
| **Viscoelastic Material** | Combination of above | Strain reaches a finite plateau + slow continued deformation | Strain increases instantaneously + slow continuous creep | Dough, cheese, yogurt, ketchup, chocolate melt |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A layer of honey 2 mm thick is placed between two parallel plates. The top plate moves at 0.5 m/s. The honey has a viscosity of 5 Pa·s. Calculate the shear stress required to maintain this motion.

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.**
- Gap thickness: $\Delta y = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Plate velocity: $v = 0.5$ m/s
- Viscosity: $\eta = 5$ Pa·s

**Step 2 — Calculate the shear rate.**

> Use the definition of shear rate from Section 2.2. The velocity of the moving plate divided by the gap gives the velocity gradient, which equals the shear rate.

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 250 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's law to compute stress.**

> Substitute the shear rate and viscosity into the constitutive equation.

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 5 \times 250 = 1250 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — State the answer with units.**

> The required shear stress is **1250 Pa** (or equivalently 1.25 kPa). This is the force per unit area the top plate exerts on the honey to keep it sliding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Rheological Conceptual Map

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical architecture of rheological knowledge**, showing how the fundamental concepts connect to the classification of materials and engineering applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[APPLIED FORCE F] --> B[STRESS sigma equals F over A]
    B --> C{Type of Material}
    C --> D[Hookean Solid]
    C --> E[Newtonian Fluid]
    C --> F[Viscoelastic Material]
    D --> G[Hookes Law tau equals G gamma]
    E --> H[Newtons Law tau equals eta gamma dot]
    F --> I[Combination of Elastic and Viscous]
    G --> J[Energy Stored]
    H --> K[Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    I --> L[Energy Partly Stored and Partly Dissipated]
    J --> M[Food Texture: Springy Chewy]
    K --> N[Food Texture: Pourable Fluid]
    L --> O[Food Texture: Doughlike Gel]
    N --> P[Engineering Use: Pump Sizing Pipe Flow]
    M --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Quality Control and Product Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Material Behaviors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[RHEOLOGICAL RESPONSE] --> S[Time Independent]
    R --> T[Time Dependent]
    S --> S1[Ideal Elastic Solid]
    S --> S2[Ideal Viscous Fluid]
    S --> S3[Non Newtonian Fluid]
    T --> T1[Thixotropic]
    T --> T2[Rheopectic]
    S1 --> S1a[tau equals G gamma]
    S2 --> S2a[tau equals eta gamma dot]
    S3 --> S3a[Shear Thinning or Shear Thickening]
    T1 --> T1a[Viscosity Decreases with Time]
    T2 --> T2a[Viscosity Increases with Time]
    S1a --> APP[Foods: Dry Pasta Carrot]
    S2a --> APP
    S3a --> APP
    T1a --> APP
    T2a --> APP
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Information Flow in a Rheological Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Sample Loaded in Rheometer] --> GEOM[Select Geometry Parallel Plate Cone Plate Couette]
    GEOM --> LOAD[Apply Controlled Stress or Strain]
    LOAD --> SENSE[Sensor Measures Response]
    SENSE --> CALC[Compute Viscosity or Modulus]
    CALC --> OUT[Plot Flow Curve or Oscillation Data]
    OUT --> INTERP[Interpret Material Behavior]
    INTERP --> DEC[Process Design Quality Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for Exams**
> KTU examiners often ask students to **draw or describe** the rheometer setup or the flow curve. The diagrams above are simplified for clarity. In the actual exam, you can sketch a **two-plate geometry with a sheared fluid in between** to illustrate the concept of simple shear.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define the term "Rheology" and explain its importance in food processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Rheology* is the science that studies the **flow and deformation of matter** under applied forces. The term was coined by Eugene Bingham in 1920 from the Greek words *rheo* (to flow) and *logos* (study). In food processing, rheology is important because it **(1)** governs the design of unit operations like pumping, mixing, and extrusion, **(2)** determines the **texture and mouthfeel** of food products, and **(3)** enables **quality control** by linking microstructure to macroscopic mechanical behavior.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Distinguish between a Newtonian fluid and a Hookean solid with the help of their constitutive equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Newtonian fluid** obeys the equation $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, where shear stress is proportional to **shear rate**, and the proportionality constant is the **viscosity η**. A **Hookean solid** obeys the equation $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$, where shear stress is proportional to **strain**, and the constant is the **shear modulus G**. In a Newtonian fluid, energy is **dissipated as heat** during flow, while in a Hookean solid, energy is **stored elastically** and recovered upon unloading.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Strain with Strain Rate**: Strain is dimensionless; strain rate has units of s⁻¹. Forgetting this loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Skipping Units**: Always write Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹, etc. A numerically correct answer without units gets only **partial credit**.
> 3. **Writing Newton's Law incorrectly**: It is $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, NOT $\tau = \eta \cdot \gamma$. Many students confuse the two. This is a **2-mark loss** in Section (a).
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to m**: In numerical problems, dimensional consistency requires SI units throughout. Missing this loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not classifying examples**: When asked to "give food examples," always mention at least **two** distinct food systems. A single example earns only partial credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 1]**
> **(a)** Define the following rheological terms with their SI units: **(i) Shear stress**, **(ii) Shear strain**, **(iii) Shear rate**, **(iv) Dynamic viscosity**, **(v) Shear modulus**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A tomato ketchup sample is being pumped through a pipe of radius 2 cm at a linear velocity of 0.4 m/s. The viscosity of the ketchup at the prevailing shear rate is measured as 8 Pa·s. Calculate: **(i)** the shear rate at the wall, **(ii)** the shear stress at the wall, and **(iii)** the kinematic viscosity of the ketchup, given its density is 1100 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks, 1.4 marks each)**:

| Term | Definition | SI Unit |
|------|------------|---------|
| Shear Stress | Force per unit area acting parallel to a surface: $\tau = F/A$ | **Pa (Pascal)** |
| Shear Strain | Ratio of displacement to perpendicular distance: $\gamma = \Delta x / \Delta y$ | **dimensionless** |
| Shear Rate | Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction: $\dot{\gamma} = dv/dy$ | **s⁻¹** |
| Dynamic Viscosity | Proportionality constant between shear stress and shear rate: $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | **Pa·s** |
| Shear Modulus | Proportionality constant between shear stress and shear strain: $G = \tau / \gamma$ | **Pa (Pascal)** |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

> **Step (i): Shear rate at the wall (2 marks)**
> For fully developed pipe flow, the wall shear rate for a Newtonian fluid is given by:
> $$\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{8v}{D}$$
> where D = 2 × radius = 2 × 0.02 = 0.04 m.
> $$\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{8 \times 0.4}{0.04} = 80 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

> **Step (ii): Shear stress at the wall (2 marks)**
> Apply Newton's law of viscosity:
> $$\tau_{wall} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{wall} = 8 \times 80 = 640 \; \text{Pa}$$

> **Step (iii): Kinematic viscosity (3 marks)**
> Apply the definition from Section 3.2:
> $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} = \frac{8}{1100}$$
> $$\nu = 0.00727 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} = 7.27 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 1]**
> **(a)** State and explain **Hooke's Law of Elasticity** and **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. Compare the energy behavior of a Hookean solid and a Newtonian fluid when the stress is removed. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **simple shear geometry** between two parallel plates. Derive the relationship between shear rate, plate velocity, and gap thickness. A fluid with viscosity 0.9 Pa·s fills the gap of 1.5 mm between two parallel plates. If the upper plate moves at 0.3 m/s, calculate the shear stress at the wall. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**:

> **Hooke's Law of Elasticity (3 marks)**:
> > *For an ideal Hookean solid, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear strain, with the shear modulus (G) as the proportionality constant. The relationship is mathematically expressed as:*

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

> The deformation is **fully recoverable** when the stress is removed. The energy input during loading is **stored as elastic potential energy** within the material and is **fully released** during unloading. No energy is dissipated. A classic food example is a firm cheese that bends slightly and returns to shape.

> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (3 marks)**:
> > *For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate, with the dynamic viscosity (η) as the proportionality constant. The relationship is:*

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

> The deformation is **irrecoverable** — the fluid continues to flow as long as stress is applied. All the input energy is **dissipated as heat** through viscous friction. A classic example is the flow of milk through a pipe.

> **Comparison (1 mark)**: In short, a Hookean solid stores energy, while a Newtonian fluid dissipates it.

**Part (b) — Geometry, Derivation, and Numerical (7 marks)**:

> **Sketch of Simple Shear (2 marks)**: Draw a rectangular block of fluid between two parallel plates of area A. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves with velocity V under applied force F. The gap between plates is h.

> **Derivation of Shear Rate (2 marks)**: From the definition,
> $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$
> Assuming a linear velocity profile (valid for a Newtonian fluid in steady shear),
> $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{V}{h}$$

> **Numerical Calculation (3 marks)**:
> Given: $\eta = 0.9$ Pa·s, $h = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $V = 0.3$ m/s.
> First compute shear rate:
> $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.3}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$
> Then apply Newton's law:
> $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 0.9 \times 200 = 180 \; \text{Pa}$$
> The shear stress at the wall is **180 Pa**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 Introduction to Rheology**

- **Rheology = Science of Flow + Deformation** (Bingham, 1920).
- The **two pillars** are **stress (Pa)** and **strain (dimensionless)**.
- **Hooke's Law** is for elastic solids: $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$.
- **Newton's Law** is for viscous fluids: $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$.
- **Viscosity units**: SI = **Pa·s**; CGS = **Poise (P)**; conversion **1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP**.
- Water at 20°C has $\eta \approx 1$ cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \eta / \rho$ has units of **m²/s**.
- **Three stresses** to remember: tensile, compressive, shear.
- Foods are often **neither purely Hookean nor Newtonian** — they are **viscoelastic**.
- Always use **SI units** in numerical problems (convert mm to m, cP to Pa·s).
- **Deborah number** $De = \lambda / t$ compares material relaxation time to observation time.
- **Rheology directly links microstructure to texture** — the central theme of this course.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biological Systems and Mechanical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Biological Systems and Mechanical Properties ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biological Systems in Food Science** refer to organized assemblies of **biopolymers** (proteins, polysaccharides, lipids) and **cellular structures** (cell walls, membranes, intracellular matrices) that constitute the structural framework of food materials. These systems exhibit **mechanical properties** that govern texture, mouthfeel, deformation behavior, and structural integrity during processing and consumption. **Mechanical Properties** of biological food systems describe the response of these materials to applied forces or deformations, classified into: - **Elastic properties** — recoverable deformation (Hookean behavior) - **Viscous properties** — irreversible flow (Newtonian behavior) - **Viscoelastic properties** — combined elastic + viscous response (time-dependent) - **Plastic properties** — permanent deformation after yield stress - **Failure properties** — fracture, rupture, cutting behavior ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Memory Foam Mattress vs. Honey** > > Imagine pressing your hand into a **memory foam mattress** — when you remove it, the foam slowly returns to its original shape. This is **elastic behavior** (energy stored and released). > > Now imagine stirring **honey** with a spoon — it flows continuously as long as force is applied, and never returns. This is **viscous behavior** (energy dissipated as heat). > > Most **biological food materials** (bread, cheese, meat, fruits) behave like a *combination* — they are **viscoelastic**. Press a piece of bread and it compresses, but slowly springs back partially. This dual personality is what food rheologists study! ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Stress (σ)** = Force per unit area, measured in **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²** - **Strain (γ or ε)** = Dimensionless ratio of deformation to original dimension - **Modulus (E or G)** = Stress/Strain, in **Pa** - **Standard acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²** - **Universal gas constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Poisson's ratio (ν)** for most food materials: **0.2 – 0.5** ### 1.4 Classification of Biological Food Systems | System Type | Examples | Dominant Biopolymer | Characteristic Behavior | |-------------|----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Plant tissue | Fruits, vegetables | Cellulose, pectin, starch | Cellular, turgor-dependent | | Animal tissue | Meat, fish | Collagen, myofibrillar proteins | Fibrous, anisotropic | | Dairy gels | Cheese, yogurt | Casein, whey proteins | Protein network, soft solid | | Cereal products | Bread, pasta | Starch, gluten | Foam/gel composite | | Fats & emulsions | Butter, mayonnaise | Triglycerides, emulsifiers | Semi-solid/solid dispersion | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Understanding the link between *microstructure* (cellular, molecular architecture) and *macro-mechanical response* (texture, firmness) is the foundational concept of Module 1. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Mechanical Concepts #### A. Stress (σ) — The Internal Force Intensity Stress quantifies the internal resisting force per unit area inside a material when external loads are applied. **Types of Stress:** - **Normal stress (σ)** — acts perpendicular to the surface (tensile or compressive) - **Shear stress (τ)** — acts parallel to the surface #### B. Strain (ε or γ) — The Deformation Measure Strain is the geometric measure of deformation relative to original dimensions. - **Normal strain (ε)** = ΔL / L₀ (dimensionless) - **Shear strain (γ)** = tan(θ) ≈ θ for small angles #### C. The Three Ideal Material Behaviors 1. **Hookean Solid (Ideal Elastic)** - Stress is **linearly proportional** to strain - Governed by **Hooke's Law** - Energy is **stored** (recoverable) - Example: Rubber band within elastic limit 2. **Newtonian Fluid (Ideal Viscous)** - Stress is **linearly proportional** to strain rate - Governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity** - Energy is **dissipated** (irrecoverable) - Example: Water, vegetable oil at low shear 3. **Viscoelastic Material (Real Biological Food)** - Combines both elastic and viscous responses - Behavior is **time-dependent** - Example: Dough, cheese, apple flesh, meat ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Name | Description | Units | |---|----------|------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Normal Stress | Force per unit area | Pa | | 2 | $\tau = F/A$ | Shear Stress | Parallel force per area | Pa | | 3 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Normal Strain | Length change ratio | dimensionless | | 4 | $\gamma = \Delta x / y$ | Shear Strain | Lateral displacement ratio | dimensionless | | 5 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus | Stiffness in tension/compression | Pa | | 6 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Shear Modulus | Stiffness in shear | Pa | | 7 | $K = -V \cdot dP / dV$ | Bulk Modulus | Volumetric stiffness | Pa | | 8 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ | Poisson's Ratio | Lateral-to-axial strain ratio | dimensionless | | 9 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | Linear elastic constitutive law | Pa | | 10 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law | Newtonian fluid behavior | Pa | | 11 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Elastic Relationship | E-G-ν relationship for isotropic solids | Pa | | 12 | $\sigma_T = \sigma_0 + E_p \cdot \varepsilon_p$ | Strain Hardening | Plastic flow with hardening | Pa | | 13 | $E(t) = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t)$ | Relaxation Modulus | Stress relaxation measurement | Pa | | 14 | $\eta(t) = \sigma(t) / \dot{\gamma}$ | Apparent Viscosity | Time-dependent viscosity | Pa·s | | 15 | $\tan \delta = G'' / G'$ | Loss Tangent | Viscoelastic phase lag indicator | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the basis of **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**, **puncture testing**, **uniaxial compression**, and **oscillatory rheometry** — all critical for food product development, quality control, and shelf-life prediction in industries ranging from dairy to baked goods. ### 2.3 Mechanical Response of Biological Systems Biological materials exhibit **anisotropic**, **non-linear**, and **time-dependent** behavior. Their mechanical response is governed by: - **Cell turgor pressure** (in plant tissues): Internal hydrostatic pressure that stiffens cells - **Cell wall composition** (cellulose, hemicellulose, pectin): Determines stiffness - **Intercellular adhesion** (middle lamella pectin): Affects fracture behavior - **Matrix composition** (collagen, myofibrils, starch granules): Determines bulk texture - **Moisture content**: Strongly affects modulus (water acts as plasticizer) ### 2.4 Stress-Strain Curve Interpretation A typical uniaxial compression curve for biological food (e.g., apple, cheese) shows: 1. **Linear elastic region** — initial recoverable deformation 2. **Yield point (σ_y)** — onset of permanent damage 3. **Plastic/failure region** — cell rupture, fracture 4. **Bioyield point** — biological cell wall rupture (unique to plant tissue) 5. **Strain hardening or softening** — final structural collapse ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Generalized Hooke's Law for Isotropic Biological Materials For an isotropic linear elastic material, stress and strain are related through a 4th-order stiffness tensor. The generalized form in 3D is: $$ \sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} C_{ijkl} \cdot \varepsilon_{kl} $$ For an **isotropic** material, the stiffness tensor reduces to **two independent constants**: **E** (Young's modulus) and **ν** (Poisson's ratio). All other moduli can be derived. **Step 1:** Start with the 3D stress-strain relations for an isotropic elastic solid. $$ \varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_{xx} - \nu(\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}) \right] $$ **Step 2:** Apply the relation between Young's modulus, Shear modulus, and Poisson's ratio. $$ G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} $$ **Step 3:** Derive the bulk modulus K from volumetric strain. $$ K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} $$ **Step 4:** Establish the full E-G-K-ν relationship. $$ E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu) $$ > **Logic:** These relationships are derived assuming linear elasticity, isotropy, and small deformations — conditions that are approximately valid for the *initial* response of biological food materials under low stress. ### 3.2 Derivation: Maxwell Model for Viscoelastic Biological Materials The **Maxwell model** represents a material as a **spring (elastic)** and **dashpot (viscous)** in **series**. **Step 1:** For series arrangement, the total strain is the sum of spring strain and dashpot strain. $$ \gamma_{total} = \gamma_{spring} + \gamma_{dashpot} $$ **Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time to get strain rate. $$ \dot{\gamma}_{total} = \dot{\gamma}_{spring} + \dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute Hooke's law for spring and Newton's law for dashpot. $$ \dot{\gamma}_{spring} = \frac{\dot{\tau}}{G} \quad ; \quad \dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} = \frac{\tau}{\eta} $$ **Step 4:** Combine to obtain the Maxwell constitutive equation. $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \frac{\tau}{\eta} $$ **Step 5:** Rearrange into the canonical form. $$ \tau + \lambda \dot{\tau} = \eta \dot{\gamma} $$ where **λ = η/G** is the **relaxation time** (units: seconds). ### 3.3 Derivation: Stress Relaxation Function for Maxwell Model **Step 1:** Apply a sudden constant strain γ₀ at t = 0 and hold it. Thus $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ for t > 0. **Step 2:** The Maxwell equation simplifies to: $$ \tau + \lambda \dot{\tau} = 0 $$ **Step 3:** Solve the first-order ODE with initial condition τ(0) = Gγ₀. $$ \frac{d\tau}{\tau} = -\frac{dt}{\lambda} $$ **Step 4:** Integrate both sides. $$ \ln(\tau) = -\frac{t}{\lambda} + C $$ **Step 5:** Apply initial condition to find C = ln(Gγ₀). $$ \tau(t) = G \gamma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda} $$ **Step 6:** Define the relaxation modulus function. $$ G(t) = G \cdot e^{-t/\lambda} $$ > **Logic:** The stress in a Maxwell material **decays exponentially** with characteristic time λ. This matches observed behavior in foods like **dough** and **molten chocolate** where stress relaxes over time after deformation. ### 3.4 Derivation: Kelvin-Voigt Model for Creep Behavior **Step 1:** In the Kelvin-Voigt model, the spring and dashpot are in **parallel**, so stress is additive. $$ \tau = \tau_{spring} + \tau_{dashpot} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute Hooke's and Newton's laws. $$ \tau = G\gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma} $$ **Step 3:** Apply a constant stress τ₀ at t = 0 (creep test). $$ \tau_0 = G\gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma} $$ **Step 4:** Solve the first-order linear ODE with γ(0) = 0. $$ \gamma(t) = \frac{\tau_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\lambda} \right) $$ where **λ = η/G** is the **retardation time**. > **Logic:** Under constant load, a Kelvin-Voigt material exhibits **delayed elastic deformation** that asymptotically approaches a finite value. This is the typical behavior of **standing dough** under gravity (creep) and **fruits under storage load**. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Spring-Dashpot Models | Property | Hooke Solid | Newton Fluid | Maxwell Model | Kelvin-Voigt Model | |----------|-------------|--------------|---------------|---------------------| | Configuration | Spring only | Dashpot only | Series (S-D) | Parallel (S+D) | | Strain recovery | Immediate, 100% | None | Partial, time-dependent | Complete, time-dependent | | Stress relaxation | None | Instantaneous | Exponential decay | None | | Creep | None | Linear with time | Linear with time | Asymptotic | | Constitutive equation | $\tau = G\gamma$ | $\tau = \eta\dot{\gamma}$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \tau/\eta + \dot{\tau}/G$ | $\tau = G\gamma + \eta\dot{\gamma}$ | | Best food example | Crisp apple (initial) | Vegetable oil | Dough, molten chocolate | Cheese, fruit flesh | ### 3.6 Worked Example: Calculating Apple Tissue Stiffness > A cylindrical apple tissue sample of diameter 15 mm and height 20 mm is compressed uniaxially. A force of 12 N produces a deformation of 0.8 mm in the linear elastic region. Calculate Young's modulus. **Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area. $$ A = \pi \cdot (d/2)^2 = \pi \cdot (0.0075)^2 $$ $$ A = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the engineering stress. $$ \sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{12}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.79 \times 10^4 \ \text{Pa} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the engineering strain. $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04 $$ **Step 4:** Calculate Young's modulus using Hooke's law. $$ E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{6.79 \times 10^4}{0.04} = 1.698 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} $$ **Result:** E ≈ **1.7 MPa** — typical of fresh apple tissue (literature range: 0.5–10 MPa depending on variety, turgor, and test conditions). ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term "viscoelasticity" with reference to biological food systems. Give two examples. **Model Answer:** > Viscoelasticity is the property of materials that exhibit **both viscous and elastic characteristics** when undergoing deformation. In biological food systems, it means the material shows an **instantaneous elastic response** (recoverable, energy stored) **combined with a time-dependent viscous flow** (irrecoverable, energy dissipated). This dual behavior arises because food materials are composed of complex biopolymer networks and cellular structures that resist deformation in both spring-like and fluid-like manners. > > **Examples:** (1) Bread dough — shows elastic recoil and viscous flow during kneading. (2) Cheese — exhibits delayed elastic recovery after compression during mastication. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between elastic and plastic deformation in biological food materials. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Elastic Deformation | Plastic Deformation | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Definition | Reversible, recoverable change in shape | Permanent, irreversible change in shape | | Stress relation | Linear (Hooke's law: σ = Eε) | Occurs beyond yield point | | Recovery | Complete upon load removal | Does not recover | | Energy | Stored elastically | Dissipated as heat/damage | | Food example | Slight pressing of fresh apple | Permanent cell rupture in overripe fruit | In biological systems, elastic deformation usually corresponds to **cell wall bending and stretching**, while plastic deformation involves **cell wall rupture, membrane failure, and irreversible structural collapse**. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper]** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt viscoelastic models** for biological food systems. Derive the constitutive equations for both models and state the stress relaxation function for the Maxwell model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical sample of cheese (diameter 25 mm, height 30 mm) is subjected to uniaxial compression in a texture analyzer. The following data is recorded in the linear elastic region: | Force (N) | Deformation (mm) | |-----------|------------------| | 1.5 | 0.3 | | 3.0 | 0.6 | | 4.5 | 0.9 | | 6.0 | 1.2 | Calculate the **Young's modulus** of the cheese. If the same cheese has a Poisson's ratio of 0.35, calculate the **shear modulus (G)** and **bulk modulus (K)**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) Solution:** **Maxwell Model:** The Maxwell model consists of a **spring (elastic element)** and a **dashpot (viscous element)** connected in **series**. For a series arrangement, the total strain is the sum of the strains in each element: $$ \gamma_{total} = \gamma_{spring} + \gamma_{dashpot} $$ Differentiating with respect to time: $$ \dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{spring} + \dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} $$ Substituting Hooke's law ($\dot{\gamma}_{spring} = \dot{\tau}/G$) and Newton's law ($\dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} = \tau/\eta$): $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \frac{\tau}{\eta} $$ Rearranging into the canonical form: $$ \tau + \lambda \dot{\tau} = \eta \dot{\gamma} $$ where **λ = η/G** is the **relaxation time**. **Stress Relaxation Function for Maxwell Model:** Applying a sudden constant strain γ₀ at t = 0: $$ \tau(t) = G \gamma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda} $$ The stress **decays exponentially** with characteristic time λ. **Kelvin-Voigt Model:** The Kelvin-Voigt model consists of a **spring and dashpot in parallel**. The total stress is the sum of stresses in each element: $$ \tau = G\gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma} $$ Under constant stress τ₀, the strain response is: $$ \gamma(t) = \frac{\tau_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\lambda} \right) $$ This represents **delayed elastic deformation** (creep). | Feature | Maxwell | Kelvin-Voigt | |---------|---------|--------------| | Element arrangement | Series | Parallel | | Stress relaxation | Yes (exponential) | No | | Creep | Yes (linear) | Yes (asymptotic) | | Strain recovery | Partial | Complete | | Food example | Dough, molten chocolate | Cheese, fruit flesh | --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area of the cylindrical sample. $$ A = \pi \cdot r^2 = \pi \cdot (0.0125)^2 $$ $$ A = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** For the first data point, calculate stress. $$ \sigma_1 = \frac{F_1}{A} = \frac{1.5}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 3056 \ \text{Pa} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the corresponding strain. $$ \varepsilon_1 = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01 $$ **Step 4:** Verify the slope is constant (linear elastic region). Computing for each data point: | Force (N) | Stress (Pa) | Strain | E = σ/ε (Pa) | |-----------|-------------|--------|--------------| | 1.5 | 3056 | 0.010 | 305,600 | | 3.0 | 6112 | 0.020 | 305,600 | | 4.5 | 9167 | 0.030 | 305,567 | | 6.0 | 12223 | 0.040 | 305,575 | The slope is **constant at ~3.056 × 10⁵ Pa**, confirming linear elastic behavior. **Step 5:** Therefore, Young's modulus is: $$ E \approx 3.06 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa} = 0.306 \ \text{MPa} $$ **Step 6:** Calculate shear modulus using G = E/[2(1 + ν)]: $$ G = \frac{3.06 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.35)} = \frac{3.06 \times 10^5}{2.7} $$ $$ G = 1.133 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa} = 0.1133 \ \text{MPa} $$ **Step 7:** Calculate bulk modulus using K = E/[3(1 − 2ν)]: $$ K = \frac{3.06 \times 10^5}{3(1 - 0.7)} = \frac{3.06 \times 10^5}{0.9} $$ $$ K = 3.40 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa} = 0.340 \ \text{MPa} $$ **Final Results:** - **E = 0.306 MPa** - **G = 0.113 MPa** - **K = 0.340 MPa** These values are consistent with typical semi-hard cheese at room temperature. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **mechanical properties of biological food systems** with emphasis on stress, strain, Young's modulus, and Poisson's ratio. Discuss how cellular structure (cell wall, turgor pressure, middle lamella) influences these properties in plant-based foods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sample of apple flesh is tested in uniaxial compression. The sample has a diameter of 20 mm and height of 15 mm. During testing, a **bioyield point** is observed at a force of 18 N, at a deformation of 1.2 mm. Calculate the **bioyield stress** and **bioyield strain**. If the Young's modulus in the elastic region is found to be 1.2 MPa, determine the **force at a deformation of 0.6 mm** (within the elastic region). Comment on the significance of the bioyield point in food quality assessment. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) Solution:** **Mechanical Properties of Biological Food Systems:** Biological food materials are **composite, heterogeneous, anisotropic**, and **time-dependent** systems. Their mechanical behavior arises from the structural hierarchy of cells, tissues, and biopolymers. **1. Stress (σ):** The internal force per unit area resisting deformation, measured in Pa. In biological systems, both normal (tensile/compressive) and shear stresses are relevant — for example, biting applies shear, while cutting applies compression. **2. Strain (ε):** The relative deformation (ΔL/L₀), dimensionless. Biological materials can sustain large strains (up to 30–40%) before failure due to their cellular architecture. **3. Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of stress to strain in the linear elastic region, indicating stiffness. For plant tissues, E ranges from 0.1 MPa (soft fruit) to 100 MPa (woody tissue). **4. Poisson's Ratio (ν):** The negative ratio of lateral to axial strain, typically 0.2–0.5 for foods. A value near 0.5 indicates near-incompressibility (most water-rich foods). **Cellular Structure Influence on Mechanical Properties:** **a) Cell Wall:** - Primary composition: **cellulose microfibrils** embedded in a matrix of **hemicellulose and pectin** - Provides **tensile strength** and resistance to turgor pressure - The orientation of microfibrils determines **anisotropic** behavior **b) Turgor Pressure:** - Internal hydrostatic pressure (typically 0.2–1.0 MPa) maintained by the vacuole - Acts as a **pre-stress** that stiffens the cell wall - Higher turgor → higher effective modulus, crisper texture - Loss of turgor (wilting) → flaccid, soft texture **c) Middle Lamella:** - Pectin-rich layer that cements adjacent cells - Governs **intercellular adhesion** - Its enzymatic degradation (by polygalacturonase during ripening) causes **cell separation** and tissue softening **d) Cellular Arrangement:** - Tightly packed small cells → stiffer tissue - Large air spaces (porosity) → lower modulus - Tissue orientation (parallel vs. perpendicular to fibers) → anisotropic properties --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area. $$ A = \pi \cdot (0.010)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the bioyield stress. $$ \sigma_{bioyield} = \frac{F_{bioyield}}{A} = \frac{18}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \sigma_{bioyield} = 5.73 \times 10^4 \ \text{Pa} = 57.3 \ \text{kPa} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the bioyield strain. $$ \varepsilon_{bioyield} = \frac{\Delta L_{bioyield}}{L_0} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ \varepsilon_{bioyield} = 0.08 $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the strain at deformation 0.6 mm. $$ \varepsilon = \frac{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04 $$ **Step 5:** Calculate the stress at this strain using Hooke's law. $$ \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = (1.2 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.04 $$ $$ \sigma = 4.8 \times 10^4 \ \text{Pa} = 48 \ \text{kPa} $$ **Step 6:** Calculate the corresponding force. $$ F = \sigma \cdot A = (4.8 \times 10^4) \cdot (3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ F = 15.08 \ \text{N} $$ **Final Results:** - **Bioyield stress** = 57.3 kPa - **Bioyield strain** = 0.08 - **Force at 0.6 mm deformation** = 15.08 N **Significance of the Bioyield Point in Food Quality:** The bioyield point is the **critical stress at which cell wall rupture begins** in plant tissue. It is significant because: 1. **Texture Quality:** It marks the threshold of perceived firmness. Beyond this point, the consumer experiences a transition from "crisp" to "mealy" or "soft" texture. 2. **Ripeness Indicator:** As fruit ripens, pectin in the middle lamella degrades, reducing the bioyield stress. A lower bioyield force indicates **advanced ripening**. 3. **Post-Harvest Assessment:** Bioyield point measurements are used to evaluate storage effects, bruising susceptibility, and shelf-life. 4. **Processing Implications:** Determines optimum conditions for cutting, slicing, and minimal processing to avoid tissue damage. 5. **Sensory Correlation:** Strongly correlated with **sensory crispness** in apples, carrots, and other fresh produce. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (2-mark deduction typical):** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m in strain calculations, leading to errors of 10⁶ magnitude. Always convert to SI units BEFORE substitution. > 2. **Sign Convention in Poisson's Ratio (1-mark deduction):** Poisson's ratio is defined as the *negative* ratio of lateral to axial strain. The negative sign is part of the definition, not optional. > 3. **Confusing Hooke's Law with Newton's Law (2-mark deduction):** Stress = E × Strain (elastic), NOT Stress = η × Strain Rate (viscous). Mixing these gives nonsense. > 4. **Forgetting to State Assumptions (1-mark deduction):** The Maxwell/Kelvin models assume linearity, isotropy, and small deformations. State these explicitly for full credit. > 5. **Incorrect Maxwell Stress Relaxation (3-mark deduction):** The relaxation is *exponential decay*, not linear. The function is τ(t) = Gγ₀·exp(−t/λ), not a straight line. > 6. **Mermaid Labels:** Do not insert asterisks or bold formatting inside Mermaid node labels in your answer scripts. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Biological Systems and Mechanical Properties** - [x] **Biological food systems** are composite structures of biopolymers (proteins, polysaccharides, lipids) and cellular components. - [x] **Stress** = Force/Area (Pa); **Strain** = ΔL/L₀ (dimensionless). - [x] **Hooke's Law** (σ = Eε) describes **ideal elastic** behavior; **Newton's Law** (τ = η·γ̇) describes **ideal viscous** behavior. - [x] **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Shear Modulus (G)**, **Bulk Modulus (K)**, and **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** are the four fundamental elastic constants for isotropic materials. - [x] The three elastic constants are related by: **E = 2G(1 + ν) = 3K(1 − 2ν)**. - [x] **Viscoelasticity** is the combined elastic + viscous response characteristic of most biological foods. - [x] **Maxwell model** (series S-D): exhibits **stress relaxation** but no full strain recovery; constitutive equation: $\tau + \lambda \dot{\tau} = \eta \dot{\gamma}$; relaxation function: $\tau(t) = G\gamma_0 e^{-t/\lambda}$. - [x] **Kelvin-Voigt model** (parallel S+D): exhibits **delayed elastic recovery** (creep) but no instantaneous relaxation; constitutive equation: $\tau = G\gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$. - [x] **Burger model** combines Maxwell + Kelvin-Voigt in series; represents both stress relaxation AND creep. - [x] **Turgor pressure** (0.2–1.0 MPa) is a major contributor to plant tissue firmness. - [x] **Cell wall composition** (cellulose, hemicellulose, pectin) and **middle lamella integrity** govern tissue strength. - [x] **Bioyield point** marks cell wall rupture in plant tissue and is a critical quality/freshness parameter. - [x] **Poisson's ratio** for most food materials lies between **0.2 and 0.5** (water-rich foods approach 0.5). - [x] **Mechanical testing methods** include: uniaxial compression, puncture, bending, impact, creep, and stress relaxation tests. - [x] **Food examples of viscoelastic behavior:** dough (Maxwell-like), cheese (Kelvin-Voigt-like), meat (Burger-like), and fruit flesh (delayed elasticity).

topics.title: Force Deformation Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force Deformation Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Force Deformation Behaviour** describes the mechanical response of a material when an external **force** is applied, quantifying how the material's shape or dimensions change under load. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Food Rheology, this concept is the foundation for understanding the **texture**, **mechanical integrity**, and **processability** of food systems.

The two fundamental quantities governing this behavior are:

- **Stress ($\sigma$)** — the internal resisting force per unit area within a material, measured in Pascals (**Pa = N/m²**).
- **Strain ($\varepsilon$)** — the relative change in dimension (length, angle, or volume) of the material, a dimensionless quantity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Force Deformation Behaviour is the gateway concept that links classical mechanics to food material science. The relationship between force, stress, and strain forms the basis of all subsequent rheological models.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Rubber Band vs. The Caramel"**
>
> Imagine pulling two substances horizontally:
>
> - A **rubber band** stretches instantly when you pull, and snaps back perfectly the moment you let go. This is **elastic** force-deformation behaviour. The deformation is fully recoverable and instantaneous.
>
> - A piece of **soft caramel (toffee)** also stretches when pulled, but slowly. When released, it does not return fully — it deforms permanently. This is **viscoelastic / plastic** force-deformation behaviour. The response is **time-dependent** and partially permanent.
>
> - A drop of **honey** flows continuously as long as a force is applied. There is no recovery. This is **viscous** force-deformation behaviour.
>
> Most real foods — bread dough, cheese, fruits, gels — behave somewhere between these extremes, making their force-deformation curves the most important tool in food rheology.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in Force Deformation

| Metric | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force | $F$ | **N (Newton)** | External action producing deformation |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | Internal resistance per unit area |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Relative deformation |
| Modulus | $E$, $G$, $K$ | **Pa** | Stiffness of the material |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework: Step-by-Step Logic

Analysing the force-deformation behaviour of any food material follows a structured four-step approach used by KTU examiners:

1. **Identify the Mode of Loading** — Is the force tensile (pulling apart), compressive (squeezing together), or shear (sliding parallel planes)? Foods in processing (e.g., extrusion, chewing, cutting) often experience **combined loading**.

2. **Quantify the Stress** — Compute the internal stress by dividing the applied force by the area over which it acts. This normalises the result, allowing comparison between samples of different sizes.

3. **Measure the Resulting Strain** — Express the deformation as a fraction (or percentage) of the original dimension. This makes the strain independent of absolute sample size.

4. **Determine the Modulus** — The ratio of stress to strain in the linear region is the modulus, characterising the material's **stiffness**. A higher modulus = stiffer material.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why normalise force by area?** A thick rope and a thin thread may be made of the same material, but the rope can carry more force simply because it has more material. Stress ($\sigma = F/A$) removes the geometry effect, isolating the intrinsic material property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use strain instead of absolute deformation?** A 5 mm stretch means nothing without knowing the original length. Strain ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$) is scale-independent and allows comparison between a 10 mm and a 100 mm sample.

## 2.3 Types of Stress and Corresponding Moduli

Food materials can be subjected to three primary types of stress, each with its own modulus:

- **Normal Stress ($\sigma$)** acts perpendicular to a surface. Sub-types:
  - *Tensile stress* (pulling)
  - *Compressive stress* (pushing)
  - Related modulus: **Young's Modulus ($E$)**

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** acts parallel to a surface, causing layers to slide.
  - Related modulus: **Shear Modulus ($G$)**

- **Hydrostatic / Volumetric Stress ($P$)** acts uniformly from all directions.
  - Related modulus: **Bulk Modulus ($K$)**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> - **Texture Analysis:** Force-deformation curves of a food bitten by an artificial jaw simulate mastication. The area under the curve is correlated to perceived "firmness" or "chewiness".
> - **Process Design:** Extruders, mixers, and pumps rely on knowing whether a food mass will behave elastically (jamming risk) or viscously (flowing freely) under operating forces.
> - **Quality Control:** Fruits and vegetables are tested in compression to predict bruising during transport.
> - **Product Development:** Formulating gels, doughs, and sauces requires tuning the modulus to achieve desired mouthfeel.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA REFERENCE — Master these for the KTU Board Exam. Note: pipe symbols written as `\vert` to preserve table formatting.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma_{eng} = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Force over original cross-sectional area | **Pa** |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Deformation over original length | dimensionless |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = \dfrac{F}{A_{inst}}$ | Force over instantaneous area | **Pa** |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | Logarithmic strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Stiffness in tension/compression | **Pa** |
| 6 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F_{\parallel}}{A}$ | Parallel force over area | **Pa** |
| 7 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$ | Tangent of shear angle | dimensionless |
| 8 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Stiffness in shear | **Pa** |
| 9 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}$ | Resistance to uniform compression | **Pa** |
| 10 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Lateral-to-axial strain ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | E–G–ν Relationship | $E = 2G\,(1+\nu)$ | Elastic constant interconversion | **Pa** |
| 12 | E–K–ν Relationship | $E = 3K\,(1-2\nu)$ | Elastic constant interconversion | **Pa** |
| 13 | Compliance | $J = \dfrac{1}{E}$ | Inverse of modulus | **Pa⁻¹** |
| 14 | Engineering Shear Strain | $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ | Difference of principal strains | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Spring Law to Young's Modulus ($E = \sigma / \varepsilon$)

**Starting Premise — Hooke's Law for a spring:**
A spring extends by an amount $x$ when a force $F$ is applied. For an ideal spring:

$$F = k \cdot x$$

where $k$ is the spring constant (**N/m**). The negative sign in textbooks indicates a restoring force; here we consider magnitude.

**Step 1 — Relate deformation to strain.**
For a prismatic bar of original length $L_0$ that elongates by an amount $x$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{x}{L_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \varepsilon \cdot L_0$$

This conversion expresses the absolute deformation $x$ as a dimensionless strain $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2 — Relate force to stress.**
For a bar with original cross-sectional area $A_0$ under axial load $F$:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F = \sigma \cdot A_0$$

This conversion replaces force with a geometry-normalised quantity.

**Step 3 — Substitute into Hooke's law.**

$$F = k \cdot x$$

$$\sigma \cdot A_0 = k \cdot (\varepsilon \cdot L_0)$$

$$\sigma = \left(\frac{k \cdot L_0}{A_0}\right) \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Define Young's Modulus.**

$$E \equiv \frac{k \cdot L_0}{A_0}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon}$$

**Interpretation:** Young's Modulus $E$ is a material constant that depends only on the intrinsic stiffness of the substance — not on the geometry of the test sample. It has units of $\dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2}$ = **Pa**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$ ($E = 2G(1+\nu)$)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a 2D element under pure shear stress $\tau$ acting on its top and bottom faces. The element is a square of side $L_0$.

**Step 1 — Resolve shear into principal stresses.**
In classical elasticity, pure shear is equivalent to a state of biaxial stress where the principal stresses act at $45°$ to the shear planes:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension at 45°)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression at 90° to 45°)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply generalised Hooke's law in 2D.**
For a linear elastic isotropic material under biaxial stress:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_1 - \nu \cdot \sigma_2\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_2 - \nu \cdot \sigma_1\right)$$

The factor $\nu$ in each equation accounts for Poisson's contraction in the perpendicular direction.

**Step 3 — Substitute the principal stresses.**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left(\tau - \nu \cdot (-\tau)\right) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left(-\tau - \nu \cdot \tau\right) = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering shear strain.**
The total engineering shear strain is the difference between the two principal strains:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the relationship.**
By definition, $G = \tau / \gamma$, so $\gamma = \tau / G$. Equating with Step 4:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Cancelling $\tau$ (non-zero) from both sides and cross-multiplying:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

**Significance for food materials:** This equation tells us that if we know any two of $(E, G, \nu)$ for an isotropic food, the third is determined. For most foods, $\nu \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$, so $E$ is roughly $2.6G$ to $3G$.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Compression of a Cylindrical Food Sample

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Numerical]**
> A cylindrical cheese sample of diameter $d = 25$ mm and original height $h_0 = 40$ mm is subjected to a uniaxial compression test on a texture analyser. The force-deformation data exhibits a linear region in which a force of $F = 50$ N produces a deformation of $\Delta h = 1.5$ mm. Calculate:
> (i) the engineering stress,
> (ii) the engineering strain, and
> (iii) the Young's modulus of the cheese in kPa.

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (25)^2}{4} = \frac{625\pi}{4} = 156.25\pi \;\text{mm}^2$$

Converting to SI units for clarity:

$$A_0 = 156.25\pi \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2 \approx 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2$$

This step uses the standard area formula for a circle and converts mm² → m².

**Step 2 — Engineering stress.**

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{50 \;\text{N}}{156.25\pi \;\text{mm}^2} = \frac{50}{490.874}\;\text{N/mm}^2 \approx 0.10186\;\text{N/mm}^2$$

Converting to kPa (1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa = 1000 kPa):

$$\sigma_{eng} \approx 0.10186 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa} = 101.86 \;\text{kPa}$$

This step normalises the force by the contact area to obtain a material-specific quantity.

**Step 3 — Engineering strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \frac{1.5\;\text{mm}}{40\;\text{mm}} = 0.0375 = 3.75\%$$

This step expresses the deformation as a fraction of the original dimension, making it scale-independent.

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus.**

$$E = \frac{\sigma_{eng}}{\varepsilon_{eng}} = \frac{101.86\;\text{kPa}}{0.0375}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 2716.3\;\text{kPa} \approx 2.72\;\text{MPa}}$$

This value lies in the typical range for semi-hard cheeses (1–10 MPa), confirming the calculation is physically realistic. The result characterises the intrinsic stiffness of the cheese in the linear elastic region.

**Cross-check (consistency of units):**

$$E = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{dimensionless}} = \text{Pa} \;\;\checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow of Force–Deformation Behaviour

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the logical taxonomy of how food materials respond to an applied force, branching from the applied load to the four canonical responses, each tied to its governing equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Applied Force F] --> L1[Mode of Loading]
    L1 --> L1a[Tensile]
    L1 --> L1b[Compressive]
    L1 --> L1c[Shear]
    L1a --> L2[Stress sigma equals F over A0]
    L1b --> L2
    L1c --> L3[Shear Stress tau equals F parallel over A]
    L2 --> M1[Young's Modulus E equals sigma over epsilon]
    L3 --> M2[Shear Modulus G equals tau over gamma]
    M1 --> R0[Material Response Class]
    M2 --> R0
    R0 --> R1[Elastic: Hookean]
    R0 --> R2[Plastic: Permanent Set]
    R0 --> R3[Viscous: Newtonian]
    R0 --> R4[Viscoelastic: Time Dependent]
    R1 --> R1a[Instant Recovery]
    R1 --> R1b[Example: Apple Skin]
    R2 --> R2a[Yield Stress sigma y]
    R2 --> R2b[Example: Butter]
    R3 --> R3a[Flow Under Load]
    R3 --> R3b[Example: Honey]
    R4 --> R4a[Maxwell Model]
    R4 --> R4b[Kelvin-Voigt Model]
    R4 --> R4c[Example: Bread Dough]
    R4 --> R4d[Example: Cheese]
    R1b --> APP[Food Quality and Texture]
    R2b --> APP
    R3b --> APP
    R4c --> APP
    R4d --> APP
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Uniaxial Compression Test Apparatus

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the functional architecture of a typical **texture analyser** (the most common instrument for measuring force-deformation behaviour in food labs), as a block-level functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BL[Load Cell BL1] --> AMP[Signal Amplifier AMP1]
    AMP --> DAQ[Data Acquisition Unit DAQ1]
    DAQ --> PROC[Processor PROC1: sigma equals F over A0]
    PROC --> STRAIN[Strain Module ST1: epsilon equals dL over L0]
    STRAIN --> MOD[Modulus Calculator MOD1: E equals sigma over epsilon]
    MOD --> DISP[Display and Plotter DSP1: Force versus Deformation Curve]
    PROC --> STORE[Storage STO1]
    STRAIN --> STORE
    SAMP[Food Sample SAMP1] --> BL
    PROBE[Moving Crosshead PRB1] --> SAMP
    PROBE --> ENC[Position Encoder ENC1]
    ENC --> DAQ
```

**Reading the diagram:** The food sample sits on a fixed base; a motorised crosshead moves down at constant speed, compressing the sample. The **load cell** measures the instantaneous force $F(t)$, while the **position encoder** measures the displacement $\Delta L(t)$. Both signals are synchronised by the data acquisition unit to produce a continuous **force-deformation curve**, which is then converted by the processor into stress–strain data, from which $E$ (or $G$, $\sigma_y$, etc.) is calculated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms "engineering stress" and "engineering strain" as used in the force-deformation analysis of food materials. Why are these preferred over absolute force and deformation values?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Engineering Stress** is defined as the instantaneous applied force divided by the **original cross-sectional area** of the sample: $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$. (1 mark)
- **Engineering Strain** is defined as the change in length (deformation) divided by the **original length**: $\varepsilon_{eng} = \Delta L / L_0$. (1 mark)
- These normalised quantities are preferred because they are **independent of the geometry** of the test sample, allowing direct comparison of mechanical properties between samples of different sizes. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between elastic, plastic, and viscous deformation. Give one food example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Elastic** (1 mark) | Instantaneous, fully recoverable deformation governed by $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | A fresh apple's skin |
| **Plastic** (1 mark) | Permanent, non-recoverable deformation after the yield stress is exceeded | Soft butter under sustained load |
| **Viscous** (1 mark) | Continuous, time-dependent flow with no recovery; $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Honey pouring from a spoon |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Define stress, strain, and Young's modulus. Starting from Hooke's law for a spring, derive the relationship $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ for a prismatic bar. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- *Stress* ($\sigma$): Internal resisting force per unit area, $\sigma = F/A_0$ (Pa).
- *Strain* ($\varepsilon$): Fractional change in length, $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless).
- *Young's Modulus* ($E$): Ratio of uniaxial stress to corresponding strain in the linear elastic region, $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (Pa).

**Step 2 — Starting equation (1 mark):** Hooke's law for a spring: $F = k\,x$, where $k$ is the spring constant and $x$ is the extension.

**Step 3 — Express in terms of stress and strain (2 marks):**
Substitute $F = \sigma A_0$ and $x = \varepsilon L_0$:

$$\sigma A_0 = k (\varepsilon L_0) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma = \left(\frac{k L_0}{A_0}\right) \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Define and identify (2 marks):** Define $E \equiv k L_0 / A_0$, which is a material constant independent of geometry. Hence:

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \varepsilon}$$

This is the uniaxial form of Hooke's law for a linear elastic material.

---

#### (b) With a suitable diagram, describe the force-deformation (or stress-strain) behaviour of a viscoelastic food material. Label the key regions and discuss the significance of the yield stress. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Description of the curve (2 marks):** A viscoelastic food (e.g., bread dough, cheese) exhibits a stress–strain curve that begins with a steep, nearly linear elastic region, transitions smoothly into a non-linear region where the modulus decreases, and finally reaches a plateau or shows strain-softening before failure. Unlike a Hookean solid, the curve is **time-dependent** — its shape changes with the rate of loading.

**Step 2 — Labelled regions of the curve (3 marks):**
- **Region I — Linear Viscoelastic Region:** Small deformations; the material obeys $\sigma \approx E \varepsilon$ approximately. The slope here gives the **initial modulus** $E_0$.
- **Region II — Yield Region:** Stress approaches the **yield stress** $\sigma_y$, the maximum stress the material can sustain without permanent (plastic) deformation.
- **Region III — Plastic / Flow Region:** Beyond $\sigma_y$, the material flows or deforms permanently; for viscoelastic foods this may be a creep-dominated plateau.
- **Region IV — Failure Region:** The curve terminates at the **fracture / failure stress** $\sigma_f$.

**Step 3 — Significance of yield stress (2 marks):**
The yield stress $\sigma_y$ is a critical design parameter in food processing. Exceeding $\sigma_y$ leads to **irreversible structural damage** (e.g., cell rupture in fruits, network breakdown in gels). Engineers must ensure that processing stresses — mixing, pumping, chewing — remain below $\sigma_y$ to preserve product quality. It is also a key **textural attribute** in sensory perception (e.g., the "biting force" required for a food).

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

#### (a) Differentiate between tensile, compressive, and shear modes of deformation. Provide the corresponding strain definitions and governing equations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Tensile Deformation (2 marks):**
- The material is **stretched** along its axis.
- *Engineering strain:* $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$
- *Governing equation:* $\sigma = E \varepsilon$
- *Example:* Pulling a strand of mozzarella cheese.

**Step 2 — Compressive Deformation (2 marks):**
- The material is **squeezed** along its axis.
- *Engineering strain:* $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (negative sign convention for compression)
- *Governing equation:* $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ (same form, sign convention differs)
- *Example:* Biting into a biscuit; uniaxial compression of a fruit.

**Step 3 — Shear Deformation (2 marks):**
- Parallel layers of the material **slide** relative to each other.
- *Engineering shear strain:* $\gamma = \tan \theta = \Delta x / \Delta y$, where $\theta$ is the shear angle.
- *Governing equation:* $\tau = G \gamma$
- *Example:* Spreading cold butter on bread; flow in a pipe.

**Step 4 — Closing statement (1 mark):** All three modes are interrelated through the elastic constants; for an isotropic material, $E$, $G$, and $\nu$ are linked by $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.

---

#### (b) A cylindrical food sample of diameter $d = 20$ mm and original height $h_0 = 30$ mm is subjected to a uniaxial compression test. A force of $F = 100$ N produces a deformation of $\Delta h = 2$ mm in the linear region. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, and Young's modulus of the sample. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (1.5 marks):**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} = 100\pi \;\text{mm}^2 \approx 314.16\;\text{mm}^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Engineering stress (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{100\;\text{N}}{314.16\;\text{mm}^2} = 0.3183\;\text{N/mm}^2 = 0.3183\;\text{MPa} = 318.3\;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering strain (1.5 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \frac{2\;\text{mm}}{30\;\text{mm}} = 0.0667 = 6.67\%$$

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus (2 marks):**

$$E = \frac{\sigma_{eng}}{\varepsilon_{eng}} = \frac{0.3183\;\text{MPa}}{0.0667} \approx 4.775\;\text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 4.78\;\text{MPa} = 4775\;\text{kPa}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (0.5 mark):** The value of $E \approx 4.78$ MPa is consistent with a **soft to medium-firm food material** such as a ripe fruit flesh or soft cheese.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm² with N/m² or omitting the conversion $1\;\text{N/mm}^2 = 1\;\text{MPa} = 10^3\;\text{kPa}$. **Always carry units** through every calculation.
>
> 2. **Confusing Engineering and True Stress/Strain:** For small deformations (less than ~10%), engineering and true values are nearly identical. For large deformations, they diverge significantly. State the assumption.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** Tensile stress/strain are conventionally positive, compressive are negative. Be explicit.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Label Diagrams:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, every diagram must have axes labelled, units mentioned, and key regions identified (linear, yield, plastic, failure). A diagram without labels gets partial credit only.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Modulus Definition:** Even if the calculation is correct, students often forget to **define** $E$, $G$, or $\nu$ before using them. Always define the symbol first.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Poisson Effect:** Lateral contraction under axial load is governed by $\nu$. Many derivations lose marks for not invoking it.
>
> 7. **Not Linking Theory to Food Examples:** A 14-mark answer that omits food applications will lose at least 2 marks under the KTU Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, which explicitly tests **Course Outcome CO1: Application of rheological principles to food systems**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Force Deformation Behaviour**
>
> ✅ **Force ($F$):** External action; SI unit **Newton (N)**.
>
> ✅ **Stress ($\sigma = F/A_0$):** Internal resistance per unit area; SI unit **Pa = N/m²**.
>
> ✅ **Strain ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$):** Dimensionless relative deformation.
>
> ✅ **Young's Modulus ($E = \sigma/\varepsilon$):** Material stiffness in tension/compression.
>
> ✅ **Shear Modulus ($G = \tau/\gamma$):** Stiffness under shear; related to $E$ by $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.
>
> ✅ **Bulk Modulus ($K = -V\Delta P/\Delta V$):** Resistance to uniform volumetric compression.
>
> ✅ **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Ratio of lateral to axial strain (negative sign by convention); typical range 0.3–0.5 for foods.
>
> ✅ **Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):** Valid only in the **linear elastic region**; foods deviate at higher strains.
>
> ✅ **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$):** Critical stress above which **permanent deformation** occurs; vital for processing design.
>
> ✅ **Four Material Classes:**
>   - *Elastic* — Instantaneous recovery (apple skin, crisp vegetables).
>   - *Plastic* — Permanent deformation (butter, dough after kneading).
>   - *Viscous* — Time-dependent flow, no recovery (honey, oil).
>   - *Viscoelastic* — Combined elastic + viscous (bread dough, cheese, gels).
>
> ✅ **Food-Specific Insight:** Most real foods are **viscoelastic**, meaning their force-deformation response is **time- and temperature-dependent** — a critical consideration for KTU exam questions on texture and processing.
>
> ✅ **Units to Memorise:** 1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa = 10³ kPa = 1 N/mm².
>
> ✅ **Key Equation Triad:** $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$, $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniaxial Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Uniaxial Stress ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Uniaxial Stress** is the state of mechanical stress where a material body is subjected to a single normal force (tensile or compressive) acting along **only one principal axis**, while all other stress components in the perpendicular directions are strictly zero. Mathematically, in the principal coordinate system, the stress tensor reduces to: $$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{11} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ where $\sigma_{11} \neq 0$ is the only non-zero stress component.
