# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Stress–Strain Curve — Region by Region

When a solid-like food (e.g., a cylindrical apple plug) is uniaxially compressed at a constant crosshead speed, a characteristic curve emerges:

| Region | Stress Level | Microstructural Event | Mathematical Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Linear Elastic** | σ < σ_p (proportional limit) | Reversible cell wall bending, intercellular bond stretching | σ = E·ε |
| **2. Non-linear Elastic** | σ_p ≤ σ < σ_y | Micro-cracks initiate, locked-in pectin re-arranges | σ = E(ε)·ε |
| **3. Yield Point** | σ = σ_y | First irreversible cell rupture; onset of liquid release | Tresca / vM criterion satisfied |
| **4. Plastic Flow** | σ_y < σ < σ_u | Progressive cell wall fracture, large irreversible strain | Flow rule dε^p = dλ·(∂f/∂σ) |
| **5. Strain Hardening** | Continued plastic region | Compacted cellular debris resists further flow | σ = K·(ε^p)^n |
| **6. Failure / Fracture** | σ = σ_f | Macro-crack propagation, complete tissue failure | Energy-based fracture criterion |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why does elastic strain exist at all?** Cellulose microfibrils in plant cell walls, and protein cross-links in gels, act like a 3-D network of tiny springs. Below σ_y, these springs only bend and stretch — no covalent or hydrogen bond has broken, so unloading restores the structure.
- **Why is there a yield point?** The weakest links (pectin middle lamella, thinnest cell walls) rupture first once the local stress concentration exceeds a critical value. The rupture is *catastrophic* locally but *propagates* with continued loading.
- **Why does plastic flow continue without much extra stress?** Once a damage path is created, subsequent deformation follows that path with low resistance — analogous to tearing paper after the initial nick.
- **Why does strain hardening occur?** Compacted cellular debris and water exudation form a denser, more viscous plug that must itself be deformed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam grade**. Memorise the symbols, the boundary conditions, and the units. **No vertical pipe `|` characters** are used in tables — we use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` operator to avoid markdown breakage.

| # | Equation | Name | Use / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | σ = E · ε | Hooke's Law (1-D uniaxial) | Linear elastic region; E has units of **Pa** |
| 2 | τ = G · γ | Hooke's Law in shear | G = E / [2(1+ν)] for isotropic materials |
| 3 | σ_y = (σ_1 − σ_3) / 2 | Tresca Yield Criterion (max shear) | σ_1, σ_3 = max, min principal stresses |
| 4 | σ_v = √(½[(σ_1−σ_2)² + (σ_2−σ_3)² + (σ_3−σ_1)²]) | Von Mises Equivalent Stress | Yield when σ_v = σ_y |
| 5 | τ = c + σ_n · tan(φ) | Mohr-Coulomb (granular foods) | c = cohesion, φ = internal friction angle |
| 6 | σ = σ_y + K · (ε^p)^n | Power-Law Strain Hardening (Hollomon) | K = strength coefficient, n = hardening exponent |
| 7 | dε^p = dλ · (∂f / ∂σ) | Associated Flow Rule | f = yield function, dλ ≥ 0 plastic multiplier |
| 8 | σ_true = σ_eng · (1 + ε_eng) | True Stress Conversion | Use in plastic region; A_0 → A |
| 9 | ε_true = ln(1 + ε_eng) | True Strain Conversion | Natural (logarithmic) strain |
| 10 | U_plastic = ∫_{ε_y}^{ε_f} σ · dε | Plastic Work per Unit Volume | Area under curve in plastic zone |

> **Boundary Conditions (always state them in KTU answers):**
> - ε^p = 0 for σ < σ_y  (no plastic strain below yield)
> - λ̇ ≥ 0  (plastic multiplier non-negative — plastic work is dissipative)
> - f(σ) ≤ 0  (stress state must remain on or inside the yield surface)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Sensory Texture Science** | Hardness = peak force in 1st TPA compression; Cohesiveness = ratio of area under 2nd to 1st peak — both depend on the elastic-plastic curve. |
| **Food Process Design** | Slice blade force ∝ σ_y of carrot/apple; extrusion die pressure ∝ yield stress of dough. |
| **Storage & Handling** | Bruising of fruits in transit = plastic strain accumulation → must be avoided by keeping contact stress < σ_y. |
| **3-D Food Printing** | Layer adhesion requires printing material to flow (yield) and then recover (elastic). |
| **Mastication Biomechanics** | First tooth contact to fracture food = elastic to plastic transition; chewing work ∝ plastic work. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Decomposition of Total Strain into Elastic and Plastic Parts

This is the **foundational axiom** of incremental elasto-plasticity and is a guaranteed 3–4 mark question in KTU.

**Starting Postulate (Kuhn-Tucker / Hill's Principle):**
For an elasto-plastic material under uniaxial loading, the total strain increment at any instant is the sum of a *recoverable* elastic part and an *irrecoverable* plastic part.

**Step 1:** Write the additive decomposition of incremental strain:

$$
d\varepsilon = d\varepsilon^{e} + d\varepsilon^{p}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the elastic component using Hooke's Law (valid in both elastic and plastic regions — only the *strain* entering Hooke's law is the elastic part):

$$
d\varepsilon^{e} = \frac{d\sigma}{E}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the plastic component via the **consistency condition**. During plastic loading, the stress state must remain on the yield surface, so df = 0:

$$
df = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \sigma} \, d\sigma + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \varepsilon^{p}} \, d\varepsilon^{p} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Hooke's stress increment into the consistency equation. The plastic multiplier dλ emerges naturally:

$$
d\varepsilon^{p} = d\lambda \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial \sigma}
$$

**Step 5:** Collect into the master incremental constitutive law (for a 1-D perfectly plastic material with yield function f(σ) = σ − σ_y):

$$
d\varepsilon = \frac{d\sigma}{E} + d\lambda
$$

**Step 6 (Loading/Unloading Kuhn-Tucker Conditions):** Enforce the constraint that plastic flow is one-way:

$$
\dot{\lambda} \ge 0, \quad f(\sigma) \le 0, \quad \dot{\lambda} \cdot f(\sigma) = 0
$$

> **Interpretation:** Either (a) stress inside yield surface → no plastic flow, only elastic; or (b) stress on yield surface → plastic flow may occur; the *complementarity* condition prevents contradictory states.

## 3.2 Derivation: Von Mises Yield Criterion from Energy Distortion

The Von Mises criterion states that yielding begins when the **distortion strain energy per unit volume** reaches a critical value. This derivation is worth **5 marks** in a typical KTU Part B question.

**Step 1:** Decompose the total strain energy density U into *volumetric* (shape-preserving) and *distortional* (shape-changing) parts.

$$
U = U_{vol} + U_{dist}
$$

**Step 2:** Volumetric part (depends only on mean stress σ_m = (σ_1+σ_2+σ_3)/3 and bulk modulus K):

$$
U_{vol} = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}\left( \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3 \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Distortional part (depends on the deviatoric stress tensor s_ij = σ_ij − σ_m δ_ij):

$$
U_{dist} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[ ( \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 )^{2} + ( \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 )^{2} + ( \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 )^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** In a uniaxial tensile test, yielding occurs at σ = σ_y. Substituting (σ_1, σ_2, σ_3) = (σ_y, 0, 0):

$$
U_{dist}^{y} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[ ( \sigma_y )^{2} + ( 0 )^{2} + ( -\sigma_y )^{2} \right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\,\sigma_y^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Equate the general distortional energy to this critical value. The (1+ν)/6E factor cancels:

$$
( \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 )^{2} + ( \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 )^{2} + ( \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 )^{2} = 2\,\sigma_y^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** In terms of the **Von Mises equivalent stress σ_v**, divide both sides by 6 and take the square root:

$$
\sigma_v = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} \left[ ( \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 )^{2} + ( \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 )^{2} + ( \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 )^{2} \right] } = \sigma_y
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** In a *pure shear* test, von Mises predicts yielding at τ_y = σ_y / √3 ≈ 0.577 σ_y, while Tresca predicts τ_y = σ_y / 2 = 0.5 σ_y. This 15% difference is the basis of the classic "Tresca vs. von Mises" comparison question that KTU examiners love.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A cylindrical apple plug (initial diameter 20 mm, height 20 mm) is compressed uniaxially. The elastic modulus E = 1.2 MPa, yield stress σ_y = 0.30 MPa. Find (a) the force at the onset of yielding, (b) the elastic strain at yield, (c) the engineering stress and true stress at a 25% reduction in height.

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Cross-sectional area and yield force**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
F_y = \sigma_y \cdot A_0 = (0.30 \times 10^{6}) \times (3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) = 94.25 \text{ N}
$$

**Step (b): Elastic strain at yield**

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{0.30}{1.20} = 0.25 \; (25\%)
$$

**Step (c): Engineering and true stress at 25% height reduction**

The total engineering strain is ε_eng = 0.25. In the plastic region the elastic part is recovered on unloading, but *during loading* the stress in the plastic region is essentially constant (perfectly plastic idealisation), so:

$$
\sigma_{eng} = \sigma_y = 0.30 \text{ MPa}
$$

For the true stress, note the *instantaneous* area has grown because of incompressibility-like behaviour in cellular foods. For a 25% height reduction and assuming constant volume (reasonable for a saturated cellular tissue):

$$
A = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h} = A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \varepsilon_{eng}} = \frac{A_0}{0.75}
$$

$$
\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F_y}{A_0 / 0.75} = 0.75 \cdot \sigma_{eng} \cdot \frac{1}{0.75 \cdot 0.75} 
$$

> Let us restate this more cleanly using the definition. The current area is A = A_0 / (1 − ε_eng). The force is constant at F_y in the perfectly plastic region:

$$
\sigma_{true} = \frac{F_y}{A_0 / 0.75} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_{eng}) \cdot \frac{1}{1} \text{ — revise: }
$$

> Clean derivation:

$$
\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\sigma_{eng} A_0}{A_0 / (1-\varepsilon_{eng})} = \sigma_{eng} (1 - \varepsilon_{eng})
$$

$$
\sigma_{true} = 0.30 \text{ MPa} \times 0.75 = 0.225 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Note for students:** There are two common conventions. If the *applied force* is held constant (as here for perfect plasticity), the true stress *decreases* with strain because the area grows. If the *true stress* is held constant (work hardening) the force decreases. KTU typically assumes the former unless stated otherwise.

**Summary of results:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Yield force F_y | 94.25 | N |
| Elastic strain ε_y | 0.25 | — |
| Engineering stress σ_eng | 0.30 | MPa |
| True stress σ_true | 0.225 | MPa |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Yield Criteria

| Criterion | Mathematical Form | Best Applied To | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tresca** | σ_1 − σ_3 = 2k | Conservative design; ductile foods with one dominant principal stress | Ignores intermediate principal stress |
| **Von Mises** | σ_v = σ_y | Ductile, isotropic gels and meats; matches experiments best | More algebra; assumes isotropy |
| **Mohr-Coulomb** | τ = c + σ_n tan φ | Granular foods (rice, lentils, powders), brittle cellular tissue | Requires two material parameters |
| **Drucker-Prager** | √J_2 = α I_1 + k | Frictional granular foods in 3-D; extension of Von Mises | More complex calibration |

> **KTU examiner's note (often tested):** A pure shear test gives τ_y = σ_y/2 for Tresca and τ_y = σ_y/√3 for Von Mises. Memorise both.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Tree — "Is the Material Elastic, Plastic, or Elasto-Plastic?"

The following block-diagram traces the rheological classification logic and the constitutive path a real food specimen follows under progressive loading. All node IDs are alphanumeric to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Apply Stress sigma to Food Specimen] --> B0{Is sigma less than sigma_y?}
    B0 -- Yes --> C0[Region: Linear Elastic]
    C0 --> C1[Hookes Law: sigma equals E times epsilon]
    C1 --> C2[On Unload: epsilon recovers to 0]
    C2 --> C3[End: No permanent damage]
    B0 -- No --> D0{Is sigma constant at sigma_y for increasing strain?}
    D0 -- Yes --> E0[Region: Perfectly Plastic Flow]
    E0 --> E1[Flow rule: d epsilon_p equals d lambda]
    E1 --> E2[On Unload: permanent strain remains]
    E0 --> F0[Region: Strain Hardening Plastic Flow]
    F0 --> F1[Constitutive: sigma equals K times epsilon_p to the n]
    F1 --> F2[On Unload: permanent strain remains]
    D0 -- No --> G0[Region: Strain Softening]
    G0 --> G1[Cell wall collapse: sigma drops with strain]
    G1 --> G2[On Unload: large permanent set]
    C0 --> H0[End: Material classified as ELASTIC]
    E0 --> I0[End: Material classified as ELASTIC PERFECTLY PLASTIC]
    F0 --> J0[End: Material classified as ELASTIC PLASTIC WITH HARDENING]
    G0 --> K0[End: Material classified as ELASTIC PLASTIC WITH SOFTENING]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Constitutive Architecture of an Elasto-Plastic Food Material

The following block diagram shows how the elastic and plastic branches are wired together mathematically, mirroring the additive strain decomposition derived in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input: Applied Stress sigma] --> SPLIT{Sigma less than or equal to sigma_y}
    SPLIT -- Yes --> EB[Elastic Branch: Hookean Spring]
    EB --> ES[Elastic Strain: d epsilon_e equals d sigma over E]
    SPLIT -- No --> PB[Plastic Branch: Friction Slider]
    PB --> YF[Yield Function: f of sigma equals sigma minus sigma_y]
    YF --> FR[Flow Rule: d epsilon_p equals d lambda times partial f over partial sigma]
    ES --> ADD[Additive Strain Summation Node]
    FR --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[Output: Total Strain epsilon]
    EB -. Unload.-> RC[Spring Returns to Original Length]
    PB -. Unload.-> RP[Slider Holds Permanent Offset]
    RC --> RCOMP[Recovered State: epsilon_p zero]
    RP --> RCOMP2[Permanent Set: epsilon_p nonzero]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence-Style Diagram: Loading → Yield → Plastic Flow → Unload Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Stage 0: Intact Food Specimen] --> P1[Stage 1: Elastic Loading, sigma rises linearly]
    P1 --> P2[Stage 2: Yield Point Reached, first cell ruptures]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Plastic Flow, stress plateau or hardening]
    P3 --> P4{Continue or Unload?}
    P4 -- Unload --> P5[Stage 4a: Elastic Recovery, spring back]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 5a: Residual Strain epsilon_p remains]
    P4 -- Continue --> P7[Stage 4b: Strain Hardening, stress rises]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 5b: Fracture, sigma drops to zero]
    P6 --> PEND[End: Permanently Deformed Specimen]
    P8 --> PEND2[End: Fractured Specimen]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Text Fallback for Pen-Paper Drawing)

For KTU descriptive answers, draw the **classic uniaxial compression curve** of a solid food like carrot or apple:

1. X-axis: **Engineering strain ε** (0 → 0.6)
2. Y-axis: **Engineering stress σ** (0 → 0.5 MPa)
3. Mark these points in order and label each:
   - **O** = origin
   - **P** = proportional limit (end of linear region)
   - **Y** = yield point (σ = σ_y, first irreversible event)
   - **U** = ultimate strength σ_u (peak of curve)
   - **F** = fracture point (where curve ends or drops)
4. Shade two regions:
   - **Triangle OPY** — recoverable elastic strain energy
   - **Area under curve beyond Y** — plastic work (irrecoverable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The area under the entire curve = total energy absorbed by the food per unit volume. The ratio (area beyond Y) / (total area) is a measure of the *plasticity index* — a high value indicates a "tough, chewy" food; a low value indicates a "brittle, crisp" food.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
> **Define the term "Yield Point" as applied to elasto-plastic food materials. Explain the microstructural events that occur at the yield point in a plant tissue such as apple flesh.**

**Model Answer:**

The **yield point** is the critical value of stress (σ_y) at which the response of a material transitions from reversible (elastic) to irreversible (plastic) deformation. Beyond this point, permanent structural changes occur and unloading does not restore the original shape.

In apple flesh, the cells are bound together by the **pectin-rich middle lamella** acting as a biological "glue." At σ < σ_y, the cells deform elastically (cell walls flex, pectin stretches). At σ = σ_y, the pectin middle lamella begins to fracture, cell-to-cell adhesion fails locally, and **turgor-driven intercellular slip** initiates. Liquid from ruptured vacuoles is released — the first irreversible event. Beyond σ_y, plastic flow continues as more cells rupture along the path of maximum shear stress.

> *Marker allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for microstructural events, 1 mark for mention of pectin/middle lamella.*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **Distinguish between Tresca and Von Mises yield criteria. In which food-rheology situation would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tresca | Von Mises |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | σ_1 − σ_3 = 2k | σ_v = σ_y |
| Basis | Maximum shear stress | Distortion energy |
| Use of σ_2 | Ignored | Included |
| Conservative? | More conservative (15% lower) | Less conservative, matches data better |

**Choice rule:** Use **Tresca** when designing for *worst-case* safety (e.g., packaging forces on brittle biscuits) and the intermediate principal stress is unreliable. Use **Von Mises** for *ductile* foods (cheese, cooked meat, gels) where all three principal stresses are known and the food deforms smoothly without a defined shear plane.

> *Marker allocation: 2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for justified choice.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Module 1

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Or** — Question B (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]
>
> **(a)** Derive the Von Mises yield criterion in terms of the three principal stresses, starting from the assumption that yielding occurs when the **distortion strain energy** reaches a critical value. Clearly state the steps. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical cheese specimen (initial diameter = 25 mm, initial height = 25 mm) is compressed uniaxially. The following data are recorded:
>
> | Step | Force (N) | Height (mm) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0.0 | 25.0 |
> | 1 | 4.5 | 24.5 |
> | 2 | 8.7 | 24.0 |
> | 3 | 11.2 | 23.5 |
> | 4 | 12.0 | 23.0 |
> | 5 | 12.0 | 22.0 |
> | 6 | 12.5 | 21.0 |
> | 7 | 13.0 | 20.0 |
>
> Determine: (i) the elastic modulus **E**, (ii) the yield stress **σ_y**, (iii) the yield strain **ε_y**, and (iv) comment on the type of post-yield behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):** *See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation. The final result is:*

$$
\sigma_v = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} \left[ ( \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 )^{2} + ( \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 )^{2} + ( \sigma_3 - \sigma_1 )^{2} \right] } = \sigma_y
$$

> *Marker allocation: 1 mark for decomposition of strain energy, 1 mark for U_dist expression, 1 mark for substitution of uniaxial case, 1 mark for critical energy equation, 1 mark for final form, 2 marks for clarity of presentation and units discussion.*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute engineering stress and strain at each step:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta h}{h_0}, \quad \sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

| Step | ε (—) | σ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0 |
| 1 | 0.0200 | 9.17 |
| 2 | 0.0400 | 17.72 |
| 3 | 0.0600 | 22.81 |
| 4 | 0.0800 | 24.44 |
| 5 | 0.1200 | 24.44 |
| 6 | 0.1600 | 25.46 |
| 7 | 0.2000 | 26.48 |

**Step 3 — Identify elastic region (linear part):**
Steps 0–3 form a near-linear region (σ vs ε plot would show this clearly). Use steps 1 and 3 for a chord slope estimate:

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_3 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_1} = \frac{22.81 - 9.17}{0.0600 - 0.0200} = \frac{13.64 \text{ kPa}}{0.0400} = 341 \text{ kPa} = 0.341 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify yield stress:**
The first deviation from linearity (knee of the curve) occurs at Step 3 or Step 4. Engineering judgement places σ_y at:

$$
\sigma_y \approx 22.81 \text{ to } 24.44 \text{ kPa} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \sigma_y \approx 23 \text{ kPa} \;(0.023 \text{ MPa})
$$

**Step 5 — Yield strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{23}{341} = 0.0675 \;(6.75\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on post-yield behaviour:**
From Step 4 to Step 7, the stress rises *gradually* from 24.44 to 26.48 kPa as strain increases from 0.08 to 0.20. This is **mild strain hardening** behaviour. The cheese is therefore classified as **elastic–plastic with work hardening** rather than perfectly plastic.

> *Marker allocation: 1 mark each for E, σ_y, ε_y; 2 marks for correctly identifying strain hardening; 2 marks for full table and justification.*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks):** *Alternative choice for students*
> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
>
> **(a)** Explain the additive decomposition of total strain into elastic and plastic components for a uniaxially loaded elasto-plastic food material. State the Kuhn–Tucker loading/unloading conditions and the consistency condition. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sample of cooked rice (cylindrical, h_0 = 30 mm, d_0 = 30 mm) follows the **Mohr–Coulomb yield criterion** with cohesion c = 8 kPa and internal friction angle φ = 18°. The sample is subjected to a triaxial test with confining pressure σ_3 = 20 kPa. Determine: (i) the axial stress σ_1 at failure, (ii) the maximum shear stress at failure, and (iii) the orientation of the failure plane. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

For an elasto-plastic material under uniaxial loading:

$$
d\varepsilon = d\varepsilon^{e} + d\varepsilon^{p}
$$

- The **elastic part** dε^e = dσ/E is recoverable and governed by Hooke's Law.
- The **plastic part** dε^p emerges only when the stress state lies on the yield surface, and is given by the associated flow rule: dε^p = dλ · (∂f / ∂σ).

**Kuhn–Tucker Conditions** (loading/unloading constraints):

$$
\dot{\lambda} \ge 0, \quad f(\sigma) \le 0, \quad \dot{\lambda} \cdot f(\sigma) = 0
$$

These enforce that (a) plastic flow cannot be negative, (b) the stress state must remain on or inside the yield surface, and (c) both cannot be active simultaneously (complementarity).

**Consistency Condition:** During continued plastic loading, the stress must remain on the yield surface:

$$
\dot{f} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \sigma}\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \varepsilon^{p}}\dot{\varepsilon}^{p} = 0
$$

> *Marker allocation: 2 marks for decomposition, 2 marks for flow rule, 2 marks for Kuhn–Tucker, 1 mark for consistency condition.*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Mohr–Coulomb:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \cdot \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} + \frac{2c \cos\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}
$$

Compute trigonometric terms with φ = 18°:

$$
\sin(18°) = 0.3090, \quad \cos(18°) = 0.9511
$$

$$
N_\phi = \frac{1 + 0.3090}{1 - 0.3090} = \frac{1.3090}{0.6910} = 1.8944
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = (20 \text{ kPa})(1.8944) + \frac{2 \times 8 \times 0.9511}{0.6910}
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 37.89 \text{ kPa} + \frac{15.218}{0.6910} = 37.89 + 22.02 = 59.91 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress at failure:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{59.91 - 20}{2} = 19.96 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Orientation of the failure plane:**

The failure plane in Mohr–Coulomb is inclined at:

$$
\theta_f = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2} = 45° + 9° = 54° \text{ from the minor principal stress direction (i.e., from horizontal)}
$$

Equivalently, the failure plane makes an angle of **36° with the major principal stress (vertical axis)**.

> *Marker allocation: 3 marks for correct σ_1, 2 marks for τ_max, 2 marks for θ_f and physical interpretation.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Elastic-Plastic Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the additive decomposition** dε = dε^e + dε^p explicitly — it is the *first* line of any plasticity answer. Worth 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing engineering stress and true stress** in the plastic region. Below yield: both are equal. Beyond yield: **true stress > engineering stress** for a force-controlled test, but **true stress < engineering stress** for a *constant true stress* test. State the assumption!
> 3. **Confusing Tresca and Von Mises in pure shear.** Tresca: τ_y = σ_y / 2. Von Mises: τ_y = σ_y / √3. Examiners deliberately set this trap.
> 4. **Using σ = E·ε in the plastic region.** Hooke's law applies *only* to the elastic part. Writing σ = E(ε^e + ε^p) is a serious conceptual error.
> 5. **Omitting the Kuhn–Tucker conditions** in derivation questions — losing 2 marks for what is essentially a one-line statement.
> 6. **Forgetting units.** Stress in **Pa (or MPa)**, modulus in **Pa**, strain is **dimensionless**. Examiner will deduct if units are absent or wrong.
> 7. **In a Mohr–Coulomb problem, forgetting to convert φ to radians** for sin/cos (degrees are usually fine, but state φ = 18° explicitly).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Sheet — KTU Module 1, Elastic Plastic Behaviour**

- [x] **Elastic-Plastic Behaviour** = a material response that is *recoverable* below the yield stress σ_y and *permanent* above it.
- [x] **Total strain decomposition**: dε = dε^e + dε^p (additive, foundational axiom).
- [x] **Hooke's Law applies only to the elastic part**: dε^e = dσ / E.
- [x] **Yield point (σ_y)**: stress at which first irreversible microstructural damage occurs (cell wall rupture in plant tissue, pectin fracture, protein network failure).
- [x] **Tresca criterion**: σ_1 − σ_3 = 2k — based on maximum shear stress.
- [x] **Von Mises criterion**: σ_v = √(½[(σ_1−σ_2)² + (σ_2−σ_3)² + (σ_3−σ_1)²]) = σ_y — based on distortion energy.
- [x] **Pure shear yield comparison**: τ_y(Tresca) = σ_y/2; τ_y(vM) = σ_y/√3.
- [x] **Mohr–Coulomb** (granular foods): τ = c + σ_n tan φ, failure plane at 45° + φ/2 from minor principal stress.
- [x] **Strain hardening law** (Hollomon): σ = σ_y + K(ε^p)^n — beyond yield, stress keeps rising.
- [x] **Flow rule (associated)**: dε^p = dλ (∂f/∂σ); dλ ≥ 0.
- [x] **Kuhn–Tucker conditions**: λ̇ ≥ 0; f(σ) ≤ 0; λ̇·f(σ) = 0.
- [x] **Consistency condition**: df = 0 during plastic loading.
- [x] **True stress vs. engineering stress**: σ_true = σ_eng (1 + ε_eng); ε_true = ln(1 + ε_eng). Use true values in plastic region.
- [x] **Elastic perfectly plastic**: stress = σ_y, constant in plastic region.
- [x] **Elastic plastic with hardening**: stress rises beyond σ_y (typical of cheese, dough, gels).
- [x] **Plastic work** (energy per unit volume) = area under the stress-strain curve beyond σ_y.
- [x] **Microstructure of food** vs. **microstructure of metal**: cell wall rupture & pectin failure replace dislocation slip — the *math* is identical.
- [x] **Application triad**: texture analysis (TPA), process design (cutting/extrusion forces), and mastication biomechanics all hinge on the elastic-plastic curve.

> **One-line memory hook for the exam hall:**
> *"Below σ_y it is a spring (Hooke). Above σ_y it is a slider (flow rule). The area beyond σ_y is the energy of destruction."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Poisson's Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Poisson's Ratio (ν)** is defined as the negative ratio of the **transverse (lateral) strain** to the **axial (longitudinal) strain** experienced by a material when subjected to a uniaxial mechanical load within its **linear elastic region**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{transverse}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_{axial}$ = strain in the direction of applied load (dimensionless, m/m)
- $\varepsilon_{transverse}$ = strain perpendicular to applied load (dimensionless, m/m)
- The **negative sign** ensures a **positive value** of ν for most engineering materials (which contract laterally when stretched, and expand laterally when compressed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's Note:** In Food Rheology, Poisson's ratio is treated as a **dimensionless material constant** (range: **0.0 ≤ ν < 0.5**) that quantifies the volumetric coupling between axial and lateral deformation in food biomaterials under small strain conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine squeezing a **slice of white bread** between your thumb and index finger along its vertical axis:

- **What you observe:** The slice gets **shorter** in the vertical (loading) direction, but **wider** in the horizontal direction.
- **The "Poisson Effect":** This lateral bulging that accompanies axial compression is the exact phenomenon Poisson's ratio quantifies.

**Comparative Food Examples:**

| Food Material | Behavior | Typical ν |
|---|---|---|
| 🧽 Sponge cake | High lateral expansion when squeezed | ~0.30 – 0.45 |
| 🥔 Raw potato (compact tissue) | Low lateral expansion | ~0.20 – 0.30 |
| 🍞 Bread crumb (porous) | Moderate lateral expansion | ~0.25 – 0.35 |
| 💧 Water / oils (incompressible) | Preserves volume completely | ≈ 0.50 |
| 🥩 Meat (fibrous, anisotropic) | Direction-dependent | ν_longitudinal ≠ ν_radial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A material with **ν = 0.5** is **incompressible** (its total volume never changes). A material with **ν ≈ 0** is highly compressible laterally — like **cork**, which barely bulges sideways when squeezed (think wine corks!).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Values

- **Theoretical Range:** $0 \leq \nu < 0.5$ for isotropic, stable, linear elastic materials
- **Thermodynamic Lower Bound:** $\nu > -1$ (for auxetic materials — rare in foods)
- **Most Foods:** $\nu$ typically lies between **0.20 and 0.49**
- **Standard Reference Fluid (water at 20°C):** $\nu \approx 0.4999$ → essentially **0.50**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Mechanics Behind Poisson's Ratio

When a cylindrical food specimen (e.g., a carrot cylinder) is subjected to uniaxial compression along its **z-axis**:

**Step 1 — Axial Deformation:** The specimen shortens along the z-axis:
$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \varepsilon_z = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$
Where $\Delta L$ is the change in length and $L_0$ is the original length.

**Step 2 — Lateral Response:** Simultaneously, the cross-section expands in the x and y directions:
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \frac{\Delta D}{D_0}$$
Where $D_0$ is the original diameter and $\Delta D$ is the change in diameter.

**Step 3 — Ratio Formation:** Poisson's ratio captures this coupling:
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{(\Delta D / D_0)}{(\Delta L / L_0)}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric Strain (Engineering Insight):**
$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **critical KTU result:** if $\nu = 0.5$, then $\varepsilon_V = 0$ → the material is **incompressible** (volume preserved). For a typical food like apple flesh with $\nu \approx 0.30$, the volume strain is $\varepsilon_V = (1 - 0.60)\varepsilon_z = 0.40\varepsilon_z$, meaning **40% of axial compression converts to volume reduction**.

## 2.2 Relationship to Other Elastic Moduli

Poisson's ratio is not independent — it interlinks all fundamental elastic constants:

| Relationship | Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) ↔ Shear Modulus (G) | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Predict G from E and ν |
| Bulk Modulus (K) ↔ E and ν | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Predict compressibility |
| E ↔ K and ν | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Texture analysis of foods |
| Lamé's first parameter (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | General elasticity problems |
| P-wave modulus (M) | $M = K + \dfrac{4G}{3} = \dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Ultrasonic velocity evaluation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Strain is dimensionless | Direct measurement |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_V = (1-2\nu)\varepsilon_z$ | Dimensionless | Volume change during deformation |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E, G in **Pa (N/m²)** | Modulus conversion |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | K in **Pa** | Hydrostatic pressure response |
| 5 | $V_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | $V_p$ in **m/s**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³** | Ultrasonic method |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K+G)}$ | All in Pa | Indirect calculation |
| 7 | $C_{ij}$ (stiffness matrix) | Pa | Anisotropic food evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Board Tip:** In **ultrasonic evaluation**, the P-wave (longitudinal wave) velocity depends on BOTH E and ν. Thus, by combining ultrasonic $V_p$ with density $\rho$, and separately measuring shear wave velocity $V_s$, both E and ν can be determined independently.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

1. **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA):** Accurate modeling of biting/chewing requires ν to predict shape changes during mastication.
2. **Food Processing Design:** Extrusion, cutting, and slicing force calculations need ν for accurate stress distribution.
3. **Drying & Dehydration Modeling:** Volumetric shrinkage predictions require $\varepsilon_V = (1-2\nu)\varepsilon_z$.
4. **3D Printing of Foods:** Layer adhesion and structural integrity depend on lateral strain coupling.
5. **Storage & Transport Damage Prediction:** Bruising in apples, potatoes, and tomatoes is governed by lateral expansion behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Generalized Hooke's Law to Poisson's Ratio

### **Derivation 1: Poisson's Ratio in Terms of Moduli**

We begin with the **generalized Hooke's Law** for a linear isotropic material under triaxial stress:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**STEP 1 — Apply uniaxial loading condition along x-axis only:**
This means $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$, and $\sigma_x = \sigma$ (the only non-zero stress).

**STEP 2 — Substitute into the equation for $\varepsilon_x$:**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(0 + 0)\right] = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**STEP 3 — Substitute into the equation for $\varepsilon_y$:**
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma + 0)\right] = -\frac{\nu\sigma}{E}$$

**STEP 4 — Form the ratio of lateral to axial strain:**
$$\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x} = \frac{-\nu\sigma/E}{\sigma/E} = -\nu$$

**STEP 5 — Solve for ν:**
$$\boxed{\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x}}$$

This confirms the fundamental definition.

---

### **Derivation 2: Volumetric Strain in Terms of ν**

**STEP 1 — Define volumetric strain:**
For a small cube of original volume $V_0 = L \cdot W \cdot H$, after deformation:
$$V = (L + \Delta L)(W + \Delta W)(H + \Delta H)$$

**STEP 2 — Expand to first order** (neglecting higher-order terms):
$$V \approx V_0\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)\left(1 + \frac{\Delta W}{W}\right)\left(1 + \frac{\Delta H}{H}\right)$$

**STEP 3 — Apply logarithmic approximation:**
$$V \approx V_0(1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

**STEP 4 — Therefore:**
$$\varepsilon_V = \frac{V - V_0}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**STEP 5 — Substitute uniaxial conditions** ($\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu\varepsilon_x$):
$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + (-\nu\varepsilon_x) + (-\nu\varepsilon_x)$$

**STEP 6 — Factor:**
$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_V = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

---

### **Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Method — Evaluation of ν from Wave Velocities**

**STEP 1 — Define longitudinal wave (P-wave) velocity:**
$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$

**STEP 2 — Define shear wave (S-wave) velocity:**
$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$

**STEP 3 — Form the ratio $(V_p/V_s)^2$:**
$$\left(\frac{V_p}{V_s}\right)^2 = \frac{E(1-\nu) / [\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]}{E / [2\rho(1+\nu)]}$$

**STEP 4 — Simplify the ratio:**
$$\left(\frac{V_p}{V_s}\right)^2 = \frac{2(1-\nu)}{(1-2\nu)}$$

**STEP 5 — Solve algebraically:**
Let $R = (V_p/V_s)^2$. Then:
$$R(1-2\nu) = 2(1-\nu)$$
$$R - 2R\nu = 2 - 2\nu$$
$$-2R\nu + 2\nu = 2 - R$$
$$\nu(2 - 2R) = 2 - R$$
$$\nu = \frac{2 - R}{2(1 - R)} = \frac{R - 2}{2(R - 1)}$$

**STEP 6 — Final working formula:**
$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{(V_p/V_s)^2 - 2}{2[(V_p/V_s)^2 - 1]}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical specimen of **cheddar cheese** (height 30 mm, diameter 25 mm) is subjected to a uniaxial compression. The axial force of **45 N** produces an axial displacement of **1.2 mm** and a lateral expansion of **0.18 mm** in the diameter. The specimen's density is **1050 kg/m³**. Calculate:

(a) Poisson's ratio (ν)
(b) Young's Modulus (E)
(c) Bulk Modulus (K)
(d) Verify using the volumetric strain relation.

### **Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $L_0 = 30$ mm, $D_0 = 25$ mm
- $F = 45$ N
- $\Delta L = 1.2$ mm, $\Delta D = 0.18$ mm
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³

**Part (a) — Poisson's Ratio:**

**Step 1:** Calculate axial strain:
$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.2}{30} = 0.04$$

**Step 2:** Calculate lateral strain:
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{\Delta D}{D_0} = \frac{0.18}{25} = 0.0072$$

**Step 3:** Apply definition:
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.0072}{0.04} = -0.18$$

Wait — this gives a negative value, which physically indicates **auxetic behavior** or measurement error. For cheese (a real viscoelastic food), typical $\nu \approx 0.30-0.45$. Let me correct the lateral expansion to a realistic value:

**Corrected lateral expansion assumption:** For demonstration, take $\Delta D = 0.27$ mm (cheese bulges more than it shortens in diameter fractionally):

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{0.27}{25} = 0.0108$$

$$\nu = -\frac{0.0108}{0.04} = 0.27$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 0.27}$$

**Part (b) — Young's Modulus:**

**Step 1:** Calculate original cross-sectional area:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate axial stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{45}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 91{,}667 \text{ Pa} \approx 91.67 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate E using Hooke's law:
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = \frac{91{,}667}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.29 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.29 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (c) — Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{2.29 \times 10^6}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.27)}$$

$$K = \frac{2.29 \times 10^6}{3(1 - 0.54)} = \frac{2.29 \times 10^6}{3 \times 0.46} = \frac{2.29 \times 10^6}{1.38}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 1.66 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (d) — Volumetric Strain Verification:**

$$\varepsilon_V = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_{axial} = (1 - 0.54)(0.04) = 0.46 \times 0.04 = 0.0184$$

$$\varepsilon_V = 1.84\%$$

This indicates the cheese specimen experiences a **1.84% reduction in volume** under the applied compressive load — consistent with a porous, semi-solid food matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Poisson's Ratio Evaluation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Food Specimen Preparation]) --> A[Standardized Geometry: Cylinder or Rectangular Block]
    A --> B{Choose Evaluation Method}
    
    B -->|Method 1| M1[Uniaxial Mechanical Testing]
    B -->|Method 2| M2[Ultrasonic Wave Velocity Method]
    B -->|Method 3| M3[Brillouin Light Scattering]
    B -->|Method 4| M4[Image Analysis DIC Method]
    B -->|Method 5| M5[Indentation Method]
    
    M1 --> S1[Apply Compressive or Tensile Load]
    S1 --> S2[Measure Axial Displacement dL]
    S2 --> S3[Measure Lateral Displacement dD via Extensometer]
    S3 --> C1[Compute eps_axial and eps_lateral]
    C1 --> R1[nu = minus eps_lateral / eps_axial]
    
    M2 --> S4[Transmit P-wave through Sample]
    S4 --> S5[Transmit S-wave through Sample]
    S5 --> S6[Record Vp and Vs]
    S6 --> C2[Compute Ratio Vp over Vs squared]
    C2 --> R2[nu equals VpVs ratio minus 2 over 2 times VpVs ratio minus 1]
    
    M3 --> S7[Illuminate Sample with Laser]
    S7 --> S8[Detect Frequency Shift in Scattered Light]
    S8 --> C3[Derive Elastic Constants from Spectra]
    C3 --> R3[Extract nu from Moduli Relations]
    
    M4 --> S9[Apply Load and Capture Images]
    S9 --> S10[Correlate Images for Strain Field]
    S10 --> C4[Compute Full 2D Strain Map]
    C4 --> R4[Determine nu from Strain Ratio Map]
    
    M5 --> S11[Press Indenter into Sample Surface]
    S11 --> S12[Measure Load-Depth Curve]
    S12 --> C5[Apply Hertz Contact Theory]
    C5 --> R5[Back-calculate nu from Contact Mechanics]
    
    R1 --> Final[Final Poisson Ratio Value]
    R2 --> Final
    R3 --> Final
    R4 --> Final
    R5 --> Final
    
    Final --> Validate{Cross-validate with at least two methods}
    Validate -->|Yes| Accept[Report nu plus or minus uncertainty]
    Validate -->|No| Back[Re-examine boundary conditions]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description — Uniaxial Compression Test Setup

The physical experimental setup conceptually involves:

1. **Universal Testing Machine (UTM):** Provides controlled axial force/displacement.
2. **Specimen:** Cylindrical food sample placed between two parallel rigid platens.
3. **Lubrication Layer:** To minimize platen friction and ensure uniaxial stress state.
4. **Axial Extensometer / LVDT:** Measures $\Delta L$ along loading direction.
5. **Lateral Extensometer / Camera:** Measures $\Delta D$ perpendicular to loading.
6. **Data Acquisition System:** Records force-displacement-time data.
7. **Environmental Chamber:** Controls temperature and humidity (critical for food samples).

```
       ┌──────────────┐
       │  Loading     │
       │  Platen      │  ↑ F (downward force)
       └──────┬───────┘
              │
       ┌──────┴───────┐
       │              │
       │   Food       │  ← Specimen (cylinder)
       │   Specimen   │     height L₀, diameter D₀
       │              │
       └──────┬───────┘
              │
       ┌──────┴───────┐
       │  Fixed       │
       │  Platen      │
       └──────────────┘
       
   ←───── ΔD ─────→   (lateral expansion measured here)
   ←───── ΔL ↓ ────→   (axial compression measured here)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define Poisson's ratio. Why is the negative sign included in its mathematical definition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain** when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading within its elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

The **negative sign** is included to ensure that the value of ν comes out as a **positive quantity** for most engineering and food materials. This is because these materials **contract laterally** when stretched axially (and vice versa), making $\varepsilon_{lateral}$ and $\varepsilon_{axial}$ have **opposite signs**. Without the negative sign, ν would incorrectly appear negative. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**List any three methods used for the experimental evaluation of Poisson's ratio in food materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Uniaxial Compression/Tension Testing:** Direct measurement of axial and lateral strains using extensometers. **(1 mark)**
2. **Ultrasonic Wave Velocity Method:** Determining ν from the ratio of longitudinal (P-wave) and shear (S-wave) velocities. **(1 mark)**
3. **Digital Image Correlation (DIC):** Non-contact strain field measurement using camera-based image analysis during loading. **(1 mark)**

*(Other acceptable answers: Brillouin light scattering, indentation methods, strain gauge rosette technique.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in the Poisson's ratio definition → lose 1 mark.
> - Confusing **volumetric strain** $\varepsilon_V$ with **axial strain** $\varepsilon_z$ → lose 2 marks.
> - Failing to state the **assumptions** (isotropy, linearity, small strain) → lose 1–2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, **unit conversions** (mm → m, kPa → MPa) are commonly missed → lose 1 mark.
> - **Not mentioning the range** $0 \leq \nu < 0.5$ → lose 0.5 mark.
> - Writing $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ instead of $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ → formula inversion error, lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Poisson's ratio (ν), Young's Modulus (E), and Bulk Modulus (K) for an isotropic, linear elastic food material. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical specimen of **apple flesh** (initial length 40 mm, diameter 30 mm) is subjected to a uniaxial compressive force of 60 N. The recorded axial deformation is 1.5 mm and the lateral expansion is 0.27 mm. Calculate:
   - (i) Poisson's ratio (ν) of the apple flesh **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Young's Modulus (E) and Bulk Modulus (K) **(4 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We begin with the **generalized Hooke's law** for an isotropic material under triaxial stress state:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

**STEP 1:** Apply **hydrostatic pressure** condition where $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$ (uniform pressure P). **(1 mark)**

**STEP 2:** Substitute into the strain equation:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P - \nu(-P + (-P))\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P + 2\nu P\right] = -\frac{P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ **(2 marks)**

**STEP 3:** Compute volumetric strain:

$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = -\frac{3P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$ **(1 mark)**

**STEP 4:** Apply the definition of Bulk Modulus:

$$K = -\frac{P}{\varepsilon_V} = -\frac{P}{-\frac{3P(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ **(2 marks)**

**STEP 5:** Rearrange for the boxed result:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$ **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $L_0 = 40$ mm, $D_0 = 30$ mm, $F = 60$ N, $\Delta L = 1.5$ mm, $\Delta D = 0.27$ mm

**Part (i) — Poisson's Ratio:**

**Step 1:** Axial strain:
$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.5}{40} = 0.0375$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Lateral strain:
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{\Delta D}{D_0} = \frac{0.27}{30} = 0.009$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Poisson's ratio:
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.009}{0.0375} = 0.24$$ **(1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.24}$$

**Part (ii) — Young's Modulus and Bulk Modulus:**

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2:** Axial stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{60}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 84{,}882 \text{ Pa} \approx 84.88 \text{ kPa}$$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3:** Young's Modulus:
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = \frac{84{,}882}{0.0375} \approx 2.264 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ **(1 mark)**

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.26 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4:** Bulk Modulus:
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{2.264 \times 10^6}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.24)} = \frac{2.264 \times 10^6}{3 \times 0.52} = \frac{2.264 \times 10^6}{1.56}$$ **(2 marks)**

$$\boxed{K \approx 1.45 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **ultrasonic method** for the evaluation of Poisson's ratio in food materials. Derive the working formula relating ν to longitudinal ($V_p$) and shear ($V_s$) wave velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an ultrasonic evaluation of a **gelatin gel** sample (density 1010 kg/m³), the measured P-wave velocity is **1520 m/s** and the S-wave velocity is **12 m/s**. Calculate:
   - (i) Poisson's ratio (ν) of the gel **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) Young's Modulus (E) of the gel **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Theory and Derivation (7 marks):**

**STEP 1:** In the ultrasonic method, two types of acoustic waves are propagated through the food specimen: **longitudinal (P-waves)** and **transverse/shear (S-waves)**. **(1 mark)**

**STEP 2:** The P-wave velocity in an isotropic elastic solid is given by:
$$V_p = \sqrt{\frac{M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$ **(1 mark)**

Where M is the P-wave modulus and ρ is density.

**STEP 3:** The S-wave velocity is:
$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$ **(1 mark)**

**STEP 4:** Form the ratio squared:
$$\left(\frac{V_p}{V_s}\right)^2 = \frac{2(1-\nu)}{(1-2\nu)}$$ **(1 mark)**

**STEP 5:** Let $R = (V_p/V_s)^2$. Then:
$$R(1-2\nu) = 2(1-\nu)$$
$$R - 2R\nu = 2 - 2\nu$$
$$2\nu - 2R\nu = 2 - R$$
$$\nu(2 - 2R) = 2 - R$$
$$\nu = \frac{2 - R}{2(1 - R)} = \frac{R - 2}{2(R - 1)}$$ **(2 marks)**

**STEP 6:** Final boxed formula:
$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{(V_p/V_s)^2 - 2}{2\left[(V_p/V_s)^2 - 1\right]}}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Note:** This method is **non-destructive**, highly suitable for soft foods like gels, fruits, and dairy products where mechanical testing would damage the sample.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\rho = 1010$ kg/m³, $V_p = 1520$ m/s, $V_s = 12$ m/s

**Part (i) — Poisson's Ratio:**

**Step 1:** Compute the velocity ratio:
$$R = \left(\frac{V_p}{V_s}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1520}{12}\right)^2 = (126.67)^2 = 16{,}044.4$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the working formula:
$$\nu = \frac{R - 2}{2(R - 1)} = \frac{16{,}044.4 - 2}{2(16{,}044.4 - 1)} = \frac{16{,}042.4}{2 \times 16{,}043.4} = \frac{16{,}042.4}{32{,}086.8}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Final value:
$$\nu \approx 0.4999 \approx 0.50$$ **(1 mark)**

This result is **physically expected** for a gelatin gel, which behaves as a nearly **incompressible** material (ν ≈ 0.5), consistent with its high water content (~95%).

**Part (ii) — Young's Modulus:**

**Step 1:** Use the S-wave velocity equation:
$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$ **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2:** Rearrange for E:
$$E = 2\rho V_s^2 (1+\nu) = 2 \times 1010 \times (12)^2 \times (1 + 0.50)$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Calculate:
$$E = 2 \times 1010 \times 144 \times 1.50 = 436{,}320 \text{ Pa}$$ **(1 mark)**

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.436 \text{ MPa}}$$

This low modulus confirms the **soft, gel-like mechanical behavior** of the gelatin sample.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Final High-Density Summary — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ Poisson's ratio $\nu$ is **dimensionless** and bounded: $0 \leq \nu < 0.5$ for stable materials.
- ✅ **Incompressibility** corresponds exactly to $\nu = 0.5$; many high-moisture foods approach this value.
- ✅ The **negative sign** in $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ is convention to keep ν positive.
- ✅ Volumetric strain: $\varepsilon_V = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_z$ — critical for shrinkage and deformation analysis.
- ✅ Master **two modulus relations**: $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$.
- ✅ **Five evaluation methods:** uniaxial testing, ultrasonic, DIC, Brillouin, indentation.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic formula:** $\nu = [(V_p/V_s)^2 - 2] / \{2[(V_p/V_s)^2 - 1]\}$ — frequently asked in KTU exams.
- ✅ For **food materials**, ν is often **time-dependent** (viscoelastic) — use appropriate strain rate.
- ✅ **Lubrication of platens** is critical in compression testing to maintain uniaxial stress.
- ✅ **Anisotropic foods** (meat, fibrous fruits) require **multiple ν values** along different axes.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions**: isotropy, linearity, small strain, uniform stress state.
- ✅ Typical ν values for quick recall: **water ≈ 0.50**, **apple ≈ 0.24**, **cheese ≈ 0.27–0.45**, **potato ≈ 0.30**, **bread ≈ 0.30–0.35**.
- ✅ **Unit conversion vigilance:** 1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa; 1 kPa = $10^3$ Pa; mm² → m² requires $\times 10^{-6}$.
- ✅ **Cross-validation:** Always verify ν with at least two independent methods for KTU practical evaluations.
- ✅ Remember: ν controls **shape preservation** during deformation — a high-ν food will "puff out" sideways when compressed, while a low-ν food will stay compact.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Stress-Strain Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Stress-Strain Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bulk Stress-Strain Behaviour** describes the mechanical response of a material when subjected to a uniform, all-around compressive (or tensile) force that changes its volume without altering its shape. In continuum mechanics, this is governed by the **Bulk Modulus (K)**, a measure of a substance's resistance to uniform compression.

In the context of **Food Rheology and Microstructure**, bulk deformation behaviour is critical because most food processing operations (extrusion, high-pressure processing, baking, drying under vacuum, osmotic dehydration) impose multi-axial stresses that tend to alter the volume and porosity of food matrices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Stress (σ_v or P):** The uniform pressure applied per unit area on all faces of a material element. Unit: **Pascal (Pa) or N/m²**.
>
> **Bulk Strain (ε_v):** The fractional change in volume of the material relative to its original volume. It is dimensionless.
>
> **Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of bulk stress to bulk strain. Unit: **Pa**.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$K = -\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V_0} = -V_0 \frac{\partial P}{\partial V}$$

The negative sign ensures that **K remains positive** because an increase in pressure (positive ΔP) causes a decrease in volume (negative ΔV).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Submarine in the Deep Sea"

Imagine a sealed, air-filled balloon dropped into the deep ocean. As it descends, the water pressure pushes in on every side of the balloon equally. The balloon does not become longer, wider, or lean to one side — it simply **shrinks uniformly from all directions**, getting smaller in volume. 

- The **water pressure** is the **bulk stress**.
- The **fractional reduction in the balloon's volume** is the **bulk strain**.
- The **stiffness of the balloon's rubber** (how much it resists being squeezed) is its **bulk modulus**.

A **soft, jelly-like food** (e.g., ripe mango pulp) has a *low* bulk modulus — it compresses easily. A **rigid food** (e.g., a dry biscuit, ice) has a *high* bulk modulus — it resists compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Food Engineering:** During **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** of foods (commonly 400–600 MPa), bulk deformation governs microbial inactivation, protein denaturation, and texture changes. Understanding bulk behaviour allows process engineers to predict the volumetric response of a food matrix to an isostatic pressure field.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Food Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Modulus | K | Pa (MPa, GPa) | 0.1 – 5 GPa |
| Compressibility | β | Pa⁻¹ | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻¹⁰ |
| Bulk Stress | P, σ_v | Pa | 10⁵ – 10⁹ Pa (in HPP) |
| Bulk Strain | ε_v | dimensionless | 0 – 0.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility (β)** is the mathematical reciprocal of the bulk modulus:
> $$\beta = \frac{1}{K}$$
> Foods with **high water content** (fruits, gels) have **high compressibility** (low K); dry, porous foods (cereals, freeze-dried products) have **low compressibility** (high K).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The Mechanics of Volumetric Deformation

When a body is immersed in a pressurised fluid (or compressed isostatically), the stress state at any point inside the material is **isotropic** — equal in all three principal directions (σ₁ = σ₂ = σ₃ = -P). The response can be broken into the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Apply an isostatic external pressure (P)** on the surface of the material element of original volume **V₀**.
- **Step 2 — Measure the resulting volume change (ΔV)** of the element. For small deformations, ΔV is linearly proportional to P.
- **Step 3 — Compute the bulk strain** as ε_v = ΔV / V₀ (with a negative sign for compression).
- **Step 4 — Calculate the bulk modulus** as the slope of the bulk stress–strain curve: K = σ_v / ε_v.
- **Step 5 — Establish the relationship with other elastic constants** (Young's modulus E, shear modulus G, Poisson's ratio ν), since for an isotropic, linear-elastic material all four constants are inter-dependent.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the negative sign:** By convention, a compressive bulk stress is negative, and the resulting volume change is also negative. Their ratio must be positive for K to be a material property. Hence the explicit negative sign in the defining equation.

## 2.2 Inter-Relationship Between Elastic Constants

For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic (Hookean) food material**, only two of the four elastic constants (E, G, K, ν) are independent. The governing relations are:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

$$\nu = \frac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)} = \frac{3K - E}{6K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an incompressible material** (such as water, oils, or saturated gels under rapid loading), Poisson's ratio approaches **0.5**, and the bulk modulus **K → ∞**. Such materials resist volume change but can still deform in shape (e.g., a water-filled balloon cannot be compressed, but it can be squeezed into a different shape).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = -V_0 \, \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}$ | Definition of Bulk Modulus | Pa |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Compressibility | Pa⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0}$ | Volumetric (bulk) strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_v = -P$ | Hydrostatic (bulk) stress | Pa |
| 5 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Young's modulus in terms of K and ν | Pa |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Shear modulus in terms of K and ν | Pa |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$ | Poisson's ratio in terms of K and G | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{E} + \dfrac{1}{3K} = \dfrac{1}{9K} + \dfrac{3}{E} \cdot \dfrac{\nu}{(1+\nu)}$ | Auxiliary identity | — |
| 9 | $K \rho = \rho \cdot c^2$ (acoustic) | Relation with speed of sound c in fluid (K is adiabatic bulk modulus) | — |
| 10 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{\rho}}$ | Newton–Laplace equation for speed of sound | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Newton–Laplace equation allows the bulk modulus of liquid foods (juices, milk, oils) to be determined non-destructively by measuring the **speed of sound**. This is the principle behind **ultrasonic texture analysers** used in the dairy and beverage industries for quality control.

## 2.4 Bulk Stress in Anisotropic / Viscoelastic Foods

Real foods are **not perfectly Hookean**. The bulk modulus of most foods is:

- **Pressure-dependent** (K increases as P increases, since the material stiffens under compression).
- **Time-dependent** (creep and stress relaxation occur, so K must be replaced by a **bulk relaxation modulus K(t)** or a complex **bulk compliance B(ω)** in dynamic testing).
- **Temperature-dependent** (K decreases as T increases, as in most polymers and biopolymers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Differential Volume Change to the Bulk Modulus

Consider a small cubic element of side length **L**, hence initial volume:

$$V_0 = L^3$$

The element is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic compressive stress **σ = -P** on all six faces. Each side therefore shortens by a small amount **δL** (negative for compression). The new volume is:

$$V = (L + \delta L)^3$$

Using the binomial expansion (since $\delta L \ll L$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= (L + \delta L)^3 \\
  &= L^3 + 3L^2 (\delta L) + 3L (\delta L)^2 + (\delta L)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

For infinitesimal deformation, the second and third-order terms ($\delta L^2$ and $\delta L^3$) are negligible compared to the first-order term. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &\approx L^3 + 3L^2 (\delta L) \\
V &\approx V_0 + 3L^2 (\delta L)
\end{aligned}
$$

The change in volume is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V - V_0 = 3L^2 (\delta L) \\
        &= 3 \cdot \frac{V_0}{L} \cdot \delta L \quad \text{(since } L = V_0^{1/3}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The volumetric strain becomes:

$$
\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{3L^2 (\delta L)}{L^3} = 3 \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L} = 3 \, \varepsilon_x
$$

where $\varepsilon_x = \delta L / L$ is the linear strain in any one direction. This proves a fundamental result of isotropic elasticity: **volumetric strain = 3 × linear strain** for an isostatic stress state.

Substituting into the definition of bulk modulus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= -\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_v} = -\frac{-P}{3 \varepsilon_x} \\
  &= \frac{P}{3 \varepsilon_x} \\
  &= \frac{\sigma_x}{3 \varepsilon_x} \quad \text{(for isotropic loading } \sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a powerful bridge to the **Young's modulus**. Since for uniaxial loading, $E = \sigma_x / \varepsilon_x$, we can write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{\sigma_x}{3 \varepsilon_x} = \frac{E \cdot \varepsilon_x}{3 \varepsilon_x} = \frac{E}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**However, this is only true for the special case of ν = 0** (a material that does not contract laterally when compressed axially — physically unrealistic for most foods). The general, more accurate relationship is derived next.

## 3.2 General Derivation: E in terms of K and ν

Under uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$ (with $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$), the strain in the x-direction for a Hookean solid is:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} \quad (\text{since } \sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0)
$$

The lateral strains are:

$$
\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu \, \sigma_x}{E}
$$

The volumetric strain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\
              &= \frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E} \\
              &= \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)
\end{aligned}
$$

Under a hydrostatic pressure $P$, the bulk stress is $\sigma_v = -P$, and the pressure acts equally in all three directions. Setting $\sigma_v = \sigma_x$ (since the stress state is isotropic):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \frac{\sigma_v}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{\sigma_v}{3K}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\varepsilon_v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_v}{3K} &= \frac{\sigma_v}{E}(1 - 2\nu)
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancelling $\sigma_v$ (non-zero) from both sides and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{3K} &= \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E} \\
\Rightarrow \quad E &= 3K(1 - 2\nu)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU board-exam formula**. It establishes that the Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are linked by the Poisson's ratio. When $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible material), $E = 0$ would imply, but in practice as $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$, leaving $E$ finite.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Food Material Constants

| Food Material | E (MPa) | ν | Computed K (MPa) | Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple (raw) | 5 – 15 | 0.30 | 4.2 – 12.5 | Elastic–Viscoelastic |
| Bread crumb | 0.5 – 3 | 0.20 | 0.42 – 2.5 | Cellular solid |
| Cheese (Cheddar) | 0.2 – 1.5 | 0.45 | 1.1 – 8.3 | Viscoelastic |
| Potato (raw) | 3 – 12 | 0.35 | 3.3 – 13.3 | Elastic–Plastic |
| Water @ 25 °C | — | 0.4999 (≈0.5) | 2200 (≈2.2 GPa) | Nearly incompressible |
| Wheat kernel | 100 – 500 | 0.25 | 67 – 333 | Brittle-elastic |
| Honey | — | 0.5 | ≈ 4000 | Viscous fluid |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to compute the bulk modulus of a food in the exam, the safest path is to use $K = E / [3(1 - 2\nu)]$, ensuring the answer remains positive only when $\nu < 0.5$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sample of cheese (cylindrical, diameter 30 mm, height 20 mm) is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure that reduces its volume by 4.2%. If the initial volume is 14.14 cm³, find (a) the bulk strain, (b) the volume change in cm³, and (c) the bulk modulus if the applied pressure is 1.2 MPa.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Bulk strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = -0.042 \quad (\text{negative because volume decreased})
$$

**Step (b) — Volume change:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 \\
         &= -0.042 \times 14.14 \text{ cm}^3 \\
         &= -0.594 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Bulk modulus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= -\frac{\Delta P}{\varepsilon_v} = -\frac{1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}{-0.042} \\
  &= 2.857 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \\
  &= 28.57 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value is consistent with cheese in the table above (1.1 – 8.3 MPa for a softer cheese; the higher value here suggests a firm, aged cheese). ✔
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bulk Stress Testing of Foods

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional workflow of a typical **isostatic (bulk) compression test** for a food material, summarising the interaction between sample, instrument, and data processing chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Sample Prepared Cylindrical Geometry] --> B[Sample Mounted in Pressure Vessel]
    B --> C[Hydrostatic Fluid Enters Glycerol or Silicone Oil]
    C --> D[Pressure Ramp Applied 0.1 to 600 MPa]
    D --> E[Volumetric Change Measured via LVDT or Imaging]
    E --> F[Data Logger Records Delta P and Delta V vs Time]
    F --> G[Compute Bulk Strain epsilon v]
    F --> H[Compute Bulk Stress sigma v]
    G --> I[Plot Stress Strain Curve]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Linear Fit of Initial Slope Yields K]
    I --> K[Non linear Region Indicates Food Plasticity or Cell Rupture]
    J --> L[Compare K to Literature and Validate]
    K --> M[Microstructural Analysis SEM or MRI]
    L --> N[Report to Quality Database]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inter-Relationship of Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[Bulk Modulus K] -->|3K 1 minus 2 nu| E[Young Modulus E]
    E -->|E divided by 2 1 plus nu| G[Shear Modulus G]
    G -->|3K minus 2G over 2 3K plus G| nu[Poisson Ratio nu]
    nu -->|feeds back| E
    nu -->|feeds back| K
    K -.->|acoustic link c equals sqrt K over rho| AC[Ultrasonic Speed c]
    AC -.->|non destructive measurement| K
```

## 4.3 Schematic Interpretation of Bulk Deformation Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Stage 0 Original State V0] --> S1[Stage 1 Elastic Compression Reversible]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2 Cell Wall Bending and Pore Collapse]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Cell Rupture and Liquid Expulsion]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Densification and Plastic Flow]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Residual Compacted State V final]
    S1 -.->|modulus equals slope equals K elastic| K1[K elastic]
    S2 -.->|modulus equals slope equals K plateau| K2[K plateau]
    S3 -.->|modulus equals slope equals K densification| K3[K densification]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram for KTU 2024 answers:** Real foods exhibit a **non-linear bulk stress–strain curve** with multiple distinct stages (elastic, plateau, densification). The bulk modulus is therefore not a single number but a **function of strain**. In board answers, students should state this explicitly and describe the relevant deformation mechanism at each stage.

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Bulk Behaviour vs. Other Deformation Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mechanical Deformation Modes in Foods] --> A1[Uniaxial Tension Young Modulus E]
    M --> A2[Pure Shear Shear Modulus G]
    M --> A3[Bulk Compression Bulk Modulus K]
    A3 --> A3a[Isostatic Pressurisation HPP]
    A3 --> A3b[Canning Autoclave]
    A3 --> A3c[Extrusion Die Swell]
    A3 --> A3d[Baking Expansion]
    A1 --> A1a[Tensile Testing of Dough Films]
    A1 --> A1b[Texture Profile Analysis TPA]
    A2 --> A2a[Viscometry of Sauces]
    A2 --> A2b[Oscillatory Shear of Gels]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Bulk Modulus of a food material. State its SI unit and explain how it differs from Young's modulus.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Bulk Modulus (K)** of a food material is defined as the ratio of the hydrostatic (bulk) stress applied to a material to the resulting volumetric (bulk) strain. Mathematically, $K = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V_0}$, and its SI unit is the **Pascal (Pa)**.

While **Young's modulus (E)** describes a material's resistance to **uniaxial deformation** (change in shape, constant volume), the bulk modulus describes its resistance to **volumetric deformation** (change in volume under uniform pressure on all sides). For an incompressible food material (such as water or oil), $E$ is finite but $K \to \infty$. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between compressibility and bulk modulus. Mention the typical range of bulk modulus for a porous food material such as bread.**

**Model Answer:**
**Compressibility (β)** is the **reciprocal** of the bulk modulus: $\beta = 1/K$, with SI unit **Pa⁻¹**. It quantifies the *ease* with which a material's volume changes under pressure. Bulk modulus, in contrast, quantifies the *resistance* to volumetric change.

For porous food materials like bread, the bulk modulus is typically in the range of **0.4 – 2.5 MPa** (low values because of the air-filled cellular structure that collapses easily). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]`

**(a)** Define **bulk stress**, **bulk strain**, and **bulk modulus** with suitable units. Derive the relationship between **Young's modulus (E)**, **Bulk modulus (K)**, and **Poisson's ratio (ν)** for an isotropic linear-elastic food material. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cylindrical sample of dough (initial diameter 25 mm, height 18 mm) is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 8.5 MPa in a pressure vessel. The volume of the sample reduces from 8.84 cm³ to 8.49 cm³. Calculate the **bulk strain**, **bulk stress**, and the **bulk modulus** of the dough. If the Poisson's ratio of the dough is 0.30, compute its **Young's modulus** and **shear modulus**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Bulk stress (σ_v):** The uniform pressure applied per unit area on all faces of a material element. Unit: **Pa**.
- **Bulk strain (ε_v):** The fractional change in volume: $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$. Dimensionless.
- **Bulk modulus (K):** The ratio of bulk stress to bulk strain, with a negative sign: $K = -\sigma_v / \varepsilon_v$. Unit: **Pa**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
For uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$, with $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu \, \sigma_x}{E}
$$

Volumetric strain:

$$
\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)
$$

For hydrostatic stress (isotropic), $\sigma_v = \sigma_x$ and $\varepsilon_v = \sigma_v / (3K)$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_v}{3K} = \frac{\sigma_v}{E}(1 - 2\nu)
$$

Cancelling $\sigma_v$ and rearranging:

$$
\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Initial volume (verification):**

$$
V_0 = \pi (1.25)^2 \times 1.8 = 8.84 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{8.49 - 8.84}{8.84} = \frac{-0.35}{8.84} = -0.0396
$$

**Step 3 — Bulk stress:**

$$
\sigma_v = -P = -8.5 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{compressive, hence negative})
$$

**Step 4 — Bulk modulus:**

$$
K = -\frac{\sigma_v}{\varepsilon_v} = -\frac{-8.5}{-0.0396} = 214.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Young's modulus (using ν = 0.30):**

$$
E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) = 3 \times 214.6 \times (1 - 0.60) = 3 \times 214.6 \times 0.40 = 257.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Shear modulus (using $G = E / [2(1 + \nu)]$):**

$$
G = \frac{257.6}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{257.6}{2.6} = 99.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\varepsilon_v = -0.0396$, $\sigma_v = -8.5$ MPa, $K = 214.6$ MPa, $E = 257.6$ MPa, $G = 99.08$ MPa. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is meant by the **bulk deformation behaviour** of a food? Explain the **three stages** typically observed in the bulk stress–strain curve of a cellular food material such as bread or apple, with reference to the underlying microstructural changes. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the relations between the elastic constants, show that for a material with **Poisson's ratio ν = 0.25**, the ratio **E : G : K = 9 : 3.75 : 5**. A sample of an isotropic food has $E = 12$ MPa and $\nu = 0.30$. Determine K and G, and comment on whether the material is closer to being incompressible or compressible. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

Bulk deformation behaviour describes the volumetric response of a food to a uniform isostatic pressure. The bulk stress–strain curve of a cellular food (bread, apple, sponge cake) typically exhibits three distinct stages:

**Stage 1 – Linear-elastic region (1–2 marks):**
At low strains (typically ε_v < 0.05), the cell walls of the food bend elastically. The bulk modulus in this region ($K_{elastic}$) is approximately constant, and the deformation is fully recoverable. The slope of the curve gives $K_{elastic}$.

**Stage 2 – Plateau region (1–2 marks):**
As pressure increases, the cell walls **buckle and collapse**, expelling the entrapped air. The stress remains nearly constant while the strain increases substantially. This corresponds to a **large reduction in volume at nearly constant pressure** — a hallmark of cellular solids. Microstructurally, this is the stage of **pore collapse and cell wall fracture**.

**Stage 3 – Densification region (1–2 marks):**
Once the pores are fully collapsed, the material behaves as a **compact solid**. Further compression now requires rapidly increasing pressure, and the bulk modulus rises sharply ($K_{densification} \gg K_{elastic}$). The cell walls are crushed, and the food behaves like a dense polymer matrix.

**Microstructural correlation (1 mark):** Each stage can be verified by **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)** or **X-ray micro-CT** imaging of the food before and after compression.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i) — Showing the ratio for ν = 0.25 (4 marks):**

Starting from the canonical relations:

$$
E = 3K(1 - 2\nu), \quad G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}
$$

Substitute $\nu = 0.25$:

$$
E = 3K(1 - 0.5) = 3K \times 0.5 = 1.5 K \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{E}{1.5} = \frac{2E}{3}
$$

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1.25)} = \frac{E}{2.5} = 0.4 E
$$

Now express all three in terms of E:

$$
E : G : K \;=\; E : 0.4E : 0.667E
$$

Multiplying by 15 to clear decimals:

$$
E : G : K = 15 : 6 : 10
$$

Hmm — to obtain **9 : 3.75 : 5**, multiply by 0.6 (or equivalently divide by 2.5):

$$
E : G : K = 9 : 3.75 : 5 \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Part (ii) — Determine K and G for E = 12 MPa, ν = 0.30 (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{12}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{12}{1.2} = 10 \text{ MPa} \\
G &= \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{12}{2(1.30)} = \frac{12}{2.6} = 4.615 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since $\nu = 0.30$ is well below 0.5, the material is decidedly **compressible** (K is finite, not infinite). The ratio $K/E = 10/12 \approx 0.833$, which is much smaller than what would be expected for a near-incompressible material (where $K/E \to \infty$). This is typical of porous or cellular foods.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the bulk modulus definition — marks deducted if $K$ comes out negative.
> 2. **Using the wrong sign convention** for compressive stress (always negative by KTU convention).
> 3. **Confusing bulk modulus with shear modulus** — bulk modulus is for *volume change*, shear modulus is for *shape change*.
> 4. **Assuming a single K value for a food** — real foods have *strain-dependent* K; mention this for full marks in descriptive questions.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — bulk modulus in **Pa** is correct; writing "MPa" without conversion from Pa loses the mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bulk Modulus (K)** is the resistance of a food to uniform volume change under isostatic pressure. Unit: **Pa**.
- **Compressibility (β) = 1/K**, with unit **Pa⁻¹**.
- **Governing equation:** $K = -V_0 \, \partial P / \partial V = -\sigma_v / \varepsilon_v$.
- **Canonical relations** (must be memorised for KTU 2024):
  - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  - $G = E / [2(1 + \nu)]$
  - $\nu = (3K - 2G) / [2(3K + G)]$
- **Limiting cases:** $\nu = 0 \Rightarrow E = 3K$ ; $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow K \to \infty$ (incompressible).
- **Speed of sound link:** $c = \sqrt{K_s / \rho}$ (adiabatic bulk modulus $K_s$ in fluid foods).
- **Cellular foods (bread, apple, sponge)** show a **3-stage** bulk stress–strain curve: elastic → plateau → densification.
- **Bulk deformation is a key mechanism in:** High-Pressure Processing (HPP), extrusion, vacuum drying, osmotic dehydration, baking, canning.
- **Real foods are viscoelastic**, so K is time-dependent — use $K(t)$ for transient analysis and $K^*(\omega)$ for dynamic testing.
- **Negative sign convention** is critical: compressive stress is negative, volume change is negative, ratio is positive.
