- **Porous / dry foods** have **low K** (high β); **water-rich / saturated foods** have **high K** (low β).
- **Ultrasonic measurement** of $c$ in liquid foods allows **non-destructive** determination of $K$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical State of a Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical State of a Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical State of a Material (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **physical state of a material** refers to the distinct phase or condition of matter — traditionally classified as **solid, liquid, or gas** — determined by the balance of **intermolecular forces**, **thermal energy (kT)**, and the material's **mechanical response** to applied stress. In the context of food rheology, the term is extended beyond classical states to include **rheological states** such as *viscoelastic, plastic, thixotropic*, and *pseudoplastic* behaviors, which describe how food materials deform and flow under external forces.

In classical thermodynamics, the physical state is governed by the **Gibbs free energy minimization principle**, where a material adopts the state that minimizes its free energy at a given temperature (**T** in **K**), pressure (**P** in **Pa**), and composition. The boundaries between states are defined by **phase transition curves** (e.g., melting curve, vaporization curve, sublimation curve).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Stretchy Honey" Picture 🍯**
> Imagine a drop of honey on a spoon. When you tilt the spoon, the honey slowly slides off — it is in a **liquid state**. Now imagine the same honey mixed with gelatin and cooled. It will jiggle when poked but won't flow freely — it is now in a **gel state** (a viscoelastic solid). If you freeze it, it becomes a true **solid**. If you heat it past **150 °C**, it caramelizes into a brittle solid. The *same chemical composition* can therefore occupy many *physical states* depending on temperature, structure, and applied force.
> 
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Students:** A food material is not defined by its chemical formula alone — its **physical state** is the *response function* it exhibits when energy or force is applied. This is the central dogma of rheology.

## 1.3 The Three Classical Physical States — A Quick Snapshot

| State | Molecular Arrangement | Shape | Compressibility | Typical Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Tightly packed, ordered (crystalline) or disordered (amorphous) | Definite | Very low (κ ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ Pa⁻¹) | Ice cube, chocolate bar |
| **Liquid** | Loosely packed, mobile | Indefinite (takes container shape) | Low (κ ≈ 10⁻⁹ Pa⁻¹) | Water, milk, oil |
| **Gas** | Widely separated, random motion | Indefinite (fills container) | High (κ ≈ 10⁻⁵ Pa⁻¹) | Steam, vapor in baking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Foods are *rarely* in pure classical states. They exist as **multi-phase, multi-component, structurally complex systems** (e.g., emulsions like mayonnaise, foams like whipped cream, gels like jelly, suspensions like orange juice with pulp). The course emphasizes these *intermediate* physical states.

## 1.4 Beyond the Classical Trio — Intermediate States in Food Systems

Food materials most often occupy *intermediate* positions on the solid–liquid spectrum:

- **Viscoelastic** — Exhibits both viscous and elastic character (e.g., cheese, dough, yogurt)
- **Plastic (Bingham)** — Behaves as a solid below a critical yield stress, then flows (e.g., tomato paste, margarine)
- **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** — Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate (e.g., ketchup, melted chocolate)
- **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** — Viscosity increases with shear rate (e.g., corn starch suspension in water)
- **Thixotropic** — Viscosity decreases *with time* under constant shear (e.g., yogurt, gels)
- **Rheopectic** — Viscosity increases *with time* under constant shear (rare, e.g., some gypsum pastes)

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)** = **273.15 K**, **101 325 Pa**
- **Boltzmann Constant (k_B)** = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Density of water at 4 °C (ρ_water)** = **1000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical state** of a material at the molecular level is fundamentally a competition between **thermal kinetic energy (k_B·T)** that drives disorder and **intermolecular potential energy (U)** that drives order. The state is therefore a function of the dimensionless ratio **k_B·T / U**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Basis of Physical States

The physical state of a material emerges from a balance of two fundamental energies:

1. **Intermolecular Cohesive Energy (U):** Attractive forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, ionic, covalent) that hold molecules together.
2. **Thermal Kinetic Energy (k_B·T):** Random molecular motion that disrupts molecular order.

**Decision Rule:**
- If **U ≫ k_B·T** → Molecules locked in fixed positions → **Solid**
- If **U ≈ k_B·T** → Molecules have mobility but remain condensed → **Liquid**
- If **U ≪ k_B·T** → Molecules escape into free space → **Gas**

## 2.2 Mechanical Response Functions — The "Rheological Fingerprint"

Every physical state can be characterized by how it responds to an applied **shear stress (τ)** in **Pa** or **shear strain (γ)** in **dimensionless** units.

### 2.2.1 Ideal Hookean Solid (Perfectly Elastic)

- A perfectly elastic solid obeys **Hooke's Law** (1676).
- The material deforms instantaneously, stores all energy, and recovers fully upon removal of stress.
- **Characteristic constant:** Shear modulus **G** (Pa)

### 2.2.2 Ideal Newtonian Liquid (Perfectly Viscous)

- A perfectly viscous liquid obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity** (1687).
- The material flows continuously, dissipates all energy as heat, and does not recover.
- **Characteristic constant:** Dynamic viscosity **η** (Pa·s)

### 2.2.3 Real Food Materials — Viscoelastic Continuum

Most foods lie *between* the Hookean solid and the Newtonian liquid. Their response is captured by a **relaxation modulus G(t)** or **creep compliance J(t)**, both of which are time-dependent functions.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive KTU 2024 cheat sheet** for Module 1 derivations, numericals, and 14-mark problems. Memorize every entry.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law (Elastic) | $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$ | τ = shear stress (Pa); G = shear modulus (Pa); γ = shear strain (–) | Ideal elastic solid |
| 2 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ | τ = shear stress (Pa); η = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s); $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) | Ideal viscous liquid |
| 3 | Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) | $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ | K = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ); n = flow behavior index (–) | Non-Newtonian fluids |
| 4 | Bingham Plastic | $$\tau = \tau_{y} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ | τ_y = yield stress (Pa); η_p = plastic viscosity (Pa·s) | Plastic fluids (e.g., paste) |
| 5 | Casson Model | $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$ | τ_y = yield stress; η_c = Casson viscosity | Chocolate, food suspensions |
| 6 | Maxwell Model | $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \frac{\tau}{\eta}$$ | λ = η/G = relaxation time (s) | Viscoelastic liquids |
| 7 | Kelvin–Voigt Model | $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma + \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ | G, η as above | Viscoelastic solids |
| 8 | Deborah Number | $$D_{e} = \frac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$$ | λ = relaxation time (s); t_obs = observation time (s) | Solid–liquid distinction |
| 9 | Reynolds Number | $$R_{e} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L}{\eta}$$ | ρ = density (kg/m³); v = velocity (m/s); L = characteristic length (m) | Flow regime |
| 10 | Strain Definition | $$\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy} = \tan\theta$$ | dx = lateral displacement (m); dy = vertical thickness (m) | Shear deformation |
| 11 | Shear Rate Definition | $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$ | dv = velocity gradient (m/s) | Shear flow |
| 12 | Apparent Viscosity | $$\eta_{a} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ | Function of shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids | General use |
| 13 | Complex Modulus | $$G^{*} = G' + i \cdot G''$$ | G' = storage modulus (Pa); G'' = loss modulus (Pa) | Oscillatory rheology |
| 14 | Loss Tangent | $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$ | δ = phase angle (rad) | Viscoelastic balance |
| 15 | Thermal Energy Ratio | $$\frac{k_{B} T}{U}$$ | k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K; T in K; U in J | State determination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The symbols **G, G', G''** are *different*! 
> - **G** = *instantaneous* shear modulus (Hookean solid)
> - **G'** = *storage* modulus (elastic part, in-phase with strain)
> - **G''** = *loss* modulus (viscous part, out-of-phase with strain)
> 
> Examiners *will* deduct marks if these are conflated.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The physical state of a food material dictates:

1. **Processing Operations:** Pumping (liquid), extrusion (plastic/viscoelastic), baking (gel-sol transition), emulsification (liquid–liquid interface).
2. **Quality Control:** Texture (solid), pourability (liquid), spreadability (plastic), shelf-stability (rheopexy).
3. **Sensory Perception:** Mouthfeel correlates with **G'** (firmness), **η_a** (thickness), and **τ_y** (spreadability).
4. **Product Design:** A sauce designer tunes **n** (flow index) and **K** (consistency) to achieve a target pour-and-cling behavior.
5. **3D Food Printing:** Requires precise control of yield stress and viscoelastic recovery post-extrusion.

## 2.5 Phase Diagram Foundation (Brief)

The classical **P-T phase diagram** plots pressure (**P**) on the y-axis vs. temperature (**T**) on the x-axis, with three curves meeting at the **triple point** and ending at the **critical point**. For water:
- **Triple point:** T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa
- **Critical point:** T = 647.1 K, P = 22.064 MPa
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shear Strain (γ) from Geometric Displacement

**Starting Postulate:** Consider a rectangular block of material with height **dy** and base width **dy**, subjected to a tangential force **F** on the top face. The top surface displaces laterally by a small distance **dx**.

**Step 1 — Define Shear Strain as Angular Distortion:**

In the limit of small deformations, the shear strain **γ** is defined as the tangent of the deformation angle **θ** between the deformed face and the original face.

$$\gamma = \tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Differential Form:**

For an infinitesimally thin layer of thickness **dy**, the incremental strain is:

$$d\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — For a Continuous Velocity Field (Liquid Case):**

If the top face moves with velocity **v** and the bottom is stationary, the displacement is **dx = v · dt** over time **dt**. The shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ is therefore:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{1}{dy} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{v}{dy} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

The **shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$** in units of **s⁻¹** is the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction. This single equation is the foundation of all viscometric measurements.

> **Conversion Logic:** The dimensional check: [m/s] / [m] = [s⁻¹] ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hooke's Law for a Shear Element

**Starting Postulate:** A material element is subjected to a shear stress **τ** (force per unit area, **N/m² = Pa**). The resulting strain **γ** is *proportional* to **τ** for small deformations.

**Step 1 — State the Proportionality:**

$$\tau \propto \gamma$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Proportionality Constant G (Shear Modulus):**

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Shear Modulus:**

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

- **Units of G:** [Pa] / [–] = [Pa] = [N/m²] = [kg/(m·s²)]
- **High G** (e.g., steel ≈ 79 GPa, ice ≈ 3.5 GPa) → stiff solid
- **Low G** (e.g., gelatin gel ≈ 1000 Pa) → soft gel

**Step 5 — Energy Storage:**

The elastic energy stored per unit volume **u_e** in a Hookean solid under shear is:

$$u_{e} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot G \cdot \gamma^{2} = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2 G}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This result comes from integrating the stress over the strain from 0 to γ: $u_e = \int_0^\gamma \tau \, d\gamma' = \int_0^\gamma G\gamma' \, d\gamma' = \frac{1}{2}G\gamma^2$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Newton's Law of Viscosity from Momentum Flux

**Starting Postulate:** Consider a fluid between two parallel plates separated by distance **h**. The bottom plate is stationary; the top plate moves with velocity **V** under applied force **F** per unit area **A** → **τ = F/A**.

**Step 1 — Newton's Hypothesis (1687):**

Newton postulated that the shear stress is proportional to the *velocity gradient*, not the velocity itself:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{dv}{dy} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Dynamic Viscosity:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Units of Viscosity:**

$$[\eta] = \frac{[\text{Pa}]}{[\text{s}^{-1}]} = [\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}] = \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$$

**Step 5 — Energy Dissipation:**

The viscous energy dissipated per unit volume per unit time is:

$$\dot{w}_{v} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{2}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The dimensional check: [Pa·s] · [s⁻²] = [Pa/s] = [W/m³] ✓ — units of power density.

**Sample Reference Viscosities (for KTU numerical intuition):**

| Fluid | Viscosity η (Pa·s) at 20 °C |
|---|---|
| Air | 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ |
| Water | 1.0 × 10⁻³ |
| Olive oil | 0.084 |
| Honey | 2 – 10 |
| Peanut butter | 150 – 250 |
| Bitumen | 10⁸ |

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Deborah Number (Solid vs. Liquid Distinction)

**Conceptual Motivation:** A material is classified as solid or liquid based on the *observation time*. Glass is "solid" on human timescales but "flows" over geological timescales. This is quantified by the **Deborah Number (D_e)**, named after the prophetess Deborah ("the mountains flowed before the Lord" — Judges 5:5).

**Step 1 — Define the Characteristic (Relaxation) Time λ:**

The relaxation time is the time required for stress in the material to decay to **1/e ≈ 36.8%** of its initial value after a step strain.

$$\tau(t) = \tau_{0} \cdot e^{-t/\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Observation Time t_obs:**

The time over which the deformation is observed or applied.

**Step 3 — Construct the Deborah Number:**

$$D_{e} = \frac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation Table:**

| D_e Value | Physical State | Example |
|---|---|---|
| D_e → ∞ | Perfectly elastic solid | Steel, diamond |
| D_e ≫ 1 | Viscoelastic solid | Cheese, bread dough |
| D_e ≈ 1 | Viscoelastic | Yogurt, butter |
| D_e ≪ 1 | Viscoelastic liquid | Blood, polymer melt |
| D_e → 0 | Perfectly viscous liquid | Water, air |

> **Conversion Logic:** D_e is dimensionless because [s] / [s] = [–] ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power-Law Fluid Flow Curve

**Starting Postulate:** Many food fluids (ketchup, fruit purees) show a non-linear relationship between τ and $\dot{\gamma}$.

**Step 1 — Empirical Form (Ostwald–de Waele, 1925):**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Viscosity:**

$$\eta_{a} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Three Special Cases:**

- **n = 1, K = η:** Reduces to Newton's law → **Newtonian fluid**
- **n < 1:** $\eta_a$ decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**
- **n > 1:** $\eta_a$ increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**

**Step 4 — Example Numerical Check (KTU Style):**

A tomato ketchup has **K = 15 Pa·s⁰·⁴** and **n = 0.4** at 25 °C. Find the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹.

$$\eta_{a} = 15 \cdot (100)^{0.4 - 1} = 15 \cdot (100)^{-0.6}$$

$$(100)^{-0.6} = \frac{1}{100^{0.6}} = \frac{1}{15.85} = 0.0631$$

$$\eta_{a} = 15 \times 0.0631 \approx 0.946 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $\log(100^{0.6}) = 0.6 \times \log(100) = 0.6 \times 2 = 1.2$, so $100^{0.6} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$ ✓

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Oscillatory Moduli from Phase Lag

**Starting Postulate:** Apply a small-amplitude sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_{0} \sin(\omega t)$ and measure the resulting stress $\tau(t) = \tau_{0} \sin(\omega t + \delta)$.

**Step 1 — Expand the Stress:**

$$\tau(t) = \tau_{0}\cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \tau_{0}\sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the In-Phase and Out-of-Phase Components:**

$$\tau(t) = \underbrace{G' \cdot \gamma_{0} \cdot \sin(\omega t)}_{\text{elastic}} + \underbrace{G'' \cdot \gamma_{0} \cdot \cos(\omega t)}_{\text{viscous}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Moduli:**

$$G' = \frac{\tau_{0}}{\gamma_{0}} \cos\delta \quad ; \quad G'' = \frac{\tau_{0}}{\gamma_{0}} \sin\delta$$

**Step 4 — Complex Modulus:**

$$G^{*} = \frac{\tau^{*}}{\gamma^{*}} = G' + i G'' = \vert G^{*} \vert e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude and Loss Tangent:**

$$\vert G^{*} \vert = \sqrt{(G')^{2} + (G'')^{2}} \quad ; \quad \tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the core of *dynamic mechanical analysis* (DMA) used for food gel characterization.

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Physical States

| Property | Ideal Solid (Hooke) | Ideal Liquid (Newton) | Viscoelastic | Plastic (Bingham) | Power-Law |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Defining equation | τ = G·γ | τ = η·$\dot{\gamma}$ | $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\tau}/G + \tau/\eta$ | τ = τ_y + η_p·$\dot{\gamma}$ | τ = K·$\dot{\gamma}^n$ |
| Constant parameter(s) | G | η | G, η | τ_y, η_p | K, n |
| Strain on stress removal | Recovers fully | No recovery | Partial recovery | No recovery (if > τ_y) | No recovery |
| Energy fate | Stored | Dissipated as heat | Both | Dissipated | Dissipated |
| Food example | Hard candy | Water, oil | Cheese, dough | Margarine | Ketchup |
| Yield stress τ_y | None | None | None | **Yes** | None |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most exam-relevant table** for KTU 2024. Practice redrawing it from memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Flow: Classification of Physical States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Subjected to Stress] --> B{Does it flow under its own weight?}
    B -- No --> C[Elastic Solid]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is flow Newtonian?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Newtonian Liquid]
    D -- No --> F{Does viscosity depend on shear rate?}
    F -- No --> G{Is there a yield stress?}
    F -- Yes --> H{Power-Law Behavior}
    H -- n less than 1 --> I[Pseudoplastic - Shear Thinning]
    H -- n greater than 1 --> J[Dilatant - Shear Thickening]
    G -- Yes --> K[Plastic Fluid - Bingham / Casson]
    G -- No --> L[Generalized Viscous Fluid]
    B -- Depends on time scale --> M{Viscoelastic Material}
    M --> N[Is solid-like response dominant?]
    N -- Yes --> O[Viscoelastic Solid - G prime greater than G double prime]
    N -- No --> P[Viscoelastic Liquid - G double prime greater than G prime]
    M --> Q{Is viscosity time-dependent under constant shear?}
    Q -- Decreases with time --> R[Thixotropic]
    Q -- Increases with time --> S[Rheopectic]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Rheological Spectrum (Solid–Liquid Continuum)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Perfect Solid - Hookean] --> A2[Viscoelastic Solid]
    A2 --> A3[Gel - Weak Solid]
    A3 --> A4[Concentrated Dispersion]
    A4 --> A5[Polymer Solution]
    A5 --> A6[Dilute Solution]
    A6 --> A7[Perfect Liquid - Newtonian]
    A1 -. D_e very high .-> A2
    A2 -. D_e greater than 1 .-> A3
    A3 -. D_e around 1 .-> A4
    A4 -. D_e less than 1 .-> A5
    A5 -. D_e much less than 1 .-> A6
    A6 -. D_e approaches 0 .-> A7
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Force-Response Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Applied Stimulus] --> ST{Stimulus Type}
    ST -- Constant Force --> CR[Creep Test]
    ST -- Constant Strain --> SR[Stress Relaxation Test]
    ST -- Sinusoidal Strain --> OT[Oscillatory Test]
    ST -- Increasing Shear Rate --> SS[Steady Shear Test]
    CR --> R1[Measure gamma of t and J of t]
    SR --> R2[Measure tau of t and G of t]
    OT --> R3[Measure G prime, G double prime, tan delta]
    SS --> R4[Measure tau versus gamma-dot, eta apparent]
    R1 --> OUT[State Identification]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    OUT --> O1[Solid]
    OUT --> O2[Liquid]
    OUT --> O3[Viscoelastic]
    OUT --> O4[Plastic]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Moduli and Phase Angle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complex Plane: G star = G prime + i G double prime] --> B[Real Axis: G prime - Storage]
    A --> C[Imaginary Axis: G double prime - Loss]
    B --> D[Magnitude: modulus of G star]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Phase Angle: delta]
    E --> F{delta Analysis}
    F -- delta equals 0 --> G[Perfectly Elastic Solid]
    F -- delta equals 90 degrees --> H[Perfectly Viscous Liquid]
    F -- delta between 0 and 90 --> I[Viscoelastic Material]
    I --> J{G prime versus G double prime}
    J -- G prime greater than G double prime --> K[Elastic-Dominated Gel]
    J -- G double prime greater than G prime --> L[Viscosity-Dominated Sol]
```

## 4.5 ASCII Force-Deformation Sketch (Fallback Schematic)

For a physical picture that cannot be replaced by Mermaid, here is the canonical shear-element diagram in text form:

```
        Applied Force F
              ↓
              ↓
     ┌────────────────┐  ← Top plate (moves by dx)
     │      A         │
     │    ●  ●        │  ← Material element
     │   ●    ●       │
     │  ●  θ   ●      │  ← Deformation angle θ
     │ ●        ●     │
     └────────────────┘  ← Bottom plate (fixed)
     │←—————  h  —————→│  ← Element thickness
     
     γ = dx / h = tan(θ)
     τ = F / A
     G = τ / γ  (Hookean solid)
     η = τ / γ̇  (Newtonian liquid)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the physical state of a material. How does the concept differ in classical thermodynamics versus food rheology?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **classical thermodynamics**, the physical state of a material refers to one of the three classical phases — *solid, liquid,* or *gas* — distinguished by molecular order, density, and the balance between **intermolecular cohesive energy (U)** and **thermal kinetic energy (k_B·T)**. The boundaries between these states are defined by phase-equilibrium curves on a P-T diagram (e.g., the **triple point of water** at T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa).

In **food rheology**, the concept is significantly broader. Food materials rarely exist in pure classical states; they are multi-phase, multi-component systems exhibiting *intermediate* rheological behaviors such as **viscoelastic, plastic, pseudoplastic, thixotropic,** and **dilatant** responses. The physical state in rheology is therefore defined by the **mechanical response function** — how the material deforms, flows, and recovers under applied stress — rather than by molecular packing alone. 

The classification is based on characteristic constants such as the **shear modulus G (Pa)**, **viscosity η (Pa·s)**, **yield stress τ_y (Pa)**, and the **Deborah number D_e**. *(3 marks distributed: 1 mark for classical definition, 1 mark for rheological extension, 1 mark for key constants.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the significance of the Deborah number in classifying the physical state of a material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Deborah number (D_e)** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of the **material's intrinsic relaxation time (λ, in s)** to the **observation or process time (t_obs, in s)**:

$$D_{e} = \frac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$$

**Significance:**

1. **Unifies Solid and Liquid Concepts (1 mark):** D_e provides a continuous, mathematical framework to distinguish solids from liquids. When **D_e → ∞**, the material behaves as a *perfect elastic solid* (e.g., steel); when **D_e → 0**, it behaves as a *perfect viscous liquid* (e.g., water). Real materials lie in between.

2. **Process-Dependent Classification (1 mark):** The same material can be classified as solid or liquid depending on the timescale. For example, glass appears solid on human timescales (D_e ≫ 1) but flows over geological timescales (D_e ≪ 1). This has direct relevance to food processing — extrusion, mastication, and storage all involve different observation times.

3. **Practical Utility (1 mark):** D_e guides food process design. A sauce with **λ = 10 s** tested at **t_obs = 1 s** has **D_e = 10** (gel-like); the same sauce under extrusion at **t_obs = 0.01 s** gives **D_e = 1000** (solid-like). Process engineers use D_e to predict whether a food will fracture, flow, or recover elastically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the mathematical expression for shear strain and shear rate from first principles. Show the geometric relationship between displacement, deformation angle, and velocity gradient.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a thin rectangular material element of thickness **dy** and base width **dy**, fixed at the bottom. Apply a tangential force on the top surface, producing a small lateral displacement **dx** of the top face relative to the bottom.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Define the **shear strain γ** as the tangent of the deformation angle **θ** between the deformed top face and the original horizontal:

$$\gamma = \tan\theta$$

For small angles, **tan θ ≈ θ (in rad)**, and from the geometry of the triangle:

$$\gamma = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** In the *differential* form for an infinitesimally thin layer:

$$d\gamma = \frac{dx}{dy}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** For a *flowing* material, the top face moves with velocity **v = dx/dt** while the bottom is stationary. The **shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$** is defined as the time derivative of strain, which equals the **velocity gradient** perpendicular to the flow direction:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dy}\right) = \frac{1}{dy}\cdot\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{v}{dy} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Dimensional analysis:** [m/s] / [m] = [s⁻¹] ✓. The shear rate has units of reciprocal seconds. Conclusion: Shear strain is dimensionless and shear rate is the velocity gradient.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A tomato sauce shows power-law behavior with consistency index K = 12 Pa·s⁰·³⁵ and flow behavior index n = 0.35. Calculate (i) the apparent viscosity at a shear rate of 50 s⁻¹, (ii) the shear stress at the same shear rate, and (iii) comment on whether the sauce is shear-thinning or shear-thickening.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Consistency index **K = 12 Pa·s⁰·³⁵**
- Flow behavior index **n = 0.35**
- Shear rate **$\dot{\gamma}$ = 50 s⁻¹**

**Step 1 — Recall the Power-Law Equation (1 mark):**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

$$\eta_{a} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$n - 1 = 0.35 - 1 = -0.65$$

$$\dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = (50)^{-0.65} = \frac{1}{50^{0.65}}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(50^{0.65}) = 0.65 \times \log(50) = 0.65 \times 1.6990 = 1.1044$

Therefore $50^{0.65} = 10^{1.1044} = 12.72$

$$\dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = \frac{1}{12.72} = 0.0786$$

**Step 3 — Compute Apparent Viscosity (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{a} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 12 \times 0.0786 = 0.943 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Shear Stress (1.5 marks):**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} = 12 \times (50)^{0.35}$$

$\log(50^{0.35}) = 0.35 \times 1.6990 = 0.5947$
$50^{0.35} = 10^{0.5947} = 3.933$

$$\tau = 12 \times 3.933 = 47.2 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Flow Type (1.5 marks):**

Since **n = 0.35 < 1**, the sauce is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. As the shear rate increases, the apparent viscosity decreases ($\eta_a$ drops from 12 Pa·s at $\dot{\gamma}$ = 1 s⁻¹ to 0.943 Pa·s at $\dot{\gamma}$ = 50 s⁻¹). This is typical of tomato-based food products due to progressive breakdown of structural flocs or alignment of suspended particles in the direction of flow. Such behavior is exploited in pumping and bottle-filling operations where the sauce must flow easily under high shear but remain thick at rest.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\eta_a$ ≈ **0.943 Pa·s**
- (ii) $\tau$ ≈ **47.2 Pa**
- (iii) **Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behavior

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 1]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With neat sketches and equations, distinguish between the mechanical behavior of an ideal Hookean solid and an ideal Newtonian liquid. Discuss how real food materials deviate from these ideal behaviors.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ideal Hookean Solid (2 marks):**

A perfectly elastic solid obeys **Hooke's Law (1676)**: stress is proportional to strain.

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

where **G = shear modulus (Pa)** is the constant of proportionality. Upon removal of stress, the material recovers its original shape *instantaneously and completely*. The energy is *stored* elastically. Examples: hard candy, ice.

**Sketch (describe):** A linear, straight-line plot of **τ vs. γ** passing through the origin with slope **G**. On removal of stress, the strain drops vertically to zero.

**Step 2 — Ideal Newtonian Liquid (2 marks):**

A perfectly viscous liquid obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687)**: stress is proportional to *shear rate*, not strain.

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where **η = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)** is the constant. The material flows continuously under any non-zero stress and *does not recover* upon stress removal. All energy is *dissipated as heat*. Examples: water, air, simple oils.

**Sketch (describe):** A linear plot of **τ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$** passing through the origin with slope **η**. No yield stress; the liquid flows even under infinitesimally small stress.

**Step 3 — Real Food Materials — Deviations (3 marks):**

| Deviation | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscoelasticity** | Combines solid-like (storage G') and liquid-like (loss G'') responses; time-dependent | Cheese, dough |
| **Yield Stress** | Requires a minimum stress τ_y before flow begins | Margarine, butter, ketchup at rest |
| **Shear-thinning (n < 1)** | Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate | Ketchup, fruit purees |
| **Shear-thickening (n > 1)** | Viscosity increases with shear rate | Corn starch slurry |
| **Thixotropy** | Viscosity decreases over time under constant shear; recovers at rest | Yogurt, some gels |
| **Rheopexy** | Viscosity increases over time under constant shear | Less common in foods |
| **Non-linear viscoelasticity** | Moduli depend on strain amplitude (large deformation) | Bread dough during kneading |

**Conclusion (built into above):** Real foods are *never* ideal Hookean solids or ideal Newtonian liquids. They form a continuous spectrum between these extremes, and characterizing them requires the full rheological toolkit: creep, stress relaxation, oscillatory, and steady-shear tests.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Define the Deborah number. A food gel has a relaxation time of 5 seconds. Calculate the Deborah number when (i) tasted in the mouth (observation time = 1 s), and (ii) extruded through a 2 mm nozzle at a flow rate that gives an observation time of 0.01 s. Comment on the physical state in each case.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1.5 marks):**

The **Deborah number (D_e)** is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the solid-like vs. liquid-like character of a material:

$$D_{e} = \frac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$$

where **λ = material relaxation time (s)** and **t_obs = observation/process time (s)**. A high D_e indicates solid-like behavior; a low D_e indicates liquid-like behavior.

**Step 2 — Given Data (0.5 mark):**

- Relaxation time **λ = 5 s**
- **Case (i):** t_obs = 1 s (oral perception)
- **Case (ii):** t_obs = 0.01 s (extrusion)

**Step 3 — Case (i): Oral Perception (2 marks):**

$$D_{e}^{(i)} = \frac{5}{1} = 5$$

Since **D_e = 5 > 1**, the gel behaves predominantly as a **viscoelastic solid** during chewing. The material has time to partially recover elastically between successive bites, contributing to the perception of *firmness* and *elasticity* in the mouth. The gel feels springy.

**Step 4 — Case (ii): Extrusion (2 marks):**

$$D_{e}^{(ii)} = \frac{5}{0.01} = 500$$

Since **D_e = 500 ≫ 1**, the gel behaves as a **strong elastic solid** during high-speed extrusion. The material does not have time to relax and may *fracture* or undergo *die swell* (elastic recovery at the nozzle exit). This is a critical consideration in 3D food printing and extrusion-based processing.

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight (1 mark):**

The same gel appears *solid* in both cases, but the **degree** of solidity differs by two orders of magnitude. **Practical implication:** to extrude the gel smoothly (D_e ≈ 1), the process time must approach λ = 5 s, which means extremely low flow rates — impractical for production. Therefore, **process design must accommodate the gel's high D_e through heated nozzles, plasticizer addition, or mechanical assist.**

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **D_e = 5** → Viscoelastic solid (firm mouthfeel)
- (ii) **D_e = 500** → Strong elastic solid (may fracture during extrusion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing G, G', G''** — These are *three different moduli* (instantaneous shear, storage, loss). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Always state units of τ (Pa), η (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹). Missing units = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Sign errors in n − 1 exponent** — In power-law calculations, write the exponent explicitly before computing.
> 4. **D_e must be dimensionless** — Always verify the units cancel. Mixing λ and t_obs in different units (s vs. ms) is a common error.
> 5. **Misclassifying Bingham vs. Power-Law** — Bingham has *yield stress*; Power-Law does *not*. Don't confuse τ_y with K.
> 6. **Skipping "comment" parts** — In KTU 14-mark questions, the final "comment" or "discuss" sub-part carries **1.5 to 2 marks**. Skipping it costs heavily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Three classical states:** Solid, Liquid, Gas — distinguished by **U vs. k_B·T** balance.
- [ ] **Hooke's Law:** $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ — elastic solid; **G in Pa**.
- [ ] **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — viscous liquid; **η in Pa·s**.
- [ ] **Power-Law:** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ — **n < 1 → shear-thinning**, **n > 1 → shear-thickening**.
- [ ] **Bingham:** $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — has **yield stress**.
- [ ] **Casson:** used for **chocolate** and **cocoa** suspensions.
- [ ] **Deborah Number:** $D_e = \lambda / t_{obs}$ — **D_e ≫ 1 → solid-like**, **D_e ≪ 1 → liquid-like**.
- [ ] **Reynolds Number:** $R_e = \rho v L / \eta$ — flow regime indicator.
- [ ] **G' (storage)** = elastic energy stored; **G'' (loss)** = viscous energy dissipated.
- [ ] **tan δ = G'' / G'** — phase lag; **tan δ < 1 → gel-like**, **tan δ > 1 → sol-like**.
- [ ] **Foods are rarely ideal** — most exhibit viscoelastic, plastic, or thixotropic behavior.
- [ ] **Shear strain is dimensionless; shear rate has units of s⁻¹.**
- [ ] **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ — varies with $\dot{\gamma}$ for non-Newtonian fluids.
- [ ] **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**; **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**.
- [ ] **Triple point of water:** T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa.
- [ ] **Complex modulus:** $G^* = G' + i \cdot G''$; magnitude: $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$.
- [ ] **Process Design Rule:** A material is **solid-like** if the process time << relaxation time.
- [ ] **Memorize the comparison matrix** — solid, liquid, viscoelastic, plastic, power-law.
- [ ] **Draw Mermaid flow charts** — they may appear in KTU's structured diagrams section.
- [ ] **Units, units, units** — never omit them in numerical answers.

**End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1, Topic: Physical State of a Material** ✅

topics.title: Classical Ideal Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classical Ideal Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Classical Ideal Materials in Food Rheology

In food rheology, **Classical Ideal Materials** are the foundational, mathematically idealised mechanical models that describe how a material responds to an applied force (stress) or deformation (strain). They form the **building blocks** for understanding the complex, real-world viscoelastic behaviour of food systems like dough, cheese, ketchup, and chocolate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The three classical ideal materials are the **Hookean Solid (Ideal Elastic Solid)**, the **Newtonian Fluid (Ideal Viscous Fluid)**, and the **Saint-Venant Body (Ideal Plastic Solid)**. Every real food material is mathematically modelled as a combination of these three elemental bodies.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions

#### (a) Hookean Solid (Ideal Elastic Body)
An ideal material that obeys **Hooke's Law** in its entirety. The stress is directly proportional to the applied strain, and all the energy input during deformation is **fully recovered** upon removal of the stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Hookean solid is a material for which the stress at any point is a linear function of the instantaneous strain at that point, with no time-dependent effects. The deformation is instantaneous, fully recoverable, and follows a single, time-independent path.

#### (b) Newtonian Fluid (Ideal Viscous Body)
An ideal fluid that obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. The stress is directly proportional to the rate of strain (velocity gradient), and all the energy input is **irreversibly dissipated** as heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Newtonian fluid is a material for which the shear stress is linearly proportional to the shear rate, with the constant of proportionality being the viscosity ($\eta$). The deformation is permanent and time-dependent.

#### (c) Saint-Venant Body (Ideal Plastic Body)
An ideal material that behaves as a **rigid body** until the applied stress reaches a critical threshold called the **yield stress** ($\sigma_y$). Once $\sigma_y$ is exceeded, the material deforms continuously without any further increase in stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Saint-Venant body exhibits no deformation for stresses below the yield stress ($\sigma < \sigma_y$) and flows indefinitely for stresses equal to or greater than the yield stress ($\sigma \geq \sigma_y$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the three ideal materials as **mechanical LEGO bricks**:

- **The Spring (Hookean Solid) 🥎** — Imagine a metal spring. When you pull it, it stretches proportionally to your force. Let go, and it snaps right back. The work you put in comes back to you. *Food example:* The elastic recoil of bread dough when poked, the bounce of a gummy candy.

- **The Dashpot (Newtonian Fluid) 🛢️** — Picture a piston sliding through a cylinder filled with thick oil. Pull it slowly, it resists proportionally to the speed. The faster you pull, the more it resists. All the energy you spend is lost as heat. *Food example:* Water, milk, fruit juice, and edible oils all behave nearly as Newtonian fluids.

- **The Friction Block (Saint-Venant) 🧱** — Imagine a brick resting on a rough surface. Push gently — it does not move. Push hard enough to overcome friction — and it slides. *Food example:* Toothpaste, ketchup, and mayonnaise do not flow until the squeeze exceeds their yield stress.

These three bricks can be combined (in series or in parallel) to build models that describe **real food materials**, which almost always show a mixture of elastic, viscous, and plastic responses.

### 1.4 The Mechanical Symbols Used in Rheology

| Element | Symbol | Governing Law | Energy Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring (Hookean) |  $\dashv\!\rotatebox{90}{\rotatebox{-90}{\scriptsize $\mid$}}\!\dashv$  | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Fully **stored** and **recoverable** |
| Dashpot (Newtonian) |  $\square$  | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Fully **dissipated** as heat |
| Stiction/Slider (Saint-Venant) |  $\frown$  | $\sigma < \sigma_y \Rightarrow \varepsilon = 0$ | None until yield |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** In KTU question papers, you will frequently be asked to draw these mechanical analogues and label them. The spring represents elasticity, the dashpot represents viscosity, and the slider represents plasticity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hookean Solid — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The Hookean solid is the **simplest and most fundamental** model of solid behaviour. It assumes:

1. **Linearity:** Stress is linearly proportional to strain.
2. **Instantaneity:** Deformation occurs the moment stress is applied, with no time delay.
3. **Perfect recovery:** Once the stress is removed, the material returns to its original shape with **zero residual strain**.
4. **Energy conservation:** The strain energy stored equals the work done on the body; no energy is lost.

**Mathematical statement (generalised 3D form):**

For a linear elastic isotropic material, the generalised Hooke's Law in tensor form is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where $\lambda$ and $G$ are the **Lamé constants**, $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta, and $\varepsilon_{kk}$ is the volumetric (trace) strain.

**Common simplified forms used in food rheology:**

- Uniaxial tension/compression: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
- Simple shear: $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$
- Hydrostatic compression: $p = K \cdot \Delta V / V$

The **Why and How** of Hooke's Law in foods:

- **Why:** Many food solids (apples, cheese, crackers) approximate linear elasticity at small strains.
- **How:** The proportionality constants $E$, $G$, and $K$ are measured experimentally and depend on the internal microstructure (cell walls, protein matrix, fat crystal network).

### 2.2 The Newtonian Fluid — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The Newtonian fluid is the **simplest model of fluid flow**. It assumes:

1. **Linearity:** Shear stress is linearly proportional to shear rate.
2. **Time independence:** The viscosity is constant; it does not change with the duration of shearing.
3. **No elasticity:** The fluid has no memory of its previous deformation.
4. **Irreversibility:** All deformation is permanent; energy is dissipated as heat.

**Mathematical statement:**

In simple shear flow:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\eta$ is the **dynamic viscosity** (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma} = d\gamma/dt$ is the shear rate (s⁻¹), and $\tau$ is the shear stress (Pa).

**Extensional flow form:**

$$\sigma = \eta_E \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$$

where $\eta_E$ is the **extensional (Trouton) viscosity**, related to shear viscosity by $\eta_E = 3\eta$ for a Newtonian fluid (the Trouton ratio).

**The Why and How:**

- **Why:** Low-concentration solutions (sugar syrups, dilute fruit juices, vegetable oils) have molecular structures that do not entangle or aggregate, so their resistance to flow is purely frictional and proportional to the rate.
- **How:** Viscosity is measured in a rotational rheometer or a capillary viscometer and reported in **Pa·s** (SI unit) or **centipoise** (cP), where 1 Pa·s = 1000 cP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The kinematic viscosity $\nu = \eta / \rho$ is also used (units: m²/s or Stokes), particularly in fluid mechanics problems.

### 2.3 The Saint-Venant Body — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

The Saint-Venant (or St. Venant) body is the simplest model of **plasticity**. It assumes:

1. **Rigid behaviour below yield:** No deformation occurs for $\sigma < \sigma_y$.
2. **Infinite flow at yield:** Once $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$, the material flows with no further resistance.
3. **No elastic or viscous component:** It is a purely frictional element.
4. **Time independence:** Yield is a stress criterion, not a time-dependent one (in the ideal case).

**Mathematical statement:**

$$\varepsilon = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \sigma < \sigma_y \\ \text{undefined (free flow)} & \text{if } \sigma \geq \sigma_y \end{cases}$$

**The Why and How:**

- **Why:** Many semi-solid foods (ketchup, tomato paste, margarine, whipped cream) have a percolating network of droplets, crystals, or biopolymers that gives them a "structure". Below a critical stress, this structure holds. Above it, the structure breaks and the food flows.
- **How:** The yield stress is determined experimentally by extrapolating the flow curve to zero shear rate, or by the vane method, or by creep-recovery tests.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Ideal Material | Constitutive Equation (1D) | Proportionality Constant | SI Unit | Energy Fate | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hookean Solid (Spring) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Young's modulus $E$ | Pa | Fully stored, recoverable | Gelatin gel, fresh apple flesh |
| Hookean Solid (Shear) | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | Shear modulus $G$ | Pa | Fully stored, recoverable | Rubber, gluten gel |
| Newtonian Fluid (Shear) | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Viscosity $\eta$ | Pa·s | Fully dissipated | Water, milk, honey |
| Newtonian Fluid (Extension) | $\sigma = \eta_E \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Extensional viscosity $\eta_E$ | Pa·s | Fully dissipated | Dough during extrusion |
| Saint-Venant Body | $\varepsilon = 0$ for $\sigma < \sigma_y$ | Yield stress $\sigma_y$ | Pa | None (rigid) below $\sigma_y$ | Toothpaste, ketchup at rest |
| Bulk Modulus Relation | $K = E \cdot [3(1-2\nu)]^{-1}$ | Bulk modulus $K$ | Pa | Energy storage | Hydrostatic food pressurisation |
| Shear-Young Relation | $G = E \cdot [2(1+\nu)]^{-1}$ | Shear modulus $G$ | Pa | Energy storage | Shear cell measurements |
| Trouton Ratio (Newtonian) | $Tr = \eta_E \cdot \eta^{-1} = 3$ | Dimensionless | — | — | Extensional vs shear flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The Lamé constants $\lambda$ and $G$ relate to $E$ and $\nu$ as: $\lambda = E\nu / [(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$ and $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$. These are high-yield questions for KTU Module 1.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Processing

- **Spring (Hookean):** Used to model the **texture and firmness** of solid foods during compression testing (e.g., TPA — Texture Profile Analysis).
- **Dashpot (Newtonian):** Used to calculate **pipe flow pressure drops** in food factories (e.g., pumping milk, chocolate, oils). The Hagen-Poiseuille equation uses $\eta$ directly.
- **Slider (Saint-Venant):** Used in **squeeze tube and squeeze-bottle dispensing** to predict the force needed to start flow. Critical for packaging line design of sauces, pastes, and creams.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Generalised Hooke's Law to the Simple Shear Form

We start from the **generalised Hooke's Law** for a linear, isotropic, elastic material and derive the simple shear form $\tau = G\gamma$.

**Step 1: Write the full tensor form of Hooke's Law.**

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 2: Recognise the indices.** The index $i$ refers to the direction of stress, and $j$ refers to the direction of the normal to the plane on which the stress acts. Summation over repeated indices is implied (Einstein notation).

**Step 3: Consider a state of pure simple shear.** In simple shear, the only non-zero strain component is $\varepsilon_{12} = \varepsilon_{21} = \gamma/2$, where $\gamma$ is the engineering shear strain. All diagonal strains ($\varepsilon_{11}, \varepsilon_{22}, \varepsilon_{33}$) are zero, and the volumetric strain $\varepsilon_{kk} = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33} = 0$.

**Step 4: Substitute into the tensor equation for the off-diagonal component $\sigma_{12}$.**

$$\sigma_{12} = \lambda \, \delta_{12} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \, \varepsilon_{12}$$

**Step 5: Simplify.** Since $\delta_{12} = 0$ (Kronecker delta is zero for unequal indices) and $\varepsilon_{kk} = 0$:

$$\sigma_{12} = 2G \cdot \varepsilon_{12} = 2G \cdot \frac{\gamma}{2} = G \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 6: Identify the stress.** In simple shear, $\sigma_{12} = \tau$ (the shear stress).

$$\boxed{\tau = G \cdot \gamma}$$

**Conclusion:** The shear stress is proportional to the shear strain, with $G$ (the shear modulus) as the constant of proportionality.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Elastic Moduli ($E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$)

We derive the relation $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ from the basic definitions of the moduli.

**Step 1: Define each modulus.**

- Young's modulus: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (uniaxial stress over axial strain)
- Shear modulus: $G = \tau / \gamma$ (shear stress over shear strain)
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$ (negative ratio of lateral to axial strain)
- Bulk modulus: $K = -p / (\Delta V/V)$ (pressure over volumetric strain)

**Step 2: Apply a uniaxial stress $\sigma$ in the $x_1$ direction.** The resulting strains are:

$$\varepsilon_{11} = \frac{\sigma}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_{22} = -\nu \frac{\sigma}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_{33} = -\nu \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 3: Decompose the stress state into volumetric and deviatoric parts.** The stress tensor is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Express the corresponding strain tensor.**

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma/E & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -\nu\sigma/E & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -\nu\sigma/E \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Add pure shear on the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane to compute $G$.** Apply an additional shear stress $\tau = \sigma_{12}$. The shear strain is $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{12}$.

**Step 6: For a pure shear state, the volumetric strain is zero, so the bulk deformation does not contribute.** The shear stress relates to the shear strain as:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = 2G \cdot \varepsilon_{12}$$

**Step 7: Equate the energy densities.** The strain energy density in uniaxial tension is:

$$U_{tension} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon_{11} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

**Step 8: The strain energy density in pure shear is:**

$$U_{shear} = \frac{1}{2} \tau \gamma = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}$$

**Step 9: Using the energy equivalence principle for an equivalent stress state, set up the energy balance.** It can be shown (via the full tensor derivation) that the relationship is:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**Step 10: Similarly, derive $K$ in terms of $E$ and $\nu$.** Using the bulk modulus definition with the volumetric strain:

$$\Delta V / V = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33} = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

Setting $K = \sigma / (\Delta V/V)$:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Energy Stored in a Hookean Solid

We derive the energy stored per unit volume in a Hookean solid under uniaxial stress.

**Step 1: Start with the constitutive law.**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2: Write the energy per unit volume as the work done by the stress over a small strain increment $d\varepsilon$.**

$$dU = \sigma \, d\varepsilon = E \cdot \varepsilon \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3: Integrate from 0 (initial undeformed state) to the final strain $\varepsilon$.**

$$U = \int_0^{\varepsilon} E \cdot \varepsilon' \, d\varepsilon' = E \left[ \frac{\varepsilon'^2}{2} \right]_0^{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ to obtain alternative forms.**

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}}$$

**Conclusion:** The energy density is **quadratic in strain** and **fully recoverable** (it is released when the stress is removed).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Dissipated in a Newtonian Fluid

We derive the rate of energy dissipation per unit volume in a Newtonian fluid under simple shear.

**Step 1: Start with the constitutive law.**

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2: The power input (energy per unit time per unit volume) is the product of stress and strain rate.**

$$\dot{W} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^2$$

**Step 3: Substitute the definition of $\dot{\gamma}$ to obtain:**

$$\boxed{\dot{W} = \eta \left( \frac{d\gamma}{dt} \right)^2}$$

**Conclusion:** The energy dissipation is **quadratic in shear rate** and is **irreversibly lost** as heat. This is why viscous heating is significant in high-shear food processes like extrusion and high-pressure homogenisation.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: The Trouton Ratio for a Newtonian Fluid

We derive the relationship $\eta_E = 3\eta$ for a Newtonian fluid in uniaxial extension.

**Step 1: For a Newtonian fluid, the most general constitutive law is:**

$$\sigma_{ij} = -p \, \delta_{ij} + 2\eta \left( \dot{\varepsilon}_{ij} - \frac{1}{3} \dot{\varepsilon}_{kk} \delta_{ij} \right)$$

This is the **Stokes hypothesis** form for an incompressible Newtonian fluid, where $\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk} = 0$ (incompressibility).

**Step 2: For an incompressible fluid, the equation reduces to:**

$$\sigma_{ij} = -p \, \delta_{ij} + 2\eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}_{ij}$$

**Step 3: Apply uniaxial extensional flow in the $x_1$ direction.** The only non-zero strain rate is $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = \dot{\varepsilon}$ (the extensional strain rate). Incompressibility requires $\dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = -\dot{\varepsilon}/2$.

**Step 4: The normal stress difference in the axial direction is:**

$$\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22} = 2\eta (\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} - \dot{\varepsilon}_{22}) = 2\eta \left( \dot{\varepsilon} - \left(-\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{2}\right) \right) = 2\eta \cdot \frac{3\dot{\varepsilon}}{2} = 3\eta \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5: The extensional viscosity is defined as:**

$$\eta_E = \frac{\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 6: Combine to get:**

$$\boxed{\eta_E = 3\eta \quad \text{(Trouton ratio for Newtonian fluid)}}$$

**Conclusion:** For a Newtonian fluid, the extensional viscosity is **always 3 times** the shear viscosity. This is the famous **Trouton's ratio** and is a high-yield KTU question.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Ideal Materials

| Property | Hookean Solid | Newtonian Fluid | Saint-Venant Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | $\tau = \eta\dot{\gamma}$ | $\varepsilon = 0$ for $\sigma < \sigma_y$ |
| Time dependence | None | Linear | None |
| Energy fate | Stored (recoverable) | Dissipated (heat) | None (rigid) |
| Flow behaviour | Instantaneous elastic | Permanent viscous flow | No flow below $\sigma_y$ |
| Creep response | Constant strain | Linearly increasing strain | Zero strain |
| Recovery after unloading | Full, instantaneous | Zero | Zero |
| Stress-strain curve | Straight line through origin | Linear $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ | Horizontal at $\sigma_y$ |
| Representative food | Bread crumb, apple flesh | Water, milk, vegetable oil | Toothpaste, ketchup |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a Newtonian fluid. Write its constitutive equation and give two food examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Newtonian fluid is an ideal fluid for which the shear stress ($\tau$) is directly proportional to the shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$), with the constant of proportionality being the viscosity ($\eta$). The constitutive equation is:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Food Examples:** (1) Water, (2) Milk, (3) Vegetable oil, (4) Dilute sugar syrup, (5) Fruit juice. *(Any two = 1 mark; definition = 1 mark; equation = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a Hookean solid and a Saint-Venant body with respect to their stress-strain behaviour.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hookean Solid | Saint-Venant Body |
|---|---|---|
| Stress-strain law | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ (linear) | $\varepsilon = 0$ for $\sigma < \sigma_y$ |
| Deformation below threshold | Instant, proportional | None (rigid) |
| Energy | Fully stored, recoverable | None below yield |
| Flow above threshold | Continues linearly | Infinite (unbounded) flow |

*(Any 3 clear distinguishing points = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Always state units explicitly** — many students lose 0.5 to 1 mark per part for omitting Pa, Pa·s, or s⁻¹.
> 2. **Hooke's Law in shear is $\tau = G\gamma$, not $\sigma = G\gamma$.** Mixing up shear and tensile notations is the most common KTU error.
> 3. **When asked to "explain" a model, draw the mechanical analogue** (spring / dashpot / slider). Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram.
> 4. **Show all derivation steps.** KTU examiners follow a step-marking scheme; do not skip intermediate algebra.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the shear modulus $G$, Young's modulus $E$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ for a linear isotropic elastic material. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a Newtonian fluid, derive the Trouton ratio and explain its physical significance in food extrusion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$:**

*Step 1:* Consider a uniaxial stress $\sigma$ applied in the $x_1$ direction on a Hookean solid. The resulting strains are:
$\varepsilon_{11} = \sigma/E$, $\varepsilon_{22} = -\nu\sigma/E$, $\varepsilon_{33} = -\nu\sigma/E$.

*Step 2:* The corresponding strain tensor is:
$\varepsilon_{ij} = \text{diag}(\sigma/E, -\nu\sigma/E, -\nu\sigma/E)$.

*Step 3:* The strain energy density in uniaxial tension is:
$U_T = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon_{11} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$.

*Step 4:* Apply pure shear stress $\tau$ on the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane. The shear strain is $\gamma = 2\varepsilon_{12}$. The strain energy density is:
$U_S = \frac{1}{2}\tau\gamma = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}$.

*Step 5:* By the principle of energy equivalence, the shear modulus relates to the elastic constants as:
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

This relation holds for all **linear, isotropic, homogeneous, elastic** materials. (7 marks: 2 for setting up tensors, 3 for derivation, 1 for energy equivalence, 1 for final result with units.)

**(b) Derivation of the Trouton Ratio:**

*Step 1:* For a Newtonian fluid, the constitutive law in tensorial form is:
$\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + 2\eta\dot{\varepsilon}_{ij}$ (assuming incompressibility, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{kk} = 0$).

*Step 2:* For uniaxial extensional flow in the $x_1$ direction, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = \dot{\varepsilon}$, and incompressibility gives $\dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = -\dot{\varepsilon}/2$.

*Step 3:* Compute the axial normal stress difference:
$\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22} = 2\eta(\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} - \dot{\varepsilon}_{22}) = 2\eta[\dot{\varepsilon} - (-\dot{\varepsilon}/2)] = 3\eta\dot{\varepsilon}$.

*Step 4:* By definition, the extensional viscosity is:
$\eta_E = (\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22})/\dot{\varepsilon} = 3\eta$.

*Step 5:* The **Trouton ratio** is:
$$T_r = \eta_E / \eta = 3$$

**Physical significance in food extrusion:** In extrusion of dough, pasta, or melted chocolate, the food is subjected to both shear and extensional deformation simultaneously. The extensional viscosity is **3 times** the shear viscosity for Newtonian materials, which means the force required to stretch the food is much higher than the force required to shear it. This must be accounted for in the design of extruder dies and screw geometries. (7 marks: 3 for derivation, 1 for result, 3 for food extrusion explanation.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the mechanical analogue of a Hookean solid. Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a Hookean solid under uniaxial tension. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Newtonian food oil has a viscosity of $0.1$ Pa·s. It flows through a pipe of diameter $0.05$ m at a flow rate of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Calculate the shear stress at the pipe wall. Take the pipe length as $2$ m. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hookean Solid — Diagram and Energy:**

**Diagram (describe in words or sketch):**
A Hookean solid is represented as a **linear spring** with one end fixed. The symbols used are a **zigzag coil** ($\wedge\wedge\wedge$).

**Derivation:**

*Step 1:* Constitutive equation: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$.

*Step 2:* Energy density (work per unit volume):
$dU = \sigma\,d\varepsilon = E\varepsilon\,d\varepsilon$.

*Step 3:* Integrate from $0$ to $\varepsilon$:
$U = \int_0^{\varepsilon} E\varepsilon'\,d\varepsilon' = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2$.

*Step 4:* Final expressions:
$$U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \quad \text{(units: J/m}^3\text{)}$$

This energy is **fully recoverable** upon unloading. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 1 for energy equation, 1 for unit and recoverability.)

**(b) Pipe Flow Calculation:**

*Step 1:* Pipe radius: $R = D/2 = 0.025$ m.

*Step 2:* Average velocity from the flow rate:
$Q = A \cdot v_{avg} = \pi R^2 \cdot v_{avg}$.
$v_{avg} = Q / (\pi R^2) = (2 \times 10^{-4}) / [\pi (0.025)^2] = (2 \times 10^{-4}) / (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.1019$ m/s.

*Step 3:* For a Newtonian fluid in laminar pipe flow (Hagen-Poiseuille), the wall shear rate is:
$\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 v_{avg} / D = 8 \times 0.1019 / 0.05 = 16.3$ s⁻¹.

*Step 4:* Wall shear stress (Newton's law):
$\tau_w = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = 0.1 \times 16.3 = 1.63$ Pa.

**Answer:** $\tau_w \approx 1.63$ Pa.

(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for velocity, 2 for wall shear rate, 1 for final shear stress with unit.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use the maximum velocity (= $2v_{avg}$) instead of the average velocity in the Hagen-Poiseuille formula. This gives a wall shear stress that is **twice** the correct value. Always use $v_{avg}$, not $v_{max}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classical Ideal Materials**

- **Hookean Solid (Spring):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (tension) or $\tau = G\gamma$ (shear). Energy **stored**, fully **recoverable**. No time effects.
- **Newtonian Fluid (Dashpot):** $\tau = \eta\dot{\gamma}$. Energy **dissipated** as heat. Deformation is **permanent** and **time-dependent**.
- **Saint-Venant Body (Slider):** $\varepsilon = 0$ for $\sigma < \sigma_y$. **Rigid** below yield, **unbounded flow** above.
- **Key elastic relations:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ and $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$. For incompressible materials, $\nu = 0.5$ and $K \to \infty$.
- **Strain energy in spring:** $U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$ (units: J/m³).
- **Power dissipation in dashpot:** $\dot{W} = \eta\dot{\gamma}^2$ (units: W/m³).
- **Trouton ratio (Newtonian):** $\eta_E = 3\eta$. This is fixed for Newtonian fluids.
- **Mechanical symbols:** Spring = coil, Dashpot = piston in cylinder, Slider = block on rough surface.
- **Lamé constants:** $\lambda = E\nu/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$, $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$.
- **Common food examples:**
  - *Hookean:* gelatin gel, fresh apple, bread crumb.
  - *Newtonian:* water, milk, vegetable oil, dilute juice.
  - *Saint-Venant:* ketchup, toothpaste, mayonnaise, tomato paste.
- **Energy classification:** Spring stores, dashpot dissipates, slider does nothing.
- **Units to remember:** $E, G, K$ in **Pa**; $\eta$ in **Pa·s**; $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**; $U$ in **J/m³**; $W$ in **W/m³**.
- **High-yield exam traps:**
  - Confusing $\sigma$ and $\tau$.
  - Forgetting units in numerical answers.
  - Mixing up shear modulus $G$ and Young's modulus $E$.
  - Assuming Trouton ratio is always 3 (only for Newtonian fluids).
  - Treating Saint-Venant as a viscous body (it is purely frictional, no viscosity).
- **Why this matters for Module 1:** These three ideal materials are the **atoms** of rheological modelling. Every more complex model (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, Bingham, Herschel-Bulkley) is built by **combining** these three elements in series or parallel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Effects (Viscoelasticity)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Time Effects (Viscoelasticity) ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Viscoelasticity** is the property of materials that exhibit both **viscous** (liquid-like, time-dependent, dissipative) and **elastic** (solid-like, energy-storing, recoverable) characteristics when subjected to deformation. In food rheology, it describes the **time-dependent mechanical response** of foods to applied stress or strain, bridging the behavior of ideal Hookean solids (springs) and Newtonian fluids (dashpots). The phenomenon falls under the broader concept of **Time Effects** in rheology because the stress-strain relationship in viscoelastic foods is **not instantaneous** — the response at time *t* depends on the loading history (i.e., what happened at *t − τ* for all past times τ). **Mathematical statement:** For an arbitrary loading history, the stress σ(t) at the present time is a function of the entire past strain history γ(t′), t′ ≤ t: $$\sigma(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} G(t - t') \, \dot{\gamma}(t') \, dt'$$ This integral formulation is known as the **Boltzmann Superposition Principle** (linear viscoelasticity). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Memory Dough" Analogy Imagine you are kneading a ball of **pizza dough** on a countertop: - If you **press quickly and release** → the dough bounces back almost fully (it behaves **elastically**, like rubber). This is the *solid-like* response. - If you **press slowly and hold** → the dough slowly oozes outward, never fully returning to its original shape (it behaves **viscously**, like honey). This is the *liquid-like* response. - If you **press and hold for a few seconds** → part of the deformation recovers (elastic memory) and part flows away (viscous dissipation). The dough **"remembers"** its original shape but also **"forgets"** (relaxes) over time. This dual personality is **viscoelasticity**. Many real foods — bread, cheese, yogurt, gelatin gels, jam, dough, chocolate — exhibit this time-dependent duality. --- ### 1.3 Why "Time Effects"? In an ideal elastic solid (Hooke's law: σ = G·γ), stress depends only on the **current** strain. In an ideal viscous fluid (Newton's law: σ = η·γ̇), stress depends only on the **current** rate of strain. **Viscoelastic materials violate both** — their response depends on **how long** the load has been applied and on the **history** of loading. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** > The topic "Time Effects (Viscoelasticity)" covers: > (a) Time-dependent material functions > (b) Creep and creep recovery > (c) Stress relaxation > (d) Dynamic (oscillatory) testing — G′, G″, tan δ > (e) Mechanical analogs — Maxwell & Kelvin–Voigt models > (f) Deborah Number as a time-scale ratio --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Shear modulus | G | **Pa** | Stiffness in shear | | Viscosity | η | **Pa·s** | Resistance to flow | | Relaxation time | λ | **s** | Characteristic time of memory decay | | Storage modulus | G′ | **Pa** | Elastic (stored) energy | | Loss modulus | G″ | **Pa** | Viscous (dissipated) energy | | Loss tangent | tan δ | dimensionless | Ratio of viscous to elastic response | | Complex modulus | G* | **Pa** | Total resistance to oscillatory deformation | | Deborah number | De | dimensionless | (Relaxation time) / (Observation time) | --- ### 1.5 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping | CO | Statement | Bloom's Level | |---|---|---| | **CO1** | Explain the fundamental rheological concepts including viscoelastic behavior, time-dependent material functions, and mechanical models | Understand (L2) | | **CO2** | Apply mathematical models (Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt) to food systems and predict creep/relaxation behavior | Apply (L3) | | **CO3** | Analyze oscillatory rheometry data (G′, G″, tan δ) for characterizing food microstructure | Analyze (L4) | ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Linear Viscoelasticity — Operational Logic Linear viscoelasticity rests on three foundational pillars: **Pillar 1: Boltzmann Superposition Principle** The total response (stress) at the present time is the **linear sum** of responses to all past strain increments. **Pillar 2: Time–Strain Separability** The material response can be separated into a function of strain amplitude and a function of elapsed time. **Pillar 3: Small Deformation Regime** The strain must be sufficiently small (typically γ < 1% for foods) so that the structure is not irreversibly disrupted, ensuring the response remains in the **linear viscoelastic region (LVR)**. --- ### 2.2 The Four Principal Time-Dependent Experiments #### (a) Creep Test Apply a **constant stress** σ₀ at t = 0 and monitor strain γ(t) over time. **Strain response (for a viscoelastic solid):** $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_0} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1}\left(1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_0} t$$ where the three terms represent: - **Instantaneous elastic deformation** (spring G₀) - **Retarded viscoelastic deformation** (Kelvin–Voigt element) - **Viscous flow** (dashpot η₀) #### (b) Creep Recovery Test At time t = t₁, the stress is removed (σ = 0). The recoverable strain is the **elastic memory** of the material. **Recoverable compliance:** $$J_r(t) = J(t) - J(t_1) \quad \text{for } t > t_1$$ A purely viscous material gives **zero recovery**; a purely elastic material gives **full instantaneous recovery**. Viscoelastic foods give **partial delayed recovery**. #### (c) Stress Relaxation Test Apply a **constant strain** γ₀ at t = 0 and monitor stress σ(t) over time. **Stress decay (Maxwell model):** $$\sigma(t) = G \cdot \gamma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}$$ The stress **decays exponentially** with characteristic time λ = η/G. #### (d) Dynamic (Oscillatory) Test Apply a **sinusoidal strain** γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt) and measure the resulting stress: $$\sigma(t) = G^*(\omega) \cdot \gamma_0 \cdot \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ where **δ** is the **phase angle** between stress and strain: - δ = 0° → purely elastic - δ = 90° → purely viscous - 0° < δ < 90° → viscoelastic --- ### 2.3 Mechanical Analogs — The Two Elementary Models #### (i) Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Series**) Represents **stress relaxation** behavior of viscoelastic liquids. > [!NOTE] > **Construction:** A spring (G) and a dashpot (η) connected end-to-end. The total strain is the sum of individual strains; stress is the same in both elements. **Governing equation:** $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Characteristic relaxation time:** $$\lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$ **Creep response:** unbounded linear flow after initial elastic jump. **Relaxation response:** exponential decay. #### (ii) Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in **Parallel**) Represents **creep recovery** behavior of viscoelastic solids. > [!NOTE] > **Construction:** A spring (G) and a dashpot (η) connected in parallel. The total stress is the sum of individual stresses; strain is the same in both. **Governing equation:** $$\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$$ **Creep response:** exponential approach to equilibrium strain σ₀/G. **Relaxation response:** constant stress (does not relax). --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Maxwell relaxation modulus | $G(t) = G_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}$ | Pa | | 2 | Maxwell relaxation time | $\lambda = \eta / G$ | s | | 3 | Kelvin–Voigt creep compliance | $J(t) = J_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right)$ | Pa⁻¹ | | 4 | Burgers model creep compliance | $J(t) = 1/G_0 + (1/G_1)(1-e^{-t/\lambda_1}) + t/\eta_0$ | Pa⁻¹ | | 5 | Storage modulus | $G^{\prime}(\omega) = G^* \cos\delta$ | Pa | | 6 | Loss modulus | $G^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = G^* \sin\delta$ | Pa | | 7 | Complex modulus magnitude | $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G^{\prime})^2 + (G^{\prime\prime})^2}$ | Pa | | 8 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = G^{\prime\prime}/G^{\prime}$ | — | | 9 | Complex viscosity | $\eta^* = G^* / \omega$ | Pa·s | | 10 | Deborah number | $De = \lambda / t_{obs}$ | — | | 11 | Oscillatory strain | $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | — | | 12 | Oscillatory stress | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$ | Pa | | 13 | Cox–Merz rule | $\vert \eta^*(\omega) \vert_{\omega=\dot{\gamma}} \approx \eta(\dot{\gamma})$ | Pa·s | | 14 | Boltzmann superposition | $\sigma(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} G(t-t')\dot{\gamma}(t')\,dt'$ | Pa | > [!WARNING] > **Table Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute value bars in formulas use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (LaTeX absolute value) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry - **Cheese stretching (pizza mozzarella):** Designers tune G′/G″ so the cheese flows during baking but recovers elasticity when lifted on a fork. - **Yogurt texture:** Gel strength characterized by G′ ≫ G″; the tan δ value predicts "mouthfeel" and spoonability. - **Chocolate tempering:** Time-temperature protocols rely on crystallization kinetics tied to relaxation times of cocoa butter polymorphs. - **Bread dough:** Creep-recovery tests predict handling behavior during industrial sheeting and proofing. - **3D food printing:** The Deborah number determines whether a printable paste will hold its shape (solid-like) or collapse (liquid-like). ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Maxwell Model Stress Relaxation **Given:** - A spring of modulus G in series with a dashpot of viscosity η. - Both elements experience the **same stress** σ (series connection). - The **total strain** γ is the sum of individual strains: γ = γ_spring + γ_dashpot. **Step 1 — Constitutive equations of elements:** $$\gamma_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{G} \quad ; \quad \gamma_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta} \, t$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain strain rates:** $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} \quad ; \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Step 3 — Apply additivity of strain rates (series):** $$\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and obtain the governing ODE:** $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ > *This is the canonical Maxwell constitutive equation.* **Step 5 — Apply stress relaxation boundary conditions:** - At t = 0⁻ : γ = 0 - At t = 0 : a step strain γ₀ is applied instantly - For t > 0 : γ = γ₀ = constant → γ̇ = 0 Therefore: $$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Step 6 — Solve the ODE:** $$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\sigma$$ $$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}dt$$ $$\ln \sigma = -\frac{G}{\eta}t + C$$ At t = 0, σ(0) = G·γ₀ (initial elastic stress). Therefore C = ln(G·γ₀). $$\sigma(t) = G \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$ > The stress decays **exponentially** with characteristic time λ. After t = λ, the stress has dropped to **36.8 % (1/e)** of its initial value. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Moduli from Maxwell Model **Given:** The Maxwell model under small oscillatory strain γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt). **Step 1 — Substitute into the constitutive ODE:** $$\dot{\gamma}(t) = \omega \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ $$\dot{\sigma} + \frac{G}{\eta}\sigma = G \, \omega \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Seek a solution of the form:** $$\sigma(t) = A \sin(\omega t) + B \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate and substitute back:** $$\dot{\sigma} = A\omega \cos(\omega t) - B\omega \sin(\omega t)$$ Plug into the ODE and match coefficients of sin(ωt) and cos(ωt): - **Coefficient of sin(ωt):** $A\omega + \frac{G}{\eta}B = 0$ - **Coefficient of cos(ωt):** $B\omega + \frac{G}{\eta}A = G\omega\gamma_0$ **Step 4 — Solve the linear system (let $G/\eta = 1/\lambda$):** $$A = -\frac{B}{\lambda\omega}, \quad B = G\gamma_0 \frac{(\lambda\omega)^2}{1+(\lambda\omega)^2}$$ After algebraic simplification: $$A = G\gamma_0 \frac{(\lambda\omega)^2}{1+(\lambda\omega)^2}, \quad B = G\gamma_0 \frac{\lambda\omega}{1+(\lambda\omega)^2}$$ **Step 5 — Identify storage and loss moduli:** Because the stress is written as σ = G′γ₀ sin(ωt) + G″γ₀ cos(ωt): $$\boxed{G^{\prime}(\omega) = G \frac{(\lambda\omega)^2}{1+(\lambda\omega)^2}}$$ $$\boxed{G^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = G \frac{\lambda\omega}{1+(\lambda\omega)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting behaviors (boundary conditions):** $$\lim_{\omega \to 0} \; G^{\prime} = 0, \quad G^{\prime\prime} = 0$$ $$\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \; G^{\prime} = G, \quad G^{\prime\prime} = 0$$ $$G^{\prime\prime}_{\max} = G/2 \quad \text{at} \quad \omega = 1/\lambda$$ > **Physical interpretation:** At low frequencies, the Maxwell element flows like a liquid (G′ → 0). At high frequencies, it locks up like a solid (G′ → G).
