The peak in G″ occurs exactly at ω = 1/λ. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Creep Compliance of the Kelvin–Voigt Model **Given:** Spring G and dashpot η in **parallel** (same strain γ, additive stress). **Step 1 — Constitutive relation:** $$\sigma(t) = G\gamma(t) + \eta\dot{\gamma}(t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply creep BC:** σ(t) = σ₀ (constant) for t ≥ 0. $$G\gamma + \eta\dot{\gamma} = \sigma_0$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange into standard ODE:** $$\dot{\gamma} + \frac{G}{\eta}\gamma = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$ **Step 4 — Solve using integrating factor** $e^{Gt/\eta}$: $$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\gamma \, e^{Gt/\eta}\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} e^{Gt/\eta}$$ Integrate from 0 to t with γ(0) = 0: $$\gamma(t) e^{Gt/\eta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[e^{Gt/\eta} - 1\right]$$ $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right], \quad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$ **Step 5 — Express as compliance** J(t) = γ(t)/σ₀: $$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{G}\left[1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right]}$$ > **Physical interpretation:** Strain **asymptotically** approaches σ₀/G (the equilibrium spring deformation). The Kelvin–Voigt element **never flows** — it represents a viscoelastic **solid**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model Predictions vs. Real Behavior | Test | Hookean Solid | Newtonian Liquid | Maxwell | Kelvin–Voigt | Real Food (e.g., cheese) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Creep | Instant jump, no flow | Linear flow forever | Jump + linear flow | Exponential rise to plateau | Jump + delayed rise + slow flow | | Creep recovery | Full instantaneous | Zero recovery | No recovery (flowed away) | Full delayed recovery | Partial delayed recovery | | Stress relaxation | Constant σ | σ → 0 instantly | Exponential decay σ → 0 | Constant σ | Partial exponential decay | | G′(ω) | G (constant) | 0 | Increases with ω | G (constant) | Increases with ω (gel-like) | | G″(ω) | 0 | ηω | Peak at ω = 1/λ | G/(ωλ) | Frequency-dependent peak | ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Viscoelasticity Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MATERIAL RESPONSE TO LOADING"] --> B{"IS RESPONSE TIME-DEPENDENT?"} B -- NO --> C["PURELY ELASTIC SOLID
Hookes Law sigma equals G times gamma"] B -- NO --> D["PURELY VISCOUS FLUID
Newtons Law sigma equals eta times gamma dot"] B -- YES --> E["VISCOELASTIC MATERIAL
Time-dependent dual response"] E --> F["TIME DOMAIN TESTS"] E --> G["FREQUENCY DOMAIN TESTS"] F --> F1["CREEP sigma constant"] F --> F2["STRESS RELAXATION gamma constant"] F --> F3["CREEP RECOVERY sigma removed"] G --> G1["OSCILLATORY SHEAR"] G1 --> G2["STORAGE MODULUS G prime elastic"] G1 --> G3["LOSS MODULUS G double prime viscous"] G1 --> G4["LOSS TANGENT tan delta equals G double prime over G prime"] E --> H["MECHANICAL ANALOGS"] H --> H1["MAXWELL SPRING plus DASH POT IN SERIES"] H --> H2["KELVIN VOIGT SPRING plus DASH POT IN PARALLEL"] H --> H3["BURGERS MAXWELL plus KELVIN VOIGT IN SERIES"] H1 --> I1["RELAXATION TIME lambda equals eta over G"] H2 --> I2["RELAXATION TIME lambda equals eta over G"] E --> J["DIMENSIONLESS INDEX"] J --> K["DEBORAH NUMBER De equals lambda over t observation"] K --> K1["De greater than 1 SOLID LIKE"] K --> K2["De less than 1 LIQUID LIKE"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanical Analog Configurations ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MAXWELL["MAXWELL MODEL SPRING AND DASHPOT IN SERIES"] M_IN["APPLIED STRAIN gamma t"] --> M_S["SPRING G"] M_S --> M_D["DASHPOT eta"] M_D --> M_OUT["STRESS sigma t same in both elements"] end subgraph KELVIN["KELVIN VOIGT MODEL SPRING AND DASHPOT IN PARALLEL"] K_IN["APPLIED STRESS sigma t"] --> K_TOP["SPRING G"] K_IN --> K_BOT["DASHPOT eta"] K_TOP --> K_OUT["STRAIN gamma t same in both elements"] K_BOT --> K_OUT end subgraph BURGERS["BURGERS MODEL MAXWELL PLUS KELVIN VOIGT"] B_IN["APPLIED STRESS"] --> B_K["KELVIN VOIGT BLOCK"] B_K --> B_M["MAXWELL BLOCK"] B_M --> B_OUT["STRAIN OUTPUT"] end ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — A Typical Oscillatory Test Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant RHEO as RHEOMETER participant CTRL as CONTROLLER participant FOOD as FOOD SAMPLE participant DATA as DATA ACQUISITION RHEO->>FOOD: Load sample between parallel plates CTRL->>RHEO: Set strain amplitude gamma0 within LVR CTRL->>RHEO: Sweep angular frequency omega from low to high RHEO->>FOOD: Apply sinusoidal strain gamma t equals gamma0 sin omega t FOOD-->>RHEO: Generate stress response sigma t RHEO->>DATA: Record stress amplitude sigma0 and phase angle delta DATA->>CTRL: Compute G prime equals sigma0 over gamma0 times cos delta DATA->>CTRL: Compute G double prime equals sigma0 over gamma0 times sin delta DATA->>CTRL: Compute tan delta equals G double prime over G prime CTRL-->>RHEO: Output viscoelastic spectrum ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compliance Notes:** > 1. All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`node`-style identifiers such as `M_S`, `K_TOP`). > 2. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) appear as standalone node names. > 3. Node labels contain **plain text only** — no bold, italics, or HTML markup inside double-quoted labels. ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]** Define viscoelasticity. Explain with an example how a food material exhibits both viscous and elastic behavior. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Viscoelasticity is the property of a material to exhibit a combination of viscous (liquid-like, time-dependent flow) and elastic (solid-like, recoverable deformation) response when subjected to mechanical stress or strain. > > **Example explanation (2 marks):** Consider **pizza cheese (mozzarella)**. When chewed slowly, it flows and deforms permanently (viscous response). However, when stretched quickly during baking or lifting, it stretches elastically and partially recovers its shape (elastic response). Thus, mozzarella exhibits both behaviors, depending on the **rate** of deformation, making it viscoelastic. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]** What is the **Deborah number**? Indicate the physical significance of De ≫ 1 and De ≪ 1. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The Deborah number (De), named after the prophetess Deborah ("the mountains flowed before the Lord"), is defined as: > > $$De = \frac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$$ > > where λ = characteristic relaxation time of the material, and t_obs = observation time of the experiment (or process time-scale). > > **Significance (2 marks):** > - **De ≫ 1** → relaxation time ≫ observation time → material behaves as an **elastic solid** (e.g., bread crust, hard cheese). > - **De ≪ 1** → relaxation time ≪ observation time → material behaves as a **viscous liquid** (e.g., honey, milk, cooking oil). > - **De ≈ 1** → genuine **viscoelastic** response (e.g., yogurt, dough, molten chocolate). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (Choose ONE out of TWO) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Maxwell model** of viscoelastic behavior. Derive the expression for stress relaxation and creep response. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food material is modeled as a Maxwell element with spring modulus **G = 500 Pa** and dashpot viscosity **η = 250 Pa·s**. (i) Calculate the **relaxation time** λ. (ii) If a step strain of γ₀ = 0.05 is applied, calculate the **stress at t = 0**, **t = λ**, and **t = 5λ**. (iii) Comment on the physical state of the material at t = 5λ. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** > **Diagram (2 marks):** > ``` > ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ > ───┤ G ├─────────┤ η ├─── > └─────┘ └─────┘ > spring dashpot > (in series) > ``` **Stress Relaxation Derivation (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Constitutive equation** (additivity of strain rates in series): $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Step 2 — Apply creep-recovery / stress-relaxation BC:** γ = γ₀ = constant → γ̇ = 0. $$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\sigma$$ **Step 3 — Integrate with σ(0) = G·γ₀:** $$\sigma(t) = G \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}, \quad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$ **Creep Response Derivation (2 marks):** For a constant applied stress σ₀, the Maxwell model gives: $$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}t$$ This is a **linear flow** after an instantaneous elastic jump — representing a viscoelastic **liquid**. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Relaxation time (1 mark):** $$\lambda = \frac{\eta}{G} = \frac{250 \, \text{Pa·s}}{500 \, \text{Pa}} = 0.5 \, \text{s}$$ **(ii) Stress values (4 marks):** Stress function: σ(t) = G·γ₀·e^(−t/λ) = (500)(0.05)·e^(−t/0.5) = 25·e^(−2t) Pa - **At t = 0:** σ(0) = 25·e⁰ = **25 Pa** - **At t = λ = 0.5 s:** σ(0.5) = 25·e^(−1) = 25 × 0.3679 = **9.197 Pa** - **At t = 5λ = 2.5 s:** σ(2.5) = 25·e^(−5) = 25 × 0.00674 = **0.1685 Pa** **(iii) Physical state at t = 5λ (2 marks):** > The stress has decayed to less than **1 %** of its initial value (≈ 0.67 %). Physically, after five relaxation times, the dashpot has flowed almost completely, the spring is essentially unloaded, and the material has **lost its internal elastic memory**. The Maxwell element now behaves essentially as a **fully relaxed viscous fluid** with negligible residual stress. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Describe the **dynamic oscillatory test** for a viscoelastic food material. Define the storage modulus (G′), loss modulus (G″), complex modulus (G*), and loss tangent (tan δ). Discuss their physical significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In an oscillatory shear test on a yogurt sample, the following data were obtained: - Stress amplitude σ₀ = 12 Pa - Strain amplitude γ₀ = 0.02 - Phase angle δ = 18° - Angular frequency ω = 10 rad/s (i) Compute G′, G″, |G*|, and tan δ. (ii) State whether the yogurt is more "solid-like" or "liquid-like" and justify. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** > **Test description (2 marks):** > In a dynamic oscillatory test, the food sample is subjected to a small-amplitude sinusoidal strain γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt) (or stress input) within the linear viscoelastic region (LVR). The resulting stress is measured as σ(t) = σ₀ sin(ωt + δ), where **δ is the phase lag** between stress and strain. By varying ω, a viscoelastic spectrum is obtained. > > **Definitions (3 marks):** > - **Storage modulus G′(ω)** = (σ₀/γ₀) cos δ — represents the **elastic (energy-storing)** portion. Recoverable deformation. > - **Loss modulus G″(ω)** = (σ₀/γ₀) sin δ — represents the **viscous (energy-dissipating)** portion. Irreversible flow. > - **Complex modulus G\*** = σ₀/γ₀ — total resistance to deformation. $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$. > - **Loss tangent tan δ** = G″/G′ — ratio of energy lost to energy stored per cycle. > > **Physical significance (2 marks):** > - **tan δ < 1 (G′ > G″):** solid-dominated gel network (e.g., set yogurt, cheese). > - **tan δ > 1 (G″ > G′):** liquid-dominated behavior (e.g., milk, dilute solutions). > - The crossover point G′ = G″ marks the **gel point / sol–gel transition**. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Computations (5 marks):** Given: σ₀ = 12 Pa, γ₀ = 0.02, δ = 18°, ω = 10 rad/s. **Complex modulus magnitude:** $$\vert G^* \vert = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} = \frac{12}{0.02} = 600 \, \text{Pa}$$ **Storage modulus:** $$G^{\prime} = \vert G^* \vert \cos\delta = 600 \times \cos(18°) = 600 \times 0.9511 = \mathbf{570.6 \, \text{Pa}}$$ **Loss modulus:** $$G^{\prime\prime} = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta = 600 \times \sin(18°) = 600 \times 0.3090 = \mathbf{185.4 \, \text{Pa}}$$ **Loss tangent:** $$\tan\delta = \frac{G^{\prime\prime}}{G^{\prime}} = \frac{185.4}{570.6} = \mathbf{0.325}$$ **Cross-check using Pythagorean identity:** $$\sqrt{(570.6)^2 + (185.4)^2} = \sqrt{325{,}584 + 34{,}373} = \sqrt{359{,}957} \approx 600 \, \text{Pa} \; \checkmark$$ **(ii) Solid-like vs. Liquid-like (2 marks):** > Since **G′ (570.6 Pa) ≫ G″ (185.4 Pa)** and **tan δ = 0.325 < 1**, the yogurt behaves predominantly as a **solid-like gel** at this frequency. The casein protein network has formed a continuous three-dimensional matrix that stores more energy than it dissipates. This is consistent with set-yogurt texture perception: it holds its shape on a spoon, recovers elastically after deformation, and exhibits the characteristic "gelled mouthfeel." --- ### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Viscoelasticity Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the BC** (1 mark lost): In Maxwell stress relaxation derivation, students often write the ODE but forget to state γ = γ₀ = constant → γ̇ = 0. Always state boundary conditions explicitly. > 2. **Confusing series vs. parallel** (1–2 marks lost): Maxwell = series (strain adds). Kelvin–Voigt = parallel (stress adds). Examiners look for this distinction. > 3. **Unit inconsistency**: η must be in Pa·s, G in Pa, so λ = η/G comes out in seconds. Always include units. > 4. **tan δ interpretation**: A common error is saying "tan δ > 1 means solid-like." It is the opposite. Solid-like ⇒ tan δ < 1. > 5. **Decimals vs. π radians**: When using cos δ and sin δ in formulas, the angle must be in **radians** (or use degree-mode calculator consistently). Mixing modes loses 1 mark. > 6. **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR)**: Strain sweep must be done FIRST before frequency sweep. Failing to mention this in dynamic test answers costs 1 mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Time Effects (Viscoelasticity)** **✅ Foundational Concepts** - Viscoelasticity = viscous (liquid) + elastic (solid) time-dependent response. - Linear viscoelasticity holds for small strains (within LVR). - Boltzmann superposition principle: stress is the integral of past strain-rate history weighted by the relaxation modulus. **✅ Creep & Recovery** - Constant stress → measure strain γ(t). - Compliance J(t) = γ(t)/σ₀. - Kelvin–Voigt: J(t) = (1/G)[1 − e^(−t/λ)] (bounded, recoverable). - Maxwell: J(t) = 1/G + t/η (unbounded, viscous flow). - Burgers: J(t) = 1/G₀ + (1/G₁)(1 − e^(−t/λ₁)) + t/η₀. **✅ Stress Relaxation** - Constant strain → measure stress σ(t). - Maxwell: σ(t) = G·γ₀·e^(−t/λ). - After 5λ, residual stress is < 1 % of initial. - Food examples: bread dough, gluten network, melted cheese. **✅ Dynamic (Oscillatory) Testing** - Strain input: γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt). - Stress output: σ(t) = σ₀ sin(ωt + δ). - G′ = |G*| cos δ; G″ = |G*| sin δ; |G*| = σ₀/γ₀. - tan δ = G″/G′. - G′ > G″ → gel/solid-like; G″ > G′ → liquid-like. - Strain sweep FIRST to identify LVR; then frequency sweep. **✅ Mechanical Models** - **Maxwell (series):** spring + dashpot. Predicts stress relaxation. Cannot predict creep recovery. - **Kelvin–Voigt (parallel):** spring + dashpot. Predicts creep recovery. Cannot predict stress relaxation. - **Standard Linear Solid (Zener):** combines both — represents real viscoelastic solids. - **Burgers Model:** Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in series — predicts both creep and relaxation. **✅ Characteristic Time & Deborah Number** - Relaxation time λ = η/G. - Deborah number De = λ / t_obs. - De ≫ 1 → elastic solid; De ≪ 1 → viscous liquid; De ≈ 1 → viscoelastic. **✅ KTU-Favorite Numericals** - Compute λ from given G and η. - Compute σ(t) at multiples of λ using e^(−t/λ). - Compute G′, G″, |G*|, tan δ from oscillatory data. - Compare G′ and G″ to decide solid-like vs. liquid-like. **✅ Common Food Examples to Remember** | Food | Behavior | De range | |---|---|---| | Water | Purely viscous | De ≪ 1 | | Honey | Viscous liquid | De ≪ 1 | | Yogurt | Weak gel (G′ > G″) | De ≈ 1 | | Cheese | Solid-like gel | De ≫ 1 | | Bread dough | Viscoelastic (creep + relaxation) | De ≈ 1 | | Chocolate (melted) | Viscous liquid | De ≪ 1 | **✅ Examiner's Mantra — Always Include in Answers** 1. The governing constitutive ODE. 2. The boundary conditions. 3. The characteristic relaxation time λ = η/G. 4. Units of every quantity. 5. Physical interpretation of the result.

topics.title: Rheological Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rheological Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Rheological Equations?

**Rheological Equations** are mathematical constitutive relations that quantitatively describe how a food material **deforms** and **flows** when subjected to an applied force (stress). They form the backbone of *Food Rheology*, linking the internal microstructure of a food to its macroscopic mechanical response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEFTT745 — Module 1):**
> Rheological equations express the functional relationship between **shear stress (τ)** and **shear rate (γ̇)** — i.e., $\tau = f(\dot{\gamma})$ — together with associated parameters such as the **yield stress (τ₀)**, **consistency coefficient (K)**, **flow behaviour index (n)**, and **apparent viscosity (ηₐ)**.

Formally, in tensor notation, a generalized rheological equation is written as:

$$\tau_{ij} = f\left(\dot{\gamma}_{ij}, \, \tau_0, \, K, \, n, \, \eta_\infty, \, \eta_0, \ldots \right)$$

where the function $f$ depends on the rheological class of the material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey vs. Water vs. Butter Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for B.Tech Students (Plain English):**
>
> - **Water** flows the moment you tip the glass. The faster you pour, the harder it "pushes back" — this perfectly linear response is the **Newtonian** behaviour. Imagine pushing a smooth ice puck across an ice rink — perfectly proportional.
> - **Honey / Ketchup** does not start flowing until you apply a minimum squeeze (the bottle must be squeezed), and once flowing, it becomes *easier* as you increase force. This is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour, governed by a *power-law* equation.
> - **Toothpaste / Butter** refuses to flow until a *critical force* (yield stress) is crossed, and then flows almost linearly. This is the **Bingham Plastic** model — like a chocolate bar that breaks only after you snap it past a certain angle.
> - **Corn-starch slurry** becomes *harder* to mix as you stir faster — it is **shear-thickening (dilatant)**, and you actually need to push harder to keep it moving.

The mathematical equations we study today formalize exactly these everyday experiences into engineering-grade, calculable relationships.

## 1.3 The Three Building Blocks of Rheology

| Block | Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | Force per unit area applied tangentially | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| Shear Strain | $\gamma$ | Relative deformation (dimensionless) | — |
| Shear Strain Rate | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt}$ | Rate of deformation | **s⁻¹** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All rheological equations in Module 1 are variations of a single master idea:** the shear stress $\tau$ is some *function* of the shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$. The differences across models lie in *which function* is chosen and *what extra parameters* (yield stress, power index, etc.) are added.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flow Behaviour (The Master Family Tree)

Rheological equations are derived by classifying materials into families based on their $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ signature. The KTU syllabus expects you to know each equation, its parameters, and the food examples that follow it.

### 2.1.1 Time-Independent (Steady-Shear) Models
These describe flow at constant shear rate — most commonly tested in KTU.

**A. Newton's Law of Viscosity (Ideal Fluid)**

$$\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\eta$ is the **constant viscosity** (Pa·s), independent of shear rate. Examples: water, milk, fruit juices, vegetable oils, air.

**B. Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model**

$$\tau = K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consistency Coefficient | $K$ | Thickness index at $\dot{\gamma}=1\,s^{-1}$ | Pa·sⁿ |
| Flow Behaviour Index | $n$ | Deviation from Newtonian | dimensionless |

- $n = 1$ → Newtonian (recovers Newton's law with $K = \eta$).
- $n < 1$ → **Pseudoplastic / Shear-thinning** (ketchup, blood, polymer melts).
- $n > 1$ → **Dilatant / Shear-thickening** (corn-starch slurry, wet sand).

**C. Bingham Plastic Model**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \, \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\tau_0$ is the **yield stress** and $\eta_p$ the *plastic viscosity*. Used for tomato paste, margarine, drilling mud.

**D. Herschel–Bulkley Model** (most general three-parameter model)

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$$

- Reduces to Bingham if $n = 1$ and to Power Law if $\tau_0 = 0$.
- Used for **mayonnaise, yogurt, chocolate, blood**.

**E. Casson Model** (popular in chocolate & blood rheology)

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}}$$

where $\eta_\infty$ is the infinite-shear viscosity. **Specialty:** fits chocolate viscosity data well.

**F. Cross Model** (four-parameter; covers both low & high shear plateaus)

$$\frac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \frac{1}{1 + (K \, \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$$

where $\eta_0$ = zero-shear viscosity, $\eta_\infty$ = infinite-shear viscosity, $K$ = time constant, $m$ = rate constant.

**G. Carreau Model**

$$\frac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \frac{1}{\left[1 + (\lambda \, \dot{\gamma})^{2}\right]^{s}}$$

where $\lambda$ = time constant, $s$ = shear-thinning exponent.

### 2.1.2 Time-Dependent Models
- **Thixotropic** — viscosity decreases with time of shearing (ketchup, yogurt).
- **Rheopectic** — viscosity increases with time of shearing (rare; some gypsum pastes).
- **Viscoelastic** — combines viscous + elastic response (dough, cheese, gelatin).

### 2.1.3 Viscoelastic Constitutive Equations
- **Hooke's Law (purely elastic):** $\tau = G \, \gamma$ — $G$ = shear modulus (Pa).
- **Newton's Law (purely viscous):** $\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}$ — $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s).
- **Maxwell Model:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}$ — single relaxation time.
- **Kelvin–Voigt Model:** $\tau = G \, \gamma + \eta \, \dot{\gamma}$.

## 2.2 Apparent Viscosity — A Critical KTU Concept

For any **non-Newtonian** fluid, the viscosity observed at a given shear rate is called the **apparent viscosity**:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

For the power-law fluid, this simplifies elegantly to:

$$\eta_a = K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Pipe flow design:** Use $\eta_a$ in Hagen–Poiseuille equation for non-Newtonian flow.
> - **Pumping power:** Shear-thinning fluids require less pumping energy at high shear.
> - **Quality control:** $\eta_a$ at standard shear rate (e.g., 50 s⁻¹) is a quality index for sauces and dressings.
> - **Process design:** Sizing mixers, extruders, heat exchangers (via $K$ and $n$).

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S. No.** | **Model Name** | **Equation** | **Parameters** | **Apparent Viscosity $\eta_a$** | **Typical Food** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ | $\eta$ | Water, milk, oil |
| 2 | Power Law | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $K, n$ | $K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Ketchup, juice concentrate |
| 3 | Bingham | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0, \eta_p$ | $\dfrac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_p$ | Tomato paste, margarine |
| 4 | Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $\tau_0, K, n$ | $\dfrac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Yogurt, mayonnaise |
| 5 | Casson | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_0, \eta_\infty$ | $\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}}} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty}\right)^{2}$ | Chocolate, blood |
| 6 | Cross | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (K \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | $\eta_0, \eta_\infty, K, m$ | $\eta_\infty + \dfrac{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty}{1 + (K \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | Polymer solutions |
| 7 | Carreau | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \dfrac{1}{[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{2}]^{s}}$ | $\eta_0, \eta_\infty, \lambda, s$ | $\eta_\infty + \dfrac{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty}{[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{2}]^{s}}$ | Molten chocolate |
| 8 | Hooke (Elastic) | $\tau = G \gamma$ | $G$ | — | Solid fat (below melting pt.) |
| 9 | Maxwell (Viscoelastic) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}$ | $G, \eta$ | — | Bread dough |
| 10 | Kelvin–Voigt | $\tau = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $G, \eta$ | — | Gelatin, soft cheese |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary-Condition Rules (KTU favourite!):**
> - Newtonian ↔ Power Law when $n = 1$, $K = \eta$.
> - Bingham ↔ Newtonian when $\tau_0 = 0$.
> - Herschel–Bulkley ↔ Power Law when $\tau_0 = 0$.
> - Power Law ↔ Bingham when $n = 1$, $K = \eta_p$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Law Parameters from Log–Log Rheogram

### Problem Statement
A food sauce follows the power-law model $\tau = K(\dot{\gamma})^{n}$. Experimental data yield the following straight-line equation on a log–log plot:

$$\log \tau = 1.85 + 0.42 \log \dot{\gamma}$$

Determine the **flow behaviour index (n)**, **consistency coefficient (K)**, and classify the fluid.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the power-law in logarithmic form.**

Start from the constitutive equation:

$$\tau = K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$$

Take logarithm on both sides:

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the linear fit.**

The experimentally fitted equation is:

$$\log \tau = 1.85 + 0.42 \log \dot{\gamma}$$

Comparing the two, the slope is $n$ and the intercept is $\log K$.

**Step 3 — Extract the flow behaviour index.**

$$n = 0.42$$

**Step 4 — Extract the consistency coefficient.**

$$K = 10^{1.85}$$

Computing the antilog step by step:

$$10^{1.85} = 10^{1} \times 10^{0.85} = 10 \times 7.08 = 70.8 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{\,n}$$

**Step 5 — Classify the fluid.**

Since $n = 0.42 < 1$, the fluid is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**.

**Step 6 — Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \,\text{s}^{-1}$.**

$$\eta_a = K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n-1} = 70.8 \times (50)^{0.42-1} = 70.8 \times (50)^{-0.58}$$

$$(50)^{-0.58} = e^{-0.58 \ln 50} = e^{-0.58 \times 3.912} = e^{-2.269} = 0.1034$$

$$\eta_a = 70.8 \times 0.1034 = 7.32 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $n = 0.42$, $K = 70.8 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{\,n}$, fluid is pseudoplastic, $\eta_a = 7.32 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ at 50 s⁻¹.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Apparent Viscosity for Herschel–Bulkley Fluid

### Step 1 — Start with the constitutive equation.**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Use the definition of apparent viscosity.**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the stress expression.**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_0 + K (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Split the fraction.**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \, (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The first term $\dfrac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}}$ is the *yield-stress contribution* (dominant at low shear); the second term is the *power-law contribution* (dominant at high shear).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship between Cross and Power-Law Models in the Intermediate Shear Region

### Step 1 — Cross model in the intermediate shear region.**

For $\eta_0 \gg \eta \gg \eta_\infty$ and $K\dot{\gamma} \gg 1$, the Cross model simplifies to:

$$\eta \approx \frac{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty}{(K \dot{\gamma})^{m}} \approx \eta_0 \, (K \dot{\gamma})^{-m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the power-law form $\eta_a = K_p \dot{\gamma}^{\,n_p - 1}$.**

Matching exponents:

$$n_p - 1 = -m \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_p = 1 - m$$

Matching coefficients:

$$K_p = \eta_0 \, K^{-m}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the Cross model reduces to a power-law form in the intermediate shear region, and the parameters are related by the above equations.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wall Shear Rate for a Non-Newtonian Fluid in Pipe Flow

For a power-law fluid flowing in a circular pipe, the Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation gives the **true wall shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}_w$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{3n + 1}{4n} \cdot \frac{8V}{D}$$

where $V$ is the average velocity, $D$ is the pipe diameter, and $n$ is the flow behaviour index. **For Newtonian ($n = 1$):** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8V/D$ (recovers the classical formula). This is essential for designing pipelines for non-Newtonian foods.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Material Under Shear"] --> B{"Has Yield Stress?"}
    B -- "No" --> C{"Is Viscosity Constant?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Is Flow Linear Above Yield?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> E["NEWTONIAN: tau = eta times gamma dot"]
    C -- "No" --> F["POWER LAW: tau = K gamma dot to n"]
    D -- "Yes" --> G["BINGHAM: tau = tau0 plus eta_p gamma dot"]
    D -- "No" --> H["HERSCHEL BULKLEY: tau = tau0 plus K gamma dot to n"]
    F --> F1["n less than 1: Pseudoplastic"]
    F --> F2["n equal to 1: Newtonian"]
    F --> F3["n greater than 1: Dilatant"]
    H --> H1["Reduces to Bingham when n equal 1"]
    H --> H2["Reduces to Power Law when tau0 equal 0"]
    E --> I["Example: Water Milk Oil"]
    F1 --> J["Example: Ketchup Blood"]
    F3 --> K["Example: Corn Starch Slurry"]
    G --> L["Example: Tomato Paste Margarine"]
    H --> M["Example: Yogurt Mayonnaise Chocolate"]
```

## 4.2 Shear Stress vs Shear Rate Curve (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["A: NEWTONIAN"]
        A1["Linear through origin"]
    end
    subgraph B["B: BINGHAM"]
        B1["Linear but starts at tau0 on y axis"]
    end
    subgraph C["C: POWER LAW Pseudoplastic"]
        C1["Curves down, n less than 1"]
    end
    subgraph D["D: POWER LAW Dilatant"]
        D1["Curves up, n greater than 1"]
    end
    subgraph E["E: HERSCHEL BULKLEY"]
        E1["Starts at tau0, curves, n less than 1"]
    end
    F["Shear Rate gamma dot on x axis"] --> A
    F --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    F --> E
    G["Shear Stress tau on y axis"] --> A
    G --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — How to Choose a Rheological Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["New Food Sample"]) --> q1{"Rheometer data available?"}
    q1 -- "No" --> assumption["Assume Newtonian for dilute liquids and proceed"]
    q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Yield stress present?"}
    q2 -- "No" --> q3{"Linear tau vs gamma dot?"}
    q2 -- "Yes" --> q4{"Linear above yield?"}
    q3 -- "Yes" --> modelA["Use NEWTON model"]
    q3 -- "No" --> q5{"Curve up or down?"}
    q4 -- "Yes" --> modelB["Use BINGHAM model"]
    q4 -- "No" --> modelC["Use HERSCHEL BULKLEY model"]
    q5 -- "Down n less than 1" --> modelD["Use POWER LAW Pseudoplastic"]
    q5 -- "Up n greater than 1" --> modelE["Use POWER LAW Dilatant"]
    modelA --> fit["Fit parameters by linear regression"]
    modelB --> fit
    modelC --> fit
    modelD --> fit
    modelE --> fit
    fit --> validate["Validate with R squared and residual analysis"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:**
> - In **Mermaid**, node IDs are alphanumeric only (e.g., `q1`, `modelA`).
> - Labels inside `"…"` must be **plain uppercase text** (no `**bold**`, no tables) to ensure clean rendering.
> - These flowcharts map the **decision tree** a food engineer follows when classifying a material from rheometer data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the consistency coefficient (K) and flow behaviour index (n) in the power-law rheological equation. Mention their units.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The power-law equation is $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$, where:
- **K** = consistency coefficient, a measure of the fluid's "thickness" or resistance to flow at a shear rate of 1 s⁻¹. **Unit: Pa·sⁿ.**
- **n** = flow behaviour index, indicates deviation from Newtonian behaviour.
  - $n = 1$ → Newtonian.
  - $n < 1$ → shear-thinning (pseudoplastic).
  - $n > 1$ → shear-thickening (dilatant).
- K is **dimension-dependent** on n.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Bingham plastic and Herschel–Bulkley fluids with one food example each.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Bingham Plastic | Herschel–Bulkley |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ |
| Exponent | Linear ($n = 1$ fixed) | Non-linear (general $n$) |
| Parameters | 2 ($\tau_0, \eta_p$) | 3 ($\tau_0, K, n$) |
| Example | Tomato paste, margarine | Yogurt, mayonnaise, chocolate |

The Herschel–Bulkley model is **more general** and reduces to Bingham when $n = 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.A.(a)** Derive the power-law rheological equation and discuss how the parameters $K$ and $n$ are obtained from a log–log rheogram. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.(b)** A food sauce obeys the Herschel–Bulkley model with $\tau_0 = 12$ Pa, $K = 4.5$ Pa·sⁿ, and $n = 0.35$. Calculate (i) the shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹, and (ii) the apparent viscosity at the same shear rate. Classify the fluid. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q.A.(a):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the model.**
The power-law model (Ostwald–de Waele) expresses shear stress as a power function of shear rate:

$$\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic linearization.**
Taking $\log$ on both sides:

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** when plotted on a log–log scale of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$.

**Step 3 — Graphical extraction.**
- **Slope** of the straight line = $n$ (flow behaviour index).
- **Intercept** on the y-axis (at $\log \dot{\gamma} = 0$, i.e., $\dot{\gamma} = 1$ s⁻¹) = $\log K$, so $K = 10^{\text{intercept}}$.

**Step 4 — Classification rule.**
- $n = 1$: Newtonian.
- $n < 1$: Pseudoplastic.
- $n > 1$: Dilatant.

---

**Model Solution — Q.A.(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\tau$ at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \,\text{s}^{-1}$ using the Herschel–Bulkley equation.**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K (\dot{\gamma})^{\,n} = 12 + 4.5 \times (100)^{0.35}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the power term.**

$$(100)^{0.35} = 10^{2 \times 0.35} = 10^{0.70} = 5.012$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\tau = 12 + 4.5 \times 5.012 = 12 + 22.55 = 34.55 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute apparent viscosity.**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{34.55}{100} = 0.3455 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Classification.**
Since $n = 0.35 < 1$ and $\tau_0 = 12$ Pa is non-zero, the fluid is a **shear-thinning yield-stress fluid** (Herschel–Bulkley type). The presence of yield stress indicates a gel-like internal microstructure (e.g., yogurt, mayonnaise).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to add the yield-stress term $\tau_0$ in the final shear stress. Always write the **full constitutive equation** and substitute, *not* just the power-law part.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q.B.(a)** With the aid of a rheogram, classify food materials based on their $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ behaviour. Define Newtonian, shear-thinning, shear-thickening, and Bingham plastic behaviours. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** The following data are obtained for a non-Newtonian food:

| Shear Rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | 10 | 20 | 50 | 100 | 200 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Stress $\tau$ (Pa) | 8 | 13 | 25 | 41 | 70 |

Fit a power-law model and determine $K$ and $n$. State the nature of the fluid. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q.B.(a):**

The rheogram is a plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$. Four fundamental classes are:

1. **Newtonian** — straight line through origin, slope = $\eta$. Example: water.
2. **Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** — curve concave down; apparent viscosity decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$. $n < 1$. Example: ketchup.
3. **Shear-thickening (dilatant)** — curve concave up; apparent viscosity increases with $\dot{\gamma}$. $n > 1$. Example: corn-starch slurry.
4. **Bingham plastic** — straight line starting at $\tau_0$ on the y-axis. Example: tomato paste.
5. **Herschel–Bulkley (general)** — curved line starting at $\tau_0$, obeying $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$.

---

**Model Solution — Q.B.(b):**

**Step 1 — Take logarithms of all data points.**

| $\log \dot{\gamma}$ | $\log \tau$ |
|---|---|
| 1.000 | 0.903 |
| 1.301 | 1.114 |
| 1.699 | 1.398 |
| 2.000 | 1.613 |
| 2.301 | 1.845 |

**Step 2 — Apply linear regression $\log \tau = a + b \log \dot{\gamma}$.**

Using the standard regression formulas:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum \log \dot{\gamma}}{5} = \frac{8.301}{5} = 1.660$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum \log \tau}{5} = \frac{6.873}{5} = 1.375$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{xy}$ and $S_{xx}$.**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.000 | 0.903 | $-0.660$ | $-0.472$ | $0.3115$ | $0.4356$ |
| 1.301 | 1.114 | $-0.359$ | $-0.261$ | $0.0937$ | $0.1289$ |
| 1.699 | 1.398 | $0.039$ | $0.023$ | $0.0009$ | $0.0015$ |
| 2.000 | 1.613 | $0.340$ | $0.238$ | $0.0809$ | $0.1156$ |
| 2.301 | 1.845 | $0.641$ | $0.470$ | $0.3013$ | $0.4109$ |
| **Sum** | | | | **0.7883** | **1.0925** |

**Step 4 — Calculate slope ($b = n$).**

$$n = b = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \frac{0.7883}{1.0925} = 0.7215$$

**Step 5 — Calculate intercept ($a = \log K$).**

$$\log K = a = \bar{y} - b \, \bar{x} = 1.375 - 0.7215 \times 1.660 = 1.375 - 1.198 = 0.177$$

**Step 6 — Compute K.**

$$K = 10^{0.177} = 1.504 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{\,n}$$

**Step 7 — Final equation and classification.**

$$\tau = 1.504 \, (\dot{\gamma})^{0.7215}$$

Since $n = 0.7215 < 1$, the fluid is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**, suitable for a pourable sauce or juice concentrate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Do **not** use natural log ($\ln$) for the linear fit — KTU expects base-10 logs so that the intercept directly gives $\log_{10} K$.
> 2. Carry at least **3 significant figures** in regression or you will lose 1 mark on accuracy.
> 3. **Always** state the rheological classification in the final line — a numerical answer without a class loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rheological equation = constitutive relation** between shear stress ($\tau$) and shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$).
- **Master model hierarchy:** Newton ⊂ Power Law ⊂ Herschel–Bulkley (most general). Bingham ⊂ Herschel–Bulkley.
- **Flow Behaviour Index (n):** $n=1$ (Newtonian), $n<1$ (pseudoplastic/shear-thinning), $n>1$ (dilatant/shear-thickening).
- **Consistency Coefficient (K):** thickness index at $\dot{\gamma}=1\,\text{s}^{-1}$, unit = Pa·sⁿ.
- **Yield Stress (τ₀):** minimum stress to initiate flow; present in Bingham, Herschel–Bulkley, Casson.
- **Apparent Viscosity (ηₐ):** $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ — a shear-rate-dependent property for non-Newtonian fluids.
- **Casson** is the **industry standard for chocolate** — the ICA (International Confectionery Association) specifies it.
- **Herschel–Bulkley** fits most structured foods: yogurt, mayonnaise, cheese, blood.
- **Maxwell** for stress relaxation; **Kelvin–Voigt** for creep — both are viscoelastic, *not* flow models.
- **Log–log plot** of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ gives a straight line for power-law; slope = $n$, intercept = $\log K$.
- **Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{8V}{D}$ — true wall shear rate for non-Newtonian flow in pipes.
- **KTU favourite one-liner:** *“$n=1$ is the dividing line; everything below is shear-thinning, everything above is shear-thickening.”*
- **Always** give rheological classification as the **last line** of any model-fitting numerical answer.
- **SI Units (memorize):** $\tau$ in **Pa**, $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**, $\eta$ in **Pa·s**, $G$ in **Pa**, $K$ in **Pa·sⁿ**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheological Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rheological Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Rheological Models

A **Rheological Model** is a mathematically formulated idealization that describes the relationship between **stress**, **strain**, and **time** in a material under applied deformation. In food science, these models translate the mechanical response of complex food matrices (sauces, dough, gels, melts, suspensions) into tractable equations that can predict processing behavior, texture perception, and shelf-life performance.

Mathematically, a rheological model is expressed through a **constitutive equation** of the general form:

$$\sigma = f(\dot{\gamma}, \gamma, t, \text{history})$$

where $\sigma$ is shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate, $\gamma$ is shear strain, and $t$ is time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 1 of PEFTT745 specifically targets **ideal body behavior (Hooke & Newton)**, **viscoelastic analogs (Maxwell & Kelvin-Voigt)**, and **time-independent non-Newtonian models (Power Law, Bingham, Herschel-Bulkley, Casson)** for food systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Simple Intuitive Picture

Imagine you have a sponge cake (solid-like) and honey (liquid-like) sitting side by side on a kitchen counter.

- Push the **honey** with a spoon: it flows **immediately and continuously** the harder you push. This is a **Newtonian liquid** — pure viscous behavior.
- Press the **sponge cake** with your finger: it deforms by a fixed amount, returns to its original shape when released, and the deformation is independent of how long you press. This is a **Hookean solid** — pure elastic behavior.
- Now consider **ketchup**: push slowly, it barely moves. Push suddenly and hard, it splatters out. Its resistance depends on **how fast** you push. This is a **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** non-Newtonian fluid — modeled by the **Power Law**.
- Finally, picture **mayonnaise**: it needs a minimum force before it starts flowing. Below that threshold, it acts like a solid; above it, it flows. This is the **yield-stress** behavior described by the **Bingham or Herschel-Bulkley model**.

Every rheological model is, in essence, a different "recipe" of spring (elastic) and dashpot (viscous) elements — the building blocks of mechanics.

## 1.3 The Two Ideal Endpoints

| Ideal Body | Mechanical Symbol | Constitutive Equation | Storage Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hookean Solid (Ideal Elastic)** | Spring | $\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$ | Energy stored reversibly |
| **Newtonian Liquid (Ideal Viscous)** | Dashpot | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Energy dissipated as heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **G** = Shear Modulus (Pa), **η** = Viscosity (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear Rate (s⁻¹), $\gamma$ = Shear Strain (dimensionless).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Real food materials are *neither* pure springs nor pure dashpots — they are **viscoelastic**, meaning they exhibit both solid-like (energy storage) and liquid-like (energy dissipation) characteristics simultaneously. Rheological models provide the mathematical language to quantify this duality.

## 1.4 The Three Branches of Rheological Modeling (KTU Module 1 Map)

1. **Ideal Mechanical Elements:** Hookean spring, Newtonian dashpot, St. Venant slider.
2. **Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Power Law, Bingham Plastic, Herschel-Bulkley, Casson.
3. **Viscoelastic Linear Models:** Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Burgers, Standard Linear Solid.

Each model is built by combining the three primitive elements (spring, dashpot, slider) in series or parallel arrangements, with each arrangement producing a unique mechanical "fingerprint."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primitive Mechanical Elements

### 2.1.1 Hookean Spring (Ideal Elastic Solid)
- **Equation:** $\sigma = G \gamma$
- **Behavior:** Instantaneous, fully recoverable deformation. Energy is stored (not dissipated).
- **Creep response:** A step stress $\sigma_0$ produces an instantaneous strain jump to $\gamma_0 = \sigma_0 / G$, which then remains constant. Upon release, strain instantly returns to zero.

### 2.1.2 Newtonian Dashpot (Ideal Viscous Fluid)
- **Equation:** $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$
- **Behavior:** Time-dependent, irrecoverable flow. All energy is dissipated as heat.
- **Creep response:** A step stress $\sigma_0$ produces a strain that grows linearly with time: $\gamma(t) = (\sigma_0 / \eta) \, t$.

### 2.1.3 St. Venant Slider (Plastic Element)
- **Concept:** A frictional contact that does not move until the applied stress exceeds a critical **yield stress** $\sigma_y$.
- **Equation:** If $\sigma < \sigma_y$, then $\dot{\gamma} = 0$. If $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$, then the slider yields.
- **Application:** The defining element for yield-stress fluids like tomato paste, margarine, and mustard.

## 2.2 Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluid Models

### 2.2.1 Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Model
- **Equation:** $\sigma = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$
- **K** = Consistency Index (Pa·sⁿ)
- **n** = Flow Behavior Index (dimensionless)
- **Special cases:**
  - $n = 1 \Rightarrow$ Newtonian fluid (with $K = \eta$)
  - $n < 1 \Rightarrow$ Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) — most foods (ketchup, chocolate melt, fruit purees)
  - $n > 1 \Rightarrow$ Shear-thickening (dilatant) — corn starch suspensions, wet sand

### 2.2.2 Bingham Plastic Model
- **Equation:** $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ for $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$; otherwise $\dot{\gamma} = 0$.
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress; $\eta_p$ = Plastic viscosity.
- **Application:** Toothpaste, oil paint, drilling muds.

### 2.2.3 Herschel-Bulkley Model
- **Equation:** $\sigma = \sigma_y + K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ for $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$; otherwise $\dot{\gamma} = 0$.
- **Most general yield-stress model** in common use; combines yield stress with power-law shear dependence.
- **Application:** Mayonnaise, tomato ketchup, blood, sludge, and most structured semi-solid foods.

### 2.2.4 Casson Model
- **Equation:** $\sqrt{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}}$
- $\eta_\infty$ = Infinite-shear viscosity.
- **Application:** Specifically developed for chocolate melts and printing inks. Two parameters: yield stress and viscosity.
- **Re-arranged form:** $\sigma = \left( \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}} \right)^2$

## 2.3 Viscoelastic Linear Models

### 2.3.1 Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in Series)
- **Governing ODE:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
- **Stress relaxation (after step strain $\gamma_0$):** $\sigma(t) = G \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}$
- **Relaxation time:** $\lambda = \eta / G$
- **Creep compliance:** $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{G} + \dfrac{t}{\eta}$ (steady-state flow, no equilibrium)

### 2.3.2 Kelvin-Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in Parallel)
- **Governing equation:** $\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$
- **Creep response (step stress $\sigma_0$):** $\gamma(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\lambda} \right)$ with $\lambda = \eta / G$
- **Stress relaxation (step strain):** A step strain causes an immediate stress jump, but the model cannot represent true relaxation (it predicts infinite stress for sudden strain). Used for creep-recovery analysis.

### 2.3.3 Burgers Model (Maxwell in Series with Kelvin-Voigt)
- **Four-parameter model** combining Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt in series.
- **Creep compliance:**
$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G_1} + \frac{1}{\eta_1} t + \frac{1}{G_2} \left( 1 - e^{-t G_2 / \eta_2} \right)$$
- Captures instantaneous elastic, delayed elastic, and viscous flow components.

### 2.3.4 Standard Linear Solid (Zener) Model
- A Maxwell unit in parallel with a spring.
- Predicts finite equilibrium stress on relaxation and finite equilibrium strain on creep.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | Constitutive Equation | Parameters | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ | Water, milk, vegetable oils, honey |
| **Power Law** | $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $K, n$ | Fruit juices, sauces, purees |
| **Bingham** | $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\sigma_y, \eta_p$ | Toothpaste, margarine |
| **Herschel-Bulkley** | $\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $\sigma_y, K, n$ | Ketchup, mayonnaise, chocolate |
| **Casson** | $\sqrt{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\sigma_y, \eta_\infty$ | Chocolate, blood |
| **Maxwell** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\sigma}/G + \sigma/\eta$ | $G, \eta$ | Bread dough, polymer melts |
| **Kelvin-Voigt** | $\sigma = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $G, \eta$ | Solid-like foods, gels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Vigilance (KTU Marker Note):** $\sigma$ is always in **Pa**, $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**, $K$ in **Pa·sⁿ**, $G$ in **Pa**, $\eta$ in **Pa·s**, $\lambda$ in **s**, $J(t)$ in **Pa⁻¹**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Rheological Models

- **Process Design:** Predicting pump power, pipeline pressure drop, and mixing torque in food manufacturing lines.
- **Quality Control:** Fingerprinting texture (mouthfeel, spreadability, pourability) of food products.
- **Sensory Correlation:** Linking instrumental rheology to consumer-perceived texture descriptors.
- **Shelf-Life & Stability:** Monitoring structural breakdown of gels, emulsions, and suspensions during storage.
- **Product Development:** Formulating new foods with target flow behavior (e.g., squeezable yogurt, squeezable honey).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation

**Starting Point:** A spring (modulus $G$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) are connected **in series**. The total strain is the sum of individual strains.

**Step 1:** Express strain additivity.

$$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \gamma_{\text{spring}} + \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time (strain rates add).

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 3:** For the spring: $\sigma = G \gamma_{\text{spring}} \Rightarrow \dot{\sigma} = G \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} \Rightarrow \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma}}{G}$.

**Step 4:** For the dashpot: $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} \Rightarrow \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute into the rate equation.

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

> **Logic Note:** This first-order linear ODE is the defining constitutive equation of the Maxwell model. The first term captures elastic resistance; the second captures viscous flow.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maxwell Stress Relaxation Modulus

**Problem:** Apply an instantaneous step strain $\gamma_0$ at $t = 0$ and hold it constant. Find $\sigma(t)$.

**Step 1:** Since $\gamma$ is constant for $t > 0$, $\dot{\gamma} = 0$.

$$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into a separable ODE.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from initial stress $\sigma(0^+) = G \gamma_0$ to $\sigma(t)$.

$$\int_{\sigma(0^+)}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{G}{\eta} \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral.

$$\ln \left( \frac{\sigma(t)}{G \gamma_0} \right) = -\frac{G}{\eta} t$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate.

$$\sigma(t) = G \gamma_0 \, e^{-t / \lambda}, \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$

**Step 6:** Define the relaxation modulus $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0$.

$$G(t) = G \, e^{-t/\lambda}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Stress decays exponentially with characteristic time $\lambda$. At $t = \lambda$, stress has decayed to $\sim 36.8\%$ of its initial value. This is the **relaxation fingerprint** of the Maxwell model.

## 3.3 Derivation: Kelvin-Voigt Creep Compliance

**Problem:** Apply a constant step stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ to the Kelvin-Voigt element. Find $\gamma(t)$.

**Step 1:** Governing equation (parallel combination shares stress).

$$\sigma_0 = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that for parallel arrangement, stress is additive but strain is shared: $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$, with $\gamma_{\text{spring}} = \gamma_{\text{dashpot}} = \gamma$.

**Step 3:** Rearrange into a first-order linear ODE in $\gamma$.

$$\eta \frac{d\gamma}{dt} + G \gamma = \sigma_0$$

**Step 4:** Solve using integrating factor $\mu(t) = e^{Gt/\eta}$.

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left( \gamma \, e^{Gt/\eta} \right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} e^{Gt/\eta}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from 0 to $t$, with initial condition $\gamma(0) = 0$.

$$\gamma(t) \, e^{Gt/\eta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot \frac{\eta}{G} \left( e^{Gt/\eta} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify.

$$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\lambda} \right), \quad \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}$$

**Step 7:** Define creep compliance $J(t) = \gamma(t) / \sigma_0$.

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\lambda} \right)$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Strain approaches the equilibrium value $\sigma_0/G$ asymptotically — the dashpot delays the spring's full extension. The Kelvin-Voigt model does **not** exhibit steady-state flow; it represents a fully recoverable (delayed-elastic) solid.

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Law Apparent Viscosity

**Starting equation:** $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^n$

**Step 1:** Define apparent viscosity $\eta_{\text{app}} = \sigma / \dot{\gamma}$.

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{K \dot{\gamma}^n}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 2:** Examine limiting cases.

- If $n = 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}} = K = \eta$ (constant) — Newtonian limit.
- If $n < 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}}$ **decreases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases — shear-thinning behavior.
- If $n > 1$: $\eta_{\text{app}}$ **increases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases — shear-thickening behavior.

**Step 3:** A logarithmic linearization is commonly used to extract $K$ and $n$ from experimental data.

$$\ln \sigma = \ln K + n \ln \dot{\gamma}$$

This is a straight line on a $\ln \sigma$ vs. $\ln \dot{\gamma}$ plot, with slope $n$ and intercept $\ln K$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Herschel-Bulkley Apparent Viscosity

**Starting equation:** $\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ (for $\sigma \geq \sigma_y$)

**Step 1:** Apparent viscosity definition.

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 2:** At very high shear rates, the yield stress term $\sigma_y / \dot{\gamma} \to 0$, so:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \to K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 3:** At very low shear rates (just above yield), the yield stress term dominates:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \to \frac{\sigma_y}{\dot{\gamma}} \quad (\text{apparent viscosity diverges as } \dot{\gamma} \to 0)$$

> **Engineering Implication:** The Herschel-Bulkley model combines a yield stress with a power-law shear dependence — a three-parameter model that captures the broad behavioral spectrum of structured food fluids.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix: Key Model Responses

| Model | Step-Strain Response $\sigma(t)$ | Step-Stress Response $\gamma(t)$ | Steady Flow? | Stress Decays? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hookean Spring** | $\sigma = G \gamma_0$ (constant) | $\gamma_0 = \sigma_0 / G$ (instant) | No | No |
| **Newtonian Dashpot** | $\sigma \to 0$ (instant) | $\gamma = (\sigma_0 / \eta) t$ (linear) | Yes | Yes (instant) |
| **Maxwell** | $G \gamma_0 e^{-t/\lambda}$ | $\sigma_0 / G + (\sigma_0 / \eta) t$ | Yes | Exponential |
| **Kelvin-Voigt** | (infinite at $t=0^+$, not physical) | $(\sigma_0/G)(1 - e^{-t/\lambda})$ | No | N/A |
| **Burgers** | $G_1 \gamma_0 e^{-t/\lambda_1}$ (approximate) | All three components | Yes | Multi-mode |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanical Analog Symbols (Concept Map)

The following Mermaid block maps the **logical architecture of rheological modeling**, showing how primitive elements combine to form increasingly complex constitutive laws.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Primitive Mechanical Elements]
    node2[Hookean Spring - Symbol S]
    node3[Newtonian Dashpot - Symbol D]
    node4[St. Venant Slider - Symbol F]

    node5[First Level Combinations]
    node6[Series Coupling S plus D]
    node7[Parallel Coupling S parallel D]
    node8[Series Coupling F plus D]

    node9[Second Level Viscoelastic Models]
    node10[Maxwell Model]
    node11[Kelvin Voigt Model]
    node12[Bingham Plastic]

    node13[Advanced Combinations]
    node14[Burgers Model]
    node15[Standard Linear Solid Zener]
    node16[Herschel Bulkley Model]
    node17[Casson Model]

    node18[Food Application Layer]
    node19[Time Independent Fluid Models]
    node20[Time Dependent Viscoelastic Models]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4

    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node6
    node2 --> node7
    node3 --> node7
    node4 --> node8
    node3 --> node8

    node6 --> node10
    node7 --> node11
    node8 --> node12

    node10 --> node14
    node11 --> node14
    node10 --> node15

    node12 --> node16
    node3 --> node17
    node4 --> node17

    node16 --> node19
    node12 --> node19
    node10 --> node20
    node11 --> node20
    node14 --> node20
    node15 --> node20

    node19 --> node18
    node20 --> node18
```

## 4.2 Maxwell Model — Series Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Applied Strain Gamma t]
    nodeB[Total Strain Splits]
    nodeC[Spring Branch - G]
    nodeD[Dashpot Branch - Eta]
    nodeE[Common Stress Sigma]
    nodeF[Constitutive ODE - dSigma by dt divided by G plus Sigma over Eta]
    nodeG[Stress Relaxation Response - G gamma naught exp minus t over lambda]
    nodeH[Relaxation Time - lambda equals Eta over G]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 Kelvin-Voigt Model — Parallel Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP[Applied Stress Sigma naught]
    nodeQ[Stress Splits Across Both Elements]
    nodeR[Spring Path - G gamma]
    nodeS[Dashpot Path - Eta dgamma by dt]
    nodeT[Stress Summation - G gamma plus Eta dgamma by dt]
    nodeU[Creep Response - sigma zero over G times one minus exp]
    nodeV[Common Strain Gamma t]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeR --> nodeT
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeU
    nodeT --> nodeV
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Right Model for a Food Material

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start - Identify Material Behavior]
    q1{Does it flow under its own weight?}
    q2{Is there a minimum stress needed to initiate flow?}
    q3{Does viscosity change with shear rate?}
    q4{Does it show time-dependent recovery after deformation?}

    n1[Newtonian Fluid - sigma equals eta dot gamma]
    y2[Yield Stress Exists - Continue]
    n2[No yield stress - Check shear dependence]
    y3[Power Law - n less than 1 shear thinning, n greater than 1 shear thickening]
    n3[Newtonian confirmed]
    y4[Bingham Plastic - linear above yield]
    hbk[Herschel Bulkley - nonlinear above yield]
    casn[Casson - chocolate or blood specific]
    yvisc[Viscoelastic Model - Maxwell, Kelvin Voigt, Burgers]
    mxl[Maxwell - if material flows over time]
    kvg[Kelvin Voigt - if material fully recovers]
    brg[Burgers - if both flow and delayed recovery]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|No| q4
    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| y2
    q2 -->|No| n2
    y2 --> q3
    n2 --> q3
    q3 -->|Yes| y3
    q3 -->|No| n1
    y3 --> hbk
    y2 -->|Linear| y4
    y2 -->|Square root| casn
    q4 -->|Yes| yvisc
    yvisc --> mxl
    yvisc --> kvg
    yvisc --> brg
```

## 4.5 Apparent Viscosity vs. Shear Rate — Qualitative Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    map[Apparent Viscosity Map across Food Fluids]
    newt[Newtonian - horizontal line]
    pt[Shear Thinning - downward slope n less than 1]
    st[Shear Thickening - upward slope n greater than 1]
    bin[Bingham - horizontal line above yield]
    hb[Herschel Bulkley - downward slope above yield]
    cas[Casson - square root curvature above yield]

    map --> newt
    map --> pt
    map --> st
    map --> bin
    map --> hb
    map --> cas
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]
**Define the Newtonian model of fluid behavior. Give two food examples that closely follow Newtonian behavior.**

**Model Answer:**
A Newtonian fluid obeys the linear constitutive equation $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$, where the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate, and the proportionality constant $\eta$ (viscosity) is independent of shear rate, deformation history, and time. The viscosity of a Newtonian fluid is solely a function of temperature and pressure. Food examples include water, milk, refined vegetable oils (sunflower, soybean), honey (at moderate temperatures), and dilute sugar solutions. For these fluids, a plot of $\sigma$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is a straight line passing through the origin, with slope equal to $\eta$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]
**Distinguish between shear-thinning and shear-thickening behavior using the Power Law model. State the relevant value of the flow behavior index $n$ for each case.**

**Model Answer:**
The Power Law model $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ characterizes the flow behavior of non-Newtonian fluids. When $n < 1$, the fluid is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** — its apparent viscosity $\eta_{\text{app}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ decreases with increasing shear rate. Examples: ketchup, chocolate melt, fruit purees. When $n > 1$, the fluid is **shear-thickening (dilatant)** — its apparent viscosity increases with shear rate. Examples: concentrated corn starch suspensions, wet sand. The special case $n = 1$ recovers the Newtonian fluid with $K = \eta$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern]

**(a) Derive the governing constitutive equation of the Maxwell model. A Maxwell element has $G = 1000 \text{ Pa}$ and $\eta = 500 \text{ Pa·s}$. A step strain of magnitude $\gamma_0 = 0.05$ is applied instantaneously. Calculate the stress at $t = 0$, $t = \lambda$, and $t = 3\lambda$.**

**(b) Compare the creep responses of the Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt models. Under what type of food material behavior would you select each model?**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Derivation of the Maxwell Constitutive Equation:**

**Step 1:** In a series arrangement, total strain is the sum of the spring strain and dashpot strain.

$$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \gamma_{\text{spring}} + \gamma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{\text{spring}} + \dot{\gamma}_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 3:** For the spring: $\sigma = G \gamma_s \Rightarrow \dot{\sigma} = G \dot{\gamma}_s$, so $\dot{\gamma}_s = \dot{\sigma}/G$.

**Step 4:** For the dashpot: $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}_d$, so $\dot{\gamma}_d = \sigma/\eta$.

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{G} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**

**Step 6:** Compute the relaxation time:

$$\lambda = \frac{\eta}{G} = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the stress relaxation formula $\sigma(t) = G \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}$:

- At $t = 0$: $\sigma(0) = (1000)(0.05) e^0 = 50 \text{ Pa}$
- At $t = \lambda = 0.5$ s: $\sigma(\lambda) = 50 \cdot e^{-1} = 50 \cdot 0.3679 \approx 18.4 \text{ Pa}$
- At $t = 3\lambda = 1.5$ s: $\sigma(3\lambda) = 50 \cdot e^{-3} = 50 \cdot 0.0498 \approx 2.49 \text{ Pa}$

> **Insight for students:** At one relaxation time, the stress has dropped to about 36.8% of its initial value. At three relaxation times, only about 5% remains — a useful rule of thumb for estimating how quickly a Maxwell-type material "forgets" its elastic stress.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Comparison of Creep Responses:**

| Feature | Maxwell Model | Kelvin-Voigt Model |
|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** |
| Creep compliance $J(t)$ | $\frac{1}{G} + \frac{t}{\eta}$ | $\frac{1}{G}(1 - e^{-t/\lambda})$ |
| Instantaneous strain at $t = 0^+$ | $\sigma_0 / G$ (finite jump) | 0 (no jump) |
| Long-time behavior | $\gamma \to \infty$ (steady flow) | $\gamma \to \sigma_0/G$ (equilibrium) |
| Recovery after stress removal | Permanent deformation remains | Full recovery to zero strain |
| Represents | Viscoelastic **fluid** (flow + relax) | Viscoelastic **solid** (delayed elastic) |

**Material Selection Criterion:**

- Choose **Maxwell** for foods that **flow over long times** but show an instantaneous elastic response followed by stress decay — e.g., bread dough during proofing, polymer melts, fresh cheese curds under sustained stress.
- Choose **Kelvin-Voigt** for foods that **fully recover** their shape (eventually) after deformation — e.g., solid gels (agar, gelatin), rubbery-textured foods, or the delayed elastic response of bread crumb under compression.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Maxwell vs. Kelvin-Voigt Trap:**
> A common error costing 2–3 marks is stating that "Maxwell represents a solid" and "Kelvin-Voigt represents a liquid." The correct classification is the **opposite**:
> - **Maxwell = Viscoelastic Fluid** (it flows indefinitely under sustained load).
> - **Kelvin-Voigt = Viscoelastic Solid** (it reaches a finite equilibrium strain and fully recovers).
> Also, students often forget that the Kelvin-Voigt model **cannot** represent true stress relaxation under a step strain — the predicted response is non-physical (infinite stress at $t = 0^+$). Examiners specifically test this.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern]

**(a) Explain the Herschel-Bulkley model in detail. How does it generalize both the Bingham Plastic and Power Law models? A food material follows $\sigma = 5 + 3 \dot{\gamma}^{0.4}$ (with $\sigma$ in Pa and $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹). Identify the yield stress, consistency index, and flow behavior index. Calculate the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$.**

**(b) Describe the Casson model. For what specific food product is it most commonly used, and why? Show that at very high shear rates, the Casson model reduces to a Newtonian fluid with viscosity $\eta_\infty$.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The Herschel-Bulkley Model — Conceptual Description:**

The Herschel-Bulkley model is a three-parameter constitutive equation written as:

$$\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n \quad \text{for } \sigma \geq \sigma_y; \quad \dot{\gamma} = 0 \text{ for } \sigma < \sigma_y$$

Here $\sigma_y$ is the **yield stress** (Pa), $K$ is the **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ), and $n$ is the **flow behavior index** (dimensionless).

**Generalization Properties:**

- **If $n = 1$:** The equation reduces to $\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}$, which is the **Bingham Plastic model** with $\eta_p = K$. So Herschel-Bulkley generalizes Bingham by allowing nonlinear post-yield behavior.
- **If $\sigma_y = 0$:** The equation reduces to $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^n$, the **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) model**. So Herschel-Bulkley generalizes Power Law by adding a yield stress.
- **If $n = 1$ and $\sigma_y = 0$:** Reduces to $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}$, i.e., **Newtonian fluid** with $\eta = K$.

The Herschel-Bulkley model is therefore the **most general three-parameter time-independent fluid model** in routine use for food systems.

**Numerical Identification:**

From the given equation $\sigma = 5 + 3 \dot{\gamma}^{0.4}$:

- Yield stress: $\sigma_y = 5 \text{ Pa}$
- Consistency index: $K = 3 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.4}$
- Flow behavior index: $n = 0.4$ (shear-thinning, since $n < 1$)

**Apparent Viscosity Calculations:**

The apparent viscosity is defined as $\eta_{\text{app}} = \sigma / \dot{\gamma} = \sigma_y / \dot{\gamma} + K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.

**At $\dot{\gamma} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$:**

$$\sigma = 5 + 3(10)^{0.4} = 5 + 3(2.512) = 5 + 7.535 = 12.535 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{12.535}{10} = 1.254 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**At $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:**

$$\sigma = 5 + 3(100)^{0.4} = 5 + 3(6.310) = 5 + 18.929 = 23.929 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{23.929}{100} = 0.2393 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Observation:** Apparent viscosity dropped from 1.254 Pa·s to 0.2393 Pa·s as shear rate increased tenfold — a clear demonstration of **shear-thinning behavior** ($n = 0.4 < 1$).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The Casson Model — Description:**

The Casson model is a two-parameter yield-stress model originally developed for printing inks and later applied to food systems. Its constitutive equation is:

$$\sqrt{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}}$$

Equivalently, by squaring both sides:

$$\sigma = \left( \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}} \right)^2$$

**Parameters:**
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $\eta_\infty$ = viscosity at infinite shear rate (Pa·s)

**Food Application:**

The Casson model is most commonly used for **chocolate melts** (cocoa liquor, dark chocolate, milk chocolate). The chocolate industry standardized on the Casson model because it accurately describes the flow behavior of molten chocolate across a wide range of shear rates encountered during conching, tempering, molding, and enrobing. It is also used for **blood rheology** in biomedical engineering.

**High-Shear-Rate Limiting Behavior:**

Starting from:

$$\sqrt{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}}$$

Divide both sides by $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$:

$$\sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}} = \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_y}}{\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty}$$

As $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$, the term $\sqrt{\sigma_y / \dot{\gamma}} \to 0$, so:

$$\sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}}} \to \sqrt{\eta_\infty}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{\sigma}{\dot{\gamma}} \to \eta_\infty$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma \to \eta_\infty \, \dot{\gamma}$$

This is the constitutive equation of a **Newtonian fluid** with viscosity $\eta_\infty$. Hence, at very high shear rates, the Casson model approaches Newtonian behavior — physically consistent with the observation that the internal structure (e.g., cocoa particle flocs in chocolate) is fully broken down at high shear.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Casson vs. Herschel-Bulkley Confusion:**
> Students often mix up the two yield-stress models. Key differentiators:
> - **Casson** is a **two-parameter** model, designed with a square-root structure specifically to fit chocolate and inks.
> - **Herschel-Bulkley** is a **three-parameter** model, more flexible and applicable to a wider range of structured foods.
> - At infinite shear, **Casson becomes Newtonian**; **Herschel-Bulkley** approaches the Power Law $K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — only Newtonian if $n = 1$.
> Examiners frequently award 2 marks just for correctly stating the parameter count of each model.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newtonian fluid:** $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — single parameter ($\eta$). Apparent viscosity constant.
- **Power Law fluid:** $\sigma = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — $n < 1$ (shear-thinning), $n > 1$ (shear-thickening), $n = 1$ (Newtonian).
- **Bingham plastic:** $\sigma = \sigma_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ — linear flow above yield; no flow below.
- **Herschel-Bulkley:** $\sigma = \sigma_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — three-parameter generalization encompassing Bingham and Power Law as special cases.
- **Casson model:** $\sqrt{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sigma_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ — chocolate's standard model; becomes Newtonian at infinite shear.
- **Maxwell model:** Spring and dashpot in **series**; viscoelastic **fluid**; stress relaxes exponentially with time constant $\lambda = \eta/G$.
- **Kelvin-Voigt model:** Spring and dashpot in **parallel**; viscoelastic **solid**; fully recoverable creep compliance $J(t) = (1/G)(1 - e^{-t/\lambda})$.
- **Burgers model:** Maxwell in series with Kelvin-Voigt; four-parameter model capturing instantaneous elasticity, delayed elasticity, and viscous flow.
- **Standard Linear Solid (Zener):** Maxwell unit in parallel with a spring; gives finite equilibrium stress on relaxation.
- **Units to memorize:** $\sigma$ (Pa), $\eta$ (Pa·s), $G$ (Pa), $K$ (Pa·sⁿ), $\lambda$ (s), $J$ (Pa⁻¹), $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹).
- **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_{\text{app}} = \sigma / \dot{\gamma}$ — varies with shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids.
- **Master classification rule:** Maxwell = fluid (flows); Kelvin-Voigt = solid (recovers).
- **For chocolate → Casson.** **For ketchup/mayonnaise → Herschel-Bulkley.** **For polymer melts/dough → Maxwell.** **For gels → Kelvin-Voigt.**
- **Linearization trick:** $\ln \sigma = \ln K + n \ln \dot{\gamma}$ — Power Law parameters extracted from log-log plot slope and intercept.
- **KTU 2024 common question pattern:** "Derive the constitutive equation and predict the response under a step stress / step strain input."
- **Always state assumptions** — linear viscoelasticity, isothermal conditions, no chemical reaction during deformation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Deformation: Shear Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Deformation: Shear Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In food rheology, **deformation** is the change in shape or size of a food material in response to an applied force or stress. Materials under stress can experience three principal modes of deformation:

1. **Shear deformation (Shear flow)** – layers of the material slide past one another.
2. **Elongational / Extensional deformation** – the material is stretched along an axis.
3. **Bulk / Volumetric deformation** – the material is compressed uniformly in all directions (change in volume).

Among these, **Shear Flow** is the most frequently encountered mode in food processing operations such as mixing, pumping, extrusion, mastication (chewing), and pipelining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Flow (KTU Definition):** Shear flow is a type of deformation in which parallel layers of the material move (slide) relative to each other under the action of a tangential (shear) force. The deformation is characterized by a change in shape without a corresponding change in volume, described mathematically by the shear strain $\gamma$ and shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** resting on a table. If you place your hand on top of the deck and push horizontally, each card slides slightly relative to the one beneath it. The bottom card stays fixed; the top card moves the most; every intermediate card moves by a small amount proportional to its height. The deck remains the same shape horizontally (no stretching of cards) but its overall shape has *deformed*.

The same happens when:
- You spread **cold honey** across bread with a knife — the thin layer of honey flows in shear between the knife and the bread surface.
- A food slurry flows through a pipe — fluid layers near the walls move slowly, central layers move fast.
- A sauce is **stirred** with a spoon — concentric layers of sauce slide past one another.

In each case, the substance is undergoing **shear flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** Shear flow produces a *change in shape* (angular distortion of volume elements) but **does not change the volume** of the material. This is its defining geometric feature and distinguishes it from extensional and bulk deformation.

### 1.3 Shear Stress and Shear Strain — The Two Field Variables

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear stress | $\tau$ | Tangential force per unit area applied parallel to a surface | **Pa** (N·m⁻²) |
| Shear strain | $\gamma$ | Relative displacement of a layer divided by the distance from the reference plane | dimensionless (rad) |
| Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | Time rate of change of shear strain | **s⁻¹** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Shear strain is dimensionless but, for small deformations, is expressed in **radians (rad)** because it represents the angular distortion of an originally right-angled element.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Kinematics of Shear Flow — "How" and "Why"

**Step 1 — Reference Geometry (The Couette Configuration).**
Consider two infinite parallel plates separated by a small gap $h$. The bottom plate is fixed; the upper plate is translated in its own plane with velocity $V$. The space between the plates is filled with the food material.

**Step 2 — No-Slip Boundary Condition.**
At both solid boundaries, the fluid velocity matches the velocity of the wall. Thus the velocity field in the gap is linear:

$$v(y) = V \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Gradient Defines Shear Rate.**
The shear rate is the spatial gradient of velocity in the direction perpendicular to flow:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{V}{h} \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Step 4 — Why Is It Called "Shear"?**
An infinitesimal square fluid element is progressively distorted into a parallelogram. The angle of distortion per unit time is the shear rate. The internal tangential force per unit area resisting this distortion is the shear stress $\tau$.

**Step 5 — Constitutive Relationship (Newtonian Limit).**
For a Newtonian food fluid (e.g., water, dilute sugar syrup, vegetable oil at room temperature):

$$\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\eta$ is the **viscosity** (Pa·s) — a measure of internal friction.

**Step 6 — Engineering Utility.**
This linear relationship is the foundation for:
- Viscosity measurement using rotational rheometers (cone-and-plate, parallel plate).
- Pipe flow pressure drop calculations (Hagen–Poiseuille law).
- Power consumption estimation for mixers, pumps, and extruders.
- Sensory texture design (the perceived "thickness" of a sauce is its viscosity under the shear rates of the tongue, $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10$–$100\ \text{s}^{-1}$).

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Sub-Types of Shear Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
