> Although shear flow is *one* deformation mode, it arises in **three geometrically distinct** configurations that the KTU examiner frequently asks about.

1. **Simple Shear (Couette Flow)** — between two parallel surfaces; velocity profile is linear; shear rate is constant.
2. **Poiseuille Flow (Tube Flow)** — pressure-driven flow through a pipe; velocity profile is parabolic; shear rate varies with radial position.
3. **Cone-and-Plate Flow** — rotational flow in a small-angle cone; shear rate is constant throughout the gap (a feature unique to this geometry, exploited in rheometry).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **essential cheat sheet** for shear-flow problems. Memorize the boxed relationships — they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question on this topic.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear stress definition | All |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{dx}{dy}$ | Shear strain (small deformation) | All |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dt} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Shear rate definition | All |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{V}{h}$ | Shear rate in parallel-plate simple shear | Couette geometry |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma}(r) = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{4}} \, r$ | Shear rate in pipe flow (radius $r$) | Poiseuille flow |
| 6 | $\dot{\gamma}_{\text{wall}} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | Wall shear rate in pipe flow | Poiseuille flow |
| 7 | $\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Newtonian fluids |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Definition of viscosity | Newtonian fluids |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Omega}{\tan \alpha}$ | Shear rate in cone-and-plate | Cone-plate rheometer |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop | Laminar pipe flow |

**Note on units:**
- $\eta$ → **Pa·s** = **kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹**
- $1\ \text{cP} = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$ (centipoise, still used in dairy/water contexts)
- Typical water viscosity at 20 °C ≈ **$1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shear Rate in Parallel-Plate Simple Shear

We start from a fundamental kinematic statement: the relative displacement of two adjacent fluid layers divided by their separation, taken in the limit, defines the shear strain.

**Step 1.** Let the bottom plate lie in the plane $y = 0$ and the top plate in the plane $y = h$. The top plate moves in the $x$-direction with constant velocity $V$.

**Step 2.** By the **no-slip boundary condition**, fluid velocity at $y = 0$ is $0$ and at $y = h$ is $V$. For a thin gap and steady state, the velocity profile is assumed linear:

$$v_{x}(y) = a \, y + b$$

**Step 3.** Apply boundary conditions:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{x}(0) &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 0 \\
v_{x}(h) &= V \quad \Rightarrow \quad a \, h = V \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{V}{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Therefore the velocity profile is:

$$v_{x}(y) = V \, \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 5.** Shear rate is the velocity gradient perpendicular to flow:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\!\left(V \, \frac{y}{h}\right) = \frac{V}{h}$$

**Conclusion.** In simple shear between parallel plates, the shear rate is **uniform** throughout the fluid and equals the plate velocity divided by the gap. For instance, if $V = 0.01\ \text{m/s}$ and $h = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, then $\dot{\gamma} = 10\ \text{s}^{-1}$ — the typical shear rate during oral processing of a sauce.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Stress from a Force Balance

Consider a fluid element of area $A$ sandwiched between two parallel plates separated by $dy$. The top plate is pulled with force $F$ in the $+x$ direction.

**Step 1.** The shear stress on the upper face is by definition:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2.** For a Newtonian fluid, the proportionality between stress and shear rate is linear:

$$\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma} = \eta \, \frac{V}{h}$$

**Step 3.** Substituting the numerical values is direct; for example, if $F = 1\ \text{N}$, $A = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $\eta = 1\ \text{Pa·s}$, $h = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{1\ \text{N}}{10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}} = 1000\ \text{Pa} \\
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{\eta^{-1} \tau}{1} = \frac{1000}{1} = 1000\ \text{s}^{-1} \\
V &= \dot{\gamma} \, h = 1000 \times 10^{-3} = 1\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wall Shear Rate in Pressure-Driven Pipe Flow (Poiseuille)

This is a frequently examined derivation; the KTU examiner expects students to know that the shear rate **in a pipe is not uniform** — it is zero at the centreline and maximum at the wall.

**Step 1.** For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ gives:

$$\Delta P \, \pi r^{2} = -\tau(r) \, 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2.** Solve for the shear stress distribution:

$$\tau(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{2L} \, r$$

The negative sign indicates that stress acts opposite to flow direction. The magnitude is:

$$\vert \tau(r) \vert = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} \, r$$

**Step 3.** Maximum shear stress occurs at the wall ($r = R$):

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2L}$$

**Step 4.** For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$, hence the shear rate distribution is:

$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = \frac{\tau(r)}{\eta} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \, r$$

**Step 5.** Wall shear rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2\eta L}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute the Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma}_{w} &= \frac{R}{2\eta L} \cdot \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}} \\
\dot{\gamma}_{w} &= \frac{8 Q}{2 \pi R^{3}} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion.** This is the **Rabinowitsch–Mooney** wall shear rate for a Newtonian fluid in a pipe. It is the bridge between volumetric flow rate and rheological measurement in capillary rheometry, and is essential for characterizing non-Newtonian food materials.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Type of Shear Flow

| Observed Situation | Geometric Model | Velocity Profile | Shear Rate Distribution | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sauce between spoon and bowl wall | Couette / simple shear | Linear | **Constant** $\dfrac{V}{h}$ | "Simple shear" |
| Milk flowing in a pipe | Poiseuille flow | Parabolic | Linear in $r$, $\dfrac{4Qr}{\pi R^{4}}$ | "Tube / pipe shear" |
| Yogurt in a rheometer gap | Cone-and-plate | Linear in $\tan\alpha$ | **Constant** $\dfrac{\Omega}{\tan\alpha}$ | "Constant-shear-rate geometry" |
| Honey dripping off a spoon | Extensional + shear (mixed) | — | — | "Combined deformation" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shear Flow Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Force F] --> B[Shear Stress tau = F over A]
    B --> C{Material Type}
    C -- Newtonian --> D[tau = eta times gamma dot]
    C -- Non-Newtonian --> E[tau equals f of gamma dot]
    D --> F[Viscosity eta = constant]
    E --> G[Apparent Viscosity varies]
    F --> H[Velocity Gradient dv by dy]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Geometric Configuration}
    I -- Parallel Plates --> J[Simple Shear, gamma dot equals V over h]
    I -- Pipe --> K[Poiseuille, gamma dot of r equals 4Q r over pi R4]
    I -- Cone and Plate --> L[gamma dot equals Omega over tan alpha]
    J --> M[Food Processing Applications]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Mixing]
    M --> O[Pumping]
    M --> P[Extrusion]
    M --> Q[Mastication]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Simple Shear (Couette Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Bottom Plate y = 0] -->|No Slip v = 0| B[Fluid Layer 1 v = V by 4]
    B --> C[Fluid Layer 2 v = V by 2]
    C --> D[Fluid Layer 3 v = 3V by 4]
    D --> E[Moving Top Plate y = h v = V]
    F[Tangential Force F] -->|Parallel to surface| A
    F --> E
    G[Original Square Fluid Element] --> H[Distorted Parallelogram]
    H --> I[Shear Strain gamma equals dx by dy]
    I --> J[Shear Rate gamma dot equals V over h]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Poiseuille (Tube) Shear Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pipe of Radius R] --> B[Pressure Drop delta P along Length L]
    B --> C[Parabolic Velocity Profile]
    C --> D[Maximum Velocity at Centerline r = 0]
    C --> E[Zero Velocity at Wall r = R due to No Slip]
    D --> F[Shear Rate gamma dot at r = 0 equals 0]
    E --> G[Shear Rate gamma dot at wall equals maximum]
    G --> H[gamma dot w equals 4Q over pi R cubed]
    F --> I[Linear Distribution gamma dot of r equals 4Q r over pi R4]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Hagen Poiseuille delta P equals 8 eta L Q over pi R4]
    J --> K[Application: Capillary Rheometry for Food Fluids]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Cone-and-Plate Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cone of Angle alpha] --> B[Rotates at Angular Velocity Omega]
    B --> C[Linear Velocity v at radius r equals Omega r]
    C --> D[Local Gap h of r equals r tan alpha]
    D --> E[Shear Rate gamma dot equals v over h equals Omega over tan alpha]
    E --> F[CONSTANT throughout the gap]
    F --> G[Advantage: Uniform Shear Field for Rheometry]
    G --> H[Application: Measuring Viscosity of Honey Chocolate Melt Sauces]
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Distinction — Shear vs Extensional vs Bulk Deformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Types of Deformation] --> B[Shear Flow]
    A --> C[Elongational Flow]
    A --> D[Bulk Deformation]
    B --> B1[Layers slide past each other]
    B --> B2[Shape changes, Volume CONSTANT]
    B --> B3[Example: Stirring honey]
    C --> C1[Material stretches along an axis]
    C --> C2[Shape changes, Volume APPROXIMATELY CONSTANT]
    C --> C3[Example: Stretching molten cheese]
    D --> D1[Uniform compression or expansion]
    D --> D2[Shape CONSTANT, Volume CHANGES]
    D --> D3[Example: Squeezing a sponge saturated with water]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define shear stress and shear rate. State the units in the SI system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Shear stress (τ):** The tangential force per unit area acting parallel to a surface of the material. Mathematically, $\tau = F/A$. **SI unit: Pascal (Pa) = N·m⁻²**. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$):** The time rate of change of shear strain; equivalently, the velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow, $\dot{\gamma} = dv/dy$. **SI unit: reciprocal second (s⁻¹)**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

Differentiate between simple shear flow and Poiseuille flow with respect to velocity profile and shear rate distribution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Shear (Couette) | Poiseuille (Tube) Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Moving boundary | Pressure gradient |
| Velocity profile | Linear in $y$ | Parabolic in $r$ |
| Shear rate distribution | **Constant** ($\dot{\gamma} = V/h$) | **Variable**; zero at centreline, maximum at wall ($\dot{\gamma}_w = 4Q/\pi R^{3}$) |

**(1 mark)** for stating the driving force distinction; **(1 mark)** for velocity profile; **(1 mark)** for shear rate distribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Part B carries 14 marks** and the candidate is offered an **internal choice** between two alternative questions within the same module. Two completely independent choices (A and B) are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the shear rate in a parallel-plate (Couette) flow geometry, starting from the no-slip boundary condition. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A Newtonian food oil with viscosity $\eta = 0.1\ \text{Pa·s}$ fills the gap of a parallel-plate rheometer. The upper plate moves at $V = 0.02\ \text{m/s}$, and the gap between the plates is $h = 1\ \text{mm}$. Calculate the shear rate, shear stress, and the force required on a plate of area $A = 25\ \text{cm}^{2}$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks:**

The lower plate is fixed at $y = 0$; the upper plate is at $y = h$ moving with velocity $V$ in the $+x$ direction. By the **no-slip boundary condition**, fluid velocity matches the wall at both surfaces.

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Boundary condition 1:} \quad v_{x}(0) = 0 \\
&\text{Boundary condition 2:} \quad v_{x}(h) = V
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming a linear velocity profile (valid for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid between closely-spaced plates):

$$v_{x}(y) = a y + b$$

Applying boundary condition 1: $0 = a(0) + b \Rightarrow b = 0$. **(1 mark)**

Applying boundary condition 2: $V = a h \Rightarrow a = V/h$. **(1 mark)**

Therefore:

$$v_{x}(y) = V \frac{y}{h} \quad \text{...(1)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

The shear rate is the velocity gradient in the direction perpendicular to flow:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv_{x}}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\!\left(V \frac{y}{h}\right) = \frac{V}{h} \quad \text{...(2)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

The shear stress for a Newtonian fluid is:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{V}{h} \quad \textbf{(1 mark for interpretation)}$$

**Significance:** The shear rate is independent of position $y$ — i.e., the entire fluid is sheared uniformly. This is the defining feature of simple shear. **(Stated in conclusion)**

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.1\ \text{Pa·s}$; $V = 0.02\ \text{m/s}$; $h = 1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$; $A = 25\ \text{cm}^{2} = 25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Shear rate:** **(2 marks)**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{V}{h} = \frac{0.02\ \text{m/s}}{10^{-3}\ \text{m}} = 20\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress:** **(2 marks)**

$$\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma} = 0.1\ \text{Pa·s} \times 20\ \text{s}^{-1} = 2\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Force on plate:** **(3 marks)**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \tau \cdot A$$

$$F = 2\ \text{Pa} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N} = 5\ \text{mN}$$

**Final Answers:** $\dot{\gamma} = 20\ \text{s}^{-1}$; $\tau = 2\ \text{Pa}$; $F = 5\ \text{mN}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the cone-and-plate rheometer geometry. Show that the shear rate in this geometry is constant throughout the fluid. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A Newtonian fruit juice ($\eta = 0.005\ \text{Pa·s}$) flows through a smooth horizontal pipe of internal diameter $D = 4\ \text{cm}$ at a volumetric flow rate $Q = 2\ \text{L/min}$. Calculate the wall shear rate, wall shear stress, and the pressure drop over a pipe length of $L = 10\ \text{m}$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Geometry:** A flat circular plate of radius $R$ forms the base; a shallow cone of half-angle $\alpha$ (very small, typically $1°$–$4°$) is positioned above it with its apex touching the plate. The cone rotates at angular velocity $\Omega$ about the vertical axis. **(2 marks)**

**Local velocity and gap:** At a radial position $r$, the linear velocity of the cone surface is $v(r) = \Omega r$. The local gap between the cone surface and the plate is $h(r) = r \tan \alpha$. **(2 marks)**

**Shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v(r)}{h(r)} = \frac{\Omega r}{r \tan \alpha} = \frac{\Omega}{\tan \alpha}$$

Since both $\Omega$ and $\alpha$ are constants (independent of $r$), $\dot{\gamma}$ is **constant throughout the fluid volume**. **(2 marks)**

**Significance for food rheology:** This constant-shear-rate property makes cone-and-plate the preferred geometry for measuring viscosity of fluid foods (chocolate melt, honey, sauces) over a wide range of shear rates without artefacts from shear-rate gradients. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.005\ \text{Pa·s}$; $D = 4\ \text{cm} \Rightarrow R = 2\ \text{cm} = 0.02\ \text{m}$; $Q = 2\ \text{L/min} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$; $L = 10\ \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Wall shear rate:** **(2 marks)**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.02)^{3}}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 5.31\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Wall shear stress:** **(2 marks)**

$$\tau_{w} = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}_{w} = 0.005 \times 5.31 = 2.655 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Pa} \approx 0.0266\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop using Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** **(3 marks)**

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}} = \frac{8 \times 0.005 \times 10 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.02)^{4}}$$

Numerator: $8 \times 0.005 \times 10 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5} = 1.333 \times 10^{-5}$

Denominator: $\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7} = 5.027 \times 10^{-7}$

$$\Delta P = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-5}}{5.027 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 26.5\ \text{Pa}$$

**Final Answers:** $\dot{\gamma}_w \approx 5.31\ \text{s}^{-1}$; $\tau_w \approx 0.0266\ \text{Pa}$; $\Delta P \approx 26.5\ \text{Pa}$ over 10 m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the no-slip boundary condition** in derivations (–2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up units** — leaving $h$ in mm without converting to metres (entire numerical answer becomes wrong; –1 to –2 marks).
> 3. **Assuming shear rate is constant in pipe flow** — it is **not**; many students incorrectly write $\dot{\gamma} = V/h$ for Poiseuille flow. The wall shear rate formula is the correct quantity for engineering design.
> 4. **Confusing shear stress and pressure** — $\Delta P$ is the *normal* pressure drop along the pipe; $\tau_w$ is the *tangential* wall stress. These differ by a factor of $L/R$.
> 5. **Stating viscosity without units** — always write **Pa·s**, never just "Pa".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Shear Flow**

- **Shear flow** is deformation by tangential sliding of layers; volume is **conserved**, shape changes.
- The three field variables are **shear stress** $\tau$ [Pa], **shear strain** $\gamma$ [dimensionless/rad], and **shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}$ [s⁻¹].
- **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — applies only to Newtonian fluids.
- **Simple shear (Couette) geometry:** velocity is linear in $y$, $\dot{\gamma} = V/h$ is constant.
- **Poiseuille (pipe) flow:** velocity is parabolic in $r$, $\dot{\gamma}(r) = \dfrac{4Qr}{\pi R^{4}}$, **maximum at the wall**.
- **Wall shear rate in a pipe:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$.
- **Wall shear stress in a pipe:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2L}$.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$.
- **Cone-and-plate geometry:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Omega}{\tan \alpha}$ — the *only* rotational geometry giving a uniform shear rate.
- **No-slip condition** is the bedrock kinematic assumption; always state it before deriving velocity profiles.
- **Units discipline:** $1\ \text{cP} = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$; report answers in SI units unless asked otherwise.
- **Distinguish shear from extension and bulk deformation** — KTU questions often contrast all three.
- **Engineering applications** of shear flow in foods: mixing, pumping, extrusion, mastication, sensory texture, and rheometer measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extensional (Elongational) Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extensional (Elongational) Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Extensional (Elongational) Flow** is a class of non-shear deformation in which fluid elements are stretched along one or more principal axes, with a corresponding compression along the orthogonal axes to satisfy mass conservation (incompressibility). In contrast to simple shear flow where velocity gradients exist perpendicular to the flow direction, in extensional flow the fluid velocity gradient is aligned with the flow direction itself, producing a stream-wise stretching of the material.

Mathematically, the velocity field in the stretching (say, $x_1$) direction takes the form:

$$v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon} \, x_1$$

where $\dot{\varepsilon}$ is the **elongational strain rate** (units: $\text{s}^{-1}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Extensional flow is fundamentally distinct from shear flow. In food processing, many real operations (extrusion, dough sheeting, bubble inflation in bread, stretching of cheese melts, fiber spinning) involve predominantly extensional deformation rather than pure shear. Therefore, characterizing a food material solely by its shear viscosity is often insufficient.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pulling a piece of **toffee or mozzarella cheese** apart with both hands.

- The hands move *away* from each other along the line connecting them.
- The cross-section of the toffee in the middle *thins down* while the length increases.
- A thin filament forms, which eventually necks and breaks.

This is **uniaxial extension** — the material is being stretched in one direction while contracting in the two perpendicular directions.

By contrast, if you take a sheet of pizza dough and stretch it outward in *all directions in the plane* (like inflating a balloon membrane), this is **biaxial extension** — stretching along two principal axes simultaneously with compression along the third (thickness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** In simple shear, fluid layers slide past one another. In extensional flow, fluid layers actively *pull away* from one another along the streamlines. This produces very different molecular/constitutive responses — long-chain polymers, starch networks, gluten strands, and protein filaments align and resist stretching in fundamentally different ways than they resist sliding.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Hencky Strain** ($\varepsilon_H$) — dimensionless logarithmic strain measure.
- **Elongational Strain Rate** ($\dot{\varepsilon}$) — units **$\text{s}^{-1}$**.
- **Extensional (Trouton) Viscosity** ($\eta_E$) — units **Pa·s**.
- **Trouton Ratio** ($T_r$) — dimensionless ratio of extensional to shear viscosity.
- **Cogswell Converging-Flow Analysis** — indirect method for estimating extensional viscosity from capillary/rheometer pressure data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Principal Types of Extensional Flow

Based on the sign of the principal strain rates in a Cartesian frame, three canonical extensional flow types arise:

1. **Uniaxial Extension** (Fiber Spinning / Tensile Stretching)
   - One axis stretched: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = +\dot{\varepsilon}$
   - Two axes compressed: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}$
   - **Food Example:** Stretching a mozzarella curd, pulling bread dough into a baguette shape, tensile testing of a noodle strand.

2. **Biaxial Extension** (Film Blowing / Bubble Inflation)
   - Two axes stretched: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = +\dot{\varepsilon}$
   - One axis compressed: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = -2\dot{\varepsilon}$
   - **Food Example:** Bubbles in bread dough during fermentation/oven rise, bubble formation in molten cheese on a pizza, sheet stretching of puff pastry.

3. **Planar (Pure Shear) Extension** (Converging Channel Flow)
   - Two axes: one stretched, one compressed in the plane
   - $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = +\dot{\varepsilon}$, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = -\dot{\varepsilon}$, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = 0$
   - **Food Example:** Flow of dough through a converging die during extrusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why & How" Step:** These three types are not arbitrary. They are the three independent *deformation modes* in linear kinematics. Any general flow field in the vicinity of a stagnation point can be decomposed into a combination of these modes. Food engineers must know which mode dominates in their process to choose the correct rheological characterization.

### 2.2 The Hencky Strain — A Logarithmic Measure

Because extensional deformations involve very large strains (a noodle can be stretched to 10× its original length), the linear engineering strain $\varepsilon = (L - L_0)/L_0$ becomes ill-defined (it depends on the starting length and is not additive). The solution is the **Hencky strain**:

$$\varepsilon_H = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

For multi-step deformations, Hencky strains simply add — a critical property.

### 2.3 Extensional Viscosity Definitions

- **Uniaxial Extensional Viscosity:** $\eta_E = \dfrac{\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$
- **Biaxial Extensional Viscosity:** $\eta_B = \dfrac{\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{33}}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$
- **Planar Extensional Viscosity:** $\eta_P = \dfrac{\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$

where the stress difference $(\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22})$ is the **tensile stress difference** driving the deformation.

### 2.4 Trouton Ratio — The Universal Benchmark

The **Trouton Ratio** compares extensional to shear viscosity and is the most-tested quantity in KTU examinations:

$$T_r = \frac{\eta_E}{\eta_s}$$

For a **Newtonian fluid**, the Trouton ratios take classical values:

| Flow Type | Trouton Ratio for Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|
| Uniaxial | $T_r = 3$ |
| Planar (pure shear) | $T_r = 4$ |
| Biaxial (equal biaxial) | $T_r = 6$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A *Newtonian* fluid exhibits Trouton ratio of exactly **3** in uniaxial extension. Any deviation from 3 indicates **non-Newtonian / viscoelastic / strain-hardening behavior** — extremely common in foods such as wheat dough, molten chocolate, melted cheese, and polymer-thickened sauces.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_H = \ln(L/L_0)$ | Hencky (logarithmic) strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = d\varepsilon_H/dt$ | Elongational strain rate | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon}\, x_1$ | Velocity field in uniaxial extension | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 4 | $\eta_E = (\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22})/\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Uniaxial extensional viscosity | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $\eta_B = (\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{33})/\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Biaxial extensional viscosity | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $T_r = \eta_E / \eta_s$ | Trouton ratio (uniaxial) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_r = 3$ (Newtonian, uniaxial) | Newtonian benchmark | — |
| 8 | $T_r = 6$ (Newtonian, biaxial) | Newtonian benchmark | — |
| 9 | $\sigma_E = 3 \eta_s \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Newtonian uniaxial stress | Pa |
| 10 | $\eta_P = \eta_E^{\text{uniaxial}} \cdot (2/3)$ | Relation: planar to uniaxial (Newtonian) | Pa·s |
| 11 | $\eta_E^{\text{app}} = \dfrac{9}{32} \dfrac{(n+1)^2}{\eta}\, \dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{app}}^{\,n-1}$ | Cogswell apparent extensional viscosity (power-law) | Pa·s |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Extrusion Cooking:** Snacks, pasta, and breakfast cereals are formed by pushing dough through a die. The dough undergoes strong **converging (planar extensional)** flow at the die entry.
- **Bread Baking:** During oven rise, $\text{CO}_2$ bubbles expand — the surrounding dough film experiences **biaxial extension**; failure leads to collapse.
- **Cheese Melting:** Stretched mozzarella for pizza experiences uniaxial elongation; **strain hardening** prevents droplets from breaking.
- **Dough Handling:** Sheeting and rolling operations involve predominantly **planar extensional** deformation of the gluten network.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hencky Strain as the Additive Logarithmic Strain

Consider a small incremental deformation. Let the instantaneous gauge length be $L(t)$. Define the *true (logarithmic) strain increment*:

$$d\varepsilon_H = \frac{dL}{L}$$

Integrating from initial length $L_0$ to current length $L$:

$$\varepsilon_H = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 1.** Apply the fundamental theorem of calculus:

$$\varepsilon_H = \Big[\ln L\Big]_{L_0}^{L}$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate at the bounds:

$$\varepsilon_H = \ln L - \ln L_0$$

**Step 3.** Use the logarithm subtraction identity:

$$\varepsilon_H = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Demonstrate additivity: For two successive stretches $L_0 \to L_1 \to L_2$:

$$\varepsilon_H^{\text{total}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_2}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right)$$

Hence $\varepsilon_H^{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_H^{(2)} + \varepsilon_H^{(1)}$. Additivity confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> The same mathematical form — integral of $dL/L$ — appears in thermodynamics (entropy change from reversible heat transfer) and in chemical kinetics (first-order integrated rate law). The Hencky strain is the natural strain measure for any process governed by an exponential time-evolution.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity Field for Uniaxial Extension

We require a homogeneous, incompressible flow field that stretches along $x_1$ and contracts symmetrically in $x_2$ and $x_3$.

**Step 1.** Assume the velocity in each direction is linear in the corresponding position:

$$v_1 = a\,x_1, \qquad v_2 = b\,x_2, \qquad v_3 = c\,x_3$$

where $a$, $b$, $c$ are constants (homogeneous flow).

**Step 2.** Apply incompressibility $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial v_1}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial v_2}{\partial x_2} + \frac{\partial v_3}{\partial x_3} = a + b + c = 0$$

**Step 3.** Apply symmetry in $x_2$ and $x_3$ (no preferred transverse direction):

$$b = c$$

**Step 4.** Combine Steps 2 and 3:

$$a + 2b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -\frac{a}{2}$$

**Step 5.** Define the elongational strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon} \equiv a$. Then:

$$v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon}\,x_1, \qquad v_2 = -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}\,x_2, \qquad v_3 = -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}\,x_3$$

The velocity gradient tensor is therefore:

$$\nabla \vec{v} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\varepsilon} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon} \end{bmatrix}$$

which is **traceless** (confirms incompressibility) and **diagonal** (purely extensional kinematics — no off-diagonal shear components).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Trouton Ratio of 3 for a Newtonian Fluid in Uniaxial Extension

We compare the dissipation of mechanical energy per unit volume in two flows:

$$\Phi = \tau : \nabla \vec{v}$$

**Step 1.** For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = \eta_s (\nabla \vec{v} + \nabla \vec{v}^T)$. The dissipation simplifies to:

$$\Phi = \eta_s \, \text{tr}\!\big[(\nabla \vec{v})^2\big]$$

**Step 2.** In simple shear, $\nabla \vec{v}$ has a single off-diagonal entry $\dot{\gamma}$:

$$\Phi_{\text{shear}} = \eta_s \dot{\gamma}^2$$

**Step 3.** In uniaxial extension, the diagonal entries are $\dot{\varepsilon}$, $-\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}$, $-\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}$. Their squares sum:

$$\Phi_{\text{ext}} = \eta_s\!\left[\dot{\varepsilon}^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\dot{\varepsilon}^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\dot{\varepsilon}^2\right] = \eta_s \cdot \tfrac{3}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}^2$$

**Step 4.** By definition, extensional viscosity satisfies $\Phi_{\text{ext}} = \eta_E \dot{\varepsilon}^2$ (one independent principal rate). Equate:

$$\eta_E \dot{\varepsilon}^2 = \tfrac{3}{2}\,\eta_s \dot{\varepsilon}^2$$

**Step 5.** Cancel $\dot{\varepsilon}^2$:

$$\eta_E = \tfrac{3}{2}\,\eta_s$$

Wait — this gives $\eta_E/\eta_s = 3/2$, not 3. The factor of 2 arises because the scalar dissipation uses the *symmetric* part. Using the conventional stress-difference definition $\eta_E = (\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22})/\dot{\varepsilon}$ and the Newtonian constitutive equation $\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22} = 2\eta_s(\dot{\varepsilon}_{11}-\dot{\varepsilon}_{22}) = 2\eta_s(\dot{\varepsilon}+\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}) = 3\eta_s\dot{\varepsilon}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_r = \frac{\eta_E}{\eta_s} = 3\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note:** The classical result $T_r = 3$ comes from the **stress-difference** convention $\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22}$, not from energy-dissipation directly. KTU exam answers should explicitly reference the constitutive relation.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Trouton Ratio of 6 for Biaxial Extension

In **equal biaxial extension**, $\dot{\varepsilon}_{11} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{22} = \dot{\varepsilon}$ and $\dot{\varepsilon}_{33} = -2\dot{\varepsilon}$.

The stress difference driving biaxial flow:

$$\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{33} = 2\eta_s(\dot{\varepsilon}_{11}-\dot{\varepsilon}_{33}) = 2\eta_s(\dot{\varepsilon} - (-2\dot{\varepsilon})) = 6\eta_s \dot{\varepsilon}$$

Hence:

$$\eta_B = 6\eta_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_r^{\text{biaxial}} = 6$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Cogswell's Converging-Flow Analysis (Outline)

When a fluid enters a die from a larger reservoir, the dominant extensional strain rate at the die-entry can be estimated from the pressure drop. For a power-law fluid with consistency $K$ and index $n$:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{app}} = \frac{3n+1}{4n}\cdot\frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$

$$\eta_E^{\text{app}} = \frac{9}{32}\cdot\frac{(n+1)^2}{\eta_{\text{app}}}\cdot\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{app}}^{\,n-1}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate, $R$ the die radius, and $\eta_{\text{app}}$ the apparent shear viscosity.

> [!NOTE]
> Cogswell's method provides an *indirect* estimate of extensional viscosity from standard capillary rheometer data — extremely valuable when dedicated extensional rheometers (e.g., filament-stretching rheometer) are unavailable, which is common in food industry labs.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Strain Hardening & Strain Rate Sensitivity

| Behavior | Signature in $\eta_E$ vs $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Food Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\eta_E = 3\eta_s$, constant | Dilute sucrose solution, water |
| **Strain-thinning in extension** | $\eta_E$ decreases with $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Polymer-thickened sauces |
| **Strain-hardening in extension** | $\eta_E$ increases with $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (or with $\varepsilon_H$) | Wheat dough, mozzarella melt, xanthan gum |
| **Strong strain-hardening** | "Extension-thickening" peak before rupture | Gluten network, egg-white foam films |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Classification of Extensional Flow Modes

The following Mermaid flowchart classifies the three principal extensional flow modes and the food-processing operations in which each dominates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Extensional Flow"] --> B["Uniaxial Extension"]
    A --> C["Biaxial Extension"]
    A --> D["Planar Pure Shear Extension"]

    B --> B1["Principal Strain Rate +e dot"]
    B --> B2["Two Transverse Rates -1/2 e dot"]
    B --> B3["Trouton Ratio Tr equals 3 for Newtonian"]
    B --> B4["Food Example: Pulling mozzarella curd"]

    C --> C1["Two In-Plane Rates +e dot"]
    C --> C2["Out-of-Plane Rate -2 e dot"]
    C --> C3["Trouton Ratio Tr equals 6 for Newtonian"]
    C --> C4["Food Example: Gas bubble in bread dough"]

    D --> D1["One Rate +e dot, One -e dot"]
    D --> D2["Out-of-Plane Rate 0"]
    D --> D3["Trouton Ratio Tr equals 4 for Newtonian"]
    D --> D4["Food Example: Converging die entry in extrusion"]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Architecture of an Extensional Rheometer (Filament-Stretching Type)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture of a filament-stretching extensional rheometer, the gold-standard instrument for measuring $\eta_E$ directly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Sample Filament"] --> MID["Midpoint Diameter Camera"]
    MID --> F1["Real-Time Diameter D t"]
    F1 --> CTRL["Feedback Controller"]
    CTRL --> ACT["Servomotor Actuator"]
    ACT --> TOP["Top Moving Plate"]
    ACT --> BOT["Bottom Fixed Plate"]
    S --- TOP
    S --- BOT
    F2["Force Transducer F t"] --> CTRL
    F3["Temperature Chamber"] --> S
    F4["Hencky Strain Calculator"] --> F1
    F5["Extensional Viscosity Output eta E"] --> F4
    F2 --> F5
```

**Interpretation of the block flow:**
1. The **sample filament** (a small disc of food material) is placed between top and bottom plates.
2. As the **top plate moves upward**, the filament thins.
3. The **midpoint camera** records the diameter $D(t)$ in real time.
4. The **force transducer** records tensile force $F(t)$.
5. The **feedback controller** adjusts plate velocity so that the mid-filament strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ remains constant (true extensional test).
6. The **Hencky strain calculator** converts $D(t)$ to $\varepsilon_H = 2\ln(D_0/D)$.
7. The **extensional viscosity output** computes $\eta_E = [F(t)/\pi(D/2)^2]/\dot{\varepsilon}$.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Trouton Ratio Comparison Across Flow Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N["Newtonian Fluid"] --> N1["Shear Viscosity eta s"]
    N1 --> T1["Uniaxial Tr equals 3"]
    N1 --> T2["Planar Tr equals 4"]
    N1 --> T3["Biaxial Tr equals 6"]
    N1 --> T4["Trouton Observed Tr greater than 3 indicates strain hardening"]
    N1 --> T5["Trouton Observed Tr less than 3 indicates shear dominated extension thinning"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]`

Define **extensional viscosity** and the **Trouton ratio**. State the Trouton ratio of a Newtonian fluid in uniaxial, planar, and biaxial extension.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition of Extensional Viscosity (1 mark):** Extensional viscosity $\eta_E$ is defined as the ratio of the tensile stress difference $(\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22})$ to the elongational strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$:

$$\eta_E = \frac{\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22}}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$$

It has units of Pa·s and characterizes the resistance of a material to stretching deformation.

- **Definition of Trouton Ratio (1 mark):** The Trouton ratio is the dimensionless ratio of the extensional viscosity to the shear viscosity:

$$T_r = \frac{\eta_E}{\eta_s}$$

- **Newtonian Trouton Ratios (1 mark):**
  - Uniaxial extension: $T_r = 3$
  - Planar (pure shear) extension: $T_r = 4$
  - Biaxial extension: $T_r = 6$

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Module 2]`

Differentiate between **simple shear flow** and **extensional flow** with two key distinguishing features.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Shear Flow | Extensional Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity gradient orientation | Perpendicular to flow direction | Aligned with flow direction |
| Deformation mechanism | Layers slide past each other | Fluid element stretches along streamlines |
| Steady-state viscosity character | Shear viscosity $\eta_s$ | Extensional viscosity $\eta_E$ |
| Streamlines | Parallel, stationary | Convergent or divergent |
| Example in food | Mixing in a stirred tank | Dough being pulled into a noodle strand |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "extensional flow has higher viscosity than shear flow" — this is **not generally true** for Newtonian fluids. The Trouton ratio of 3 only applies specifically to *uniaxial extension*. Avoid making such absolute statements.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of neat velocity-gradient diagrams, describe the three principal types of extensional flow. Give one food-processing example for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Trouton ratio of 3** for a Newtonian fluid undergoing uniaxial elongational flow. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Uniaxial Extension (2 marks):**
A fluid element is stretched along a single axis ($x_1$) with symmetric compression along the two transverse axes ($x_2$, $x_3$). Velocity field:

$$v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon} x_1, \quad v_2 = -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon} x_2, \quad v_3 = -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon} x_3$$

Diagonal velocity-gradient tensor with entries $+\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}$.

**Food Example:** Stretching of mozzarella cheese curd during pasta-filata processing, or pulling wheat dough into a baguette.

**2. Biaxial Extension (2 marks):**
Two in-plane axes ($x_1, x_2$) are stretched equally while the third axis ($x_3$, thickness) compresses at twice the rate. Velocity field:

$$v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon} x_1, \quad v_2 = \dot{\varepsilon} x_2, \quad v_3 = -2\dot{\varepsilon} x_3$$

**Food Example:** Inflation of a $\text{CO}_2$ gas bubble within bread dough during oven spring; bubble formation in melted cheese on a pizza surface.

**3. Planar (Pure Shear) Extension (2 marks):**
One in-plane axis stretched, the other in-plane axis compressed at the same rate, and the out-of-plane axis undeformed. Velocity field:

$$v_1 = \dot{\varepsilon} x_1, \quad v_2 = -\dot{\varepsilon} x_2, \quad v_3 = 0$$

**Food Example:** Flow of dough through a converging section of an extruder die entry region.

**Closing remark (1 mark):** All three types satisfy $\text{tr}(\nabla \vec{v}) = 0$, confirming incompressibility, and have zero off-diagonal entries (no rotational/shear components).

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Newtonian constitutive equation:

$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p\,\mathbf{I} + 2\eta_s \mathbf{D}$$

where $\mathbf{D} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \vec{v} + \nabla \vec{v}^T)$ is the rate-of-deformation tensor.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** In uniaxial extension, $\mathbf{D}$ is diagonal:

$$\mathbf{D} = \text{diag}\!\left(\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the normal-stress differences:

$$\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22} = 2\eta_s\!\left(\dot{\varepsilon} - \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}\right)\right) = 2\eta_s \cdot \tfrac{3}{2}\dot{\varepsilon} = 3\eta_s \dot{\varepsilon}$$

$$\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{33} = 3\eta_s \dot{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(identical by symmetry)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Define extensional viscosity:

$$\eta_E = \frac{\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}}{\dot{\varepsilon}} = \frac{3\eta_s \dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}} = 3\eta_s$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Form the Trouton ratio:

$$T_r = \frac{\eta_E}{\eta_s} = \frac{3\eta_s}{\eta_s} = 3$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Assumptions:
- Incompressible Newtonian fluid
- Homogeneous, steady uniaxial extensional flow
- No body forces
- Isothermal conditions
- No-slip boundary condition not required (kinematic definition only)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Define **Hencky strain**. Show that it is additive for successive deformations. A noodle strand is stretched from $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$ to $L_1 = 80\,\text{mm}$ and then further to $L_2 = 120\,\text{mm}$. Compute the total Hencky strain. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **strain hardening** in extensional flow? Explain its importance in (i) bread dough during oven rise, and (ii) mozzarella cheese stretching. A wheat dough exhibits Trouton ratio of $T_r = 12$ at strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0.1\,\text{s}^{-1}$. If the shear viscosity is $\eta_s = 500\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, determine the extensional viscosity and the tensile stress difference. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define Hencky strain:
The Hencky (logarithmic, true) strain is the integral of incremental strain $dL/L$ from initial to current length:

$$\varepsilon_H = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

It is dimensionless and additive for sequential deformations.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Prove additivity:

For $L_0 \to L_1 \to L_2$:

$$\varepsilon_H^{(1)} = \ln(L_1/L_0), \qquad \varepsilon_H^{(2)} = \ln(L_2/L_1)$$

$$\varepsilon_H^{(1)} + \varepsilon_H^{(2)} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_2}{L_0}\right) = \varepsilon_H^{\text{total}}$$

Hence proven.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Numerical computation:

$$\varepsilon_H^{(1)} = \ln\!\left(\frac{80}{50}\right) = \ln(1.6) = 0.4700$$

$$\varepsilon_H^{(2)} = \ln\!\left(\frac{120}{80}\right) = \ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$

$$\varepsilon_H^{\text{total}} = 0.4700 + 0.4055 = 0.8755 \quad (\approx 0.876)$$

Cross-check: $\varepsilon_H^{\text{total}} = \ln(120/50) = \ln(2.4) = 0.8755$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The Hencky strain $0.876$ corresponds to a true stretch ratio of $e^{0.876} \approx 2.4$, i.e., a 140% increase in length — consistent with the noodle-strand deformation.

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define strain hardening:
Strain hardening (extension thickening) is the phenomenon in which the extensional viscosity $\eta_E$ *increases* with accumulated Hencky strain $\varepsilon_H$ (or with strain rate in some materials). The Trouton ratio therefore exceeds the Newtonian benchmark of 3 (or 4/6 for other modes).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Importance in food processing:
- **(i) Bread dough during oven rise:** Strain hardening of the gluten-starch film surrounding each $\text{CO}_2$ bubble delays rupture. Without strain hardening, gas cells would coalesce and the loaf would collapse; with it, the bubble film stabilizes and produces an open, aerated crumb.
- **(ii) Mozzarella stretching:** Strain hardening in molten casein networks allows the curd to be pulled into long, thin strings without premature breakage, enabling the characteristic fibrous "pasta filata" texture of pizza cheese.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute extensional viscosity:

$$\eta_E = T_r \cdot \eta_s = 12 \times 500 = 6000\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute tensile stress difference:

$$\sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22} = \eta_E \cdot \dot{\varepsilon} = 6000 \times 0.1 = 600\,\text{Pa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall on Trouton Ratio Problems:**
> - Always check whether $T_r = 3$ (Newtonian uniaxial), 4 (planar), or 6 (biaxial) is the correct benchmark.
> - Do not confuse $\eta_E$ with $\eta_s$ when substituting into Trouton ratio — they have the *same units* but represent *different physical phenomena*.
> - Students frequently forget to include units in the final answer; **KTU valuation key deducts 0.5 marks** for missing units on numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Extensional flow = stretching along streamlines** (not sliding layers like shear).
- [x] **Three canonical modes:** Uniaxial, Biaxial, Planar — distinguishable by signs of principal strain rates.
- [x] **Hencky strain** $\varepsilon_H = \ln(L/L_0)$ is the correct (additive) large-deformation strain measure.
- [x] **Extensional strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$.
- [x] **Extensional viscosity** $\eta_E = (\sigma_{11}-\sigma_{22})/\dot{\varepsilon}$ in uniaxial case.
- [x] **Trouton Ratio $T_r = \eta_E/\eta_s$ — Newtonian benchmarks: 3 (uniaxial), 4 (planar), 6 (biaxial).**
- [x] **Velocity-gradient tensor in uniaxial extension** is diagonal with entries $+\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}, -\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\varepsilon}$ (traceless for incompressibility).
- [x] **Strain hardening** → Trouton ratio $> 3$ in uniaxial; critical for bread dough bubble stability and mozzarella stretchability.
- [x] **Strain thinning in extension** → Trouton ratio $< 3$ in uniaxial; observed in some weak gels and emulsions.
- [x] **Cogswell method** allows indirect estimation of $\eta_E$ from capillary rheometer pressure-drop data — useful in industrial food labs.
- [x] **Food applications:** Extrusion (planar), dough sheeting (planar), bread bubble growth (biaxial), cheese stretching (uniaxial), noodle tensile testing (uniaxial), filament stretching rheometer (direct measurement).
- [x] **Examiner red flags to avoid:** Confusing shear and extensional viscosity, omitting units, forgetting to justify incompressibility, citing $T_r = 3$ for a non-Newtonian fluid without specifying the actual value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volumetric Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Volumetric Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In food rheology, **Volumetric Flow** refers to the measurement of the volume of a fluid (food material) that passes through a defined cross-sectional area per unit time under an applied pressure gradient. It is fundamentally described by the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar, steady, isothermal, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid through a straight circular tube of constant cross-section.

Mathematically, the volumetric flow rate is expressed as:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s), $R$ is the tube radius (m), $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across length $L$ (Pa), $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and $L$ is the tube length (m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Volumetric flow characterization is the backbone of capillary rheometry — the primary technique used in the food industry to characterize high-viscosity fluids such as chocolate, molten sugar, starch pastes, and dairy concentrates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Volumetric Flow Rate (Q):** The scalar quantity representing the volume of fluid transported per unit time across a section. It is distinct from *mass flow rate* ($\dot{m} = \rho Q$) and *momentum flow rate*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cylindrical water pipe** connected to an overhead tank. Open the tap, and water rushes out. If you were to measure the **volume of water collected in a bucket in 1 second**, that value (in litres/second) is the volumetric flow rate.

Now, replace water with a **thick food slurry** (say, tomato ketchup). If you apply the same pressure, less volume will flow out because ketchup is more viscous. If you **double the pipe radius**, the flow rate increases by a factor of **$2^4 = 16$** — this dramatic fourth-power relationship is what makes pipe diameter the single most powerful design parameter in food processing lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Food Engineering Analogy:** Consider the **flow of molten chocolate** through the die of an extruder. The confectionery engineer tunes the screw speed (which controls $\Delta P$) and die diameter (which controls $R$) to achieve the desired volumetric throughput in tonnes/hour — this is directly a Hagen-Poiseuille design problem.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | Typical Food Industry Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | **Pa·s** | $10^{-3}$ (milk) to $10^{3}$ (dough) |
| Tube radius | $R$ | **m** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Pressure gradient | $\Delta P/L$ | **Pa/m** | $10^{2}$ to $10^{6}$ |
| Shear rate (wall) | $\dot{\gamma}_w$ | **s⁻¹** | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{4}$ |
| Reynolds number | $Re$ | dimensionless | $< 2100$ (laminar) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Governing Assumptions

For the classical Hagen–Poiseuille derivation, the following assumptions are made:

- **Steady flow** — velocity at any point does not change with time.
- **Laminar (streamline) flow** — fluid moves in parallel layers without turbulence; $Re < 2100$.
- **Incompressible fluid** — density $\rho$ is constant.
- **Newtonian behaviour** — shear stress linearly proportional to shear rate.
- **Fully developed flow** — velocity profile is invariant along the axial direction.
- **No-slip at wall** — fluid velocity equals zero at the tube wall.
- **Isothermal conditions** — viscosity does not vary with temperature.
- **Rigid, smooth, straight tube** of circular cross-section.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

A force balance is performed on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:

- **Driving force:** Pressure force acting on the cross-sectional area $\pi r^2$ → $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$.
- **Resisting force:** Shear stress acting on the lateral cylindrical surface of area $2\pi r L$ → $\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$.
- At equilibrium (steady state), the driving force equals the resisting force.

This yields a linear shear-stress distribution across the radius, which is then integrated using the Newtonian constitutive equation $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$ to obtain the **parabolic velocity profile**:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2})$$

The maximum velocity occurs at the centreline ($r = 0$) and the velocity is zero at the wall ($r = R$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** — primary tool for capillary viscometry in food labs |
| 2 | $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2})$ | Parabolic velocity profile of a Newtonian fluid in a tube |
| 3 | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | Centreline (maximum) velocity |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | **Apparent wall shear rate** for Newtonian fluids |
| 5 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$ | **Wall shear stress** — independent of fluid nature |
| 6 | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_{w}}{\dot{\gamma}_{w}} = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q}$ | **Apparent viscosity** from capillary measurements |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = \dfrac{3 n + 1}{4 n} \dot{\gamma}_{w,app}$ | **Rabinowitsch correction** for power-law fluids (e.g., most foods) |
| 8 | $\tau_{w,true} = \dfrac{(R + \Delta R)\Delta P}{2 L}$ | **Bagley correction** — accounts for entrance pressure losses |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \dfrac{2 \rho Q}{\pi R \mu}$ | **Reynolds number** for tube flow (laminar if $Re < 2100$) |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | **Fanning friction factor** for laminar Newtonian pipe flow |
| 11 | $K = \dfrac{3 n + 1}{4 n}$ | Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction factor |
| 12 | $\eta_{a} = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q}$ | Alternative form of apparent viscosity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** The Rabinowitsch equation is the **single most-tested formula** in Module 2. The factor $\frac{3n+1}{4n}$ is critical for converting the apparent shear rate (computed from $4Q/\pi R^3$) to the true shear rate for **power-law** food fluids such as tomato sauce, fruit purees, and melted cheese.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Food Industry

- **Capillary rheometer design:** Predicts the pressure required to pump viscous food products through narrow orifices.
- **Pipe sizing in food processing plants:** Determines the optimal pipe diameter for dairy, brewing, and beverage lines to minimize pumping cost while maintaining sanitary flow.
- **Quality control:** Comparing measured vs. theoretical $Q$ detects fouling, biofilm buildup, or partial blockages in CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems.
- **Extruder die design:** Used to compute throughput of pasta, snack, and cereal extruders.
- **Laminar flow cabinet characterization:** Validates sterile filling operations in pharmaceutical/food packaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Most foods (ketchup, yogurt, mayonnaise, melted chocolate) are **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** and follow the **Ostwald–de Waele power-law model** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n < 1$. For these, the basic Hagen–Poiseuille equation must be corrected using the **Rabinowitsch–Mooney** analysis to extract true rheological parameters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Newtonian Fluid)

### Step 1: Setup and Free-Body Diagram
Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$, coaxial with a straight horizontal tube of radius $R$.

### Step 2: Force Balance on the Cylindrical Element
The pressure force acts on the two end faces of the cylinder (area $\pi r^{2}$ each):

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2}$$

The shear force resists the motion on the lateral cylindrical surface (area $2\pi r L$):

$$F_{shear} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium Condition
At steady state, the net force on the fluid element is zero:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 4: Solve for Shear Stress Distribution
Rearranging the equilibrium equation:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

This shows that **shear stress is a linear function of radial position** — maximum at the wall ($r = R$) and zero at the centreline ($r = 0$).

### Step 5: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity
For a Newtonian fluid:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

The negative sign indicates velocity decreases with increasing radius.

### Step 6: Equate and Integrate
Substituting:

$$-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r$$

### Step 7: First Integration — Velocity Gradient
Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot r^{2} + C_{1}$$

### Step 8: Apply the No-Slip Boundary Condition
At $r = R$, $u = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot R^{2} + C_{1}$$

$$C_{1} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$$

### Step 9: Velocity Profile Equation
Substituting $C_{1}$ back:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

### Step 10: Second Integration — Volumetric Flow Rate
The volumetric flow rate is the integral of velocity over the cross-sectional area:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substituting the velocity profile:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr$$

### Step 11: Evaluate the Integral
$$\int_{0}^{R} R^{2} r \, dr = \frac{R^{4}}{2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr = \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

### Step 12: Final Hagen–Poiseuille Equation
$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apparent Wall Shear Rate

The average velocity across the cross-section is:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

The wall shear rate (velocity gradient at $r = R$) is obtained by differentiating $u(r)$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = -\left.\frac{du}{dr}\right|_{r=R} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \mu L}$$

Now, expressing $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ in terms of $Q$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \mu L} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This expression is the **Newtonian wall shear rate**. It is *apparent* for non-Newtonian fluids because the velocity profile is no longer exactly parabolic (it becomes blunter for shear-thinning fluids).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rabinowitsch–Mooney Equation (Power-Law Fluid)

### Step 1: Power-Law Constitutive Equation
For a food fluid following the Ostwald–de Waele model:

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

where $K$ is the consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ is the flow behaviour index (dimensionless).

### Step 2: Shear Stress Distribution (Same as Newtonian)
The force balance still holds because it depends only on geometry:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

### Step 3: Wall Shear Stress
At $r = R$:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}$$

### Step 4: Invert the Constitutive Equation
From $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$:

$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = \left(\frac{\tau(r)}{K}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 5: Local Velocity Gradient
$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = -\frac{du}{dr} = \left(\frac{\tau(r)}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 6: Integrate to Find Velocity Profile
$$du = -\left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} r^{1/n} dr$$

$$u(r) = -\left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} r^{(n+1)/n} + C_{2}$$

### Step 7: Apply No-Slip Boundary Condition
At $r = R$, $u = 0$:

$$C_{2} = \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} R^{(n+1)/n}$$

### Step 8: Final Velocity Profile
$$u(r) = \frac{n}{n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$

### Step 9: Integrate for Volumetric Flow Rate
$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi n}{n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{(n+1)/n} r - r^{(2n+1)/n}\right) dr$$

### Step 10: Evaluate the Integral
$$\int_{0}^{R} R^{(n+1)/n} r \, dr = R^{(n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{R^{2}}{2} = \frac{R^{(3n+1)/n}}{2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{R} r^{(2n+1)/n} dr = \frac{R^{(3n+1)/n}}{(3n+1)/n} = \frac{n R^{(3n+1)/n}}{3n+1}$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{(n+1)/n} r - r^{(2n+1)/n}\right) dr = \frac{R^{(3n+1)/n}}{2} - \frac{n R^{(3n+1)/n}}{3n+1}$$

Factoring out $R^{(3n+1)/n}$:

$$= R^{(3n+1)/n} \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{n}{3n+1}\right) = R^{(3n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{3n+1 - 2n}{2(3n+1)} = R^{(3n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{n+1}{2(3n+1)}$$

### Step 11: Substitute Back into Q
$$Q = \frac{\pi n}{n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot R^{(3n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{n+1}{2(3n+1)}$$

The $(n+1)$ terms cancel:

$$Q = \frac{\pi n}{2(3n+1)} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} R^{(3n+1)/n}$$

### Step 12: Express Apparent Wall Shear Rate
Rearranging and noting that $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = 4Q/(\pi R^{3})$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{3}}{4} \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{app}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\pi R^{3}}{4} \dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{\pi n}{2(3n+1)} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} R^{(3n+1)/n}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{2n}{3n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} R^{(1-n)/n}$$

### Step 13: Wall Shear Stress in Terms of $\dot{\gamma}_{app}$
Since $\tau_{w} = \Delta P R/(2L)$ and using the power-law relation $\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = (\tau_{w}/K)^{1/n}$:

$$\frac{\dot{\gamma}_{app}}{\dot{\gamma}_{w,true}} = \frac{3n+1}{4n}$$

### Step 14: Final Rabinowitsch–Mooney Equation
$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = \frac{3n+1}{4n} \dot{\gamma}_{w,app} = \frac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The Rabinowitsch correction factor $\frac{3n+1}{4n}$ is **> 1 for shear-thinning fluids** ($n < 1$). This means the *true* shear rate at the wall is *greater* than the apparent Newtonian value. For $n = 1$ (Newtonian), the factor equals 1, recovering the Newtonian result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mean Velocity and Average Shear Rate Relationship

For a power-law fluid, the relationship between mean velocity and wall shear rate is:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{n}{3n+1} \cdot u_{max}$$

where $u_{max} = \dfrac{n}{n+1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P R}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n} R$ is the centreline velocity.

This is critical for designing food processing pumps where the volumetric throughput is fixed by production requirements.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A tomato concentrate (power-law fluid, $K = 12$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.45$) flows through a capillary tube of radius $R = 2$ mm and length $L = 0.5$ m under a pressure drop $\Delta P = 50$ kPa. Calculate the volumetric flow rate.

**Step 1:** Compute the wall shear stress:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3})(50 \times 10^{3})}{2 \times 0.5} = 100 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the true wall shear rate using the power-law model:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \left(\frac{\tau_{w}}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{100}{12}\right)^{1/0.45}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = (8.333)^{2.222} \approx 128.4 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi n R^{3}}{3n+1} \dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{\pi (0.45)(2 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{3(0.45)+1} \times 128.4$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi (0.45)(8 \times 10^{-9})}{2.35} \times 128.4$$

$$Q \approx \frac{1.131 \times 10^{-8}}{2.35} \times 128.4 \approx 6.18 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 6.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L/s} = 2.22 \text{ L/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This low throughput (≈ 2 L/h) confirms that viscous tomato concentrate requires **larger diameter piping** or **higher pumping pressures** in industrial scale-up. Doubling the radius would increase $Q$ by a factor of $2^{(3n+1)/n} = 2^{4.67} \approx 25.4$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define volumetric flow rate. State the Hagen–Poiseuille equation and identify each term with its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is the volume of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time, measured in **m³/s** (SI). The Hagen–Poiseuille equation describes steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a straight circular tube:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

where $R$ = tube radius (m), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and $L$ = tube length (m). This equation forms the basis of capillary viscometry used in food rheology.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**What is the Rabinowitsch correction? Why is it essential in food rheology?**

**Model Answer:**
The Rabinowitsch correction converts the apparent Newtonian wall shear rate ($4Q/\pi R^{3}$) into the **true wall shear rate** for non-Newtonian power-law fluids:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = \frac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

It is essential in food rheology because most foods (ketchup, yogurt, fruit purees, melted chocolate) are **non-Newtonian** with $n \neq 1$. Without this correction, the rheological parameters $K$ and $n$ obtained from capillary measurements would be erroneous, leading to incorrect pump and pipe sizing in food processing plants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**A tomato concentrate is being pumped through a stainless-steel capillary tube of radius $R = 1.5$ mm and length $L = 0.4$ m. The pressure drop is $\Delta P = 80$ kPa. The fluid follows the power-law model with $K = 15$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.40$.**

**(a)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for a Newtonian fluid, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction, calculate the volumetric flow rate and the apparent viscosity for the tomato concentrate. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, laminar, incompressible flow.
2. Newtonian behaviour, isothermal conditions.
3. Rigid, smooth, straight tube of uniform circular cross-section.
4. No-slip at the wall; fully developed flow.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$. Force balance:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Step 2:** Solve for shear stress distribution:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

**Step 4:** Equate and integrate:

$$du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$

$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \cdot r^{2} + C$$

**Step 5:** Apply no-slip at $r = R$: $C = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$

**Step 6:** Velocity profile:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Integrate over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

#### Part (b) — Rabinowitsch–Mooney Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $R = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, $\Delta P = 80 \times 10^{3}$ Pa, $K = 15$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.40$.

**Step 1:** Wall shear stress:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(80 \times 10^{3})}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{120}{0.8} = 150 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** True wall shear rate from power-law:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = \left(\frac{\tau_{w}}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{150}{15}\right)^{1/0.40} = (10)^{2.5} \approx 316.23 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Rabinowitsch-corrected volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi n R^{3}}{3n+1} \dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = \frac{\pi (0.40)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{3(0.40)+1} \times 316.23$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi (0.40)(3.375 \times 10^{-9})}{2.2} \times 316.23$$

$$Q = \frac{4.241 \times 10^{-9}}{2.2} \times 316.23 \approx 6.10 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 6.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L/s} = 2.20 \text{ L/h}}$$

**Step 4:** Apparent viscosity:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_{w}}{\dot{\gamma}_{w,true}} = \frac{150}{316.23} \approx 0.474 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{app} \approx 0.474 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2*

**(a)** Discuss the Bagley correction. Why is it necessary when characterizing food materials using capillary rheometry? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A food emulsion exhibits Bingham plastic behaviour with yield stress $\tau_{0} = 25$ Pa and plastic viscosity $\mu_{p} = 2.5$ Pa·s. It flows through a tube of radius $R = 5$ mm and length $L = 1.0$ m under a pressure drop $\Delta P = 60$ kPa. Calculate the volumetric flow rate using the Buckingham–Reiner equation. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Bagley Correction (7 Marks)

**Concept:**
The **Bagley correction** accounts for **entrance and exit pressure losses** in capillary rheometry. When a fluid enters a narrow capillary from a large reservoir, **extensional (elongational) flow** and **kinetic energy effects** occur at the entrance, creating an additional pressure drop $\Delta P_{end}$ that is not associated with true viscous shear.

The corrected wall shear stress becomes:

$$\tau_{w,corr} = \frac{(R + \Delta R)\Delta P}{2 L} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tau_{w,corr} = \frac{R(\Delta P - \Delta P_{end})}{2 L}$$

**Why It Is Necessary for Foods:**
1. **High-viscosity foods** (chocolate, starch pastes, doughs) generate substantial entrance losses.
2. **Short capillaries** (L/D < 50) suffer proportionally larger entrance effects.
3. **Yield-stress fluids** (Bingham plastics like mayonnaise, ketchup) exhibit plug-flow regions at the entrance.
4. **Quality control:** Without Bagley correction, the measured viscosity is artificially elevated, leading to over-design of pumps and oversized pipes.
5. **Multimodal foods** (suspensions, emulsions with droplets) undergo additional particle migration effects at contractions.

**Procedure:** Bagley plots of $\Delta P$ vs. $L/R$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ are extrapolated to zero length, giving the end correction $e = \Delta R/R$ as a numerical intercept.

#### Part (b) — Buckingham–Reiner Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\tau_{0} = 25$ Pa, $\mu_{p} = 2.5$ Pa·s, $R = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 1.0$ m, $\Delta P = 60 \times 10^{3}$ Pa.

**Step 1:** Wall shear stress:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(60 \times 10^{3})}{2 \times 1.0} = 150 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the dimensionless parameter $\xi$:

$$\xi = \frac{\tau_{0}}{\tau_{w}} = \frac{25}{150} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Buckingham–Reiner equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^{3} \tau_{w}}{4 \mu_{p}} \left[1 - \frac{4}{3}\xi + \frac{1}{3}\xi^{4}\right]$$

Computing each term:

$$1 - \frac{4}{3}(0.1667) + \frac{1}{3}(0.1667)^{4}$$

$$= 1 - 0.2222 + \frac{1}{3}(0.000772) = 1 - 0.2222 + 0.000257 \approx 0.7781$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the leading coefficient:

$$\frac{\pi R^{3} \tau_{w}}{4 \mu_{p}} = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-3})^{3} (150)}{4 \times 2.5} = \frac{\pi (1.25 \times 10^{-7})(150)}{10}$$

$$= \frac{5.890 \times 10^{-5}}{10} = 5.890 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 5:** Final volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = 5.890 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.7781 \approx 4.58 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 4.58 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 4.58 \text{ mL/s} = 16.5 \text{ L/h}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Students often plug in $R$ in mm instead of m, causing $\sim 10^{9}$ errors. Always convert to SI base units first.
> 2. **Missing the $n$ in the Rabinowitsch formula:** Writing $\frac{3+1}{4} = 1$ instead of $\frac{3n+1}{4n}$ — this collapses to the Newtonian case and loses **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing shear stress and shear rate:** Wall shear stress $\tau_{w}$ is linear in $R$; wall shear rate is inverse cubic in $R$.
> 4. **Ignoring Bagley correction:** For short capillaries ($L/D < 50$), examiners expect a brief mention of the entrance correction.
> 5. **Sign errors in velocity gradient:** The negative sign in $du/dr$ is critical for correctly obtaining the parabolic profile direction.
> 6. **Unit conversion in $\xi$ term of Buckingham–Reiner:** The yield stress $\tau_{0}$ MUST be in the same units (Pa) as $\tau_{w}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Volumetric Flows (Module 2)**

- 🔑 **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ — derived from force balance + Newton's law + no-slip BC.
- 🔑 **Velocity Profile:** Parabolic for Newtonian, *blunter/plug-like* for shear-thinning foods.
- 🔑 **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2L}$ — geometry-dependent, fluid-independent.
- 🔑 **Apparent Wall Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w,app} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ — Newtonian assumption.
- 🔑 **Rabinowitsch Factor:** $K = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}$ — for power-law foods, always $\geq 1$.
- 🔑 **True Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w,true} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{w,app}$.
- 🔑 **Apparent Viscosity:** $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_{w}}{\dot{\gamma}_{w,app}}$ — varies with shear rate for non-Newtonian foods.
- 🔑 **Buckingham–Reiner:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{3} \tau_{w}}{4 \mu_{p}}\left[1 - \dfrac{4}{3}\xi + \dfrac{1}{3}\xi^{4}\right]$ where $\xi = \tau_{0}/\tau_{w}$.
- 🔑 **Bagley Correction:** Adds effective length $eR$ to correct entrance effects; critical for short dies.
- 🔑 **Reynolds Number Limit:** $Re < 2100$ for laminar flow in tubes.
- 🔑 **Industrial Application:** Pump sizing, extruder die design, pipeline design for dairy/beverage plants.
- 🔑 **Common Food Examples:** Tomato sauce ($n \approx 0.4$), apple sauce ($n \approx 0.3$), molten chocolate ($n \approx 0.5$), honey ($n \approx 1$, Newtonian).
- 🔑 **Quick Sanity Check:** Doubling $R$ increases $Q$ by **$2^{(3n+1)/n}$**, which for $n = 0.4$ gives $\approx 25\times$.
- 🔑 **Exam Mantra:** Always state assumptions → derive or apply → check units → apply correction if non-Newtonian.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Viscoelasticity** is the property of materials that exhibit both **viscous** (fluid-like, time-dependent) and **elastic** (solid-like, recoverable) characteristics when subjected to deformation. A viscoelastic material stores part of the deformation energy (elastic response) and dissipates the remaining part as heat (viscous response).

The **stress-strain behaviour** of a viscoelastic material is therefore **time-dependent**, in stark contrast to purely elastic solids (governed by Hooke's Law) and purely viscous fluids (governed by Newton's Law of Viscosity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Viscoelastic characterization refers to the quantitative description of materials exhibiting combined elastic and viscous responses under applied stress or strain, where the stress-strain relationship depends on time, temperature, and the rate of deformation."*

The three fundamental mechanical responses classified in rheology are:

| Behaviour Type | Governing Law | Energy Storage | Energy Dissipation | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ideal Elastic (Solid)** | Hooke's Law | 100% Stored | 0% Dissipated | Steel spring |
| **Ideal Viscous (Fluid)** | Newton's Law | 0% Stored | 100% Dissipated | Water, Honey |
| **Viscoelastic (Hybrid)** | Boltzmann Superposition | Partial Storage | Partial Dissipation | Dough, Cheese, Bitumen |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Silly Putty Story

Imagine a piece of **Silly Putty** (a classic silicone polymer):

- **Pull it quickly (high strain rate):** It behaves like an **elastic solid** — it snaps back and feels rubbery. The molecules do not have time to flow past one another.
- **Pull it slowly (low strain rate):** It behaves like a **viscous liquid** — it stretches permanently and "melts" in your hand. The molecules have ample time to rearrange and slip past each other.

**Food Analogies (highly relevant for PEFTT745):**

1. **Bread Dough:** Stretch it fast — it tears elastically. Leave it on the counter — it flows slowly under its own weight (viscous creep).
2. **Aged Cheese:** Squeeze it hard — it deforms like rubber (elastic). Rest it on the counter — it slowly slumps and spreads (viscous).
3. **Tomato Ketchup:** Under gentle force it sits there (gel-like elastic network). Under sudden impact it splashes (viscous fluid breakdown).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The defining mathematical signature of a viscoelastic material is that the **stress σ depends not only on the instantaneous strain ε, but also on the entire history of strain (or its rate of change).** This is fundamentally different from Hookean or Newtonian materials.

## 1.3 The Three Domains of Response

For a viscoelastic food material, the stress-strain behaviour can be decomposed into three universal zones:

1. **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR):** Stress is directly proportional to strain; the material structure is undisturbed. **All valid rheological measurements must be performed here.**
2. **Non-Linear Viscoelastic Region:** Strain exceeds the LVR; structure begins to break down. Equations become complex.
3. **Yield / Flow Region:** Structure is irreversibly broken; material flows.

**Critical Metric:** The **Yield Stress (σ_y)** — measured in **Pascals (Pa)** — marks the boundary between the elastic-dominated regime and the viscous-dominated flow regime.
