<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Elementary Building Blocks

Every viscoelastic model is constructed by combining the two ideal mechanical elements:

### Element A — The Elastic Spring (Hookean Solid)
- Represents **instantaneous, recoverable** deformation.
- Follows **Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
- Symbol: A zig-zag coil (representing a metallic spring).
- Physical analogue in foods: the **entanglement network** of biopolymers (e.g., gluten in dough, casein in cheese).

### Element B — The Viscous Dashpot (Newtonian Fluid)
- Represents **time-dependent, irrecoverable** deformation.
- Follows **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$
- Symbol: A piston moving in a cylinder filled with oil.
- Physical analogue in foods: the **flow of solvent** (water, oil) and small molecules past a polymer matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - $\sigma$ = Stress (Pa)
> - $\varepsilon$ = Strain (dimensionless, m/m)
> - $E$ = Elastic Modulus / Young's Modulus (Pa)
> - $G$ = Shear Modulus (Pa), where $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ for isotropic materials
> - $\eta$ = Viscosity (Pa·s)
> - $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ = Strain rate (s⁻¹)

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Linear Viscoelastic Models

### Model 1 — Maxwell Model (Spring and Dashpot in SERIES)

**How it works:** Both elements experience the **same stress**, but strains **add up**.

- Total strain: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$
- Same stress throughout: $\sigma = \sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot}$
- Resulting constitutive equation (the **Master Equation of the Maxwell Model**):

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{E}\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Best represents:** Materials that show **stress relaxation** (e.g., molten polymers, bread dough relaxation).

### Model 2 — Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring and Dashpot in PARALLEL)

**How it works:** Both elements experience the **same strain**, but stresses **add up**.

- Total stress: $\sigma = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$
- Same strain throughout: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{spring} = \varepsilon_{dashpot}$
- Resulting constitutive equation:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Best represents:** Materials that show **creep** but **do not relax** (e.g., solid-like gels, cross-linked rubber, set yogurt).

### Model 3 — Burgers Model (Maxwell + Kelvin-Voigt in SERIES)

A four-parameter combination that captures **instantaneous elasticity, delayed elasticity, and viscous flow simultaneously** — the most realistic model for food systems.

$$\sigma + \left(\frac{\eta_1}{E_1} + \frac{\eta_1}{E_2} + \frac{\eta_2}{E_2}\right)\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\eta_1 \eta_2}{E_1 E_2}\ddot{\sigma} = \eta_1 \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{\eta_1 \eta_2}{E_2}\ddot{\varepsilon}$$

## 2.3 The Two Fundamental Experiments

### Experiment A — Creep Test
- **Procedure:** Apply a **constant stress** $\sigma_0$ suddenly and measure the resulting strain $\varepsilon(t)$ over time.
- **Output:** The **Creep Compliance** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ (units: Pa⁻¹).
- **Utility:** Tells us how a material deforms under sustained load (e.g., a chocolate bar left on a warm shelf).

### Experiment B — Stress Relaxation Test
- **Procedure:** Apply a **constant strain** $\varepsilon_0$ suddenly and measure the resulting stress $\sigma(t)$ over time.
- **Output:** The **Relaxation Modulus** $G(t) = \sigma(t) / \varepsilon_0$ (units: Pa).
- **Utility:** Tells us how internal stresses decay (e.g., dough relaxation during sheeting in a bakery).

### Experiment C — Oscillatory (Dynamic) Test
- **Procedure:** Apply a **sinusoidally oscillating** strain (or stress) of small amplitude within the LVR.
- **Output:** The **Complex Modulus** $G^{*}$ (storage modulus $G'$ and loss modulus $G''$).
- **Utility:** The most experimentally powerful tool for characterizing foods, since it is **non-destructive** and can probe a wide frequency range.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all high-yield formulas for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Memorize these before any numerical problem.**

| Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | Pa | Pure elastic response |
| Newton's Law | $\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Pa | Pure viscous response |
| Maxwell Model ODE | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dot{\sigma} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | s⁻¹ | Stress relaxation |
| Kelvin-Voigt Model ODE | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon + \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Pa | Creep without flow |
| Maxwell Relaxation Time | $\lambda_M = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | s | Time to relax 63.2% of initial stress |
| Kelvin Retardation Time | $\lambda_K = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | s | Time to reach 63.2% of equilibrium strain |
| Maxwell Stress Decay | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{\eta} t\right)$ | Pa | Stress at time $t$ |
| Kelvin Creep Strain | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{\eta} t\right)\right]$ | dimensionless | Strain at time $t$ |
| Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$ | Pa⁻¹ | Material fingerprint in creep |
| Relaxation Modulus | $G(t) = \dfrac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$ | Pa | Material fingerprint in relaxation |
| Complex Modulus | $G^{*} = G' + i G''$ | Pa | Total viscoelastic resistance |
| Storage Modulus | $G' = \vert G^{*}\vert \cos \delta$ | Pa | Elastic (solid-like) component |
| Loss Modulus | $G'' = \vert G^{*}\vert \sin \delta$ | Pa | Viscous (liquid-like) component |
| Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of viscous to elastic behaviour |
| Deborah Number | $De = \dfrac{\lambda}{t_{obs}}$ | dimensionless | Solid vs. fluid tendency |

## 2.5 Classification Rule Using the Loss Tangent

The dimensionless parameter $\tan \delta$ is the **single most important diagnostic** in KTU board questions on dynamic testing:

- If $\tan \delta \gg 1$ (typically $\tan \delta > 1$) $\Rightarrow$ **Viscous-dominated** (fluid-like, e.g., ketchup, melted chocolate).
- If $\tan \delta \ll 1$ (typically $\tan \delta < 1$) $\Rightarrow$ **Elastic-dominated** (solid-like, e.g., set yogurt, cheese curd).
- If $\tan \delta = 1$ $\Rightarrow$ The **gel point** or **sol-gel transition** (e.g., gelatin setting point).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, viscoelastic characterization is essential for:

1. **Quality Control:** Predicting the texture and mouthfeel of products.
2. **Process Design:** Sizing pumps, mixers, extruders, and pipe diameters.
3. **Shelf-Life Prediction:** Estimating how a product will deform or sag over months of storage.
4. **Formulation Engineering:** Designing fat replacers using the principle of viscoelastic mimicry.
5. **3D Food Printing:** Selecting inks with appropriate yield stress and elasticity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** All algebraic transitions must be shown explicitly. No "it can be shown that" steps are accepted in board answers. Below, every derivation is fully expanded.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation

**Given:** A spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in **series**.

**Step 1 — Define strain compatibility.** For elements in series, the total strain is the sum of individual strains:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2 — Define stress equilibrium.** For elements in series, the stress transmitted through each element is identical:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot} = \sigma$$

**Step 3 — Write Hooke's Law for the spring.** Since the stress is the same in both elements, isolate strain in the spring:

$$\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Write Newton's Law for the dashpot.** Since the stress is the same, isolate strain rate in the dashpot:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate the spring equation with respect to time.** This converts strain to strain rate:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} = \frac{1}{E} \dot{\sigma}$$

**Step 6 — Sum the strain rates.** Differentiating the compatibility equation from Step 1:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} + \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the results from Steps 4 and 5:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{E} \dot{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Result:** This is the **Master Constitutive Equation of the Maxwell Model**, relating the strain rate to the stress and the rate of change of stress.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maxwell Stress Relaxation Response

**Given:** A Maxwell material is suddenly subjected to a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$, then held constant.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary conditions:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \quad \text{for all} \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the boundary condition.** Since strain is constant, its derivative is zero:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Maxwell constitutive equation:**

$$0 = \frac{1}{E} \dot{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the variables.** Move the $\sigma / \eta$ term to the other side:

$$\frac{1}{E} \dot{\sigma} = -\frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables.** Multiply both sides by $E$ and divide by $\sigma$:

$$\frac{\dot{\sigma}}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.** Apply initial condition $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{1}{\sigma} \, d\sigma = -\frac{E}{\eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integrals.** The left side gives a logarithm, the right side a linear term:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{E}{\eta} t$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{\eta} t\right)$$

**Step 9 — Define the relaxation time.** Let $\lambda_M = \eta / E$:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_M}\right)}$$

**Result:** The stress decays **exponentially** with a characteristic time constant $\lambda_M$. At $t = \lambda_M$, the stress has decayed to approximately **36.8%** of its initial value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kelvin–Voigt Model Constitutive Equation

**Given:** A spring and a dashpot connected in **parallel**.

**Step 1 — Strain compatibility.** For elements in parallel, both undergo the same strain:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} = \varepsilon_{dashpot} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Stress equilibrium.** For elements in parallel, total stress is the sum:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

**Step 3 — Write Hooke's Law for the spring:**

$$\sigma_{spring} = E \, \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Write Newton's Law for the dashpot:**

$$\sigma_{dashpot} = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two stress contributions:**

$$\boxed{\sigma = E \, \varepsilon + \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}}$$

**Result:** This is the **Master Constitutive Equation of the Kelvin–Voigt Model**. Note that there is no term involving $\dot{\sigma}$, meaning this model cannot predict stress relaxation — only creep.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kelvin–Voigt Creep Response

**Given:** A Kelvin–Voigt material is suddenly subjected to a constant stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary conditions:**

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \quad \text{for all} \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon(t) + \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into standard first-order ODE form.** Divide by $\eta$:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) + \frac{E}{\eta} \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the integrating factor.** For an equation of the form $\dot{y} + p y = q$, the integrating factor is $\exp(p t)$:

$$\text{Integrating Factor} = \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply the ODE by the integrating factor:**

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E}{\eta} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right)$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the left side as an exact derivative:**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\varepsilon(t) \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right)\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right)$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$.** Apply initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) - 0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \int_0^t \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} \tau\right) d\tau$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_0^t \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} \tau\right) d\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 9 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\varepsilon(t) \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{E}{\eta} t\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 10 — Divide by the exponential and define retardation time $\lambda_K = \eta / E$:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_K}\right)\right]}$$

**Result:** The strain **asymptotically approaches** the equilibrium value $\sigma_0 / E$ with characteristic time $\lambda_K$. At $t = \lambda_K$, the strain has reached approximately **63.2%** of its equilibrium value.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Guide for KTU Problems

| Material Behaviour Observed | Correct Model | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Instantaneous elastic deformation + flow over time + stress relaxation | **Maxwell Model** | Series spring allows instantaneous jump in strain |
| Delayed but recoverable deformation, no permanent flow | **Kelvin–Voigt Model** | Parallel spring ensures strain recovery |
| All four: instantaneous elasticity, delayed elasticity, viscous flow, full stress relaxation | **Burgers Model** | Four-parameter Maxwell + Kelvin-Voigt |
| Solid-like at low frequencies, liquid-like at high frequencies | **Maxwell Model** | Dashpot dominates at short times |
| Liquid-like at low frequencies, solid-like at high frequencies | **Kelvin–Voigt Model** | Spring dominates at short times |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define viscoelasticity. How does the stress-strain behaviour of a viscoelastic material differ from that of a purely elastic solid and a purely viscous fluid?

**Model Answer (Board Standard, ~150 words):**

Viscoelasticity is the property of a material that exhibits both elastic (solid-like) and viscous (fluid-like) responses when subjected to deformation. In a purely elastic solid, the stress depends only on the instantaneous strain following Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$), and the deformation is fully recoverable. In a purely viscous fluid, the stress depends only on the strain rate following Newton's Law ($\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$), and the deformation is irrecoverable.

In contrast, a viscoelastic material's stress depends on both the strain and the strain rate (and on the entire deformation history). The deformation is only partially recoverable, and energy is both stored elastically and dissipated viscously as heat. Examples include bread dough, cheese, and molten polymers. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is the relaxation time of a Maxwell model? Write the expression for stress decay.

**Model Answer:**

The relaxation time $\lambda_M$ of a Maxwell model is defined as the characteristic time required for the stress to decay to approximately 36.8% ($1/e$) of its initial value when a constant strain is applied.

$$\lambda_M = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

where $\eta$ is the dashpot viscosity (Pa·s) and $E$ is the spring modulus (Pa). The stress decay is given by:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\lambda_M}\right)$$

A larger $\lambda_M$ means slower relaxation (more solid-like), while a smaller $\lambda_M$ means faster relaxation (more fluid-like). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat mechanical analog diagram, derive the constitutive equation of the Maxwell model. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Maxwell viscoelastic material has a spring modulus $E = 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$ and dashpot viscosity $\eta = 10^7 \, \text{Pa·s}$. A constant strain of $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$ is suddenly applied. Calculate: (i) the initial stress, (ii) the relaxation time, and (iii) the stress after 200 seconds. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 and Section 3.2 for the complete derivation. The key results are:

- Constitutive equation: $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dot{\sigma} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
- Stress decay: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, \exp(-Et/\eta)$
- Diagram: Spring and dashpot in series.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Initial stress:** For a Maxwell model under sudden strain $\varepsilon_0$, the dashpot resists only gradual deformation; at $t = 0$ the entire strain is borne by the spring:

$$\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 = (10^5 \, \text{Pa})(0.01) = 1000 \, \text{Pa}$$

**(ii) Relaxation time:**

$$\lambda_M = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{10^7}{10^5} = 100 \, \text{s}$$

**(iii) Stress after 200 s:** Using the exponential decay:

$$\sigma(200) = 1000 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{200}{100}\right) = 1000 \, \exp(-2) = 1000 \times 0.1353 = 135.3 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_0 = 1000 \, \text{Pa}$, $\lambda_M = 100 \, \text{s}$, $\sigma(200 \, \text{s}) = 135.3 \, \text{Pa}$. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat mechanical analog diagram, derive the constitutive equation of the Kelvin–Voigt model and obtain its creep response under constant stress. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Kelvin–Voigt material has $E = 5 \times 10^4 \, \text{Pa}$ and $\eta = 2 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa·s}$. A constant stress $\sigma_0 = 500 \, \text{Pa}$ is applied suddenly at $t = 0$. Calculate: (i) the equilibrium strain, (ii) the retardation time, and (iii) the strain at $t = 80 \, \text{s}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.3 and Section 3.4 for the complete derivation. The key results are:

- Constitutive equation: $\sigma = E \varepsilon + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$
- Creep strain: $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\!\left[1 - \exp(-Et/\eta)\right]$
- Diagram: Spring and dashpot in parallel.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Equilibrium strain** (as $t \to \infty$, exponential term vanishes):

$$\varepsilon_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{500}{5 \times 10^4} = 0.01$$

**(ii) Retardation time:**

$$\lambda_K = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^4} = 40 \, \text{s}$$

**(iii) Strain at $t = 80 \, \text{s}$:**

$$\varepsilon(80) = 0.01 \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{80}{40}\right)\right] = 0.01 \left[1 - \exp(-2)\right] = 0.01 \times 0.8647 = 0.008647$$

**Final Answer:** $\varepsilon_{\infty} = 0.01$, $\lambda_K = 40 \, \text{s}$, $\varepsilon(80 \, \text{s}) = 8.647 \times 10^{-3}$. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEFTT745 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Maxwell with Kelvin–Voigt at the diagram level.** Maxwell is **SERIES** (→ spring → dashpot →). Kelvin–Voigt is **PARALLEL** (spring and dashpot side by side, sharing load). Mixing these up costs the diagram mark (typically 2 of 7 marks).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state units explicitly.** Every numerical answer must carry units (Pa, s, Pa·s, dimensionless). A correct number without units loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 3. **In Kelvin–Voigt, attempting to derive a stress relaxation response.** The Kelvin–Voigt model CANNOT predict stress relaxation (the parallel spring prevents stress decay). If asked, say so explicitly — partial marks are awarded for correct reasoning.
>
> 4. **Dropping the negative sign in the exponential.** Students often write $\exp(Et/\eta)$ instead of $\exp(-Et/\eta)$. This is a fatal sign error that doubles the predicted stress/stress — full marks are lost in derivation questions.
>
> 5. **Not defining the relaxation/retardation time.** Always introduce $\lambda = \eta / E$ explicitly before using it — examiners look for this definition.
>
> 6. **In the creep-compliance question, using the wrong model.** If the material recovers fully after unloading → Kelvin–Voigt. If it shows permanent deformation → Maxwell or Burgers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Viscoelasticity** = combined elastic (energy-storing) and viscous (energy-dissipating) behaviour.
- **Maxwell Model** = spring **SERIES** dashpot. Best for stress relaxation; predicts permanent flow in creep.
- **Kelvin–Voigt Model** = spring **PARALLEL** dashpot. Best for creep; cannot predict stress relaxation.
- **Burgers Model** = Maxwell + Kelvin–Voigt in series; most realistic for foods; 4 parameters.
- **Maxwell ODE:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dot{\sigma} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$.
- **Kelvin–Voigt ODE:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$.
- **Relaxation time:** $\lambda_M = \eta / E$.
- **Maxwell stress decay:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\lambda_M)$.
- **Kelvin–Voigt creep strain:** $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\lambda_K)\right]$.
- **Creep compliance:** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ in units of **Pa⁻¹**.
- **Relaxation modulus:** $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$ in units of **Pa**.
- **Complex modulus:** $G^{*} = G' + iG''$ with magnitude $\vert G^{*}\vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$.
- **Storage modulus $G'$** = elastic (in-phase) response; **Loss modulus $G''$** = viscous (out-of-phase) response.
- **Loss tangent:** $\tan \delta = G''/G'$.
- **$\tan \delta > 1$** → viscous dominated (fluid); **$\tan \delta < 1$** → elastic dominated (gel/solid); **$\tan \delta = 1$** → gel point.
- **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR)** is the prerequisite for all valid oscillatory measurements.
- **Deborah Number $De = \lambda / t_{obs}$**: high $De$ → solid-like, low $De$ → fluid-like.
- **Food examples:** dough (viscoelastic solid), ketchup (viscous fluid with yield stress), cheese (weak gel), molten chocolate (viscous fluid).
- **Always state units** (Pa, s, Pa·s, dimensionless) in numerical answers.
- **Always draw the mechanical analog diagram** in 7-mark derivation questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Creep

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Creep** is defined as the time-dependent, continuous, and irreversible (or partially recoverable) deformation of a viscoelastic material subjected to a *constant applied stress* over a prolonged period. In the context of food rheology, creep testing is a fundamental *quasi-static* mechanical characterization technique used to probe the long-term viscoelastic response of soft solid and semi-solid food materials (gels, doughs, cheese, fats, etc.).

Mathematically, creep compliance is expressed as:

$$J(t) = \frac{\gamma(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

where $J(t)$ is the **creep compliance** (measured in $\text{Pa}^{-1}$), $\gamma(t)$ is the time-dependent strain, and $\sigma_0$ is the instantaneously applied constant stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Creep is the experimental method used to characterize the *linear viscoelastic region (LVR)* long-term behavior. It is distinct from stress relaxation (where strain is held constant and stress decays). Understanding creep is *essential* for predicting food texture during storage, transportation, and processing (e.g., cheese sagging on a shelf, dough spreading under its own weight).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a **block of soft cheddar cheese** on a kitchen counter and stacking a heavy cookbook on top of it. If you return after 6 hours, the cheese will be visibly flattened — it has *flowed* under the constant weight. This slow, time-dependent deformation is **creep**.

A few key intuitive points for students:

- **Constant Force, Not Constant Distance**: The textbook stays the same weight (constant stress), but the cheese continues to deform (strain increases with time).
- **Time is the Critical Variable**: If you remove the book after 5 seconds, the cheese nearly regains its shape. If you remove it after 5 hours, a permanent "dent" remains. This *partial recovery* is the fingerprint of viscoelasticity.
- **Why It Matters in Food Science**: Cheeses sag on pizza, yogurt sets under gravity, dough relaxes during proofing — these are all everyday manifestations of creep.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Standard units of creep compliance $J(t)$: **Pa⁻¹** (or **1/Pa**)
> - Standard units of creep strain $\gamma(t)$: **dimensionless** (or **% strain**)
> - Standard test temperature for reference: typically **25 °C** (room temperature) unless otherwise specified.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Materials by Creep Response

| Material Type | Creep Behavior Example (Food) | Mechanical Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Elastic Solid (Hookean)** | Hard candy (glass state) | Instantaneous strain, no time-dependence |
| **Ideal Viscous Liquid (Newtonian)** | Water, honey, cooking oil | Linear strain growth with time (no recovery) |
| **Viscoelastic Solid** | Cheese, butter, gelatin gel | Instantaneous strain + retarded strain + partial recovery |
| **Viscoelastic Liquid** | Ketchup, melted chocolate, yogurt | Instantaneous strain + retarded strain + steady flow (no equilibrium) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Creep Testing

When a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied at time $t = 0$ and held constant, the resulting strain response $\gamma(t)$ reveals three fundamental mechanical components in a viscoelastic food material:

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown:

1. **Instantaneous Elastic Strain ($\gamma_e$):** At the moment of stress application, the material deforms instantly (like a spring). This represents the **Hookean solid** component.
   - Analogous to: Pressing a ball — it deforms immediately.

2. **Retarded Viscoelastic Strain ($\gamma_{ve}(t)$):** Over time, the strain grows at a *decreasing rate* until it approaches a plateau (for solids) or a linear asymptote (for liquids). This delayed response is governed by *retardation times* $\tau_i$.
   - Analogous to: Slowly pressing your hand into memory foam.

3. **Steady-State Viscous Flow ($\gamma_v(t)$):** For viscoelastic *liquids* (e.g., melted chocolate, yogurt), a constant rate of strain $\dot{\gamma}_v$ persists indefinitely, governed by the viscosity $\eta_0$.
   - Analogous to: Honey flowing off a spoon at a constant rate.

4. **Recovery Phase (Unloading):** When stress is removed at time $t_1$, the instantaneous elastic strain recovers immediately, and the viscoelastic strain recovers gradually over time. The residual permanent strain corresponds to the purely viscous flow component.

---

## 2.2 The Burgers Model — Mathematical Foundation of Creep

The **Burgers model** is the most widely used four-parameter mechanical analog for representing creep behavior in foods. It consists of a **Maxwell element** (spring $G_1$ + dashpot $\eta_1$ in series) connected in **series** with a **Kelvin-Voigt element** (spring $G_2$ + dashpot $\eta_2$ in parallel).

### 2.2.1 Components of the Burgers Model:

| Element | Mechanical Role | Food Example Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Spring $G_1$ (Maxwell) | Instantaneous elastic deformation | Rigid protein network |
| Dashpot $\eta_1$ (Maxwell) | Irreversible viscous flow | Fat melting / liquid drainage |
| Spring $G_2$ (Kelvin) | Recoverable elastic storage | Weak gel crosslinks |
| Dashpot $\eta_2$ (Kelvin) | Time-delayed viscous resistance | Interstitial fluid friction |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-priority reference** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) on this topic. Memorize all symbols and units.

| Equation Name | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{\gamma(t)}{\sigma_0}$ | Normalized strain response | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| Burgers Total Strain | $\gamma(t) = \gamma_e + \gamma_{ve}(t) + \gamma_v(t)$ | Additive decomposition of strain | dimensionless |
| Instantaneous Strain | $\gamma_e = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{G_1}$ | Pure elastic response | dimensionless |
| Retarded Strain | $\gamma_{ve}(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right]$ | Kelvin-Voigt delayed response | dimensionless |
| Viscous Flow Strain | $\gamma_v(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} \cdot t$ | Maxwell dashpot flow | dimensionless |
| Retardation Time | $\tau_{ret} = \dfrac{\eta_2}{G_2}$ | Characteristic time of delayed strain | seconds (s) |
| Steady-State Compliance | $J_e^0 = \dfrac{1}{G_1} + \dfrac{1}{G_2}$ | Equilibrium compliance (solids) | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| Zero-Shear Viscosity | $\eta_0 = \eta_1$ (Maxwell dashpot) | Long-term viscous resistance | Pa·s |
| Total Burgers Compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{G_1} + \dfrac{1}{G_2}\!\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau_{ret}}\right] + \dfrac{t}{\eta_1}$ | Master creep equation | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Pitfall:** $\tau_{ret}$ has units of **seconds**, not Pascal. Do not confuse it with stress. Always convert minutes/hours of experimental time into seconds before computing $t/\tau_{ret}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food process engineering, creep data is *directly applied* to:

- **Shelf-life design of cheese and butter**: Predicting sagging and deformation under packaging weight.
- **Dough handling**: Optimizing resting/proofing times for bread making.
- **Texture perception correlation**: Long creep compliance correlates with the *mouthfeel* of soft foods (creaminess vs. firmness).
- **Chocolate tempering and coating**: Predicting sagging of chocolate coatings on biscuits during cooling.
- **3D food printing**: Ensuring printed structures hold their shape under gravity post-extrusion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Burgers Model Creep Equation

We will derive the total strain response $\gamma(t)$ of the Burgers model step-by-step, ensuring every algebraic transition is explicitly visible for KTU valuation purposes.

---

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Differential Equation

The Burgers model combines a **Maxwell element** and a **Kelvin-Voigt element** in **series**. For elements in series, **strains are additive** and **stresses are equal**.

Let the total strain be:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_M(t) + \gamma_K(t)$$

where $\gamma_M(t)$ is the Maxwell strain and $\gamma_K(t)$ is the Kelvin-Voigt strain. The applied stress is identical in both:

$$\sigma_0 = \sigma_M = \sigma_K$$

---

### 3.1.2 Maxwell Element Constitutive Equation

For the Maxwell element (spring $G_1$ in series with dashpot $\eta_1$), the strain rate is the sum of the spring strain rate and the dashpot strain rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}_M = \dot{\gamma}_{spring} + \dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}_M}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_M}{\eta_1}$$

Since creep applies a *constant stress* $\sigma_M = \sigma_0$, we have $\dot{\sigma}_M = 0$. Therefore:

$$\dot{\gamma}_M = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1}$$

Integrating with respect to time with initial condition $\gamma_M(0) = \sigma_0 / G_1$:

$$\gamma_M(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} \cdot t$$

This gives us the **instantaneous elastic strain** plus the **linear viscous flow strain**.

---

### 3.1.3 Kelvin-Voigt Element Constitutive Equation

For the Kelvin-Voigt element (spring $G_2$ in parallel with dashpot $\eta_2$), the total stress is the sum of the individual stresses at equal strain:

$$\sigma_K = G_2 \cdot \gamma_K + \eta_2 \cdot \dot{\gamma}_K$$

Substituting $\sigma_K = \sigma_0$:

$$\sigma_0 = G_2 \cdot \gamma_K + \eta_2 \cdot \dot{\gamma}_K$$

Rearranging into a standard first-order linear ODE:

$$\dot{\gamma}_K + \frac{G_2}{\eta_2} \cdot \gamma_K = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_2}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Solving the Kelvin-Voigt ODE

The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = \exp(G_2 t / \eta_2)$. Multiplying both sides:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[\gamma_K \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right)\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_2} \exp\!\left(\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right)$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$ with initial condition $\gamma_K(0) = 0$ (Kelvin-Voigt spring is unstressed at $t=0$):

$$\gamma_K(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right) - 1\right]$$

Dividing both sides by $\exp(G_2 t / \eta_2)$:

$$\gamma_K(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right)\right]$$

Defining the **retardation time** as:

$$\tau_{ret} = \frac{\eta_2}{G_2}$$

we obtain the compact form:

$$\gamma_K(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right]$$

---

### 3.1.5 Total Burgers Model Strain

Adding $\gamma_M(t)$ and $\gamma_K(t)$:

$$\boxed{\;\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} \cdot t\;}$$

Or equivalently, dividing by $\sigma_0$ to obtain the **creep compliance**:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G_1} + \frac{1}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_1}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Board Exam Style):**
> A cylindrical cheese sample of height $h = 20$ mm is subjected to a constant compressive stress of $\sigma_0 = 500$ Pa at $t = 0$. The Burgers parameters are $G_1 = 2000$ Pa, $G_2 = 800$ Pa, $\eta_1 = 5 \times 10^4$ Pa·s, and $\eta_2 = 4000$ Pa·s.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the instantaneous strain, retardation time, and the strain at $t = 10$ s.
> **(b)** Predict the steady-state compliance and the long-term viscous flow rate.

### Solution:

**Part (a):**

Instantaneous strain at $t = 0$:

$$\gamma_e = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} = \frac{500}{2000} = 0.25 \quad \text{(i.e., 25\% strain)}$$

Retardation time:

$$\tau_{ret} = \frac{\eta_2}{G_2} = \frac{4000}{800} = 5 \text{ s}$$

Strain at $t = 10$ s — we evaluate each term separately:

Kelvin-Voigt retarded strain:

$$\gamma_{ve}(10) = \frac{500}{800}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{10}{5}\right)\right] = 0.625 \times \left[1 - e^{-2}\right]$$

$$= 0.625 \times \left[1 - 0.1353\right] = 0.625 \times 0.8647 = 0.5404$$

Viscous flow strain:

$$\gamma_v(10) = \frac{500}{5 \times 10^4} \times 10 = \frac{5000}{5 \times 10^4} = 0.1$$

Total strain at $t = 10$ s:

$$\gamma(10) = 0.25 + 0.5404 + 0.1 = 0.8904 \quad \text{(i.e., ~89\% strain)}$$

**Part (b):**

Steady-state compliance:

$$J_e^0 = \frac{1}{G_1} + \frac{1}{G_2} = \frac{1}{2000} + \frac{1}{800} = 0.0005 + 0.00125 = 0.00175 \; \text{Pa}^{-1}$$

Long-term viscous strain rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}_v = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} = \frac{500}{5 \times 10^4} = 0.01 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always show unit cancellation in each step. Examiners award partial credit for dimensional consistency even if the final numerical answer is incorrect.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Burger Model Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four-element mechanical analog of the Burgers model, which is the canonical representation used in KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Constant Stress sigma0 Applied at t = 0"]:::inputNode --> B["MAXWELL ELEMENT Series Combination"]:::maxwellNode
    B --> C["KELVIN VOIGT ELEMENT Parallel Combination"]:::kelvinNode
    C --> D["TOTAL CREEP STRAIN gamma of t"]:::outputNode

    B --> B1["Spring G1 Instantaneous Elastic Strain"]:::springNode
    B --> B2["Dashpot eta1 Linear Viscous Flow"]:::dashpotNode

    C --> C1["Spring G2 Recoverable Elastic Storage"]:::springNode
    C --> C2["Dashpot eta2 Delayed Viscous Resistance"]:::dashpotNode

    B1 -.-> E["Instantaneous Response at t equals 0"]:::annotNode
    C2 -.-> F["Retarded Strain Timescale tau equals eta2 over G2"]:::annotNode
    B2 -.-> G["Irreversible Flow Strain Rate sigma0 over eta1"]:::annotNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef maxwellNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kelvinNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef springNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dashpotNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef annotNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Creep Test Protocol (KTU Lab Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Prepare cylindrical food sample uniform geometry"]:::stepNode --> P2["Step 2: Equilibrate sample at test temperature typically 25 C"]:::stepNode
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Mount sample on rheometer plate or creep fixture"]:::stepNode
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Apply constant stress sigma0 within LVR instantaneously"]:::stepNode
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Record strain gamma of t versus time for creep period t equals 0 to t1"]:::stepNode
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Remove stress at t equals t1 and start recovery period"]:::stepNode
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Record recovery strain versus time until equilibrium"]:::stepNode
    P7 --> P8["Step 8: Fit data to Burgers model extract G1 G2 eta1 eta2"]:::stepNode
    P8 --> P9["Step 9: Calculate creep compliance J of t and retardation time tau"]:::stepNode

    classDef stepNode fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Typical Creep Curve of a Food Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["INSTANTANEOUS REGION t equals 0"]
        A0["Strain jumps to sigma0 over G1"]:::instantNode
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["RETARDED REGION t increases 0 to t1"]
        B0["Strain grows asymptotically toward plateau"]:::retardedNode
        B1["Curve governed by Kelvin Voigt element"]:::annotNode
        B2["Time constant tau equals eta2 over G2"]:::annotNode
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["STEADY STATE FLOW REGION t greater than t1"]
        C0["Strain increases linearly with slope sigma0 over eta1"]:::flowNode
        C1["Pure viscous flow phase"]:::annotNode
    end

    subgraph PHASE4["RECOVERY REGION after stress removal"]
        D0["Instantaneous elastic recovery sigma0 over G1"]:::recoveryNode
        D1["Gradual viscoelastic recovery approaching plateau"]:::annotNode
        D2["Residual permanent strain equals viscous flow component"]:::annotNode
    end

    A0 --> B0 --> C0 --> D0

    classDef instantNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef retardedNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flowNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recoveryNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef annotNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#333
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison: Solid vs. Liquid Viscoelastic Creep Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Apply constant stress sigma0"]:::inputNode --> Q2["Does the strain reach a finite plateau?"]:::decisionNode

    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["VISCOELASTIC SOLID e.g. cheese, butter, gelatin"]:::solidNode
    Q2 -- NO continues to flow --> Q4["VISCOELASTIC LIQUID e.g. ketchup, yogurt, melted chocolate"]:::liquidNode

    Q3 --> Q3A["Steady state compliance Je0 finite"]:::detailNode
    Q3 --> Q3B["Recovery is complete except for small residual"]:::detailNode
    Q3 --> Q3C["Zero shear viscosity eta0 effectively infinite"]:::detailNode

    Q4 --> Q4A["Strain rate constant equals sigma0 over eta1"]:::detailNode
    Q4 --> Q4B["Recovery leaves permanent deformation"]:::detailNode
    Q4 --> Q4C["Zero shear viscosity eta0 finite and measurable"]:::detailNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef solidNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef liquidNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detailNode fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define creep compliance. Why is it preferred over raw strain data in food rheology?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Creep compliance $J(t)$ is defined as the *time-dependent ratio of strain to the applied constant stress*:

$$J(t) = \frac{\gamma(t)}{\sigma_0} \quad [\text{units: Pa}^{-1}]$$

**Reasons for preference (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Normalization**: It allows direct comparison between samples tested at *different stress levels*, provided the test is conducted within the linear viscoelastic region (LVR).
2. **Material-specific signature**: It is an intrinsic material property independent of the magnitude of applied stress, making it useful for comparing different food formulations.
3. **Cross-laboratory comparability**: Published $J(t)$ curves can be directly compared across different studies and instruments.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt elements with the help of neat diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Maxwell Element | Kelvin-Voigt Element |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** |
| Strain | Strains are **additive**: $\gamma = \gamma_s + \gamma_d$ | Strains are **equal**: $\gamma_s = \gamma_d$ |
| Stress | Stresses are **equal**: $\sigma = \sigma_s = \sigma_d$ | Stresses are **additive**: $\sigma = \sigma_s + \sigma_d$ |
| Creep Response | Instantaneous + linear viscous flow (no plateau) | Exponential approach to a finite plateau |
| Recovery | Partial (only the elastic component recovers) | Complete (returns to original shape) |
| Food Example | Honey flowing steadily | Cheese resisting under sustained load |

(Diagrams: 1 mark; Tabular comparison: 1.5 marks; Examples: 0.5 mark)

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic diagram, derive the creep compliance expression for the **four-parameter Burgers model**. Clearly define each parameter and state its units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food gel exhibits Burgers behavior with $G_1 = 1500$ Pa, $G_2 = 500$ Pa, $\eta_1 = 3 \times 10^4$ Pa·s, and $\eta_2 = 2500$ Pa·s. A constant shear stress of $400$ Pa is applied for 8 seconds. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   - (i) The retardation time $\tau_{ret}$
   - (ii) The instantaneous strain $\gamma_e$
   - (iii) The total strain at $t = 8$ s
   - (iv) The creep compliance at $t = 8$ s

---

#### Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Schematic diagram (1 mark):* The Burgers model consists of a Maxwell unit (spring $G_1$ in series with dashpot $\eta_1$) connected in **series** with a Kelvin-Voigt unit (spring $G_2$ in parallel with dashpot $\eta_2$).

*Derivation steps (6 marks):*

Total strain:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_M(t) + \gamma_K(t)$$

For Maxwell element under constant stress, solving $\dot{\gamma}_M = \sigma_0 / \eta_1$:

$$\gamma_M(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} \cdot t$$

For Kelvin-Voigt element under constant stress $\sigma_0 = G_2 \gamma_K + \eta_2 \dot{\gamma}_K$, solving the first-order ODE with $\gamma_K(0) = 0$:

$$\gamma_K(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{G_2 t}{\eta_2}\right)\right]$$

Total strain:

$$\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} \cdot t$$

Creep compliance:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{G_1} + \frac{1}{G_2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_{ret}}\right)\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_1}$$

Units: $G_1, G_2$ in **Pa**; $\eta_1, \eta_2$ in **Pa·s**; $\tau_{ret}$ in **s**; $J(t)$ in **Pa⁻¹**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** Retardation time:

$$\tau_{ret} = \frac{\eta_2}{G_2} = \frac{2500}{500} = 5 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(ii)** Instantaneous strain:

$$\gamma_e = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} = \frac{400}{1500} = 0.2667 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii)** Total strain at $t = 8$ s:

Kelvin-Voigt term:

$$\gamma_{ve}(8) = \frac{400}{500}\left[1 - e^{-8/5}\right] = 0.8 \times \left[1 - 0.2019\right] = 0.8 \times 0.7981 = 0.6385$$

Viscous flow term:

$$\gamma_v(8) = \frac{400}{3 \times 10^4} \times 8 = \frac{3200}{30000} = 0.1067$$

Total:

$$\gamma(8) = 0.2667 + 0.6385 + 0.1067 = 1.0119 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iv)** Creep compliance at $t = 8$ s:

$$J(8) = \frac{\gamma(8)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1.0119}{400} = 2.530 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **creep recovery test** in detail. How is it used to distinguish between a viscoelastic solid and a viscoelastic liquid? Provide suitable food examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cream sample is subjected to a creep test. At time $t_1 = 60$ s, the stress is removed. The total strain at $t_1$ is $\gamma(60) = 0.45$. After long recovery, the residual permanent strain is $\gamma_{\text{residual}} = 0.08$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   - (i) The recovered strain
   - (ii) The percentage recovery
   - (iii) The recovered compliance and residual compliance, given the applied stress was $\sigma_0 = 100$ Pa
   - (iv) Comment on whether the cream is more solid-like or liquid-like based on recovery data.

---

#### Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Recovery Test Explanation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** A creep recovery test follows a creep-loading phase. After holding constant stress $\sigma_0$ for a duration $t_1$, the stress is suddenly removed (set to zero) and the strain recovery is monitored as a function of time.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Apply constant stress $\sigma_0$ for time $t_1$ and record strain.
2. At $t = t_1$, abruptly remove the stress.
3. Continue recording strain — it decreases with time as the material partially regains its shape.
4. The recovery curve is analyzed using the same Burgers parameters.

**Solid vs Liquid Distinction (2 marks):**

- **Viscoelastic Solid (e.g., cheese, gelatin, butter):** Strain returns to a finite plateau with *complete or near-complete recovery*. The permanent (residual) strain is *very small*.
- **Viscoelastic Liquid (e.g., ketchup, yogurt, melted chocolate):** A significant *permanent residual strain* remains after recovery because of the irreversible viscous flow component.

**Food Examples (1 mark):** Cheese = solid-like; Chocolate sauce = liquid-like.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i)** Recovered strain:

$$\gamma_{\text{recovered}} = \gamma(60) - \gamma_{\text{residual}} = 0.45 - 0.08 = 0.37 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(ii)** Percentage recovery:

$$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{0.37}{0.45} \times 100 = 82.22\% \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii)** Compliances:

Recovered compliance:

$$J_{\text{recovered}} = \frac{0.37}{100} = 3.7 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

Residual compliance:

$$J_{\text{residual}} = \frac{0.08}{100} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iv) Comment:** With an 82.22% recovery, the cream is **predominantly solid-like (viscoelastic solid)** with a small but measurable liquid component (≈18% residual). This is consistent with the *creamy texture* of dairy creams, which hold their shape but flow slowly.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up creep and stress relaxation:** Creep = *constant stress, strain increases*. Stress relaxation = *constant strain, stress decreases*. Examiners frequently set trap questions on this distinction.
> 2. **Wrong retardation time formula:** Students often write $\tau = G/\eta$ instead of $\tau = \eta/G$. The correct formula is $\tau_{ret} = \eta_2 / G_2$ (dashpot over spring).
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $\tau_{ret}$ is in **seconds**, $\eta$ is in **Pa·s**, $G$ is in **Pa**. Showing inconsistent units loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Neglecting the recovery phase:** A complete creep answer must discuss *both* the loading phase and the recovery phase.
> 5. **Confusing compliance J(t) with modulus G(t):** Compliance is the inverse of modulus in the elastic limit but not in general viscoelastic response.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> - [x] Creep = time-dependent strain under **constant stress**.
> - [x] Creep compliance $J(t) = \gamma(t) / \sigma_0$ in units of **Pa⁻¹**.
> - [x] Burgers model = Maxwell element (series) + Kelvin-Voigt element (series).
> - [x] Four parameters: $G_1$, $G_2$, $\eta_1$, $\eta_2$.
> - [x] Instantaneous strain: $\gamma_e = \sigma_0 / G_1$.
> - [x] Retarded strain: $\gamma_{ve}(t) = (\sigma_0/G_2)[1 - e^{-t/\tau_{ret}}]$.
> - [x] Viscous flow strain: $\gamma_v(t) = (\sigma_0/\eta_1) \cdot t$.
> - [x] Retardation time: $\tau_{ret} = \eta_2 / G_2$ in **seconds**.
> - [x] Steady-state compliance: $J_e^0 = 1/G_1 + 1/G_2$.
> - [x] Solid-like food = finite plateau + near-complete recovery (e.g., cheese).
> - [x] Liquid-like food = continuous flow + significant permanent strain (e.g., honey, ketchup).
> - [x] Recovery test is the definitive method to classify a food as solid-like or liquid-like.
> - [x] Always convert time units to **seconds** before substituting into $t/\tau_{ret}$.
> - [x] Show full unit cancellations in numerical problems to gain partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Relaxation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Relaxation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stress Relaxation** is a transient viscoelastic test in which a **sudden, constant strain** (deformation) is instantaneously applied to a material and held fixed, while the resulting **stress developed within the material is monitored as a function of time**. The stress is observed to decay (relax) gradually from an initial maximum value toward an equilibrium value as the molecular/network structure of the material reorganizes to relieve the internally stored elastic energy.

Mathematically, the boundary conditions for a stress relaxation experiment are:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \cdot H(t) \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma(t) \text{ is measured}$$

where $H(t)$ is the **Heaviside step function** and $\gamma_0$ is the instantaneously imposed constant strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Stress relaxation is one of the three primary viscoelastic tests, alongside **creep** and **dynamic (oscillatory) testing**. It directly probes the **short-term and long-term elastic memory** of food biopolymer networks (gels, doughs, meat proteins, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Silly Putty & Rubber Band

Imagine you stretch a piece of **silly putty** very quickly with your hands and then hold it at a fixed stretched length using a clamp. The instant you stretch it, you feel a strong **pulling-back force (high stress)**. If you keep holding the silly putty at that same stretched length, you will feel the pulling-back force **gradually decrease** over several minutes until the putty seems to "give up" resisting and starts to flow. This is **stress relaxation** — the material internally rearranges to dissipate the stress while the deformation is locked in place.

In contrast, a **steel spring** held at fixed length would not relax (purely elastic). A **honey droplet** would relax instantly to zero stress (purely viscous). Real foods lie somewhere in between — they are **viscoelastic**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Relaxation Modulus** $G(t)$ or $E(t)$ — measured in **Pascal (Pa)** — defined as $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\gamma_0$.
- **Characteristic Relaxation Time** $\tau$ — measured in **seconds (s)** — the time required for the stress to decay to $\approx 36.8\%$ ($\,1/e\,$) of its initial value.
- **Equilibrium Modulus** $G_e$ — long-time stress per unit strain (Pa).
- **Zero-Time Modulus** $G_0$ — instantaneous stress per unit strain (Pa).

> [!NOTE]
> For food materials, $\tau$ values can range from **microseconds** (low-viscosity solutions) to **hours** (glassy biopolymers, aged cheese, hard doughs), making stress relaxation a powerful probe across vastly different time scales.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Framework — Why Does Stress Relax?

When a strain is suddenly applied to a viscoelastic food material, three competing molecular events occur simultaneously:

1. **Elastic (instantaneous) response** — Bond stretching in the polymer network, governed by the elastic modulus $E$ or $G$.
2. **Viscous flow (time-dependent) dissipation** — Chain disentanglement, slippage of entanglements, and reptation of polymer molecules past one another.
3. **Network reconfiguration** — Reformation of secondary bonds (hydrogen, hydrophobic, electrostatic) into a less-stressed equilibrium state.

The **net observable stress** is the sum of these contributions. As time progresses, the viscous component dissipates the elastic energy, and the stress decays.

### 2.2 The Maxwell Model (Single Element)

The simplest conceptual model for stress relaxation is the **Maxwell element** — a Hookean spring (elastic, modulus $E$) connected in **series** with a Newtonian dashpot (viscous, viscosity $\eta$).

**Operational logic of the Maxwell element under constant strain:**

- **Step 1 (t = 0⁺):** The dashpot cannot move instantaneously, so the entire strain is taken by the spring → $\sigma_0 = E \cdot \gamma_0$.
- **Step 2 (t > 0):** The dashpot begins to flow, gradually relieving the spring. The spring contracts as the dashpot extends, maintaining the total strain $\gamma_0$ constant.
- **Step 3 (t → ∞):** In a single Maxwell element, the spring eventually fully relaxes → $\sigma \to 0$ (no equilibrium modulus).

### 2.3 The Generalized Maxwell Model (Wiechert Model)

Real foods exhibit **multiple relaxation processes** (e.g., side-chain rotations, backbone reptation, network strand rearrangements). Each process has its own characteristic time $\tau_i$. A **Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) model** superimposes $N$ Maxwell elements in **parallel**:

$$G(t) = G_e + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_i}$$

where:
- $G_e$ = equilibrium (long-time) modulus from purely elastic parallel springs
- $G_i$ = modulus contribution of the $i^{th}$ Maxwell branch
- $\tau_i = \eta_i / G_i$ = relaxation time of the $i^{th}$ element

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Boundary condition | $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 H(t)$ | dimensionless | Heaviside step |
| 2 | Single Maxwell stress decay | $\sigma(t) = E \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | Pa | Mono-exponential |
| 3 | Relaxation modulus (Maxwell) | $G(t) = E \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | Pa | For shear |
| 4 | Characteristic relaxation time | $\tau = \eta \,/\, E$ | s | **Key KTU formula** |
| 5 | Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) | $G(t) = G_e + \sum G_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$ | Pa | Multi-mode relaxation |
| 6 | Stress at one relaxation time | $\sigma(\tau) = \sigma_0 / e \approx 0.368 \, \sigma_0$ | Pa | Definition of $\tau$ |
| 7 | Zero-time modulus | $G_0 = G_e + \sum G_i$ | Pa | $t=0$ limit |
| 8 | Steady-state compliance (related) | $J_e = 1 / G_e$ | Pa$^{-1}$ | Equilibrium creep compliance |
| 9 | Spectrum intensity (discrete) | $G_i$ | Pa | Each mode's weight |
| 10 | Boltzmann superposition principle | $\sigma(t) = \int_0^t G(t-t') \, \dot{\gamma}(t') \, dt'$ | Pa | Linear viscoelasticity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Trap:** In the formula table, the **division symbol** has been carefully written as `\,/\,` (slash with thin spaces) and never as the markdown table pipe `\vert`. This is essential to avoid breaking the markdown table structure in your own notes too.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Science

Stress relaxation tests are extensively used to:

- **Bread dough characterization** — measuring gluten network strength and relaxation for processability predictions.
- **Cheese texture analysis** — distinguishing Cheddar, Mozzarella, and processed cheeses by their relaxation spectra.
- **Meat tenderness evaluation** — assessing myofibrillar protein restructuring post-mortem.
- **Edible film/coating design** — predicting in-mouth stress decay and mouthfeel.
- **Extrusion and 3D food printing** — determining whether printed structures will hold shape or slump.
- **Quality control of biopolymer gels** (agar, gelatin, pectin, alginate) — monitoring gel network maturation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress Relaxation Function for a Single Maxwell Element

**Starting Constitutive Equation:**

The Maxwell model is described by a first-order linear ODE relating strain rate to stress and stress rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}(t) = \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t)}{E} + \frac{\sigma(t)}{\eta}$$

**Textual Logic:** The total strain rate equals the elastic strain rate (stress rate divided by modulus) plus the viscous strain rate (stress divided by viscosity). This is the constitutive definition of the Maxwell element.

**Apply the Stress Relaxation Boundary Condition:**

For a stress relaxation test, the strain is held constant after a step at $t = 0$:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma}(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

**Substitute into the constitutive equation:**

$$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t)}{E} + \frac{\sigma(t)}{\eta}$$

**Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE:**

$$\dot{\sigma}(t) + \frac{E}{\eta} \, \sigma(t) = 0$$

**Recognize the coefficient as the inverse relaxation time:**

Let $\tau = \eta / E$, so $E/\eta = 1/\tau$. The ODE becomes:

$$\dot{\sigma}(t) + \frac{\sigma(t)}{\tau} = 0$$

**Solve using separation of variables:**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

**Integrate both sides:**

$$\ln \sigma = -\frac{t}{\tau} + C$$

**Exponentiate to obtain the general solution:**

$$\sigma(t) = A \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $A$ is the integration constant determined by initial conditions.

**Apply the initial condition (t = 0⁺):** The dashpot cannot move instantaneously, so the spring takes the full strain:

$$\sigma(0^+) = E \, \gamma_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = E \, \gamma_0$$

**Final stress relaxation function for a single Maxwell element:**

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E \, \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relaxation Modulus and Its Physical Meaning

**Definition of the relaxation modulus:**

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\gamma_0}$$

**Substitute the stress relaxation solution:**

$$G(t) = \frac{E \, \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}}{\gamma_0}$$

**Simplify:**

$$\boxed{G(t) = E \, e^{-t/\tau}}$$

**Special-case evaluation:**

- At $t = 0$: $G(0) = E$ (instantaneous elastic modulus)
- At $t = \tau$: $G(\tau) = E / e \approx 0.368 \, E$ (stress has decayed to ~36.8%)
- At $t \to \infty$: $G(\infty) = 0$ (complete relaxation in single Maxwell element)

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) Model Superposition

**Conceptual Setup:** Consider $N$ Maxwell branches in parallel, plus a single free spring representing the equilibrium modulus. The total stress is the sum of stresses in each parallel branch because strain is uniform across all parallel elements.

**Branch stress contribution:** Each Maxwell branch $i$ contributes:

$$\sigma_i(t) = G_i \, \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$$

**Equilibrium spring contribution:** A purely elastic spring in parallel contributes:

$$\sigma_e(t) = G_e \, \gamma_0$$

**Total stress (parallel superposition):**

$$\sigma(t) = G_e \, \gamma_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \, \gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$$

**Divide by $\gamma_0$ to get the relaxation modulus:**

$$\boxed{G(t) = G_e + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A food gel (pectin) sample is subjected to a stress relaxation test. A sudden shear strain $\gamma_0 = 0.05$ is applied. The single-element Maxwell parameters are $E = 2000$ Pa and $\eta = 1000$ Pa·s. Calculate: (a) the relaxation time, (b) the initial stress, (c) the stress at $t = 1$ s, and (d) the time for the stress to fall to 10% of $\sigma_0$.

**Part (a) — Relaxation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{1000 \text{ Pa·s}}{2000 \text{ Pa}} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

**Part (b) — Initial stress (t = 0⁺):**

$$\sigma_0 = E \, \gamma_0 = 2000 \times 0.05 = 100 \text{ Pa}$$

**Part (c) — Stress at t = 1 s:**

$$\sigma(1) = 100 \times e^{-1/0.5} = 100 \times e^{-2} = 100 \times 0.1353 = 13.53 \text{ Pa}$$

**Part (d) — Time for stress to reach 10% of $\sigma_0$:**

Set $\sigma(t) = 0.10 \, \sigma_0$:

$$0.10 = e^{-t/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(0.10) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

$$t = -\tau \ln(0.10) = -0.5 \times (-2.3026) = 1.151 \text{ s}$$

**Answer Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|----------|-------|-------|
| $\tau$ | 0.5 | s |
| $\sigma_0$ | 100 | Pa |
| $\sigma(1\text{ s})$ | 13.53 | Pa |
| $t_{10\%}$ | 1.151 | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if the **units are written explicitly** in every numerical answer. Never leave unit-less final values.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Relation Between Stress Relaxation and the Boltzmann Superposition Principle

The Boltzmann superposition principle states that for a **linear viscoelastic** material, the stress at time $t$ is the linear sum of contributions from all past strain increments:

$$\sigma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} G(t - t') \, \dot{\gamma}(t') \, dt'$$

**Application to step strain:** $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 H(t)$ implies $\dot{\gamma}(t) = \gamma_0 \delta(t)$ (Dirac delta). Substituting:

$$\sigma(t) = \int_{0}^{t} G(t - t') \, \gamma_0 \, \delta(t') \, dt' = \gamma_0 \, G(t)$$

This confirms that the relaxation modulus is the fundamental material function that fully characterizes the linear viscoelastic stress response to a step strain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress Relaxation Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Mounting in Rheometer] --> B[Equilibration at Test Temperature]
    B --> C[Apply Step Strain gamma0 Instantly]
    C --> D[Lock Strain to Constant gamma0]
    D --> E[Acquire Stress sigma vs Time t]
    E --> F[Compute Relaxation Modulus Gt = sigma / gamma0]
    F --> G[Plot log Gt vs log t]
    G --> H{Choose Model}
    H -->|Single mode| I[Single Maxwell: Gt = E exp -t/tau]
    H -->|Multi mode| J[Wiechert: Gt = Ge + sum Gi exp -t/taui]
    H -->|Continuous| K[Continuous Spectrum Htau]
    I --> L[Extract E and tau]
    J --> M[Extract Ge, Gi, taui via NLLS Fitting]
    K --> N[Inverse Laplace Transform]
    L --> O[Report Material Viscoelastic Fingerprint]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Mechanical Analogue Schematic — Maxwell and Generalized Maxwell Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Maxwell1["Maxwell Element 1 (Branch 1)"]
        S1[Spring G1] --- D1[η1]
    end
    subgraph Maxwell2["Maxwell Element 2 (Branch 2)"]
        S2[Spring G2] --- D2[η2]
    end
    subgraph Maxwell3["Maxwell Element 3 (Branch 3)"]
        S3[Spring G3] --- D3[η3]
    end
    subgraph EqSpring["Equilibrium Spring"]
        EQS[Spring Ge]
    end
    Maxwell1 --- Maxwell2
    Maxwell2 --- Maxwell3
    Maxwell3 --- EqSpring
    P[Applied Step Strain gamma0] --> Maxwell1
    EqSpring --> OUT[Total Stress sigma t]
```

### 4.3 Typical Stress Relaxation Curve — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[t = 0: Strain Applied Instantly] --> B[sigma0 = E gamma0]
    B --> C[t = tau: sigma = 0.368 sigma0]
    C --> D[2tau: sigma = 0.135 sigma0]
    D --> E[3tau: sigma = 0.050 sigma0]
    E --> F[t >> tau: sigma approaches 0 for Maxwell]
    F --> G{Generalized Model?}
    G -->|Yes| H[sigma approaches Ge gamma0 plateau]
    G -->|No| I[sigma approaches 0]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Where Stress Relaxation Fits in Viscoelastic Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Material Sample] --> B{Viscoelastic Test Type}
    B -->|Constant Stress| C[Creep Test]
    B -->|Constant Strain| D[Stress Relaxation Test]
    B -->|Oscillatory| E[Dynamic Mechanical Analysis]
    D --> F[Time Domain Response]
    F --> G[Relaxation Modulus Gt]
    G --> H[Convert to Frequency Domain]
    H --> I[Storage G prime and Loss G double prime]
    I --> J[Master Curve Construction]
    J --> K[Time Temperature Superposition]
    K --> L[Engineering Design Parameters]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams:** Because Mermaid cannot render true 2D mechanical drawings of springs and dashpots, the **block-level functional architecture** in 4.1–4.4 conveys the same information as a physical schematic — the workflow, the parallel arrangement of Maxwell branches, the decay of stress, and the position of stress relaxation in the broader rheological testing landscape.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define stress relaxation. What is the relaxation modulus?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stress relaxation is a transient viscoelastic test in which a **sudden constant strain** $\gamma_0$ is applied to a material and held fixed, while the **decay of stress** $\sigma(t)$ is recorded over time.

The **relaxation modulus** $G(t)$ is defined as the time-dependent stress per unit imposed strain:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\gamma_0}$$

For a single Maxwell element, $G(t) = E \, e^{-t/\tau}$ where $\tau = \eta/E$ is the characteristic relaxation time. For a generalized Maxwell model, $G(t) = G_e + \sum G_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$. The relaxation modulus characterizes the material's **elastic memory** and is a fundamental material function in linear viscoelasticity.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for relaxation modulus expression, 1 mark for significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Explain the physical significance of the relaxation time $\tau$ in the Maxwell model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relaxation time $\tau = \eta / E$ is a **characteristic material time scale** that quantifies the rate at which the material transitions from elastic-dominant to viscous-dominant behavior.

Physically, $\tau$ represents the time required for the stress to decay to $\approx 36.8\%$ ($1/e$) of its initial value when the material is held at constant strain.

**Interpretation guidelines (commonly tested):**

- If $t \ll \tau$: the material behaves **elastically** (dashpot barely moves).
- If $t \approx \tau$: viscoelastic transition regime.
- If $t \gg \tau$: the material behaves **viscously** (spring fully relaxed).
- **High $\tau$** → more elastic, longer memory (e.g., aged cheese, hard dough).
- **Low $\tau$** → more fluid-like, short memory (e.g., dilute biopolymer solutions).

The **Deborah number** $De = \tau_{\text{obs}} / \tau_{\text{material}}$ compares observation time to $\tau$ and is widely used in food processing.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the stress relaxation function for a **single Maxwell element** subjected to a step strain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheat dough sample is tested in stress relaxation mode. A step shear strain of $\gamma_0 = 0.02$ is applied. The recorded data are best fitted by a **three-element generalized Maxwell model** with parameters:

$$G_e = 500 \text{ Pa}, \quad G_1 = 800 \text{ Pa}, \, \tau_1 = 0.1 \text{ s}; \quad G_2 = 400 \text{ Pa}, \, \tau_2 = 5 \text{ s}$$

Calculate: (i) the initial stress, (ii) the stress at $t = 1$ s, and (iii) the equilibrium stress as $t \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- Constitutive ODE: $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\sigma}/E + \sigma/\eta$ — **(1 mark)**
- Apply boundary condition $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ for $t > 0$ — **(1 mark)**
- Rearrange to $\dot{\sigma} + (E/\eta)\sigma = 0$ — **(1 mark)**
- Define $\tau = \eta/E$, solve ODE — **(1 mark)**
- General solution $\sigma(t) = A e^{-t/\tau}$ — **(1 mark)**
- Initial condition $\sigma(0^+) = E\gamma_0 \Rightarrow A = E\gamma_0$ — **(1 mark)**
- Final: $\sigma(t) = E\gamma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ with sketch — **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Initial stress** at $t = 0$:

$$G(0) = G_e + G_1 + G_2 = 500 + 800 + 400 = 1700 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_0 = G(0) \cdot \gamma_0 = 1700 \times 0.02 = \boxed{34 \text{ Pa}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Stress at $t = 1$ s:**

$$G(1) = 500 + 800 \cdot e^{-1/0.1} + 400 \cdot e^{-1/5}$$

$$= 500 + 800 \cdot e^{-10} + 400 \cdot e^{-0.2}$$

$$= 500 + 800 \times (4.54 \times 10^{-5}) + 400 \times 0.8187$$

$$= 500 + 0.0363 + 327.49 = 827.53 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma(1) = 827.53 \times 0.02 = \boxed{16.55 \text{ Pa}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) Equilibrium stress as $t \to \infty$:** All exponentials vanish:

$$G(\infty) = G_e = 500 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_\infty = 500 \times 0.02 = \boxed{10 \text{ Pa}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Question A):**
> 1. **Forgetting the equilibrium term** $G_e$ — many students write $G(t) = \sum G_i e^{-t/\tau_i}$ only and lose 2–3 marks in part (b)(iii).
> 2. **Unit errors in $\tau$** — $\tau_1 = 0.1$ s means $t/\tau_1 = 10$ (very large), making $e^{-10} \approx 0$. Students often compute $e^{-0.1}$ by mistake.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch** in part (a) — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for the **labeled diagram** of spring–dashpot in series.
> 4. **Not stating the boundary condition** explicitly ($\dot{\gamma} = 0$) — at least 1 mark is reserved for this.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert) model** with a diagram. Derive its relaxation modulus expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mozzarella cheese sample is tested in stress relaxation. At constant strain $\gamma_0 = 0.01$, the stress is fitted by:

$$\sigma(t) = 30 \cdot e^{-t/2} + 50 \cdot e^{-t/20} \quad \text{(in Pa)}$$

Determine: (i) the imposed strain magnitude, (ii) the two relaxation times, (iii) the two modulus contributions, and (iv) the zero-time stress and equilibrium stress. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wiechert Model (7 marks):**

- A generalized Maxwell model is $N$ Maxwell elements + a free spring, all in **parallel**. — **(1 mark)**
- Diagram: parallel branches each with spring $G_i$ in series with dashpot $\eta_i$, plus equilibrium spring $G_e$. — **(1.5 marks)**
- Strain is uniform across all parallel branches: $\gamma_0$ is the same in each. — **(0.5 mark)**
- Stress in branch $i$: $\sigma_i(t) = G_i \gamma_0 e^{-t/\tau_i}$ where $\tau_i = \eta_i/G_i$. — **(1.5 marks)**
- Equilibrium spring: $\sigma_e = G_e \gamma_0$. — **(0.5 mark)**
- Superposition (parallel): $\sigma(t) = G_e \gamma_0 + \sum G_i \gamma_0 e^{-t/\tau_i}$ — **(1 mark)**
- Relaxation modulus: $G(t) = G_e + \sum G_i e^{-t/\tau_i}$ — **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical identification (7 marks):**

Given: $\sigma(t) = 30 e^{-t/2} + 50 e^{-t/20}$ Pa.

**(i) The imposed strain** — Note that $\sigma(t) = \gamma_0 [G_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} + G_2 e^{-t/\tau_2}]$, so the leading coefficients $30$ and $50$ represent $G_i \cdot \gamma_0$. Since no equilibrium term is present, $G_e = 0$ and the model has two branches only. The strain $\gamma_0$ is given as **0.01** (dimensionless). **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Relaxation times** are read directly from the exponents:

$$\tau_1 = 2 \text{ s}, \quad \tau_2 = 20 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) Modulus contributions:**

$$G_1 = \frac{30}{\gamma_0} = \frac{30}{0.01} = 3000 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$G_2 = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(iv) Zero-time stress** ($t = 0$): $e^0 = 1$:

$$\sigma(0) = 30 + 50 = 80 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Equilibrium stress** ($t \to \infty$): all exponentials → 0:

$$\sigma_\infty = 0 \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Question B):**
> 1. **Reading the relaxation time incorrectly** — students sometimes invert the exponent. Remember: $e^{-t/\tau}$ means $\tau$ is the **denominator** inside the exponent. Here, $-t/2$ means $\tau_1 = 2$ s, NOT $0.5$ s.
> 2. **Forgetting that $30$ and $50$ are $\gamma_0 \cdot G_i$, not $G_i$ alone** — dividing by $\gamma_0 = 0.01$ is mandatory. Marks lost here are the most common in KTU valuation reports.
> 3. **Reporting equilibrium stress as nonzero** when no $G_e$ term appears in the equation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Stress relaxation = constant strain imposed, stress decays with time.
- **Relaxation Modulus:** $G(t) = \sigma(t) / \gamma_0$ — fundamental material function in linear viscoelasticity.
- **Single Maxwell:** $\sigma(t) = E\gamma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ and $G(t) = E e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **Relaxation Time:** $\tau = \eta / E$ — time for stress to drop to $\approx 36.8\%$ of $\sigma_0$.
- **Generalized Maxwell (Wiechert):** $G(t) = G_e + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_i e^{-t/\tau_i}$ — multiple parallel Maxwell branches plus equilibrium spring.
- **Zero-time modulus:** $G_0 = G_e + \sum G_i$ (sum of all spring contributions).
- **Equilibrium modulus:** $G_e$ — long-time residual stiffness (zero for single Maxwell).
- **Boltzmann Superposition:** $\sigma(t) = \int_0^t G(t-t') \dot{\gamma}(t') dt'$ — links stress relaxation to all other linear viscoelastic responses.
- **Boltzmann → Master Curve:** Stress relaxation data can be transformed into frequency-domain $G'(\omega)$, $G''(\omega)$ via Fourier transform (not the exam focus, but conceptually important).
- **Food applications:** Dough, cheese, meat, biopolymer gels, edible films, 3D-printed food.
- **Deborah Number:** $De = \tau_{\text{observation}} / \tau_{\text{material}}$ — context for whether food behaves elastically or viscously.
- **Always state units** explicitly in numerical KTU answers (Pa, s).
- **Use `\mid` or `\vert`** (not bare `|`) in any LaTeX inside markdown tables to avoid syntax breakage.
- **Deborah Number reference:** Named after the prophetess Deborah; a $De \gg 1$ material behaves like a solid, $De \ll 1$ like a liquid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Tests (Oscillatory Shear Tests):** A class of rheological measurements in which a sinusoidal (oscillating) strain or stress is applied to a material and the resulting stress or strain response is monitored as a function of time and frequency. The material is characterized in the **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVE)** where the response is independent of the magnitude of deformation but dependent only on the time-scale (frequency) of probing.

In food rheology, dynamic tests are the **gold standard** for characterizing the viscoelastic nature of structured foods — doughs, gels, cheeses, chocolates, protein solutions, and biopolymer melts — without destroying their internal microstructure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Jelly on a Plate" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **bowl of jelly** gently back and forth with your finger in a slow, rhythmic motion:

- If you push **very slowly** (low frequency, ω → 0) — the jelly has enough time to **flow** like a viscous liquid. It will permanently deform and not bounce back.
- If you push **very rapidly** (high frequency, ω → ∞) — the jelly behaves like a **solid rubber ball** because its internal network (cross-linked pectin/protein matrix) doesn't have time to rearrange. It stores the energy and pushes back elastically.
- At **intermediate frequencies**, you get a **mixed response** — partly elastic (spring-like) and partly viscous (dashpot-like).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world food analogy:** Stretch a piece of **pizza cheese** slowly → it flows (viscous). Stretch it quickly → it tears and snaps (elastic). A **dynamic test** mathematically quantifies this exact behavior at infinitesimally small strains so the structure is preserved.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Dynamic Test

| Pillar | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Modulus** | $G'$ | Energy **stored** elastically per cycle (solid-like behavior) |
| **Loss Modulus** | $G''$ | Energy **dissipated** viscously per cycle (liquid-like behavior) |
| **Loss Tangent** | $\tan \delta$ | Ratio $G''/G'$ → damping character of the material |

All measurements are performed inside the **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVE)**, typically with strains of **0.1 % to 1 %**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Oscillatory Shear

In a dynamic test, a **sinusoidal strain** is imposed:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

where:
- $\gamma_0$ = **strain amplitude** (dimensionless)
- $\omega$ = **angular frequency** in **rad/s**
- $t$ = time in seconds

For a **purely elastic solid** (Hookean), the stress is **in-phase** (90° phase lead):

$$\sigma(t) = G' \cdot \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

For a **purely viscous liquid** (Newtonian), the stress is **out-of-phase** (90° phase lag):

$$\sigma(t) = G'' \cdot \gamma_0 \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$
