For a **viscoelastic material**, the response is a **composite** with a phase shift $\delta$ between $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$:

$$\sigma(t) = G' \cdot \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \cdot \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

This is the **master constitutive equation** of dynamic rheology.

## 2.2 Why Use Complex Notation? (Euler's Form)

Euler's formula $e^{i\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + i \sin(\omega t)$ lets us combine the in-phase and out-of-phase components into a single **complex modulus**:

$$G^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\gamma^*(t)} = G'(\omega) + i \, G''(\omega)$$

where:
- $G'(\omega)$ = **Storage (Elastic) Modulus** — the **real part** of $G^*$
- $G''(\omega)$ = **Loss (Viscous) Modulus** — the **imaginary part** of $G^*$
- $i = \sqrt{-1}$

The **magnitude** of the complex modulus is:

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$$

The **loss tangent** (damping factor) is:

$$\tan \delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Rule:** A material behaves as a **solid** when $G' > G''$ (i.e., $\tan \delta < 1$).
> A material behaves as a **liquid** when $G'' > G'$ (i.e., $\tan \delta > 1$).
> The **crossover point** ($G' = G''$) marks the **gel point** or **sol–gel transition**.

## 2.3 Complex Viscosity

The complex viscosity $\eta^*$ is the dynamic analog of steady-shear viscosity:

$$\eta^*(\omega) = \frac{G^*(\omega)}{i\omega} = \eta'(\omega) - i\,\eta''(\omega)$$

where:
- $\eta'(\omega) = G''/\omega$ = **viscous component** (out-of-phase)
- $\eta''(\omega) = G'/\omega$ = **elastic component** (in-phase)

Magnitude of complex viscosity:

$$\vert \eta^* \vert = \frac{\sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}}{\omega}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU University Exam High-Yield — memorize the table before any Part A/B question on dynamic tests.**

| # | Formula | Definition / Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Imposed sinusoidal strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma(t) = G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$ | Viscoelastic stress response | **Pa** |
| 3 | $G^* = G' + i G''$ | Complex modulus | **Pa** |
| 4 | $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$ | Magnitude of complex modulus | **Pa** |
| 5 | $\tan \delta = G''/G'$ | Loss tangent (damping factor) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta^* = G^*/(i\omega)$ | Complex viscosity | **Pa·s** |
| 7 | $\eta' = G''/\omega$ | Dynamic viscosity (loss) | **Pa·s** |
| 8 | $\eta'' = G'/\omega$ | Dynamic rigidity (storage) | **Pa·s** |
| 9 | $\vert \eta^* \vert = \vert G^* \vert / \omega$ | Magnitude of complex viscosity | **Pa·s** |
| 10 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency conversion | **rad/s** |
| 11 | $G' \propto \omega^n$ (for gels) | Power-law gel scaling | **Pa** |
| 12 | $\eta^*(\omega) = \eta(\dot\gamma)$ at $\omega = \dot\gamma$ | **Cox–Merz Rule** | **Pa·s** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food formulation:** Predicting mouthfeel, spreadability, and texture of butter, margarine, and cream.
- **Gelation studies:** Tracking the **gel point** during pectin/protein gel formation (yogurt, tofu, surimi).
- **Stability assessment:** Time-sweep tests detect structural breakdown in emulsions (mayonnaise, sauces).
- **Quality control:** Verifying chocolate tempering and cocoa butter crystallization in industry.
- **Process engineering:** Master curves allow prediction of food behavior during extrusion, pumping, and storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Strain Input to Viscoelastic Stress Output

**Step 1 — Impose a sinusoidal strain** on the sample at angular frequency $\omega$:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — The strain rate (relevant to the viscous contribution)** is the time derivative:

$$\dot\gamma(t) = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \gamma_0 \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Boltzmann Superposition Principle** — the stress response is a linear sum of:
- An **elastic** contribution proportional to strain,
- A **viscous** contribution proportional to strain rate.

$$\sigma(t) = G' \cdot \gamma(t) + \eta' \cdot \dot\gamma(t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $\gamma(t)$ and $\dot\gamma(t)$:

$$\sigma(t) = G' \cdot \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + \eta' \cdot \gamma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the loss modulus** through the identity $\eta' \cdot \omega \equiv G''$:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \underbrace{G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)}_{\text{Elastic (in-phase)}} + \underbrace{G'' \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)}_{\text{Viscous (out-of-phase)}}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU equation** for dynamic oscillatory response.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Complex Modulus Identity

**Step 1 — Write the strain as a complex exponential:**

$$\gamma^*(t) = \gamma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}$$

**Step 2 — The stress is a complex response with phase lag $\delta$:**

$$\sigma^*(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{i(\omega t + \delta)}$$

**Step 3 — Define the complex modulus as the ratio:**

$$G^* = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\gamma^*(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} \, e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 4 — Expand using Euler's formula:**

$$G^* = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\left[\cos\delta + i \sin\delta\right]$$

**Step 5 — Match the real and imaginary parts** to the storage and loss moduli:

$$\boxed{G' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta \qquad G'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta}$$

Hence:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert G^* \vert = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dynamic Test Types and Their Use

| Test Type | Variable Swept | Typical Outcome | Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude Sweep** (Strain sweep) | $\gamma_0$ at fixed $\omega$ | Determines LVE limit; finds $\gamma_c$ (critical strain) | Identifying the structural yield point of yogurt, dough |
| **Frequency Sweep** | $\omega$ at fixed $\gamma_0$ (in LVE) | Plots of $G'(\omega), G''(\omega), \eta^*(\omega)$ | Comparing texture of cheese vs. butter |
| **Time Sweep** | $t$ at fixed $\omega, \gamma_0$ | Monitors $G'(t), G''(t)$ over time | Gelation kinetics of pectin/protein |
| **Temperature Sweep** | $T$ at fixed $\omega, \gamma_0$ | Detects melting/crystallization transitions | Chocolate tempering, fat crystallization |
| **Creep-Recovery** (quasi-dynamic) | Step-stress | Compliance $J(t)$ | Long-term behavior of dough during proofing |
| **Stress Relaxation** | Step-strain | Relaxation modulus $G(t)$ | Cheese stretching, bread freshness |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A food gel is subjected to a dynamic strain $\gamma(t) = 0.01 \sin(10 t)$ (with $t$ in seconds). The measured stress is $\sigma(t) = 250 \sin(10t) + 100 \cos(10t)$ Pa. Calculate $G'$, $G''$, $\vert G^* \vert$, and $\tan\delta$.

**Step 1 — Identify $\gamma_0$ and $\omega$:**

From $\gamma(t) = 0.01 \sin(10t)$: $\quad \gamma_0 = 0.01$, $\quad \omega = 10$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Compare to canonical stress equation:**

The given stress is $\sigma(t) = 250 \sin(10t) + 100 \cos(10t)$.

Match with $\sigma(t) = G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$:

- $G' \gamma_0 = 250 \Rightarrow G' = 250 / 0.01 = 25{,}000$ **Pa**
- $G'' \gamma_0 = 100 \Rightarrow G'' = 100 / 0.01 = 10{,}000$ **Pa**

**Step 3 — Magnitude of complex modulus:**

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(25000)^2 + (10000)^2} = \sqrt{6.25 \times 10^8 + 1.0 \times 10^8}$$

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{7.25 \times 10^8} = 2.6926 \times 10^4 \approx 26{,}926 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Loss tangent:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{10000}{25000} = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

Since $G' (25000) > G'' (10000)$ and $\tan\delta = 0.40 < 1$, the gel is **predominantly elastic / solid-like**, but with a notable viscous component. This is typical of a **weak biopolymer gel** (e.g., low-concentration agar or pectin gel).

**Step 6 — Phase angle in degrees:**

$$\delta = \arctan(0.40) = 21.8^\circ$$

A phase angle of $21.8^\circ$ is characteristic of a **strong but not perfectly elastic** gel network.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Block-Level Architecture of a Dynamic Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillatory Drive Motor] --> B[Force / Torque Transducer]
    B --> C[Geometry Cone Plate Parallel Plate Couette]
    C --> D[Food Sample in LVE Region]
    D --> E[Strain / Stress Transducer]
    E --> F[Signal Processor Phase Detection]
    F --> G[Computation of Gprime and Gdoubleprime]
    G --> H[Frequency Sweep Output]
    G --> I[Amplitude Sweep Output]
    G --> J[Time Sweep Output]
    G --> K[Master Curve Time Temperature Superposition]
    H --> L[Interpretation Solid Liquid Gel]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Viscoelastic Response Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin Dynamic Test] --> Q1{Is strain in LVE region?}
    Q1 -- No --> Warn[Warning: Nonlinear regime, data invalid]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Compare Gprime and Gdoubleprime}
    Q2 -- Gprime greater than Gdoubleprime --> Solid[Predominantly Elastic Solid like Gel Network]
    Q2 -- Gdoubleprime greater than Gprime --> Liquid[Predominantly Viscous Liquid like Solution]
    Q2 -- Gprime equals Gdoubleprime --> GP[Gel Point Sol Gel Transition]
    Solid --> S1[Strong gel Gprime plateau independent of omega]
    Solid --> S2[Weak gel Gprime increases with omega]
    Liquid --> L1[Entangled polymer solution]
    Liquid --> L2[Dilute biopolymer solution]
    GP --> G1[Critical gelation frequency]
    GP --> G2[Crossover point at omega c]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Master Curve Construction via TTS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Frequency Sweep at T1] --> C[Shift along omega axis]
    B[Raw Frequency Sweep at T2] --> C
    D[Raw Frequency Sweep at T3] --> C
    C --> E[Superimposed Master Curve]
    E --> F[Shift factor aT]
    F --> G{WLF Equation for T greater than Tg}
    F --> H[Arrhenius Equation for T less than Tg]
    G --> E
    H --> E
    E --> I[Predict long term behavior from short term data]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description: Cox–Merz Rule Visual Mapping

| Test Type | Domain | Visualization |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady Shear** | Viscosity $\eta$ vs. shear rate $\dot\gamma$ | A monotonic decay curve in log-log space |
| **Dynamic (Oscillatory)** | $\vert \eta^* \vert$ vs. angular frequency $\omega$ | A monotonic curve, often superimposable on steady-shear data |
| **Cox–Merz Superposition** | $\eta(\dot\gamma) = \vert \eta^*(\omega) \vert$ at $\dot\gamma = \omega$ | The two curves collapse onto a single master curve for many foods |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Cox–Merz rule** is an empirical correlation valid for many **homogeneous** food systems (e.g., polymer solutions, melted chocolate) but **fails** for highly structured foods with yield stress (e.g., mayonnaise, ketchup, gels).

## 4.5 Mechanical Analogs Linked to Dynamic Behavior

| Model | Schematic | $G'(\omega)$ Behavior | $G''(\omega)$ Behavior | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maxwell** (spring + dashpot in series) | Spring S in series with Dashpot D | Increases as $\omega^2$ | Increases as $\omega$ | Dilute polymer solution |
| **Kelvin–Voigt** (spring + dashpot in parallel) | Spring S in parallel with Dashpot D | Constant plateau | $\omega$ dependent minimum | Cross-linked rubbery food |
| **Burgers** (Maxwell + Kelvin in series) | Maxwell block in series with Kelvin block | Combined response | Combined response | Dough, bread dough |
| **Standard Linear Solid** (Zener) | Maxwell in parallel with spring | Plateau + high-ω rise | Maximum at intermediate $\omega$ | Many food gels |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define storage modulus $G'$ and loss modulus $G''$. What is the physical significance of their ratio $\tan\delta$?**

**Model Answer:**

**Storage modulus $G'$:** The real part of the complex modulus $G^*$, representing the energy stored elastically per cycle of oscillation. It quantifies the **solid-like** (elastic) character of the material. Units: **Pa**.

**Loss modulus $G''$:** The imaginary part of $G^*$, representing the energy dissipated as heat per cycle. It quantifies the **liquid-like** (viscous) character. Units: **Pa**.

**Loss tangent $\tan\delta$:** The ratio $G''/G'$. It is the **damping factor** of the material. A value $<1$ indicates a solid-dominated response; $>1$ indicates a liquid-dominated response; $=1$ defines the **gel point**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q2. What is the Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVE), and why must dynamic tests be performed inside it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **LVE region** is the range of strains (or stresses) over which the structure of the material remains intact and the rheological response is **linear** — meaning $G'$ and $G''$ are independent of strain amplitude.

Dynamic tests **must** be performed inside the LVE because:
1. The **superposition principle** (Boltzmann) holds only in linearity.
2. The derived moduli $G'$ and $G''$ become meaningful material properties only in the LVE.
3. Outside the LVE, the microstructure is **destroyed** progressively, leading to non-linear responses and irreproducible data.

The LVE is identified by an **amplitude (strain) sweep**, and the boundary is the **critical strain** $\gamma_c$ (typically 0.1 % to 1 % for foods).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the stress response $\sigma(t)$ for a viscoelastic food material subjected to a sinusoidal strain input. Clearly define the storage modulus, loss modulus, and complex modulus. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A wheat flour dough is tested dynamically. The imposed strain is $\gamma(t) = 0.005 \sin(6.28 t)$. The measured stress response is $\sigma(t) = 1500 \sin(6.28t) + 600 \cos(6.28t)$ Pa. Compute $G'$, $G''$, $\vert G^* \vert$, $\tan\delta$, and interpret whether the dough is solid-like or liquid-like at this frequency. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Imposed strain input** [1 mark]:

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Strain rate (for viscous contribution)** [1 mark]:

$$\dot\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Viscoelastic stress by superposition** [2 marks]:

$$\sigma(t) = G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + \eta' \gamma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Using $G'' = \eta' \omega$:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)}$$

**Step 4 — Definitions** [3 marks]:

- $G' = $ Storage modulus — real part of $G^*$ — elastic energy stored.
- $G'' = $ Loss modulus — imaginary part of $G^*$ — viscous energy dissipated.
- $G^* = G' + iG'' = $ Complex modulus combining both.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify $\gamma_0$ and $\omega$** [1 mark]:

$\gamma_0 = 0.005$, $\omega = 6.28$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Match to canonical stress form** [2 marks]:

- $G' \gamma_0 = 1500 \Rightarrow G' = 1500 / 0.005 = 3.0 \times 10^5$ **Pa**
- $G'' \gamma_0 = 600 \Rightarrow G'' = 600 / 0.005 = 1.2 \times 10^5$ **Pa**

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $G^*$** [1 mark]:

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(3.0 \times 10^5)^2 + (1.2 \times 10^5)^2} = \sqrt{9.0 \times 10^{10} + 1.44 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{10.44 \times 10^{10}} = 3.231 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Loss tangent** [1 mark]:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{3.0 \times 10^5} = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation** [2 marks]:

Since $G' > G''$ and $\tan\delta = 0.40 < 1$, the dough exhibits a **predominantly solid-like (elastic)** response at this frequency, consistent with a developed gluten network. The phase angle $\delta = \arctan(0.40) \approx 21.8^\circ$ confirms a **moderately elastic** system typical of optimally mixed bread dough.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVE)** and describe the **amplitude sweep** test used to identify it. Include a sketch of $G'$ vs. strain showing the LVE plateau and the onset of non-linearity. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** With the help of a frequency sweep, explain the differences between a **strong gel**, a **weak gel**, and a **dilute polymer solution**. Use typical sketches of $G'(\omega)$ and $G''(\omega)$ to support your answer. **[(7 marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of LVE** [2 marks]:

The LVE is the strain range over which $G'$ and $G''$ are **constant** (independent of $\gamma_0$). Within this region, the Boltzmann superposition principle holds and the microstructure is undisturbed.

**Step 2 — Amplitude sweep procedure** [2 marks]:

- Fix the frequency (typically $\omega = 1$ or $10$ rad/s).
- Stepwise increase the strain amplitude $\gamma_0$ from $0.01\,\%$ to $100\,\%$.
- Record $G'(\gamma_0)$ and $G''(\gamma_0)$.

**Step 3 — Sketch description** [3 marks]:

```
G' (Pa)
  |__________ (LVE plateau, G' constant)
  |          \
  |           \   (non-linear / structure breakdown)
  |            \__________
  |________________________→ strain γ₀
        ↑
     γ_c (critical strain)
```

- Below $\gamma_c$: $G' = $ constant (LVE plateau).
- Above $\gamma_c$: $G'$ drops sharply, marking **yielding** and structural breakdown.
- The same pattern is mirrored in $G''$, often with a small peak at $\gamma_c$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Strong gel** [2 marks]:

$G'$ and $G''$ are **frequency-independent** (parallel horizontal lines) with $G' > G''$. Examples: firm cheese, hard gelatin gel, set yogurt.

**Step 2 — Weak gel** [2 marks]:

Both $G'$ and $G''$ show a **weak power-law dependence** on $\omega$ ($G' \propto \omega^n$, $G'' \propto \omega^n$, with $0 < n < 1$). $G' > G''$ throughout. Example: skim milk gel, low-fat yogurt, tomato paste.

**Step 3 — Dilute polymer solution** [2 marks]:

At low $\omega$: $G'' > G'$ (viscous terminal behavior). At high $\omega$: $G' > G''$ (elastic plateau). The **crossover** $G' = G''$ marks the characteristic relaxation time $\tau = 1/\omega_c$. Example: dilute xanthan or guar solution.

**Step 4 — Schematic comparison** [1 mark]:

| System | Low $\omega$ | High $\omega$ | Crossover |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strong gel | $G' > G''$ | $G' > G''$ (both flat) | None |
| Weak gel | $G' > G''$ | $G' > G''$ (sloped) | None |
| Dilute solution | $G'' > G'$ | $G' > G''$ | Yes, at $\omega_c$ |

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners flag and deduct marks for:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $G'$ with $\eta'$.** $G'$ is a modulus (Pa), not a viscosity (Pa·s). Do not write $G'$ in Pa·s.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $\omega$.** Always state $\omega$ in **rad/s** and convert from $f$ (Hz) using $\omega = 2\pi f$.
> 3. **Not specifying the LVE condition.** Any dynamic test answer that omits "performed within the LVE" loses at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing $G''$ and $\tan\delta$ definitions.** $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ — examiners check the numerator/denominator order strictly.
> 5. **Calculating $\delta$ in radians, not degrees.** When asked for the phase angle in degrees, always use $\arctan(G''/G') \times (180/\pi)$.
> 6. **Forgetting the Cox–Merz rule caveat:** It is an **empirical** rule valid for homogeneous systems only — it **fails for structured foods with yield stress**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — Dynamic Tests**

- **Dynamic test** = oscillatory (sinusoidal) strain or stress imposed on a sample; measured response gives $G'$ and $G''$.
- **Three moduli to master:** $G'$ (storage, solid-like), $G''$ (loss, liquid-like), $G^* = G' + iG''$ (complex).
- **Loss tangent:** $\tan\delta = G''/G'$. Values $<1$ → solid; $>1$ → liquid; $=1$ → gel point.
- **Phase angle $\delta$** is the lag between stress and strain. $0^\circ$ = Hookean solid; $90^\circ$ = Newtonian liquid; intermediate = viscoelastic.
- **LVE (Linear Viscoelastic Region)** must be respected; identified via an **amplitude sweep** at fixed $\omega$.
- **Critical strain $\gamma_c$** marks the LVE boundary (typical food range: **0.1 % – 1 %**).
- **Frequency sweep** reveals time-scale dependence: strong gels are frequency-independent, weak gels follow power laws, solutions show a crossover.
- **Time-temperature superposition (TTS)** builds **master curves** using **WLF** ($T > T_g$) or **Arrhenius** ($T < T_g$) shift factors.
- **Cox–Merz rule:** $\eta(\dot\gamma) = \vert \eta^*(\omega) \vert$ at $\dot\gamma = \omega$ — empirical, valid for homogeneous systems only.
- **Canonical stress equation (MEMORIZE):** $\sigma(t) = G' \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)$.
- **Conversions:** $\omega = 2\pi f$; $\eta' = G''/\omega$; $\eta'' = G'/\omega$.
- **Mechanical analogs:** Maxwell → liquid-like relaxation; Kelvin–Voigt → solid-like delayed elasticity; Burgers → combined (dough).
- **Food examples:** Strong gel (cheese), weak gel (yogurt), polymer solution (dilute xanthan), viscoelastic liquid (chocolate melt).
- **Master equation for modulus magnitude:** $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$.
- **Golden exam tip:** Always state **units explicitly** (Pa for moduli, Pa·s for viscosities, rad/s for $\omega$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscoelastic Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscoelastic Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Viscoelastic behaviour** is the time-dependent mechanical response of a material that exhibits combined characteristics of both **purely elastic solids** (energy storage, Hookean) and **purely viscous fluids** (energy dissipation, Newtonian). When a viscoelastic material is subjected to an applied stress or strain, the resultant deformation (or stress) is a function of both **magnitude** and **time (or frequency)**.

Mathematically, the constitutive equation for a linear viscoelastic material is expressed as:

$$
\sigma(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} G(t - \tau) \, \frac{d\gamma(\tau)}{d\tau} \, d\tau
$$

where:
- $\sigma(t)$ = shear stress at time $t$
- $G(t-\tau)$ = relaxation modulus
- $\gamma(\tau)$ = shear strain at past time $\tau$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Foods are rarely purely viscous or purely elastic. Most structured foods (gels, doughs, cheeses, jams, meat batters) exhibit **viscoelastic behaviour**, which must be characterized using both transient (creep, stress relaxation) and dynamic (oscillatory) tests.

## 1.2 Two Limiting Ideal Responses

| Ideal Type | Model Element | Constitutive Equation | Energy Behaviour |
|------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Elastic Solid (Hookean)** | Spring | $\sigma = G \cdot \gamma$ | Stores energy completely |
| **Viscous Fluid (Newtonian)** | Dashpot | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Dissipates energy completely |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Silly Putty & Bread Dough" Example**
>
> - Imagine stretching a **rubber band** (purely elastic): when you pull and release, it snaps back instantly with all energy returned. This is **Hookean** behaviour.
> - Imagine pouring **honey** (purely viscous): it flows forever and never recovers. The energy is lost as heat. This is **Newtonian** behaviour.
> - Now take **bread dough** or **silly putty**: pull it slowly — it stretches and recovers. Pull it fast — it snaps (more elastic). Push it gently — it flows slowly (more viscous). **The response depends on TIME.** This is **viscoelasticity**.
>
> In foods: **Cheese spreads** flow slowly under their own weight but recover partially when spread (viscoelastic). **Egg white** behaves elastically during rapid whipping but flows viscously when left standing.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Relaxation Modulus $G(t)$** — measured in **Pa (Pascal)**
- **Creep Compliance $J(t)$** — measured in **Pa⁻¹ (1/Pa)**
- **Storage Modulus $G'$** — measured in **Pa**
- **Loss Modulus $G''$** — measured in **Pa**
- **Complex Modulus $G^*$** — measured in **Pa**
- **Loss Tangent $\tan \delta$** — dimensionless
- **Complex Viscosity $\eta^*$** — measured in **Pa·s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR)

The **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVR)** is the range of stress/strain within which the material's structure is not irreversibly disrupted. Within the LVR, the moduli ($G'$, $G''$) are independent of strain amplitude. **All oscillatory measurements for viscoelastic characterization MUST be conducted within the LVR.**

## 2.2 Mechanical Models of Viscoelasticity

### (A) Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in SERIES)

The Maxwell model represents **stress relaxation** behaviour.

Constitutive equation:
$$
\sigma + \frac{\eta}{G} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = \eta \frac{d\gamma}{dt}
$$

- **Relaxation time** $\lambda = \eta / G$ (seconds)
- Under constant strain $\gamma_0$, stress decays exponentially:
$$
\sigma(t) = G \cdot \gamma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\lambda}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Maxwell model **fails to describe creep recovery** because it predicts complete flow (no recovery). Best suited for **uncross-linked polymer melts and dilute solutions**.

### (B) Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in PARALLEL)

Constitutive equation:
$$
\sigma(t) = G \cdot \gamma(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{dt}
$$

Under constant stress $\sigma_0$, the creep response is:
$$
\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G}\left[1 - e^{-t/\lambda}\right]
$$

where retardation time $\lambda = \eta / G$.

> [!NOTE]
> The Kelvin–Voigt model **predicts full recovery** upon stress removal but **cannot represent stress relaxation** at $t = 0$. Best suited for **cross-linked gels and solid-like foods**.

### (C) Burgers Model (Maxwell ∥ Kelvin–Voigt in SERIES)

Combines both models in series to capture instantaneous elasticity, delayed elasticity, viscous flow, and elastic recovery:

$$
\gamma(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{G_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{G_2}\left[1 - e^{-t/\lambda_2}\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_3}t
$$

Four parameters: $G_1$, $G_2$, $\eta_2$, $\eta_3$.

## 2.3 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Testing — The KTU Gold Standard

When a sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied within the LVR, the resulting stress is:
$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)
$$

where $\delta$ is the **phase angle** ($0^\circ$ = purely elastic; $90^\circ$ = purely viscous).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|-----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| Complex Modulus | $G^* = \sigma_0 / \gamma_0$ | Total stiffness | Pa |
| Storage Modulus | $G' = \vert G^* \vert \cos\delta$ | Elastic (energy stored) | Pa |
| Loss Modulus | $G'' = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta$ | Viscous (energy lost) | Pa |
| Loss Tangent | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | Solid vs liquid character | dimensionless |
| Complex Viscosity | $\eta^* = G^*/\omega$ | Frequency-dependent viscosity | Pa·s |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\eta' = G''/\omega$ | — | Pa·s |
| Relaxation Time | $\lambda = \eta / G$ | Characteristic time | s |
| Retardation Time | $\lambda = \eta / G$ | Characteristic time | s |

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of $\tan\delta$

- $\tan \delta \ll 1$ → **Elastic-dominated** (gel, solid food)
- $\tan \delta \gg 1$ → **Viscous-dominated** (liquid food)
- $\tan \delta = 1$ → **Gel point / crossover point** (used to determine gelation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Viscoelastic characterization is critical in food process engineering for designing **mixing operations, pumping systems, extrusion dies, and texture prediction**. For example, knowing $G'$ vs frequency helps determine whether a chocolate melt will sag during enrobing, or whether a dough can withstand mechanical handling in a continuous bread line.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress Relaxation in a Maxwell Element

**Step 1 — Setup:**
A Maxwell element consists of a Hookean spring (modulus $G$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected **in series**. Both experience the **same stress** $\sigma$, while the total strain is the **sum of individual strains**:

$$
\gamma_{total} = \gamma_{spring} + \gamma_{dashpot}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each contribution:**
For the spring (Hooke's law):
$$
\gamma_{spring} = \frac{\sigma}{G}
$$

For the dashpot (Newton's law):
$$
\dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta} \implies \gamma_{dashpot} = \int \frac{\sigma}{\eta}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the total strain:**
$$
\dot{\gamma}_{total} = \dot{\gamma}_{spring} + \dot{\gamma}_{dashpot} = \frac{1}{G}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply constant strain condition (stress relaxation):**
Under a step strain $\gamma_0$ held constant, $\dot{\gamma}_{total} = 0$:
$$
0 = \frac{1}{G}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the first-order ODE:**
Rearranging:
$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\sigma
$$
Integrating with initial condition $\sigma(0) = G\gamma_0$:
$$
\sigma(t) = G\gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \frac{\eta}{G}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Maxwell element exhibits **exponential stress decay** characterized by the single relaxation time $\lambda$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of $G'$, $G''$, and $\tan\delta$ from Oscillatory Input

**Step 1 — Apply sinusoidal strain:**
$$
\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Express strain rate:**
$$
\dot{\gamma}(t) = \gamma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Maxwell constitutive equation:**
$$
\sigma + \lambda \dot{\sigma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}
$$

Differentiating the assumed stress:
$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)
$$
$$
\dot{\sigma}(t) = \sigma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t + \delta)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$
\sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta) + \lambda \sigma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t + \delta) = \eta \gamma_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand using angle identities and equate coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$:**

After expansion and coefficient matching:

$$
\sigma_0 \cos\delta - \lambda \sigma_0 \omega \sin\delta = 0
$$
$$
\sigma_0 \sin\delta + \lambda \sigma_0 \omega \cos\delta = \eta \gamma_0 \omega
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the simultaneous equations:**
$$
\tan\delta = \frac{1}{\lambda \omega}
$$
$$
\sigma_0 = \frac{\eta \gamma_0 \omega}{\sqrt{1 + (\lambda \omega)^2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Express moduli:**
$$
G' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta = \frac{G(\lambda \omega)^2}{1 + (\lambda \omega)^2}
$$
$$
G'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta = \frac{G(\lambda \omega)}{1 + (\lambda \omega)^2}
$$
$$
\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{1}{\lambda \omega}
$$

**Step 8 — Limiting cases verification:**

| Condition | Result | Behaviour |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| $\omega \to 0$ (low frequency) | $G' \to 0$, $G'' \to 0$ | Liquid-like (flow) |
| $\omega \to \infty$ (high frequency) | $G' \to G$, $G'' \to 0$ | Solid-like (elastic) |
| $\omega = 1/\lambda$ | $G' = G''$ | **Crossover point** |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Maxwell viscoelastic food material has $G = 1000$ Pa and $\eta = 100$ Pa·s. A step strain of $\gamma_0 = 0.05$ is applied at $t = 0$.

**(a) Calculate the relaxation time:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{\eta}{G} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

**(b) Calculate the stress at $t = 0.2$ s:**
$$
\sigma(0.2) = G\gamma_0 \, e^{-t/\lambda} = (1000)(0.05)\, e^{-0.2/0.1} = 50 \cdot e^{-2}
$$
$$
\sigma(0.2) = 50 \times 0.1353 = 6.77 \text{ Pa}
$$

**(c) Time for stress to drop to 1% of initial value:**
$$
0.01 = e^{-t/\lambda} \implies t = -\lambda \ln(0.01) = (0.1)(4.605) = 0.461 \text{ s}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanical Model Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Stress or Strain] --> B[Linear Viscoelastic Region LVR Check]
    B --> C{Test Type}
    C -->|Transient| D[Creep and Stress Relaxation]
    C -->|Dynamic| E[Oscillatory Shear]
    D --> F[Maxwell Model Series Spring Dashpot]
    D --> G[Kelvin Voigt Model Parallel Spring Dashpot]
    D --> H[Burgers Model Maxwell in Series with Kelvin Voigt]
    E --> I[Sinusoidal Strain gamma0 sin omega t]
    I --> J[Storage Modulus G Prime Elastic Response]
    I --> K[Loss Modulus G Double Prime Viscous Response]
    J --> L[Complex Modulus G Star and tan delta]
    K --> L
    F --> M[Relaxation Time lambda equals eta over G]
    G --> N[Retardation Time lambda equals eta over G]
    L --> O[Material Classification Solid Like Gel vs Liquid Like Sol]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Sweep Interpretation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Frequency Sweep] --> B[Low Frequency Region]
    B --> C[G Double Prime Dominant]
    C --> D[Liquid Like Behaviour]
    D --> E[Material Flows Over Long Times]
    E --> F[High Frequency Region]
    F --> G[G Prime Dominant]
    G --> H[Solid Like Behaviour]
    H --> I[Material Resists Deformation Rapidly]
    I --> J[Crossover Frequency]
    J --> K[G Prime equals G Double Prime]
    K --> L[Identify Gel Point or Characteristic Relaxation]
```

## 4.3 Stress Relaxation vs Creep Recovery Comparison

| Test Mode | Input | Output Measured | Best Characterized By |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Stress Relaxation | Constant strain $\gamma_0$ | Decay of stress $\sigma(t)$ | Maxwell model |
| Creep Recovery | Constant stress $\sigma_0$ | Growth of strain $\gamma(t)$ | Kelvin–Voigt model |
| Dynamic Oscillation | Sinusoidal strain | $G'$, $G''$, $\tan\delta$ | Frequency-domain moduli |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note (Fallback Rationale):** Since viscoelastic behaviour involves abstract time-dependent mechanical response rather than physical hardware, the Mermaid block diagrams above map the **functional architecture** of analytical pathways instead of attempting physical instrumentation schematics. This preserves KTU conceptual clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define viscoelastic behaviour. How does it differ from purely elastic and purely viscous behaviour?**

**Model Answer:**
Viscoelastic behaviour is the time-dependent mechanical response of a material that exhibits properties intermediate between a Hookean elastic solid (energy storage, $\sigma = G\gamma$) and a Newtonian viscous fluid (energy dissipation, $\sigma = \eta\dot{\gamma}$). Unlike a purely elastic solid (instantaneous recoverable deformation) or purely viscous fluid (irreversible flow), a viscoelastic material shows partial energy storage and partial energy dissipation, with the response dependent on the rate or duration of loading. Foods like cheese, dough, and gels are typical viscoelastic materials.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the significance of the loss tangent ($\tan\delta$) in characterizing viscoelastic foods?**

**Model Answer:**
The loss tangent $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ is a dimensionless ratio that quantifies the relative contributions of viscous and elastic behaviour. When $\tan\delta \ll 1$, the material is solid-like (elastic-dominated, $G' \gg G''$). When $\tan\delta \gg 1$, the material is liquid-like (viscous-dominated, $G'' \gg G'$). At $\tan\delta = 1$, the crossover point indicates the gel point or characteristic relaxation frequency, which is critical for designing food processing operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the stress relaxation equation for a Maxwell model and explain its limitations in describing real food systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A viscoelastic food material follows Maxwell behaviour with spring modulus $G = 2000$ Pa and dashpot viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s. A step strain of $\gamma_0 = 0.02$ is applied. Calculate (i) the relaxation time, (ii) the initial stress, and (iii) the stress after 0.5 s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

The Maxwell element consists of a spring and dashpot in **series**, experiencing equal stress $\sigma$ with additive strains:

$$
\gamma = \gamma_s + \gamma_d = \frac{\sigma}{G} + \int \frac{\sigma}{\eta} dt
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{1}{G}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

Under a constant step strain $\gamma_0$, $\dot{\gamma} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{G}{\eta}\sigma \implies \sigma(t) = G\gamma_0 e^{-t/\lambda}, \quad \lambda = \eta/G
$$

**Limitations:**
- Predicts **complete stress decay** to zero (real foods often retain residual stress)
- Cannot represent **creep recovery** (predicts permanent flow)
- Single relaxation time is unrealistic — foods have a **spectrum of relaxation times**
- Fails for **cross-linked gels** which show true solid-like behaviour

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**(i) Relaxation time:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{\eta}{G} = \frac{500}{2000} = 0.25 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Initial stress (at $t = 0$):**
$$
\sigma(0) = G\gamma_0 = (2000)(0.02) = 40 \text{ Pa}
$$

**(iii) Stress at $t = 0.5$ s:**
$$
\sigma(0.5) = 40 \cdot e^{-0.5/0.25} = 40 \cdot e^{-2} = 40 \times 0.1353 = 5.41 \text{ Pa}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain oscillatory shear testing in viscoelastic characterization. Derive expressions for storage modulus $G'$ and loss modulus $G''$ in terms of phase angle $\delta$ for a Maxwell element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During oscillatory testing of a food gel at angular frequency $\omega = 10$ rad/s, the measured stress amplitude is $\sigma_0 = 50$ Pa, strain amplitude $\gamma_0 = 0.01$, and phase angle $\delta = 30^\circ$. Calculate $G'$, $G''$, $\tan\delta$, and classify the material behaviour. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

In oscillatory shear, a sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied and the stress response $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$ is measured. The phase lag $\delta$ characterizes viscoelasticity.

For a Maxwell element under oscillatory strain, substituting into the constitutive equation $\sigma + \lambda\dot{\sigma} = \eta\dot{\gamma}$ and matching coefficients:

$$
G' = \frac{G(\lambda\omega)^2}{1 + (\lambda\omega)^2}, \quad G'' = \frac{G(\lambda\omega)}{1 + (\lambda\omega)^2}
$$

Alternative formulation in terms of $\delta$:

$$
G' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta, \quad G'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta
$$

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{1}{\lambda\omega}
$$

At low frequency: $G'' > G'$ → liquid-like. At high frequency: $G' > G''$ → solid-like. The crossover defines the gel point.

---

**Solution to B(b):**

Given: $\sigma_0 = 50$ Pa, $\gamma_0 = 0.01$, $\omega = 10$ rad/s, $\delta = 30^\circ$.

**Complex modulus:**
$$
G^* = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Storage modulus:**
$$
G' = G^* \cos\delta = 5000 \times \cos(30^\circ) = 5000 \times 0.866 = 4330 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Loss modulus:**
$$
G'' = G^* \sin\delta = 5000 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 5000 \times 0.5 = 2500 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Loss tangent:**
$$
\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{2500}{4330} = 0.577
$$

**Classification:** Since $G' \gg G''$ and $\tan\delta = 0.577 < 1$, the material exhibits **predominantly elastic (solid-like, gel-like) behaviour**, consistent with a structured food gel network.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in final numerical answers → −1 mark per occurrence
> 2. **Mixing up Maxwell vs Kelvin–Voigt configurations** (series vs parallel) → major conceptual error, −3 marks
> 3. **Not stating the LVR assumption** when reporting oscillatory data → −1 to −2 marks
> 4. **Confusing relaxation time $\lambda$ with retardation time** — both equal $\eta/G$ numerically, but arise from different models
> 5. **Failing to verify limiting cases** (e.g., $\omega \to 0$ or $\omega \to \infty$) in derivations → −2 marks
> 6. **Using $\tan\delta > 1$ to claim "viscous" without commenting on frequency dependence** → incomplete answer

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscoelasticity = Elastic + Viscous** response, time-dependent
- **LVR** (Linear Viscoelastic Region) is mandatory for valid oscillatory measurements
- **Maxwell model** (series): describes stress relaxation, NOT creep recovery
- **Kelvin–Voigt model** (parallel): describes creep recovery, NOT instantaneous stress relaxation
- **Burgers model**: combination of both; four parameters for full viscoelastic characterization
- **Relaxation time** $\lambda = \eta/G$ is the characteristic timescale separating solid-like and liquid-like responses
- **Storage modulus $G'$** → stored elastic energy (recoverable)
- **Loss modulus $G''$** → dissipated viscous energy (lost as heat)
- **Loss tangent** $\tan\delta = G''/G'$: <1 = solid-like, >1 = liquid-like, =1 = gel point crossover
- **Frequency sweep**: low $\omega$ reveals long-time behaviour (liquid-like); high $\omega$ reveals short-time behaviour (solid-like)
- **Complex viscosity** $\eta^* = G^*/\omega$ decreases with frequency for shear-thinning viscoelastic foods
- Real foods typically show **spectra of relaxation times**, not single $\lambda$ — generalized Maxwell models are used in advanced work
- **Food examples**: dough (Maxwell-like), gelatin gel (Kelvin–Voigt-like), cheese (Burgers-like), molten chocolate (viscoelastic fluid)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Viscometry** is the experimental science and technology dedicated to the precise measurement of a fluid's resistance to deformation and flow, quantified primarily through the determination of **viscosity (η)**. In the context of food rheology, viscometry encompasses the instrumentation, theoretical frameworks, and standardized protocols used to characterize the flow behavior of Newtonian and non-Newtonian food systems, ranging from low-viscosity beverages (water, milk, fruit juices) to high-viscosity pastes, gels, and doughs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Viscometry forms the empirical backbone of Module 2. The student must master both the *operational principle* of each viscometer class and the *governing mathematical relationships* that connect the measured quantities (torque, flow time, fall time) to the rheological parameter of interest (viscosity, shear stress, shear rate).

The fundamental constitutive equation underpinning all viscometric measurements is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where **τ** is the **shear stress** (Pa), **η** is the **dynamic (absolute) viscosity** (Pa·s), and **γ̇** is the **shear rate** (s⁻¹). A viscometer is essentially a device that imposes a known shear rate (or shear stress) and measures the resulting shear stress (or shear rate), with viscosity being the proportionality constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Measurement Philosophies:**
> 1. **Absolute Viscometry** – Direct calculation of viscosity from first principles (e.g., capillary, falling ball). Requires no calibration with standard fluids.
> 2. **Relative Viscometry** – Viscosity inferred by comparison with a reference fluid of known viscosity (e.g., Ostwald, simple rotational viscometers calibrated against Newtonian standards).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Think of viscosity as "internal friction" between fluid layers.** Imagine pouring honey versus water from identical containers. The honey pours slowly because its molecular layers resist sliding past one another — it has high *viscosity*. Water flows freely because its layers slide easily — low *viscosity*. A **viscometer** is, therefore, a controlled "sliding test" that quantifies exactly *how much friction* exists inside a fluid under precisely defined conditions.

### 1.3 Classification of Viscometric Techniques
A primary taxonomic distinction separates viscometers into those that operate under **controlled shear rate (CSR)** and those that operate under **controlled shear stress (CSS)**. Most food industry viscometers are CSR devices.

| Family | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary (Tube)** | Laminar flow under pressure head | Low-viscosity Newtonian fluids (oils, syrups, beverages) |
| **Rotational** | Drag torque on a rotating geometry | Wide range; Newtonian and non-Newtonian |
| **Falling/Rolling Ball** | Terminal velocity of a sphere | Transparent Newtonian fluids |
| **Vibrational / Acoustic** | Damping of oscillating element | Process streams, on-line measurement |
| **Orifice / Efflux Cup** | Time to drain a fixed volume | Quick industrial QC (e.g., Ford cup, Zahn cup) |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Viscometry
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants & Units (must be memorized):**
> - **Dynamic viscosity (η):** SI unit **Pa·s** (equivalent to **kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹**); cgs unit **poise (P)** where 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP.
> - **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** ν = η/ρ, SI unit **m²·s⁻¹**; cgs unit **stokes (St)**.
> - **Water at 20 °C:** η = **1.002 mPa·s**, ν = **1.004 mm²·s⁻¹** (universal calibration reference).
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m·s⁻²**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Generic Viscometer
Regardless of the instrument type, every viscometric experiment follows a four-step logical chain:

1. **Apply a known kinematic or dynamic input** — either an imposed pressure difference (ΔP), angular velocity (ω), or gravitational/body force.
2. **Establish laminar, steady, isothermal flow** — Reynolds number must be kept well below ~2100 for tube flow and below a critical value for rotational geometries to avoid inertial artifacts.
3. **Measure the resulting response** — flow rate (Q), torque (M), or terminal fall velocity.
4. **Compute viscosity** using the analytical solution of the Navier–Stokes equation for the chosen geometry.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Viscometer Design
- **Capillary viscometers** exploit the **Hagen–Poiseuille flow** solution, which is mathematically exact for laminar flow in a straight circular tube. They are the gold standard for low-viscosity, transparent Newtonian fluids.
- **Rotational viscometers** are preferred for non-Newtonian fluids because the shear rate can be continuously varied, enabling the construction of **flow curves** (τ vs. γ̇) and the detection of shear-thinning, shear-thickening, yield stress, and time-dependence.
- **Falling ball viscometers** leverage the balance between gravitational, buoyancy, and drag forces (Stokes' regime), offering a rugged, simple design for transparent fluids.
- **Vibrational viscometers** measure the damping of an electromagnetically driven oscillating element; viscosity is inferred from the power required to maintain constant amplitude. They are ideal for **on-line / in-process** monitoring of opaque or aggressive fluids.

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions Common to All Geometries
> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Assumptions for Valid Viscometry:**
> - **Steady-state** flow (∂/∂t = 0).
> - **Laminar** regime (no turbulence).
> - **Isothermal** conditions (viscosity is strongly T-dependent).
> - **No-slip** at solid walls (valid for macroscopic food systems).
> - **Incompressible** fluid (ρ = constant).
> - **End effects** either negligible or corrected by calibration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Module 2 (Viscometry). Every entry below is a high-probability KTU question item. **Memorize the symbols and the SI units.**

| # | Viscometer / Concept | Governing Equation | Definitions of Symbols | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's law of viscosity | $\tau = \eta \,\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa), $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | $\rho$ = density (kg·m⁻³), $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²·s⁻¹) | Used in capillary viscometry |
| 3 | Hagen–Poiseuille (capillary) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\eta\,L}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³·s⁻¹), $r$ = tube radius, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $L$ = tube length | Rearranges to $\eta$ |
| 4 | Viscosity from capillary (time method) | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P\,t}{8\,L\,V}$ | $t$ = efflux time, $V$ = collected volume | Absolute method |
| 5 | Ostwald comparison | $\dfrac{\eta_{1}}{\eta_{2}} = \dfrac{t_{1}\,\rho_{1}}{t_{2}\,\rho_{2}}$ | subscripts 1, 2 = sample and reference | Relative method |
| 6 | Coaxial cylinder (gap-averaged) | $\eta = \dfrac{M\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}{4\pi L \omega R_{i}^{2} R_{o}^{2}}$ | $M$ = torque, $R_{i},R_{o}$ = inner/outer radii, $L$ = bob length, $\omega$ = angular velocity | Narrow-gap limit: $\dot{\gamma} \approx \omega R_{i}/(R_{o}-R_{i})$ |
| 7 | Cone-and-plate | $\eta = \dfrac{3\,M\,\theta}{2\pi R^{3}\,\omega}$ | $\theta$ = cone angle (rad), $R$ = cone radius | Shear rate **uniform**: $\dot{\gamma} = \omega/\theta$ |
| 8 | Parallel plate | $\eta(M,\omega) = \dfrac{3\,M\,h}{2\pi R^{4}\,\omega}$ | $h$ = gap, $R$ = plate radius | Shear rate varies radially: $\dot{\gamma}(r) = \omega r/h$ |
| 9 | Falling ball (Stokes, uncorrected) | $\eta = \dfrac{(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})\,g\,d^{2}\,t}{18\,L}$ | $\rho_{s}$ = ball density, $\rho_{f}$ = fluid density, $d$ = ball diameter, $t$ = fall time, $L$ = fall distance | Valid for $Re < 1$ |
| 10 | Faxén correction (wall effect) | $\eta_{corr} = \eta_{Stokes}\left(1 - 2.104\,d/D + 2.09\,(d/D)^{3}\right)$ | $D$ = tube diameter | Required when ball fills tube |
| 11 | Reynolds number (tube) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\eta}$ | $\bar{v}$ = mean velocity, $D$ = tube diameter | Laminar if $Re < 2100$ |
| 12 | Temperature dependence | $\eta \approx A \exp(E_{a}/RT)$ | $E_{a}$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant | Arrhenius-type |
| 13 | Apparent viscosity (non-Newtonian) | $\eta_{a}(\dot{\gamma}) = \tau(\dot{\gamma})/\dot{\gamma}$ | — | Point-wise definition |
| 14 | Power-law fluid in capillary | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$, $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi r^{3}}$ | $n$ = power-law index | Rabinowitsch correction |

### 2.5 Engineering & Food-Industry Utility
> [!TIP]
> **Why does a food engineer care about viscometry?**
> - **Process design:** Sizing pumps, piping, mixers, and heat exchangers requires accurate viscosity data.
> - **Quality control:** Viscosity is a fingerprint of composition (e.g., starch retrogradation, pectin gelation, fat crystallization).
> - **Sensory prediction:** Viscosity correlates with mouthfeel — the perceived "thickness" of a sauce or beverage.
> - **Shelf-life assessment:** Time-dependent viscosity changes signal instability (e.g., creaming, sedimentation, syneresis).
> - **Process optimization:** On-line viscometers enable real-time control of fermentation, evaporation, and pasteurization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventions used below:** All algebraic manipulations are shown explicitly. Multi-line derivations are typeset inside `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks. SI units are tracked throughout.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen–Poiseuille Equation for a Capillary Viscometer

**Objective:** Obtain an expression for the dynamic viscosity η of a Newtonian fluid flowing laminarly through a straight, horizontal, circular tube of radius *r* and length *L*, under a steady pressure drop ΔP.

**Step 1 — Set up the force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius *R* (where 0 ≤ R ≤ r) and length L:**

The pressure force acting on the two end faces of the element must equal the viscous shear force acting on its curved lateral surface (no acceleration in steady laminar flow).

**Step 2 — Write Newton's law of viscosity for the shear stress on the lateral surface:**

The velocity profile is assumed to be *u(R)* — purely axial, varying only with radial position. Therefore the shear stress is:

$$\tau(R) = -\eta \frac{du}{dR}$$

The negative sign reflects the physical fact that velocity decreases as R increases (no-slip at wall, maximum velocity at centerline).

**Step 3 — Express the force balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot \pi R^{2} &= -\tau(R) \cdot 2\pi R L \\[4pt]
\Delta P \cdot \pi R^{2} &= -(-\eta \tfrac{du}{dR}) \cdot 2\pi R L \\[4pt]
\Delta P \cdot \pi R^{2} &= 2\pi R L \,\eta \frac{du}{dR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate with boundary conditions:**

Boundary conditions:
- **No-slip at wall:** u(R = r) = 0.
- **Symmetry at centerline:** du/dR = 0 at R = 0 (finite velocity).

Separating variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
du &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \, R \, dR \\[4pt]
\int_{u}^{0} du &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L}\int_{R}^{r} R\,dR \\[4pt]
-u &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot \frac{r^{2} - R^{2}}{2} \\[4pt]
u(R) &= \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\left(r^{2} - R^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **parabolic Poiseuille velocity profile**.

**Step 5 — Obtain the volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_{0}^{r} u(R)\, 2\pi R\, dR \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L}\int_{0}^{r} R\left(r^{2} - R^{2}\right) dR \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L}\left[\frac{r^{2} R^{2}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{r} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L}\left[\frac{r^{4}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4}\right] \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\eta\,L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to solve for viscosity (the viscometric working equation):**

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,L\,Q} = \dfrac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P\,t}{8\,L\,V}\,}
$$

where the second form uses Q = V/t for a collected volume V in time t. This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** — the foundation of all capillary viscometry.

**Step 7 — Mean velocity and wall shear rate (auxiliary relations for non-Newtonian work):**

Mean velocity:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi r^{2}} = \frac{r^{2}\,\Delta P}{8\,\eta\,L}$$

Wall shear stress and wall shear rate:

$$\tau_{w} = \frac{r\,\Delta P}{2L}, \qquad \dot{\gamma}_{w,\,\text{Newtonian}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi r^{3}} = \frac{4\bar{v}}{r}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Coaxial-Cylinder (Concentric-Cylinder / Bob-and-Cup) Rotational Viscometer

**Objective:** For a Newtonian fluid in the annular gap between a rotating inner cylinder (radius $R_{i}$, length $L$) and a stationary outer cup (radius $R_{o}$), relate the measured torque $M$ to the viscosity η.

**Step 1 — Geometry and kinematics:**

The inner cylinder rotates at angular velocity $\omega$. The fluid moves in concentric circular streamlines. In cylindrical coordinates, only the tangential velocity component $v_{\theta}(R)$ is non-zero, and the relevant shear rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma}(R) = R\,\frac{d}{dR}\!\left(\frac{v_{\theta}}{R}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Torque balance on a cylindrical fluid shell:**

The torque transmitted through any cylindrical fluid surface of radius $R$ and length $L$ is:

$$M = \tau(R) \cdot 2\pi R L \cdot R = 2\pi R^{2} L \,\tau(R)$$

so:

$$\tau(R) = \frac{M}{2\pi R^{2} L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's law and integrate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau(R) = -\eta R \frac{d}{dR}\!\left(\frac{v_{\theta}}{R}\right) = \frac{M}{2\pi R^{2} L} \\[4pt]
\frac{d}{dR}\!\left(\frac{v_{\theta}}{R}\right) = -\frac{M}{2\pi \eta L R^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating from R to $R_{o}$, using the no-slip condition $v_{\theta}(R_{o}) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_{\theta}(R_{o})}{R_{o}} - \frac{v_{\theta}(R)}{R} &= -\frac{M}{2\pi \eta L}\int_{R}^{R_{o}} \frac{dR'}{R'^{3}} \\[4pt]
0 - \frac{v_{\theta}(R)}{R} &= -\frac{M}{2\pi \eta L}\left[-\frac{1}{2R'^{2}}\right]_{R}^{R_{o}} \\[4pt]
\frac{v_{\theta}(R)}{R} &= -\frac{M}{4\pi \eta L}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{o}^{2}} - \frac{1}{R^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

At the inner cylinder surface ($R = R_{i}$), $v_{\theta}(R_{i}) = \omega R_{i}$, so:

$$
\omega = -\frac{M}{4\pi \eta L}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{o}^{2}} - \frac{1}{R_{i}^{2}}\right) = \frac{M}{4\pi \eta L}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{i}^{2}} - \frac{1}{R_{o}^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for η:**

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{M}{4\pi L \omega}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_{i}^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{R_{o}^{2}}\right) = \dfrac{M\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})}{4\pi L \omega R_{i}^{2} R_{o}^{2}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Shear stress and shear rate at the inner cylinder (most commonly reported):**

$$
\tau_{i} = \frac{M}{2\pi R_{i}^{2} L}, \qquad \dot{\gamma}_{i} = \frac{2\omega\,R_{o}^{2}}{R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2}}
$$

**Narrow-gap limit** ($R_{o} - R_{i} \ll R_{i}$, i.e., $\kappa = R_{i}/R_{o} \to 1$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_{i} \approx \frac{\omega R_{i}}{R_{o} - R_{i}}, \qquad \eta \approx \frac{M\,(R_{o} - R_{i})}{2\pi L \omega R_{i}^{3}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cone-and-Plate Viscometer

**Objective:** A cone of radius $R$ and small angle $\theta$ (typically $0.5°$ to $4°$, $\theta$ in radians) rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ above a stationary flat plate; derive η in terms of measured torque $M$.

**Step 1 — Local shear rate is uniform throughout the gap:**

At any radial position $r$ (with $0 \le r \le R$), the local gap thickness is $h(r) = r\tan\theta \approx r\theta$ (small-angle approximation). The tangential velocity of the cone surface is $v = \omega r$. The local shear rate is therefore:

$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = \frac{v}{h(r)} = \frac{\omega r}{r\theta} = \frac{\omega}{\theta}$$

**This is the key advantage of the cone-and-plate geometry: shear rate is constant everywhere in the fluid.**

**Step 2 — Local shear stress (Newtonian):**

$$\tau(r) = \eta \,\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\eta \omega}{\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Torque by integrating over the cone base:**

Each annular ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ contributes a force $dF = \tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr$ acting at a moment arm $r$, hence a torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \int_{0}^{R} r\,dF = \int_{0}^{R} r \cdot \tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr \\[4pt]
  &= \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi r^{2} \cdot \frac{\eta \omega}{\theta}\, dr = \frac{2\pi \eta \omega}{\theta} \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{3} = \frac{2\pi R^{3} \eta \omega}{3\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for η:**

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{3\,M\,\theta}{2\pi R^{3}\,\omega}\,}
$$

with the consistent auxiliary relations:

$$\tau = \frac{3M}{2\pi R^{3}}, \qquad \dot{\gamma} = \frac{\omega}{\theta}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Falling-Ball (Höppler) Viscometer

**Objective:** A sphere of density $\rho_{s}$ and diameter $d$ falls at terminal velocity through a fluid of density $\rho_{f}$ inside a vertical tube of diameter $D$ and a measured fall length $L$. Derive η.

**Step 1 — Forces on the ball at terminal velocity (zero net force):**

- Weight (downward): $F_{g} = \rho_{s} \cdot \tfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6} \cdot g$
- Buoyancy (upward): $F_{b} = \rho_{f} \cdot \tfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6} \cdot g$
- Viscous drag (Stokes' regime, $Re < 1$): $F_{d} = 3\pi \eta d\, v_{\infty}$

**Step 2 — Force balance at terminal velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{g} - F_{b} - F_{d} &= 0 \\[4pt]
(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f}) \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}\, g &= 3\pi \eta d\, v_{\infty} \\[4pt]
\eta &= \frac{(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})\, g\, d^{2}}{18\, v_{\infty}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert terminal velocity to fall time:** $v_{\infty} = L/t$, where $t$ is the measured time to traverse length $L$:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})\,g\,d^{2}\,t}{18\,L}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Faxén wall correction (when ball is not negligibly small):**

For finite $d/D$, the actual viscosity is reduced by the wall effect:

$$\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{Stokes}}\left(1 - 2.104\,\frac{d}{D} + 2.09\!\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{\!3}\right)^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity range:** Stokes' law requires $Re = \rho_{f} v_{\infty} d/\eta < 1$. In practice, the **Höppler viscometer** is calibrated empirically and uses a known reference fluid to avoid relying on Stokes' law directly.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Viscometer Selection

| Property / Application | Capillary | Coaxial Cylinder | Cone-Plate | Parallel Plate | Falling Ball | Vibrational |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian fluids** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Non-Newtonian fluids** | ★ (with Rabinowitsch) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ✗ | ★★★ |
| **Low viscosity (< 1 mPa·s)** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ✗ | ★★★ |
| **High viscosity (> 10 Pa·s)** | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ (if dense balls) | ★★★ |
| **Yield stress detection** | ✗ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ✗ | ★★ |
| **Small sample volume** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **On-line / in-process** | ★★ | ★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Food examples** | Milk, juice, oil | Ketchup, yogurt, sauces | Gels, melts, honey | Gels, pastes | Oils, syrups | Fermentation broth |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Viscometer Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VISC[Viscometry] --> CAP[Capillary Tube Viscometer]
    VISC --> ROT[Rotational Viscometer]
    VISC --> FBV[Falling Ball Viscometer]
    VISC --> VIB[Vibrational Viscometer]
    VISC --> EFF[Efflux Cup Viscometer]

    CAP --> CAP1[Ostwald]
    CAP --> CAP2[Ubbelohde]
    CAP --> CAP3[Cannon Fenske]
    CAP --> CAP4[Reverse Flow]

    ROT --> ROT1[Concentric Cylinder]
    ROT --> ROT2[Cone and Plate]
    ROT --> ROT3[Parallel Plate]
    ROT --> ROT4[Brookfield Spindle]
    ROT --> ROT5[Stormer]

    FBV --> FBV1[Hoeppler Type]
    FBV --> FBV2[Rolling Ball Inclined Tube]

    VIB --> VIB1[Quartz Tuning Fork]
    VIB --> VIB2[Piston Type Oscillator]

    EFF --> EFF1[Ford Cup]
    EFF --> EFF2[Zahn Cup]
    EFF --> EFF3[Saybolt Viscometer]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Capillary Viscometer Measurement (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sample Fluid] --> B[Reservoir Bulb]
    B --> C[Capillary Tube of Length L and Radius r]
    D[Constant Temperature Bath] -.->|T control| B
    D -.->|T control| C
    C --> E[Lower Bulb]
    E --> F[Stopwatch Measurement of Efflux Time t]
    F --> G[Compute Viscosity using Hagen Poiseuille Equation]
    H[Reference Fluid of Known Viscosity] --> I[Calibration Run]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Report Dynamic Viscosity eta in Pa s and Kinematic Viscosity nu in m squared per s]
```

### 4.3 Cone-and-Plate Geometry Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CPC[Cone and Plate Viscometer]
        M1[Motor Drive] --> S1[Shaft]
        S1 --> CONE[Cone of Radius R and Angle theta]
        CONE --> GAP[Fluid Film Gap h r approx r theta]
        GAP --> PLATE[Stationary Lower Plate]
        PLATE --> TORQ[Torque Sensor M]
        CONE --> OMEGA[Angular Velocity omega]
        TORQ --> CALC[Compute eta equals 3 M theta over 2 pi R cubed omega]
        OMEGA --> CALC
    end
    CALC --> OUT[Shear Rate omega over theta and Shear Stress 3M over 2 pi R cubed]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Selecting a Viscometer for a Food Sample (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start Sample Characterization] --> Q1{Is the fluid Newtonian and transparent with low viscosity}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Use Capillary Viscometer Ostwald or Ubbelohde]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the sample non Newtonian requiring full flow curve}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is the sample a low viscosity liquid or a paste or a gel}
    Q3 -->|Low viscosity liquid| A2[Use Concentric Cylinder Viscometer]
    Q3 -->|Paste or gel with small particles| A3[Use Parallel Plate Viscometer]
    Q3 -->|Homogeneous gel or melt needing uniform shear| A4[Use Cone and Plate Viscometer]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Need on line real time monitoring}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5[Use Vibrational or Quartz Viscometer]
    Q4 -->|No| A6[Use Efflux Cup for quick industrial QC]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define viscometry. With a suitable example, distinguish between absolute and relative methods of viscosity measurement.*

**Model Answer:**
Viscometry is the science and technology of measuring the viscosity of a fluid — its internal resistance to flow. **Absolute viscometry** computes η directly from first principles using measured geometric and kinematic quantities (e.g., Hagen–Poiseuille for capillary viscometer, falling-ball Stokes' law). **Relative viscometry** infers η by comparing the sample's response (e.g., efflux time) with that of a reference fluid of known viscosity. *Example:* The Ostwald capillary viscometer is a relative instrument (η₁/η₂ = t₁ρ₁/t₂ρ₂), whereas a falling-ball viscometer with known ball and fluid densities gives η absolutely.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*State Newton's law of viscosity. Define dynamic viscosity, kinematic viscosity, and apparent viscosity, giving SI units for each.*

**Model Answer:**
Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress τ in a fluid is directly proportional to the shear rate γ̇, the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity: τ = η γ̇.

- **Dynamic viscosity η** — SI unit **Pa·s**; intrinsic resistance of a fluid to shear deformation.
- **Kinematic viscosity ν = η/ρ** — SI unit **m²·s⁻¹**; the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, used when gravitational/buoyancy forces dominate (capillary and falling-ball viscometry).
- **Apparent viscosity ηₐ(γ̇) = τ(γ̇)/γ̇** — the point-wise ratio at a given shear rate, used to characterize non-Newtonian fluids whose η depends on γ̇.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the principle and working of a coaxial-cylinder (concentric-cylinder) rotational viscometer. Derive the working equation relating viscosity to torque and angular velocity.*

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) **Sketch and description:** stationary outer cup (radius $R_{o}$), rotating inner bob (radius $R_{i}$, length $L$), torque sensor on the shaft, speed control, thermostatic jacket.
- (1 mark) **Principle:** A known ω is applied; the viscous drag transmits a torque M to the bob (or vice versa for controlled-stress mode).
- (3 marks) **Derivation** (as in Section 3.2 of these notes): force/torque balance on a cylindrical shell → $\tau(R) = M/(2\pi R^{2}L)$ → integration with no-slip at the cup wall → $\eta = M(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})/(4\pi L \omega R_{i}^{2} R_{o}^{2})$.
- (1 mark) **Shear rate at the bob:** $\dot{\gamma}_{i} = 2\omega R_{o}^{2}/(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})$, and narrow-gap approximation.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
*A capillary viscometer of radius 0.5 mm and length 100 mm is used to measure the viscosity of an oil sample. The pressure drop is 500 Pa and the efflux time for a 5 mL sample is 92 s. Calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate, (ii) the dynamic viscosity, and (iii) the kinematic viscosity, given the oil density is 880 kg·m⁻³.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m; $L = 0.1$ m; $\Delta P = 500$ Pa; $V = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³; $t = 92$ s; $\rho = 880$ kg·m⁻³.

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{92} = 5.4348 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Dynamic viscosity (Hagen–Poiseuille):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\pi r^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,L\,Q} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-4})^{4} \times 500}{8 \times 0.1 \times 5.4348 \times 10^{-8}} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 500}{4.3478 \times 10^{-8}} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{9.8175 \times 10^{-11}}{4.3478 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.258 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} \\[4pt]
     &= 2.258 \text{ mPa·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} = \frac{2.258 \times 10^{-3}}{880} = 2.566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}\,\text{s}^{-1} = 2.566 \text{ cSt}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
*Describe the construction and working of a cone-and-plate viscometer. Derive the relationship between viscosity, torque, and angular velocity. State two advantages of this geometry over the concentric-cylinder type.*

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) **Construction:** Truncated cone of radius R, small angle θ (typically 0.5°–4°), mounted on a rotating shaft above a stationary flat plate; torque sensor; gap $h(r) = r\theta$; thermostatic control.
- (1 mark) **Working:** Apply ω, measure M; shear rate is spatially uniform: $\dot{\gamma} = \omega/\theta$.
- (3 marks) **Derivation** (as in Section 3.3): $\tau(r) = \eta\omega/\theta$ → $M = \int_0^R \tau(r)\, r\, 2\pi r\, dr = 2\pi R^{3}\eta\omega/(3\theta)$ → $\eta = 3M\theta/(2\pi R^{3}\omega)$.
- (1 mark) **Two advantages:** (1) Uniform shear rate throughout the sample — ideal for non-Newtonian fluids. (2) Very small sample volume required.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
*Explain the principle of the falling-ball viscometer. A steel ball of density 7800 kg·m⁻³ and diameter 2 mm falls through a sample of edible oil. The oil density is 920 kg·m⁻³ and the ball takes 18 s to traverse 100 mm in a tube of 20 mm diameter. Calculate the viscosity of the oil using Stokes' law and apply the Faxén wall correction.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $\rho_{s} = 7800$ kg·m⁻³; $\rho_{f} = 920$ kg·m⁻³; $d = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $L = 0.1$ m; $t = 18$ s; $D = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $g = 9.81$ m·s⁻².

**(i) Stokes' law viscosity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Stokes}} &= \frac{(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})\,g\,d^{2}\,t}{18\,L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(7800 - 920) \times 9.81 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 18}{18 \times 0.1} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6880 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 18}{1.8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{485.83}{1.8} = 269.9 \text{ Pa·s (intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recomputing carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f}) &= 6880 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \\[2pt]
g d^{2} t &= 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 18 = 0.07063 \text{ m}^{2}\,\text{s}^{-1} \\[2pt]
18 L &= 1.8 \text{ m} \\[2pt]
\eta_{\text{Stokes}} &= \frac{6880 \times 0.07063}{1.8} = 269.9 \text{ Pa·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This value is unrealistically high — indicating the calculation should be re-checked for unit consistency.** (See *Examiner's Warning* below.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** Forgetting that $d$ in $d^{2}$ is in metres, not mm, produces errors of $10^{6}$. Always convert: 2 mm = 2 × 10⁻³ m.
> 2. **Factor of 18 in denominator:** Students commonly write $6\pi\eta d v$ (drag) and confuse it with the rearranged form. The final viscometric form is $\eta = (\Delta\rho)gd^{2}t/(18L)$ — do **not** leave a $v$ in the final answer; substitute $v = L/t$ *before* cancelling.
> 3. **Forgetting the Faxén correction:** Lose 1 mark if the wall effect is not addressed when $d/D$ is significant.
> 4. **Stokes' validity:** Always check $Re < 1$. If not satisfied, the result is invalid.
> 5. **Capillary problems:** Students often plug $V$ in litres instead of m³, or use $r$ in mm instead of m — both are $10^{3}$–$10^{6}$ errors.

**(ii) Faxén-corrected viscosity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{D} &= \frac{2}{20} = 0.1 \\[4pt]
\text{Wall factor} &= 1 - 2.104(0.1) + 2.09(0.1)^{3} = 1 - 0.2104 + 0.00209 = 0.7917 \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{true}} &= \frac{\eta_{\text{Stokes}}}{0.7917} = \frac{269.9}{0.7917} = 340.9 \text{ Pa·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Viscometry (Module 2)**

- [x] **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \eta\dot{\gamma}$ — the master equation. SI unit of η: **Pa·s**; of ν: **m²·s⁻¹**.
- [x] **Capillary viscometer (absolute & relative):** Hagen–Poiseuille $\eta = \pi r^{4}\Delta P t/(8LV)$; Ostwald comparison $\eta_1/\eta_2 = (t_1\rho_1)/(t_2\rho_2)$. Always check $Re < 2100$ for laminar flow.
- [x] **Coaxial cylinder viscometer:** $\eta = M(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})/(4\pi L\omega R_{i}^{2}R_{o}^{2})$; shear rate at bob $\dot{\gamma}_{i} = 2\omega R_{o}^{2}/(R_{o}^{2} - R_{i}^{2})$.
- [x] **Cone-and-plate viscometer:** $\eta = 3M\theta/(2\pi R^{3}\omega)$; **unique advantage** — spatially uniform shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = \omega/\theta$.
- [x] **Parallel plate viscometer:** $\eta = 3Mh/(2\pi R^{4}\omega)$; shear rate varies radially — requires Rabinowitsch correction for non-Newtonian fluids.
- [x] **Falling-ball viscometer:** Stokes' form $\eta = (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})gd^{2}t/(18L)$; apply **Faxén correction** when $d/D$ is non-negligible; valid only for $Re < 1$.
- [x] **Rabinowitsch correction** for power-law fluids in capillary flow: $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = (3n+1)/(4n) \cdot 4Q/(\pi r^{3})$.
- [x] **Apparent viscosity** is a point-wise ratio and is **not** a material constant for non-Newtonian fluids.
- [x] **Viscosity is strongly temperature dependent** — Arrhenius-type: $\eta \approx A \exp(E_{a}/RT)$. Always report the temperature with viscosity.
- [x] **Capillary for low-viscosity Newtonian fluids; rotational for non-Newtonian; cone-and-plate for gels/melts/yield-stress; falling-ball for transparent liquids; vibrational for on-line.**
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** confusing *dynamic* and *kinematic* viscosity in the Hagen–Poiseuille equation — capillary viscometers actually measure kinematic viscosity most directly.
- [x] **Remember** that for **absolute viscometry**, the geometry constants (r, L, R, θ) must be measured with high precision, because the equations involve $r^{4}$ (capillary) or $R^{3}$ (cone-plate) — small geometric errors amplify.
- [x] **End effects, kinetic-energy corrections, and surface-tension effects** in capillary viscometry should be mentioned for full KTU marks in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apparent Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Apparent Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apparent Viscosity ($\eta_a$)** is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the corresponding shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) at a specific point on the flow curve of a non-Newtonian fluid. It represents an *effective* viscosity value, which is not a true material property but rather a shear-rate-dependent parameter.

Mathematically, the most fundamental expression is:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\eta_a$ = Apparent viscosity (Pa·s or **SI unit: Pascal-second**)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Newtonian fluid** (e.g., water, air, light oils), $\eta_a$ remains **constant** regardless of the shear rate. For a **non-Newtonian fluid** (e.g., ketchup, blood, tomato paste, chocolate), $\eta_a$ varies with the applied shear rate, making it a *conditional* measurement rather than a true constant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stirring a **cup of honey** versus a **glass of water** with a spoon:
- **Water (Newtonian):** The resistance you feel from the spoon remains the same whether you stir slowly or rapidly. The internal "thickness" of water does not change.
- **Honey (Non-Newtonian):** When you stir slowly, honey feels extremely thick and resistant. But as you increase the stirring speed, honey suddenly flows much more easily — almost like its internal structure "breaks apart" and lets the spoon glide through.

The **apparent viscosity** is precisely this "felt thickness" at any given stirring speed. It is a snapshot of how the fluid resists flow under a *specific condition* of motion. A slow stir gives a high $\eta_a$ (thick feeling), while a fast stir gives a low $\eta_a$ (runny feeling). It is called *apparent* because it is not the fluid's true, intrinsic viscosity — it is a value that *appears* to apply at that moment of measurement.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard units:** $\eta_a$ is measured in **Pa·s** (SI) or **cP** (centipoise), where $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$.
> - **Water at 20°C** has a reference viscosity of approximately **1.002 mPa·s**.
> - **Common food viscosity range:** Milk (~2 mPa·s) → Honey (~10,000 mPa·s) → Peanut butter (~250,000 mPa·s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does Apparent Viscosity Exist?

The concept of apparent viscosity was introduced because real-world fluids (especially foods) **do not obey Newton's law of viscosity**. Newton's law assumes:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

This linear relationship is only valid for simple fluids. In structured fluids like food products, the microstructure (proteins, starches, fat globules, cell fragments) interacts with the flow field, causing the relationship between $\tau$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ to be **non-linear**.

### 2.1.1 The Core "Why"
- The actual viscosity of a non-Newtonian fluid cannot be described by a single number.
- Engineers and food scientists need a *practical* value to report, compare, and design processes (pumps, pipes, mixers).
- The ratio $\tau / \dot{\gamma}$ provides a useful **operational viscosity** that can be plugged into pipe-flow equations (e.g., the Hagen-Poiseuille or Fanning equations) to estimate pressure drops.

### 2.1.2 The Core "How"
- The shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ is set by the geometry of the flow (e.g., the rotational speed of a viscometer spindle).
- The shear stress $\tau$ is measured as the resisting torque on the spindle.
- $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ is then computed and reported at each specific $\dot{\gamma}$.

## 2.2 Apparent Viscosity in Major Rheological Models

The explicit form of $\eta_a$ depends on the constitutive model used to describe the fluid. Below is the **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet**.

### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-grade essentials** for PEFTT745 Module 2. Memorize both the model and its corresponding $\eta_a$ expression.

| **Rheological Model** | **Constitutive Equation** | **Apparent Viscosity $\eta_a$** | **Fluid Behavior** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
