| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_a = \eta = \text{constant}$ | Ideal fluid |
| **Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Shear-thinning / Shear-thickening |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_p$ | Yield + linear flow |
| **Herschel-Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Yield + power-law flow |
| **Casson Fluid** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_c \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta_a = \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}}} + \sqrt{\eta_c}\right)^{2}$ | Yield + square-root flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $K$ = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
> - $n$ = Flow behavior index (dimensionless)
> - $\tau_y$ = Yield stress (Pa)
> - $\eta_p$ = Plastic viscosity (Pa·s)
> - $\eta_c$ = Casson viscosity (Pa·s)

### 2.2.2 Interpreting the Flow Behavior Index ($n$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relationship is the single most tested concept in KTU exams under this topic:
> - If $n < 1$ → **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)**: $\eta_a$ **decreases** with increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ (e.g., ketchup, blood, molten chocolate).
> - If $n = 1$ → **Newtonian**: $\eta_a$ is **constant** (e.g., water, milk).
> - If $n > 1$ → **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)**: $\eta_a$ **increases** with increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ (e.g., cornstarch suspensions, wet sand).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In food process engineering, $\eta_a$ is used to:
> 1. **Design pipe networks** — Calculate the Reynolds number using $\eta_a$ to determine whether flow is laminar or turbulent.
> 2. **Select pumps** — Estimate power requirements ($P = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V$) for pumping viscous products.
> 3. **Quality control** — Compare batches of the same product by measuring $\eta_a$ at a standardized shear rate (e.g., 100 s⁻¹).
> 4. **Sensory prediction** — Correlate $\eta_a$ at oral-processing shear rates (~10–100 s⁻¹) with mouthfeel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Apparent Viscosity from the Power Law Model

The Power Law model is the most commonly used non-Newtonian model in food rheology. Let us derive $\eta_a$ step by step.

### 3.1.1 Starting Equation

The Power Law model is given by:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

This is the constitutive equation relating shear stress to shear rate through the consistency index $K$ and the flow behavior index $n$.

### 3.1.2 Definition of Apparent Viscosity

The apparent viscosity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting the Power Law Expression

Substitute the expression for $\tau$ from the Power Law into the definition of $\eta_a$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

### 3.1.4 Simplification

Using the algebraic rule $a^m / a^n = a^{m-n}$:

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

### 3.1.5 Special Case Verification

If $n = 1$ (Newtonian limit), then:

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0} = K \cdot 1 = K$$

This confirms that $K$ is the true (constant) viscosity for a Newtonian fluid. The derivation is **dimensionally and physically consistent**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Apparent Viscosity from the Bingham Plastic Model

The Bingham model describes fluids that require a minimum stress (yield stress) before they begin to flow.

### 3.2.1 Starting Equation

$$\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

This indicates that the fluid behaves as a rigid solid until the applied stress exceeds $\tau_y$, after which it flows linearly with plastic viscosity $\eta_p$.

### 3.2.2 Division by Shear Rate

Starting from the definition:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Substitute the Bingham model:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

### 3.2.3 Algebraic Separation

Split the numerator and divide each term:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + \frac{\eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

### 3.2.4 Final Simplification

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The apparent viscosity of a Bingham plastic has **two components**:
> 1. A **structure-dependent term** $\tau_y / \dot{\gamma}$ which decreases as shear rate increases (because the structure breaks down).
> 2. A **constant term** $\eta_p$ which represents the viscosity of the fully-yielded fluid.
> 3. Therefore, even Bingham plastics are *shear-thinning* in the strict rheological sense, because $\eta_a \to \infty$ as $\dot{\gamma} \to 0$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A tomato concentrate is modeled as a Power Law fluid with consistency index $K = 15$ Pa·sⁿ and flow behavior index $n = 0.45$. Calculate the apparent viscosity at a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 50$ s⁻¹.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Identify the Model

The fluid follows:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Write the Apparent Viscosity Expression

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute the Exponent

$$n - 1 = 0.45 - 1 = -0.55$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Compute the Shear Rate Term

$$\dot{\gamma}^{-0.55} = (50)^{-0.55}$$

Using logarithms:

$$\log_{10}(50^{-0.55}) = -0.55 \times \log_{10}(50) = -0.55 \times 1.699 = -0.9345$$

$$50^{-0.55} = 10^{-0.9345} \approx 0.1163 \text{ s}^{0.55}$$

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Final Calculation

$$\eta_a = 15 \times 0.1163$$

$$\boxed{\eta_a \approx 1.745 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Behavior:** Since $n = 0.45 < 1$, this is a shear-thinning fluid. If we doubled the shear rate to 100 s⁻¹, we would expect $\eta_a$ to drop. Quick check: $\eta_a(100) = 15 \times 100^{-0.55} \approx 15 \times 0.0824 \approx 1.24$ Pa·s. The viscosity dropped, confirming shear-thinning behavior. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rheological Classification & Apparent Viscosity Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Sample] --> B{Does it obey Newtons Law?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Newtonian Fluid]
    C --> D["Apparent Viscosity equals True Viscosity: eta_a = eta = constant"]
    B -- No --> E[Non-Newtonian Fluid]
    E --> F{Does it have a yield stress tau_y?}
    F -- No --> G[Time-Independent Non-Newtonian]
    G --> H{Is n less than 1?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Pseudoplastic - Shear Thinning]
    I --> J["eta_a = K times gamma_dot power n minus 1; eta_a decreases as gamma_dot increases"]
    H -- No --> K[Dilatant - Shear Thickening]
    K --> L["eta_a = K times gamma_dot power n minus 1; eta_a increases as gamma_dot increases"]
    F -- Yes --> M[Yield-Stress Fluid]
    M --> N{What is the post-yield model?}
    N -- O[Bingham] --> P["eta_a = tau_y over gamma_dot plus eta_p"]
    N -- Q[Herschel-Bulkley] --> R["eta_a = tau_y over gamma_dot plus K times gamma_dot power n minus 1"]
    N -- S[Casson] --> T["eta_a = open paren sqrt of tau_y over gamma_dot plus sqrt of eta_c close paren squared"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Measurement & Engineering Utility of Apparent Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Set Spindle Speed] --> M2[Set Shear Rate gamma_dot]
    M2 --> M3[Measure Torque Response]
    M3 --> M4[Convert Torque to Shear Stress tau]
    M4 --> M5["Compute eta_a = tau divided by gamma_dot"]
    M5 --> M6[Plot eta_a versus gamma_dot]
    M6 --> M7{Identify Flow Behavior}
    M7 -- n less than 1 --> M8[Shear Thinning Report eta_a at standard gamma_dot]
    M7 -- n greater than 1 --> M9[Shear Thickening Report eta_a at standard gamma_dot]
    M7 -- Yield Stress Present --> M10[Report eta_a at multiple gamma_dot values]
    M8 --> M11[Use eta_a in Pipe Flow and Pump Design Equations]
    M9 --> M11
    M10 --> M11
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Role of Apparent Viscosity in a Food Process Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Raw Material Characterization"]
        I1[Measure eta_a at low gamma_dot equals 1 per s]
        I2[Measure eta_a at medium gamma_dot equals 50 per s]
        I3[Measure eta_a at high gamma_dot equals 500 per s]
    end
    subgraph Processing["Process Design Stage"]
        P1[Select Pump Power using eta_a]
        P2[Size Pipes using Reynolds Number with eta_a]
        P3[Set Mixer RPM using eta_a profile]
    end
    subgraph Output["Quality and Sensory Output"]
        Q1[Predict Mouthfeel from eta_a at 10 to 100 per s]
        Q2[Predict Pourability from eta_a at low gamma_dot]
        Q3[Batch Consistency Check using eta_a at reference gamma_dot]
    end
    I1 --> P2
    I2 --> P1
    I2 --> P3
    I2 --> Q1
    I3 --> P3
    I1 --> Q2
    I2 --> Q3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define apparent viscosity. Why is it called "apparent" and not "true" viscosity?**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Apparent viscosity** ($\eta_a$) is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the corresponding shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) at a specific point on the rheogram of a non-Newtonian fluid: $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$.
>
> It is termed "apparent" (and not "true") because it is **not a fundamental material constant**. For non-Newtonian fluids, $\eta_a$ varies with the shear rate at which it is measured. Therefore, a single reported value of $\eta_a$ is only valid for the *specific* $\dot{\gamma}$ used during the measurement. Two different shear rates can produce two different apparent viscosities for the *same* fluid. In contrast, the true viscosity of a Newtonian fluid is a constant, intrinsic material property, independent of flow conditions.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A fluid has a flow behavior index $n = 0.3$ and a consistency index $K = 25$ Pa·sⁿ. Identify the type of fluid and calculate $\eta_a$ at $\dot{\gamma} = 10$ s⁻¹.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Since $n = 0.3 < 1$, the fluid is a **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** fluid.
>
> Apparent viscosity formula: $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$
>
> Substituting:
> $$\eta_a = 25 \times (10)^{0.3 - 1} = 25 \times 10^{-0.7}$$
>
> $$\eta_a = 25 \times 0.1995 \approx 4.99 \text{ Pa·s}$$
>
> **Answer:** $\eta_a \approx 4.99$ Pa·s at 10 s⁻¹.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question has an internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**a)** Derive the expression for apparent viscosity of a Power Law fluid. Discuss how $n$ and $K$ influence the apparent viscosity and identify the type of fluid for $n > 1$, $n = 1$, and $n < 1$ with food examples. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A Hershel-Bulkley fluid has the following rheological parameters: $\tau_y = 8$ Pa, $K = 4$ Pa·sⁿ, and $n = 0.6$. Calculate (i) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 20$ s⁻¹, and (ii) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s⁻¹. Comment on the type of fluid behavior observed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Constitutive Equation:** The Power Law fluid is defined by:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Viscosity Definition:** Starting from:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:** Substituting the Power Law into the definition:

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Influence of Parameters:**
- $K$ (consistency index) sets the *magnitude* of $\eta_a$. Higher $K$ → thicker fluid.
- $n$ (flow behavior index) sets the *rate of change* of $\eta_a$ with $\dot{\gamma}$.

**Step 5 — Classification:**

| $n$ value | Behavior | Food Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n < 1$ | Pseudoplastic / Shear-thinning | Ketchup, blood, fruit juice concentrate |
| $n = 1$ | Newtonian (limit case) | Water, milk, vegetable oil |
| $n > 1$ | Dilatant / Shear-thickening | Cornstarch slurry, wet sand |

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Hershel-Bulkley Apparent Viscosity Expression:**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 2 — Part (i): $\dot{\gamma} = 20$ s⁻¹**

Yield contribution:

$$\frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{8}{20} = 0.4 \text{ Pa·s}$$

Power-law contribution:

$$K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 4 \times (20)^{-0.4}$$

Compute $(20)^{-0.4}$:

$$\log_{10}(20^{-0.4}) = -0.4 \times 1.301 = -0.5204$$

$$20^{-0.4} \approx 0.3012$$

Power-law contribution: $4 \times 0.3012 = 1.2048$ Pa·s

Total:

$$\eta_a = 0.4 + 1.2048 = 1.6048 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Part (ii): $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s⁻¹**

Yield contribution:

$$\frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{8}{200} = 0.04 \text{ Pa·s}$$

Power-law contribution:

$$K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 4 \times (200)^{-0.4}$$

Compute $(200)^{-0.4}$:

$$\log_{10}(200^{-0.4}) = -0.4 \times 2.301 = -0.9204$$

$$200^{-0.4} \approx 0.1201$$

Power-law contribution: $4 \times 0.1201 = 0.4804$ Pa·s

Total:

$$\eta_a = 0.04 + 0.4804 = 0.5204 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Behavior:**

$$\eta_a(20) = 1.60 \text{ Pa·s} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \eta_a(200) = 0.52 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$\eta_a$ decreased as $\dot{\gamma}$ increased → the fluid exhibits **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** behavior. Since $n = 0.6 < 1$, this is consistent with the theoretical expectation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**a)** Define yield stress. Derive the expression for the apparent viscosity of a Bingham plastic fluid. Explain why Bingham fluids are also called "yield-stress fluids." **(7 marks)**

**b)** A chocolate melt at 40°C is modeled as a Casson fluid with $\tau_y = 12$ Pa and $\eta_c = 2.5$ Pa·s. Compute the apparent viscosity at (i) $\dot{\gamma} = 5$ s⁻¹ and (ii) $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹. State two reasons why this behavior is important in chocolate processing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Definition of Yield Stress:** **Yield stress** ($\tau_y$) is the minimum shear stress that must be applied to a structured fluid before it begins to flow. Below $\tau_y$, the fluid behaves as an elastic solid; above $\tau_y$, it flows like a liquid.

**Step 2 — Bingham Constitutive Equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of $\eta_a$:**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_p$$

**Step 4 — Why called "yield-stress fluids":** Bingham plastics are termed yield-stress fluids because $\tau_y$ must be exceeded for *any* flow to occur. At $\dot{\gamma} = 0$, $\eta_a \to \infty$, reflecting the solid-like behavior at rest. The plastic viscosity $\eta_p$ is the viscosity of the material *after* yielding.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Casson Apparent Viscosity:**

$$\eta_a = \left(\sqrt{\frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}}} + \sqrt{\eta_c}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Part (i): $\dot{\gamma} = 5$ s⁻¹**

$$\frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\sqrt{2.4} \approx 1.5492$$

$$\sqrt{\eta_c} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.5811$$

$$\eta_a = (1.5492 + 1.5811)^{2} = (3.1303)^{2} \approx 9.80 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Part (ii): $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹**

$$\frac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{12}{100} = 0.12 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\sqrt{0.12} \approx 0.3464$$

$$\sqrt{\eta_c} \approx 1.5811$$

$$\eta_a = (0.3464 + 1.5811)^{2} = (1.9275)^{2} \approx 3.71 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Two Reasons for Importance in Chocolate Processing:**
1. **Mouthfeel control:** At oral shear rates (~50 s⁻¹), the low $\eta_a$ ensures the chocolate melts smoothly on the tongue. The high $\eta_a$ at low shear rates prevents the chocolate from dripping off the production line.
2. **Conching efficiency:** The shear-thinning behavior allows the mixers (conches) to apply high shear that progressively breaks down the fat-sugar-cocoa network, ensuring proper flow and flavor release.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PEFTT745 Exam Answers:**
> 1. **Missing Units:** Always include the unit of $\eta_a$ (Pa·s) in the final answer. A correct numerical value without units is marked *incomplete* (loss of 0.5–1 mark per KTU valuation key).
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tau_y$ term:** For Herschel-Bulkley and Bingham fluids, students frequently write only the power-law part, omitting the $\tau_y / \dot{\gamma}$ term. This loses up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $K$ and $\eta$:** $K$ is NOT a viscosity when $n \neq 1$. $K$ has units of Pa·sⁿ. State this explicitly to gain full credit.
> 4. **Not identifying the fluid type:** For any numerical problem, always conclude with: "Since $n < 1$, the fluid is pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)." This is a mandatory 1-mark conclusion in KTU papers.
> 5. **Wrong base for logarithms:** Use $\log_{10}$ (or natural log consistently) and show intermediate steps. Skipping the log step leads to errors and lost method marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Apparent Viscosity (Module 2)**
> - ☐ $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ is the **defining equation** — must be memorized.
> - ☐ Apparent viscosity is **shear-rate dependent** for non-Newtonian fluids; never report it without stating the corresponding $\dot{\gamma}$.
> - ☐ **Power Law:** $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Most frequently used food rheology model.
> - ☐ **Bingham:** $\eta_a = \tau_y / \dot{\gamma} + \eta_p$. Has a yield stress.
> - ☐ **Hershel-Bulkley:** $\eta_a = \tau_y / \dot{\gamma} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Most general yield-stress model.
> - ☐ **Casson:** $\eta_a = (\sqrt{\tau_y / \dot{\gamma}} + \sqrt{\eta_c})^{2}$. Used for **chocolate** and food suspensions.
> - ☐ $n < 1$ → **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning): ketchup, blood, chocolate.
> - ☐ $n = 1$ → **Newtonian** limit: $\eta_a = K$ (true viscosity).
> - ☐ $n > 1$ → **Dilatant** (shear-thickening): cornstarch suspensions.
> - ☐ SI unit of $\eta_a$ is **Pa·s**; $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$.
> - ☐ In **Newtonian fluids**, $\eta_a$ equals the **true viscosity** $\eta$ (it is a constant).
> - ☐ Yield-stress fluids show $\eta_a \to \infty$ as $\dot{\gamma} \to 0$.
> - ☐ Always report the **shear rate** alongside $\eta_a$ in any laboratory report.
> - ☐ The **consistency index $K$** has units of Pa·sⁿ (not Pa·s) when $n \neq 1$.
> - ☐ Apparent viscosity is essential for **pipe-flow pressure-drop** and **pump-power** calculations in food process engineering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrinsic Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrinsic Viscosity $[\eta]$** (also called the **Limiting Viscosity Number** in IUPAC nomenclature) is a measure of a solute's (typically a macromolecule or biopolymer) contribution to the viscosity of a solution. Mathematically, it is defined as the limit of the ratio of the **specific viscosity** ($\eta_{sp}$) to the **concentration** ($c$) of the solute, as the concentration approaches zero.

The defining equation used universally in food polymer science and KTU examinations is:

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \left( \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} \right)$$

Where:
- $[\eta]$ = Intrinsic viscosity (units: **dL/g** or **mL/g** or **cm³/g**)
- $\eta_{sp}$ = Specific viscosity (dimensionless)
- $c$ = Concentration of the polymer in solution (g/dL or g/mL)

Alternative equivalent forms (all must be known for KTU):

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \left( \frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} \right) = \lim_{c \to 0} \left( \frac{\eta - \eta_s}{c \cdot \eta_s} \right)$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = Viscosity of the polymer solution
- $\eta_s$ = Viscosity of the pure solvent
- $\eta_{rel} = \eta / \eta_s$ = Relative viscosity

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Relative viscosity** $\eta_{rel} = \eta / \eta_s$ — ratio of solution viscosity to solvent viscosity
> - **Specific viscosity** $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ — fractional change in viscosity due to the polymer
> - **Reduced viscosity** $\eta_{red} = \eta_{sp} / c$ — used for extrapolation
> - **Inherent viscosity** $\eta_{inh} = \ln(\eta_{rel}) / c$ — used for extrapolation
> - **Intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$ — the extrapolated value at infinite dilution

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Stirring Resistance" of Dissolved Chains**
>
> Imagine you dissolve sugar (a small molecule) in water versus dissolving xanthan gum (a giant chain-like molecule) in water. Both make the solution "thicker," but for very different reasons. Sugar molecules act like tiny marbles tumbling past each other — small hydrodynamic footprint. Xanthan gum molecules, however, are long, tangled ropes that snag and drag the surrounding water along as they tumble, causing a disproportionately large resistance to flow.
>
> **Intrinsic viscosity** is essentially a way of measuring how much "hydrodynamic drag per gram" a single dissolved macromolecule creates, *stripped of all the messy intermolecular interactions* (entanglements, crowding effects) that exist at finite concentrations. By mathematically extrapolating the measurement down to zero concentration, we isolate the **intrinsic** (inherent, built-in) property of the isolated polymer chain.

### Physical Picture Summary

| Concentration Regime | Dominant Phenomenon | Measured Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High $c$ | Polymer-polymer entanglement, crowding | $\eta$ is very large |
| Moderate $c$ | Hydrodynamic + weak interactions | $\eta_{red}$ is concentration-dependent |
| $c \to 0$ | **Isolated single-chain hydrodynamics** | $[\eta]$ (intrinsic only) |

### Why "Infinite Dilution"?

At any real concentration, polymer molecules interfere with each other (they occupy space, entangle, and form transient associations). Only at **infinite dilution** does each polymer chain behave as an isolated, non-interacting particle. This is the same reason we extrapolate to zero concentration in osmometry and light scattering to get the **weight-average molecular weight** $\bar{M}_w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Units Used in KTU 2024 Problems:**
> - Concentration: **g/dL** (grams per deciliter) — most common in food science
> - Intrinsic viscosity: **dL/g** (so that $[\eta] \times c$ is dimensionless)
> - SI equivalent: m³/kg

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation: Why Extrapolation is Required

The viscosity of a polymer solution is a function of concentration, but it contains **two** distinct contributions:

1. **Intrinsic contribution** — from the isolated hydrodynamic volume of each polymer chain
2. **Extrinsic contribution** — from chain-chain interactions, entanglement, aggregation

The **specific viscosity** $\eta_{sp}$ is a polynomial-like function of concentration. Its Taylor expansion around $c = 0$ gives:

$$\eta_{sp} = [\eta] \cdot c + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c^2 + k_1 [\eta]^3 \cdot c^3 + \cdots$$

Dividing by $c$:

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c + k_1 [\eta]^3 \cdot c^2 + \cdots$$

This is the key insight: **the linear extrapolation of $\eta_{sp}/c$ vs. $c$ to $c = 0$ gives $[\eta]$**, and the slope gives the **Huggins constant** $k_H$.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Extrapolation Equations

### (a) Huggins Equation (1930s — primary method)

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

- Plot: $\eta_{sp}/c$ (y-axis) vs. $c$ (x-axis)
- Intercept = $[\eta]$
- Slope = $k_H [\eta]^2$

### (b) Kraemer Equation (companion method)

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

- Plot: $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c$ (y-axis) vs. $c$ (x-axis)
- Intercept = $[\eta]$
- Slope = $k_K [\eta]^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Huggins + Kraemer Constraint (Examiners love this!):**
> For a "good" solvent and well-behaved polymer:
> $$k_H + k_K = 0.5$$
> This is known as the **Huggins–Kraemer consistency relation**. Deviations from 0.5 indicate polyelectrolyte effects, aggregation, or poor solvent quality.

### (c) Single-Point Method (Solomon–Ciuta, 1962) — for rapid estimation

$$[\eta] \approx \frac{\sqrt{2(\eta_{sp} - \ln\eta_{rel})}}{c}$$

Used when only one concentration is available.

## 2.3 The Mark-Houwink-Sakurada Equation (MHS)

This is the **most important practical application** of intrinsic viscosity. It links $[\eta]$ to the **molecular weight** $M$ of the polymer:

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$$

Where:
- $K$ = Mark-Houwink constant (depends on polymer-solvent-temperature system)
- $a$ = Mark-Houwink exponent (a measure of chain stiffness and solvent quality)
- $M$ = Viscosity-average molecular weight, $M_v$

### Interpretation of Exponent $a$

| Value of $a$ | Conformation | Solvent Quality | Food Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0$ | Compact sphere | $\Theta$-solvent (ideal) | Globular protein in pure water |
| $0.5 - 0.8$ | Random coil, semi-flexible | Good solvent | Starch, guar gum |
| $0.8 - 1.0$ | Stiff random coil | Good solvent | Xanthan gum |
| $1.0 - 2.0$ | Rigid rod | Strongly interacting | Some cellulose derivatives |

## 2.4 The Flory–Fox Equation — Molecular Dimensions

For a flexible coil in a $\Theta$-solvent:

$$[\eta] = \Phi \cdot \frac{(\bar{r_0^2})^{3/2}}{M}$$

Where:
- $\Phi$ = Flory's universal constant $\approx 2.5 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹ (in cgs units)
- $\bar{r_0^2}$ = Mean-square end-to-end distance
- $M$ = Molecular weight

This equation shows that **$[\eta]$ is essentially a measure of the hydrodynamic volume per unit mass** of the dissolved polymer.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Use | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}(\eta_{sp}/c)$ | Definition | Master equation | dL/g |
| 2 | $\eta_{rel} = \eta / \eta_s$ | Relative viscosity | Input | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ | Specific viscosity | Input | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins | Graphical extrap. | dL/g |
| 5 | $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c = [\eta] + k_K[\eta]^2 c$ | Kraemer | Graphical extrap. | dL/g |
| 6 | $k_H + k_K = 0.5$ | Consistency | Validity check | dimensionless |
| 7 | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$ | Mark–Houwink | MW determination | dL/g |
| 8 | $M_v = \left[\sum w_i M_i^a \right]^{1/a}$ | Viscosity-avg MW | Definition | g/mol |
| 9 | $[\eta] = \Phi (\bar{r_0^2})^{3/2}/M$ | Flory–Fox | Size info | — |
| 10 | $t_{rel} = t / t_s$ (capillary) | Time-based rel. visc. | Capillary data | dimensionless |
| 11 | $[\eta] \approx \frac{\sqrt{2(\eta_{sp}-\ln\eta_{rel})}}{c}$ | Solomon–Ciuta | Single-point | dL/g |
| 12 | $\nu_{sp} = \rho(t - k/t)$ | Kinematic correction | Capillary correction | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Capillary Viscometer Note:** For dilute solutions in Ubbelohde / Ostwald viscometers, $\eta_{rel} \approx t_{rel} = t/t_s$ (time ratio) because density corrections are negligible. KTU problems frequently use this simplification.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Science

Intrinsic viscosity is the **workhorse measurement** for characterizing food biopolymers because it is:

- **Non-destructive** — sample is recoverable
- **Absolute** — does not require calibration standards of identical polymer
- **Fast** — single capillary run per concentration
- **Inferential** — gives molecular weight, conformation, and solvent quality in **one** experiment

**Industrial food applications** (typical KTU contextual questions):
1. **Starch modification** — monitoring depolymerization during acid or enzymatic hydrolysis
2. **Pectin characterization** — distinguishing high-methoxy (HM) from low-methoxy (LM) pectins
3. **Protein denaturation** — monitoring conformational changes (globule $\to$ coil)
4. **Hydrocolloid quality control** — batch-to-batch consistency of guar, xanthan, CMC, alginate
5. **Shelf-life prediction** — degradation kinetics of thickening agents

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Huggins Equation from First Principles

We start from the **relative viscosity** $\eta_{rel} = \eta/\eta_s$ and use the Taylor series of $\ln(1+x)$:

$$\ln(\eta_{rel}) = \ln\left(1 + (\eta_{rel} - 1)\right) = \ln(1 + \eta_{sp})$$

Expanding $\ln(1 + \eta_{sp})$ as a power series in $\eta_{sp}$ (valid for small $\eta_{sp}$, i.e., dilute solution):

$$\ln(1 + \eta_{sp}) = \eta_{sp} - \frac{\eta_{sp}^2}{2} + \frac{\eta_{sp}^3}{3} - \cdots$$

Substitute $\eta_{sp} = [\eta] c + k_H [\eta]^2 c^2 + \cdots$ from Section 2.1:

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = \frac{1}{c}\left[\eta_{sp} - \frac{\eta_{sp}^2}{2} + \cdots\right]$$

Working out the algebra:

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = \frac{[\eta]c + k_H[\eta]^2 c^2 - \frac{1}{2}([\eta]c)^2 + \cdots}{c}$$

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c - \frac{[\eta]^2 c}{2} + \cdots$$

Grouping the $c$-linear terms:

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + \left(k_H - \frac{1}{2}\right)[\eta]^2 c + \cdots$$

Defining $k_K \equiv k_H - \frac{1}{2}$, we recover the **Kraemer equation**:

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + k_K[\eta]^2 c$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication of $k_H - k_K = 0.5$:** The relation $k_K = k_H - 0.5$ directly produces the consistency rule $k_H + k_K = 0.5$. This is **not** a coincidence — it follows rigorously from the series expansion of $\ln(1+\eta_{sp})$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Flory–Fox Equation (Sketch)

In a $\Theta$-solvent, the polymer behaves as an **unperturbed random coil**. The mean-square end-to-end distance is:

$$\bar{r_0^2} = n \cdot l^2$$

where $n$ is the number of segments and $l$ is the segment length. The **hydrodynamic volume** of the coil is:

$$V_h \propto (\bar{r_0^2})^{3/2}$$

The intrinsic viscosity represents the specific volume of solvated polymer:

$$[\eta] = \nu \cdot V_h / M$$

where $\nu$ is a numerical prefactor (the **Flory constant** $\Phi$). Combining:

$$[\eta] = \Phi \cdot \frac{(\bar{r_0^2})^{3/2}}{M}$$

This is the Flory–Fox equation. **Physical meaning**: $[\eta]$ measures the **hydrodynamic volume per unit mass** of the polymer coil in solution.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** The flow times for a pectin solution at four concentrations and for the pure solvent (in a Ubbelohde viscometer at 25°C) are given. Determine $[\eta]$ and $k_H$.

| $c$ (g/dL) | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0 (solvent) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $t$ (s) | 152.3 | 178.8 | 207.1 | 236.9 | 121.5 |

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{rel} = t/t_s$:**

At $c = 0.10$: $t_{rel} = 152.3 / 121.5 = 1.2535$
At $c = 0.20$: $t_{rel} = 178.8 / 121.5 = 1.4716$
At $c = 0.30$: $t_{rel} = 207.1 / 121.5 = 1.7045$
At $c = 0.40$: $t_{rel} = 236.9 / 121.5 = 1.9494$

**Step 2 — Compute $\eta_{sp} = t_{rel} - 1$ and $\eta_{sp}/c$:**

| $c$ | $t_{rel}$ | $\eta_{sp}$ | $\eta_{sp}/c$ (dL/g) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.10 | 1.2535 | 0.2535 | 2.535 |
| 0.20 | 1.4716 | 0.4716 | 2.358 |
| 0.30 | 1.7045 | 0.7045 | 2.348 |
| 0.40 | 1.9494 | 0.9494 | 2.374 |

**Step 3 — Linear regression of $\eta_{sp}/c$ vs. $c$:**

Let $x = c$, $y = \eta_{sp}/c$. Compute:

$$\bar{x} = 0.25, \quad \bar{y} = 2.404$$

$$S_{xx} = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.05$$

$$S_{xy} = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = -0.0800 + 0.0115 - 0.0140 + 0.0825 = 0.0000$$

Computing term-by-term carefully:

$(0.10 - 0.25)(2.535 - 2.404) = (-0.15)(0.131) = -0.01965$

$(0.20 - 0.25)(2.358 - 2.404) = (-0.05)(-0.046) = 0.00230$

$(0.30 - 0.25)(2.348 - 2.404) = (0.05)(-0.056) = -0.00280$

$(0.40 - 0.25)(2.374 - 2.404) = (0.15)(-0.030) = -0.00450$

$$S_{xy} = -0.01965 + 0.00230 - 0.00280 - 0.00450 = -0.02465$$

**Slope** = $S_{xy}/S_{xx} = -0.02465 / 0.05 = -0.493$ dL²/g²

**Intercept** = $\bar{y} - \text{slope} \cdot \bar{x} = 2.404 - (-0.493)(0.25) = 2.404 + 0.123 = 2.527$ dL/g

$$\boxed{[\eta] \approx 2.53 \text{ dL/g}}$$

$$k_H = \frac{\text{slope}}{[\eta]^2} = \frac{-0.493}{(2.527)^2} = \frac{-0.493}{6.385} = -0.0772$$

**Step 4 — Validity check (Huggins–Kraemer):**

A negative $k_H$ is **unphysical** for a non-aggregating polymer (typical $k_H \in [0.3, 0.4]$ for flexible coils in good solvents). The negative value here signals:
- Experimental noise
- Or that the linear approximation is insufficient at $c = 0.4$
- Or pectin aggregation at higher concentrations

In a real KTU problem set, the data would give a positive slope and a physically reasonable $k_H \approx 0.30$–$0.40$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method Selection Guide

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Multi-concentration data, dilute regime | Huggins + Kraemer (both) | Highest accuracy + cross-check via $k_H+k_K=0.5$ |
| Only one concentration available | Solomon–Ciuta | Single-point approximation |
| Strongly charged polymer (e.g., pectin, CMC) | Huggins at very low $c$ + added salt | Polyelectrolyte effects cause upward curvature |
| Quality control in industry | Single-point + calibrated standard | Speed |
| Research / publication | Full Huggins–Kraemer + Mark–Houwink | Rigor |
| Very low molecular weight ($< 10^4$ Da) | Differential viscometer | Signal-to-noise too low in capillary |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: How an Intrinsic Viscosity Measurement Becomes a Molecular Weight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: PREPARE DILUTE SOLUTIONS"] --> B["Step 2: MEASURE FLOW TIMES t"]
    B --> C["Step 3: COMPUTE t_rel AND eta_sp"]
    C --> D["Step 4: COMPUTE eta_sp per c"]
    D --> E["Step 5: PLOT eta_sp per c vs c"]
    E --> F["Step 6: LINEAR EXTRAPOLATE TO c EQUALS 0"]
    F --> G["INTERCEPT EQUALS INTRINSIC VISCOSITY"]
    F --> H["SLOPE EQUALS kH TIMES eta SQUARED"]
    G --> I["Step 7: APPLY MARK HOUWINK EQUATION"]
    I --> J["MOLECULAR WEIGHT M"]
    H --> K["Step 8: VALIDATE kH PLUS kK EQUALS 0.5"]
    K --> L["REPORT FINAL eta AND M WITH QUALITY FLAGS"]
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Three Extrapolation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DATA REQUIRED"]
        I1["Capillary flow times t at several c"]
        I2["Solvent flow time t_s"]
    end

    INPUT --> M1["METHOD A HUGGINS"]
    INPUT --> M2["METHOD B KRAEMER"]
    INPUT --> M3["METHOD C SOLOMON CIUTA"]

    M1 --> O1["Plot eta_sp per c vs c, intercept is eta"]
    M2 --> O2["Plot ln eta_rel per c vs c, intercept is eta"]
    M3 --> O3["Direct formula eta equals sqrt of 2 times eta_sp minus ln eta_rel all over c"]

    O1 --> OUT["FINAL OUTPUT eta in dL per g"]
    O2 --> OUT
    O3 --> OUT

    OUT --> APP["APPLICATION MARK HOUWINK TO GET MW"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Influence of Polymer Conformation on the Exponent $a$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MARK HOUWINK EXPONENT a"] --> B["a EQUALS 0 COMPACT GLOBULE"]
    A --> C["a 0.5 THETA SOLVENT"]
    A --> D["a 0.7 to 0.8 FLEXIBLE COIL GOOD SOLVENT"]
    A --> E["a 1.0 STIFF CHAIN"]
    A --> F["a 1.7 to 2.0 RIGID ROD"]

    B --> G["FOOD EXAMPLE GLOBULAR PROTEIN"]
    C --> H["FOOD EXAMPLE STARCH NEAR THETA"]
    D --> I["FOOD EXAMPLE XANTHAN GUAR"]
    E --> J["FOOD EXAMPLE HIGH MOLECULAR WEIGHT ALGINATE"]
    F --> K["FOOD EXAMPLE SOME CELLULOSE DERIVATIVES"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Sources of Error and Their Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ANOMALOUS INTRINSIC VISCOSITY DATA"] --> B["Upward curvature in Huggins plot"]
    A --> C["Downward curvature in Huggins plot"]
    A --> D["kH outside 0.3 to 0.5 range"]
    A --> E["kH plus kK far from 0.5"]

    B --> F["POLYELECTROLYTE EFFECT OR AGGREGATION"]
    C --> G["CONCENTRATION TOO HIGH OR STRUCTURAL BREAKDOWN"]
    D --> H["POSSIBLE ASSOCIATION OR NON GAUSSIAN CHAIN"]
    E --> I["DATA INCONSISTENCY RECHECK t AND c VALUES"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Define intrinsic viscosity. How is it distinguished from relative, specific, and reduced viscosities?**

**Model Answer:**

Intrinsic viscosity, denoted $[\eta]$, is defined as the limit of the ratio of specific viscosity to concentration as concentration approaches zero:

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}\left(\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c}\right)$$

It has units of **dL/g** and represents the contribution of an isolated polymer chain to the solution viscosity at infinite dilution. The distinctions are:

| Term | Definition | Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Relative viscosity $\eta_{rel}$ | $\eta/\eta_s$ | $> 1$ |
| Specific viscosity $\eta_{sp}$ | $\eta_{rel} - 1$ | $> 0$ |
| Reduced viscosity $\eta_{red}$ | $\eta_{sp}/c$ | Concentration-dependent |
| Inherent viscosity $\eta_{inh}$ | $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c$ | Concentration-dependent |
| **Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$** | $\lim_{c \to 0}(\eta_{sp}/c)$ | **Concentration-independent** |

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for table/differentiation, 1 for units and significance)

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**State and explain the Mark–Houwink equation. What does the exponent $a$ indicate about polymer conformation?**

**Model Answer:**

The Mark–Houwink equation is:

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$$

where $K$ and $a$ are constants that depend on the polymer–solvent–temperature system, and $M$ is the viscosity-average molecular weight.

The exponent $a$ is an **indicator of polymer conformation and solvent quality**:
- $a = 0$ : compact sphere (e.g., globular protein in aqueous media)
- $a = 0.5$ : random coil in a $\Theta$-solvent (ideal, unperturbed)
- $a = 0.5$ to $0.8$ : random coil in a good solvent
- $a = 1.0$ : semi-stiff chain
- $a \to 2.0$ : rigid rod

(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for explanation of $K$ and $a$, 1 for the conformational interpretation)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Huggins and Kraemer equations starting from the definition of specific viscosity. Show that the relation $k_H + k_K = 0.5$ must hold for dilute polymer solutions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The intrinsic viscosities of three pectin samples of identical chemical type were measured as 3.5, 5.2, and 7.8 dL/g in the same solvent at 25°C. Using the Mark–Houwink equation with $K = 1.4 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $a = 0.78$, estimate the viscosity-average molecular weight of each sample and comment on the conformational implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta}{\eta_s}, \qquad \eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$$

**Step 2 — Series expansion of $\eta_{sp}$ in $c$:**

In the dilute regime, $\eta_{sp}$ is a power series in concentration:

$$\eta_{sp} = [\eta] c + k_H [\eta]^2 c^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Huggins equation (divide by $c$):**

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c + \cdots$$

Linear extrapolation to $c = 0$ gives intercept $= [\eta]$. **This is the Huggins equation.**

**Step 4 — Kraemer equation derivation:**

Using $\ln(1+x) = x - x^2/2 + x^3/3 - \cdots$ for small $x$:

$$\ln(\eta_{rel}) = \ln(1 + \eta_{sp}) = \eta_{sp} - \frac{\eta_{sp}^2}{2} + \cdots$$

Substitute the series for $\eta_{sp}$:

$$\ln(\eta_{rel}) = [\eta]c + k_H[\eta]^2 c^2 - \frac{1}{2}([\eta]c)^2 + \cdots$$

$$\ln(\eta_{rel}) = [\eta]c + \left(k_H - \frac{1}{2}\right)[\eta]^2 c^2 + \cdots$$

Dividing by $c$:

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 c + \cdots$$

where $k_K = k_H - \frac{1}{2}$. **This is the Kraemer equation.**

**Step 5 — Consistency relation:**

Adding Huggins and Kraemer:

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} + \frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = 2[\eta] + (k_H + k_K)[\eta]^2 c$$

But using the algebraic identity $\ln(1+\eta_{sp}) + (1 - \eta_{rel}) = \eta_{rel} - 1 - \eta_{sp} - \eta_{sp}^2/2 + \cdots$, and grouping terms yields the identity:

$$k_H + k_K = \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the Huggins equation correctly but forget to *derive* the Kraemer equation. Marks are split ~3 for Huggins, ~3 for Kraemer, and ~1 for the consistency relation. You will lose 3 marks if you only state both equations without showing the derivation steps.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Mark–Houwink rearrangement:**

$$M = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}$$

With $K = 1.4 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.78$:

**Sample 1: $[\eta] = 3.5$ dL/g**

$$M_1 = \left(\frac{3.5}{1.4 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.78} = (25{,}000)^{1.282}$$

$$\ln M_1 = 1.282 \cdot \ln(25{,}000) = 1.282 \cdot 10.127 = 12.983$$

$$M_1 = e^{12.983} \approx 4.36 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Sample 2: $[\eta] = 5.2$ dL/g**

$$\frac{5.2}{1.4 \times 10^{-4}} = 37{,}143$$

$$\ln M_2 = 1.282 \cdot \ln(37{,}143) = 1.282 \cdot 10.523 = 13.491$$

$$M_2 = e^{13.491} \approx 7.18 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Sample 3: $[\eta] = 7.8$ dL/g**

$$\frac{7.8}{1.4 \times 10^{-4}} = 55{,}714$$

$$\ln M_3 = 1.282 \cdot \ln(55{,}714) = 1.282 \cdot 10.929 = 14.011$$

$$M_3 = e^{14.011} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{6} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Conformational comment:** The exponent $a = 0.78$ indicates that pectin behaves as a **semi-flexible random coil in a good solvent** in this system. The increase in $[\eta]$ with sample index reflects higher chain length and larger hydrodynamic volume, consistent with native, less-degraded pectin chains.

| Sample | $[\eta]$ (dL/g) | $M_v$ (g/mol) | Qualitative description |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 3.5 | $\approx 4.4 \times 10^5$ | Low-MW pectin (e.g., LMP) |
| 2 | 5.2 | $\approx 7.2 \times 10^5$ | Medium-MW pectin (e.g., RAP) |
| 3 | 7.8 | $\approx 1.2 \times 10^6$ | High-MW pectin (e.g., HMP) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Marks lost if students (i) forget to take the $1/a$ power correctly, (ii) confuse the **viscosity-average** molecular weight $M_v$ with $\bar{M}_w$ or $\bar{M}_n$, or (iii) state that all three samples have different conformations — they have the **same** conformation; only the chain length (and hence $[\eta]$) differs. The exponent $a$ is a property of polymer–solvent–temperature, not of the individual sample.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** What is intrinsic viscosity? With the help of suitable equations, explain how the Huggins and Kraemer plots are used to determine intrinsic viscosity experimentally. Discuss the physical significance of the Huggins constant $k_H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The flow times measured at 25°C in an Ubbelohde viscometer for an alginate solution at three concentrations and for the pure solvent are given below. Determine $[\eta]$, the Huggins constant $k_H$, and the molecular weight (given $K = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ dL/g, $a = 1.0$). Also verify the Huggins–Kraemer consistency relation by computing $k_K$ from $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c$ data. **(7 marks)**

| $c$ (g/dL) | 0.15 | 0.30 | 0.45 | 0 (solvent) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $t$ (s) | 145.2 | 173.0 | 203.4 | 119.8 |

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ is defined as:

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}\left(\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c}\right)$$

It has units of dL/g and represents the **hydrodynamic volume per unit mass** of an isolated polymer chain in solution, stripped of all intermolecular interactions.

**Huggins plot (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$$

A plot of $\eta_{sp}/c$ (y-axis) vs. $c$ (x-axis) yields a straight line. The **y-intercept** is $[\eta]$, and the **slope** is $k_H [\eta]^2$.

**Kraemer plot (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\ln(\eta_{rel})}{c} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 c$$

A plot of $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c$ vs. $c$ also gives a straight line with the same y-intercept $[\eta]$. **Two independent extrapolations** to the same intercept validate the measurement.

**Physical significance of $k_H$ (1.5 marks):**
- $k_H$ is a measure of **pairwise polymer–polymer interactions** in solution.
- Typical values: $k_H \approx 0.3$–$0.4$ for flexible random coils in good solvents; $k_H \approx 0.5$ in $\Theta$-solvents; $k_H > 0.5$ indicates **aggregation**; $k_H < 0$ indicates **preferential adsorption** of solvent on the chain.
- $k_H$ is **independent of molecular weight** for a given polymer-solvent-temperature system.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{rel} = t/t_s$, with $t_s = 119.8$ s:**

| $c$ | $t$ | $t_{rel}$ | $\eta_{sp}$ | $\eta_{sp}/c$ | $\ln(t_{rel})/c$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.15 | 145.2 | 1.2120 | 0.2120 | 1.4133 | 1.2780 |
| 0.30 | 173.0 | 1.4441 | 0.4441 | 1.4803 | 1.2255 |
| 0.45 | 203.4 | 1.6978 | 0.6978 | 1.5507 | 1.1725 |

Detailed computations for $c = 0.15$:

$$t_{rel} = 145.2 / 119.8 = 1.2120$$

$$\eta_{sp} = 0.2120$$

$$\eta_{sp}/c = 0.2120 / 0.15 = 1.4133$$

$$\ln(\eta_{rel}) = \ln(1.2120) = 0.19218$$

$$\ln(\eta_{rel})/c = 0.19218 / 0.15 = 1.2812$$

(Repeating for all rows as shown above.)

**Step 2 — Linear regression for Huggins:**

Let $x_i = c_i$, $y_i = (\eta_{sp}/c)_i$.

$\bar{x} = 0.30$, $\bar{y} = (1.4133 + 1.4803 + 1.5507)/3 = 1.4814$

$S_{xx} = (0.15-0.30)^2 + (0.30-0.30)^2 + (0.45-0.30)^2 = 0.0225 + 0 + 0.0225 = 0.0450$

$S_{xy} = (-0.15)(1.4133-1.4814) + (0)(1.4803-1.4814) + (0.15)(1.5507-1.4814)$

$S_{xy} = (-0.15)(-0.0681) + 0 + (0.15)(0.0693) = 0.01022 + 0 + 0.01040 = 0.02061$

$\text{Slope} = 0.02061 / 0.0450 = 0.4581$ dL²/g²

$\text{Intercept} = 1.4814 - 0.4581 \times 0.30 = 1.4814 - 0.1374 = 1.3440$ dL/g

$$\boxed{[\eta] = 1.344 \text{ dL/g}}$$

**Step 3 — Huggins constant:**

$$k_H = \frac{\text{slope}}{[\eta]^2} = \frac{0.4581}{(1.344)^2} = \frac{0.4581}{1.8063} = 0.2536$$

**Step 4 — Mark–Houwink calculation:**

With $a = 1.0$:

$$M = \frac{[\eta]}{K} = \frac{1.344}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.72 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Kraemer regression (for cross-check):**

$y_i = \ln(\eta_{rel})/c$: $\bar{y} = (1.2812 + 1.2255 + 1.1725)/3 = 1.2264$

$S_{xy}^{K} = (-0.15)(1.2812-1.2264) + 0 + (0.15)(1.1725-1.2264)$

$S_{xy}^{K} = (-0.15)(0.0548) + 0 + (0.15)(-0.0539) = -0.00822 - 0.00809 = -0.01631$

$\text{Slope}_K = -0.01631 / 0.0450 = -0.3624$

$\text{Intercept}_K = 1.2264 - (-0.3624)(0.30) = 1.2264 + 0.1087 = 1.3351$ dL/g ✓ (matches $[\eta] = 1.344$ within rounding)

$$k_K = \frac{-0.3624}{(1.344)^2} = -0.2007$$

**Step 6 — Consistency check:**

$$k_H + k_K = 0.2536 + (-0.2007) = 0.0529$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Diagnostic interpretation:** The Huggins–Kraemer sum $0.053$ deviates significantly from the theoretical value $0.5$. This is a **classic signature of a polyelectrolyte (alginate is in fact a polyelectrolyte — a copolymer of mannuronate and guluronate with –COO⁻ groups)**, or of inadequate ionic-strength buffering. To obtain a reliable $k_H$ in such systems, the experiment must be repeated in the presence of a low-molarity salt (e.g., 0.01–0.1 M NaCl) to suppress polyelectrolyte effects.

**Final Results Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $[\eta]$ | $1.34$ dL/g |
| $k_H$ | $0.254$ |
| $k_K$ | $-0.201$ |
| $k_H + k_K$ | $0.053$ (theoretical: 0.5) |
| $M_v$ | $6.72 \times 10^4$ g/mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> 1. **Significant figures:** Keep at least 4 decimal places in intermediate calculations; round only at the end. (Loss of 1 mark if $[\eta]$ is reported as 1.3 instead of 1.34.)
> 2. **Interpretation of $k_H$:** A value outside the 0.3–0.5 range is **not** "wrong" — it is a **diagnostic** of polyelectrolyte or aggregation behavior. KTU expects you to *explain* this, not ignore it.
> 3. **Units:** Forgetting to convert $t$ ratio to $\eta_{rel}$ via the density assumption $\rho \approx \rho_s$ is a common error. (Loss of 2 marks.)
> 4. **Sign of slope:** For Huggins, slope is **positive**; for Kraemer, slope is **negative** (because $k_K < 0.5 < k_H$). Students often reverse the Kraemer equation and lose full sub-part marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Intrinsic Viscosity**

### Must-Recall Equations (Box-of-Life for KTU)
- $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}(\eta_{sp}/c)$ — **the master definition**
- $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1 = (\eta/\eta_s) - 1$
- $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ — **Huggins**
- $\ln(\eta_{rel})/c = [\eta] + k_K[\eta]^2 c$ — **Kraemer**
- $k_H + k_K = 0.5$ — **consistency**
- $[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$ — **Mark–Houwink**
- $[\eta] = \Phi \cdot (\bar{r_0^2})^{3/2} / M$ — **Flory–Fox**

### Must-Recall Facts
- **Units:** $[\eta]$ in dL/g when $c$ in g/dL
- **Sign of $k_H$:** Positive in normal regime ($k_H \approx 0.3$–$0.5$); deviates for polyelectrolytes
- **Physical meaning:** $[\eta]$ = hydrodynamic volume per unit mass of an isolated polymer coil
- **Exponent $a$ interpretation:** 0 = sphere; 0.5 = $\Theta$-coil; 0.5–0.8 = random coil good solvent; 1 = stiff chain; $\to 2$ = rod
- **Capillary viscometer shortcut:** $\eta_{rel} \approx t_{rel} = t/t_s$ for dilute aqueous solutions
- **Single-point alternative:** $[\eta] \approx \sqrt{2(\eta_{sp} - \ln\eta_{rel})}/c$ (Solomon–Ciuta)

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using $\eta_{red}$ in place of $\eta_{sp}/c$ in the Huggins equation
- ❌ Plotting $c$ on the y-axis instead of x-axis
- ❌ Reporting $[\eta]$ without specifying units
- ❌ Forgetting to take the $1/a$ power when inverting Mark–Houwink
- ❌ Assuming $k_H$ and $k_K$ are independent — they are linked by $k_H + k_K = 0.5$
- ❌ Confusing $M_v$ (viscosity-average) with $M_w$ (weight-average) and $M_n$ (number-average)
- ❌ Applying the Mark–Houwink equation outside the calibration range of $K$ and $a$
- ❌ Ignoring polyelectrolyte effects for charged biopolymers (alginate, pectin, CMC, carrageenan)

### High-Yield Food-Science Examples to Memorize
- **Starch (amylose):** $a \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ in DMSO or 0.1 M NaOH
- **Xanthan gum:** $a \approx 1.0$ (semi-rigid)
- **Alginate:** polyelectrolyte, requires added salt
- **Pectin:** $a \approx 0.78$–$1.0$ depending on degree of esterification
- **Globular proteins:** $a \approx 0$ in native state; increases on denaturation

### Last-Minute Exam Tip
> If asked "what is intrinsic viscosity?" in two lines, write:
> 1. **Definition:** limit of $\eta_{sp}/c$ as $c \to 0$
> 2. **Significance:** hydrodynamic volume per unit mass of an isolated macromolecule
> Then add: **Mark–Houwink** connects it to **molecular weight**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Newtonian Fluids — Formal Definition A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid whose rheological behavior obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity** in its strictest, most fundamental form. The shear stress ($\tau$) developed within the fluid is **directly proportional** to the rate of shear strain (shear rate, $\dot{\gamma}$), and the proportionality constant — the **dynamic viscosity ($\eta$)** — remains **constant**, independent of the magnitude or duration of the applied shear. Mathematically expressed as: $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ The constant $\eta$ is measured in **Pa·s (Pascal-seconds)** or equivalently **N·s/m²** in SI units, and **cP (centipoise)** in CGS (where $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEFTT745 / Module 2):** > Newtonian fluids form the **baseline reference** for all food rheology measurements. Every deviation from this linear, time-independent behavior is classified as **non-Newtonian**, which is the *real interest zone* in food applications since the vast majority of foods (sauces, doughs, batters, gels, melts) exhibit non-Newtonian behavior. ## 1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids — Formal Definition A **non-Newtonian fluid** is any fluid in which the relationship between shear stress ($\tau$) and shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) is **not linear**, and for which the apparent viscosity is a function of one or more of the following variables: **shear rate, shear history, time, or temperature**. In other words, the viscosity is **not constant** but varies with the kinematic and thermodynamic state of the material. The general constitutive equation is written as: $$\tau = \eta(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ where $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ denotes the **apparent (shear-dependent) viscosity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Focus Point:** When the question says *"classify fluids based on rheological behavior"*, examiners expect students to write: (1) the defining equation of a Newtonian fluid, (2) the statement of deviation for non-Newtonian, and (3) a **flow curve sketch** showing $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$. A diagram always secures additional credit. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are stirring two glasses of liquid on a table: - **Glass A (Newtonian — e.g., water, milk, refined oils):** As you stir faster, the resistance increases **proportionally and predictably**. If you double the stirring speed, the resistance doubles. The liquid "feels the same" at slow or fast stirring — only the magnitude changes, not the character. - **Glass B (Non-Newtonian — e.g., tomato ketchup, honey, toothpaste):** As you stir faster, the resistance might **drop dramatically** (shear-thinning, like ketchup flowing off a spoon) or **increase** (shear-thickening, like a cornstarch slurry that hardens on a sudden impact). Some even "remember" being stirred (thixotropy) or stiffen with continuous stirring (rheopexy). > [!TIP] > **Real-world culinary analogy:** > - **Water** → Newtonian (the gold-standard baseline). > - **Honey** → Newtonian but with very **high viscosity** (the line is straight, just steeper). > - **Tomato ketchup** → Non-Newtonian **shear-thinning** (you shake the bottle to make it flow). > - **Cornstarch + water** → Non-Newtonian **shear-thickening** (run your fingers through it slowly, it is liquid; punch it, it turns solid). > - **Yogurt** → Non-Newtonian **yield stress fluid** (it does not flow until you apply a minimum force). ## 1.4 The Shear Stress and Shear Rate — Foundational Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | | Shear stress | $\tau$ | Force per unit area acting tangentially on a fluid layer | **Pa** (N/m²) | | Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma}$ | Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction | **s⁻¹** | | Dynamic viscosity | $\eta$ | Internal friction / resistance to flow | **Pa·s** | | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $\eta / \rho$ (viscosity/density ratio) | **m²/s** | | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{ap}$ | $\tau / \dot{\gamma}$ at a given $\dot{\gamma}$ | **Pa·s** | > [!NOTE] > The **dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C** is **$\eta_{water} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$** (or **1.002 cP**). This value is the universal benchmark against which all other fluid viscosities are compared in food rheology labs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Fluids Based on Rheological Behavior Food materials (and fluids in general) are classified by plotting **shear stress ($\tau$)** on the y-axis against **shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** on the x-axis. This plot is called the **flow curve** or **rheogram**. ### 2.1.1 Newtonian Fluids (Linear) - The flow curve is a **straight line** passing through the **origin (0,0)**. - Slope = $\eta$ (constant). - **Examples:** Water, milk, refined vegetable oils, fruit juices (filtered), air, most simple sugar solutions. - **No yield stress, no time-dependence, no shear history effect.** ### 2.1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids (Non-Linear or Time-Dependent) These are sub-classified into three major families: **A. Time-Independent (Shear History-Insensitive) Non-Newtonian Fluids** | Sub-class | Mathematical Model | Curve Shape | Real Food Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $0 < n < 1$ | Concave down, no yield stress | Ketchup, fruit purees, melted chocolate, blood | | **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$ | Concave up, no yield stress | Cornstarch suspensions, wet sand | | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Straight line, intercept on y-axis | Toothpaste, margarine (cold), some butter | | **Casson Plastic** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | Curved, intercept on y-axis | Chocolate (molten), tomato sauce | | **Herschel-Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Curved, intercept on y-axis | Bread dough, fondant, some gels | **B. Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids** - **Thixotropic:** Apparent viscosity **decreases** with time under constant shear, and **recovers** when shear is removed (structural breakdown & rebuilding). Example: yogurt, some gels, mustard. - **Rheopectic:** Apparent viscosity **increases** with time under constant shear. Example: rare; some protein suspensions, printer ink. **C. Viscoelastic Fluids** - Exhibit both **viscous (liquid-like)** and **elastic (solid-like)** behavior simultaneously. Example: bread dough, molten cheese, polymer melts, biopolymer solutions. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Behavior - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** arises because long-chain molecules (starch, proteins, gums like xanthan) align in the direction of flow under shear, reducing entanglement and internal friction. Apparent viscosity drops. - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** arises in dense suspensions of closely packed particles; under shear, particles push apart, the fluid volume appears to expand ("dilate"), and resistance rises sharply. - **Yield stress** behavior is caused by an internal **three-dimensional network** (e.g., fat crystal network, protein gel matrix) that must be broken before flow initiates. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — it covers approximately 80% of the numerical questions asked in KTU ESE for this module.** | # | Model / Concept | Governing Equation | Parameters & Units | Curve Through Origin? | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 1 | **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ (Pa·s) | **Yes** | | 2 | **Power-law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $K$ (Pa·sⁿ) — consistency index; $n$ (—) — flow behavior index | Yes | | 3 | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_{0}$ (Pa) — yield stress; $\eta_{p}$ (Pa·s) — plastic viscosity | **No** (intercept $\tau_{0}$) | | 4 | **Casson** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_{0}$ (Pa); $\eta_{c}$ (Pa·s) — Casson viscosity | **No** (intercept $\tau_{0}$) | | 5 | **Herschel-Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\tau_{0}$ (Pa); $K$ (Pa·sⁿ); $n$ (—) | **No** (intercept $\tau_{0}$) | | 6 | Apparent viscosity (general) | $\eta_{ap} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | — | | 7 | Power-law apparent viscosity | $\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | — | | 8 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | m²/s | — | > [!TIP] > **Flow behavior index ($n$) quick reference (KTU favourite short question):** > - $n = 1$ → Newtonian (special case of power-law). > - $n < 1$ → Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning). > - $n > 1$ → Dilatant (shear-thickening). > - $K$ is large → highly viscous; $K$ is small → "runny". ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry - **Pipe-line design:** Pumping ketchup vs. water requires different pressure drops; non-Newtonian models are essential. - **Quality control:** Consistency index $K$ and flow index $n$ are used as **sensory correlates** of "thickness" and "mouthfeel". - **Process design:** Mixing, extrusion, coating, spray-drying, and filling all depend on rheological data. - **Shelf-life prediction:** Time-dependent fluids (thixotropic) help predict settling, syneresis, and structural collapse in stored products. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Law to the Power-Law Model The fundamental starting point is **Newton's Law of Viscosity** for a Newtonian fluid: $$\tau_{yx} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{yx}$$ where the subscript $yx$ denotes stress acting in the $x$-direction on a plane normal to the $y$-axis (standard rheological tensor notation). This assumes $\eta$ is a **scalar constant** at constant $T$ and $P$. For a non-Newtonian, time-independent fluid, we generalize this by writing $\eta$ as a function of $\dot{\gamma}$: $$\tau = \eta(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ A particularly useful empirical form is the **Ostwald-de Waele (power-law) model**: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** - **Step 1 — Special case check:** If we set $n = 1$, then $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{1} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$. Comparing with $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, we see that $K$ reduces to $\eta$. Thus, the power-law model **contains Newtonian behavior** as a special case when $n=1$. - **Step 2 — Apparent viscosity derivation:** Apparent viscosity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at any operating point: $$\eta_{ap} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ Substituting $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ into the numerator: $$\eta_{ap} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$ - **Step 3 — Logarithmic linearization (for graphical data fitting):** Taking the natural log of both sides of $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$: $$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + n \cdot \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$ This is a **straight line** when $\ln(\tau)$ is plotted against $\ln(\dot{\gamma})$, with slope $n$ and intercept $\ln(K)$. This is the standard method used in food rheology labs to extract $n$ and $K$ from experimental data. - **Step 4 — Physical interpretation of $n$:** - If $n < 1$: $\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Since $n-1 < 0$, increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ *decreases* $\eta_{ap}$ → **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. - If $n > 1$: $\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Since $n-1 > 0$, increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ *increases* $\eta_{ap}$ → **dilatant (shear-thickening)**. - If $n = 1$: $\eta_{ap} = K = \eta$ (constant) → **Newtonian**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Power-Law and Bingham/Herschel-Bulkley The **Bingham plastic** model postulates a finite yield stress $\tau_{0}$ plus a linear viscous term: $$\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ The **Herschel-Bulkley (H-B) model** generalizes Bingham by replacing the linear viscous term with a power-law term: $$\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ **Step-by-step limit checks:** - If $\tau_{0} = 0$ in H-B: $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ → reduces to **power-law** (Newtonian if $n=1$). - If $n = 1$ in H-B: $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{1}$ → reduces to **Bingham plastic** (with $K = \eta_{p}$). - If $\tau_{0} = 0$ AND $n = 1$: $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ → reduces to **Newtonian** (with $K = \eta$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy of fluid models (KTU favourite 7-mark question):** > Newtonian ⊂ Power-law (n=1 case) ⊂ Bingham (τ₀=0 case) ⊂ Herschel-Bulkley (most general). > Master this nested hierarchy — examiners love asking it. ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern) **Problem:** A fruit puree at 25 °C obeys the power-law model. At a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$, the shear stress is $\tau = 35 \text{ Pa}$. At $\dot{\gamma} = 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$, the shear stress is $\tau = 90 \text{ Pa}$. Find (a) the flow behavior index $n$ and consistency index $K$, and (b) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$. Classify the fluid. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup two equations using the power-law model:** $$\tau_{1} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{1}^{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 35 = K \cdot (50)^{n}$$ $$\tau_{2} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{2}^{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 90 = K \cdot (200)^{n}$$ **Step 2 — Divide equation 2 by equation 1** (this eliminates $K$): $$\frac{90}{35} = \frac{K \cdot (200)^{n}}{K \cdot (50)^{n}}$$ $$2.5714 = \left(\frac{200}{50}\right)^{n} = (4)^{n}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $n$ by taking natural log:** $$\ln(2.5714) = n \cdot \ln(4)$$ $$0.9445 = n \cdot 1.3863$$ $$n = \frac{0.9445}{1.3863} = 0.6813 \approx 0.68$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $K$** by substituting $n$ back into equation 1: $$35 = K \cdot (50)^{0.6813}$$ $$(50)^{0.6813} = e^{0.6813 \cdot \ln(50)} = e^{0.6813 \cdot 3.9120} = e^{2.6652} = 14.37$$ $$K = \frac{35}{14.37} = 2.435 \text{ Pa·s}^{n}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:** $$\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 2.435 \cdot (100)^{0.6813 - 1}$$ $$(100)^{-0.3187} = e^{-0.3187 \cdot \ln(100)} = e^{-0.3187 \cdot 4.6052} = e^{-1.4673} = 0.2306$$ $$\eta_{ap} = 2.435 \cdot 0.2306 = 0.5616 \text{ Pa·s} \approx 0.562 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 6 — Classification:** Since $n = 0.68 < 1$, the fruit puree is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. > [!TIP] > **KTU numerical check — always verify dimensional consistency:** $K$ has units of $\text{Pa·s}^{n}$, not just Pa·s. Do not lose the superscript $n$ — it is a common valuation pitfall. # 4. For Bingham, draw a **straight line starting at $\tau_{0}$ on y-axis**. Always label axes with symbols and units. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B] **Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two food examples for each.** **Model Answer:** A **Newtonian fluid** obeys Newton's law of viscosity strictly — shear stress is directly proportional to shear rate, and the proportionality constant (viscosity $\eta$) is independent of shear rate. The flow curve is a straight line passing through the origin. $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ A **non-Newtonian fluid** does not obey this linear relationship — the apparent viscosity varies with shear rate, time, or shear history. The flow curve is non-linear or has a yield stress. $$\tau = \eta(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ | | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Food example 1** | Water | Tomato ketchup | | **Food example 2** | Refined sunflower oil | Bread dough | | **Viscosity behavior** | Constant | Variable (shear-dependent) | | **Flow curve** | Linear through origin | Curved or has yield stress | *(Mark allocation: Definition 1M, equation 1M, comparison table or examples 1M.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **What is the significance of the flow behavior index 'n' in the power-law model? How is the fluid classified for different values of n?** **Model Answer:** The **flow behavior index $n$** is a dimensionless parameter in the **power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) model**, $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$. It quantifies the **degree of deviation from Newtonian behavior** and characterizes the shear-thinning or shear-thickening nature of the fluid. **Classification of fluid based on $n$:** - **$n = 1$** → **Newtonian fluid** (the model reduces to $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, with $K = \eta$). - **$n < 1$** → **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** fluid. Apparent viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate. Examples: ketchup, fruit purees. - **$n > 1$** → **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** fluid. Apparent viscosity increases with increasing shear rate. Examples: cornstarch slurry, wet sand. *(Mark allocation: Definition of n 1M, three classification cases with examples 2M.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled rheogram, explain the rheological behavior of (i) Newtonian fluid, (ii) Pseudoplastic fluid, and (iii) Bingham plastic fluid. Mention two food examples for each. **Model Answer:** A **rheogram** is a plot of shear stress ($\tau$) versus shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$), and it is the standard tool for classifying fluid behavior in food rheology. **(i) Newtonian Fluid:** - The flow curve is a **straight line passing through the origin**. - Slope = constant viscosity $\eta$. - Equation: $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$. - **Food examples:** Water, milk, refined vegetable oils, filtered fruit juices, sugar solutions. **(ii) Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) Fluid:** - The flow curve is a **concave-down curve passing through the origin** (no yield stress, but slope decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$). - Obeys power-law: $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $0 < n < 1$. - Apparent viscosity $\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ decreases with increasing $\dot{\gamma}$. - **Food examples:** Tomato ketchup, fruit purees, melted chocolate, blood, xanthan gum solutions. **(iii) Bingham Plastic Fluid:** - The flow curve is a **straight line that does not pass through the origin**; it intersects the shear stress axis at a finite value $\tau_{0}$ (the **yield stress**). - Below $\tau_{0}$, the material behaves as a solid (no flow). Above $\tau_{0}$, it flows with a constant plastic viscosity $\eta_{p}$. - Equation: $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$. - **Food examples:** Cold margarine, toothpaste, mustard (when undisturbed), some butter formulations. *(Mark allocation: Rheogram sketch with axes 1M; each fluid explanation 2M = 6M; examples integrated.)* **(b) [7 Marks]** A salad dressing is found to follow the Casson model with yield stress $\tau_{0} = 4 \text{ Pa}$ and Casson viscosity $\eta_{c} = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s}$. Calculate the shear stress at a shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and the apparent viscosity at this shear rate. Comment on whether the dressing is shear-thinning or not. **Model Solution:** The Casson model is given by: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{\tau_{0}}$:** $$\sqrt{\tau_{0}} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$:** $$\sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 100} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$ **Step 3 — Sum the two terms to get $\sqrt{\tau}$:** $$\sqrt{\tau} = 2 + 7.071 = 9.071 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$ **Step 4 — Square to obtain shear stress $\tau$:** $$\tau = (9.071)^{2} = 82.28 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 5 — Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:** $$\eta_{ap} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{82.28}{100} = 0.8228 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 6 — Comment on shear-thinning behavior:** The Casson model is a **non-Newtonian** model with a finite yield stress. To check for shear-thinning, we compute $\eta_{ap}$ at a different $\dot{\gamma}$ (e.g., $\dot{\gamma} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$): $$\sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 10} = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$ $$\sqrt{\tau} = 2 + 2.236 = 4.236 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$ $$\tau = (4.236)^{2} = 17.94 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\eta_{ap}(10) = \frac{17.94}{10} = 1.794 \text{ Pa·s}$$ Since $\eta_{ap}$ **decreases** from $1.794 \text{ Pa·s}$ (at $\dot{\gamma} = 10 \text{ s}^{-1}$) to $0.823 \text{ Pa·s}$ (at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$), the salad dressing exhibits **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) behavior** — consistent with the Casson model's known behavior for many food emulsions. *(Mark allocation: Casson equation 1M, step-wise substitution 3M, apparent viscosity 1M, comparison & comment 2M.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Herschel-Bulkley model. Show that it is a generalization of the Newtonian, Power-law, and Bingham plastic models. Discuss its applicability to food systems with two examples. **Model Answer:** The **Herschel-Bulkley (H-B) model** is a three-parameter rheological model that combines a **yield stress** with a **power-law viscous term**. It is the most general of the common time-independent non-Newtonian models used in food rheology. **Governing equation:** $$\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ where: - $\tau_{0}$ = yield stress (Pa), - $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ), - $n$ = flow behavior index (dimensionless). **Generalization proof — limit cases:** - **Case 1 — $\tau_{0} = 0$:** Equation becomes $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$, which is the **power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) model**. If additionally $n = 1$, then $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ → **Newtonian fluid** with $K = \eta$. - **Case 2 — $n = 1$:** Equation becomes $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$, which is the **Bingham plastic model** with plastic viscosity $\eta_{p} = K$. - **Case 3 — $\tau_{0} = 0$ and $n = 1$:** Equation becomes $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ → **Newtonian fluid**. **Hierarchy:** $$\text{Newtonian} \subset \text{Power-law} \subset \text{Bingham} \subset \text{Herschel-Bulkley}$$ **Applicability to food systems:** The H-B model is especially suited for foods that exhibit **both a yield stress AND a non-linear flow curve above the yield**, which is the case for many structured foods. **Food examples:** 1. **Bread dough** — has a measurable yield stress and non-linear shear-thinning behavior above it. 2. **Fondant icing** — needs a yield stress to hold its shape, but flows smoothly under spreading stress. *(Mark allocation: H-B equation 1M, three generalization cases 3M, hierarchy statement 1M, food examples 2M.)* **(b) [7 Marks]** A tomato sauce at 30 °C follows the power-law model. Using the data below, determine the consistency index $K$ and flow behavior index $n$: | Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa) | | :---: | :---: | | 10 | 18 | | 100 | 95 | Calculate the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and classify the fluid. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write the power-law model** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ for both data points: $$18 = K \cdot (10)^{n}$$ $$95 = K \cdot (100)^{n}$$ **Step 2 — Divide the second equation by the first** to eliminate $K$: $$\frac{95}{18} = \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{n} = (10)^{n}$$ $$5.2778 = (10)^{n}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $n$ using logarithm:** $$n = \frac{\ln(5.2778)}{\ln(10)} = \frac{1.6644}{2.3026} = 0.7228 \approx 0.72$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $K$ by back-substitution:** $$18 = K \cdot (10)^{0.7228}$$ $$(10)^{0.7228} = e^{0.7228 \cdot \ln(10)} = e^{0.7228 \cdot 2.3026} = e^{1.6643} = 5.277$$ $$K = \frac{18}{5.277} = 3.412 \text{ Pa·s}^{n}$$ **Step 5 — Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$:** $$\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 3.412 \cdot (50)^{0.7228 - 1} = 3.412 \cdot (50)^{-0.2772}$$ $$(50)^{-0.2772} = e^{-0.2772 \cdot \ln(50)} = e^{-0.2772 \cdot 3.9120} = e^{-1.0844} = 0.3385$$ $$\eta_{ap} = 3.412 \cdot 0.3385 = 1.155 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 6 — Classification:** Since $n = 0.72 < 1$, the tomato sauce is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** — which is consistent with real tomato sauces (e.g., ketchup). *(Mark allocation: Two equations 1M, n derivation 2M, K derivation 1M, apparent viscosity 2M, classification 1M.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks in KTU ESE:** > > 1. **Forgetting the units of $K$:** Writing "$K = 3.4$ Pa·s" instead of "$K = 3.4 \text{ Pa·s}^{n}$". The superscript $n$ is **mandatory** when $n \neq 1$. > > 2. **Confusing "apparent viscosity" with "consistency index":** Apparent viscosity is **a function of $\dot{\gamma}$**; $K$ is a **constant** (model parameter). Do not interchange them. > > 3. **Not stating the flow-curve shape:** When asked to "classify", always describe the rheogram shape in words: "straight line through origin" / "concave-down curve through origin" / "straight line with y-axis intercept". Verbal description alone secures 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Mixing up thixotropy and pseudoplasticity:** Pseudoplasticity is **time-independent**; thixotropy is **time-dependent**. Pseudoplasticity is a steady-state property; thixotropy requires observation of viscosity vs. time at constant shear. > > 5. **Forgetting to verify $n=1$ as the Newtonian limit:** If asked "show that power-law reduces to Newtonian when $n=1$", students often skip the substitution step. Always show: $n = 1 \Rightarrow \tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{1} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}$; comparing with $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ gives $K = \eta$. > > 6. **Unit errors in kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \eta / \rho$. Units: $\text{Pa·s} / (\text{kg/m}^{3}) = \text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$. A common slip is writing $\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}^{2}$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-second revision summary — read this the night before the exam:** - **Newtonian fluid:** $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$; $\eta$ = constant; flow curve = straight line through origin. Examples: water, milk, refined oils. - **Non-Newtonian fluid:** $\eta$ varies with $\dot{\gamma}$, time, or shear history. - **Power-law model:** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$; contains Newtonian as the special case $n=1$, $K=\eta$. - **Apparent viscosity (power-law):** $\eta_{ap} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** $n < 1$; $\eta_{ap}$ drops with $\dot{\gamma}$. Examples: ketchup, chocolate, blood, fruit purees. - **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** $n > 1$; $\eta_{ap}$ rises with $\dot{\gamma}$. Examples: cornstarch slurry, wet sand. - **Bingham plastic:** $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$; has yield stress $\tau_{0}$ and linear post-yield behavior. Examples: toothpaste, cold butter. - **Casson model:** $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}} + \sqrt{\eta_{c} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$; used for chocolate, tomato sauce. - **Herschel-Bulkley model:** $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$; most general time-independent model; reduces to power-law if $\tau_{0}=0$ and to Bingham if $n=1$. Used for bread dough, fondant. - **Thixotropy:** time-dependent **decrease** in viscosity under constant shear (structural breakdown). Examples: yogurt, gels, mustard. - **Rheopexy:** time-dependent **increase** in viscosity under constant shear (rare in foods). - **Viscoelasticity:** dual viscous + elastic response. Examples: dough, molten cheese, polymer melts. - **Model hierarchy:** Newtonian ⊂ Power-law ⊂ Bingham ⊂ Herschel-Bulkley. - **Data fitting trick:** Plot $\ln(\tau)$ vs $\ln(\dot{\gamma})$ → straight line of slope $n$ and intercept $\ln(K)$. - **Key unit benchmarks:** $\eta_{water}(20°C) = 1.002 \text{ cP} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$; $K$ in $\text{Pa·s}^{n}$; $n$ dimensionless; $\tau_{0}$ in Pa. - **Always** state units, **always** mention food examples, **always** sketch the rheogram when asked to "explain" or "classify".

topics.title: Pseudoplastic Flow Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pseudoplastic Flow Behaviour
