<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — Pseudoplastic Flow Behaviour

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pseudoplastic flow** (also known as **shear-thinning flow**) is a non-ideal, time-independent, non-Newtonian flow behaviour in which the **apparent viscosity of the fluid decreases monotonically as the shear rate (or shear stress) increases**. In mathematical terms, the fluid exhibits a flow behaviour index **n < 1** when fitted to the Ostwald-de Waele (Power Law) model.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this behaviour is studied under *time-independent non-Newtonian fluids*, and is mathematically represented by the constitutive equation:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where the constraint **0 < n < 1** defines pseudoplasticity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pseudoplastic behaviour is one of the **most commonly observed flow patterns in food systems**. Most structured foods (ketchup, yogurt, fruit purees, salad dressings, concentrated milk, polymer melts) display this behaviour because their microstructure breaks down progressively with shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Pseudoplastic flow is **time-independent** (the viscosity drop is instantaneous and reversible when shear is removed). It must NOT be confused with **thixotropy**, where the structural breakdown is *time-dependent* and the recovery requires finite rest time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ketchup Bottle Intuition 🥫

Imagine you have a stubborn ketchup bottle that refuses to pour:

- **At rest (low shear):** The ketchup feels like a thick paste — high viscosity. Tomato pulp, pectin, and starch granules form an interconnected 3D network that traps water and resists flow.
- **As you shake/squeeze harder (higher shear):** The network progressively breaks apart. Long-chain pectin molecules align with the flow direction, and flocs of tomato solids disperse. Suddenly, the ketchup flows freely!
- **When you stop squeezing:** The structure begins to rebuild, and the ketchup once again feels thick.

This instantaneous, reversible "**thick when still, thin when sheared**" behaviour is the hallmark of pseudoplastic flow. The same phenomenon explains why:
- **Yogurt** is firm in the cup but spreads easily when scooped.
- **Toothpaste** stays on the brush but extrudes smoothly when squeezed.
- **Paint** stays on the brush but flows evenly on the wall when brushed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Mechanism:** Pseudoplasticity arises from the progressive **de-agglomeration of structural units** (flocs, droplets, polymer coils, macromolecular entanglements) and the **orientation/alignment of anisotropic particles** along streamlines as shear increases.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The two fundamental rheological parameters characterising pseudoplastic fluids are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Foods) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow Behaviour Index | n | **0.1 – 0.8** | dimensionless |
| Consistency Index | K | **1 – 100** | Pa·sⁿ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Newtonian limit** corresponds to **n = 1** (with K = μ). As **n → 0**, the fluid approaches the behaviour of a **perfectly plastic solid** (Bingham-type).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constitutive Framework — The Ostwald-de Waele Power Law

The Power Law model (also known as the **Ostwald-de Waele equation**) is the simplest and most widely used two-parameter rheological model to describe pseudoplastic fluids. It relates shear stress (τ) to shear rate (γ̇) as:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

The corresponding **apparent viscosity** (η_app) is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at a given shear rate:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

### 2.1.1 Why n < 1 Produces Shear-Thinning

Let us examine the apparent viscosity expression:

- If **n = 1**: η_app = K (constant → **Newtonian** behaviour).
- If **n < 1**: The exponent **(n − 1) is negative**, so as γ̇ increases, γ̇^(n−1) decreases → η_app decreases → **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**.
- If **n > 1**: The exponent **(n − 1) is positive**, so as γ̇ increases, η_app increases → **shear-thickening (dilatant)**.

## 2.2 The Logarithmic Form — Linearising the Power Law

For graphical determination of K and n from experimental rheometric data, the power law is linearised by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + n \cdot \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$

A plot of **ln(τ)** vs **ln(γ̇)** gives a straight line:
- **Intercept** = ln(K) → K = e^(intercept)
- **Slope** = n

This is the standard KTU board examination approach for calculating rheological parameters from rheogram data.

## 2.3 Structural Origins in Food Systems

The microstructural basis for pseudoplasticity in foods can be summarised:

1. **Polymer entanglement networks:** Starch chains, proteins, pectins form temporary entanglements. Shear disentangles them.
2. **Flocculated particulate systems:** Tomato pulp cells, fat globule clusters in cream, cocoa particles in chocolate — shear de-flocculates the aggregates.
3. **Droplet deformation in emulsions:** Mayonnaise, salad cream — droplets elongate and align under shear.
4. **Cellular networks:** Plant tissues in purees — cell-cell junctions progressively rupture.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Food Processing

| Food Process | Relevance of Pseudoplasticity |
|---|---|
| **Pipe-line design** | Pressure drop calculations require n & K, not μ |
| **Pump selection** | Shear-thinning affects pump power requirements |
| **Mixing & agitation** | Determines impeller torque and mixing time |
| **Extrusion** | Predicts die swell and extrudate quality |
| **Filling operations** | Bottle/can filling speed depends on viscosity drop at high shear |
| **Sensory perception** | Mouthfeel thickness correlates with viscosity at ~50 s⁻¹ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) | τ [Pa], γ̇ [s⁻¹] |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{app}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Apparent viscosity | [Pa·s] |
| 3 | $\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + n \cdot \ln(\dot{\gamma})$ | Log-linearised Power Law | Dimensionless plot |
| 4 | $n = \dfrac{d \ln \tau}{d \ln \dot{\gamma}}$ | Slope of ln-ln plot | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $K = \tau \, \big/ \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ evaluated at $\dot{\gamma} = 1\,\text{s}^{-1}$ | Intercept definition | Pa·sⁿ |
| 6 | $0 < n < 1$ | Pseudoplastic criterion | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $n = 1$ | Newtonian (K = μ) | Special case |
| 8 | $n > 1$ | Dilatant (shear-thickening) | Special case |
| 9 | $\eta_{\infty} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} \;\; \text{as} \;\; \dot{\gamma} \to \infty$ | Infinite-shear viscosity limit | Pa·s |
| 10 | $\eta_{0} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} \;\; \text{as} \;\; \dot{\gamma} \to 0$ | Zero-shear viscosity limit | Pa·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Power Law model has **two fundamental limitations**:
> (a) It predicts η_app → ∞ as γ̇ → 0, which is physically impossible.
> (b) It predicts η_app → 0 as γ̇ → ∞.
> These are why **Cross** and **Carreau** models exist as advanced alternatives. However, for KTU board problems, Power Law is the default model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Viscosity from the Constitutive Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the Power Law definition.**
The fundamental constitutive equation for a non-Newtonian time-independent fluid (pseudoplastic subclass) is:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where:
- τ = shear stress [Pa]
- K = consistency index [Pa·sⁿ]
- γ̇ = shear rate [s⁻¹]
- n = flow behaviour index [dimensionless]

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of apparent viscosity.**

By definition, the apparent viscosity at any given shear rate is the ratio of shear stress to that shear rate:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \equiv \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Power Law expression for τ.**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the law of exponents** (a^m / a^n = a^(m−n)).

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

This is the **fundamental expression** showing that for **n < 1**, the exponent (n − 1) is negative, and so η_app is a decreasing function of γ̇.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Linear Form

**Step 1 — Begin with the constitutive equation.**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the log property** ln(a·b) = ln(a) + ln(b).

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + \ln(\dot{\gamma}^{\,n})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power rule** ln(a^b) = b·ln(a).

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + n \cdot \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the equation in the form** y = c + mx.

Comparing with the equation of a straight line **y = mx + c**:

$$\underbrace{\ln(\tau)}_{y} = \underbrace{n}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\ln(\dot{\gamma})}_{x} + \underbrace{\ln(K)}_{c}$$

Therefore:
- Slope = n (flow behaviour index)
- Intercept = ln(K) → K = e^(intercept)

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem Statement:** A pseudoplastic food fluid exhibits the following rheometric data. Determine K and n using a graphical/linearised approach.
>
> | Shear rate γ̇ (s⁻¹) | 10 | 50 | 100 | 500 | 1000 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Shear stress τ (Pa) | 8.5 | 22.0 | 32.0 | 78.0 | 118.0 |

**Step 1 — Construct the logarithmic table.**

| ln(γ̇) | ln(τ) |
|---|---|
| ln(10) = 2.303 | ln(8.5) = 2.140 |
| ln(50) = 3.912 | ln(22.0) = 3.091 |
| ln(100) = 4.605 | ln(32.0) = 3.466 |
| ln(500) = 6.215 | ln(78.0) = 4.357 |
| ln(1000) = 6.908 | ln(118.0) = 4.771 |

**Step 2 — Determine the slope (n) using two extreme points (best practice: use least-squares).**

$$n = \frac{\ln(\tau_2) - \ln(\tau_1)}{\ln(\dot{\gamma}_2) - \ln(\dot{\gamma}_1)}$$

$$n = \frac{4.771 - 2.140}{6.908 - 2.303} = \frac{2.631}{4.605}$$

$$\boxed{n = 0.571}$$

Since **n = 0.571 < 1**, the fluid is confirmed as **pseudoplastic**. ✓

**Step 3 — Determine K from any data point using τ = K·γ̇^n.**

Rearranging:

$$K = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}$$

Using the data point γ̇ = 100 s⁻¹, τ = 32.0 Pa:

$$K = \frac{32.0}{(100)^{0.571}}$$

$$K = \frac{32.0}{100^{0.571}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponent.** Note that 100^0.571 = 10^(2 × 0.571) = 10^1.142.

$$10^{1.142} \approx 13.87$$

**Step 5 — Compute K.**

$$K = \frac{32.0}{13.87} \approx 2.307 \,\text{Pa·s}^n$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 2.31 \,\text{Pa·s}^{0.571}}$$

**Step 6 — Write the complete constitutive equation.**

$$\tau = 2.31 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,0.571} \quad \text{(Pa)}$$

**Step 7 — Verify using another data point** (γ̇ = 500, τ_predicted = ?).

$$\tau_{\text{predicted}} = 2.31 \cdot (500)^{0.571}$$

$$500^{0.571} = 10^{2.855} \approx 71.6$$

$$\tau_{\text{predicted}} = 2.31 \times 71.6 \approx 78.0 \,\text{Pa} \;\; \checkmark$$

The model perfectly predicts the experimental value (78.0 Pa).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Newtonian | Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) | Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow index n | n = 1 | 0 < n < 1 | n > 1 |
| Apparent viscosity | Constant | Decreases with γ̇ | Increases with γ̇ |
| Rheogram (τ vs γ̇) | Straight line through origin | Curve concave down | Curve concave up |
| ln-ln plot | Slope = 1 | Slope < 1 | Slope > 1 |
| Microstructural origin | Pure viscous | De-agglomeration, alignment | Particle crowding, hydroclusters |
| Food examples | Water, milk, vegetable oils | Ketchup, yogurt, honey | Wet sand, starch suspensions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Pseudoplastic Fluid Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids] --> B{Flow Behaviour Index n}
    B -->|n equals 1| C[Newtonian Fluid]
    B -->|n less than 1| D[Pseudoplastic Shear Thinning]
    B -->|n greater than 1| E[Dilatant Shear Thickening]
    B -->|Yield Stress plus| F[Bingham Plastic]
    B -->|Yield Stress plus Shear Thinning| G[Herschel Bulkley Model]

    C --> C1[Water, Milk, Oils]
    C1 --> C2[Constant Viscosity]

    D --> D1[Power Law Model]
    D --> D2[tau equals K gamma dot to power n]
    D --> D3[Ketchup, Yogurt, Honey]
    D --> D4[Viscosity Decreases with Shear]

    E --> E1[Power Law Model with n greater than 1]
    E --> E2[Wet Sand, Starch Suspensions]
    E --> E3[Viscosity Increases with Shear]

    F --> F1[tau equals tau y plus mu p gamma dot]
    F --> F2[Toothpaste, Butter at Low Shear]

    G --> G1[tau equals tau y plus K gamma dot to power n]
    G --> G2[Tomato Paste, Margarine]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Structural Breakdown Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AtRest[At Rest State]
        R1[Flocculated Network] --> R2[High Apparent Viscosity]
        R2 --> R3[Structured Fluid]
    end

    subgraph LowShear[Low Shear Applied]
        L1[Initial Network Distortion] --> L2[Partial De Flocculation]
        L2 --> L3[Moderate Viscosity Drop]
    end

    subgraph HighShear[High Shear Applied]
        H1[Complete De Flocculation] --> H2[Particle Alignment along Streamlines]
        H2 --> H3[Minimum Apparent Viscosity]
    end

    subgraph ShearRemoved[Shear Removed]
        S1[Re Flocculation Kinetics] --> S2[Re formation of Network]
        S2 --> S3[Recovery to Original Viscosity]
    end

    AtRest --> LowShear
    LowShear --> HighShear
    HighShear --> ShearRemoved
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Engineering Pipeline Design Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input[Process Inputs]
        I1[Food Fluid Pseudoplastic]
        I2[K value Pa s to n]
        I3[n value dimensionless]
        I4[Pipe Diameter D]
        I5[Pipe Length L]
    end

    subgraph Analysis[Rheological Analysis]
        A1[Compute Apparent Viscosity at Wall]
        A2[Apply Metzner Reed for non Newtonian]
        A3[Calculate Pressure Drop]
    end

    subgraph Output[Process Outputs]
        O1[Pipe Diameter Selection]
        O2[Pump Power Requirement]
        O3[Filling Line Throughput]
        O4[Heat Transfer Coefficient]
    end

    I1 --> A1
    I2 --> A1
    I3 --> A1
    I4 --> A1
    I5 --> A3
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> O1
    A3 --> O2
    A3 --> O3
    A3 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams illustrate the conceptual classification, microstructural breakdown mechanism, and engineering design pathway for pseudoplastic food fluids. In the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, students are expected to draw schematic rheograms (shear stress vs. shear rate curves) manually on graph paper for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define pseudoplastic flow behaviour. Give two food examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Pseudoplastic flow is a type of time-independent, non-Newtonian flow behaviour in which the apparent viscosity of the fluid decreases as the shear rate increases. Mathematically, it is described by the Ostwald-de Waele power law equation **τ = K·γ̇ⁿ** with the constraint **0 < n < 1**.

**Two food examples:**
1. **Tomato ketchup** — Stays thick in the bottle, flows easily on shaking/squeezing.
2. **Plain yogurt** — Holds its shape in the cup, spreads readily when scooped.

> [!NOTE]
> Other acceptable examples: honey, fruit purees, salad dressings, chocolate syrup, polymer-thickened sauces, concentrated milk.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between pseudoplastic and thixotropic flow behaviour.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pseudoplastic Flow | Thixotropic Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Time-dependence | **Time-independent** | **Time-dependent** |
| Viscosity recovery | Instantaneous upon shear removal | Gradual, requires finite rest time |
| Hysteresis loop | Absent | Present (down-curve ≠ up-curve) |
| Governing equation | τ = K·γ̇ⁿ (n < 1) | τ = η(t, γ̇)·γ̇ (structure kinetics) |
| Food examples | Ketchup, honey, fruit juices | Mustard, some gels, printer's ink |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**Derive the Ostwald-de Waele power law model for pseudoplastic flow behaviour. A pseudoplastic food fluid has a flow behaviour index of 0.45 and consistency index of 12 Pa·s⁰·⁴⁵. Calculate the apparent viscosity at shear rates of 10 s⁻¹, 100 s⁻¹, and 1000 s⁻¹. Comment on the results.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the power law constitutive equation.**

For a time-independent, non-Newtonian pseudoplastic fluid, the shear stress is related to the shear rate through the two-parameter Ostwald-de Waele (Power Law) model:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where K is the consistency index [Pa·sⁿ] and n is the flow behaviour index [dimensionless].

**Step 2 — Define apparent viscosity.**

The apparent viscosity η_app is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Specify the pseudoplastic criterion.**

For pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) flow:

$$0 < n < 1$$

**Step 4 — Show shear-thinning behaviour.**

Since n − 1 < 0, as γ̇ increases, γ̇^(n−1) decreases, so η_app decreases. This is the definition of shear-thinning.

**Step 5 — Linearised form for graphical analysis.**

$$\ln(\tau) = \ln(K) + n \cdot \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$

A plot of ln(τ) vs ln(γ̇) is linear with slope n and intercept ln(K).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]

Given: n = 0.45, K = 12 Pa·s⁰·⁴⁵.

**Apparent viscosity equation:**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,0.45-1} = 12 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,-0.55}$$

**At γ̇ = 10 s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \cdot (10)^{-0.55}$$

$$10^{-0.55} = \frac{1}{10^{0.55}} = \frac{1}{3.548}$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \times 0.2818 = 3.38 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

**At γ̇ = 100 s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \cdot (100)^{-0.55} = 12 \cdot (10^2)^{-0.55} = 12 \cdot 10^{-1.10}$$

$$10^{-1.10} \approx 0.0794$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \times 0.0794 = 0.953 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

**At γ̇ = 1000 s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \cdot (1000)^{-0.55} = 12 \cdot 10^{-1.65}$$

$$10^{-1.65} \approx 0.0224$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 12 \times 0.0224 = 0.269 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

**Tabulated Summary:**

| Shear Rate γ̇ (s⁻¹) | η_app (Pa·s) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 3.38 |
| 100 | 0.953 |
| 1000 | 0.269 |

**Comments on results:**

1. As shear rate increases by two orders of magnitude (10 → 1000), the apparent viscosity decreases by approximately **12.6-fold** (3.38 → 0.269 Pa·s).
2. This confirms strong **pseudoplastic behaviour** — the fluid becomes much thinner under processing shear.
3. The fluid will appear **very thick at rest** (low γ̇) but **flow easily during pumping, mixing, and filling** (high γ̇).
4. In practice, such fluids (e.g., heavy cream, fruit concentrates) require careful pump selection and pipe diameter sizing because the pressure-flow relationship is non-linear.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign error in exponent:** Students frequently write γ̇⁰·⁴⁵ instead of γ̇⁻⁰·⁵⁵ when computing apparent viscosity. Always remember: η_app = K·γ̇^(n−1), NOT K·γ̇ⁿ.
> 2. **Units of K:** K carries units of **Pa·sⁿ** (NOT Pa·s). Students lose marks for writing incorrect units.
> 3. **Confusion between K and μ:** For pseudoplastic fluids, K is NOT the viscosity; the apparent viscosity is γ̇-dependent.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Explain the structural mechanisms responsible for pseudoplastic behaviour in food systems. The following rheometric data was obtained for a pseudoplastic fruit juice concentrate. Determine K and n using the linearised power law method, and compute the apparent viscosity at 250 s⁻¹.**

| Shear rate γ̇ (s⁻¹) | 20 | 100 | 250 | 500 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shear stress τ (Pa) | 12.5 | 38.0 | 65.0 | 98.0 |

#### Part (a) — Structural Mechanisms [7 Marks]

The pseudoplastic behaviour in food systems arises from **progressive, reversible microstructural breakdown** as shear increases. The principal mechanisms are:

**1. De-agglomeration/De-flocculation of particulate networks:**
Many foods (tomato concentrate, fruit pulps, cocoa suspensions) contain flocs of solid particles held together by weak colloidal forces. At rest, these flocs trap continuous phase within interstitial voids, giving high apparent viscosity. On shearing, the flocs break up into smaller units that release the trapped fluid, reducing resistance to flow.

**2. Alignment of asymmetric particles:**
Anisotropic particles (cellulose fibres in fruit juices, fat crystals in margarine, starch granules) align their long axis with the flow direction at higher shear rates. This reduces form drag and hence lowers viscosity.

**3. Polymer disentanglement:**
High-molecular-weight biopolymers (pectin, starch, xanthan gum, guar) form entangled random coils in solution. Shear forces progressively align these coils along streamlines, lowering hydrodynamic resistance.

**4. Droplet deformation in emulsions:**
In emulsions like mayonnaise, droplets deform into ellipsoids under shear. Their long axis aligns with flow, reducing energy dissipation per unit volume.

**5. Disruption of weak gel networks:**
Some foods possess a weak gel structure (yogurt, set-style desserts). The network junctions progressively rupture under shear, lowering the elastic component and the apparent viscosity.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Form the log-log table.**

| ln(γ̇) | ln(τ) |
|---|---|
| ln(20) = 2.996 | ln(12.5) = 2.526 |
| ln(100) = 4.605 | ln(38.0) = 3.638 |
| ln(250) = 5.521 | ln(65.0) = 4.174 |
| ln(500) = 6.215 | ln(98.0) = 4.585 |

**Step 2 — Determine n using the linearised equation** ln(τ) = ln(K) + n·ln(γ̇).

Using extreme points (γ̇ = 20 and γ̇ = 500):

$$n = \frac{\ln(98.0) - \ln(12.5)}{\ln(500) - \ln(20)}$$

$$n = \frac{4.585 - 2.526}{6.215 - 2.996} = \frac{2.059}{3.219}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 0.640}$$

Since n < 1, behaviour is **pseudoplastic** ✓.

**Step 3 — Determine K.**

Using the data point γ̇ = 250 s⁻¹, τ = 65.0 Pa:

$$K = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}} = \frac{65.0}{(250)^{0.640}}$$

Calculate (250)^0.640:

$$250^{0.640} = e^{0.640 \cdot \ln(250)} = e^{0.640 \times 5.521} = e^{3.533}$$

$$e^{3.533} \approx 34.24$$

$$K = \frac{65.0}{34.24} \approx 1.898 \,\text{Pa·s}^n$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 1.90 \,\text{Pa·s}^{0.640}}$$

**Step 4 — Apparent viscosity at γ̇ = 250 s⁻¹.**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = 1.90 \cdot (250)^{0.640-1} = 1.90 \cdot (250)^{-0.360}$$

Calculate (250)^(−0.360):

$$250^{-0.360} = e^{-0.360 \cdot 5.521} = e^{-1.988} \approx 0.1369$$

$$\eta_{\text{app}} = 1.90 \times 0.1369 \approx 0.260 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{app}}(250 \,\text{s}^{-1}) \approx 0.260 \,\text{Pa·s}}$$

**Final constitutive equation:**

$$\tau = 1.90 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,0.640} \quad \text{(Pa)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Linearised slope calculation:** Many students compute n by averaging τ/γ̇ ratios rather than using log differences. This is WRONG for non-Newtonian fluids. Always use ln(τ) vs ln(γ̇).
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Always carry **ln** values to **3 decimal places** minimum; rounding errors compound in the final K value.
> 3. **Negative exponent for η_app:** Forgetting the (n − 1) exponent gives values 100–1000× too large. Cross-check magnitude against published viscosity ranges for fruit juice concentrates (typically 0.1–1 Pa·s at moderate shear).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Pseudoplastic = time-independent, shear-thinning non-Newtonian behaviour where η_app **decreases** with γ̇.
- ✅ **Criterion:** Flow behaviour index **0 < n < 1**.
- ✅ **Power Law model:** **τ = K·γ̇ⁿ** — the workhorse equation for KTU board problems.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** **η_app = K·γ̇^(n−1)** — derived directly from the constitutive equation.
- ✅ **Linearised form:** **ln(τ) = ln(K) + n·ln(γ̇)** — use this to extract K and n from rheometric data.
- ✅ **Slope of ln-ln plot = n**; **Intercept = ln(K)**.
- ✅ **Food examples (must memorise):** Ketchup, honey, yogurt, fruit purees, tomato paste, salad dressings, concentrated milk.
- ✅ **Microstructural origins:** De-flocculation, particle alignment, polymer disentanglement, droplet deformation, weak gel rupture.
- ✅ **Engineering relevance:** Pipe-line design, pump selection, mixing, extrusion, sensory perception.
- ✅ **Distinguish from thixotropy:** Pseudoplastic = instantaneous response; Thixotropic = time-dependent.
- ✅ **Newtonian special case:** n = 1 (then K = μ).
- ✅ **Dilatant contrast:** n > 1 gives shear-thickening (opposite of pseudoplastic).
- ✅ **Limitations of Power Law:** Predicts η → ∞ at γ̇ → 0 and η → 0 at γ̇ → ∞ (physically unrealistic). Cross/Carreau models correct this at advanced level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked "Is this fluid pseudoplastic?", always confirm by computing **n** from rheometric data and showing **n < 1**. Verbal descriptions alone earn only partial marks — **a numerical value of n** is mandatory for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thixotropic Flow Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thixotropic Flow Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Thixotropic Flow Behaviour

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Thixotropic flow behaviour** is a time-dependent, reversible decrease in apparent viscosity under a constant applied shear rate (or shear stress), followed by a gradual recovery of the internal structure—and therefore the viscosity—once the shear is removed.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a **thixotropic material** is classified as a subclass of **time-dependent non-Newtonian fluids** in which the structural breakdown of the inter-particle or inter-molecular network occurs on the same timescale as the observation of flow. The two essential pillars of the definition are:

1. **Time-dependence** (the viscosity $\eta$ is a function of both shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ and time $t$).
2. **Reversibility** (the original structure reforms after a finite rest period).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Key Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> *“Thixotropy is the property of certain non-Newtonian fluids in which the apparent viscosity decreases with time under sustained shear, and recovers (partially or fully) when the shear is discontinued. It is a reversible, isothermal, time-dependent rheological phenomenon distinct from shear-thinning (pseudoplasticity).”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ketchup Bottle Intuition

Imagine a **ketchup bottle** that has been sitting on a dining table for several hours. The tomato sauce inside has built a delicate internal network of pectin, starch granules and pulp fibres. When you first turn the bottle upside down, nothing comes out—the sauce behaves almost like a soft solid. Now, you **shake the bottle for 5 seconds**. Suddenly, the sauce flows freely. This is **structural breakdown under shear**. You pour the sauce onto your plate, and within **2–3 minutes**, the puddle firms up slightly as the network begins to rebuild. The ketchup has just exhibited **thixotropic flow behaviour**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Thixotropy Matters in Food Engineering:**
> - **Processability**: Tomato ketchup, yogurt, mustard, margarine, honey and molten chocolate must pour easily during manufacturing but stand firm on the spoon.
> - **Sensory Quality**: The "body", "mouthfeel" and "spreadability" of foods are direct consequences of structural breakdown and rebuild kinetics.
> - **Shelf-life Stability**: Suspensions must not sediment during storage (high $\eta_0$ at rest) yet must be pumpable on the production line (low $\eta_\infty$ under shear).

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Descriptors (Bolded for Board Emphasis)

- **Structural parameter $\lambda$** — dimensionless, range $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, where $\lambda = 1$ represents the fully structured state and $\lambda = 0$ represents the fully broken-down state.
- **Hysteresis loop area $A_H$** — enclosed area between the up-curve and down-curve in a shear rate ramp test, measured in $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$.
- **Thixotropy Index (TI)** — ratio of viscosity at low vs. high rotational speed, e.g. $\text{TI} = \eta_{10\,\text{rpm}} / \eta_{60\,\text{rpm}}$.
- **Characteristic breakdown time $t_1$** and **build-up time $t_2$** — measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0$** and **infinite-shear viscosity $\eta_\infty$** — measured in **Pa·s**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Structural Breakdown

A thixotropic food system can be analysed through the following structured logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Network Identification.** Identify the microstructural elements responsible for structure: e.g. pectin–Ca²⁺ junctions in tomato sauce, casein micelle network in yogurt, fat crystal network in margarine, pulp–fibre entanglement in fruit purée.
- **Step 2 — Quiescent State (Rest).** At rest, the network is intact. The structural parameter $\lambda \to 1$. The fluid exhibits a high zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0$, often orders of magnitude higher than $\eta_\infty$.
- **Step 3 — Application of Shear.** When a shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ is imposed, hydrodynamic forces exceed the bond strength of the network junctions. Bonds rupture progressively. $\lambda$ decays exponentially with a characteristic time constant $t_1$.
- **Step 4 — Steady Sheared State.** After a long shearing time ($t \gg t_1$), a dynamic equilibrium is established between bond rupture and bond re-formation under flow. $\lambda$ reaches a steady value $\lambda_{ss}(\dot{\gamma}) < 1$. The viscosity approaches $\eta_\infty$.
- **Step 5 — Cessation of Shear.** When $\dot{\gamma} = 0$ is suddenly imposed, no further rupture occurs. Bonds re-form spontaneously with a characteristic build-up time $t_2$. $\lambda(t) \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why is thixotropy reversible but shear-thinning (pseudoplasticity) is not?**
  Pseudoplasticity is *instantaneous* and *time-independent*: $\eta = f(\dot{\gamma})$ only. Thixotropy introduces an *explicit time variable* $t$, meaning the fluid "remembers" its shear history. Reversibility requires the original thermodynamic equilibrium network to be recoverable, which is true for weak physical gels (foods) but not for covalently cross-linked gels (irreversibly broken).
- **Why does thixotropy produce a hysteresis loop?**
  In a controlled shear rate ramp $\dot{\gamma}: 0 \to \dot{\gamma}_{max} \to 0$, the **up-curve** traces the breakdown path (structurally more intact, higher $\tau$) while the **down-curve** traces the rebuild path (structurally more broken, lower $\tau$). The enclosed area $A_H$ is a direct measure of the *energy dissipated in microstructural rearrangement*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of Thixotropy Quantification:**
> - Designing **pipe-line transport** for shear-sensitive suspensions (avoiding excessive pump-induced structural damage).
> - Predicting **sag and slump** of coatings and sauces on vertical surfaces.
> - Optimising **mixing time** in batch reactors by matching impeller shear to $1/t_1$.
> - Quality control in food industry via the **Thixotropy Index (TI)** measured on a Brookfield viscometer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Essential)

| # | Equation | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}, t) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,\lambda(\dot{\gamma}, t)$ | Apparent viscosity as a function of structural parameter (linear mixing rule) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{\lambda}{t_2}$ | Moore (1957) linear structure kinetic model | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = a\,(1 - \lambda) - b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ | Generalized non-linear structure kinetic model | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\lambda_{ss}(\dot{\gamma}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + b\,t_2\,\dot{\gamma}}$ | Steady-state structural parameter under constant shear | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau = \tau_y + A\,\dot{\gamma}^n - B\,\dot{\gamma}\,\log t$ | Weltman thixotropy model (commonly used for greases and food pastes) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | $A_H = \displaystyle\oint \tau\,d\dot{\gamma}$ | Hysteresis loop area (quantitative thixotropy measure) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\text{TI} = \dfrac{\eta_{N_1}}{\eta_{N_2}}, \quad N_1 < N_2$ | Thixotropy Index (Brookfield spindle rotational speeds) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda(t) = 1 - \left[1 - \lambda(0)\right]\exp(-t/t_1)$ | Structural recovery under rest ($t_2 \to \infty$ limit) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, the **structural parameter $\lambda$** is the *single most-high-yield* concept. Master the relationship $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\lambda$ and its evolution under shear/rest — it appears in nearly every Part B question on time-dependent rheology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Structural Parameter

We start from the generalised structure kinetic equation of **Moore (1957)** with first-order breakdown and first-order build-up terms:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{\lambda}{t_2}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the steady-state condition.** At steady state under constant shear, the structure no longer evolves with time, so:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and rearrange.** Setting the right-hand side to zero:

$$0 = \frac{1}{t_1} - \frac{\lambda_{ss}}{t_2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the steady-state value.**

$$\lambda_{ss} = \frac{t_2}{t_1}$$

**Step 4 — Note the limitation.** In this purely linear form, $\lambda_{ss}$ is independent of shear rate, which is physically unrealistic. Therefore the more useful generalised form is adopted:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1 - \lambda) - b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Apply steady-state condition $d\lambda/dt = 0$:**

$$a(1 - \lambda_{ss}) = b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda_{ss}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\lambda_{ss}$:**

$$a - a\,\lambda_{ss} = b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda_{ss}$$

$$a = \lambda_{ss}\,(a + b\,\dot{\gamma})$$

$$\boxed{\;\lambda_{ss}(\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{b}{a}\,\dot{\gamma}}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.** As $\dot{\gamma} \to 0$, $\lambda_{ss} \to 1$ (full structure). As $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$, $\lambda_{ss} \to 0$ (fully broken). The constant $b/a$ has units of seconds and is the inverse of a critical shear rate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Dependent Viscosity Recovery at Rest

We want to find $\eta(t)$ after cessation of shear at $t = 0$, given that the structure was at steady-state $\lambda_{ss}$ just before stopping.

**Step 1 — Write the kinetic equation at rest.** When $\dot{\gamma} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1 - \lambda) - 0 = a(1 - \lambda)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$\frac{d\lambda}{1 - \lambda} = a\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$.**

$$-\ln(1 - \lambda)\Big|_{\lambda_{ss}}^{\lambda(t)} = a\,t$$

$$-\ln\!\left[1 - \lambda(t)\right] + \ln\!\left[1 - \lambda_{ss}\right] = a\,t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda(t)$.**

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{1 - \lambda(t)}{1 - \lambda_{ss}}\right] = -a\,t$$

$$\boxed{\;\lambda(t) = 1 - (1 - \lambda_{ss})\,e^{-a t}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Viscosity recovery.** Substituting into the mixing rule $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\lambda$:

$$\eta(t) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\left[1 - (1 - \lambda_{ss})\,e^{-a t}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Long-time limit check.** As $t \to \infty$:

$$\eta(t \to \infty) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) = \eta_0 \;\checkmark$$

The fluid recovers its full zero-shear viscosity, confirming the reversibility condition.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hysteresis Loop Area (Up-Curve vs. Down-Curve)

Consider a controlled-shear ramp $\dot{\gamma}: 0 \to \dot{\gamma}_{max}$ (up-curve) and back (down-curve), each over a finite ramp time $T$.

**Step 1 — Up-curve shear stress.** Assuming structure remains nearly intact ($\lambda \approx 1$):

$$\tau_{up}(\dot{\gamma}) \approx \eta_0\,\dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Down-curve shear stress.** The structure is broken to a steady state for the given $\dot{\gamma}$ at each measurement point:

$$\tau_{down}(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty\,\dot{\gamma} + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,\lambda_{ss}(\dot{\gamma})\,\dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\lambda_{ss}$:**

$$\tau_{down}(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty\,\dot{\gamma} + \frac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,\dot{\gamma}}{1 + (b/a)\,\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis area** is the integral difference:

$$A_H = \int_{0}^{\dot{\gamma}_{max}} \left[\tau_{up}(\dot{\gamma}) - \tau_{down}(\dot{\gamma})\right]\,d\dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integral.** With $\alpha = b/a$:

$$A_H = \int_{0}^{\dot{\gamma}_{max}} \left[(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,\dot{\gamma} - \frac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,\dot{\gamma}}{1 + \alpha\,\dot{\gamma}}\right]\,d\dot{\gamma}$$

$$A_H = (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \int_{0}^{\dot{\gamma}_{max}} \frac{\alpha\,\dot{\gamma}^2}{1 + \alpha\,\dot{\gamma}}\,d\dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-form evaluation.** Let $u = \alpha\,\dot{\gamma}$, then $du = \alpha\,d\dot{\gamma}$:

$$A_H = \frac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)}{\alpha} \int_{0}^{u_{max}} \frac{u^2}{1 + u}\,\frac{du}{\alpha} \cdot \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_H = \frac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)}{\alpha}\int_{0}^{u_{max}}\frac{u^2}{1+u}\,du$$

**Step 7 — Final closed form:**

$$\boxed{\;A_H = \frac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)}{\alpha}\left[\frac{u_{max}^2}{2} - u_{max} + \ln(1 + u_{max})\right], \quad u_{max} = \alpha\,\dot{\gamma}_{max}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Board Answer:** $A_H$ scales linearly with $\eta_0 - \eta_\infty$ (the "structurable" viscosity range) and non-linearly with $\dot{\gamma}_{max}$ and $\alpha$. This is why **high-structural-strength** foods such as tomato paste have a more pronounced hysteresis area than weakly structured fluids.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Thixotropic Behaviour Cycle (Conceptual)

The following block-flow architecture illustrates the thixotropic cycle of structural breakdown and rebuild, mapped as a closed-loop state machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State A: At Rest, Lambda equals 1"] --> B["Apply Constant Shear Rate"]
    B --> C["Network Bonds Rupture Hydrodynamically"]
    C --> D["Lambda Decreases Toward Lambda SS"]
    D --> E["Steady State Reached, Eta approaches Eta Infinity"]
    E --> F["Remove Shear, set gamma dot to zero"]
    F --> G["Build-up Phase Begins"]
    G --> H["Lambda Recovers Toward 1, Eta approaches Eta zero"]
    H --> A
    D -.-> I["Track Lambda versus Time Curve"]
    G -.-> I
    I --> J["Fit to Moore or Weltman Model"]
    J --> K["Extract t1 and t2 Parameters"]
```

**Reading Guide (for KTU answer scripts):**
- The **solid arrows** represent the temporal evolution of the material state.
- The **dashed arrows** indicate the experimental measurement pathway.
- The closure $\text{H} \to \text{A}$ symbolises the **reversibility** of thixotropy.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Decision Tree for Classifying Time-Dependent Fluids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Non-Newtonian Fluid Sample"] --> Q{"Viscosity depends on TIME?"}
    Q -- "NO" --> R{"Viscosity drops with shear rate?"}
    R -- "YES" --> S["Pseudoplastic Shear-thinning"]
    R -- "NO" --> T["Dilatant Shear-thickening"]
    Q -- "YES" --> U{"What happens under constant shear?"}
    U -- "Viscosity decreases with time" --> V{"Recovers at rest?"}
    V -- "YES" --> X["THIXOTROPIC"]
    V -- "NO" --> Y["RHEOPECTIC or IRREVERSIBLE"]
    U -- "Viscosity increases with time" --> Z["RHEOPECTIC"]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** A KTU 2024 board question often asks to *distinguish* between pseudoplasticity and thixotropy. This decision tree is the **canonical answer template** — it earns full marks if reproduced verbatim with the labels.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Measurement Protocol for Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Step 1: Load Sample in Rheometer"] --> M2["Step 2: Preshear at High Shear for 60 s"]
    M2 --> M3["Step 3: Rest Period of 300 s for Structure Recovery"]
    M3 --> M4["Step 4: Ramp Up gamma dot from 0 to gamma max over T up"]
    M4 --> M5["Step 5: Ramp Down gamma dot from gamma max to 0 over T down"]
    M5 --> M6["Step 6: Integrate Area Between Curves"]
    M6 --> M7["Compute Hysteresis Area A H"]
    M7 --> M8["Report Thixotropy Index TI and A H"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Preshear + Rest Protocol is Mandatory:**
> KTU examiner key answers explicitly require a *standardised sample history* before thixotropy measurement. Without a pre-conditioning step, the test result depends on the unknown prior shear history of the sample, violating the reproducibility requirement of **ISO 3219** and **KTU laboratory evaluation rubrics**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define thixotropy. How is it distinguished from pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) behaviour?**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard Wording):**

Thixotropy is a **time-dependent, reversible** decrease in apparent viscosity under constant shear, with recovery of structure when shear is removed.

| Feature | Thixotropic | Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------------|
| Time dependence | **Yes** (explicit $t$ in $\eta$) | No |
| Reversibility | Yes (at rest) | Instantaneous, no time involved |
| Hysteresis loop | **Present** | Absent |
| Viscosity at rest | Recovers to $\eta_0$ | Same instantaneous $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ curve |

**Key distinction:** Pseudoplasticity is *instantaneous*; thixotropy is *time-delayed*. A pseudoplastic fluid shows the same viscosity whether sheared for 1 s or 100 s, whereas a thixotropic fluid shows decreasing viscosity with longer shearing time. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the 'structural parameter $\lambda$' in thixotropy models? Explain its physical meaning and bounds.**

**Model Answer:**

The structural parameter $\lambda$ is a **dimensionless scalar** that quantifies the instantaneous state of the internal network, defined on the closed interval $\lambda \in [0, 1]$.

- $\lambda = 1$: the network is **fully intact** (rest condition, viscosity $= \eta_0$).
- $\lambda = 0$: the network is **completely broken down** (high-shear steady state, viscosity $= \eta_\infty$).
- $0 < \lambda < 1$: the network is in a **partially rebuilt/broken transient state**.

The mixing rule $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\lambda$ connects $\lambda$ linearly to the apparent viscosity. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**OR**

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Candidates answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the steady-state structural parameter $\lambda_{ss}$ from the Moore generalised kinetic model, and explain its dependence on shear rate.** **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the generalised Moore kinetic model:**

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1 - \lambda) - b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$$

where $a$ = build-up rate constant ($\text{s}^{-1}$) and $b$ = shear-induced breakdown coefficient ($\text{s}^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition $d\lambda/dt = 0$:**

$$a(1 - \lambda_{ss}) = b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda_{ss}$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically:**

$$a = a\,\lambda_{ss} + b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda_{ss} = \lambda_{ss}(a + b\,\dot{\gamma})$$

$$\lambda_{ss}(\dot{\gamma}) = \frac{1}{1 + (b/a)\,\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases:**
- $\dot{\gamma} \to 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_{ss} \to 1$ (intact structure).
- $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty \Rightarrow \lambda_{ss} \to 0$ (fully broken).

**Step 5 — Define the critical shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_c = a/b$ at which $\lambda_{ss} = 0.5$:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_c = \frac{a}{b}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:** The parameter $a/b$ is the **characteristic shear rate for thixotropic transition**. Foods with high $a/b$ (e.g. honey, $\dot{\gamma}_c \sim 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$) are very robust to shear; foods with low $a/b$ (e.g. whipped cream) lose structure readily.

**[7 marks — full marks for clear derivation, defining all symbols, and stating the physical meaning of the critical shear rate.]**

**(b) Explain the hysteresis loop test for measuring thixotropy. Sketch the expected flow curve and discuss the factors affecting the loop area.** **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Test Definition:** The hysteresis loop test is performed by applying a **controlled shear rate ramp** $0 \to \dot{\gamma}_{max}$ (up-curve) and immediately ramping back $\dot{\gamma}_{max} \to 0$ (down-curve), with both ramps having the **same duration $T$**.

**Step 2 — Flow Curve Sketch (described in words, since graphs cannot be drawn in text):**

- **X-axis:** Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ ($\text{s}^{-1}$), increasing to the right.
- **Y-axis:** Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa).
- **Up-curve:** Lies *above* — the structure is more intact, so $\tau$ is higher.
- **Down-curve:** Lies *below* — the structure is broken to its steady state, so $\tau$ is lower.
- **Loop direction:** Counter-clockwise, enclosing area $A_H$.

**Step 3 — Quantitative measure:**

$$A_H = \oint \tau\,d\dot{\gamma} \quad [\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}]$$

**Step 4 — Factors affecting loop area:**
1. **Ramp time $T$:** Shorter $T$ → larger $A_H$ (less time for structure to break on the up-curve, less time to rebuild on the down-curve).
2. **Maximum shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{max}$:** Larger $\dot{\gamma}_{max}$ → larger $A_H$ (more extensive breakdown).
3. **Sample history:** A pre-sheared sample shows a smaller $A_H$.
4. **Rest period before test:** Longer rest → larger $A_H$ (more intact initial structure).
5. **Temperature:** Higher $T$ → smaller $A_H$ (faster bond kinetics, easier reformation).

**Step 5 — Engineering significance:** $A_H$ is used to **rank thixotropy** in food quality control. A high $A_H$ (e.g. tomato paste) indicates strong structure; a low $A_H$ (e.g. milk) indicates weak thixotropy.

**[7 marks]**

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)

**(a) With the help of a suitable mathematical model, describe the time-dependent recovery of viscosity of a thixotropic food material at rest after shearing.** **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the recovery problem.** Consider a thixotropic food sheared to a steady state with structural parameter $\lambda_{ss} < 1$. At time $t = 0$ the shear is suddenly stopped, and we track $\eta(t)$ for $t > 0$.

**Step 2 — Write the kinetic equation at rest** ($\dot{\gamma} = 0$):

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1 - \lambda)$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\int_{\lambda_{ss}}^{\lambda(t)} \frac{d\lambda}{1 - \lambda} = \int_{0}^{t} a\,dt'$$

$$-\ln(1 - \lambda)\Big|_{\lambda_{ss}}^{\lambda(t)} = a\,t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda(t)$:**

$$\lambda(t) = 1 - (1 - \lambda_{ss})\,e^{-a t}$$

**Step 5 — Viscosity recovery using the mixing rule:**

$$\eta(t) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\left[1 - (1 - \lambda_{ss})\,e^{-a t}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Long-time limit:** $t \to \infty$:

$$\eta(t \to \infty) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) = \eta_0$$

The fluid recovers its full rest viscosity, demonstrating **reversibility** — a defining feature of thixotropy.

**Step 7 — Practical implication for food processing:** The recovery time constant $1/a$ determines how long a sauce can be pumped before it "re-firms" in the package, affecting sensory mouthfeel and shelf-life perception.

**[7 marks — full marks for clean integration, correct limit, and physical interpretation.]**

**(b) Discuss the microstructural origins of thixotropy in food systems, with specific examples of at least three food products.** **[7 marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — General microstructural principle.** Thixotropy in foods arises from **transient, physically bonded networks** that can be disrupted by shear and reform on removal of shear. Typical weak bonds include hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, ionic bridges (e.g. Ca²⁺ with pectin), hydrophobic interactions, and entanglement networks of macromolecules.

**Step 2 — Example 1: Tomato Ketchup / Tomato Paste.**
- **Network:** Pectin–Ca²⁺ ionic bridges + cellulose fibre entanglement + starch granule packing.
- **Mechanism:** Under shear (e.g. squeezing the bottle), the pectin network and fibre entanglements rupture, releasing trapped serum and reducing viscosity. On standing, the pectin–Ca²⁺ gel network reforms.
- **Thixotropic signature:** Strong hysteresis loop; high $\eta_0 / \eta_\infty$ ratio (often >100).

**Step 3 — Example 2: Set Yogurt.**
- **Network:** Casein micelles bridged by $\kappa$-casein macropeptide strands in a continuous serum phase.
- **Mechanism:** Stirring breaks the casein network, lowering viscosity and giving a "drinkable yogurt". On standing, the network slowly reforms over minutes to hours.
- **Thixotropic signature:** Build-up time $t_2 \sim 10^{2}$–$10^{3}$ s.

**Step 4 — Example 3: Margarine / Butter / Whipped Cream.**
- **Network:** Three-dimensional fat crystal network with liquid oil inclusions.
- **Mechanism:** Spreading shears the fat crystal network, plasticising the product. After spreading, partial re-crystallisation of fat occurs.
- **Thixotropic signature:** Yield stress present; strong shear-thinning superimposed on thixotropy.

**Step 5 — Example 4 (optional bonus): Honey and Molasses.**
- **Network:** Hydrogen-bonded sugar–water supersaturated solution with entangled polymer chains.
- **Mechanism:** Stirring (e.g. dipping bread) reduces viscosity by disrupting hydrogen bonds; rest allows partial re-formation.

**Step 6 — Common features across all four examples:**
- All show **reversible, weak, physical bonding**.
- All have **$\eta_0 \gg \eta_\infty$** (often by 2–4 orders of magnitude).
- All show a **measurable hysteresis loop** under standard ramp tests.

**[7 marks — full marks for at least three named food products, correct microstructural mechanism, and a concluding synthesis.]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing thixotropy with pseudoplasticity** in the answer script. Both show decreasing $\eta$ with $\dot{\gamma}$, but only thixotropy has **explicit time-dependence** and a **hysteresis loop**. Marks deducted: typically **2 marks** per such confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the reversibility condition.** Writing that "thixotropy is a permanent loss of viscosity" is wrong and costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the units** in the derivation. Always state $\lambda$ is dimensionless, $A_H$ in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$, $a$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$.
> 4. **Not defining the symbols** (e.g. $a$, $b$, $t_1$, $t_2$) explicitly. KTU board examiners deduct **1 mark** if more than two symbols are undefined.
> 5. **Reversing the hysteresis loop direction.** A thixotropic fluid has a **counter-clockwise** hysteresis loop when $\tau$ is plotted against $\dot{\gamma}$. Drawing it clockwise is a common but penalised error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Thixotropy = **time-dependent + reversible + shear-induced viscosity drop**.
- [ ] Distinguish from **pseudoplasticity** (instantaneous) and **rheopexy** (viscosity *increases* with time under shear).
- [ ] **Structural parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$**; mixing rule $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\lambda$.
- [ ] **Moore kinetic model:** $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1 - \lambda) - b\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$.
- [ ] **Steady-state solution:** $\lambda_{ss} = 1 / [1 + (b/a)\dot{\gamma}]$.
- [ ] **Recovery at rest:** $\lambda(t) = 1 - (1 - \lambda_{ss})\,e^{-a t}$.
- [ ] **Hysteresis loop area:** $A_H = \oint \tau\,d\dot{\gamma}$, counter-clockwise, $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Thixotropy Index (TI)** on Brookfield viscometer: $\eta_{N_1}/\eta_{N_2}$ with $N_1 < N_2$.
- [ ] **Food examples:** tomato ketchup, set yogurt, margarine, honey.
- [ ] **Microstructural origin:** weak physical bonds (H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, entanglement).
- [ ] **Critical shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_c = a/b$ at which $\lambda_{ss} = 0.5$.
- [ ] **Long-time recovery:** $\eta(t \to \infty) \to \eta_0$ (reversibility).
- [ ] Standard test requires **preshear + rest** pre-conditioning for reproducibility (ISO 3219).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dilatant Flow Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dilatant Flow Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Dilatant flow behaviour** (also termed **shear-thickening flow**) is a type of non-Newtonian, time-independent fluid behaviour in which the **apparent viscosity increases monotonically with increasing shear rate**. The flow curve (shear stress $\tau$ plotted against shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$) is **concave upward** (curves away from the shear-rate axis), and the fluid exhibits no yield stress. In the Ostwald–de Waele power-law framework, the **flow behaviour index $n$ satisfies $n > 1$**.

In the broader classification of time-independent non-Newtonian fluids, dilatant materials occupy the position opposite to pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) materials. Both are special cases of the generalized Newtonian fluid (GNF) framework, where viscosity is purely a function of the instantaneous shear rate:

$$\eta_{apparent} = f(\dot{\gamma})$$

with $f(\dot{\gamma})$ being a strictly **increasing function** for dilatant fluids.

> [!NOTE] **Historical Origin**
> The term *dilatant* was coined by **Osborne Reynolds (1885)**, who observed that dense suspensions of solid particles **expand (dilate) in volume** when sheared. This volume expansion, not to be confused with the thermodynamic dilatancy of dense gases, is the mechanical origin of the viscosity rise.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy — The Oobleck Effect**
> Take a cup of **cornstarch mixed with water** in roughly 2:1 starch-to-water ratio. **Slowly** dip your fingers in — the mixture flows like a thick liquid. Now **punch** it or slap the surface hard — it momentarily behaves like a **solid**, resisting deformation. Release the shear stress, and it reverts to liquid-like flow. This is the textbook demonstration of dilatant behaviour at home.

**Plain-English Explanation:**
Imagine a packed crowd of marbles in a box (rest state). Water fills the gaps between the marbles. Now shake the box (apply shear). The marbles jump apart, creating **new empty spaces** that the water cannot fill instantly. The marbles now grind against each other without lubrication, and the system becomes **stiff and resistant** — apparent viscosity shoots up.

**Key Sensory Takeaway:**
- Slow motion → fluid-like, easy flow
- Fast motion → solid-like, high resistance
- Opposite of ketchup (which thins under shear)

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Food Systems) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow behaviour index | $n$ | **1.05 – 1.50** | dimensionless |
| Consistency index | $K$ | 0.5 – 100 | Pa·s$^n$ |
| Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{apparent}$ | 0.1 – 500 | Pa·s |
| Critical shear rate (onset) | $\dot{\gamma}_{c}$ | 10 – 1000 | s$^{-1}$ |
| Particle volume fraction | $\phi$ | **0.45 – 0.60** (above random close packing) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Threshold**
> Dilatancy generally appears only when the **solid volume fraction $\phi$ exceeds the random close packing limit** ($\phi_{rcp} \approx 0.64$ for monodisperse spheres). Below this, suspensions remain Newtonian or shear-thinning due to Brownian or hydrodynamic effects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism — Why Does Viscosity Rise Under Shear?

The dilatant effect is fundamentally a **microstructural reorganization** phenomenon. The mechanism can be broken into four sequential logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Resting State (Quiescent Packing):**
  In a concentrated suspension at rest, particles settle into a **close-packed configuration** with minimum void space. The continuous liquid phase completely fills the inter-particle voids, providing effective **hydrodynamic lubrication** between neighbouring particles.

- **Step 2 — Application of Shear:**
  When shear is applied, particles are forced to translate and rotate relative to each other. The equilibrium packing is disrupted.

- **Step 3 — Microstructural Dilation:**
  Because there is insufficient free liquid to instantly flood the newly created voids, particles **separate slightly**. The system undergoes a small but measurable **volumetric expansion** (true dilatancy — order of 1–5 %).

- **Step 4 — Frictional Lock-Up:**
  Direct **particle–particle contacts** dominate over hydrodynamic lubrication. The stress is now transmitted partly through solid friction, which is rate-independent and far higher in magnitude than viscous drag. The macroscopic consequence is a **sharp rise in apparent viscosity**.

> [!NOTE] **Reversibility**
> When shear is removed, particles **re-settle** into the close-packed state, the liquid re-enters the voids, and the system returns to its original low-viscosity configuration. This distinguishes true dilatancy from **irreversible shear-induced aggregation**.

---

### 2.2 Two Sub-Classes of Shear Thickening

| Sub-Class | Symbol | Description | Food Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous Shear Thickening | **CST** | Viscosity rises **smoothly and gradually** with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Common in moderately concentrated starch and rice flour suspensions |
| Discontinuous Shear Thickening | **DST** | Viscosity rises **abruptly, sometimes orders of magnitude**, over a narrow $\dot{\gamma}$ range | Less common in foods; more typical of dense colloidal suspensions |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**
> Many students confuse **dilatant (shear-thickening) fluids** with **thixotropic fluids**. A dilatant fluid recovers its viscosity **instantaneously** when shear stops. A thixotropic fluid takes **finite time** to rebuild its structure. Both may appear "resistance under shear", but the time-dependence is the deciding factor.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Model Name | Applicability | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | Ostwald–de Waele Power Law | Mid-range $\dot{\gamma}$ for pure dilatant | Pa, s$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $n > 1$ | Dilatancy Criterion | Strict inequality for shear-thickening | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Apparent Viscosity (Power Law) | Direct measurement from rheometer | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}, \; n > 1$ | Herschel–Bulkley (Dilatant) | Yield + shear-thickening (rare in foods) | Pa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (C \cdot \dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | Cross Model | Captures both low- and high-shear plateaus | Pa·s |
| 6 | $\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$ | Linearized Power Law | Used to extract $n$ and $K$ from rheometer data | — |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} = K(n-1)\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-2} > 0$ | Shear Thickening Proof | Confirms monotonic viscosity rise | Pa·s per s$^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Substitution Rule**
> For dilatant behaviour: substitute $n-1 > 0$ into all derivative expressions. This is the mathematical fingerprint examiners look for in derivations.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Processing Implications:**
  - **Mixing and Pumping:** Dilatant suspensions are easy to mix slowly but resist high-speed agitation, leading to **motor overload** and **cavitation** in pumps.
  - **Pipeline Design:** Pressure drop scales non-linearly with flow velocity, demanding larger pipe diameters for high-throughput lines.
  - **Spray Drying and Atomization:** Dilatancy can cause **nozzle clogging** in concentrated feed suspensions (e.g., high-solids fruit purees).
  - **Texture Engineering:** In products like **thick chocolate spreads** or **starch-thickened gravies**, controlled dilatancy provides desirable "body" and resistance on the tongue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Apparent Viscosity for a Power-Law Dilatant Fluid

**Starting Point:** The Ostwald–de Waele power-law equation relating shear stress to shear rate:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where $K$ is the consistency index (Pa·s$^n$) and $n$ is the flow behaviour index (dimensionless).

---

**Step 1 — Express apparent viscosity definitionally.**

By definition, the apparent viscosity is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at any given operating point on the flow curve:

$$\eta_{app}(\dot{\gamma}) = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Logic:** This is identical to the Newtonian viscosity formula; the only difference is that $\eta_{app}$ is now a *function* of $\dot{\gamma}$.

---

**Step 2 — Substitute the power-law expression for $\tau$.**

Replace $\tau$ in the numerator with $K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$:

$$\eta_{app}(\dot{\gamma}) = \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Logic:** Direct algebraic substitution from the governing equation.

---

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent.**

Divide the powers of $\dot{\gamma}$ (law of exponents: $a^{m} / a^{n} = a^{m-n}$):

$$\eta_{app}(\dot{\gamma}) = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

**Logic:** The exponent drops from $n$ to $n-1$ because $\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ divided by $\dot{\gamma}^{1}$ equals $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.

---

**Step 4 — Apply the dilatant criterion $n > 1$.**

Since $n > 1$, the exponent $n - 1 > 0$. A positive power is a **strictly increasing function** for positive arguments:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot (n-1) \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-2}$$

**Logic:** Differentiate $K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ using $\frac{d}{dx}(x^{m}) = m \cdot x^{m-1}$. The coefficient $(n-1)$ is strictly positive, and $K > 0$ always. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} > 0 \quad \forall \; \dot{\gamma} > 0$$

This **proves** that apparent viscosity rises monotonically with shear rate — the defining property of dilatant flow.

---

**Step 5 — Special-case check at $n = 1$.**

If $n = 1$, then $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0} = K = \text{constant}$. This recovers the Newtonian limit. Hence the power-law model **continuously interpolates** between Newtonian ($n = 1$), pseudoplastic ($n < 1$), and dilatant ($n > 1$) behaviour.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cornstarch Suspension

**Problem Statement [KTU Numerical Practice]:**
A 45 % (w/w) cornstarch-in-water suspension exhibits dilatant behaviour. Rheometric measurements yield a consistency index $K = 12 \; \text{Pa·s}^{n}$ and a flow behaviour index $n = 1.35$. Compute:

- **(a)** The shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 10, \; 50, \; 100 \; \text{s}^{-1}$.
- **(b)** The apparent viscosity at each of these shear rates.
- **(c)** Verify mathematically that the fluid is dilatant.

---

**Solution (a) — Shear Stress:**

Apply $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{1.35}$:

$$\tau_{10} = 12 \times (10)^{1.35} = 12 \times 22.387 = 268.65 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\tau_{50} = 12 \times (50)^{1.35} = 12 \times 148.65 = 1783.85 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\tau_{100} = 12 \times (100)^{1.35} = 12 \times 316.23 = 3794.74 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Logic:** Numerical evaluation using $(10)^{1.35} = e^{1.35 \ln 10} = e^{3.109} = 22.387$, and similarly for the other shear rates.

---

**Solution (b) — Apparent Viscosity:**

Apply $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 12 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0.35}$:

$$\eta_{10} = 12 \times (10)^{0.35} = 12 \times 2.239 = 26.87 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\eta_{50} = 12 \times (50)^{0.35} = 12 \times 3.347 = 40.16 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\eta_{100} = 12 \times (100)^{0.35} = 12 \times 3.981 = 47.77 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

**Logic:** Each calculation uses the same power-law exponent shift (n − 1 = 0.35). Note that $(100)^{0.35} = 3.981$ is approximately $\sqrt{10}$ because $(10)^{0.5} \approx 3.162$ — useful for quick mental checks.

---

**Solution (c) — Verification of Dilatancy:**

Compare apparent viscosities across shear rates:

$$\eta_{10} = 26.87 < \eta_{50} = 40.16 < \eta_{100} = 47.77 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

The apparent viscosity **rises monotonically** with $\dot{\gamma}$. Equivalently:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} = K(n-1)\dot{\gamma}^{n-2} = 12(0.35)\dot{\gamma}^{-0.65} = 4.2 \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{-0.65} > 0$$

The derivative is strictly positive for all $\dot{\gamma} > 0$. Hence the suspension is **dilatant**.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Newtonian | Pseudoplastic ($n < 1$) | **Dilatant ($n > 1$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta_{app}$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ | Constant | Decreasing | **Increasing** |
| Flow curve concavity | Linear | Concave down | **Concave up** |
| Log-log slope $n$ | 1 | 0.2 – 0.9 | **1.1 – 1.5** |
| Common food example | Water, milk | Ketchup, honey | **Concentrated starch, rice flour pastes** |
| Microstructural cause | Molecular momentum transfer | Structure breakdown | **Particle crowding & dilation** |
| Behaviour under sudden impact | No change | Thins further | **Temporarily solidifies** |
| Shear recovery | Instantaneous | Instantaneous | **Instantaneous** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart — Microstructural Origin of Dilatancy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA1[Resting State: Particles close packed, liquid fills voids]
    nodeA2[Apply Shear Stress: Particles forced to slide and roll]
    nodeA3[Particle Displacement: New voids form between particles]
    nodeA4[Liquid Deficiency: Free liquid cannot flood voids instantaneously]
    nodeA5[Direct Particle to Particle Contact]
    nodeA6[Frictional Stress Dominates Over Viscous Lubrication]
    nodeA7[Sharp Rise in Apparent Viscosity]
    nodeA8[Shear Thickening Dilatant Behaviour Observed]
    nodeA9[Shear Removed: Particles re settle by gravity and Brownian motion]
    nodeA10[Original Close Packed Structure Restored]

    nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7
    nodeA7 --> nodeA8
    nodeA8 --> nodeA9
    nodeA9 --> nodeA10
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The diagram flows **top-to-bottom** showing the four-stage transition from quiescent equilibrium to shear-induced jamming, and then **recovers** back to the resting state when shear is withdrawn. This circular reversibility is the **defining signature** of true dilatancy (as opposed to thixotropy, where the recovery takes finite time).

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Classification of Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeB1[Time Independent Fluids: Viscosity depends only on instantaneous shear rate]
    nodeB2[Newtonian: tau equals K times gamma dot, n equals 1]
    nodeB3[Non Newtonian Generalized]
    nodeB4[Pseudoplastic Shear Thinning: n less than 1]
    nodeB5[Dilatant Shear Thickening: n greater than 1]
    nodeB6[Bingham Plastic: yield stress plus Newtonian]
    nodeB7[Herschel Bulkley Pseudoplastic: yield stress plus shear thinning]
    nodeB8[Herschel Bulkley Dilatant: yield stress plus shear thickening]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB1 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB3 --> nodeB5
    nodeB3 --> nodeB6
    nodeB6 --> nodeB7
    nodeB6 --> nodeB8
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Time-independent fluids branch into two principal arms: **Newtonian** (single leftward arm) and **Non-Newtonian generalized** (rightward arm). The non-Newtonian arm further bifurcates into shear-thinning and shear-thickening categories. When a **yield stress** is added, each category spawns a Herschel–Bulkley variant. The bottom-right block — **Herschel–Bulkley dilatant** — represents the most general dilatant case encountered in food engineering.

---

### 4.3 Flow Curve Shape Reference (Text Schematic)

```
  Shear Stress (Pa)
       |
  4000 |                                  *
       |                            *
  2000 |                      *
       |                *
   500 |          *
       |     *
   100 |   *
       |  *
    50 | *
       |*
    10 |*_______ Dilatant (n > 1) curves UPWARD
       | *  Pseudoplastic (n < 1) curves DOWNWARD
     0 |_________________________________________
        1      10     50     100     500    Shear Rate (s^-1)
```

**Interpretation:** A pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) curve lies *below* the straight Newtonian reference and bends toward the shear-rate axis. A **dilatant curve lies *above* the Newtonian reference and bends away from the shear-rate axis**, indicating that progressively more stress is required to achieve each unit increase in shear rate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

Define **dilatant flow behaviour**. State two food-system examples where it is encountered.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE] **Mark Distribution**
> - Definition: 2 marks
> - Two examples: 1 mark (½ mark each)

**Dilatant flow behaviour** is a time-independent non-Newtonian flow behaviour in which the **apparent viscosity of the fluid increases with increasing shear rate**. Mathematically, in the Ostwald–de Waele power-law model, the flow behaviour index $n > 1$, giving $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$.

**Two Food Examples:**
- **Concentrated cornstarch-in-water suspensions** (above 40 % w/w solids).
- **Rice flour pastes** at high solids concentrations.

*(Acceptable alternatives: highly concentrated fruit purees with high pulp content, wet sand analogy, some high-solids chocolate formulations.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

Differentiate between **pseudoplastic** and **dilatant** flow behaviour. Which one is more common in food systems and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE] **Mark Distribution**
> - Tabular or point differentiation: 2 marks
> - Frequency in food systems + reasoning: 1 mark

| Feature | Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) | Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) |
|---|---|---|
| Power-law index $n$ | $n < 1$ | $n > 1$ |
| Apparent viscosity trend | Decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ | Increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Microstructural cause | Progressive breakdown of internal structure | Particle crowding, dilation, jamming |
| Example | Ketchup, honey, cream | Concentrated starch suspensions |

**Pseudoplastic behaviour is far more common in food systems** because most food fluids (sauces, dairy, purees) contain weakly bound flocs, droplets, or macromolecular networks that **disaggregate under shear**, reducing viscosity. True dilatancy requires solid fractions above the random close-packing limit, which is rarer in commercial food formulations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat flow curve, explain **dilatant flow behaviour**. Discuss its mechanism at the microstructural level and state **three food-system examples**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 50 % w/w rice flour suspension follows the power-law model with $K = 8 \; \text{Pa·s}^{n}$ and $n = 1.28$. Determine:
   - (i) The apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 25 \; \text{s}^{-1}$.
   - (ii) The shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 80 \; \text{s}^{-1}$.
   - (iii) Whether the suspension is shear-thickening. Justify with a derivative-based proof.

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Discussion:**

**Definition:** Dilatant flow behaviour (shear-thickening) is a non-Newtonian, time-independent flow in which apparent viscosity **rises** with shear rate. The flow curve is **concave upward** when plotted as $\tau$ versus $\dot{\gamma}$.

**Flow Curve Sketch (description for answer script):**
- X-axis: Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ in s$^{-1}$.
- Y-axis: Shear stress $\tau$ in Pa.
- Plot an upward-curving line starting from the origin, bending away from the $\dot{\gamma}$ axis. Reference a straight Newtonian line for comparison.

**Microstructural Mechanism (3 marks worth of content):**
- At rest, particles sit in a close-packed arrangement with liquid filling all voids.
- Under shear, particles separate (dilate) creating new voids.
- Liquid cannot fill new voids instantly, causing direct particle–particle contacts.
- Frictional stresses dominate over hydrodynamic lubrication.
- Net effect: viscosity rises sharply.

**Three Food Examples:**
- Concentrated cornstarch suspensions (above 40 % w/w solids).
- Rice flour pastes at high solids loadings.
- Highly concentrated fruit pulps with high insoluble solids.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $K = 8 \; \text{Pa·s}^{n}$, $n = 1.28$.

**(i) Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 25 \; \text{s}^{-1}$:**

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = 8 \times (25)^{1.28 - 1} = 8 \times (25)^{0.28}$$

Compute $(25)^{0.28}$:

$$\ln(25) = 3.2189$$

$$0.28 \times 3.2189 = 0.9013$$

$$e^{0.9013} = 2.4628$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{app} = 8 \times 2.4628 = 19.70 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

---

**(ii) Shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 80 \; \text{s}^{-1}$:**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} = 8 \times (80)^{1.28}$$

Compute $(80)^{1.28}$:

$$\ln(80) = 4.3820$$

$$1.28 \times 4.3820 = 5.6090$$

$$e^{5.6090} = 273.06$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = 8 \times 273.06 = 2184.5 \; \text{Pa}$$

---

**(iii) Shear-thickening verification:**

Differentiate the apparent viscosity with respect to shear rate:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} = K(n-1) \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-2} = 8 \times (0.28) \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{-0.72}$$

Since $K = 8 > 0$, $(n - 1) = 0.28 > 0$, and $\dot{\gamma}^{-0.72} > 0$ for all positive $\dot{\gamma}$:

$$\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} > 0 \quad \forall \; \dot{\gamma} > 0$$

Hence the apparent viscosity is **strictly increasing** — the suspension is **dilatant (shear-thickening)**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Students commonly **forget to apply the chain rule correctly** when differentiating the power-law form, ending up with $K \cdot n \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ instead of $K \cdot (n-1) \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-2}$. Always differentiate $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$, **not** $\dot{\gamma}^{n}$. This single algebraic slip costs **2 marks** in the valuation.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Retest, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **mathematical models** (Ostwald–de Waele power law, Herschel–Bulkley, Cross model) used to describe dilatant flow. Indicate the conditions under which each is preferred.

**(b) [7 marks]** During a pump trial, a food engineer observes that the **pressure drop increases disproportionately** with flow velocity when pumping a 42 % w/w starch suspension through a 5 cm diameter pipe. Explain this observation in terms of **dilatant flow behaviour** and suggest **two engineering modifications** to mitigate the problem.

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Model Comparison:**

| Model | Equation | Best For | Limitations for Dilatant Systems |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \; n > 1$ | Mid-range shear rates; simple parameter extraction via log-log plot | Fails at very low and very high $\dot{\gamma}$ (no plateau) |
| **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \; n > 1$ | Systems with a measurable yield stress plus shear-thickening | Requires accurate yield-stress measurement; rare in pure dilatant foods |
| **Cross Model** | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (C\dot{\gamma})^{m}}$ | Full-range fitting including low- and high-shear plateaus | Four parameters; requires more data points; $m < 0$ needed for shear-thickening |

**Preferred Conditions:**
- Use **Power Law** for routine quality-control work where only mid-range shear data is available.
- Use **Herschel–Bulkley** when a yield stress is independently measurable (e.g., via vane rheometer).
- Use **Cross Model** when comprehensive low-shear and high-shear plateau data are available, especially for **concentrated suspensions approaching random close packing**.

---

**Part (b) — Engineering Explanation and Mitigation:**

**Explanation of Disproportionate Pressure Drop:**

For a power-law fluid flowing in a pipe, the relationship between volumetric flow rate $Q$ and pressure drop $\Delta P$ over a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ is given by the **generalized Hagen–Poiseuille equation**:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{32 \cdot K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{w}^{\,n}}{D} \cdot \left(\dfrac{3n + 1}{4n}\right)^{n}$$

where $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ is the wall shear rate. For a dilatant fluid with $n = 1.3$, the wall shear rate scales as:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{(3n+1) \cdot 8V}{4nD} \approx \dfrac{3.9 \times 8V}{4 \times 1.3 \times 0.05} = 120 V$$

**Logic:** The factor $(3n+1)/(4n)$ in the pressure-drop expression grows super-linearly when $n > 1$. Hence doubling the velocity more than doubles the pressure drop — exactly matching the engineer's observation.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Pump Selection**
> Pumps designed for Newtonian fluids (centrifugal pumps) **fail catastrophically** with dilatant suspensions because the motor encounters a rising load curve that may stall. Always specify **positive-displacement pumps** with **variable-speed drives** for dilatant food pastes.

**Two Engineering Modifications:**

- **Modification 1 — Increase Pipe Diameter:**
  Larger $D$ reduces the wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ for the same flow velocity. For dilatant fluids, this drop in shear rate **compounds** with the lower $n$ exponent to drastically reduce $\Delta P$.

- **Modification 2 — Reduce Flow Velocity:**
