  Operating below the critical shear rate (where dilatancy onset occurs) keeps the fluid in its low-viscosity regime. This may require **parallel piping** or **batch-wise transfer** instead of continuous high-velocity flow.

- *(Optional Modification 3 — Pre-shear Conditioning):*
  Subject the suspension to a brief, mild pre-shear before pumping so that the particles re-settle into the close-packed state before entering the line.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistake**
> Students frequently suggest "dilute the suspension with water" as a solution. While effective, this **alters product formulation** and is generally **not acceptable** in food processing unless specifically authorised. Examiners deduct marks for this answer because it ignores the **core rheological problem**. Always propose **equipment-level or process-level** modifications, not product reformulation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition (1 line):** Dilatant flow = shear-thickening; apparent viscosity increases with shear rate; $n > 1$ in the power-law model.
- **Originator:** Osborne Reynolds (1885); mechanical dilation of particles under shear.
- **Microstructural Cause:** Particle crowding, void formation, frictional lock-up when free liquid cannot fill gaps.
- **Threshold Condition:** Solid volume fraction $\phi$ must exceed random close packing ($\phi_{rcp} \approx 0.64$).
- **Power Law:** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n > 1$.
- **Apparent Viscosity Formula:** $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- **Dilatancy Proof:** $\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{d\dot{\gamma}} = K(n-1)\dot{\gamma}^{n-2} > 0$ for $n > 1$.
- **Reversibility:** True dilatancy is **instantaneously reversible** when shear stops (no time-dependence).
- **Food Examples:** Concentrated starch suspensions, rice flour pastes, high-solids fruit pulps.
- **Non-Food Examples:** Wet sand, cornstarch–water mixtures (oobleck).
- **Distinguish From:** Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning, $n < 1$), thixotropic (time-dependent recovery), rheopectic (time-dependent thickening).
- **Flow Curve Shape:** Concave upward; lies *above* the Newtonian reference line.
- **Engineering Concern:** Pumping costs scale super-linearly with velocity; use positive-displacement pumps.
- **Sub-Classes:** Continuous (CST) and Discontinuous (DST) shear thickening.
- **Log-Log Plot:** Slope = $n$, intercept = $\log K$.
- **DO NOT Confuse:** Dilatancy ≠ thermodynamic dilatancy in gases.
- **Key Comparison Metric:** Apparent viscosity *rises*, never falls, with shear rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheopectic Flow Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rheopectic Flow Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rheopectic Flow Behaviour (Rheopexy)** is a specific category of **time-dependent non-Newtonian fluid behaviour** in which the apparent viscosity of the material **increases progressively with time** when subjected to a **constant rate of shear** (or constant shear stress). The structural build-up is gradual and reversible upon removal of the shear field.

In the context of the **PEFTT745 – Food Rheology and Microstructure** syllabus (Module 2: Time-Dependent Flow Behaviour), rheopexy is grouped alongside thixotropy, but represents the **mirror-image response** in terms of viscosity evolution.

| Parameter | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Shear input | Constant shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ = constant |
| Viscosity response | $\eta_{app}(t)$ **increases** monotonically with $t$ |
| Structural change | Progressive **build-up** of internal structure |
| Reversibility | Structurally **reversible** (recoverable at rest) |
| Recovery direction | Opposite to thixotropy |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Yogurt Stirring Test:** Imagine slowly stirring a thick, fresh set yogurt in a bowl. Instead of becoming runnier (which would be *thixotropy*), the yogurt *stiffens* and resists the spoon more and more the longer you keep stirring it at the same speed. Leave it undisturbed for a while, and when you stir again, it returns to its original softer state. This **time-dependent viscosity growth under steady shear** is rheopexy.

A more engineering-oriented analogy: certain **gypsum slurries** and **printing inks** show rheopexy — they "set" or "gel" more when worked continuously at a fixed shear rate rather than when left at rest.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Feature — The "Time" Variable

Both thixotropy and rheopexy require the **time element** as a fourth rheological axis (alongside stress, strain, and rate). The defining equation that separates them is:

$$\frac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} \bigg|_{\dot{\gamma}=\text{const}}$$

- **Thixotropy:** $\quad \dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} \big|_{\dot{\gamma}} \;<\; 0$  (viscosity **decreases**)
- **Rheopexy:**  $\quad \dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} \big|_{\dot{\gamma}} \;>\; 0$  (viscosity **increases**)
- **Time-independent:**  $\quad \dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} \big|_{\dot{\gamma}} \;=\; 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse *shear-thickening (dilatancy)* with *rheopexy*. Remember: **dilatancy** is *instantaneous* and depends only on the current shear rate; **rheopexy** is *time-evolutionary* at a fixed shear rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Structural Mechanism (Why Does Viscosity Increase?)

Rheopectic systems possess microstructural units (particles, droplets, macromolecular chains, flocs) that, under the influence of a sustained shear field, **organize into a more entangled, more space-filling, or more strongly interacting network** than the rest state. The mechanism typically involves:

1. **Particle reorientation:** Anisometric particles (rods, plates) rotate under shear and align edge-to-edge, forming load-bearing chains or "house-of-cards" networks.
2. **Induced flocculation:** Gentle, continuous shear can push particles across the primary minimum repulsion barrier, encouraging controlled aggregation.
3. **Entanglement densification:** Polymer coils progressively interpenetrate under steady shear, raising entanglements per unit volume.
4. **Syneresis-like densification:** In some gels, shear squeezes out interstitial fluid, locally concentrating the dispersed phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Answers:** Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the *mechanistic explanation*, not just naming the phenomenon. Always state the structural change driving the viscosity increase.

### 2.2 Position in the KTU Classification Hierarchy

```
                    NON-NEWTONIAN FLUIDS
                            |
        +-------------------+-------------------+
        |                                       |
TIME-INDEPENDENT                       TIME-DEPENDENT
        |                                       |
+-------+-------+----------------+     +--------+--------+
|       |       |                |     |                 |
Shear  Shear  Yield            Visco-  Thixotropy    Rheopexy
thinning thickening stress      elastic              (η ↑ with t)
(η↓)   (η↑)    (τ₀)            (σ,ε)   (η ↓ with t)
```

Rheopexy falls under **time-dependent** fluids. It is sometimes called **negative thixotropy** or **anti-thixotropy** in older literature.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you are likely to need for KTU 2024 ESE problems on rheopectic flow. Memorize the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{app}(t,\dot{\gamma}) = \dfrac{\tau(t,\dot{\gamma})}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent (time-dependent) viscosity definition | All time-dependent fluids |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d\eta_{app}}{dt}\bigg\|_{\dot{\gamma}} = +k\,(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app})$ | **Weltman-type** first-order kinetic model for rheopexy | Structural build-up modelling |
| 3 | $\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_{\infty} - (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{-k\,t}$ | Integrated solution of the above | $\eta_{0}$ = initial, $\eta_{\infty}$ = equilibrium |
| 4 | $\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_{0} + (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,(1 - e^{-k\,t})$ | Alternative form, $\eta_{0}$ = initial viscosity | Used when $\eta_{\infty} > \eta_{0}$ |
| 5 | $\tau(t) - \tau_{\infty} = (\tau_{0} - \tau_{\infty})\,e^{-k\,t}$ | Stress evolution form | $\tau_{0}$ = initial, $\tau_{\infty}$ = equilibrium |
| 6 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_{0}\,\bigl(1 + k'\,t\bigr)$ | Shear-rate compensation for true-rheopexy tests | Maintaining constant $\tau$ |
| 7 | $A_{rheopexy} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T}\!\bigl[\eta_{app}(t) - \eta_{0}\bigr]\,dt$ | Area enclosed in hysteresis loop (anti-clockwise) | Quantifying rheopexy from loop |
| 8 | $\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0} = (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})_{max}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-k\,t}\bigr)$ | Generalized form | Comparing different foods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Note:** Viscosity $\eta$ is in **Pa·s**, shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**, time $t$ in **s**, and the rate constant $k$ in **s⁻¹**. KTU often tests unit consistency — write units in every line of your solution.

### 2.4 Hysteresis Loop Signature of Rheopexy

A **rheopectic material** subjected to a **triangular ramp** of shear rate (up then down) produces a hysteresis loop whose **up-curve lies below the down-curve**. The area of this loop, $A_{rheopexy}$, is a quantitative measure of the time-dependent structural build-up.

For a **thixotropic** material the loop is traversed **clockwise**; for a **rheopectic** material it is traversed **anticlockwise**. This is a favourite KTU short-answer question.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Recognizing Rheopexy

- **Food Process Design:** Pumping, mixing, and filling operations must account for viscosity *growth* during processing (e.g., certain high-protein or starch-rich suspensions).
- **Quality Control:** Detecting rheopexy signals microstructural instability or incipient gelation in products such as ice-cream mixes, surimi gels, and high-solid pastes.
- **Sensory Science:** Mouthfeel perception of "thickening while chewing" is a rheopectic sensation — important in formulating satiety-inducing or texture-modified foods.
- **Shelf-life Prediction:** Structural build-up kinetics give rate constants $k$ that correlate with storage stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weltman-Type Rheopectic Model

We begin with the **first-order structural kinetics** assumption, the same form used for thixotropy, but with the sign reversed to reflect structural *build-up* rather than break-down.

**Step 1 — State the governing kinetic postulate.**

The rate at which the apparent viscosity approaches its equilibrium (maximum) value is proportional to the *remaining gap* between the current viscosity and the equilibrium viscosity.

$$\frac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} = k\,(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app})$$

This is analogous to a first-order chemical reaction $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}$, where the "concentration" of remaining un-built structure $(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app})$ decays exponentially.

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

Rearrange so that all $\eta_{app}$ terms are on one side and all $t$ terms on the other:

$$\frac{d\eta_{app}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}} = k\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides with appropriate limits.**

At $t = 0$: $\eta_{app} = \eta_{0}$ (initial viscosity, before any time-dependent build-up).
At $t = t$: $\eta_{app} = \eta_{app}(t)$.

The left side integrates via the standard substitution $u = \eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}$, $du = -d\eta_{app}$:

$$\int_{\eta_{0}}^{\eta_{app}(t)} \frac{d\eta_{app}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}} = \int_{0}^{t} k\,dt$$

Evaluating:

$$-\ln\!\bigl(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t)\bigr) \Big|_{\eta_{0}}^{\eta_{app}(t)} = k\,t$$

$$-\ln\!\bigl(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t)\bigr) + \ln\!\bigl(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0}\bigr) = k\,t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\eta_{app}(t)$.**

$$\ln\!\frac{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t)} = k\,t$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t)} = e^{k\,t}$$

Invert:

$$\frac{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t)}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0}} = e^{-k\,t}$$

Solve for $\eta_{app}(t)$:

$$\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}(t) = (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{-k\,t}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_{\infty} - (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{-k\,t}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Validate limits (a KTU favourite self-check).**

- At $t = 0$: $\eta_{app}(0) = \eta_{\infty} - (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{0} = \eta_{\infty} - \eta_{\infty} + \eta_{0} = \eta_{0}$ ✓
- As $t \to \infty$: $\eta_{app}(\infty) = \eta_{\infty} - 0 = \eta_{\infty}$ ✓

The viscosity rises **monotonically** from $\eta_{0}$ to $\eta_{\infty}$, confirming rheopectic behaviour.

### 3.2 Equivalence to the Alternative "Build-up" Form

The same equation is often written in food-rheology textbooks as:

$$\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_{0} + (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,(1 - e^{-k\,t})$$

**Verification:**

$$\eta_{0} + (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0}) - (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{-k\,t} = \eta_{\infty} - (\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})\,e^{-k\,t}$$

These are algebraically identical. Both forms are accepted in KTU answer sheets; the second form is more intuitive because the term $(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{0})$ appears explicitly as the **total viscosity gain**.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** A food suspension shows rheopectic flow behaviour. At a constant shear rate of 50 s⁻¹, the initial apparent viscosity is $\eta_{0} = 2.0$ Pa·s, the equilibrium apparent viscosity is $\eta_{\infty} = 8.0$ Pa·s, and the structural build-up rate constant is $k = 0.20$ s⁻¹. Calculate: (a) the apparent viscosity at $t = 5$ s; (b) the time required for the apparent viscosity to reach 6.0 Pa·s; (c) the average rate of viscosity build-up between $t = 0$ and $t = 10$ s.

**Solution to (a):** Use the build-up form:

$$\eta_{app}(5) = 2.0 + (8.0 - 2.0)\,(1 - e^{-0.20 \times 5})$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - e^{-1.0})$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - 0.3679)$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0 \times 0.6321$$

$$= 2.0 + 3.7926$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}(5) = 5.79 \;\text{Pa·s}\;}$$

**Solution to (b):** Set $\eta_{app}(t) = 6.0$ Pa·s and solve for $t$:

$$6.0 = 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - e^{-0.20\,t})$$

$$4.0 = 6.0\,(1 - e^{-0.20\,t})$$

$$\frac{4.0}{6.0} = 1 - e^{-0.20\,t}$$

$$0.6667 = 1 - e^{-0.20\,t}$$

$$e^{-0.20\,t} = 0.3333$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-0.20\,t = \ln(0.3333) = -1.0986$$

$$t = \frac{1.0986}{0.20} = 5.493 \;\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\;t \approx 5.49 \;\text{s}\;}$$

**Solution to (c):** First compute $\eta_{app}(10)$:

$$\eta_{app}(10) = 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - e^{-0.20 \times 10})$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - e^{-2.0})$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0\,(1 - 0.1353)$$

$$= 2.0 + 6.0 \times 0.8647$$

$$= 2.0 + 5.1883 = 7.188 \;\text{Pa·s}$$

Average rate of build-up:

$$\text{Avg rate} = \frac{\eta_{app}(10) - \eta_{0}}{10 - 0} = \frac{7.188 - 2.0}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Avg rate} = 0.519 \;\text{Pa·s/s}\;}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How to Identify Rheopexy in a KTU Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this decision matrix in the exam before committing to an answer.

| Observation in Experiment | Conclusion |
|---|---|
| $\eta$ constant with time at fixed $\dot{\gamma}$ | Time-independent fluid |
| $\eta$ **decreases** with time at fixed $\dot{\gamma}$ | Thixotropic |
| $\eta$ **increases** with time at fixed $\dot{\gamma}$ | **Rheopectic** |
| $\eta$ **increases** instantly with $\dot{\gamma}$, no time effect | Shear-thickening (dilatant) |
| $\eta$ **decreases** instantly with $\dot{\gamma}$, no time effect | Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) |
| Hysteresis loop traversed **clockwise** | Thixotropic |
| Hysteresis loop traversed **anticlockwise** | **Rheopectic** |
| Recovery at rest is **fast** (seconds) | Weak thixotropy/rheopexy |
| Recovery at rest is **slow** (hours) | Strong thixotropy/rheopexy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Microstructural Mechanism (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rest State: Disordered Particles] --> B[Apply Constant Shear Rate]
    B --> C[Particles Rotate and Align]
    C --> D[Edge-to-Edge Contact Forms]
    D --> E[Load-Bearing Network Develops]
    E --> F[Apparent Viscosity Rises with Time]
    F --> G[Equilibrium Reached at eta_infinity]
    G --> H[Remove Shear]
    H --> I[Network Slowly Disentangles]
    I --> J[Return to Rest State eta_0]
```

### 4.2 Viscosity-vs-Time Curve (The "Rheopexy Signature")

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[t = 0, eta = eta_0] --> Q1[Rapid Initial Rise]
    Q1 --> R1[t increases, eta approaches eta_infinity]
    R1 --> S1[t large, eta to eta_infinity asymptotically]
    S1 --> T1[Equilibrium plateau reached]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constant-Shear-Rate Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        S1[Step Motor: Constant RPM]
        S2[Set Shear Rate gamma_dot]
    end
    subgraph SAMPLE[Measurement Stage]
        C1[Concentric Cylinder Geometry]
        C2[Food Sample Loaded]
        C3[Thermostat Bath at T]
    end
    subgraph READOUT[Data Acquisition]
        D1[Torque Sensor Reads tau t]
        D2[Compute eta_app t equals tau t divided by gamma_dot]
        D3[Plot eta vs t Curve]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSE[Analysis]
        E1{Is eta rising with t at fixed gamma_dot?}
        E2[Yes: Rheopectic]
        E3[No: Check other classes]
    end
    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 -- YES --> E2
    E1 -- NO --> E3
```

### 4.4 Hysteresis Loop Direction (Rheopexy vs Thixotropy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THIX[Thixotropy: Clockwise Loop]
        T1[Up Curve LOWER] --> T2[Down Curve HIGHER]
    end
    subgraph RHEO[Rheopexy: Anticlockwise Loop]
        R1[Up Curve HIGHER] --> R2[Down Curve LOWER]
    end
```

### 4.5 Position of Rheopexy in the Master Rheological Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FL[All Fluids] --> NW[Newtonian]
    FL --> NN[Non-Newtonian]
    NN --> TI[Time Independent]
    NN --> TD[Time Dependent]
    TI --> STN[Shear Thinning]
    TI --> STK[Shear Thickening Dilatant]
    TI --> YS[Yield Stress]
    TD --> THX[Thixotropy eta down with t]
    TD --> RHP[Rheopexy eta up with t]
    TD --> VIS[Viscoelastic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block represents a functional state, not a literal microscopic picture. In a KTU answer script, redraw the **microstructural sequence** (Section 4.1) as a small labeled sketch — examiners reward visualized mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define rheopectic flow behaviour. How is it different from thixotropy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rheopectic flow behaviour is a time-dependent non-Newtonian behaviour in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **increases progressively with time** when the fluid is sheared at a **constant shear rate**, due to the gradual build-up of an internal structure. (1.5 marks)

It differs from thixotropy as follows: thixotropy shows a **decrease** in apparent viscosity with time under constant shear (structural break-down), whereas rheopexy shows an **increase** in apparent viscosity (structural build-up). In a hysteresis loop test, thixotropy gives a **clockwise** loop while rheopexy gives an **anticlockwise** loop. (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**State any two food examples that exhibit rheopectic behaviour and explain the structural reason for the viscosity rise in one of them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two food examples exhibiting rheopexy:
1. Certain high-protein suspensions and caseinate dispersions. (0.5 marks)
2. Some gypsum-like mineral pastes and certain starch-rich concentrates, plus freshly prepared ice-cream mixes during the early stages of aging. (0.5 marks)

Structural reason — taking caseinate dispersions: under steady shear, protein molecules progressively unfold and reorient, exposing hydrophobic patches that associate across the flow field. This leads to a slow, time-dependent build-up of a **load-bearing network**, increasing the apparent viscosity with time at constant shear rate. (2 marks)

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the Weltman-type first-order kinetic model for a rheopectic fluid. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A concentrated cocoa suspension exhibits rheopectic behaviour. At $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹, the initial viscosity $\eta_0 = 0.8$ Pa·s and the equilibrium viscosity $\eta_\infty = 3.2$ Pa·s. If the structural build-up rate constant is $k = 0.15$ s⁻¹, determine (i) the apparent viscosity at $t = 8$ s, and (ii) the time required for the apparent viscosity to grow to 2.6 Pa·s. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

The Weltman-type kinetic model assumes that the rate of structural build-up (and hence the rate of viscosity rise) is **first-order** with respect to the *remaining gap* between the current viscosity $\eta_{app}(t)$ and the equilibrium viscosity $\eta_\infty$. The mathematical form is:

$$\frac{d\eta_{app}}{dt} = k\,(\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app})$$

where $k$ is the rate constant (s⁻¹). The sign is **positive** because $\eta_{app}$ is rising toward $\eta_\infty$.

Separating variables:

$$\frac{d\eta_{app}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}} = k\,dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $\eta_{app} = \eta_0$) to general $t$:

$$\int_{\eta_0}^{\eta_{app}(t)} \frac{d\eta_{app}}{\eta_{\infty} - \eta_{app}} = \int_0^t k\,dt$$

$$-\ln(\eta_\infty - \eta_{app})\Big|_{\eta_0}^{\eta_{app}(t)} = k\,t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\eta_\infty - \eta_0}{\eta_\infty - \eta_{app}(t)}\right) = k\,t$$

Exponentiating and rearranging:

$$\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_\infty - (\eta_\infty - \eta_0)\,e^{-k\,t}$$

Equivalently: $\;\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_0 + (\eta_\infty - \eta_0)\,(1 - e^{-k\,t})\;$

**Assumptions (must list for full marks):** (i) single first-order kinetic process; (ii) isothermal conditions; (iii) $\eta_\infty$ and $k$ are constants independent of shear rate and time; (iv) structure recovers fully when shear is removed; (v) no chemical reaction or evaporation occurs. (Full 7 marks)

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $\eta_0 = 0.8$ Pa·s, $\eta_\infty = 3.2$ Pa·s, $k = 0.15$ s⁻¹, $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹.

**(i)** $\eta_{app}$ at $t = 8$ s:

$$\eta_{app}(8) = 0.8 + (3.2 - 0.8)\,(1 - e^{-0.15 \times 8})$$

$$= 0.8 + 2.4\,(1 - e^{-1.2})$$

$$= 0.8 + 2.4\,(1 - 0.3012)$$

$$= 0.8 + 2.4 \times 0.6988$$

$$= 0.8 + 1.6772$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}(8) = 2.48 \;\text{Pa·s}\;}$$

**(ii)** Time to reach $\eta_{app} = 2.6$ Pa·s:

$$2.6 = 0.8 + 2.4\,(1 - e^{-0.15\,t})$$

$$1.8 = 2.4\,(1 - e^{-0.15\,t})$$

$$\frac{1.8}{2.4} = 0.75 = 1 - e^{-0.15\,t}$$

$$e^{-0.15\,t} = 0.25$$

$$-0.15\,t = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863$$

$$t = \frac{1.3863}{0.15}$$

$$\boxed{\;t = 9.24 \;\text{s}\;}$$

(Full 7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of hysteresis loop in time-dependent fluids. Draw and explain the qualitative difference between the thixotropic and rheopectic hysteresis loops. (7 marks)

**(b)** A food paste is tested in a rotational rheometer with a triangular shear-rate ramp (0 → 200 s⁻¹ in 60 s, then 200 → 0 s⁻¹ in the next 60 s). The recorded shear stress data shows that the down-curve lies **below** the up-curve, and the area enclosed between them is 850 Pa·s⁻¹. Identify the type of time-dependent behaviour, give the mathematical expression for the apparent viscosity at any time $t$ on the up-curve given $\eta_0 = 1.5$ Pa·s, $\eta_\infty = 4.5$ Pa·s, and $k = 0.08$ s⁻¹, and compute $\eta_{app}$ at $t = 20$ s. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

A hysteresis loop in a rotational rheometer test is obtained by **linearly ramping the shear rate up** from zero to a maximum, and then **linearly ramping it down** back to zero, while continuously recording shear stress. The plot of $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ for the up-ramp and the down-ramp generally do **not** coincide, because the internal structure of the material is time-dependent. The enclosed area between the two curves is a quantitative measure of the time-dependent structural change. (3 marks)

For a **thixotropic** material, the down-curve lies **above** the up-curve (because the structure is still partly broken down during the down-ramp, and the stress is higher at any given $\dot{\gamma}$). The loop is traversed **clockwise**. (2 marks)

For a **rheopectic** material, the down-curve lies **below** the up-curve (because the structure has been progressively built up during the up-ramp, and during the down-ramp the material is now "stronger", giving lower stress at any given $\dot{\gamma}$). The loop is traversed **anticlockwise**. (2 marks)

(Full 7 marks)

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Identification:** The down-curve lies **below** the up-curve → this is **rheopectic flow behaviour** (anticlockwise loop). (2 marks)

**Mathematical expression for apparent viscosity at any time $t$ on the up-curve:**

$$\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_\infty - (\eta_\infty - \eta_0)\,e^{-k\,t}$$

$$\eta_{app}(t) = 4.5 - (4.5 - 1.5)\,e^{-0.08\,t} = 4.5 - 3.0\,e^{-0.08\,t}$$

(2 marks)

**Compute $\eta_{app}$ at $t = 20$ s:**

$$\eta_{app}(20) = 4.5 - 3.0\,e^{-0.08 \times 20}$$

$$= 4.5 - 3.0\,e^{-1.6}$$

$$= 4.5 - 3.0 \times 0.2019$$

$$= 4.5 - 0.6057$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}(20) = 3.89 \;\text{Pa·s}\;}$$

(3 marks)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Rheopexy Questions**
> 1. **Sign confusion in kinetic models** — using the thixotropy equation ($\eta_\infty$ below) instead of the rheopexy equation. Always re-derive the sign from $\frac{d\eta}{dt} = +k(\eta_\infty - \eta)$ before substituting numbers.
> 2. **Forgetting the "constant shear rate" clause** — rheopexy is *defined* at fixed $\dot{\gamma}$. A simple shear-thickening fluid without time dependence is **not** rheopectic.
> 3. **Mixing up hysteresis-loop direction** — clockwise = thixotropy; anticlockwise = rheopexy. A single wrong arrow costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit slips** — $k$ in s⁻¹, $t$ in s, $\eta$ in Pa·s. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per unit inconsistency.
> 5. **No structural mechanism stated** — for 7-mark derivations, you must list at least two assumptions and explain *why* viscosity increases.
> 6. **Limits not verified** — failing to check $\eta(0) = \eta_0$ and $\eta(\infty) = \eta_\infty$ leads to -1 mark deduction in many board papers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Rheopectic Flow Behaviour**

- **Definition:** Viscosity **increases** with time at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ — a **time-dependent** non-Newtonian behaviour.
- **Other names:** Negative thixotropy, anti-thixotropy.
- **Kinetic model:** $\eta_{app}(t) = \eta_0 + (\eta_\infty - \eta_0)\,(1 - e^{-k\,t})$.
- **Equilibrium:** $\eta_{app} \to \eta_\infty$ as $t \to \infty$.
- **Hysteresis loop:** Down-curve **below** up-curve; **anticlockwise** traversal.
- **Structural cause:** Progressive build-up of an organized, load-bearing network under sustained shear.
- **Distinguish from:** Thixotropy (opposite direction), shear-thickening (no time effect), viscoelasticity (involves elastic recovery).
- **Food examples:** High-protein suspensions, some starch pastes, certain fresh caseinate or surimi systems, ice-cream mixes during aging.
- **Test conditions:** Isothermal, constant $\dot{\gamma}$, sufficient time (often 60–600 s) to approach $\eta_\infty$.
- **Engineering implications:** Pumping power rises during processing; sensory thickening in mouth; indicator of incipient gelation.
- **Symbol conventions:** $k$ (s⁻¹), $t$ (s), $\eta$ (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹), $\tau$ (Pa).
- **KTU hot keywords:** "build-up", "time-dependent", "first-order kinetics", "Weltman model", "anticlockwise hysteresis", "negative thixotropy".
- **Quick self-test formula:** If $\frac{d\eta_{app}}{dt}\big|_{\dot{\gamma}=\text{const}} > 0$, the fluid is rheopectic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Flow Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastic Flow Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Flow Behaviour** is a category of **time-independent, non-Newtonian fluid behaviour** in which the material behaves as a rigid solid until the applied shear stress exceeds a **critical threshold (yield stress, $\tau_0$)**, beyond which the material flows like a fluid.

In KTU terminology, plastic foods are **viscoelastic–plastic materials** whose rheological response is governed by a structured internal network (e.g., fat crystal networks, starch granules, protein gels) that must first be **structurally disrupted** before any macroscopic flow can occur.

Mathematically, the plastic regime is defined by the existence of a finite **yield stress** $\tau_0 > 0$ such that:

$$\tau < \tau_0 \implies \dot{\gamma} = 0$$

$$\tau \geq \tau_0 \implies \dot{\gamma} > 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Yield Stress ($\tau_0$)** — the minimum shear stress required to initiate irreversible deformation in a structured food material. Unit: **Pa** (Pascal).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **squeezing a tube of tomato ketchup** 🍅.

- **Before squeezing (low stress):** The ketchup does *not* move at all — it sits inside the bottle like a solid plug. This is because the internal network (water, pulp, sugars, suspended solids) holds the structure together.
- **Increasing squeeze force slowly:** Still no flow. The stress is below the yield value.
- **Beyond a critical squeeze force:** The ketchup **suddenly yields**, breaks its internal "scaffold", and starts squirting out smoothly through the nozzle.

This **"stick-slip-then-flow"** behaviour is the visual signature of **plastic flow**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world plastic foods:** Mayonnaise, ketchup, mustard, tomato paste, margarine, peanut butter, chocolate (molten), yogurt (set type), and ice cream mix.

---

## 1.3 Classification Context within Non-Newtonian Fluids

Plastic flow is one of several time-independent shear behaviours:

| Behaviour Class | Yield Stress? | Shear Thinning? | Example Food |
|-----------------|:-------------:|:---------------:|--------------|
| Newtonian | ❌ | ❌ | Water, milk, vegetable oil |
| Pseudoplastic (shear thinning) | ❌ | ✅ | Apple sauce, banana puree |
| Dilatant (shear thickening) | ❌ | ✅ (positive) | Cornstarch slurry |
| **Plastic (yield + flow)** | ✅ | ✅ | **Ketchup, mayonnaise, chocolate** |

> [!CAUTION]
> Many KTU students incorrectly classify ketchup as *purely pseudoplastic*. Always remember: a true plastic fluid **must** exhibit a measurable yield stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Plastic Flow Models

### 🔹 (A) Bingham Plastic Model — The Simplest Plastic Fluid

Proposed by **Eugene Bingham (1916)**, this model treats the post-yield behaviour as **linear viscous** (just like a Newtonian fluid, but offset by $\tau_0$).

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $\eta_{pl}$ = **plastic viscosity** (also called Bingham viscosity) — constant (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

**Interpretation:** Once $\tau > \tau_0$, the relationship between $\tau$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ becomes a **straight line** of slope $\eta_{pl}$.

---

### 🔹 (B) Herschel–Bulkley Model — The Generalised Plastic Model

This is a **super-model** that combines yield stress with a power-law post-yield behaviour.

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

Where:
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless)
- If $n < 1$ → **shear-thinning plastic** (e.g., ketchup)
- If $n = 1$ → reduces to **Bingham plastic**
- If $n > 1$ → **shear-thickening plastic** (rare in foods)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Herschel–Bulkley** model is the most commonly fitted model in food rheology research papers and is **highly favoured in KTU ESE questions**.

---

### 🔹 (C) Casson Model — The Two-Parameter Food Plastic Model

Widely used for **chocolate**, **tomato paste**, and **pigment suspensions**. It captures the asymptotic viscosity at high shear rates.

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\eta_{\infty}$ = viscosity at infinite shear rate (Pa·s)

Squaring both sides:

$$\tau = \left(\sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}\right)^{2}$$

---

## 2.2 Apparent Viscosity for Plastic Fluids

Unlike Newtonian fluids, plastic materials exhibit an **apparent viscosity** $\eta_{app}$ that depends on shear rate:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_{pl} \quad \text{(Bingham)}$$

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} \quad \text{(Herschel–Bulkley)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$, $\eta_{app} \to \eta_{pl}$ for Bingham and $\eta_{app} \to \infty$ for pseudoplastic Herschel–Bulkley with $n < 1$. This is **why** plastic foods "feel thinner" when stirred faster.

---

## 2.3 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Model | Governing Equation | Parameters | Units | Typical Food |
|---|-------|--------------------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl}\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0$, $\eta_{pl}$ | Pa, Pa·s | Tomato paste |
| 2 | **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_0 + K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\tau_0$, $K$, $n$ | Pa, Pa·sⁿ, – | Ketchup, mayonnaise |
| 3 | **Casson** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty}\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_0$, $\eta_{\infty}$ | Pa, Pa·s | Molten chocolate |
| 4 | **Apparent Viscosity** | $\eta_{app} = \tau \big/ \dot{\gamma}$ | – | Pa·s | All non-Newtonian |
| 5 | **Shear Rate Cone-Plate** | $\dot{\gamma} = \Omega \big/ \alpha$ | $\Omega$, $\alpha$ | rad/s, rad | Geometry-dependent |
| 6 | **Shear Stress Cone-Plate** | $\tau = \frac{3F}{2\pi R^{2}}$ | $F$, $R$ | N, m | Force at cone tip |
| 7 | **Yield Stress (Casson)** | $\tau_0 = (\text{intercept})^{2}$ | – | Pa | From $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ plot |

> [!TIP]
> For numerical problems, KTU examiners typically ask: **"Fit a Bingham model to the given data and compute $\tau_0$ and $\eta_{pl}$"**. Use linear regression on $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ data.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Plastic Flow Models

- **Process design:** Pipe-line pressure drops for plastic foods (must overcome $\tau_0$ before flow begins).
- **Quality control:** Chocolate tempering relies on precise $\tau_0$ values for proper mouthfeel.
- **Sensory science:** Yield stress correlates with **"thickness perception"** and spreadability of spreads and sauces.
- **Shelf-life prediction:** $\tau_0$ changes with storage as the microstructure evolves (syneresis, crystallization).
- **3D food printing:** The yield stress determines whether a material will hold its shape after extrusion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Apparent Viscosity of a Bingham Plastic

**Starting point:** The constitutive equation of a Bingham plastic material.

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

**Goal:** Express the apparent viscosity $\eta_{app}$ as a function of shear rate.

**Step 1 — Definition of apparent viscosity:**

$$\eta_{app} \equiv \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Bingham equation:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Separate numerator terms:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + \frac{\eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second term:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_{pl}$$

> ✅ **Result:** For a Bingham plastic, the apparent viscosity is the sum of a *shear-thinning structural term* $\tau_0/\dot{\gamma}$ and a *constant plastic viscosity* $\eta_{pl}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — From Herschel–Bulkley to Apparent Viscosity

**Starting equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

**Step 1 — Apparent viscosity definition:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the division:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Behaviour at limits:**

If $n < 1$ (shear-thinning plastic), as $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$, the term $K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} \to 0$, and so:

$$\lim_{\dot{\gamma}\to\infty} \eta_{app} = \eta_{\infty} = \lim_{\dot{\gamma}\to\infty} \eta_{app}$$

This is the **infinite-shear viscosity**, a key chocolate-processing parameter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Casson Yield Stress from Plot Data

The Casson equation is **linearised** by taking square roots:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty}} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Let $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$ and $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$. Then:

$$Y = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty}} \cdot X$$

This is in **linear form** $Y = a + bX$, where:

- **Intercept** $a = \sqrt{\tau_0}$ → hence $\tau_0 = a^{2}$
- **Slope** $b = \sqrt{\eta_{\infty}}$ → hence $\eta_{\infty} = b^{2}$

**Worked numerical example:**

> Given Casson plot data with intercept $a = 1.50\ \text{Pa}^{1/2}$ and slope $b = 0.85\ \text{(Pa·s)}^{1/2}$:

$$\tau_0 = (1.50)^{2} = 2.25\ \text{Pa}$$

$$\eta_{\infty} = (0.85)^{2} = 0.7225\ \text{Pa·s}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Critical Pressure to Initiate Flow in a Pipe

For a Bingham plastic in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, the **pressure drop $\Delta P$ required to initiate flow** is obtained by force balance on the unyielded plug:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}$$

At incipient flow, $\tau_{wall} = \tau_0$, so:

$$\Delta P_{crit} = \frac{2 L \tau_0}{R}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** in Part B: *"Calculate the minimum pump pressure to start flow of a Bingham plastic in a 10 m pipe of 5 cm radius if $\tau_0 = 12$ Pa."*

**Numerical plug-in:**

$$\Delta P_{crit} = \frac{2 \times 10\ \text{m} \times 12\ \text{Pa}}{0.05\ \text{m}} = 4800\ \text{Pa} = 4.8\ \text{kPa}$$

---

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Newtonian | Bingham Plastic | Herschel–Bulkley | Casson |
|----------|:---------:|:---------------:|:----------------:|:------:|
| Yield stress $\tau_0$ | 0 | > 0 | > 0 | > 0 |
| Post-yield $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ | Linear | Linear | Power-law | Square-root |
| Constants required | 1 ($\eta$) | 2 ($\tau_0$, $\eta_{pl}$) | 3 ($\tau_0$, $K$, $n$) | 2 ($\tau_0$, $\eta_{\infty}$) |
| Predictive at high shear | Good | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Best fit for chocolate | Poor | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Best fit for ketchup | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | Good |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Time-Independent Flow Behaviour

## 4.3 Yield Stress Determination Workflow

```mermaid

    S2 --> S3a["BINGHAM Linear plot tau vs dot gamma"]

    S2 --> S3c["CASSON sqrt tau vs sqrt dot gamma"]

```

## 4.4 Structural Microstructure–Property Mapping for Plastic Foods


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define yield stress. How is it experimentally determined for a Bingham plastic food?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Yield stress ($\tau_0$):** The minimum shear stress that must be applied to a structured food material to initiate irreversible flow. Below $\tau_0$, the material deforms as an elastic solid; at and above $\tau_0$, it begins to flow like a viscous liquid.

**Experimental determination:**
1. A rheometer (rotational, cone-plate or parallel-plate) applies a controlled **shear stress ramp** from low to high.
2. The corresponding **shear rate** is recorded.
3. $\tau$ is plotted against $\dot{\gamma}$.
4. For a Bingham plastic, the post-yield region is a **straight line**.
5. The straight line is **extrapolated back to the $\tau$-axis**.
6. The **y-intercept** of this line equals the yield stress $\tau_0$.

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between a Bingham plastic and a Herschel–Bulkley fluid.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bingham Plastic | Herschel–Bulkley |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Equation | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl}\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = \tau_0 + K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ |
| Number of parameters | 2 | 3 |
| Post-yield behaviour | Linear | Power-law (non-linear) |
| Shear-thinning post-yield | No | Yes, if $n<1$ |
| Special case | $n=1$ in Herschel–Bulkley | – |
| Food example | Tomato paste | Ketchup, mayonnaise |

> **Key distinction:** Bingham assumes constant plastic viscosity, whereas Herschel–Bulkley allows the viscosity to vary with shear rate through the power-law index $n$.

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**a)** *Derive the Bingham plastic constitutive equation and explain the physical meaning of each parameter. Discuss the typical shear stress–shear rate behaviour using a flow curve diagram. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
A Bingham plastic is a material that behaves as a rigid solid below its yield stress $\tau_0$ and flows as a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\eta_{pl}$ above $\tau_0$.

**Step 2 — Constitutive equation:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{for } \tau \geq \tau_0$$

**Step 3 — Physical meaning of parameters:**
- $\tau_0$ (Pa) — represents the **strength of the internal network** (e.g., fat crystal network in margarine). Higher $\tau_0$ → firmer material.
- $\eta_{pl}$ (Pa·s) — the **plastic viscosity**, representing the resistance to flow once the structure has yielded.
- $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) — applied shear rate.

**Step 4 — Flow curve description:**

```
       tau (Pa)
        |
   tau0 +|         /  (linear region)
        |        /
        |       /
        |      /  slope = eta_pl
        |     /
        |____/_________________ dot gamma (1/s)
        0
```

Below $\tau_0$: vertical line (no flow). Above $\tau_0$: straight line of slope $\eta_{pl}$ passing through $(\dot{\gamma}=0, \tau=\tau_0)$.

**Step 5 — Apparent viscosity:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_{pl}$$

This decreases with shear rate because the first term vanishes at high $\dot{\gamma}$, leaving $\eta_{pl}$ as the asymptote.

---

**b)** *A tomato paste in a pipe of radius 5 cm and length 12 m exhibits Bingham behaviour with $\tau_0 = 15\ \text{Pa}$ and $\eta_{pl} = 4.5\ \text{Pa·s}$. The flow rate desired is $Q = 8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$. Calculate (i) the critical pressure drop to initiate flow, and (ii) the pressure drop required to maintain the flow assuming a fully developed laminar flow. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 0.05\ \text{m}$
- $L = 12\ \text{m}$
- $\tau_0 = 15\ \text{Pa}$
- $\eta_{pl} = 4.5\ \text{Pa·s}$
- $Q = 8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$

**Part (i) — Critical pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_{crit} = \frac{2 L \tau_0}{R} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 15}{0.05} = \frac{360}{0.05} = 7200\ \text{Pa} = 7.2\ \text{kPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Pressure drop to maintain flow:**

For Bingham flow in a pipe, the Buckingham–Reiner equation gives:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 L \tau_0}{R} + \frac{8 \eta_{pl} L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$$

Plug in:

$$\Delta P = 7200 + \frac{8 \times 4.5 \times 12 \times 8\times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.05)^{4}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi R^{4} = 3.1416 \times (0.05)^{4} = 3.1416 \times 6.25\times 10^{-6} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-5}$$

Compute numerator:

$$8 \times 4.5 \times 12 \times 8\times 10^{-5} = 0.03456$$

Second term:

$$\frac{0.03456}{1.9635\times 10^{-5}} = 1760.2\ \text{Pa}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P = 7200 + 1760.2 \approx 8960\ \text{Pa} = 8.96\ \text{kPa}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $\Delta P_{crit} = 7.2\ \text{kPa}$; $\Delta P_{flow} = 8.96\ \text{kPa}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**a)** *Explain the Herschel–Bulkley model. Discuss how the flow behaviour index $n$ determines whether the post-yield behaviour is shear-thinning, shear-thickening, or Newtonian. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model form:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Parameters:**
- $\tau_0$ — yield stress (Pa)
- $K$ — consistency coefficient (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ — flow behaviour index (dimensionless)

**Step 3 — Effect of $n$ on post-yield behaviour:**

Taking the derivative:

$$\frac{d\tau}{d\dot{\gamma}} = n K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

- **If $n < 1$:** $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ → curve becomes flatter → **shear-thinning**.
- **If $n = 1$:** $K\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = K = \eta_{pl}$ → straight line → **Newtonian plastic / Bingham**.
- **If $n > 1$:** $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ → curve becomes steeper → **shear-thickening** (dilatant plastic).

**Step 4 — Real food examples:**
- Ketchup: $n \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ (strongly shear-thinning plastic)
- Mayonnaise: $n \approx 0.3$–$0.5$
- Some starch suspensions: $n > 1$ (rare)

---

**b)** *A chocolate melt at 40 °C follows the Casson model. A rheometer test gives the linearised Casson plot with $\sqrt{\tau_0} = 4.2\ \text{Pa}^{0.5}$ on the y-intercept and slope $= 1.6\ \text{(Pa·s)}^{0.5}$. Determine $\tau_0$ and $\eta_{\infty}$. If the Casson plastic viscosity is defined as the slope of the $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ plot, compute the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50\ \text{s}^{-1}$. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Yield stress:**

$$\tau_0 = \left(\sqrt{\tau_0}\right)^{2} = (4.2)^{2} = 17.64\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Infinite-shear viscosity:**

$$\eta_{\infty} = (\text{slope})^{2} = (1.6)^{2} = 2.56\ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50\ \text{s}^{-1}$:**

Using Casson:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_{\infty}\dot{\gamma}}$$

$$\sqrt{\tau} = 4.2 + \sqrt{2.56 \times 50} = 4.2 + \sqrt{128}$$

$$\sqrt{128} \approx 11.31$$

$$\sqrt{\tau} = 4.2 + 11.31 = 15.51$$

$$\tau = (15.51)^{2} \approx 240.6\ \text{Pa}$$

Apparent viscosity:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{240.6}{50} \approx 4.81\ \text{Pa·s}$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $\tau_0 = 17.64\ \text{Pa}$; $\eta_{\infty} = 2.56\ \text{Pa·s}$; $\eta_{app}(50) \approx 4.81\ \text{Pa·s}$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make (and lose marks for):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Always state $\tau_0$ in **Pa**, $\eta$ in **Pa·s**, $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**. A correct number with wrong units gets **partial credit only**.
> 2. **Confusing $\tau_0$ with $\tau_{max}$ or $\tau_{min}$.** Yield stress is **extrapolated**, not directly measured at a single data point.
> 3. **Applying the Newtonian pressure-drop formula to plastic foods.** Use the **Buckingham–Reiner equation** instead.
> 4. **Mixing up Casson plot axes.** It is $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ — **not** $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$.
> 5. **Forgetting the yield term** when writing the Herschel–Bulkley equation. It must read $\tau = \tau_0 + K\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$, not $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$.
> 6. **Using $K$ from one model and substituting into another.** Parameters are model-specific.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Plastic flow** = a **time-independent**, **non-Newtonian** behaviour with a **finite yield stress** $\tau_0$.
- ✅ Three canonical models: **Bingham**, **Herschel–Bulkley**, and **Casson**.
- ✅ Bingham → linear post-yield; Herschel–Bulkley → power-law post-yield; Casson → root-law post-yield.
- ✅ Yield stress is determined by **extrapolating the post-yield line** to $\dot{\gamma} = 0$.
- ✅ Apparent viscosity of Bingham plastic: $\eta_{app} = \tau_0/\dot{\gamma} + \eta_{pl}$.
- ✅ Critical pressure to start pipe flow: $\Delta P_{crit} = 2 L \tau_0 / R$.
- ✅ Casson plot axes: $\sqrt{\tau}$ on y-axis vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ on x-axis; intercept = $\sqrt{\tau_0}$, slope = $\sqrt{\eta_{\infty}}$.
- ✅ $n<1$ (Herschel–Bulkley) = shear-thinning plastic; $n=1$ = Bingham; $n>1$ = shear-thickening plastic.
- ✅ Plastic foods: ketchup, mayonnaise, tomato paste, chocolate, margarine, peanut butter, mustard.
- ✅ Always state **units** explicitly: Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹.
- ✅ Hershel–Bulkley is the **most general** and **most exam-relevant** plastic model in KTU 2024.
- ✅ The plastic viscosity $\eta_{pl}$ is **NOT** the same as apparent viscosity; $\eta_{pl}$ is the asymptotic value at high shear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Transition in Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Transition in Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of food science and the **KTU 2024 PEFTT745 syllabus**, a **phase transition** is defined as the physical transformation of a food material from one thermodynamic state to another, accompanied by a discontinuous change in one or more of its physical properties (density, enthalpy, entropy, specific volume, refractive index, or rheological behaviour) at a characteristic temperature, pressure, or composition.

In food systems, the principal phase transitions of engineering importance are:

- **Glass Transition (Rubber ↔ Glass transition)**: The second-order, non-equilibrium transition of an amorphous (non-crystalline) food matrix from a hard, brittle, glassy state to a soft, rubbery, viscoelastic state upon heating through the **glass transition temperature (T_g)**.
- **Melting (Crystalline Solid → Liquid)**: The first-order transition where a crystalline component (e.g., lipid triacylglycerols, sucrose, ice) absorbs its **latent heat of fusion (ΔH_f)** and loses long-range molecular order.
- **Crystallization / Devitrification (Amorphous → Crystalline)**: Nucleation and ordered growth of crystals within an amorphous food matrix.
- **Freezing (Liquid Water → Ice)**: The liquid–solid transition of water, which dominates the **freezing curve** of all high-moisture foods.
- **Sublimation (Ice → Water Vapour)**: Direct solid–vapour transition, the principle behind **freeze-drying (lyophilization)**.
- **Evaporation / Boiling (Liquid → Vapour)**: Liquid–vapour phase change, the basis of concentration and dehydration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must clearly distinguish between a **first-order transition** (involving a latent heat and a discontinuity in volume, e.g., melting) and a **second-order transition** (no latent heat but a discontinuity in the *derivatives* of free energy, e.g., glass transition). The glass transition is widely classified as a kinetic, pseudo-second-order event in food polymers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **ladder made of jelly**. When the jelly is ice-cold (say, in your freezer at −20 °C), the ladder is rock-hard — it would shatter if you dropped it. This is the **glassy state**, where molecules are essentially "frozen" in place with no mobility. Now slowly warm the ladder. As you cross a critical temperature (around −10 °C for many foods), the ladder suddenly becomes flexible and rubbery — you can bend it without breaking it. It has now entered the **rubbery state**. This dramatic softening is the **glass transition**. Push it further with heat, and the molecules gain enough energy to slide past each other and flow — a **melting** or **crystallization** event.

A real-world culinary example: **Honey**. Fresh honey is a viscous, supercooled liquid (rubbery/viscous state). If you crystallize it, it becomes a soft solid (sugar crystals in a saturated syrup). Old, low-moisture honey can even enter a glassy state — that's why it pours slowly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why should a Food Engineer care about phase transitions?**
> 1. They dictate **shelf life** (caking, stickiness, collapse, recrystallization in powders).
> 2. They govern **texture** (chocolate bloom, ice cream coarsening, starch retrogradation).
> 3. They determine the **optimal processing window** (freeze-drying, extrusion, baking, freezing storage).
> 4. They control **microbial stability** through the role of water activity (a_w) and T_g.

## 1.3 The Six Thermodynamic States of Water in Foods

A high-moisture food can exist in several "states" depending on temperature and pressure. Recognising these is essential for the **state diagram** (covered in SECTION 2):

| Notation | State | Description |
|----------|-------|-------------|
| W₁ | Liquid water (freezable) | Freezes upon cooling below 0 °C |
| W₂ | Unfreezable / bound water | Does not freeze at typical sub-zero temps |
| I | Ice (crystalline) | First-order solid phase of H₂O |
| V | Water vapour | Gaseous phase |
| S | Solid matrix (crystalline) | Fat, sugar crystals |
| G | Glassy matrix | Amorphous polymer, low mobility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used Throughout KTU 2024:** Water activity **a_w** is dimensionless and ranges from **0** (bone-dry) to **1.00** (pure water). The relationship **a_w ≈ p/p_0** is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour above the food to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Glass Transition — A Molecular Mobility Concept

When a liquid food is cooled rapidly, the molecules do not have time to rearrange into a crystalline lattice. Instead, viscosity rises by **~10³ per 10 °C drop** until the material effectively becomes a solid — a **glass**. The temperature at which this happens is the **glass transition temperature, T_g**.

Key points for the KTU examiner:
- T_g is **not a single sharp temperature**; it is operationally defined as the temperature at which viscosity η ≈ **10¹² Pa·s**.
- T_g depends on the **measurement technique** (DSC onset vs. midpoint, DMA tan δ peak, dielectric).
- For binary food polymer–water systems, T_g is strongly depressed by water — water acts as a **plasticizer**.
- The classical criterion for storage stability of dry/semi-dry foods is to keep **T_storage < T_g**.

## 2.2 First-Order vs. Second-Order Transitions

| Feature | First-Order (Melting, Freezing) | Second-Order (Glass Transition) |
|---------|--------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Latent heat (ΔH) | **Non-zero** (J/g) | Effectively zero |
| Discontinuity in V, S | **Yes** (volume jump) | No jump, but a change in slope |
| Discontinuity in C_p, α | No | **Yes** (heat capacity jump ΔC_p) |
| Reversibility on cooling | Yes (equilibrium) | Kinetic — depends on cooling rate |
| KTU example | Melting of cocoa butter | Glass transition of amorphous lactose |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic — Why T_g Drops with Water

1. Small water molecules insert themselves between polymer chains.
2. They break inter-chain **hydrogen bonds**, increasing free volume.
3. Chain segmental mobility rises.
4. Less thermal energy is now needed to reach the "rubbery" regime.
5. Therefore, T_g decreases monotonically with moisture content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Gordon-Taylor and Fox equations (Section 3) are used to predict T_g of formulated foods from the T_g and mass fraction of each component. This is critical when designing **intermediate-moisture foods (IMF)** and **freeze-dried products**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Name / Description | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|------------------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $T_{g,mix} = w_1 T_{g1} + w_2 T_{g2} + \ldots$ | Simple mass-weighted (ideal) mixing | First estimate of T_g of blend |
| 2 | $T_g = \dfrac{w_s T_{gs} + k \, w_w T_{gw}}{w_s + k \, w_w}$ | **Gordon–Taylor equation** (with k) | Predicting T_g of biopolymer–water system |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | **Fox equation** | Predicting T_g of copolymer/amorphous blends |
| 4 | $\ln a_w = -\,\dfrac{\Delta H_{m}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^0}\right)$ | **Freezing-point depression** (Clausius form) | Relates a_w to T_m in frozen foods |
| 5 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression (cryoscopic) | Molal freezing-point constant K_f |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d \ln a_w}{d(1/T)} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{st}}{R}$ | **Clausius–Clapeyron** for sorption | Sorption isotherm temperature dependence |
| 7 | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | **WLF equation** | Time–temperature superposition; C₁ ≈ 17.4, C₂ ≈ 51.6 K |
| 8 | $T_m = T_m^0 - \dfrac{R\, (T_m^0)^2}{\Delta H_f}\,x_{solute}$ | Melting-point depression of a frozen solvent | Frozen food matrix with dissolved solutes |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P}{P_0} = a_w = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_m}{RT}\,(P - P_{sat})\right)$ | Kelvin equation (capillary) | Pore-water activity in porous foods |
| 10 | $a_w + x_b = 1$ (approx., for low-MW sugars) | Rule-of-thumb in IMF design | Designing moisture migration barriers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - Gas constant **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - Latent heat of fusion of pure water **ΔH_fus(ice) = 333.5 kJ/kg = 6.01 kJ/mol**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water **ΔH_vap ≈ 2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C
> - Cryoscopic constant of water **K_f = 1.86 K·kg/mol**
> - T_g of anhydrous starch ≈ **230 °C**; T_g of water ≈ **−135 °C**

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Freeze-drying**: T_g of the frozen concentrate must be above the drying-surface temperature to avoid "collapse" (Roos & Karel, 1991 — generic principle, not citation). Products with high sugar content (fruits) collapse easily because of low T_g.
- **Powdered milk & instant coffee**: Stickiness occurs when **T_surface > T_g** during spray drying; caking occurs in storage if **T_storage > T_g**.
- **Chocolate**: Polymorphic transitions of cocoa butter (Form V → Form VI) cause **fat bloom** — a textbook KTU example of solid–solid first-order transition.
- **Frozen food quality**: Ice crystal size depends on **freezing rate** and **T_g'** (the glass transition of the maximally freeze-concentrated matrix, ≈ −32 to −35 °C for most foods).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Gordon–Taylor Equation (Binary Polymer–Water System)

The Gordon–Taylor equation is a workhorse in food T_g modelling. Here is the formal derivation from thermodynamic and free-volume arguments.

**Starting premise (free-volume theory):**
The specific volume of a liquid *V_L* and its glass *V_G* extrapolated to the same temperature differ by the **excess free volume**, $\Delta V_f$. At the glass transition, the free volume fraction $f = \Delta V_f / V_L$ reaches a critical value $f_g$ (≈ 0.025) that is **assumed universal** for all amorphous materials (a key tenet of free-volume theory, as per the Simha–Boyer rule).

**Step 1 — Definition of the free-volume fraction:**

$$f_g = \Delta \alpha \, (T_g - T_2) \approx \text{constant for all polymers}$$

where $\Delta \alpha$ is the difference in thermal expansion coefficients between the rubbery (supercooled-liquid) state and the glassy state, and $T_2$ is a reference temperature.

**Step 2 — For component *i* with mass fraction $w_i$ and density $\rho_i$:**

$$\Delta \alpha_i (T_{gi} - T_2) = \Delta \alpha_j (T_{gj} - T_2) = \text{constant} = f_g$$

**Step 3 — Additive mixing rule for the blend (ideal volume additivity):**

$$\Delta \alpha_{mix} = w_1 \Delta \alpha_1 + w_2 \Delta \alpha_2$$

$$T_{g,mix} - T_2 = \dfrac{w_1 \Delta \alpha_1 (T_{g1} - T_2) + w_2 \Delta \alpha_2 (T_{g2} - T_2)}{w_1 \Delta \alpha_1 + w_2 \Delta \alpha_2}$$

**Step 4 — Since each $(T_{gi} - T_2)$ equals the same constant $C = f_g / \Delta \alpha_i$ for all *i*:**

$$T_{g,mix} - T_2 = C = f_g \cdot \dfrac{1}{w_1 \Delta \alpha_1 + w_2 \Delta \alpha_2} \cdot (w_1 + w_2)$$

With $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, the above simplifies to:

$$T_{g,mix} = T_2 + \dfrac{f_g}{w_1 \Delta \alpha_1 + w_2 \Delta \alpha_2}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminating the unmeasurable $T_2$ and the $f_g/\Delta \alpha$ products, and introducing an empirical interaction constant $k$ (a fitting parameter; $k = \Delta \alpha_2 / \Delta \alpha_1$ for component 1 as the polymer):**

$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k \, w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k \, w_2}$$

This is the **Gordon–Taylor equation**, with $k$ typically ranging from ~0.1 (high-MW polymer) to ~5 (sugar-rich systems) in foods.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A spray-dried fruit juice powder contains **fructose (w₁ = 0.30, T_g1 = 100 °C = 373 K)** and **water (w₂ = 0.04, T_g2 = −135 °C = 138 K)**. Estimate the glass transition temperature of the powder using the Gordon–Taylor equation with **k = 2.5**. Predict whether the powder will be glassy at a storage temperature of **25 °C = 298 K**.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Identify all variables:**

$$w_1 = 0.30, \quad T_{g1} = 373\ \text{K}, \quad w_2 = 0.04, \quad T_{g2} = 138\ \text{K}, \quad k = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Gordon–Taylor formula:**

$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k\,w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\,w_2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$w_1 T_{g1} = 0.30 \times 373 = 111.9\ \text{K}$$

$$k\,w_2 T_{g2} = 2.5 \times 0.04 \times 138 = 0.10 \times 138 = 13.8\ \text{K}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 111.9 + 13.8 = 125.7\ \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$w_1 + k\,w_2 = 0.30 + 2.5 \times 0.04 = 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.40$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$T_g = \dfrac{125.7}{0.40} = 314.25\ \text{K} \;\approx\; 41.1\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 6 — Storage stability verdict:**

$$T_{\text{storage}} = 298\ \text{K} = 25\ ^{\circ}\text{C} \;<\; T_g = 314.25\ \text{K} = 41.1\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Since **T_storage < T_g**, the powder is in the **glassy state** at room temperature and is **predicted to be shelf-stable** (no caking, no stickiness).

> [!NOTE]
> If the moisture content were to rise to w₂ = 0.10 (10 % w/w water), the calculation would yield a lower T_g, possibly below 25 °C, leading to stickiness. This is why **moisture-barrier packaging** is critical for hygroscopic powders.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Transition Models

| Model | Form | Strength | Weakness |
|-------|------|----------|----------|
| Fox | $1/T_g$ additive | Simple, works for random copolymers | Ignores interaction energy |
| Gordon–Taylor | Linear in $T_g$ with $k$ | Captures plasticizer effect; one-fit parameter $k$ | Assumes ideal volume mixing |
| Couchman–Karasz | $T_g \Delta C_p$ additive | Thermodynamically consistent | Requires $\Delta C_p$ data |
| Kwei | Adds $q\,w_1 w_2$ term | Handles strong H-bonding | Two fitting parameters |

> **Choosing the right model (KTU perspective):** For a sugar–water system, **Gordon–Taylor** is the KTU-blessed default; for protein–water systems, **Couchman–Karasz** gives a thermodynamically rigorous estimate because $\Delta C_p$ of proteins is well characterised.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic — Hierarchy of Phase Transitions in Foods

The block diagram below maps the principal phase transitions encountered in food matrices, grouped by the phase involved.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Food Matrix]:::root --> A1[Solid Phases]
    A0 --> A2[Liquid Phases]
    A0 --> A3[Gas Phases]

    A1 --> B1[Crystalline Solid]
    A1 --> B2[Amorphous Solid]

    B1 --> C1[Melting T_m]
    B1 --> C2[Polymorphic Transition]
    B1 --> C3[Sublimation]

    B2 --> C4[Glass Transition T_g]
    B2 --> C5[Devitrification Crystallization]
    B2 --> C6[Sticking or Caking above T_g]

    A2 --> D1[Liquid Water]
    A2 --> D2[Freezable Water]

    D1 --> E1[Evaporation Boiling]
    D1 --> E2[Supercooling]

    D2 --> F1[Ice Formation Freezing]
    D2 --> F2[Glass Transition of Freeze Concentrate T_g Prime]

    A3 --> G1[Water Vapour]
    G1 --> H1[Condensation]
    G1 --> H2[Desorption from Food]

    C1 --> I1[Liquid Melt]
    C5 --> I1
    E2 --> I1

    I1 --> J1[Rubbery Viscoelastic State]
    J1 --> K1[Above T_g Mobility High]
    J1 --> K2[Below T_g Mobility Low Glass]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f;
    classDef root2 fill:#4a7c59,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a7c59;
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — State Diagram of a Food (Concept Map)

The state diagram is a graph of temperature versus composition (e.g., water mass fraction) that identifies the equilibrium phases and the glass-transition curve. Below is the block-level functional map showing how a state diagram is built.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Axes: Temperature T vs Solute Mass Fraction x] --> S2[Equilibrium Phase Boundary Tm vs x]
    S1 --> S3[Glass Transition Curve Tg vs x]
    S1 --> S4[Ice Liquidus Line]
    S1 --> S5[Tg Prime Intersection at x_g Prime]

    S2 --> R1[Region I Crystalline Solute plus Ice]
    S3 --> R2[Region II Rubbery Amorphous]
    S4 --> R3[Region III Glassy Amorphous]
    S5 --> R4[Region IV Freeze Concentrated Glass plus Ice]

    R1 --> Z[State Diagram Output]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z

    Z --> APP1[App: Freeze Drying Collapse Window]
    Z --> APP2[App: Storage Stability Limit]
    Z --> APP3[App: Crystallization Onset Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Determining T_g by Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Sample Sealed in Aluminium Pan] --> P1[Equilibrate at 20C above T_g]
    P1 --> P2[Cool at 10 C per min to 50C below expected T_g]
    P2 --> P3[Hold 5 min for isothermal relaxation]
    P3 --> P4[Heat at 10 C per min to record Heat Flow]
    P4 --> P5{Identify Step Change in Cp}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Read Onset as T_g]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Repeat with Different Cooling Rate]
    P6 --> P8[Report T_g Onset Midpoint and Endset]
    P7 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three reported T_g values (onset, midpoint, endset) differ slightly; the KTU 2024 examiner typically accepts the **midpoint** as the reference, with the **onset** used for shelf-life predictions (more conservative).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define glass transition temperature (T_g) in the context of food polymers. State two factors that lower the T_g of a food system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The glass transition temperature (T_g) is the temperature at which an amorphous food polymer changes from a hard, brittle, **glassy state** to a soft, rubbery, viscoelastic state. Operationally, it is the temperature at which the viscosity of the supercooled liquid reaches **10¹² Pa·s**. It is a kinetic, pseudo-second-order transition observed in non-crystalline (amorphous) components of food.
- **Two factors that lower T_g (1 mark, 0.5 each):**
  1. **Increase in moisture content (water acts as a plasticizer):** water molecules insert between polymer chains, increasing free volume and reducing T_g.
  2. **Increase in low-molecular-weight plasticizers** such as sugars, glycerol, or polyols.
  *(Other acceptable: increased plasticizer concentration, lower plasticizer molecular weight, presence of fats/oils, and higher thermal history of the sample.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a first-order and a second-order phase transition with one food example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | First-Order | Second-Order |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Latent heat | Absorbed / released | Effectively zero |
| Discontinuity in | Volume, entropy, enthalpy | Heat capacity $C_p$, expansion coefficient $\alpha$ |
| Example in food | Melting of ice / cocoa butter | Glass transition of amorphous lactose |

- **First-order example (1 mark):** Melting of ice at 0 °C, or melting of cocoa butter (Forms I–V → Form VI at ~36 °C).
- **Second-order example (1 mark):** Glass transition of amorphous sucrose, starch, or freeze-concentrated fruit juice.
- **Differentiating definition (1 mark):** A first-order transition shows a discontinuity in the first derivative of Gibbs free energy; a second-order transition shows a discontinuity only in the *second* derivative.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module Internal Choice)

> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Module 2 (full-module question) carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two parallel questions. Both versions are solved below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gordon–Taylor equation** for predicting the glass transition temperature of a binary amorphous food system. Clearly state the meaning of each variable and discuss how the value of the interaction constant **k** reflects the plasticizing efficiency of water. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dried fruit preparation contains **sucrose (T_g = 60 °C, w₁ = 0.65)** and **water (T_g = −135 °C, w₂ = 0.05)**, with the remainder being insoluble fibre. Using the **Gordon–Taylor equation with k = 3.0**, estimate T_g. Will the product be glassy at a storage temperature of 30 °C? Comment on its likely shelf stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Statement of the equation (2 marks):**
$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k\,w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\,w_2}$$
where $w_1$ = mass fraction of polymer (component 1), $w_2$ = mass fraction of water/plasticizer (component 2), $T_{g1}, T_{g2}$ = respective glass transition temperatures (K), and $k$ = empirical interaction constant.

2. **Origin and assumptions (2 marks):** Derived from free-volume theory. Assumes: (i) the critical free-volume fraction $f_g$ at $T_g$ is a universal constant (≈ 0.025) for all amorphous materials, (ii) volumes of mixing are additive, and (iii) thermal expansion coefficients differ between glassy and rubbery states.

3. **Meaning of k (2 marks):** $k = \Delta \alpha_2 / \Delta \alpha_1$ is the ratio of thermal-expansion differences. A **higher k** indicates a **stronger plasticizing effect of component 2 on component 1**. For sugar–water systems, k ≈ 4–7; for starch–water, k ≈ 0.1–0.3.

4. **Engineering significance (1 mark):** Gordon–Taylor enables prediction of $T_g$ as a function of moisture content, which is essential for designing **intermediate-moisture foods (IMF)** and predicting stickiness/caking during spray drying.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Convert all temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark):**
$$T_{g1} = 60 + 273 = 333\ \text{K}, \quad T_{g2} = -135 + 273 = 138\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Gordon–Taylor (1 mark):**
$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k\,w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\,w_2} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 333 + 3.0 \times 0.05 \times 138}{0.65 + 3.0 \times 0.05}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator (1 mark):**
$$0.65 \times 333 = 216.45\ \text{K}$$
$$3.0 \times 0.05 \times 138 = 0.15 \times 138 = 20.70\ \text{K}$$
$$\text{Numerator} = 216.45 + 20.70 = 237.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator (1 mark):**
$$0.65 + 0.15 = 0.80$$

**Step 5 — Compute T_g (1 mark):**
$$T_g = \dfrac{237.15}{0.80} = 296.44\ \text{K} \approx 23.3\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with storage temperature (1 mark):**
$$T_{\text{storage}} = 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 303\ \text{K} > T_g = 296.44\ \text{K}$$
The product is **above its T_g** at 30 °C storage.

**Step 7 — Shelf-stability verdict (1 mark):** Because $T_{\text{storage}} > T_g$, the amorphous sucrose phase is in the **rubbery state**, with high molecular mobility. The product is **likely to undergo caking, stickiness, and possibly sucrose crystallization** during storage. The shelf life is poor; either reduce moisture content or store below 23 °C.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is **water activity (a_w)**? Sketch a **typical sorption isotherm for a high-moisture food** at 25 °C and label the three regions (types of water) along with their approximate a_w ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During freeze-drying of a strawberry pulp, the **freezing point of the pulp is depressed to −2.5 °C**. Assuming ideal-dilute behaviour, calculate the **molal concentration** of dissolved solutes in the unfrozen liquid using $K_f = 1.86\ \text{K·kg/mol}$. If the same pulp were dried to a moisture content of 15 % (w/w) and stored at 25 °C, will the product remain glassy? Use the **Fox equation** with $T_{g,\text{solids}} = 70\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $T_{g,\text{water}} = -135\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$. Comment briefly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition of water activity (2 marks):** Water activity $a_w$ is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour above the food to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature:
$$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} \quad (0 \le a_w \le 1)$$
It is a measure of the **available (unbound) water** in a food and is the master variable controlling microbial growth, lipid oxidation, and non-enzymatic browning.

2. **Sketch description (3 marks):**
   - **Region I (0 – ~0.25 a_w):** *Monolayer water* — tightly bound, unfreezable, held to polar sites of the matrix.
   - **Region II (~0.25 – ~0.75 a_w):** *Multilayer water* — progressively more mobile, governs most deterioration reactions.
   - **Region III (~0.75 – 1.0 a_w):** *Free (capillary) water* — behaves like bulk water, supports microbial growth.

3. **Engineering significance (2 marks):** A typical isotherm is sigmoidal (Type II). It is essential for predicting **moisture migration in multi-component foods** (e.g., biscuits with wet filling) and for selecting the right packaging humidity.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Part 1 — Molal solute concentration (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the cryoscopic equation:
$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$
where $\Delta T_f = 2.5\ \text{K}$ (freezing-point depression from 0 °C to −2.5 °C).

**Step 2:** Solve for $m$:
$$m = \dfrac{\Delta T_f}{K_f} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.86} = 1.344\ \text{mol/kg}$$

**Step 3:** State the assumption: **ideal-dilute solution**, $K_f$ is constant, complete ionisation not accounted for (would double if NaCl with van 't Hoff factor $i = 2$).

---

**Part 2 — Glass transition prediction by Fox equation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Mass fractions of solids and water in the 15 %-moisture product:
$$w_w = 0.15, \quad w_s = 0.85$$

**Step 2:** Convert to Kelvin:
$$T_{gs} = 70 + 273 = 343\ \text{K}, \quad T_{gw} = -135 + 273 = 138\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Fox equation**:
$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_w}{T_{gw}} + \dfrac{w_s}{T_{gs}}$$
$$\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{0.15}{138} + \dfrac{0.85}{343} = 0.001087 + 0.002478 = 0.003565\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for T_g:
$$T_g = \dfrac{1}{0.003565} = 280.5\ \text{K} = 7.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 5 — Verdict:** Storage at **25 °C = 298 K > T_g = 280.5 K**, so the product is **above T_g** → in the **rubbery state**. The dried strawberry pulp will likely **cake, stick, and lose crispness** at 25 °C. Recommended action: dry further to ≤ 10 % moisture, or store refrigerated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** before applying the Fox or Gordon–Taylor equations. → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing mass and mole fractions** in the Fox equation. Fox uses *mass* fractions; Couchman–Karasz uses *mole* fractions. → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Using T_g of water as 0 °C** instead of −135 °C (the textbook glass-transition temperature, not the melting point). → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Reporting T_g as the comparison temperature** without explicit comment on the *glassy vs. rubbery* state → loses the shelf-life application mark.
> 5. **Not specifying which value of k** was used in Gordon–Taylor → vague answers lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring units** in the freezing-point calculation (mol/kg must be stated explicitly).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Phase transition = change of physical state** with a discontinuity in a thermodynamic property.
- [x] **First-order transitions** (melting, freezing, sublimation, condensation) have a **latent heat ΔH ≠ 0**.
- [x] **Second-order (glass) transitions** show a jump in **heat capacity ΔC_p**, no latent heat.
- [x] **Glass transition temperature T_g** is the hallmark of *amorphous* food polymers; above T_g, molecular mobility is high and shelf life plummets.
- [x] **Water is a powerful plasticizer**: it lowers T_g. The greater the moisture, the lower the T_g.
- [x] **Gordon–Taylor** (with parameter $k$) is the KTU-preferred model for **sugar–water and biopolymer–water** systems.
- [x] **Fox equation** is the simplest 1/T_g additive model — useful for *random copolymer-like* mixtures.
- [x] **WLF equation** relates shift factors to T_g for time–temperature superposition of viscoelastic data.
- [x] **Freezing-point depression** of the food matrix depends on solute concentration; the glass transition of the *maximally freeze-concentrated* phase is **T_g′ ≈ −32 to −35 °C** for most foods.
- [x] **Storage rule of thumb**: keep **T_storage < T_g** to maximise shelf stability of dried/semi-dry foods.
- [x] **Water activity a_w** is the master parameter for microbial stability: most bacteria need $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $> 0.87$, xerophilic moulds $> 0.65$.
- [x] **Engineering applications to recall**: spray-drying collapse, freeze-drying collapse, chocolate fat bloom, ice-cream coarsening, sugar crystallization in confectionery, caking of instant powders, recrystallization of frozen foods, starch retrogradation in bread staling.
- [x] **Always convert °C → K** when applying thermodynamic equations. **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**. **K_f (water) = 1.86 K·kg/mol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
