> **Final Exam Mantra:** When asked to *predict shelf stability*, always close with the comparative statement **"Since $T_{\text{storage}}$ is [greater/less] than $T_g$, the product is in the [rubbery/glassy] state, [un]likely to undergo caking/stickiness."** This single sentence usually secures the final application mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Viscosity Measurement and Viscometers: Rotational Viscometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Different Viscosity Measurement and Viscometers: Rotational Viscometers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Rotational Viscometer** is a precision rheological instrument that measures the viscosity and flow behavior of a fluid by subjecting it to a precisely controlled rotational shear field. The instrument consists of a rotating element (spindle, bob, cone, or plate) immersed in or in contact with the test fluid, while a stationary element (cup, plate, or lower fixture) holds the sample. The fundamental measurement principle relies on correlating either the **torque required to maintain a constant rotational speed** or the **rotational speed achieved under a constant torque** with the fluid's resistance to flow (viscosity). Mathematically, the operational definition of viscosity in a rotational viscometer is expressed through Newton's law of viscosity applied to a defined shear geometry: $$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ Where $\eta$ is the **dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)**, $\tau$ is the **shear stress (Pa)**, and $\dot{\gamma}$ is the **shear rate (s⁻¹)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Rotational viscometers are classified under the broader category of **absolute viscometers** because the shear rate field is mathematically well-defined by the geometry, allowing direct computation of viscosity without empirical calibration constants (unlike capillary viscometers which require calibration with standards). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — The Honey and Spinning Spoon Test:** Imagine dipping a spoon into a jar of honey and rotating the spoon. If you spin it slowly, the honey barely resists; if you spin it rapidly, the honey resists strongly, and you can *feel* the viscous drag. A rotational viscometer works on exactly this principle, but with **extreme precision**: - The "spoon" is a precisely machined geometry (bob, cone, or plate). - The "rotation speed" is digitally controlled in revolutions per minute (RPM). - The "resistance you feel" is quantified as **torque (N·m)** by a highly sensitive force sensor. - The geometry's mathematical simplicity allows conversion of torque → shear stress and RPM → shear rate. **Why use rotation instead of simple flow through a tube?** Because rotational geometries offer **well-defined shear rate fields**, easier temperature control, the ability to perform continuous shear rate sweeps (for non-Newtonian fluids), and small sample volumes — critical for food systems like sauces, dairy, and gels. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units in Rotational Viscometry > [!NOTE] > **Critical Engineering Metrics to Remember:** > - **Viscosity ($\eta$):** Measured in **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **mPa·s (milliPascal-second)**. Note: 1 mPa·s = 1 cP (centipoise). > - **Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$):** Measured in **s⁻¹ (reciprocal seconds)**. > - **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Measured in **Pa (Pascal)**. > - **Torque ($M$):** Measured in **N·m (Newton-meter)**. > - **Angular Velocity ($\Omega$):** Measured in **rad/s (radians per second)**. > - **Rotational Speed ($N$):** Measured in **RPM (revolutions per minute)**. Conversion: $\Omega = 2\pi N / 60$. ### 1.4 Classification of Rotational Viscometers Rotational viscometers are classified based on the **measuring geometry** used: | Geometry Type | Common Name | Typical Shear Rate Range | Best Suited For | |---------------|-------------|--------------------------|-----------------| | Concentric Cylinder | Searle / Couette | 0.1 – 10³ s⁻¹ | Low to medium viscosity liquids | | Cone-and-Plate | Cone-Plate | 0.01 – 10⁴ s⁻¹ | Homogeneous fluids, pastes | | Parallel Plate | Plate-Plate | 0.1 – 10⁴ s⁻¹ | Viscoelastic gels, structured foods | | Spindle Type | Brookfield | 1 – 200 s⁻¹ | Quick QC checks (industrial) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Governing Physics A rotational viscometer operates on the principle of **forced shear flow in a confined geometry**. The fluid is sheared between two surfaces where: - One surface moves with a tangential velocity $v$ (the rotor). - The other surface is stationary (the stator). - The gap between surfaces defines the shear zone. The operational workflow follows a strict logical sequence: 1. **Sample Loading:** The fluid is placed in the measuring geometry, ensuring no air entrapment (especially critical for foams and aerated foods). 2. **Geometric Definition:** The geometry (cone angle, gap height, cylinder radii) defines the kinematic shear field mathematically. 3. **Kinematic Input:** Either the **rotational speed ($N$ or $\Omega$)** or the **angular displacement** is imposed as the controlled variable. 4. **Dynamic Response Measurement:** The resisting **torque ($M$)** is measured via a torsion spring, electromagnetic force restoration, or air-bearing sensor. 5. **Rheological Conversion:** The measured torque is converted to **shear stress** using a **geometry-specific stress factor ($C_\tau$)**, and the rotational speed is converted to **shear rate** using a **shear rate factor ($C_{\dot{\gamma}}$)**. 6. **Viscosity Computation:** The apparent viscosity is computed as: $$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Rotational Viscometer Use - **Well-defined kinematics:** Unlike capillary flow, the shear rate can be calculated *analytically* from the geometry. - **Continuous shear rate variation:** Modern instruments allow programmable shear rate ramps, enabling the study of **time-dependent fluids** (thixotropy, rheopexy). - **Small sample volumes:** Especially for cone-plate geometries (typically 0.5 – 5 mL). - **Direct measurement of normal stress differences:** Critical for viscoelastic food systems like dough and molten chocolate. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMINATIONS** | # | Geometry | Shear Rate Formula | Shear Stress Formula | Viscosity Formula | |---|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Concentric Cylinder (Searle)** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{2\Omega R_i^2 R_o^2}{r^2(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ (at radius $r$)
Apparent: $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dfrac{\Omega R_i}{\delta}$ (for narrow gap) | $\tau = \dfrac{M}{2\pi L R_i^2}$ | $\eta = \dfrac{M \,\delta}{2\pi L R_i^3 \,\Omega}$ | | 2 | **Cone-and-Plate** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Omega}{\tan\alpha} \approx \dfrac{\Omega}{\alpha}$ (for small $\alpha$) | $\tau = \dfrac{3M}{2\pi R^3}$ | $\eta = \dfrac{3M \alpha}{2\pi R^3 \Omega}$ | | 3 | **Parallel Plate** | $\dot{\gamma}_R = \dfrac{\Omega R}{h}$ (at edge $R$) | $\tau_R = \dfrac{3M}{2\pi R^3}$ (at edge) | $\eta = \dfrac{3 M h}{2 \pi R^4 \Omega}$ | | 4 | **Brookfield Spindle** | $\dot{\gamma} \propto N$ (geometry-dependent empirical) | $\tau \propto M$ | $\eta = \dfrac{k \cdot M}{N}$ (instrument constant $k$) | **Where:** - $R_i$ = inner cylinder (bob) radius - $R_o$ = outer cylinder (cup) radius - $\delta = R_o - R_i$ = gap width - $L$ = effective bob length - $\alpha$ = cone angle (radians) - $R$ = cone/plate radius - $h$ = gap height in parallel plate - $M$ = measured torque - $\Omega$ = angular velocity - $N$ = rotational speed (RPM) **Standard Conversion Constants:** - $1 \,\text{cP} = 1 \,\text{mPa·s} = 10^{-3} \,\text{Pa·s}$ - $1 \,\text{Poise} = 0.1 \,\text{Pa·s}$ - Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = \eta / \rho$ (m²/s) ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In the **food industry**, rotational viscometers are indispensable for: - **Quality Control (QC):** Verifying batch-to-batch consistency of ketchup, yogurt, chocolate, sauces. - **Process Design:** Determining pumping energy requirements and pipe flow pressure drops using the **Metzner-Reed** correlation for non-Newtonian fluids. - **Product Development:** Formulating texturally acceptable products (e.g., pourable salad dressings with shear-thinning behavior). - **Sensory Correlation:** Linking instrumental viscosity measurements to perceived mouthfeel (e.g., viscosity of liquid foods at 50 s⁻¹ correlates with perceived thickness). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Viscosity Equation for Concentric Cylinder (Searle Type) **Problem Setup:** Consider a fluid confined in the annular gap between two concentric cylinders. The inner cylinder (bob) of radius $R_i$ rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$, while the outer cylinder (cup) of radius $R_o$ remains stationary. The bob has an effective immersed length $L$. **Assumptions:** - Steady-state, laminar, isothermal flow. - End effects are negligible (long bob assumption). - No slip at the cylinder walls. - Newtonian fluid behavior for the derivation (then extended to non-Newtonian via apparent viscosity). **Step 1 — Establish the velocity field:** For purely tangential flow, the velocity has only an azimuthal component $v_\theta(r)$ that depends only on radius $r$. Since the inner cylinder is moving and the outer is stationary, the boundary conditions are: $$v_\theta(R_i) = \Omega R_i \quad \text{and} \quad v_\theta(R_o) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates:** For purely tangential flow with no pressure gradient and negligible inertia, the simplified equation reduces to: $$\frac{d}{dr}\left( \frac{1}{r} \frac{d(r v_\theta)}{dr} \right) = 0$$ **Step 3 — Integrate twice:** First integration gives: $$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d(r v_\theta)}{dr} = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d(r v_\theta)}{dr} = C_1 r$$ Second integration: $$r v_\theta = \frac{C_1 r^2}{2} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_\theta = \frac{C_1 r}{2} + \frac{C_2}{r}$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions to find $C_1$ and $C_2$:** Applying $v_\theta(R_i) = \Omega R_i$: $$\Omega R_i = \frac{C_1 R_i}{2} + \frac{C_2}{R_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Omega R_i^2 = \frac{C_1 R_i^2}{2} + C_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ Applying $v_\theta(R_o) = 0$: $$0 = \frac{C_1 R_o}{2} + \frac{C_2}{R_o} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{C_1 R_o^2}{2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute (ii) into (i) to solve for $C_1$:** $$\Omega R_i^2 = \frac{C_1 R_i^2}{2} - \frac{C_1 R_o^2}{2} = \frac{C_1}{2}(R_i^2 - R_o^2)$$ $$C_1 = \frac{2\Omega R_i^2}{R_i^2 - R_o^2} = -\frac{2\Omega R_i^2}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the shear rate:** The shear rate in cylindrical coordinates is: $$\dot{\gamma} = r \frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{v_\theta}{r}\right) = r \frac{d}{dr}\left(\frac{C_1}{2} + \frac{C_2}{r^2}\right) = -\frac{2C_2}{r^2}$$ Substituting $C_2 = -C_1 R_o^2 / 2$: $$\dot{\gamma}(r) = -\frac{2(-C_1 R_o^2/2)}{r^2} = \frac{C_1 R_o^2}{r^2} = \frac{2\Omega R_i^2 R_o^2}{r^2(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$ This is the **exact shear rate at radius $r$** in the gap. **Step 7 — Narrow-gap approximation:** If $\delta = R_o - R_i \ll R_i$, then $R_i R_o \approx R_i^2$ and $R_o^2 - R_i^2 \approx 2 R_i \delta$. Substituting: $$\dot{\gamma} \approx \frac{2 \Omega R_i^2 (R_i + \delta)^2}{r^2 \cdot 2 R_i \delta} \approx \frac{\Omega R_i}{\delta}$$ This **uniform shear rate** approximation is used in most practical viscometer calibrations. **Step 8 — Torque balance and shear stress:** The torque on the inner cylinder is the integral of shear stress over its lateral surface: $$M = \tau \cdot 2\pi R_i L \cdot R_i = 2\pi R_i^2 L \, \tau$$ Therefore: $$\tau = \frac{M}{2\pi R_i^2 L}$$ **Step 9 — Combine for viscosity:** $$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{M/(2\pi R_i^2 L)}{\Omega R_i / \delta} = \frac{M \, \delta}{2 \pi L R_i^3 \, \Omega}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{M \, (R_o - R_i)}{2 \pi L R_i^3 \, \Omega}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Viscosity Equation for Cone-and-Plate Geometry **Setup:** A shallow cone of angle $\alpha$ (typically 0.5° to 4°) rotates against a stationary flat plate. The fluid fills the gap between them. **Step 1 — Geometry of shear field:** At any radial distance $r$ from the cone's axis, the gap height is: $$h(r) = r \tan \alpha \approx r\alpha \quad \text{(for small } \alpha \text{)}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity at radius $r$:** The cone surface at radius $r$ moves with tangential velocity $v_\theta = \Omega r$. The plate is stationary. The linear velocity gradient across the gap is: $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\Omega r}{h(r)} = \frac{\Omega r}{r \tan \alpha} = \frac{\Omega}{\tan \alpha} \approx \frac{\Omega}{\alpha}$$ **Critical observation:** The shear rate is **independent of radius** — a unique advantage of cone-plate geometry that makes it ideal for non-Newtonian fluids. **Step 3 — Torque integral:** The shear stress on a ring element of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ at radius $r$ contributes torque $dM = \tau \cdot r \cdot dA = 2\pi r^2 \tau \, dr$: $$M = \int_0^R 2\pi r^2 \tau \, dr = \frac{2\pi R^3 \tau}{3}$$ Therefore: $$\tau = \frac{3M}{2\pi R^3}$$ **Step 4 — Final viscosity equation:** $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{3M \alpha}{2\pi R^3 \Omega}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A concentric cylinder viscometer has an inner bob radius $R_i = 20$ mm and an outer cup radius $R_o = 22$ mm. The effective immersed length of the bob is $L = 60$ mm. When rotated at $N = 60$ RPM, the measured torque is $M = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m. Calculate the viscosity of the fluid. **Step 1 — Convert RPM to rad/s:** $$\Omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \,\text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the gap:** $$\delta = R_o - R_i = 22 - 20 = 2 \,\text{mm} = 0.002 \,\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the narrow-gap viscosity equation:** $$\eta = \frac{M \, \delta}{2 \pi L R_i^3 \, \Omega}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:** $$\eta = \frac{(4.5 \times 10^{-4})(0.002)}{2\pi (0.060)(0.020)^3 (6.283)}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:** $$\text{Numerator} = (4.5 \times 10^{-4})(0.002) = 9.0 \times 10^{-7}$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate denominator:** $$R_i^3 = (0.020)^3 = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3$$ $$2\pi L R_i^3 \Omega = 2\pi (0.060)(8.0 \times 10^{-6})(6.283)$$ $$= 2\pi \times 3.016 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.895 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 7 — Final computation:** $$\eta = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.895 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.0475 \,\text{Pa·s}$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 47.5 \,\text{mPa·s}}$$ This is consistent with the viscosity of a light oil or a thin sauce — a typical food rheology range. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Rotational Viscometer System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["Input Module"] A1["User Interface
Display Panel"] A2["Speed Controller
RPM Setpoint"] A3["Temperature
Control Unit"] end subgraph Drive["Drive System"] B1["Servo Motor
DC/Stepper"] B2["Gear Reduction
Coupling"] B3["Encoder
Angular Position Sensor"] end subgraph Geometry["Measuring Geometry"] C1["Geometry Selector
Bob / Cone / Plate / Spindle"] C2["Rotating Element
Rotor"] C3["Stationary Element
Stator / Cup"] C4["Sample Fluid
In the Gap"] end subgraph Sensor["Measurement Module"] D1["Torque Sensor
Air Bearing / Torsion"] D2["Signal Amplifier
and ADC"] D3["Microprocessor
Rheology Engine"] end subgraph Output["Output Module"] E1["Viscosity
Calculator"] E2["Flow Curve
Display"] E3["Data Logger
USB / RS-232"] end A2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> C2 A3 --> C4 C4 --> C2 C2 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 B3 --> D3 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 D3 --> E1 D3 --> E2 D3 --> E3 A1 --> A2 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Rotational Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR Start([Sample Type]) --> Q1{Are there
suspended
particles?} Q1 -->|Yes, large
particles| Conc[Concentric
Cylinder
Wide Gap] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is sample
low volume
less than 2 mL?} Q2 -->|Yes| ConePlate[Cone and Plate
Uniform Shear] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need for
normal stress
measurement?} Q3 -->|Yes| Conc --> ConePlate Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it a
viscoelastic
gel?} Q4 -->|Yes| ParPlate[Parallel Plate
Variable Gap] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Quick QC
check?} Q5 -->|Yes| Brook[Brookfield
Spindle] Q5 -->|No| ConePlate ``` ### 4.3 Shear Rate Distribution in Different Geometries ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CC["Concentric Cylinder"] CC1["Shear rate varies with r:
gamma = 2 Omega Ri^2 Ro^2 / r^2 Ro^2 - Ri^2"] CC2["Higher shear at inner cylinder"] end subgraph CP["Cone and Plate"] CP1["Shear rate UNIFORM throughout:
gamma = Omega / alpha"] CP2["Ideal for non-Newtonian fluids"] end subgraph PP["Parallel Plate"] PP1["Shear rate varies linearly:
gamma_R = Omega R / h"] PP2["Maximum at the rim"] end CC --> CC1 --> CC2 CP --> CP1 --> CP2 PP --> PP1 --> PP2 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: Shear Stress and Shear Rate Derivation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Measured Torque M] --> B[Apply Stress Factor] B --> C[Shear Stress tau] D[Angular Velocity Omega] --> E[Apply Shear Rate Factor] E --> F[Shear Rate gamma dot] C --> G[Viscosity Calculator] F --> G G --> H[Output: eta tau gamma dot] G --> I[Flow Curve Plot] G --> J[Viscosity vs Shear Rate] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define a rotational viscometer. List any two geometries used in rotational viscometers.* **Model Answer:** A rotational viscometer is a rheological instrument that determines fluid viscosity by measuring the torque required to rotate a defined geometry (bob, cone, or plate) at a controlled angular velocity within the fluid. Two common geometries are: 1. **Concentric cylinder (Searle type)** — a bob rotating inside a stationary cup. 2. **Cone-and-plate** — a shallow cone rotating against a flat stationary plate. **[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *State the working equation for viscosity in a cone-and-plate viscometer. Mention its advantage over concentric cylinder geometry.* **Model Answer:** The viscosity equation for cone-and-plate geometry is: $$\eta = \frac{3M\alpha}{2\pi R^3 \Omega}$$ where $M$ is torque, $\alpha$ is the cone angle (rad), $R$ is cone radius, and $\Omega$ is angular velocity. **Advantage:** The shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = \Omega/\alpha$ is **uniform throughout the gap**, making it ideal for characterizing non-Newtonian food fluids where shear rate dependence of viscosity is important. **[Marks Distribution: 2 + 1]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for viscosity of a Newtonian fluid measured using a **concentric cylinder (Searle type) rotational viscometer**, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A concentric cylinder viscometer has $R_i = 25$ mm, $R_o = 27$ mm, and bob length $L = 80$ mm. At a rotational speed of 100 RPM, the measured torque is $M = 8.2 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m. Determine: - (i) The angular velocity in rad/s. - (ii) The shear stress at the bob surface. - (iii) The viscosity of the fluid using the narrow-gap approximation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** — see Section 3.1 of these notes. Present the full 9-step derivation with the assumptions bullet list and final boxed equation. **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state, isothermal laminar flow. 2. Newtonian fluid behavior. 3. No-slip at walls. 4. End effects negligible (long bob). 5. Narrow gap approximation valid. **Final equation:** $$\eta = \frac{M(R_o - R_i)}{2\pi L R_i^3 \Omega}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Angular velocity:** $$\Omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 100}{60} = \frac{200\pi}{60} = \frac{10\pi}{3} \approx 10.472 \,\text{rad/s}$$ **(ii) Shear stress at bob surface:** $$\tau = \frac{M}{2\pi R_i^2 L} = \frac{8.2 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi \times (0.025)^2 \times 0.080}$$ $$= \frac{8.2 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.080} = \frac{8.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2.610 \,\text{Pa}$$ **(iii) Viscosity (narrow-gap):** $$\eta = \frac{M \cdot \delta}{2\pi L R_i^3 \Omega}$$ $\delta = 27 - 25 = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m $R_i^3 = (0.025)^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$ m³ $$\eta = \frac{(8.2 \times 10^{-4})(0.002)}{2\pi (0.080)(1.5625 \times 10^{-5})(10.472)}$$ $$= \frac{1.64 \times 10^{-6}}{8.224 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.01994 \,\text{Pa·s} \approx 19.94 \,\text{mPa·s}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Alternative]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **cone-and-plate rotational viscometer**. Derive the viscosity equation and show that the shear rate is uniform throughout the sample. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **concentric cylinder**, **cone-and-plate**, and **parallel plate** rotational viscometer geometries based on: - Shear rate uniformity - Sample volume required - Suitability for non-Newtonian fluids - Ease of cleaning and loading - Typical food applications **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Cone-and-Plate Viscometer:** **Working Principle:** A low-angle cone ($\alpha \approx 1°$ to $4°$) rotates against a stationary flat plate. The fluid fills the thin wedge-shaped gap. The angular velocity $\Omega$ is imposed, and the resulting torque $M$ is measured. **Derivation** (see Section 3.2): $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\Omega}{\alpha} \quad \text{(independent of } r \text{ → uniform)}$$ $$\tau = \frac{3M}{2\pi R^3}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{3M\alpha}{2\pi R^3\Omega}}$$ **Key Advantage:** Since $\dot{\gamma}$ is **uniform across the entire sample**, every fluid element experiences the same shear conditions — making this geometry the gold standard for non-Newtonian fluid characterization. **(b) Comparative Analysis:** | Criterion | Concentric Cylinder | Cone-and-Plate | Parallel Plate | |-----------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------| | **Shear Rate Uniformity** | Non-uniform (varies as $1/r^2$) | Perfectly uniform | Linear variation: max at rim | | **Sample Volume** | 5 – 50 mL | 0.5 – 5 mL | 0.5 – 3 mL | | **Non-Newtonian Suitability** | Limited (for wide gaps) | Excellent | Good (with Rabinowitsch correction) | | **Cleaning & Loading** | Difficult (narrow gap) | Moderate | Easy (open geometry) | | **Food Applications** | Milk, juices, oils | Chocolate, sauces, gels | Yogurt, dough, spreads | | **Particle Tolerance** | High (wide gap) | Low (small gap) | Moderate | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing RPM with rad/s without proper conversion is the #1 cause of lost marks. Always convert $N$ (RPM) → $\Omega$ (rad/s) using $\Omega = 2\pi N/60$. > 2. **Forgetting Constants:** In the cone-plate equation, the factor is $3M/(2\pi R^3)$ — students often incorrectly write $M/(2\pi R^3)$ or forget the factor of 3. > 3. **Narrow-Gap Assumption Validity:** Students apply the narrow-gap approximation without checking if $\delta/R_i < 0.1$. Always validate! > 4. **Sign Convention:** Torque magnitude is always positive; do not include negative signs in viscosity equations. > 5. **Cone Angle Units:** The cone angle $\alpha$ must be in **radians**, not degrees. Convert: $\alpha_{rad} = \alpha_{deg} \times \pi/180$. > 6. **Reynolds Number Check:** For non-Newtonian fluids, ensure the Taylor number is below the critical value to avoid Taylor vortices (secondary flow instability). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Rotational Viscometers** ✅ A **rotational viscometer** imposes a controlled shear via a rotating geometry and measures the resulting **torque** to compute viscosity. ✅ The fundamental equation is always $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$, where both $\tau$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ are derived from the specific geometry. ✅ **Three primary geometries:** Concentric Cylinder (Searle), Cone-and-Plate, and Parallel Plate. ✅ **Concentric Cylinder:** Use $R_i$, $R_o$, and $L$. Shear rate is **non-uniform** (varies with $r$); narrow-gap approximation gives uniform shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = \Omega R_i/\delta$. ✅ **Cone-and-Plate:** Use cone radius $R$ and angle $\alpha$. Shear rate is **perfectly uniform** = $\Omega/\alpha$. Best for non-Newtonian food fluids. ✅ **Parallel Plate:** Use radius $R$ and gap $h$. Shear rate varies **linearly** with radius, max at the outer edge. ✅ **Brookfield viscometers** are spindle-type, used for quick industrial QC — calibration is empirical with instrument-specific constants. ✅ **Conversions to memorize:** - $N$ (RPM) → $\Omega$ (rad/s): multiply by $2\pi/60$ - $\alpha$ (degrees) → $\alpha$ (radians): multiply by $\pi/180$ - $1 \,\text{cP} = 1 \,\text{mPa·s}$ ✅ **Material selection:** Stainless steel or titanium bobs for food contact; hygienic designs (CIP/SIP compatibility) required for dairy and brewery applications. ✅ **Temperature control:** Critical — viscosity is highly temperature-dependent; use Peltier or circulating bath jackets for $\pm 0.1\,°$C precision. ✅ **Shear rate range for food applications:** - Pouring: $\sim 10$ s⁻¹ - Mixing/stirring: $\sim 50$ – $100$ s⁻¹ - Pipe flow: $\sim 100$ – $1000$ s⁻¹ - High-shear processing (homogenization): $\sim 10^3$ – $10^4$ s⁻¹ ✅ **Key advantage of rotational viscometers:** Ability to perform **continuous shear rate sweeps** for non-Newtonian characterization, unlike capillary viscometers which require multiple capillaries. ✅ **Limitations:** Not suitable for very low viscosity fluids (e.g., water at room temp — use capillary); secondary flows (Taylor vortices) can occur at high speeds; sample evaporation at high temperatures and high shear.

topics.title: Vibrational (Oscillation) Viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibrational (Oscillation) Viscometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vibrational (Oscillation) Viscometer** is a viscometric instrument that determines the viscosity of a fluid by forcing a submerged sensor element (typically a sphere, rod, plate, or piston) to oscillate at a fixed or variable frequency, and correlating the resistive force (drag) or the energy dissipation per cycle back to the fluid's viscous and elastic properties.

Mathematically, the instrument transduces a **sinusoidal mechanical input** (displacement or velocity) into a **sinusoidal mechanical output** (force or torque), and from the amplitude ratio and phase lag, both the **dynamic viscosity (η′)** and the **storage/elastic modulus (G′)** can be extracted in a single measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The vibrational viscometer belongs to the family of *absolute rheometers* because its operating equation is derivable from first principles of fluid mechanics (Stokes' drag extended to oscillatory flow), without requiring calibration against standard fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Fluid Domain:** Vibrational viscometers are exceptionally well suited for **opaque, suspensions-containing, settling-prone, or high-viscosity food systems** such as chocolate, tomato ketchup, fruit pulps, and doughs — where traditional rotational viscometers fail due to optical obstruction or particle settling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you push a **stick deep into a swimming pool** and then move it gently back and forth in a slow, rhythmic motion. The water **resists** your motion. If the water were thin (like alcohol), the stick would slice through easily and you would barely feel any drag. If the water were thick (like honey), you would feel a strong, sluggish resistance that lags behind your motion.

Now imagine you did this with the stick attached to an **electric motor** that always oscillates it at a *known speed* (say 100 back-and-forth cycles per minute). The motor would have to **work harder** to keep up the oscillation in honey than in water. By simply measuring **how much extra power** the motor must supply to keep the stick oscillating steadily, you can figure out the **thickness (viscosity)** of the liquid.

That, in essence, is what a vibrational viscometer does — but with extreme precision and mathematical exactness.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Tell-Tale Clues:** The instrument does not only measure *how hard* the motor must push (which gives **viscosity**), but also *when* it must push relative to the stick's position (which gives **elasticity / solid-like behavior**). This dual information is why oscillation rheometry is a *complete* rheological tool.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

The following key quantities are measured or computed by a vibrational viscometer (SI units given in **bold**):

- **Oscillation frequency (ω)** — **rad·s⁻¹**
- **Amplitude of displacement (x₀)** — **m**
- **Amplitude of velocity (v₀ = ωx₀)** — **m·s⁻¹**
- **Damping coefficient (c)** — **N·s·m⁻¹**
- **Inertial coefficient / added mass (m*)** — **kg**
- **Dynamic (shear) viscosity (η′)** — **Pa·s**
- **Storage modulus (G′)** — **Pa**
- **Loss modulus (G″)** — **Pa**
- **Complex viscosity (η*)** — **Pa·s**
- **Density of fluid (ρ)** — **kg·m⁻³**
- **Sensor radius (r)** — **m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Stepwise Logical Breakdown

The instrument works on the principle of **forced damped oscillation**. The sequence of operation is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Electromagnetic Excitation:** A driver (electromagnetic coil or piezo actuator) forces the sensor to oscillate sinusoidally with displacement $x(t) = x_0 \sin(\omega t)$, where $x_0$ is the amplitude and $\omega$ the angular frequency.

- **Step 2 — Fluid Response:** The fluid exerts a resistive force $F(t)$ on the sensor. Because the fluid is *viscoelastic*, the force has **two components**:
  1. An **in-phase** (viscous) component proportional to velocity $v = \dot{x} = \omega x_0 \cos(\omega t)$ — this carries the viscosity information.
  2. An **out-of-phase** (inertial/elastic) component proportional to acceleration $\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x_0 \sin(\omega t)$ — this carries the elasticity / added mass information.

- **Step 3 — Force Transduction:** A sensor (often a second coil acting as a velocity pickup, or a strain gauge) measures the instantaneous force or velocity of the sensor.

- **Step 4 — Lock-in Detection:** A phase-sensitive detector (lock-in amplifier) extracts the **in-phase amplitude** and **quadrature amplitude** of the response signal relative to the driving signal.

- **Step 5 — Mathematical Decoding:** The two amplitudes are converted into the **damping coefficient (c)** and the **added mass coefficient (m\*)**, which are then mapped to viscosity and density.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Forced Oscillation:** Static or slow-flow measurements fail for fragile, structured, or settling systems. Oscillation perturbs the fluid *gently* (small amplitude) and yields *time-resolved* information about the microstructure (e.g., gel networks in yogurt, fat crystal networks in chocolate).

## 2.2 Hydrodynamic Theory — Stokes' Drag Extended to Oscillation

For a **sphere of radius r** oscillating in a Newtonian fluid with low Reynolds number ($Re \ll 1$), the instantaneous force is given by the **Boussinesq–Basset history force equation**:

$$
F(t) = -6\pi \eta r\, v(t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left(\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right) \frac{dv}{dt} - 6 r^2 \sqrt{\pi \rho \eta} \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{1}{\sqrt{t-\tau}} \frac{dv(\tau)}{d\tau} d\tau
$$

The three terms represent:
1. **Steady Stokes drag** — viscous dissipation, depends on η.
2. **Added mass / virtual mass** — inertial coupling, depends on ρ.
3. **Basset history term** — memory effect from the unsteady wake.

For a sinusoidal steady state, this reduces to a **complex force–velocity relationship**:

$$
F(t) = -\bigl[\,6\pi \eta r\, +\, 6\pi r^2 \sqrt{2 \eta \rho \omega}\,\bigr]\, v(t)\, +\, \bigl[\,\rho V\, +\, \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V\,\bigr]\,\dot{v}(t)
$$

where the **effective damping** depends on $\sqrt{\omega}$ and the **effective inertia** is the sphere mass plus one-half the displaced fluid mass (added mass).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equation Bank — Memorize These for the ESE (End Semester Exam).** All entries below are examinable in **Part A (3-mark) definition/fill-in questions** and as a **7-mark Part B derivation sub-question**.

| # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $v(t) = \omega x_0 \cos(\omega t)$ | $v$=velocity, $\omega$=angular freq, $x_0$=displacement amp | Sensor velocity in forced oscillation |
| 2 | $F_{drag} = 6\pi \eta r v$ | $F$=Stokes drag, $\eta$=viscosity, $r$=sphere radius, $v$=velocity | Steady viscous force on sphere |
| 3 | $F_{inertial} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V \dot{v}$ | $V$=sphere volume, $\rho$=fluid density, $\dot{v}$=acceleration | Added-mass inertial force |
| 4 | $F_{Basset} = 6 r^2 \sqrt{\pi \rho \eta} \int \frac{\dot{v}(\tau)}{\sqrt{t-\tau}} d\tau$ | History of acceleration | Memory effect in unsteady flow |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{c}{6\pi r}$ | $c$=measured damping coefficient | Direct viscosity readout |
| 6 | $\eta^{*} = \eta' - i\eta''$ | $\eta'$=dynamic visc, $\eta''=G'/\omega$ | Complex viscosity |
| 7 | $G^{*} = G' + iG''$ | $G'$=storage, $G''$=loss modulus | Complex modulus |
| 8 | $G' = \omega \eta'' = \tfrac{F_0}{x_0}\cos\delta$ | $\delta$=phase lag | Elastic part of response |
| 9 | $G'' = \omega \eta' = \tfrac{F_0}{x_0}\sin\delta$ | Phase lag | Viscous part of response |
| 10 | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | Loss tangent | Solid-like vs liquid-like index |
| 11 | $Re_{osc} = \dfrac{\rho \omega r^2}{\eta}$ | Oscillatory Reynolds number | Regime check (low Re for Stokes) |
| 12 | $\delta_v = \sqrt{2\eta/(\rho \omega)}$ | Viscous penetration depth | Thickness of fluid layer dragged |
| 13 | $P_{diss} = \pi c \omega x_0^2$ | $P$=power dissipated per cycle | Energy balance method |
| 14 | $Q^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$ | Internal friction, $\Delta E$=energy lost/cycle | Damping quality factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** In *food process control* (e.g., online chocolate viscosity monitoring), the **oscillatory Reynolds number ($Re_{osc}$)** and **viscous penetration depth ($\delta_v$)** are the two key dimensionless numbers a process engineer monitors to ensure the measurement is in the *linear viscoelastic regime* — i.e., the food microstructure is being probed, not destroyed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Online chocolate conche monitoring** — fat-crystal network strength quantified via $G'$.
- **Yogurt gelation kinetics** — time-sweep oscillation at low strain tracks network formation.
- **Tomato paste hot-break process control** — viscosity measured in-line in pipes with no flow disruption.
- **Slurry / pulp pipelines** — vibration viscometers clamp onto the pipe exterior and infer viscosity from the pipe's response, eliminating any contact with the abrasive fluid.
- **Pharmaceutical ointment QC** — non-invasive measurement of thixotropy under oscillatory stress.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Complex Force on an Oscillating Sphere (Boussinesq–Basset Solution)

**Objective:** Starting from the unsteady Stokes equation, derive the complex force acting on a sphere oscillating as $v(t) = v_0 e^{i\omega t}$ in a Newtonian fluid of viscosity η and density ρ.

**Starting Point — Unsteady Linearized Momentum Equation:**

$$
\rho \frac{\partial \vec{u}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p + \eta \nabla^2 \vec{u}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume Harmonic Time Dependence.** Let the velocity field take the form $\vec{u}(\vec{r},t) = \vec{u}(\vec{r}) e^{i\omega t}$. The time derivative $\partial \vec{u}/\partial t$ becomes $i\omega \vec{u}(\vec{r}) e^{i\omega t}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Momentum Equation.** Dividing through by $e^{i\omega t}$, we obtain a steady-state Helmholtz-type equation:

$$
i\omega \rho\, \vec{u}(\vec{r}) = -\nabla p + \eta \nabla^2 \vec{u}(\vec{r})
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Complex Wavenumber.** Define $k^2 = -i\omega\rho/\eta = -i/(\nu/\omega)$, where $\nu = \eta/\rho$ is the kinematic viscosity. This gives:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{u} = k^2 \vec{u}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve in Spherical Coordinates.** For purely radial oscillation along the $z$-axis, the velocity field has the form $u_r(r,\theta) = A(r)\cos\theta$. Substituting into the Helmholtz equation and applying the boundary conditions:

- At the sphere surface ($r = r_0$): $u_r = v_0 \cos\theta$ (no-slip)
- At infinity ($r \to \infty$): $u_r \to 0$

yields the solution:

$$
A(r) = v_0\, r_0^3 \left[\dfrac{1}{r^3} - \dfrac{k}{r^2}\dfrac{K_{3/2}(kr)}{K_{1/2}(kr_0)}\right] \frac{r_0^2}{r^2}
$$

After applying the modified Bessel function identities, the **drag force on the sphere** is:

$$
F = -6\pi \eta r_0 v_0\, \left[\dfrac{k r_0 + 1}{(k r_0)^2 + 1} + \dfrac{3 k r_0}{(k r_0)^2 + 1}\cdot \dfrac{1}{1+i}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Separate into Real (Damping) and Imaginary (Inertial) Parts.** Using $k r_0 = (1-i) r_0 / \delta_v$, where $\delta_v = \sqrt{2\eta/(\rho\omega)}$ is the viscous penetration depth, and after algebraic simplification:

$$
F = -\left[\,6\pi \eta r_0\, \left(1 + \dfrac{r_0}{\delta_v}\right) + 6\pi r_0^2 \sqrt{\tfrac{\eta \rho \omega}{2}}\,\right] v_0\, -\, \left[\,\tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_0^3 \rho\, \omega\,\right] v_0\, i
$$

**Step 6 — Identify Coefficients.** Rearranging into the form $F = -c\, v_0 - i\omega m^* v_0$:

- **Effective damping coefficient:**

$$
c = 6\pi \eta r_0\left(1 + \dfrac{r_0}{\delta_v}\right) + 6\pi r_0^2 \sqrt{\tfrac{\eta \rho \omega}{2}}
$$

- **Added mass coefficient:**

$$
m^* = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r_0^3 \rho
$$

- **Viscosity recovery (low-frequency limit, $r_0 \ll \delta_v$):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{c}{6\pi r_0}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Take-Away:** The factor $\sqrt{\eta \rho \omega / 2}$ in the damping coefficient is the *signature* of oscillatory viscometry — it represents the energy radiated as vorticity into the fluid. The viscosity readout must correct for this term at high oscillation frequencies.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Complex Viscosity from Oscillatory Shear (Small Amplitude)

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the complex modulus $G^{*}$ and the complex viscosity $\eta^{*}$ under small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS).

**Step 1 — Define the Oscillatory Strain Input.** Apply a shear strain:

$$
\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Oscillatory Stress Output.** For a linear viscoelastic material, the stress response is also sinusoidal but leads the strain by a phase angle $\delta$:

$$
\tau(t) = \tau_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta) = \tau_0 \cos\delta \sin(\omega t) + \tau_0 \sin\delta \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify In-Phase and Out-of-Phase Components.**

- The **in-phase** stress amplitude is $\tau_0 \cos\delta$ — this is the *elastic* (storage) response.
- The **out-of-phase** stress amplitude is $\tau_0 \sin\delta$ — this is the *viscous* (loss) response.

**Step 4 — Define the Moduli.** Storage modulus and loss modulus are defined as:

$$
G' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta
$$
$$
G'' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta
$$

**Step 5 — Form the Complex Modulus.**

$$
G^{*} = G' + iG'' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} e^{i\delta}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Strain Rate.** Differentiating the strain:

$$
\dot{\gamma}(t) = \omega \gamma_0 \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 7 — Form the Complex Viscosity.** The complex viscosity is the ratio of complex stress to complex strain rate. Since $\dot\gamma = i\omega \gamma$ in the complex notation, we obtain:

$$
\eta^{*} = \dfrac{G^{*}}{i\omega} = \dfrac{G''}{\omega} - i\dfrac{G'}{\omega}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify Real and Imaginary Parts.**

$$
\eta' = \dfrac{G''}{\omega} \quad \text{(dynamic viscosity, in phase with strain rate)}
$$
$$
\eta'' = \dfrac{G'}{\omega} \quad \text{(out-of-phase component, elastic contribution)}
$$

**Step 9 — Magnitude and Phase.**

$$
\vert \eta^{*} \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}}{\omega}
$$
$$
\delta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{G''}{G'}\right)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior of Foods Under Oscillation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Board questions often ask *"What does a high $G'/G''$ ratio mean for a food product?"* — Use the matrix below as a ready reference for analytical answers.

| Material State | $G'$ vs $G''$ | $\tan\delta$ | Physical Interpretation | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strong gel | $G' \gg G''$ | $\tan\delta \ll 1$ | Elastic, solid-like network | Set yogurt, gelatin |
| Weak gel | $G' > G''$ (modest) | $\tan\delta \approx 0.1-0.3$ | Soft elastic network | Ketchup, sour cream |
| Gelling sol (transition) | $G' = G''$ | $\tan\delta = 1$ | Gel point / cross-over | Yoghurt gelation time |
| Viscous liquid | $G'' \gg G'$ | $\tan\delta \gg 1$ | Liquid-like, energy dissipative | Honey at high T, milk |
| Purely viscous (Newtonian) | $G' = 0$ | $\tan\delta = \infty$ | No elasticity | Water, mineral oil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vibrational Viscometer

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional signal chain** of a typical vibrational viscometer. Each block represents a hardware / signal-processing stage; arrows denote the *flow of information or energy* between stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function Generator - Sinusoidal Drive Signal] --> B[Power Amplifier]
    B --> C[Electromagnetic Driver Coil]
    C --> D[Oscillating Sensor - Sphere or Piston]
    D --> E[Fluid Sample in Test Cell]
    E --> F[Drag Force on Sensor]
    F --> G[Pickup Coil or Strain Gauge Transducer]
    G --> H[Signal Conditioner - Amplifier and Filter]
    H --> I[Lock-In Amplifier - Phase Sensitive Detector]
    I --> J[In-Phase Amplitude Channel]
    I --> K[Quadrature Amplitude Channel]
    J --> L[Computation Unit - Damping Coefficient c]
    K --> M[Computation Unit - Added Mass m star]
    L --> N[Viscosity Readout - Pa.s]
    M --> O[Density Readout - kg per cubic meter]
    N --> P[Display and Data Logger]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Optional Feedback to Process Control PLC]
```

## 4.2 Operating Regimes of Oscillatory Measurement

The diagram below classifies the **three operating regimes** of a vibrational viscometer and their physical significance. Knowing which regime a measurement belongs to is essential for choosing the correct formula and avoiding errors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Oscillatory Measurement] --> S{Frequency Range}
    S -->|Low omega, r0 less than delta_v| T[Quasi-Steady Stokes Regime]
    S -->|Intermediate omega, r0 comparable to delta_v| U[Transition Regime]
    S -->|High omega, r0 greater than delta_v| V[Inertia Dominated Regime]
    T --> T1[Viscosity recovered from c over 6 pi r0]
    U --> U1[Full Basset equation required, no simplification]
    V --> V1[Stokes layer thin, viscous penetration depth governs]
```

## 4.3 Signal Processing Chain — Phasor Domain

The following diagram illustrates how a single sinusoidal force measurement is **decomposed into two orthogonal phasor components** using a lock-in amplifier. This is the heart of how one instrument extracts *both* viscosity and elasticity from a single oscillation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Measured Force F of t] --> Y[Multiplication by Reference Sin omega t]
    X --> Z[Multiplication by Reference Cos omega t]
    Y --> Y1[Low Pass Filter - LPF]
    Z --> Z1[Low Pass Filter - LPF]
    Y1 --> Y2[In-Phase Component F cos delta]
    Z1 --> Z2[Quadrature Component F sin delta]
    Y2 --> AA[Damping Coefficient c proportional to F sin delta]
    Z2 --> AB[Inertial Coefficient m star proportional to F cos delta]
    AA --> AC[Viscosity eta equals c over 6 pi r0]
    AB --> AD[Density rho from m star over displaced volume]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading of the Diagrams:** Notice that **the same physical measurement** (force vs. time) yields *two independent scalar outputs* (in-phase and quadrature). This is the central elegance of oscillatory rheometry: **one sensor, one excitation, two material functions (η and G′)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT745]` **Define vibrational viscometer. Mention any two advantages over rotational viscometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vibrational viscometer is an instrument that determines fluid viscosity by oscillating a submerged sensor at a known frequency and measuring the resistive force (or energy dissipation) on the sensor. **Two advantages:** (i) No moving mechanical parts in the fluid that could damage fragile food structures (non-invasive) — *1 mark*. (ii) Provides both viscous and elastic information simultaneously from a single measurement — *1 mark*. (iii) Suitable for opaque, settling, or particle-laden suspensions where optical methods fail — *1 mark*.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT745]` **What is meant by complex viscosity? State its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Complex viscosity $\eta^{*}$ is the frequency-dependent analogue of steady-shear viscosity, defined as the ratio of complex stress to complex strain rate under oscillatory shear. **Mathematical form:** $\eta^{*} = \eta' - i\eta'' = G^{*}/(i\omega)$ — *1.5 marks*. Where $\eta' = G''/\omega$ is the dynamic viscosity and $\eta'' = G'/\omega$ is the elastic component — *1.5 marks*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT745]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the complex force on a sphere of radius $r_0$ oscillating with velocity $v_0 e^{i\omega t}$ in a Newtonian fluid. Show that in the low-frequency limit the viscosity is recovered as $\eta = c/(6\pi r_0)$ where $c$ is the damping coefficient. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A vibrational viscometer uses a sphere of radius 2 mm oscillating at 50 Hz in a Newtonian food oil. The measured damping coefficient is $c = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s·m⁻¹. Calculate the viscosity. If the oscillation frequency is increased to 200 Hz, the damping coefficient rises to $7.8 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s·m⁻¹. Explain the rise qualitatively using the oscillatory Reynolds number concept. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Governing equation.** Start with the unsteady linearized Stokes equation:

$$
\rho \frac{\partial \vec{u}}{\partial t} = -\nabla p + \eta \nabla^2 \vec{u} \quad \text{— \textit{1 mark}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute harmonic time dependence** $u(r,t) = u(r) e^{i\omega t}$:

$$
i\omega \rho\, \vec{u} = -\nabla p + \eta \nabla^2 \vec{u} \quad \text{— \textit{1 mark}}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce complex wavenumber** $k^2 = -i\omega\rho/\eta$:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{u} - k^2 \vec{u} = 0 \quad \text{— \textit{1 mark}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions** at $r = r_0$ (no-slip: $u_r = v_0\cos\theta$) and at $r \to \infty$ ($u_r \to 0$) — *1 mark*.

**Step 5 — Solve and integrate stress on the sphere** to obtain the complex force:

$$
F = -6\pi \eta r_0\, v_0 \left[\dfrac{k r_0 + 1}{(k r_0)^2 + 1} + \dfrac{3 k r_0}{(k r_0)^2 + 1}\cdot \dfrac{1}{1+i}\right] \quad \text{— \textit{1 mark}}
$$

**Step 6 — Take low-frequency limit** $kr_0 \to 0$ (i.e., $r_0 \ll \delta_v$). The bracketed factor $\to 1$:

$$
F = -6\pi \eta r_0\, v_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = \dfrac{c}{6\pi r_0} \quad \text{— \textit{2 marks}}
$$

### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute viscosity at 50 Hz** using $\eta = c/(6\pi r_0)$ — *1 mark*:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{6\pi (2 \times 10^{-3})} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7699 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1194\ \text{Pa·s}
$$

**Step 2 — At 200 Hz, naively use the same formula** — *1 mark*:

$$
\eta_{app} = \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{-3}}{6\pi (2 \times 10^{-3})} = 0.2068\ \text{Pa·s}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the discrepancy is due to frequency dependence.** The Basset history term contributes an extra damping $\propto \sqrt{\eta\rho\omega}$ at higher $\omega$ — *2 marks*.

**Step 4 — Compute the oscillatory Reynolds number at each frequency.** For a Newtonian fluid with the measured $\eta$, assuming $\rho \approx 900$ kg/m³ for the oil:

At 50 Hz: $Re_{osc} = \rho\omega r_0^2/\eta = (900)(2\pi\cdot 50)(2\times10^{-3})^2/0.1194 \approx 0.948$

At 200 Hz: $Re_{osc} = (900)(2\pi\cdot 200)(2\times10^{-3})^2/0.1194 \approx 3.79$ — *2 marks*.

**Step 5 — Qualitative explanation:** As $\omega$ increases by 4×, $Re_{osc}$ also increases by 4×, moving out of the low-frequency Stokes regime into the inertia-influenced regime. The viscous penetration depth $\delta_v = \sqrt{2\eta/(\rho\omega)}$ shrinks as $1/\sqrt{\omega}$, so the Basset term — which scales as $\sqrt{\omega}$ — becomes significant. The instrument now over-estimates the viscosity if the simple low-frequency formula is used. A full Basset correction is required — *1 mark*.

---

> **Q3B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT745]` **(a)** With a neat sketch of the operating principle, explain how a vibrational viscometer measures both viscosity and elasticity in a single test. Define the complex modulus $G^{*}$, storage modulus $G'$, loss modulus $G''$ and the loss tangent $\tan\delta$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a chocolate melt at 40 °C, oscillatory shear at 1 Hz and strain amplitude 0.5% gives $G' = 4.2$ Pa and $G'' = 6.8$ Pa. Calculate the complex modulus, the complex viscosity, and the phase angle. State whether the chocolate behaves more like a solid or a liquid at this condition. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Operating principle:** A sensor is oscillated sinusoidally by an electromagnetic driver. The fluid exerts a drag force $F(t)$ that is decomposed by a lock-in amplifier into in-phase and quadrature components relative to the drive signal — *2 marks*.

**Step 2 — The in-phase component** (in phase with strain rate) gives the **loss modulus $G''$** = viscous response — *1 mark*.

**Step 3 — The quadrature component** (in phase with strain) gives the **storage modulus $G'$** = elastic response — *1 mark*.

**Step 4 — Definitions:**

- $G^{*} = G' + iG''$ — complex modulus — *0.5 marks*
- $G' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\cos\delta$ — storage modulus — *0.5 marks*
- $G'' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\sin\delta$ — loss modulus — *0.5 marks*
- $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ — loss tangent — *0.5 marks*
- $\delta$ = phase angle between stress and strain — *1 mark*

### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute complex modulus magnitude** — *1 mark*:

$$
\vert G^{*} \vert = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2} = \sqrt{4.2^2 + 6.8^2} = \sqrt{17.64 + 46.24} = \sqrt{63.88} = 7.99\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute complex viscosity** at $\omega = 2\pi(1) = 6.283$ rad/s — *2 marks*:

$$
\eta^{*} = \dfrac{G^{*}}{i\omega} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vert \eta^{*} \vert = \dfrac{\vert G^{*} \vert}{\omega} = \dfrac{7.99}{6.283} = 1.272\ \text{Pa·s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute phase angle** — *2 marks*:

$$
\delta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{G''}{G'}\right) = \arctan\left(\dfrac{6.8}{4.2}\right) = \arctan(1.619) = 58.3^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation** — *2 marks*: Since $G'' > G'$ (and $\tan\delta = 1.619 > 1$), the chocolate melt at 40 °C behaves **more like a viscous liquid than an elastic solid**. This is expected because chocolate is in its molten state above the melting point of cocoa butter (~32–34 °C). The result also implies that the conching / tempering process must be assessed at lower temperatures where the fat crystal network dominates.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting units:** A frequent error in numerical problems is writing $G'$ in Pa without explicitly writing the unit, costing 0.5 marks.
> - **Confusing $G'$ with $G''$:** Many students swap the in-phase and out-of-phase components. **Mnemonic:** $G'$ is **G-prime** = **P**rime = in phase with strain (elastic, storage). $G''$ is in phase with **strain rate** (viscous, loss).
> - **Missing the "small amplitude" condition in SAOS:** If the question says "small amplitude," the derivation assumes *linear* viscoelasticity. Always state the linearity assumption explicitly — this single sentence often fetches 1 mark.
> - **Sign error in complex viscosity formula:** Some texts write $\eta^{*} = \eta' + i\eta''$. KTU's convention is $\eta^{*} = \eta' - i\eta''$. Always follow the KTU-prescribed convention or you will lose a mark on a definition question.
> - **Forgetting to interpret the result:** Numerical answers without concluding "this means the fluid is solid-like / liquid-like" are penalized 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Punchlist — Vibrational Viscometer (Module 3):**

- **Core idea:** A sensor is forced to oscillate; the fluid's viscous + elastic response is decoded from the force signal.
- **Two output material functions per measurement:** dynamic viscosity η′ AND storage modulus G′ — the unique selling point of oscillation rheometry.
- **Key historical names to know:** Stokes (steady drag), Boussinesq–Basset (unsteady drag with history term), Bode (lock-in amplifier), Cox–Merz rule (relates complex viscosity to steady-shear viscosity).
- **Stokes drag law (steady form, the foundation):** $F = 6\pi \eta r v$ — must be derivable from scratch in the exam.
- **Low-frequency limit formula:** $\eta = c/(6\pi r)$ — this is the working equation of the simplest vibrational viscometer.
- **High-frequency correction:** an extra $\sqrt{\omega}$ damping term from the Basset history force must be added at higher frequencies.
- **Viscous penetration depth** $\delta_v = \sqrt{2\eta/(\rho\omega)}$ — thickness of the fluid layer that "sees" the oscillation. Always check $r_0 \ll \delta_v$ before applying the low-frequency formula.
- **Oscillatory Reynolds number** $Re_{osc} = \rho\omega r_0^2/\eta$ — regime indicator. If $Re_{osc} \ll 1$, Stokes regime is valid.
- **SAOS region required:** Strain amplitude must be in the **Linear Viscoelastic Region (LVE)** — typically 0.1–1% for food gels; otherwise nonlinear effects corrupt the data.
- **Complex modulus $G^{*} = G' + iG''$** — always remember: $G'$ = storage (elastic, in phase with strain), $G''$ = loss (viscous, in phase with strain rate).
- **Complex viscosity $\eta^{*} = G^{*}/(i\omega)$** — magnitude $|\eta^{*}| = \sqrt{G'^2+G''^2}/\omega$.
- **Phase angle δ** = arctan(G″/G′) — $\delta = 0°$ = pure solid; $\delta = 90°$ = pure liquid; $\delta = 45°$ = gel point.
- **Loss tangent $\tan\delta = G''/G'$** — > 1 = liquid-like; < 1 = solid-like.
- **Power dissipated per cycle:** $P_{diss} = \pi c \omega x_0^2$ — this is the energy approach to measuring viscosity.
- **Advantages over rotational viscometer:** no contact damage to fragile foods, suitable for opaque / particulate / settling fluids, gives both η and G′ simultaneously, suitable for in-line process control.
- **Limitations:** requires the sample to be in a defined geometry; signal-to-noise ratio drops for very low-viscosity fluids; high-frequency measurements may exceed the LVE for weak gels.
- **Common food applications:** chocolate conche monitoring, yogurt gelation, ketchup hot-break control, sauce QC, dough rheology, fruit pulp viscosity.
- **Two typical instrument designs to remember:** (1) **Resonance type** — drives the sensor at its natural frequency and tracks frequency shift + bandwidth to extract η and ρ. (2) **Forced-oscillation type** — drives at a fixed ω and measures force amplitude + phase.
- **Cross-over point** where $G' = G''$ marks the **gel point** in time-sweep oscillatory tests — a frequently-asked KTU question (4 marks).
- **Cox–Merz rule:** $\eta^{*}(\omega) = \eta(\dot{\gamma})$ evaluated at $\dot{\gamma} = \omega$ — useful for *predicting* steady-shear viscosity from oscillatory data; works well for many polymer solutions and some foods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bostwick Consistometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bostwick Consistometer**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bostwick Consistometer (Definition)**
> The Bostwick Consistometer is a **simple, gravity-operated empirical rheological instrument** used to determine the **consistency** of viscous, paste-like, and semi-fluid food materials (such as ketchup, tomato paste, sauces, purées, jams, mayonnaise, and baby foods) by measuring the **distance traveled by a fixed volume of sample under its own weight** over a standardized time interval through an inclined channel.

The result is reported as the **Bostwick Distance (BD)** in **cm** over a fixed duration of **30 seconds** (cm/30 s). A larger Bostwick value indicates a **less viscous / more flowable** product, while a smaller value indicates a **more viscous / less flowable** product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The Bostwick Consistometer is categorized under **empirical / one-point rheological methods** for high-viscosity food systems. It does NOT measure the fundamental viscosity η directly; instead, it provides an **operational index of consistency** that correlates with the apparent viscosity and yield stress of the material.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Saucer" Intuition

Imagine placing a small **saucer of honey** on a slightly tilted table. The faster the honey creeps forward on its own, the "runnier" it is. The Bostwick Consistometer works on exactly this principle — but in a **standardized, measurable, and reproducible** manner.

Think of it this way:

| Everyday Analogy | Bostwick Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Honey on a tilted plate | Sample spreading in the trough |
| How far the honey creeps in 30 s | Bostwick Distance (cm) |
| Thinner honey → creeps more | Higher BD value |
| Thicker honey → barely moves | Lower BD value |

So a **Bostwick value of 8 cm/30 s** for tomato ketchup, for instance, is a *quality control fingerprint* that food manufacturers monitor to ensure the consumer-experienced "squeezability" remains consistent from batch to batch.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standards in the Bostwick Test**
> - **Standard test time:** $t = 30$ seconds (also commonly reported at 60 s)
> - **Standard temperature:** **20 °C** (sometimes 25 °C)
> - **Sample volume:** typically **100 mL**
> - **Trough length:** **25.0 cm** (graduated)
> - **Trough width:** **5.0 cm**
> - **Leveling tolerance:** the trough must be perfectly horizontal (verified using the built-in bubble level)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Principle — Structured Logic

The Bostwick Consistometer operates on the **gravity-driven free-surface flow** principle. The flow is governed by an interplay between **gravitational body force**, **viscous resistance**, and (for non-Newtonian foods) the **yield stress**.

The system can be understood in the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Leveling & Calibration**
  The trough is leveled using a built-in spirit level and adjustable feet. Any tilt would falsely accelerate the sample and produce a non-representative distance.

- **Step 2 — Sample Loading**
  A precisely measured volume (≈100 mL) of the food sample, pre-equilibrated to the test temperature, is poured into the **small reservoir compartment** located behind the **spring-loaded gate** at one end of the trough.

- **Step 3 — Gate Release (t = 0)**
  The gate is lifted, exposing the sample to the open trough. Simultaneously, a stopwatch is started. The sample begins to flow under gravity along the horizontal trough.

- **Step 4 — Free Flow Phase**
  As the leading edge of the sample advances, the flow depth $h(x,t)$ decreases with distance $x$ and time $t$. The driving pressure is the hydrostatic head $\rho g h$, while the resisting shear stress depends on the rheological model of the food.

- **Step 5 — Distance Measurement (t = 30 s)**
  After exactly 30 seconds, the gate is closed, and the **maximum distance traveled by the leading edge** is read from the graduated scale engraved on the trough floor. This distance is the **Bostwick value**.

- **Step 6 — Reporting**
  The Bostwick Distance is reported in **cm / 30 s**. For high-viscosity samples (e.g., heavy pastes) where flow is minimal, the result at **60 s** is sometimes reported for better resolution.

---

### Why the Bostwick Works (and Why It Doesn't Measure True Viscosity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Bostwick is an **empirical index**, not a fundamental rheometer. The "viscosity" inferred from it is an **apparent viscosity** evaluated at a single, ill-defined shear rate. The flow in the trough is **non-viscometric** (free-surface, non-uniform), so converting BD to η requires empirical correlations specific to the product.

The shear rate experienced by the sample in the Bostwick trough is **not constant** — it decreases as the sample spreads and the depth drops. Therefore, for **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** food materials, the Bostwick value depends on:

- The **flow behavior index** $n$ (lower $n$ = more shear-thinning = more sensitive to time)
- The **consistency index** $K$
- The **yield stress** $\sigma_y$ (if the material is a yield-stress fluid)
- The **density** $\rho$ of the food

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bostwick Distance (raw output) | $BD = x_{max}\big\vert_{t = 30\,s}$ | $x_{max}$ in **cm** | Maximum leading-edge distance in 30 s |
| 2 | Apparent viscosity (Newtonian estimate) | $\eta_{app} \approx \dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^2\, t}{L \cdot BD}$ | $\rho$ = kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h_0$ = initial depth (m), $t$ = 30 s, $L$ = trough length (m) | Crude estimate of viscosity from BD |
| 3 | Driving hydrostatic pressure | $\Delta P = \rho\, g\, h(x,t)$ | Pa | Local pressure driving flow |
| 4 | Power-law viscosity (for shear-thinning foods) | $\eta(\dot\gamma) = K\, \dot\gamma^{n-1}$ | $K$ in Pa·sⁿ, $n$ dimensionless | Generalized Newtonian model |
| 5 | Yield stress criterion (Bingham / Herschel-Bulkley) | $\vert \tau \vert > \sigma_y$ for flow | $\sigma_y$ in Pa | Material will not flow if shear stress < yield stress |
| 6 | Empirical BD-to-η correlation (general food paste) | $\eta_{app} \approx \dfrac{a}{BD} + b$ | $a$, $b$ are product-specific empirical constants | Used in QC labs for routine monitoring |
| 7 | Temperature correction (Arrhenius-type) | $\eta(T) = \eta_0\, \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$ | $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in K | BD is highly temperature-sensitive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Equation 2 is a *first-order* Newtonian estimate only. In the KTU examination, full credit is given when the student clearly **states the assumption** (Newtonian flow, constant depth, no slip) before applying it.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bostwick Consistometer is the **workhorse of the food industry QC laboratory** because it is:

- **Cheap** (≈1/100th the cost of a rotational rheometer)
- **Fast** (result in 30 s)
- **Robust** (no electronics, no calibration drift)
- **Operator-friendly** (line technicians can run it)
- **Industrially standardized** (ASTM, CODEX, ISO food-grade specifications)

It is used by **Heinz, Nestlé, Unilever, and most condiment manufacturers** to certify batch release of ketchup, mustard, BBQ sauce, mayonnaise, and baby food purées.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Driving Force in the Bostwick Trough

The driving force for sample flow in the Bostwick trough is the **gravitational hydrostatic pressure** generated by the column of food material itself.

**Step 1:** Consider a small element of the sample in the trough, of depth $h$ (measured vertically from the trough floor to the free surface), width $w$ (trough width), and infinitesimal length $dx$.

**Step 2:** The hydrostatic pressure at the base of this element is given by Pascal's principle:

$$P = \rho\, g\, h$$

**Step 3:** The net horizontal force acting on the element (since the trough is level, only the pressure gradient drives flow) is:

$$dF = -w\, h\, \dfrac{dP}{dx}\, dx$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $P = \rho g h$ and recognizing that the depth $h$ varies with $x$ and $t$:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dx} = \rho\, g\, \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the pressure gradient driving the flow is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dx} = \rho\, g\, \dfrac{\partial h(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The local pressure gradient is proportional to the local **surface slope** $\partial h / \partial x$. The flow stops when the surface becomes flat, i.e., when $\partial h / \partial x = 0$. This is why a more viscous sample (which spreads slowly) keeps a steep leading-edge profile and travels a shorter distance.

---

### Derivation 2 — Newtonian Apparent Viscosity from Bostwick Distance

For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress at any depth $y$ below the free surface in a gravity-driven film is balanced by the hydrostatic pressure gradient:

$$\tau(y) = \rho\, g\, (h - y)\, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 1:** The maximum shear stress occurs at the trough floor ($y = 0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \rho\, g\, h\, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = \eta\, \dot\gamma$. Integrating across the depth to obtain the volumetric flow rate per unit width $q$:

$$q = -\dfrac{h^3}{3\eta}\, \rho\, g\, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** Applying the **lubrication approximation** and conservation of mass for a rectangular trough of width $w$:

$$\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t} + \dfrac{1}{w}\dfrac{\partial (w\, q)}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Combining the two equations and integrating over the wetted length $L = BD$:

$$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^2\, t}{2\, L^2} \quad \text{where}\quad L = BD$$

**Step 5:** Converting to a practical form (with $h_0$ the initial sample depth, $t = 30$ s, $L$ in cm):

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}\;\left[\text{Pa·s}\right]\;=\;\dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^{2}\cdot 30}{2 \cdot BD^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Dimensional check:**
- Numerator: $(\text{kg/m}^3) \cdot (\text{m/s}^2) \cdot (\text{m}^2) \cdot (\text{s}) = \text{Pa·s}$
- Denominator: $(\text{m}^2)$ → result in Pa·s ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of this derivation:** It assumes Newtonian behavior. For real foods (ketchup, sauces), this gives only an **order-of-magnitude** estimate. Industry prefers to use **product-specific empirical correlations** (e.g., $\eta_{app} = a/BD + b$) calibrated against a rotational rheometer.

---

### Derivation 3 — Influence of Yield Stress on Bostwick Distance

Many foods (ketchup, mustard, tomato paste) are **yield-stress fluids** described by the **Herschel–Bulkley model**:

$$\tau = \sigma_y + K\, \dot\gamma^{n}$$

**Step 1:** Flow occurs only where the local shear stress exceeds the yield stress $\sigma_y$:

$$\rho\, g\, h\, \dfrac{dh}{dx} > \sigma_y$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging to find the condition for the **leading edge to advance**:

$$\dfrac{dh}{dx} > \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho\, g\, h}$$

**Step 3:** Integrating this condition gives the **critical yield stress** above which the sample will not flow measurably in 30 s:

$$\sigma_y^{crit} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^{2}}{2 \cdot BD}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, for two foods with identical Bostwick Distance but different densities, the denser one has a **higher effective yield stress**. This is why Bostwick values are **density-dependent** and must be reported along with the food's density for rigorous comparison.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Bostwick Reading Interpretation

| Bostwick Distance (cm/30 s) | Consistency Class | Typical Food Examples | Industrial Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $BD < 2$ | Very thick / paste-like | Heavy tomato paste, cream cheese | Needs mechanical pumping, not pourable |
| $2 \le BD < 6$ | Thick | Ketchup, mayonnaise, mustard | Squeezable from a bottle |
| $6 \le BD < 12$ | Medium | Salad dressings, fruit purées | Pourable but coats a spoon |
| $12 \le BD < 20$ | Thin | Some sauces, dilute purées | Free-flowing |
| $BD \ge 20$ | Very thin | Broths, milk, juices | The Bostwick is **not suitable** (use a Ford/Zahn cup or rotational rheometer) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bostwick Consistometer

    E --> F[Sample Reservoir Compartment]
    G[Stopwatch External] --> H[Operator Reads Leading Edge Distance]
    F --> I[Filled with 100 mL of Test Sample]
    I --> J[Gate Released at t = 0]

    K --> L[Sample Spreads Along Trough]

    M --> N[Gate Closes]
    N --> O[Read Leading Edge Position on Scale]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Bostwick Consistometer and state its industrial applications in the food industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bostwick Consistometer is an **empirical rheological instrument** used to measure the consistency of viscous and semi-fluid food materials by determining the **distance traveled by a fixed volume of sample** under gravity in a standardized horizontal trough over a fixed time interval of **30 seconds**, with the result reported in **cm/30 s**.

**Industrial applications:**
1. **Quality control** of ketchup, sauces, mayonnaise, mustard, and salad dressings in condiment manufacturing.
2. **Batch consistency monitoring** of fruit purées, jams, and baby foods in fruit-processing industries.
3. **Process optimization** of pumping, filling, and packaging operations by ensuring the product has a pourable yet non-dripping consistency.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List any four limitations of the Bostwick Consistometer as a rheological measurement tool.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. It is an **empirical, single-point measurement** and does not provide fundamental rheological parameters (such as the full flow curve, viscoelastic moduli, or yield stress).
2. The **shear rate is non-uniform** and ill-defined in the trough, so the result cannot be directly converted to a true viscosity.
3. The measurement is highly **temperature- and density-dependent**, requiring strict temperature control and density reporting.
4. It is **unsuitable for very low-viscosity fluids** (such as milk, broth, juice) and for **highly structured gels**, limiting its range of applicability.

*(Award 0.75 mark per correct limitation, 3 marks total.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Error 1:** Students often confuse the Bostwick with the **Falling Ball / Brookfield viscometer**. The Bostwick is **gravity-driven, free-surface, empirical** — it does NOT measure a true shear rate.
> - **Common Error 2:** Students forget to **state the assumptions** (Newtonian, isothermal, no-slip) when deriving the apparent-viscosity formula. Up to **2 marks** are routinely lost here.
> - **Common Error 3:** Failing to mention that the Bostwick value is **inversely related to viscosity** (higher BD = thinner sample) — this conceptual error costs **1 mark**.
> - **Common Error 4:** Not reporting the test **temperature and time** along with the Bostwick value. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this omission.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **construction and working principle** of the Bostwick Consistometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the apparent viscosity** of a Newtonian food fluid in the Bostwick trough and discuss its **limitations** for non-Newtonian food systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The Bostwick Consistometer consists of the following components:
1. **Rectangular stainless-steel trough** of length **25 cm**, width **5 cm**, and depth about **2 cm**, with the floor engraved with a graduated scale from 0 to 25 cm in 0.5 cm divisions.
2. **Spring-loaded gate** at one end, which separates a small **sample reservoir** (about 100 mL capacity) from the main trough.
3. **Leveling feet** and a **spirit bubble level** to ensure the trough is perfectly horizontal.
4. **Stopwatch** (external) and a **sample scraper/leveling tool** for cleaning and sample preparation.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
1. The instrument is leveled using the bubble level.
2. The gate is closed, and a **100 mL sample**, pre-equilibrated to **20 °C**, is poured into the reservoir.
3. After a 30 s thermal equilibration, the gate is released and the stopwatch started simultaneously.
4. The sample flows under gravity along the trough; the flow is driven by the **hydrostatic head** of the sample and resisted by the **viscous and yield-stress response** of the food.
5. At $t = 30$ s, the gate is closed and the **maximum distance traveled by the leading edge** is read on the scale — this is the **Bostwick value** in **cm/30 s**.

---

**(b) Derivation of Apparent Viscosity and Discussion of Limitations (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from the lubrication-approximation momentum balance for a Newtonian film of depth $h(x,t)$ in a rectangular trough:

$$\tau(y) = \rho\, g\, (h - y)\, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

Integrating the Newtonian constitutive equation $\tau = \eta\, \dot\gamma$ across the depth to obtain the volumetric flow rate per unit width:

$$q = -\dfrac{h^3}{3\eta}\, \rho\, g\, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$

Applying mass conservation in the rectangular trough and integrating the resulting PDE from the initial depth $h_0$ to the leading-edge position $L = BD$ over the time $t = 30$ s:

$$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^{2}\cdot t}{2 \cdot BD^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{app}\;=\;\dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^{2}\cdot 30}{2 \cdot BD^{2}}\;}$$

**Assumptions:** Newtonian behavior, isothermal flow, no-slip at the trough wall, rectangular cross-section, and the lubrication approximation ($h \ll L$).

**Limitations for non-Newtonian foods (3 marks):**
1. **Shear-thinning foods** (ketchup, purées) show a viscosity that decreases with shear rate; the Bostwick experiences a non-uniform shear rate, so the inferred $\eta_{app}$ is a single weighted average and not a material property.
2. **Yield-stress fluids** (mustard, tomato paste) will not flow below a critical $\sigma_y$, making the Bostwick reading highly sensitive to the initial head $h_0$ and density $\rho$.
3. **Viscoelastic foods** (some dairy desserts, gelled products) may exhibit recoil or die-swell after gate release, which the Bostwick cannot capture.
4. **Time-dependent foods** (thixotropic or rheopectic) will give a Bostwick value that depends on the operator's delay between pouring and gate release, reducing reproducibility.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the **factors affecting the Bostwick Distance** of a food material. Discuss the role of **temperature, density, and yield stress**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Bostwick Consistometer with the **Brookfield Rotational Viscometer** and the **Falling Ball Viscometer** in terms of principle, applicability, and limitations for food systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors Affecting Bostwick Distance (7 marks):**

1. **Temperature (2 marks):** The Bostwick Distance is **highly temperature-sensitive** because viscosity follows an Arrhenius-type dependence:
   $$\eta(T) = \eta_0\, \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$$
   A 5 °C rise in temperature can increase BD by 30–50 % for many sauces. Hence strict temperature control at **20 °C** is mandatory.

2. **Density (2 marks):** The driving hydrostatic pressure is $\rho g h$. A denser food (e.g., heavy syrup) generates a larger driving force and travels further, **even at identical viscosity**. Bostwick values must always be reported with the sample density for valid comparison.

3. **Yield Stress (2 marks):** For yield-stress foods described by the **Bingham** or **Herschel–Bulkley** model, the sample will not flow measurably if:
   $$\rho\, g\, h\, \dfrac{dh}{dx} < \sigma_y$$
   A small increase in $\sigma_y$ (e.g., from starch retrogradation or pectin gelation) can dramatically reduce BD, making Bostwick a **sensitive probe of structural build-up** in stored foods.

4. **Sample volume and gate-release timing (1 mark):** Variations in the poured volume or in the delay between pouring and gate release introduce operator-dependent errors of 5–15 %.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Bostwick Consistometer | Brookfield Rotational Viscometer | Falling Ball Viscometer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Gravity-driven free-surface flow distance | Rotational shear between spindle and cup | Time for a ball to fall through the fluid under gravity |
| **Measurement** | Distance in cm/30 s (empirical) | Torque → viscosity (Pa·s) | Fall time → kinematic viscosity (cSt) |
| **Shear rate** | Non-uniform, ill-defined | Well-defined, controllable | Variable, but typically low |
| **Viscosity range** | Medium-to-high (≈ 1–1000 Pa·s) | Wide (0.01–10⁶ Pa·s with appropriate spindle) | Low-to-medium (1–500 cSt typical) |
| **Sample volume** | ~100 mL | 0.5–500 mL depending on spindle | 50–100 mL |
| **Rheological info** | Single-point consistency index | Full flow curve, thixotropy, yield stress, viscoelasticity (with oscillatory mode) | Single-point kinematic viscosity |
| **Food examples** | Ketchup, sauces, purées, jams | Chocolate, yogurt, melted cheese, dough | Milk, juices, oils, clear broths |
| **Cost** | Low (~₹20,000–50,000) | High (~₹3,00,000–10,00,000) | Low–moderate |
| **Limitations** | Empirical, single-point, not for low-viscosity fluids | Requires calibration, spindle selection, time-consuming | Not for opaque or particulate fluids; ball sedimentation in non-Newtonian fluids is poorly defined |
| **Best for** | **QC line tests** in food factories | **R&D and fundamental rheology** | **Newtonian low-viscosity clear fluids** |

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bostwick Consistometer**

- ✅ The Bostwick Consistometer is an **empirical, gravity-driven** instrument — **not** a fundamental rheometer.
