- ✅ Result is reported as **Bostwick Distance (BD) in cm / 30 s** at a standard **20 °C**.
- ✅ **Higher BD = thinner / more flowable** food; **Lower BD = thicker / more viscous** food.
- ✅ Trough dimensions: **25 cm long × 5 cm wide**, with a **spring-loaded gate** and **bubble level**.
- ✅ Sample volume: **~100 mL**; pre-equilibrated for **30 s** before gate release.
- ✅ Driving force: **hydrostatic head** $\rho g h$; Resisting force: **viscous + yield-stress** response.
- ✅ Apparent viscosity (Newtonian estimate):
  $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\rho\, g\, h_0^{2}\cdot t}{2 \cdot BD^{2}}$$
- ✅ **Bostwick vs. Brookfield:** Bostwick is empirical + cheap + fast; Brookfield is fundamental + expensive + versatile.
- ✅ **Bostwick is unsuitable for:** very thin fluids (use Ford/Zahn cup), very thick gels (use penetrometer), and foods with large particles (use a tubular viscometer).
- ✅ BD is **temperature-, density-, and yield-stress-dependent** — always report test conditions.
- ✅ The instrument is a **quality-control workhorse** in condiment, sauce, and baby-food manufacturing worldwide.
- ✅ When deriving equations, **always state assumptions** (Newtonian, isothermal, no-slip, lubrication) to earn full KTU marks.
- ✅ **ASTM F1080** and **CODEX CXS** standards reference the Bostwick method for tomato-product consistency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure-Driven Flow Viscometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure-Driven Flow Viscometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Pressure-Driven Flow Viscometer** (also known as a **Capillary Rheometer** or **Tube Viscometer**) is a class of rheometric instruments in which a test fluid is forced through a precision-bored cylindrical die (capillary) of known radius $R$ and length $L$ under a controlled pressure drop $\Delta P$, enabling the determination of viscosity and flow behavior from the resulting flow rate $Q$. The fundamental physical law governing this measurement is the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**, which describes fully developed, laminar, axisymmetric flow of an incompressible fluid in a circular duct.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure-driven viscometers are the most commonly used instruments for characterizing food fluids in industrial pipelines (e.g., milk, fruit juices, sauces, chocolate) because the geometry exactly mimics real processing pipelines.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are squeezing a thick toothpaste tube. The harder you press (more pressure), the faster the paste comes out. Now imagine several toothpaste tubes connected end-to-end:
- A **shorter tube** requires higher pressure for the same flow rate (friction acts over less length).
- A **narrower tube** dramatically restricts flow (radius enters the equation to the **fourth power**).
- A **thicker paste** requires much more pressure.

A capillary rheometer is essentially a highly engineered version of this. We know the tube dimensions precisely, we set or measure the pressure drop, and we measure the output flow rate. From these three known quantities, we calculate the fluid's resistance to flow (viscosity).

### Working Principle Summary
1. Fluid is held in a reservoir (barrel) at controlled temperature.
2. A piston or pressurized gas applies force, generating pressure $P_0$.
3. Fluid flows through a capillary die of radius $R$ and length $L$ to atmosphere ($P_L$).
4. Pressure drop $\Delta P = P_0 - P_L$ is measured (often via a pressure transducer upstream).
5. Volumetric flow rate $Q$ is measured (mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ divided by density $\rho$).

### Key Physical Quantities
- **Apparent Viscosity ($\eta_a$):** measured viscosity at a specific shear rate, in **Pa·s** or **mPa·s**.
- **Wall Shear Stress ($\tau_w$):** the shear stress at the tube wall, in **Pa**.
- **Apparent Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}_a$):** the shear rate at the wall assuming Newtonian behavior, in **s⁻¹**.
- **Standard SI units:** pressure in **Pa**, length in **m**, flow rate in **m³/s**, radius in **m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for Exams:** A capillary rheometer operates on the principle that for **fully developed laminar flow** in a tube, the shear stress varies linearly from zero at the centerline to a maximum at the wall, and the flow rate is a function of the pressure gradient, tube geometry, and fluid viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Hagen-Poiseuille Law

For steady, laminar, isothermal, incompressible flow of a **Newtonian fluid** through a straight circular tube, a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ gives:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L$$

The negative sign arises because the shear stress on the cylinder surface acts opposite to the flow direction. Solving for the shear stress distribution gives the **fundamental pressure-driven flow relationship**:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

This reveals three critical facts:
- The shear stress is **zero at the centerline** ($r = 0$).
- The shear stress is **maximum at the wall** ($r = R$), giving the **Wall Shear Stress** $\tau_w$.
- The shear stress varies **linearly** with radial position.

### 2.2 The Wall Shear Stress Equation

Substituting $r = R$:

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2L}}$$

This equation is **exact for any fluid (Newtonian or non-Newtonian)** as long as the flow is laminar and fully developed — it is derived purely from a momentum balance, not from any constitutive assumption.

### 2.3 Velocity Profile and Flow Rate for a Newtonian Fluid

For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$ (the minus sign because velocity decreases with increasing $r$). Integrating the constitutive equation with the no-slip boundary condition $v_z(R) = 0$ gives the classic **parabolic (Poiseuille) velocity profile**:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

The volumetric flow rate is obtained by integrating the velocity profile over the tube cross-section:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

This is the famous **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**.

### 2.4 Apparent Shear Rate and Apparent Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, differentiating $v_z(r)$ gives the shear rate distribution:

$$\dot{\gamma}(r) = -\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

At the wall ($r = R$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2\mu L} = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu}$$

Eliminating $\Delta P$ between the flow rate and wall shear rate equations yields the **Apparent Shear Rate**:

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}}$$

The **Apparent Viscosity** (often mistakenly called simply "viscosity" for non-Newtonian fluids) is defined as:

$$\boxed{\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a}}$$

### 2.5 The Rabinowitsch Correction (Non-Newtonian Fluids)

For a **non-Newtonian fluid**, the parabolic velocity profile is no longer valid. The relationship between $Q$ and $\tau_w$ is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^3}{\tau_w^3} \int_0^{\tau_w} \tau^2 \dot{\gamma}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Applying **Leibniz's rule** and differentiation yields the **Rabinowitsch-Mooney Equation** for the true wall shear rate:

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left[\frac{3n' + 1}{4n'}\right]}$$

where:

$$n' = \frac{d(\ln \tau_w)}{d(\ln \dot{\gamma}_a)}$$

- For a Newtonian fluid: $n' = 1$, giving $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a$.
- For a **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** fluid: $n' < 1$.
- For a **shear-thickening (dilatant)** fluid: $n' > 1$.

### 2.6 The Bagley Correction (End Effects)

In real capillaries, fluid experiences additional pressure losses at the **entry** (contraction from barrel to capillary) and **exit** (re-expansion) regions due to:
- **Extensional (elongational) flow** at the contraction.
- **Viscous dissipation** as streamlines re-form.
- **Recoil effects** at the exit.

These "end effects" mean the measured $\Delta P$ is not entirely consumed by viscous shear in the capillary. The corrected wall shear stress using the **Bagley correction factor** $e$ is:

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P}{2\left(\frac{L}{R} + e\right)}}$$

The Bagley correction is determined experimentally by plotting $\Delta P$ versus $L/R$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}_a$ for several dies of different lengths. The line is extrapolated to $\Delta P = 0$; the negative $L/R$ intercept is $e$.

### 2.7 The Mooney Equation (Multiple Capillaries)

When dies of the **same $L/R$ ratio** but different absolute radii are used, end effects are equivalent. The Mooney equation allows determination of true wall shear rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^3}{1} \int \cdots$$

In practice, the Mooney-Rabinowitsch analysis uses a plot of $\log Q$ vs $\log \Delta P$ to identify the power-law index.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure-driven viscometers are indispensable in:
- **Food industry:** Quality control of ketchup, chocolate, yogurt, molten sugar, and sauces.
- **Polymer processing:** Determining melt flow index (MFI) and extrudate swell.
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Characterizing ointments and syrups.
- **Cosmetics:** Toothpaste, lotions, creams.
- **Pipeline design:** Calculating pumping power requirements for food fluid transport.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Equation** | **Applicability** | **SI Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2L}$ | Any fluid (Newtonian/non-Newtonian) | Pa |
| Hagen-Poiseuille Flow | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Newtonian only | m³/s |
| Apparent Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Geometric (always valid) | s⁻¹ |
| Apparent Viscosity | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$ | Definition | Pa·s |
| Rabinowitsch True Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left[\dfrac{3n' + 1}{4n'}\right]$ | Non-Newtonian (general) | s⁻¹ |
| Bagley-Corrected Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\left(\frac{L}{R} + e\right)}$ | With end correction | Pa |
| Power-Law Index from Data | $n' = \dfrac{d(\ln \tau_w)}{d(\ln \dot{\gamma}_a)}$ | Local slope of log-log plot | dimensionless |
| Shear Stress Distribution | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | Fully developed laminar flow | Pa |
| Maximum Velocity (Newtonian) | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$ | Newtonian | m/s |
| Mean Velocity (Newtonian) | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{2}$ | Newtonian | m/s |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation from a Force Balance

**Step 1 — Define the control volume.**
Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$, coaxial with the capillary tube. The fluid flows in the $+z$ direction.

**Step 2 — Apply a momentum force balance.**
The pressure force on the upstream face is $P_0 \cdot \pi r^2$. The pressure force on the downstream face is $P_L \cdot \pi r^2$. The shear force on the cylindrical surface resists motion in the $+z$ direction and equals $\tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L$.

For steady-state (zero net force):

$$P_0 \cdot \pi r^2 - P_L \cdot \pi r^2 - \tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the equation.**
Dividing through by $\pi r^2$:

$$(P_0 - P_L) - \frac{2\tau(r) L}{r} = 0$$

Defining $\Delta P = P_0 - P_L$:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constitutive equation for a Newtonian fluid.**

$$\tau(r) = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

Equating:

$$-\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with the no-slip boundary condition $v_z(R) = 0$.**

$$\int_{v_z}^{0} dv_z = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{r}^{R} r' \, dr'$$

$$-v_z = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2 - r^2}{2}$$

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Step 6 — Integrate the velocity profile to get the flow rate.**

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Derivation of the Rabinowitsch Correction

**Step 1 — General integral for volumetric flow rate of a generalized fluid.**
For any fluid with constitutive equation $\dot{\gamma} = f(\tau)$, the flow rate through a tube is:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Since $v_z(r) = \int_r^R \dot{\gamma}(r') \, dr'$, and using the substitution $\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ so that $r = \frac{2L\tau}{\Delta P}$ and $dr = \frac{2L}{\Delta P} d\tau$:

$$Q = \frac{8\pi L^2}{\Delta P^3} \int_0^{\tau_w} \tau^2 \dot{\gamma}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $\tau_w$.**

$$\frac{dQ}{d\tau_w} = \frac{8\pi L^2}{\Delta P^3} \cdot \tau_w^2 \cdot \dot{\gamma}(\tau_w)$$

**Step 3 — Express $\dot{\gamma}(\tau_w)$ in terms of $Q$ and $\tau_w$.**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{\Delta P^3}{8\pi L^2 \tau_w^2} \cdot \frac{dQ}{d\tau_w}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce apparent shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q/(\pi R^3)$ and use $R = 2L\tau_w/\Delta P$.**

$$R^3 = \frac{8L^3 \tau_w^3}{\Delta P^3}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4Q \Delta P^3}{\pi \cdot 8 L^3 \tau_w^3} = \frac{Q \Delta P^3}{2\pi L^3 \tau_w^3}$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio $\dot{\gamma}_w / \dot{\gamma}_a$.**

$$\frac{\dot{\gamma}_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{1}{4 \tau_w^3 / Q \cdot dQ/d\tau_w} = \frac{1}{3 + \frac{d\ln Q}{d\ln \tau_w}}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize that $\frac{d\ln Q}{d\ln \tau_w} = \frac{1}{n'}$.**

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left[\frac{3n' + 1}{4n'}\right]}$$

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Board Exam

| **Feature** | **Capillary Rheometer** | **Rotational Rheometer** | **Falling Ball / Falling Needle** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Pressure drop $\Delta P$ | Torque $M$ | Gravity |
| Measured quantity | Flow rate $Q$ | Angular velocity $\Omega$ | Terminal velocity $v_t$ |
| Best for | High shear rates (10²–10⁶ s⁻¹) | Low-to-moderate shear rates | Very viscous fluids |
| Sample volume | Small–moderate | Small | Moderate |
| Wall slip correction | Difficult | Easy (vane geometries) | N/A |
| End effects | Major (needs Bagley) | None (Couette geometries) | Minor |
| Food applications | Pumping, extrusion | Structure build-up, thixotropy | Honey, syrups |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Schematic of a Capillary (Pressure-Driven) Rheometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pressurized Gas Supply or Servo-Hydraulic Piston] --> B[Reservoir Barrel containing Test Fluid]
    B --> C[Pressure Transducer P0]
    C --> D[Capillary Die of Radius R and Length L]
    D --> E[Atmospheric Exit PL]
    E --> F[Collection Vessel on Balance]
    F --> G[Mass Flow Rate m dot Logger]
    G --> H[Compute Q = m dot divided by density]
    C --> I[Compute Delta P = P0 - PL]
    H --> J[Calculate tau_w and gamma dot_a]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply Rabinowitsch and Bagley Corrections]
    K --> L[Plot Viscosity Curve]
```

### Diagram 2: Flow Profile Inside the Capillary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Capillary Cross Section]
        C1[Centerline r = 0] -->|"tau = 0, v = v_max"| C2[Linear shear stress growth]
        C2 --> C3[Wall r = R, tau = tau_w, v = 0]
    end
    subgraph S2[Shear Stress Distribution]
        T1[tau axis] -->|"Linear increase with r"| T2[tau = tau_w at wall]
    end
    subgraph S3[Velocity Profile Newtonian]
        V1[Parabolic Poiseuille profile] --> V2[v_max at center]
    end
```

### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Data Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[Raw Measurements: Delta P, Q, L, R, T] --> bagley[Bagley Correction Step]
    bagley --> stress[Compute Corrected Wall Shear Stress tau_w]
    raw --> apparent[Compute Apparent Shear Rate gamma dot_a]
    apparent --> rabino[Rabinowitsch Correction]
    stress --> rabino
    rabino --> truewall[True Wall Shear Rate gamma dot_w]
    stress --> visc[Compute True Viscosity eta = tau_w divided by gamma dot_w]
    truewall --> visc
    visc --> plot[Generate Flow Curve: eta versus gamma dot_w]
```

### Diagram 4: Bagley Plot Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    xaxis[L divided by R on X axis] --> plot[Linear Plot of Delta P versus L divided by R at constant gamma dot_a]
    plot --> extrap[Extrapolate Line to Delta P = 0]
    extrap --> bag[Negative X intercept equals Bagley Correction e]
```

### Diagram 5: Rabinowitsch Plot Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    logx[log gamma dot_a on X axis] --> logy[log tau_w on Y axis]
    logy --> slope[Local Slope at each Point equals n prime]
    slope --> corr[Plug n prime into Rabinowitsch Equation]
    corr --> true[True Wall Shear Rate Obtained]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Apparent Shear Rate" as applied to capillary rheometry. State its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer:**
The apparent shear rate is the shear rate calculated at the wall of a capillary assuming the fluid is Newtonian, using only the measured volumetric flow rate and the tube radius.

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$

It is "apparent" because it equals the true wall shear rate only for Newtonian fluids. For non-Newtonian fluids, the true wall shear rate differs and must be obtained using the Rabinowitsch correction.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the Bagley correction and why is it needed in capillary rheometry?**

**Model Answer:**
The Bagley correction accounts for the additional pressure losses that occur at the entry and exit of a capillary die, which are not associated with fully developed viscous shear flow in the die itself.

These losses arise due to:
1. Extensional flow at the contraction from the barrel into the capillary.
2. Viscous dissipation as streamlines re-form after the contraction.
3. Recoil effects at the exit.

Without correction, the wall shear stress computed as $\tau_w = R\Delta P / (2L)$ would overestimate the true shear stress because $\Delta P$ includes energy dissipation in regions other than the capillary.

The corrected expression is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P}{2\left(\frac{L}{R} + e\right)}$$

where $e$ is the Bagley correction factor, determined experimentally by extrapolating a plot of $\Delta P$ versus $L/R$ (at constant $\dot{\gamma}_a$) to zero pressure drop.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students often forget to include units in numerical answers — always state **Pa·s**, **s⁻¹**, **Pa**, **m³/s** explicitly.
> 2. A common error is applying the Newtonian formula $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a$ to a non-Newtonian fluid without invoking the Rabinowitsch correction.
> 3. The Bagley correction is often skipped — examiners specifically test whether students can write the corrected wall shear stress equation.
> 4. In logarithmic plots, students frequently confuse $\log_{10}$ with $\ln$; for the Rabinowitsch index $n'$, the natural logarithm is implied unless otherwise stated.
> 5. Many students forget to convert radius from **mm to m** and pressure from **kPa to Pa** before substituting into SI equations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for the laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular capillary tube. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tomato concentrate flows through a capillary rheometer with radius $R = 1.5 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 100 \text{ mm}$. The pressure drop is $\Delta P = 50 \text{ kPa}$ and the measured volumetric flow rate is $Q = 8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Assuming the concentrate behaves as a Newtonian fluid in this test, calculate: **(i)** the wall shear stress, **(ii)** the apparent shear rate, and **(iii)** the apparent viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
*(See "Derivation 1" in Section 3 for the complete derivation.)*

**Key assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow.
2. Laminar flow regime ($\text{Re} < 2100$).
3. Incompressible fluid.
4. Isothermal conditions.
5. Fully developed velocity profile (no entrance effects — alternatively corrected by Bagley).
6. Newtonian constitutive behavior: $\tau = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$.
7. No-slip at the wall: $v_z(R) = 0$.
8. Axisymmetric, purely axial flow.

**Final Result:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
$R = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
$\Delta P = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
$Q = 8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**(i) Wall Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2L} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(50 \times 10^3)}{2(0.1)}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{75}{0.2} = 375 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Apparent Shear Rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{4(8 \times 10^{-8})}{\pi (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \cdot 3.375 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{1.0603 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_a \approx 30.18 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Apparent Viscosity:**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{375}{30.18} \approx 12.42 \text{ Pa·s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the operational principle of a capillary rheometer. List the major sources of measurement error and the corrections applied. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a non-Newtonian food fluid tested in a capillary rheometer at three flow rates, the following data are obtained:

| $Q$ (m³/s) | $\Delta P$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| $1 \times 10^{-7}$ | $2 \times 10^{4}$ |
| $3 \times 10^{-7}$ | $4.5 \times 10^{4}$ |
| $9 \times 10^{-7}$ | $9 \times 10^{4}$ |

The capillary has $R = 1 \text{ mm}$ and $L = 50 \text{ mm}$. Bagley correction $e = 0.5$. Determine: **(i)** the Rabinowitsch index $n'$, **(ii)** the corrected wall shear stress at the highest flow rate, and **(iii)** the true wall shear rate at the highest flow rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A capillary rheometer consists of:
1. **Barrel/Reservoir:** Holds the test fluid at controlled temperature.
2. **Piston or pressurization system:** Generates pressure drop $\Delta P$.
3. **Capillary die:** Precision-bored tube of radius $R$ and length $L$.
4. **Pressure transducer:** Measures upstream pressure $P_0$.
5. **Flow measurement system:** Collects extrudate on a balance or uses a flow meter.

**Operational Principle:**
The fluid is forced through the capillary under a known pressure drop. After steady-state is achieved, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is measured. From these, $\tau_w$ and $\dot{\gamma}_a$ are computed using the formulas given in Section 2.

**Major Sources of Error and Corrections:**

| **Source** | **Correction** |
|---|---|
| End effects (entry/exit pressure losses) | **Bagley correction** ($e$ factor) |
| Non-Newtonian velocity profile assumption | **Rabinowitsch correction** ($n'$ factor) |
| Wall slip | Use multiple dies or Mooney technique |
| Kinetic energy effects | Negligible for viscous food fluids; otherwise, use kinetic correction |
| Viscous heating (at high shear rates) | Use adiabatic / isothermal corrections |
| Pressure hole errors | Use flush-mounted transducers |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $R = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $L = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $e = 0.5$.

**(i) Rabinowitsch index $n'$:**

Compute $\dot{\gamma}_a$ and $\tau_w$ (uncorrected) for each point:

For $Q_1 = 1 \times 10^{-7}$:
$\dot{\gamma}_{a1} = \dfrac{4(1 \times 10^{-7})}{\pi (10^{-3})^3} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 10^{-9}} = 127.32 \text{ s}^{-1}$

$\tau_{w1} = \dfrac{R \Delta P_1}{2L} = \dfrac{(10^{-3})(2 \times 10^4)}{2(0.05)} = \dfrac{20}{0.1} = 200 \text{ Pa}$

For $Q_3 = 9 \times 10^{-7}$:
$\dot{\gamma}_{a3} = \dfrac{4(9 \times 10^{-7})}{\pi (10^{-3})^3} = 1145.9 \text{ s}^{-1}$

$\tau_{w3} = \dfrac{(10^{-3})(9 \times 10^4)}{0.1} = 900 \text{ Pa}$

Slope on log-log plot:

$$n' = \frac{\log(\tau_3/\tau_1)}{\log(\dot{\gamma}_{a3}/\dot{\gamma}_{a1})} = \frac{\log(900/200)}{\log(1145.9/127.32)} = \frac{\log(4.5)}{\log(9)}$$

$$n' = \frac{0.6532}{0.9542} = 0.685$$

**(ii) Corrected wall shear stress at the highest flow rate:**

$$\tau_{w,\text{corr}} = \frac{\Delta P_3}{2\left(\frac{L}{R} + e\right)} = \frac{9 \times 10^4}{2\left(\frac{0.05}{0.001} + 0.5\right)} = \frac{90000}{2(50.5)} = \frac{90000}{101}$$

$$\tau_{w,\text{corr}} \approx 891.09 \text{ Pa}$$

**(iii) True wall shear rate at the highest flow rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_{a3} \left[\frac{3n' + 1}{4n'}\right] = 1145.9 \left[\frac{3(0.685) + 1}{4(0.685)}\right]$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = 1145.9 \left[\frac{3.055}{2.740}\right] = 1145.9 \times 1.1150$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w \approx 1277.7 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**True Viscosity:**
$\eta = \tau_{w,\text{corr}} / \dot{\gamma}_w = 891.09 / 1277.7 \approx 0.697 \text{ Pa·s}$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capillary rheometer** measures viscosity by forcing fluid through a tube under known $\Delta P$ and measuring $Q$.
- The **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$ is the foundation — valid only for Newtonian fluids.
- The **wall shear stress** $\tau_w = R \Delta P / (2L)$ is **exact for any fluid** because it comes from a momentum balance, not a constitutive assumption.
- The **apparent shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q / (\pi R^3)$ assumes Newtonian flow and is purely geometric.
- The **Bagley correction** addresses entry and exit pressure losses; use multiple $L/R$ dies to determine $e$.
- The **Rabinowitsch correction** converts apparent shear rate to true wall shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids: $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a (3n' + 1)/(4n')$.
- The **Rabinowitsch index** $n' = d(\ln \tau_w)/d(\ln \dot{\gamma}_a)$ is the local slope of a log-log flow curve.
- For a **Newtonian fluid**, $n' = 1$ and the Rabinowitsch correction reduces to $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a$.
- For **shear-thinning fluids**, $n' < 1$, so $\dot{\gamma}_w > \dot{\gamma}_a$ (the Newtonian formula underestimates the true shear rate).
- For **shear-thickening fluids**, $n' > 1$, so $\dot{\gamma}_w < \dot{\gamma}_a$.
- The shear stress varies **linearly** with radial position: $\tau(r) = \Delta P \cdot r / (2L)$.
- The velocity profile for a Newtonian fluid is **parabolic**, with maximum at the centerline and zero at the wall.
- **Mean velocity** is exactly **half** the maximum velocity for Newtonian flow.
- Wall slip, viscous heating, and kinetic energy effects are additional corrections beyond Bagley and Rabinowitsch.
- Capillary rheometers excel at **high shear rates** ($10^2$–$10^6 \text{ s}^{-1}$) typical of food processing.
- Always convert **mm → m**, **kPa → Pa**, and report answers with explicit **SI units**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capillary Tubes and Falling Sphere Type
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capillary Tubes and Falling Sphere Type

# Capillary Tubes and Falling Sphere Viscometers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Capillary Tube Viscometers

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A *Capillary Tube Viscometer* is a flow-through instrument used to determine the **absolute or kinematic viscosity** of a Newtonian fluid by measuring the time taken for a fixed volume of the liquid to flow under gravity through a precisely manufactured narrow-bore capillary tube of known length and radius. The operating principle is rooted in the **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** for laminar, steady, fully-developed flow of an incompressible fluid in a cylindrical tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Capillary viscometers are the **primary reference instruments** for Newtonian food fluids like milk, fruit juices, edible oils, honey, and sucrose solutions. They are classified as *absolute viscometers* (giving viscosity in Pa·s directly) or *kinematic viscometers* (giving mm²/s).

**Common Industrial & Laboratory Variants:**
1. **Ostwald Viscometer** – Simple U-tube design, fixed volume, semi-micro.
2. **Ubbelohde Viscometer** – Suspended-level design, volume-independent (used for dilute polymer solutions and serums).
3. **Cannon-Fenske Viscometer** – Reverse-flow design, used for opaque fluids like crude oil, dark syrups.
4. **Ostwald-Cannon-Fenske Routine Viscometer** – Standardized for industrial QC.

### 1.2 Falling Sphere Viscometer

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A *Falling Sphere Viscometer* (most commonly the **Höppler Viscometer**) determines the **dynamic viscosity** of a fluid by timing the terminal velocity of a calibrated solid sphere falling under gravity through the test liquid contained in an inclined (typically 80° to vertical) precision-bore glass tube. The principle is governed by **Stokes' Law** for a sphere moving through a viscous medium under creeping-flow (Re < 1) conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 10° tilt from vertical (or 80° from horizontal in some designs) in the Höppler viscometer is engineered to **eliminate wall effects** and ensure the sphere travels along the tube axis without erratic lateral motion, providing highly accurate readings for viscous food systems such as chocolate, ketchup, and molten fats.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Capillary Tube Analogy — "The Straw Test":**
Imagine drinking a thick milkshake through a thin coffee stirrer straw. The narrower the straw and the longer the path, the harder (more pressure) it takes to pull liquid through. A capillary viscometer works on the same idea: by precisely controlling the tube dimensions and the driving head of liquid, the *resistance to flow* is mathematically linked to *viscosity*. Faster-flowing honey = lower viscosity measurement; slow-flowing treacle = higher reading.

**Falling Sphere Analogy — "The Marble in Honey Jar":**
Drop a steel ball bearing into a jar of cooking oil versus a jar of cold honey. In oil, the ball drops quickly; in honey, it sinks lazily. The time taken to fall a fixed distance is a direct measure of how "thick" the fluid is. The Höppler viscometer formalizes this everyday observation into a precise laboratory instrument, but the tube is tilted to mimic a "rolling" descent that prevents the ball from grazing the walls.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** *g* = **9.80665 m/s²**
- **Density of water at 20 °C:** ρ_w = **998.2 kg/m³**
- **Viscosity of water at 20 °C:** η_w = **1.002 mPa·s**
- **Laminar flow criterion (capillary):** Reynolds number **Re < 2100**
- **Laminar flow criterion (falling sphere):** Reynolds number **Re < 1** (Stokes regime)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capillary Tube Viscometer — Operational Logic

The capillary viscometer works on the following stepwise physical sequence:

1. A fixed volume *V* of test liquid is introduced into the upper reservoir bulb of the U-tube.
2. The liquid is drawn up (or allowed to flow down) past the upper etched mark *M₁*.
3. The fluid flows under its own hydrostatic head (gravity) through the precision capillary of length *L* and radius *R*.
4. The time *t* required for the meniscus to travel from upper mark *M₁* to lower mark *M₂* is measured using a stopwatch (resolution ±0.1 s).
5. The viscosity is computed by relating flow rate *Q = V/t* to the pressure drop via the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**.

**The Core 'Why':**
- A *narrower* capillary (smaller *R*) increases flow resistance enormously (∝ *R*⁴), amplifying sensitivity to small viscosity differences.
- A *longer* capillary increases the frictional surface area, again increasing measurable pressure drop.
- The use of a constant-temperature bath is **non-negotiable** because food fluids are highly temperature-sensitive (viscosity of oil halves for every ~10 °C rise).

### 2.2 Falling Sphere (Höppler) Viscometer — Operational Logic

1. A precision glass sphere of known radius *r* and density *ρ_s* is loaded into the inclined tube filled with the test fluid.
2. The sphere is allowed to fall (or rolled) through the fluid.
3. The time *t* for the sphere to traverse the calibrated distance *L* between two etched marks is recorded.
4. At terminal velocity, **three forces balance**: gravitational pull (down), buoyant force (up), and viscous drag (up).
5. Viscosity is computed via the **Stokes Equation** (with the Höppler geometric correction).

**The Core 'Why':**
- A *denser* sphere (e.g., steel) is used for low-viscosity fluids; *lighter* spheres (glass, hollow) for high-viscosity foods.
- The **inclination angle (10°–80°)** keeps the sphere in contact-free axial motion, removing *Couette wall effects*.
- The instrument is calibrated with a *reference fluid* of known viscosity (often **0.5%–100% glycerol solutions** or **NIST traceable oils**) to compensate for non-idealities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Application | Standard Units (SI) |
|---|----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** — pressure drop for laminar flow in a tube of length *L* and radius *R* | Pa |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | Volumetric flow rate in capillary | m³/s |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$ | **Viscosity from capillary (absolute form)** | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\eta_{1}}{\eta_{2}} = \dfrac{\rho_{1} \, t_{1}}{\rho_{2} \, t_{2}}$ | **Comparative method** using reference fluid (eliminates need to know *R*, *L* exactly) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | **Kinematic viscosity** definition | m²/s |
| 6 | $F_{D} = 6 \pi \eta r v$ | **Stokes' Drag Force** on a sphere in creeping flow | N |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{2 \, g \, r^{2} (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 \, v}$ | **Viscosity from falling sphere** (Stokes' Law, *v* = terminal velocity = *L/t*) | Pa·s |
| 8 | $v_{t} = \dfrac{2 \, g \, r^{2} (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 \, \eta}$ | **Terminal velocity** of falling sphere | m/s |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{2 r \rho_{f} v}{\eta} < 1$ | **Stokes regime validity check** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{w} = 1 - 2.104(d/D) + 2.09(d/D)^{3} - 0.95(d/D)^{5}$ | **Faxén Wall Correction** (for falling sphere viscometer, *d* = sphere dia, *D* = tube dia) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{corr} = \dfrac{\eta_{measured}}{C_{w}}$ | Wall-corrected viscosity | Pa·s |
| 12 | $Re_{cap} = \dfrac{2 R \rho \bar{v}}{\eta} < 2100$ | **Laminar flow check** for capillary | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For capillary viscometers, the *comparative method* (Equation 4) is **most commonly asked** in 14-mark derivations. For falling sphere, the **Faxén correction** (Equation 10) is frequently tested in Part A (3 marks).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** Capillary viscometers calibrate milk UHT processes and cream standardization.
- **Edible Oil Refining:** Falling ball viscometers monitor oil viscosity for hydrogenation control.
- **Chocolate Manufacture:** Falling sphere methods verify cocoa butter viscosity during tempering.
- **Beverage Quality Control:** Ostwald viscometers measure sugar syrup Brix conversions.
- **Pharma/Food Hyphenation:** Both types serve as **on-line CIP (Clean-in-Place) validation tools**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a cylindrical element of fluid of radius *r* and length *L* flowing under a pressure difference Δ*P* in a capillary of radius *R*.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Cylindrical Fluid Element:**
The pressure force pushing the cylinder forward equals the viscous shear force resisting it. The pressure force acts on the cross-sectional area *πr²*:

$$
F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2}
$$

The shear force acts on the lateral surface *2πrL* through shear stress *τ*:

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \cdot 2 \pi r L
$$

**Step 2 — Equating Forces at Steady State (no acceleration):**

$$
\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \tau \cdot 2 \pi r L
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Local Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Applying Newton's Law of Viscosity (τ = η · γ̇):**
For a Newtonian fluid in laminar tube flow, the velocity gradient is $\dot{\gamma} = -\dfrac{dv}{dr}$:

$$
-\eta \dfrac{dv}{dr} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2L}
$$

**Step 5 — Separating Variables and Integrating (no-slip at r = R, max velocity at r = 0):**

$$
\int_{v}^{0} dv = - \int_{r}^{R} \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 \eta L} dr
$$

$$
v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

This is the classic **parabolic Hagen-Poiseuille velocity profile**.

**Step 6 — Integrating Across the Cross-Section to Get the Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \left[ \dfrac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \eta L}
$$

**Final Form — Hagen-Poiseuille Equation:**

$$
\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}}
$$

**Step 7 — Re-arranging to Solve for Viscosity in the Viscometer Experiment:**
The flow rate equals the measured volume *V* divided by the efflux time *t*:

$$
Q = \dfrac{V}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stokes' Law for Falling Sphere Viscometer

**Starting Premise:** A sphere of radius *r* and density *ρ_s* falls through a fluid of density *ρ_f* and viscosity *η*. Three forces act: gravity (down), buoyancy (up), and viscous drag (up).

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram and Force Identification:**

$$
F_{gravity} = \rho_{s} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \cdot g
$$

$$
F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{f} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \cdot g
$$

$$
F_{drag} = 6 \pi \eta r v \quad \text{(Stokes' drag, creeping flow)}
$$

**Step 2 — At Terminal Velocity, Net Force = 0:**

$$
F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} - F_{drag} = 0
$$

$$
\tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} g \left( \rho_{s} - \rho_{f} \right) = 6 \pi \eta r v_{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Terminal Velocity:**

$$
v_{t} = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g \left( \rho_{s} - \rho_{f} \right)}{9 \eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Inverting to Solve for Viscosity:**
Measured efflux time *t* corresponds to *v_t = L/t*:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{2 \, r^{2} \, g \, t \, (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 L}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Faxén Wall Correction

When the sphere is close to the tube wall (*d/D > 0.05*), the drag force is modified. Faxén (1924) showed:

$$
F_{drag,\,wall} = \dfrac{F_{drag,\,Stokes}}{C_{w}}
$$

where *C_w* is the Faxén correction factor:

$$
C_{w} = 1 - 2.104 \left( \dfrac{d}{D} \right) + 2.09 \left( \dfrac{d}{D} \right)^{3} - 0.95 \left( \dfrac{d}{D} \right)^{5}
$$

The corrected viscosity becomes:

$$
\eta_{true} = \eta_{measured} \cdot C_{w}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Module 3, 7-Mark Sub-Question):**
>
> An Ostwald viscometer has a capillary of diameter 0.5 mm and length 100 mm. The efflux time for 5 mL of oil (density 920 kg/m³) is 280 s, while for water (density 998 kg/m³) at the same temperature it is 45 s. The viscosity of water at this temperature is 1.00 mPa·s. Calculate the viscosity of the oil in Pa·s.

**Solution Using Comparative Method (Equation 4):**

$$
\dfrac{\eta_{oil}}{\eta_{water}} = \dfrac{\rho_{oil} \cdot t_{oil}}{\rho_{water} \cdot t_{water}}
$$

$$
\eta_{oil} = \eta_{water} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{oil} \cdot t_{oil}}{\rho_{water} \cdot t_{water}} = 1.00 \times \dfrac{920 \times 280}{998 \times 45}
$$

$$
\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{257600}{44910} = 5.736 \; \text{mPa·s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{oil} \approx 5.74 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** This is within the typical range of edible oils at 25 °C (5–100 mPa·s). ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capillary (Ostwald) Viscometer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean Viscometer with Chromic Acid] --> B[Dry with Acetone and Air Stream]
    B --> C[Mount in Constant Temp Bath 20C]
    C --> D[Pipette 5 mL of Reference Fluid Water]
    D --> E[Draw Liquid Above Upper Mark M1]
    E --> F[Release and Start Stopwatch]
    F --> G[Stop at Lower Mark M2 Record t_ref]
    G --> H[Repeat 3 Times Average t_ref]
    H --> I[Clean and Dry Viscometer]
    I --> J[Pipette 5 mL of Test Fluid Food Oil]
    J --> K[Repeat Efflux Measurement t_test]
    K --> L[Compute eta_test via Comparative Equation]
    L --> M[Output Viscosity Pa s]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Falling Sphere Höppler Viscometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Charge Calibration Sphere into Tube] --> B[Fill Tube with Test Fluid]
    B --> C[Equilibrate 10 min in Bath at T]
    C --> D[Invert Tube and Lock at 80 deg Tilt]
    D --> E[Sphere Falls Past Upper Mark M1]
    E --> F[Start Timer]
    F --> G[Sphere Falls Past Lower Mark M2]
    G --> H[Stop Timer Record t]
    H --> I[Compute Terminal Velocity L over t]
    I --> J[Apply Stokes Equation to Solve for eta]
    J --> K[Apply Faxen Wall Correction Cw]
    K --> L[Output Corrected Dynamic Viscosity]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Viscometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Fluid is Newtonian?} -->|Yes| CapTest{Flow Time less than 200 s?}
    Start -->|No| FSVTest[Use Falling Sphere or Cone Plate]
    CapTest -->|Yes| Cap1[Use Ostwald or Cannon Fenske]
    CapTest -->|No| Cap2[Use Reverse Flow Cannon Fenske]
    FSVTest -->|Viscosity 1 to 1000 Pa s| FSV1[Use Hoppler Falling Sphere]
    FSVTest -->|Viscosity greater than 1000 Pa s| FSV2[Use Rotational Viscometer]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Force-Balance Free-Body Diagram (Falling Sphere)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Fg[Weight Fg = 4/3 pi r cubed rho_s g] --> NetForce[Net Force at Terminal Velocity = 0]
    Fb[Buoyancy Fb = 4/3 pi r cubed rho_f g] --> NetForce
    Fd[Stokes Drag Fd = 6 pi eta r v] --> NetForce
    NetForce --> Result[eta = 2 g r squared rho_s minus rho_f divided by 9 v]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Stokes' Law as applied to the falling sphere viscometer. List the assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**
Stokes' Law states that the viscous drag force *F_D* experienced by a sphere of radius *r* moving with velocity *v* through a viscous medium is given by:
$$F_D = 6 \pi \eta r v$$
Assumptions: (1) Sphere is rigid and smooth, (2) Fluid is Newtonian, (3) Flow is in the creeping/laminar regime (*Re* < 1), (4) Fluid is unbounded (infinite medium — corrected by Faxén factor in practice), (5) No-slip at the sphere surface, (6) Incompressible fluid.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. What is the maximum Reynolds number for the flow to remain laminar in a capillary?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hagen-Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate *Q* of a Newtonian fluid in laminar flow through a circular tube:
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$
The maximum Reynolds number for laminar flow in a smooth pipe is **Re ≈ 2100**. Above this, transition to turbulent flow occurs.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation starting from a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element in a capillary. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**(b) An Ubbelohde viscometer has a capillary radius of 0.25 mm. A 10 mL sample of glycerol solution takes 320 s to flow between the marks. The same volume of water takes 55 s at 20 °C. Given η_water = 1.00 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, ρ_glycerol = 1260 kg/m³, ρ_water = 998 kg/m³, calculate the viscosity of the glycerol solution. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** [See full step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1 above. Marks are awarded as: force balance 2, integration 2, parabolic profile 1, final Hagen-Poiseuille 1, assumptions 1.]

**(b)** Apply the comparative method:

$$
\eta_{glycerol} = \eta_{water} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{glycerol} \cdot t_{glycerol}}{\rho_{water} \cdot t_{water}}
$$

$$
\eta_{glycerol} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{1260 \times 320}{998 \times 55}
$$

$$
\eta_{glycerol} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{403200}{54890} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.346
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{glycerol} = 7.35 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s} = 7.35 \; \text{mPa·s}}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a Höppler falling sphere viscometer. Explain how Stokes' equation is applied to determine viscosity. (7 marks)**

**(b) A steel ball (ρ_s = 7850 kg/m³, d = 3 mm) falls through a sample of corn syrup in a falling sphere viscometer at 25 °C. The ball traverses 100 mm in 18.5 s. The density of syrup is 1380 kg/m³. Ignoring wall corrections, calculate the viscosity of the syrup. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** The Höppler viscometer consists of a precision-bore glass tube inclined at an angle of 10°–80° from vertical, surrounded by a water jacket for temperature control. A calibrated sphere (steel, glass, or hollow) is loaded into the top. The tube is filled with the test fluid, and after thermal equilibration, the sphere is allowed to roll down. The time to traverse the calibrated distance between two etched marks is recorded. From Equation (7):
$$\eta = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g t (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 L}$$
where *r* = 1.5 mm = 0.0015 m, *L* = 0.1 m, *t* = 18.5 s.

**(b)** Substituting:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{2 \times (0.0015)^{2} \times 9.81 \times 18.5 \times (7850 - 1380)}{9 \times 0.1}
$$

Numerator: $2 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 18.5 \times 6470$
$= 2 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 18.5 \times 6470$

Step-by-step:
$9.81 \times 18.5 = 181.485$
$181.485 \times 6470 = 1{,}174{,}208$
$2 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$
Numerator $= 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1{,}174{,}208 = 5.284$

Denominator: $9 \times 0.1 = 0.9$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{5.284}{0.9} \approx 5.87 \; \text{Pa·s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{syrup} \approx 5.87 \; \text{Pa·s}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting density DIFFERENCE** (ρ_s − ρ_f), not just ρ_s. Many students write 7850 instead of 6470.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** *r* must be in meters (convert 1.5 mm → 0.0015 m).
> 3. **Ignoring Reynolds check:** Always verify *Re* < 1. Here, *v* = 0.1/18.5 = 0.0054 m/s, *Re* = (2 × 0.0015 × 1380 × 0.0054) / 5.87 ≈ 0.0038 ✓
> 4. **Wall correction skipped:** For *d/D* > 0.05, Faxén correction is essential.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capillary viscometers** use the **Hagen-Poiseuille Law**; they are best for *Newtonian, low-to-medium viscosity* food fluids.
- **Falling sphere viscometers** use **Stokes' Law**; best for *opaque, high-viscosity* foods (syrups, chocolate).
- The **comparative method** ($\eta_1/\eta_2 = \rho_1 t_1 / \rho_2 t_2$) is the **KTU-preferred** way to avoid absolute instrument calibration.
- **Temperature control is critical:** For most food oils, a 1 °C error can change viscosity by 5–10%.
- **Validity windows:** Capillary *Re* < 2100; Falling sphere *Re* < 1.
- **Wall correction (Faxén)** is mandatory when sphere diameter > 5% of tube diameter.
- **Capillary equation is R⁴ sensitive:** A 2× error in *R* = 16× error in η.
- **Stokes' equation is r² sensitive:** A 2× error in *r* = 4× error in η.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille velocity profile** is **parabolic** — a frequently-asked diagram in KTU boards.
- **Units to memorize:** Pa·s (dynamic), m²/s or cSt (kinematic), poiseuille = Pa·s, 1 cP = 1 mPa·s.
- **Assumptions checklist for derivations:** Steady, laminar, incompressible, isothermal, Newtonian, no-slip, fully-developed flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Gelometer for Solid Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsion Gelometer for Solid Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Torsion Gelometer** is a precision mechanical testing instrument used in food rheology to determine the **gel strength** (or *breaking strength*) of solid and semi-solid food gels by applying a controlled **torsional (twisting) load** to a cylindrical gel specimen until structural failure occurs. The instrument quantifies the resistance of the gel network to shear-induced fracture, providing a direct measure of the cohesive energy density of the three-dimensional biopolymer matrix (e.g., gelatin, agar, pectin, starch, or polysaccharide gels).

The fundamental measured output is the **angle of twist** (in degrees) required to fracture the gel under a known applied torque, from which the **gel strength** (in gram-cm or N·m) is computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The torsion gelometer is classified under *empirical rheological methods* for *solid foods*. It is distinct from the **Bloom Gelometer** (plunger penetration) and from oscillatory rheometry (sinusoidal small-strain testing). The torsion method is specifically suited for **brittle, elastic-dominant food gels** with low plastic deformation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you insert a thin metal wire into the top center of a firm fruit jelly (like a gummy candy sitting on a plate). Now imagine you twist the wire — like turning a key. At first, the jelly resists. But the more you twist, the more the internal sugar–protein network is stressed. Eventually, at some critical twist, the jelly **cracks and shears apart**. 

The amount of twist needed to break it tells you how "strong" the gel is. A weak jelly (like over-diluted gelatin) breaks after just a small twist. A firm jelly (like a Turkish delight or a high-Bloom gelatin) requires much more twist before it gives way.

**The torsion gelometer automates this twisting experiment** with a fixed, known force and a precisely measured twist angle — giving you a reproducible, scientific "toughness number" instead of a finger-feel estimate.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard gel specimen geometry:** Right circular cylinder, typically **12.5 mm diameter × 25 mm length** (or 1 cm × 2 cm depending on the model).
- **Applied force range:** Typically **10 g to 1000 g** via calibrated weight pans.
- **Twist range:** **0° to 360°**, with graduation precision of **1°**.
- **Standard units of gel strength:** **gram-centimeter (g·cm)** or equivalently **dyne-centimeter (dyne·cm)** in CGS.
- **Torsion wire material:** Hardened spring steel with calibrated torsional constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Gel Strength (GS):** The product of the applied torque and the angular displacement at fracture. It represents the *work* required to break the gel per unit volume. A higher GS implies a stiffer, more cross-linked gel network.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Instrument Construction — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The torsion gelometer consists of the following structural elements:

1. **Base Plate & Vertical Stand:** Provides a rigid, vibration-isolated mounting frame.
2. **Sample Clamp (Lower Collet):** A fixed jaw that rigidly grips the bottom of the cylindrical gel specimen, preventing slippage and base rotation.
3. **Torsion Wire (Upper Collet + Wire):** A calibrated steel wire attached to the top of the specimen. The wire transmits torsional moment from the loading system to the gel.
4. **Loading Pulley / Drum:** A horizontal circular disc (radius *r*) fixed coaxially to the torsion wire. A string is wound around the drum, and a known weight (W) is suspended from the string's free end.
5. **Weight Pan & Calibrated Masses:** A set of standard weights (typically 10 g, 20 g, 50 g, 100 g, 200 g, 500 g) used to apply a known gravitational force to the pulley.
6. **Protractor Scale:** A graduated angular scale (0° to 360°) concentric with the torsion wire. A pointer attached to the rotating wire indicates the instantaneous twist angle.
7. **Sample Temperature Jacket (optional):** A water-jacketed sleeve for thermostatic control at temperatures like **10 °C** (standard for gelatin) or **25 °C**.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The torsion gelometer operates on **Newton's Third Law of rotational mechanics**: an applied torque on a rigid wire produces a proportional angular twist governed by the wire's torsional stiffness, and this twist is transmitted to the attached gel specimen.

**Operational Sequence (Why and How):**

- **Step 1 — Specimen Mounting:** *Why:* We need a fixed reference frame. *How:* The cylindrical gel is gripped at its base by the lower collet. The upper collet grips the top surface of the gel, and is mechanically connected to the torsion wire.
- **Step 2 — Zero Reference:** *Why:* To eliminate pre-load artifacts. *How:* The protractor pointer is set to 0° before the weight is applied. The torsion wire is in its unstrained, relaxed state.
- **Step 3 — Application of Load:** *Why:* To introduce a known torque. *How:* A calibrated weight *W* is gently placed on the pan. Gravity pulls the pan down, the string unwinds from the pulley, and the pulley rotates, applying a torque to the wire.
- **Step 4 — Stress Transfer to Gel:** *Why:* The gel must experience shear stress, not the wire alone. *How:* The torsion wire transmits its twist to the upper collet, which shears the top surface of the gel relative to the fixed bottom. This creates a **pure shear deformation field** along the gel's vertical axis.
- **Step 5 — Angular Measurement at Fracture:** *Why:* The failure point defines gel strength. *How:* As the wire twists, the gel's internal network accumulates elastic strain energy. At a critical twist angle **θ_crit**, the gel fractures (visible crack appears, or load drops sharply). The pointer reading at this moment is recorded.
- **Step 6 — Gel Strength Calculation:** *Why:* To convert mechanical readings into a standardized material property. *How:* Apply the gel strength formula (see Section 2.3) using the known *W*, *r*, and measured *θ_crit*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Torsion (and not Compression or Tension)?** Solid food gels like gelatin are **brittle and weak in tension** but exhibit **higher cohesive strength in shear**. Torsion loading creates a predominantly shear stress field at the fracture plane, which more closely mimics the mastication (chewing) action of teeth on a brittle gel. It also avoids edge-crushing artifacts seen in uniaxial compression.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Standard Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|--------|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Applied weight | $W$ | — | grams (g) or Newtons (N) | Calibrated dead-weight load |
| 2 | Pulley radius (moment arm) | $r$ | — | cm or m | Lever arm of the string on the drum |
| 3 | Applied torque | $T$ | $T = W \cdot r$ | g·cm or N·m | Rotational moment delivered to the wire |
| 4 | Twist angle at fracture | $\theta$ | measured | degrees (°), converted to radians | Angular shear deformation at break |
| 5 | **Gel Strength (GS)** | $GS$ | $GS = T \cdot \theta = W \cdot r \cdot \theta$ | **g·cm·deg** or N·m·rad | Energy-based toughness metric |
| 6 | Shear stress at fracture | $\tau_f$ | $\tau_f = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$ | Pa or g/cm² | Stress on outer fiber of cylinder |
| 7 | Shear strain at fracture | $\gamma_f$ | $\gamma_f = \dfrac{r_g \, \theta}{L}$ | dimensionless | Engineering shear strain |
| 8 | Modulus of rigidity (rigidity modulus) | $G$ | $G = \dfrac{\tau_f}{\gamma_f}$ | Pa | Elastic shear stiffness of the gel |
| 9 | Gel strength in CGS (common form) | $GS_{CGS}$ | $GS = W \cdot \theta$ | g·deg | Simplified form assuming unit arm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** In many KTU textbooks, the gelometer's pulley arm is normalized to a unit radius (1 cm), so the formula simplifies to $GS = W \cdot \theta$ in units of **g·degree**. Always confirm the specific instrument calibration before writing the exam answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Quality control in gelatin production:** Each batch of food-grade gelatin is graded by its gel strength (a "250 Bloom" gelatin has a characteristic torsion/firmness value).
- **Pectin gel characterization:** Used in jam, jelly, and confectionery manufacture to optimize sugar-acid-pectin ratios.
- **Starch gel retrogradation studies:** Tracks firmness changes during storage of bread, noodles, and puddings.
- **Hydrocolloid development (xanthan, carrageenan, alginate):** Provides a quick, low-cost alternative to full rheometer testing.
- **Textural comparison of tofu, paneer, cheese curds:** Determines optimal coagulation endpoints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gel Strength from First Principles of Torsion

We begin from the **fundamental torque equation of rotational statics** applied to the gelometer's pulley system, then connect it to the **gel specimen's shear failure criterion**.

---

#### Derivation A — Applied Torque on the Gel

**Step 1: Define the force delivered by the hanging weight.**

A mass $m$ suspended under gravity $g$ produces a downward force $F = m \cdot g$. However, in the **gram-force convention** (standard for gelometer data sheets), we use weight $W$ directly in grams, treating gram-force as the unit.

$$
F = W \quad [\text{gram-force}]
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the geometric lever arm.**

The string is wound around a cylindrical drum of radius $r$ (the moment arm). The torque produced about the drum's central axis is the product of the tangential force and the moment arm.

$$
T = F \cdot r
$$

Substituting $F = W$:

$$
T = W \cdot r
$$

**Step 3: Interpret $T$ in physical terms.**

$T$ is the moment of force (torque) transmitted by the string to the pulley, which is rigidly coupled to the torsion wire, which in turn drives the upper collet of the gel. The torque $T$ is the **rotational analog of a force**: it is the cause of angular deformation.

---

#### Derivation B — Angular Twist and the Gel's Shear Strain

**Step 4: Define angular displacement.**

As the weight falls and unwinds the string, the pulley rotates through an angle $\theta$. This is the **angle of twist** in radians (or degrees, with conversion factor $\pi/180$).

$$
\theta \quad [\text{rad or deg}]
$$

**Step 5: Relate the twist to shear strain in the cylindrical gel.**

For a solid cylinder of radius $r_g$ and length $L$ fixed at its base, with the top surface rotated by $\theta$ radians, the **engineering shear strain** at the outer fiber is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r_g \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

This is the classical formula for torsion of a circular shaft, derived by recognizing that the top face displaces by an arc length $s = r_g \cdot \theta$ relative to the fixed bottom, and shear strain is the ratio of this arc to the cylinder's length $L$.

---

#### Derivation C — Critical Shear Stress at Fracture

**Step 6: Apply the torsion formula for circular shafts.**

For a solid circular cross-section, the **maximum shear stress** (occurring at the outer fiber) under torque $T$ is given by the polar section modulus relation:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_g}{J}
$$

where $J = \dfrac{\pi \, r_g^4}{2}$ is the polar moment of inertia of a solid circle.

**Step 7: Substitute $J$ and simplify.**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_g}{\pi r_g^4 / 2} = \frac{2 \, T}{\pi \, r_g^3}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of diameter $d = 2 r_g$:

$$
\tau_f = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}
$$

This is the **fracture shear stress** $\tau_f$ — the maximum stress the gel network can sustain before its cross-linked structure fails.

---

#### Derivation D — Modulus of Rigidity (Gel Stiffness)

**Step 8: Apply Hooke's Law in shear.**

Within the elastic limit, shear stress is proportional to shear strain:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma
$$

**Step 9: Substitute the fracture values to find the failure modulus.**

$$
G = \frac{\tau_f}{\gamma_f} = \frac{2 \, T / (\pi r_g^3)}{r_g \theta / L} = \frac{2 \, T \, L}{\pi \, r_g^4 \, \theta}
$$

This $G$ is the **modulus of rigidity** (shear modulus) of the gel, an intrinsic material property. For a 10% gelatin gel at 10 °C, typical $G$ values are in the range **1–10 kPa**.

---

#### Derivation E — Master Equation for Gel Strength

**Step 10: Combine $T$ and $\theta$ into a single material metric.**

The product $T \cdot \theta$ has units of energy (force × distance, in rotational form). It represents the **torsional energy absorbed** by the gel up to the point of fracture. This is defined as the **Gel Strength (GS)**:

$$
\boxed{GS = T \cdot \theta = W \cdot r \cdot \theta}
$$

**Step 11: Simplification to the common KTU form.**

Most torsion gelometers are designed with $r = 1\,\text{cm}$ (a normalized moment arm). In that case:

$$
\boxed{GS = W \cdot \theta \quad [\text{g·deg}]}
$$

This is the **most frequently tested form** in KTU University Exams.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 10% gelatin gel cylinder (diameter $d = 1.25\,\text{cm}$, length $L = 2.5\,\text{cm}$) is tested in a torsion gelometer with pulley radius $r = 1.0\,\text{cm}$. The applied weight is $W = 50\,\text{g}$, and the gel fractures at a twist angle of $\theta = 80°$. Calculate: (a) the gel strength, (b) the shear stress at fracture, (c) the shear strain at fracture, and (d) the modulus of rigidity.

**Solution:**

**(a) Gel Strength**

$$
GS = W \cdot r \cdot \theta = 50 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 80 = 4000 \;\text{g·cm·deg}
$$

**(b) Shear stress at fracture** (using $d = 1.25\,\text{cm}$):

$$
\tau_f = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3} = \frac{16 \cdot (50 \cdot 1.0)}{\pi \cdot (1.25)^3} = \frac{800}{\pi \cdot 1.953125}
$$

$$
\tau_f = \frac{800}{6.1359} \approx 130.4 \;\text{g/cm}^2
$$

**(c) Shear strain at fracture** (using $r_g = d/2 = 0.625\,\text{cm}$ and $L = 2.5\,\text{cm}$):

$$
\gamma_f = \frac{r_g \cdot \theta}{L} = \frac{0.625 \cdot 80}{2.5} = 20.0 \;\text{(dimensionless, if } \theta \text{ in deg)}
$$

*(Note: For rigorous SI consistency, convert $\theta = 80° \times \pi/180 = 1.396\,\text{rad}$, giving $\gamma_f = 0.349\,\text{rad}$.)*

**(d) Modulus of rigidity**

$$
G = \frac{\tau_f}{\gamma_f} = \frac{130.4}{20.0} = 6.52 \;\text{g/cm}^2 \text{ per deg}
$$

Or in SI, $G \approx 6.52 \cdot g / (\pi/180) \approx 3.74 \times 10^3\,\text{Pa} = 3.74\,\text{kPa}$, which is in the realistic range for a 10% gelatin gel.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Interdependencies

| Parameter | Increases When | Effect on GS | Effect on $G$ | Effect on $\tau_f$ | Effect on $\gamma_f$ |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Weight $W$ ↑ | Stiffer gel, more cross-links | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ≈ constant |
| Twist angle $\theta$ ↑ | More compliant, ductile gel | ↑ | ↓ | ≈ constant | ↑ |
| Gel concentration ↑ | Denser polymer network | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Temperature ↑ | Weaker gel (melting/softening) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Cross-linker (e.g., Ca²⁺ for pectin) ↑ | Stronger ionic bridges | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| pH deviation from isoelectric point | Altered protein folding | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The torsion gelometer provides *one number* (GS), but through the derived formulas, it can be decomposed into a **stress** and a **strain**, and then a **modulus**. This makes the simple torsion test a remarkably rich characterization tool — a major reason it appears in KTU Module 3 and in industry-grade QC laboratories.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Torsion Gelometer

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional subsystems, signal/data flow, and physical force chain of a torsion gelometer. This is a **block-level functional architecture**, chosen over a literal physical drawing to clearly represent the cause-effect-energy chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dead Weight W] -->|Gravitational Force F| B[Loading Pulley Drum]
    B -->|Torque T = W*r| C[Calibrated Torsion Wire]
    C -->|Transmits Angular Twist| D[Upper Collet Gel Clamp]
    D -->|Applies Shear Stress| E[Cylindrical Gel Specimen]
    F[Lower Collet] -->|Rigid Base Fixity| E
    E -->|Resists via Network Strength| G[Elastic Shear Strain Energy]
    G -->|Critical Point Reached| H[Gel Fracture Event]
    H -->|Pointer Stops Moving| I[Protractor Scale Reading]
    I -->|Angle theta_crit| J[Data Acquisition]
    B -->|Known r and W| J
    J -->|Apply GS = W*r*theta| K[Gel Strength Output]
    J -->|Apply tau_f = 16T / pi d^3| L[Fracture Shear Stress]
    J -->|Apply G = tau_f / gamma_f| M[Modulus of Rigidity]
    K --> N[KTU Final Report]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Force, Energy, and Information Flow — Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mechanical[Mechanical Force Chain]
        M1[Weight W] --> M2[Pulley Torque T]
        M2 --> M3[Wire Twist]
        M3 --> M4[Gel Shear]
    end
    subgraph Material[Material Response]
        Mat1[Elastic Deformation] --> Mat2[Strain Energy Build-up]
        Mat2 --> Mat3[Crack Initiation]
        Mat3 --> Mat4[Fracture]
    end
    subgraph Information[Information Output]
        Info1[Pointer Angle theta] --> Info2[Gel Strength GS]
        Info1 --> Info3[Stress tau_f]
        Info1 --> Info4[Modulus G]
    end
    M4 --> Mat1
    Mat4 --> Info1
```

### 4.3 Operational Decision Flowchart (Test Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Prepare Gel Cylinder] --> S2[Mount in Lower Collet]
    S2 --> S3[Attach Upper Collet to Torsion Wire]
    S3 --> S4[Set Protractor to Zero]
    S4 --> S5{Select Calibrated Weight W}
    S5 --> S6[Apply Weight Gently]
    S6 --> S7[Observe Pointer Rotation]
    S7 --> S8{Gel Fractured?}
    S8 -->|No| S7
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Record Twist Angle theta_crit]
    S9 --> S10[Compute GS = W * r * theta]
    S10 --> S11[Repeat in Triplicate]
    S11 --> S12[Compute Mean and Std Dev]
    S12 --> S13[Report with Confidence Interval]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a torsion gelometer. Mention its primary application in food quality control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **torsion gelometer** is a mechanical instrument used in food rheology to determine the **gel strength** of solid and semi-solid food materials by applying a controlled **torsional (twisting) load** to a cylindrical gel specimen and measuring the **angle of twist at fracture**.

**Primary Application:** It is widely used in the **quality control of gelatin, pectin, agar, starch, and polysaccharide-based gels** in the food industry. Specifically, it is used to grade food-grade gelatin batches, optimize pectin formulations for jams and jellies, monitor starch retrogradation in bakery and pasta products, and characterize hydrocolloid performance during product development. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**
**List any three differences between the torsion gelometer and the Bloom gelometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Torsion Gelometer | Bloom Gelometer |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Loading mode | Torsional (twisting) load | Compressive (plunger penetration) |
| Measured output | Angle of twist at fracture (θ) | Depth of penetration (mm) |
| Specimen stress state | Pure shear | Combined shear and compression |
| Sensitivity to surface defects | Moderate | High (plunger tip is sensitive) |
| Sample size | 12.5 mm × 25 mm cylinder | Standardized jelly in a Bloom bottle |
| Best suited for | Brittle, elastic gels | Soft to medium-firm gels |

(3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference with comparison)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a torsion gelometer** used for solid foods. Mention the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **gel strength (GS)** of a food gel in terms of the applied weight $W$, pulley radius $r$, and twist angle $\theta$. A 12% agar gel (cylinder of $d = 1.0\,\text{cm}$, $L = 2.0\,\text{cm}$) is tested in a torsion gelometer with $r = 1.0\,\text{cm}$ and $W = 30\,\text{g}$. The gel fractures at $\theta = 120°$. Calculate the **gel strength**, **shear stress at fracture**, and **modulus of rigidity**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Torsion Gelometer (7 marks):**

The torsion gelometer consists of the following key components:

1. **Vertical stand and base plate** — provides rigid support.
2. **Lower collet (base clamp)** — rigidly holds the bottom of the gel cylinder.
3. **Cylindrical gel specimen** — the test sample.
4. **Upper collet** — grips the top of the gel, attached to a torsion wire.
5. **Calibrated torsion wire** — transmits twist from pulley to gel.
6. **Loading pulley** — a drum of radius $r$ around which a string is wound.
7. **Weight pan and standard masses** — apply known dead-weight load $W$.
8. **Protractor scale** — measures angle $\theta$ of twist.

**Working Principle (Stepwise):**

- The gel cylinder is mounted between the lower and upper collets.
- A known weight $W$ is suspended from the string wound on the pulley.
- The weight causes a torque $T = W \cdot r$ on the torsion wire.
- The torsion wire twists by an angle $\theta$, transmitting shear to the gel.
- The top of the gel rotates relative to the fixed bottom, generating a shear stress field.
- The gel stores elastic strain energy in its polymer network.
- At a critical twist angle $\theta_c$, the network fractures, and the pointer stops moving.
- The reading $\theta_c$ is recorded, and gel strength is calculated as $GS = W \cdot r \cdot \theta_c$.

(7 marks — 2 for diagram description, 3 for component roles, 2 for working sequence)

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**

The applied torque on the gel via the pulley is:

$$
T = W \cdot r
$$

The angular twist at fracture is $\theta$. The product $T \cdot \theta$ represents the energy absorbed by the gel up to fracture, defined as the **gel strength**:

$$
GS = T \cdot \theta = W \cdot r \cdot \theta
$$

For a normalized gelometer with $r = 1\,\text{cm}$:

$$
GS = W \cdot \theta
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $W = 30\,\text{g}$, $r = 1.0\,\text{cm}$, $\theta = 120°$, $d = 1.0\,\text{cm}$, $L = 2.0\,\text{cm}$.

**(i) Gel Strength:**

$$
GS = W \cdot r \cdot \theta = 30 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 120 = 3600 \;\text{g·cm·deg}
$$

**(ii) Shear stress at fracture:**

First compute torque:

$$
T = W \cdot r = 30 \cdot 1.0 = 30 \;\text{g·cm}
$$

Then:

$$
\tau_f = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3} = \frac{16 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot (1.0)^3} = \frac{480}{\pi} \approx 152.8 \;\text{g/cm}^2
$$

**(iii) Modulus of rigidity:**

Convert $\theta$ to radians:

$$
\theta = 120° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \approx 2.094 \;\text{rad}
$$

Shear strain:

$$
\gamma_f = \frac{r_g \cdot \theta}{L} = \frac{0.5 \cdot 2.094}{2.0} \approx 0.524
$$

Modulus of rigidity:

$$
G = \frac{\tau_f}{\gamma_f} = \frac{152.8}{0.524} \approx 291.6 \;\text{g/cm}^2
$$

Converting to SI ($1\,\text{g/cm}^2 = 98.07\,\text{Pa}$):

$$
G \approx 291.6 \cdot 98.07 \approx 2.86 \times 10^4 \,\text{Pa} = 28.6\,\text{kPa}
$$

(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 5 for stepwise numerical evaluation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of torsion testing** in food gels. Why is torsion preferred over uniaxial compression for brittle food gels like gelatin? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the gel strength** measured by a torsion gelometer. How do (i) gel concentration, (ii) temperature, (iii) pH, and (iv) cross-linking ions influence the GS value? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Principle of Torsion Testing (7 marks):**

**Principle:** A torsion gelometer applies a known **torsional moment (torque)** $T$ to a cylindrical gel specimen fixed at its base. The top of the gel is rotated by the torsion wire, creating a **shear stress** in the gel that increases with the applied twist angle. When the **critical shear stress** of the gel's polymer network is exceeded, the gel fractures. The **angle of twist at fracture** is measured, and from $T$ and $\theta$, the gel strength is computed.

**Why torsion is preferred for brittle gels:**

1. **Stress state compatibility:** Brittle gels (gelatin, agar, pectin) are weak in tension and prone to edge-cracking under compression. Torsion produces a **pure shear stress field** that better characterizes the cohesive strength of the network.
2. **No edge artifacts:** Uniaxial compression concentrates stress at the contact platens, causing premature fracture. Torsion distributes stress uniformly along the cylindrical surface.
3. **Closer to mastication:** Chewing produces shearing and tearing, not pure compression. Torsion more closely simulates the **oral processing** of brittle gels.
4. **Wide measurable range:** By varying the weight and torque, a torsion gelometer can measure a broad range of gel strengths — from very soft (e.g., 2% agar) to very firm (e.g., 15% high-Bloom gelatin).
5. **Reproducibility:** The dead-weight loading and protractor scale give highly reproducible readings, suitable for **industrial QC**.

(7 marks — 3 for principle, 4 for justification over compression)

**(b) Factors Affecting Gel Strength (7 marks):**

**(i) Gel concentration ↑** → **GS increases** (markedly). Higher polymer concentration means more chain entanglements, more junction zones, and a denser 3D network. Example: doubling gelatin from 5% to 10% can increase GS by **3–5×**.

**(ii) Temperature ↑** → **GS decreases**. Heat disrupts hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic associations that stabilize the gel network. Near the gel-sol transition temperature, GS drops sharply to zero. Standard torsion tests on gelatin are done at **10 °C** to ensure reproducibility.

**(iii) pH deviation from the isoelectric point (pI)** → **GS decreases** (for protein gels). At pI, proteins have minimum net charge, maximum attractive interactions, and strongest gels. Moving away from pI increases electrostatic repulsion, weakening the network.

**(iv) Cross-linking ions (e.g., Ca²⁺ for alginate/pectin, K⁺ for carrageenan)** → **GS increases** with ion concentration up to an optimum. The "egg-box" model of alginate gels requires Ca²⁺ to bridge guluronate blocks. Beyond the optimum, excess ions can cause **syneresis** (water expulsion) and brittleness, slightly reducing GS.

Other factors include: gel aging time (post-setting), cooling rate, presence of sugars (raise gel strength up to a point), and mechanical history (shear prior to testing can break the incipient network).

(7 marks — 1.5 marks per factor with scientific justification)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Torsion Gelometer Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion trap (–2 marks typical):** Many students write $GS = W \cdot \theta$ without specifying that this assumes $r = 1\,\text{cm}$. Always state the assumption or write the full $GS = W \cdot r \cdot \theta$ form to be safe.
> 
> 2. **Degree vs. radian error (–1 to –2 marks):** When computing $\gamma_f$ or $G$, you **must** convert $\theta$ to radians. Using degrees directly in $\gamma = r_g \theta / L$ gives a numerically wrong strain and a $G$ value off by a factor of **~57.3** ($180/\pi$).
> 
> 3. **Confusing $GS$ with $G$ (–2 marks):** Gel strength ($GS$) is *energy-like* (g·cm·deg or g·deg), while modulus of rigidity ($G$) is a *stress/strain ratio* (Pa or g/cm²). Do not interchange them in the final answer.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the polar moment of inertia in $\tau_f$ (–2 marks):** The fracture shear stress uses the polar section modulus of a **solid circular cross-section**: $J = \pi d^4 / 32$, leading to $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$. Writing $\tau = T/A$ (axial) is wrong and loses easy marks.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the diagram description in 7-mark questions (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for **neat block diagram** + component roles. A diagram with at least 6 labeled components is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Torsion gelometer** = mechanical instrument that measures **gel strength** by twisting a cylindrical gel until fracture.
- ✅ **Primary measured output:** Angle of twist at fracture, $\theta_{crit}$ (degrees or radians).
- ✅ **Primary calculated output:** Gel strength $GS = W \cdot r \cdot \theta$ in **g·cm·deg** (or simplified to $GS = W \cdot \theta$ if $r = 1\,\text{cm}$).
- ✅ **Fracture shear stress:** $\tau_f = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$ for a solid cylinder.
- ✅ **Shear strain in cylinder:** $\gamma_f = \dfrac{r_g \cdot \theta_{rad}}{L}$.
- ✅ **Modulus of rigidity:** $G = \tau_f / \gamma_f$ in Pa or g/cm².
- ✅ **Torsion > Compression for brittle gels** because torsion avoids edge-crushing and produces pure shear.
- ✅ **Standard specimen:** 12.5 mm diameter × 25 mm length cylinder (check instrument manual).
- ✅ **Standard temperature:** 10 °C for gelatin-based gels.
- ✅ **GS increases** with: gel concentration ↑, cross-linker ↑, cooling time ↑, sugar (to optimum), closeness to pI for proteins.
- ✅ **GS decreases** with: temperature ↑, deviation from pI, prolonged storage (syneresis), excessive shear pre-treatment.
- ✅ **Common applications:** QC of gelatin, pectin, agar, starch, alginate, carrageenan, and protein-based food gels.
- ✅ **Always convert degrees → radians** when computing strain or modulus. Always **state the pulley radius** assumption when using the simplified GS formula.
