- ✅ **Distinguish:** $GS$ (energy-like, g·deg) vs. $G$ (modulus, Pa) vs. $\tau_f$ (stress, Pa or g/cm²) vs. $\gamma_f$ (dimensionless).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Texture of Foods: Dough Testing Instruments
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Texture of Foods: Dough Testing Instruments

# Texture of Foods: Dough Testing Instruments

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dough Texture (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Dough texture refers to the **composite mechanical and rheological response** of a hydrated flour–protein–starch matrix under applied force, deformation, or flow. It is a multi-parameter attribute encompassing hardness, extensibility, elasticity, adhesiveness, and viscoelasticity, which collectively govern gas retention, machinability, and final product quality of baked goods.

**Dough Testing Instruments** are standardized mechanical devices used to quantify dough behavior under controlled stress/strain conditions, providing empirical or fundamental rheological data. They are broadly classified into three categories per KTU 2024 nomenclature:

1. **Fundamental Rheometers** — measure absolute rheological functions (e.g., capillary, rotational, oscillatory).
2. **Empirical Testers** — measure performance under standardized but arbitrary geometry (e.g., Farinograph, Mixograph).
3. **Imitative/Process-Simulating Testers** — replicate actual processing conditions (e.g., Extensograph, Alveograph, Kieffer Rig).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Dough is a Building, Instruments are its Quality Tests"**
> Imagine a baker constructing a bakery. The dough is the *raw structural material* (like concrete), and the testing instruments are the *quality-control tests* engineers run on that material before pouring.
> - The **Farinograph** is like a **concrete-mixer test** — how long does it mix? How much water does it need? When does it collapse?
> - The **Mixograph** is a **rapid version of the same**, telling you in minutes instead of hours.
> - The **Extensograph** is a **tensile-strength test** — how far can you stretch the dough before it tears? (Like pulling concrete rods apart.)
> - The **Alveograph** is a **burst-test** — inflate a dough bubble until it pops, just like testing a balloon's elasticity.

This analogy helps a student visualize: dough, like any viscoelastic material, has *resistance* (strength), *extensibility* (stretchability), and *stability* (how long it holds shape) — all measurable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The standard reference metrics (per **ICC, AACC, and ISO 5530-1** standards) are:

- **Water Absorption (WA)**: % water required to bring dough to a target consistency of **500 ± 20 Brabender Units (BU)**. *(Bold: 500 BU is the standard target.)*
- **Mixing Tolerance Index (MTI)**: BU drop 5 minutes after peak.
- **Dough Development Time (DDT)**: Time to reach peak consistency, typically **5–9 minutes** for wheat flour.
- **Stability**: Time dough maintains >500 BU consistency, typically **7–12 minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **1 Brabender Unit (BU)** corresponds approximately to a torque of **1 mN·m** on the Farinograph mixer shaft, calibrated against a 100 g·cm reference weight.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three-Tier Logic

All dough testing instruments operate on a common physical logic — apply a controlled **stress (σ or τ)** and measure the resulting **strain (ε or γ)** or vice versa. The differences lie in the **geometry of deformation**:

1. **Shear-dominated** instruments (Farinograph, Mixograph) — dough is sheared between rotating and stationary elements.
2. **Extensional-dominated** instruments (Extensograph, Alveograph, Kieffer Rig) — dough is stretched uniaxially or biaxially.
3. **Combined/compression** instruments (TPA, Texture Analyzer with probes) — dough is compressed and decompressed cyclically.

### 2.2 Deep-Dive: Instrument-by-Instrument Theory

#### 2.2.1 Farinograph (Brabender, Germany — ICC 115/1, AACC 54-21)

- **Geometry**: Twin Z-arm sigma blades rotating at **63 ± 1 rpm** in a temperature-controlled (30 ± 0.2 °C) mixing bowl of **300 g** capacity.
- **Working Principle**: As flour hydrates and gluten develops, **torque on the mixing blades increases**. The torque is electronically recorded as a **Brabender Unit (BU)** vs. **time (min)** curve.
- **Output Parameters**:
  - **Water Absorption (WA, %)**: Flour weight basis; the formula is given below.
  - **Arrival Time**: Time to reach first intercept of 500 BU line.
  - **Peak Time / DDT**: Time to maximum consistency.
  - **Stability (Stab)**: Difference (min) between arrival and departure time.
  - **Mixing Tolerance Index (MTI)**: BU drop 5 min after peak.
  - **Departure Time**: Time to fall below 500 BU after peak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip**: The Farinograph curve is essentially a **"M-shaped" or "cone-shaped" plot**. Examiners frequently ask students to *label the curve regions*. Memorize: rise → peak → plateau → drop.

#### 2.2.2 Mixograph (National Mfg., USA — AACC 54-40A)

- **Geometry**: Four vertical planetary pins rotate at **88 ± 2 rpm** in a bowl containing **10 g** of flour (vs. 300 g for Farinograph).
- **Working Principle**: Similar torque-recording principle but uses **fixed water absorption** (typically based on a protein-water regression).
- **Curve Interpretation**: Midline of the mixing trace yields **mixing time, peak height, peak width, and curve integral (energy)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to remember for KTU exams**: Farinograph = *water-titration* approach; Mixograph = *fixed-water* approach. The Mixograph requires ~85% less flour, making it ideal for early-generation wheat breeding selection.

#### 2.2.3 Extensograph (Brabender — ICC 114/1, AACC 54-10)

- **Geometry**: Cylindrical dough sample (150 g) is stretched horizontally by a hook traveling at constant speed.
- **Working Principle**: After a fixed rest time (45, 90, or 135 min), the dough is stretched until rupture. The **resistance to extension (R₅₀, BU)** and **extensibility (E, mm)** are recorded.
- **Output Curve**: Force-vs-distance plot. **Area under the curve** = dough energy.
- **Proving**: Three sequential curves (45, 90, 135 min) reveal *time-dependent strengthening or weakening* of the dough.

#### 2.2.4 Alveograph (Chopin, France — ICC 121, AACC 54-30A)

- **Geometry**: A flat circular dough disc (clamped at the edges) is inflated by air pressure at constant flow rate (~96 L/h) until it bursts.
- **Working Principle**: Biaxial extension of the dough bubble. The pressure-vs-time curve is recorded.
- **Three Key Indices**:
  - **P (mm)**: Maximum over-pressure → *dough tenacity / resistance*.
  - **L (mm)**: Length of curve at zero-pressure baseline → *dough extensibility*.
  - **W (10⁻⁴ J)**: Area under the curve → *baking strength / deformation energy*.
- **P/L Ratio**: A dimensionless index of *dough balance* (ideal ≈ 0.8–1.2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equation**: W = 6.54 × S, where S is the area (cm²) of the alveogram. The constant **6.54** has units of **10⁻⁴ J/cm²** and is calibration-defined by Chopin Technologies.

#### 2.2.5 Kieffer Dough & Gluten Extensibility Rig

- **Geometry**: A small dough strip (~50 mm × 5 mm) is hooked and pulled upward by a Texture Analyzer.
- **Working Principle**: Uniaxial extension at constant rate. Measures **peak force (resistance)** and **distance to rupture (extensibility)**.
- **Advantage**: Uses only ~5–10 g flour per test, suitable for gluten network research.

#### 2.2.6 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) for Dough — *Adopted from General TPA (Friedman et al., 1963)*

- **Working Principle**: A cylindrical probe (typically 12.7 mm diameter) compresses a dough cube (~25 mm) to a defined strain (typically 30–75%) twice in succession, mimicking the **two-bite mastication** of Texture Profile Analysis.
- **Seven (and more) TPA Parameters**:
  - **Hardness (H₁)**: Peak force of first compression.
  - **Adhesiveness (A₃)**: Negative area of first bite (work to pull probe off).
  - **Springiness (S₂)**: Ratio of recovered to original height after first compression.
  - **Cohesiveness (A₂/A₁)**: Ratio of work of second to first compression.
  - **Gumminess**: Hardness × Cohesiveness.
  - **Chewiness**: Gumminess × Springiness.
  - **Resilience (A₄/A₅)**: Ratio of recovery area to penetration area in first bite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert**: Students frequently confuse **Adhesiveness** (negative work, sticky behavior) with **Cohesiveness** (internal binding strength). They are *inversely related* in dough — high protein → high cohesiveness, low adhesiveness.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Farinograph Water Absorption (WA)** | $\text{WA} = \dfrac{V}{F} \times 100$ | $V$ = water volume (mL), $F$ = flour mass (g) | % |
| 2 | **Mixing Tolerance Index (MTI)** | $\text{MTI} = R_{P} - R_{P+5}$ | $R_P$ = resistance at peak, $R_{P+5}$ = 5 min after peak | BU |
| 3 | **Alveograph W (Baking Strength)** | $W = 6.54 \cdot S$ | $S$ = area under P–L curve | $10^{-4}$ J |
| 4 | **P/L Ratio** | $\dfrac{P}{L}$ | $P$ = max overpressure, $L$ = extensibility | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Extensograph Energy (Area)** | $E_d = \int R(x) \, dx$ | $R(x)$ = resistance as function of distance $x$ | BU·mm |
| 6 | **Shear Stress (Capillary)** | $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $r$ = radius, $L$ = length | Pa |
| 7 | **Apparent Shear Rate** | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi r^{3}}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | **TPA Hardness** | $H_1 = F_{\text{peak,1}}$ | Peak force on first compression | N |
| 9 | **TPA Cohesiveness** | $C = A_2 / A_1$ | Areas under 2nd and 1st bites | dimensionless |
| 10 | **TPA Springiness** | $S_2 = d_2 / d_1$ | Recovered vs. original height | dimensionless |
| 11 | **TPA Chewiness** | $\text{Chew} = H_1 \cdot C \cdot S_2$ | Composite textural index | N |
| 12 | **Mixograph Curve Integral (Energy)** | $E_m = \int_0^{t_f} M(t) \, dt$ | $M(t)$ = midline mixing torque | BU·min |
| 13 | **G' (Storage Modulus)** | $G' = \tau_0 / \gamma_0 \cdot \cos\delta$ | Elastic response | Pa |
| 14 | **G'' (Loss Modulus)** | $G'' = \tau_0 / \gamma_0 \cdot \sin\delta$ | Viscous response | Pa |
| 15 | **tan δ (Loss Tangent)** | $\tan\delta = G'' / G'$ | Viscoelastic balance | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Pipes in Tables**: The use of `\vert` instead of $\vert$ above avoids breaking markdown table syntax in raw renderer. In hand-written exam solutions, plain vertical bars are fine.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dough testing instruments are critical in:

- **Cereal breeding programs**: Rapid Mixograph (10 g) selection of high-protein lines before field trials.
- **Industrial bakeries**: Farinograph data sets the **water addition in mixers** (typically 60–70% of flour weight) — wrong WA causes dry crumbly or sticky dough.
- **Pasta and noodle manufacturing**: Alveograph W > 250 indicates "strong" wheat for durum semolina.
- **Gluten-free product development**: Kieffer Rig and oscillatory rheometers characterize alternative protein networks (rice, sorghum, pulse).
- **Quality control labs**: Farinograph + Extensograph + Alveograph together classify wheat into *strong, medium, weak* commercial grades.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Farinograph Water Absorption from Titration Data

The Farinograph's WA is determined by titrating water into a fixed flour mass until the dough reaches 500 BU consistency. Suppose a flour sample of mass $F = 300$ g absorbs a water volume $V = 195$ mL to reach 500 BU.

**Step 1 — Express the ratio of water mass to flour mass:**

The density of water is $\rho_w = 1.00$ g/mL at 30 °C. Therefore water mass is:

$$m_w = V \cdot \rho_w = 195 \text{ mL} \cdot 1.00 \text{ g/mL} = 195 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the water absorption definition (on 14% moisture basis):**

$$\text{WA} = \frac{m_w}{F} \times 100 = \frac{195}{300} \times 100 = 65.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Correct for flour moisture content (if moisture ≠ 14%):**

The as-is flour mass must be adjusted. For a flour of actual moisture $M$%, the flour-solids basis is:

$$F_{\text{corr}} = F \times \frac{100 - M}{100 - 14}$$

For $M = 13.5\%$:

$$F_{\text{corr}} = 300 \times \frac{100 - 13.5}{100 - 14} = 300 \times \frac{86.5}{86.0} = 301.7 \text{ g}$$

Therefore the corrected WA is:

$$\text{WA}_{\text{corr}} = \frac{195}{301.7} \times 100 = 64.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Calculation Style**: Always state the assumption that $\rho_w = 1.00$ g/mL. Examiners award 1 mark for this clarity in 14-mark problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Alveograph W from P, L Parameters

The Alveograph curve plots pressure (P-axis) against time/extension. The geometric constants relating this to the W index are:

**Step 1 — Express the area of the alveogram (S) in terms of P and L:**

A simplified empirical correlation (Chopin Technologies) for dough with moderate extensibility is:

$$S = k \cdot P \cdot L$$

where $k = 0.30$ cm²/(mm·mm) is a calibration constant. For $P = 80$ mm and $L = 110$ mm:

$$S = 0.30 \times 80 \times 110 = 2640 \text{ cm}^2$$

Hmm, this is far too large. Re-checking units: $P$ and $L$ are recorded in **mm on a paper chart**, and $S$ is measured in **cm² on the same paper**. The actual conversion is:

$$W = 1.5 \times S' \quad \text{(when } S' \text{ is in cm}^2 \text{ of chart paper)}$$

For the modern AlveoLink sensor: the pressure signal is in **mbar** and bubble volume in **mL**; W is computed digitally as:

$$W = 2.10 \times 10^{-4} \cdot P_{\max} \cdot V_{\text{burst}}$$

with $P_{\max}$ in mmH₂O and $V_{\text{burst}}$ in mL.

**Step 2 — Numerical Example (paper-chart method):**

If the area $S$ of the alveogram is $S = 38$ cm², then:

$$W = 6.54 \times S = 6.54 \times 38 = 248.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 248.5 \text{ (Chopin units)}$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

$$W > 280 \Rightarrow \text{Strong wheat (bread flour)}$$
$$180 < W < 280 \Rightarrow \text{Medium (all-purpose)}$$
$$W < 180 \Rightarrow \text{Weak (cake/pastry)}$$

Thus $W = 248.5$ → Medium strength flour.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TPA Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness (Worked Example)

A TPA test on a dough cube (25 mm height) yields the following data:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Peak force of 1st compression ($H_1$) | 12.4 N |
| Peak force of 2nd compression ($H_2$) | 8.9 N |
| Work of 1st compression ($A_1$) | 78.6 N·mm |
| Work of 2nd compression ($A_2$) | 51.2 N·mm |
| Recovered height after 1st bite ($d_2$) | 18.7 mm |
| Original compression depth ($d_1$) | 20.0 mm |
| Adhesive force area ($A_3$) | 2.1 N·mm |

**Step 1 — Hardness:**

$$H_1 = 12.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Cohesiveness:**

$$\text{Cohesiveness} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{51.2}{78.6} = 0.651$$

**Step 3 — Springiness:**

$$\text{Springiness} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{18.7}{20.0} = 0.935$$

**Step 4 — Gumminess:**

$$\text{Gumminess} = H_1 \times C = 12.4 \times 0.651 = 8.07 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Chewiness:**

$$\text{Chewiness} = \text{Gumminess} \times S_2 = 8.07 \times 0.935 = 7.55 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Adhesiveness:**

$$\text{Adhesiveness} = A_3 = 2.1 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a firm, elastic, slightly chewy dough — characteristic of medium-protein bread flour (10–12% protein). The springiness of 0.935 indicates near-complete height recovery, suggesting a well-developed gluten network.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capillary Rheometry of Dough

For dough extrusion through a capillary die, the shear stress at the wall is derived from **force balance on a fluid element**.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid slug in the capillary:**

The pressure force pushing the slug forward is $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$. The shear force resisting motion is $\tau_w \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot L$. At steady state:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau_w \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the wall shear stress $\tau_w$:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

**Step 3 — Express the apparent shear rate (assuming Newtonian approximation):**

For a fluid with volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a circular capillary:

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

For a parabolic velocity profile, $u(r) = u_{\max} (1 - r^2/R^2)$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{\max}}{2} = \frac{\pi R^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\tau_w R}{2\eta}$$

Solving and rearranging gives the **apparent shear rate**:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4 Q}{\pi r^3}$$

**Step 4 — Apparent viscosity:**

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}$$

**Step 5 — Rabinowitsch correction (for non-Newtonian fluids like dough):**

Dough is shear-thinning (pseudoplastic). The true shear rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot \frac{3n + 1}{4n}$$

where $n$ is the **power-law flow behavior index**, obtained from:

$$n = \frac{d \log \tau_w}{d \log \dot{\gamma}_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note**: Capillary rheometry of dough is rarely used in cereal labs because dough contains particulates (bran, germ) that clog small dies. Industrial applications include pasta and snack extrusion where dies are 1–5 mm diameter.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Extensograph Energy from Force-Distance Curve

The Extensograph trace provides $R(x)$ (resistance, BU) vs. $x$ (extension, mm). The **dough energy** is:

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$E_d = \int_0^{x_{\text{rupture}}} R(x) \, dx \quad [\text{BU} \cdot \text{mm}]$$

**Step 2 — For a linear-trend region (resistance $\approx$ R₅₀ constant):**

$$E_d \approx R_{50} \times x_{\text{rupture}}$$

For $R_{50} = 480$ BU and rupture at $x = 140$ mm:

$$E_d = 480 \times 140 = 67{,}200 \text{ BU} \cdot \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to SI energy:**

Using calibration $1$ BU $\cdot$ mm $\approx 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ J:

$$E_d = 67{,}200 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.168 \text{ J}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy**: When asked to "comment on dough strength", quote the *number with units* AND the *classification range* (e.g., "Extensograph energy of 95 cm² at 90 min corresponds to a strong bread-making flour"). Examiners award 2 marks for proper classification.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Instrument | Deformation Type | Sample Size (g) | Key Output | Test Duration | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Farinograph** | Shear (mixing) | 300 | WA, DDT, Stability, MTI | 20–30 min | Process setting |
| **Mixograph** | Shear (planetary) | 10 | Peak time, Width, Integral | 5–8 min | Breeding |
| **Extensograph** | Uniaxial extension | 150 | R₅₀, E, Energy | 45–135 min/proof | Gas retention |
| **Alveograph** | Biaxial extension | 250 | P, L, W, P/L | 30 min | Wheat grading |
| **Kieffer Rig** | Uniaxial extension | 5–10 | Peak force, Distance | 5 min | Research, R&D |
| **TPA** | Compression | 20–50 | H, C, S, Gum, Chew | 5 min | Textural fingerprinting |
| **Capillary** | Shear (flow) | 50+ | η(γ̇), n, K | 10 min | Extrusion design |
| **DMA/Oscillatory** | Oscillatory shear | 2–5 | G', G'', tan δ | 10 min | Viscoelasticity |
