| 4 | $P = 2\pi \, N \, \tau$ | Power consumption (W) at the screw shaft |
| 5 | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \, N \, \tau_{\text{net}}}{m}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy (J/kg); $m$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) |
| 6 | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot \tau_{\text{net}}}{Q}$ | Alternative SME form; $\omega = 2\pi N$ (rad/s), $Q$ = throughput (kg/s) |
| 7 | $t_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{barrel}}}{Q}$ | Mean residence time; $V_{\text{barrel}}$ = effective free volume |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{\delta}$ | Apparent shear rate inside the screw channel |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{\text{die}} = \dfrac{4 \, L_d \, Q \, \mu}{R^{4} \, \pi}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop across a cylindrical die (radius $R$, length $L_d$) |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{puff}} = \rho_{\text{bulk}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{\text{atm}}}\right)$ | Empirical puffing correlation; $\rho_{\text{puff}} \downarrow$ as $\Delta P \uparrow$ |

> **Note on Units:** $N$ in rad/s when computing $\omega$. If $N$ is in rpm, then $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The above equations are not academic curiosities — they are used **daily in industry** for:
- **Scale-up:** Predicting throughput of a new 200 mm screw from a 75 mm pilot unit.
- **Product Design:** Selecting the right SME window (low SME → dense pellets; high SME → expanded puffs).
- **Energy Auditing:** The extruder motor is the largest single energy consumer in a snack line; SME quantifies its specific load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1: Net Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder (Drag + Pressure Flow)**

### 3.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a single-screw extruder in the metering zone. The screw channel is "unwrapped" into a flat plate of width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (where $e$ is the flight width) and length $L$, inclined at the helix angle $\phi$. The wall on the trailing side moves at velocity $V = \pi D N$ relative to the stationary barrel.

Assumptions:
- Steady-state, isothermal flow.
- Melt is a Newtonian fluid with viscosity $\mu$ (we will relax this later).
- Film thickness at the flight walls is negligible.
- No leakage between flight tip and barrel.

### 3.2 Velocity Field by Superposition

The channel flow has two components:
- **Drag flow** (Couette-type): caused by the relative barrel motion.
- **Pressure flow** (Poiseuille-type): caused by the axial pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ pushing the melt backwards.

By superposition, the local velocity $v_z(y)$ across the channel depth $\delta$ is:

$$v_z(y) = V \, \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{\delta}\right) - \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \, y(\delta - y)$$

where $y$ is measured from the stationary barrel wall.

### 3.3 Integration to Get Volumetric Flow

The volumetric flow rate is obtained by integrating $v_z$ across the channel cross-section. For a channel of width $W$ and depth $\delta$:

$$Q = W \int_{0}^{\delta} v_z(y) \, dy$$

**Step 1 — Integrate the drag-flow term:**

$$Q_d = W \int_{0}^{\delta} V\!\left(1 - \dfrac{y}{\delta}\right) dy = W \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{\delta}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} W V \delta$$

Substituting $V = \pi D N$ and $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e \approx \pi D \sin\phi$:

$$Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \, (\pi D \sin\phi) \, (\pi D N) \, \delta = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \, \delta \sin\phi}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the pressure-flow term** (note the negative sign because pressure flow opposes drag flow):

$$Q_p = W \int_{0}^{\delta} \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} y(\delta - y) \, dy = W \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \dfrac{\delta^{3}}{6} = \dfrac{W \delta^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

Substituting $W \approx \pi D \sin\phi$:

$$Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D \delta^{3} \Delta P \sin\phi}{12 \mu L}$$

**Step 3 — Combine by superposition (pressure flow subtracts from drag flow):**

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \delta \sin\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D \delta^{3} \Delta P \sin\phi}{12 \mu L}$$

Replacing the product $\sin\phi$ common to both terms and rearranging to the standard textbook form:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \delta \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D \delta^{3} \Delta P \sin^{2}\phi}{12 \mu L}$$

> This is the **governing throughput equation** of a single-screw cooking extruder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 2: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)**

### 3.4 Power Balance at the Screw Shaft

The mechanical power delivered by the motor to the screw shaft is the torque $\tau_{\text{shaft}}$ (here we use $\tau$ as torque in N·m, distinguished from shear-stress by context) times the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \omega \cdot \tau_{\text{shaft}} = 2\pi N \cdot \tau_{\text{shaft}} \quad [\text{W}]$$

The "net" (useful) torque is the shaft torque minus the bearing and no-load losses. Let the net torque be $\tau_{\text{net}}$.

### 3.5 From Power to Specific Energy

SME is the mechanical energy imparted **per unit mass** of product:

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft,net}}}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{2\pi N \cdot \tau_{\text{net}}}{\dot{m}}$$

with $\dot{m}$ in kg/s. The units of SME are J/kg, or equivalently Wh/kg when divided by 3600.

**Numerical Example (Typical KTU-level calculation):**

Given:
- $N = 300$ rpm
- $\tau_{\text{net}} = 80$ N·m
- $\dot{m} = 1.5$ kg/s

Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$N = 300 \, \dfrac{\text{rev}}{\text{min}} \times \dfrac{2\pi \, \text{rad}}{1 \, \text{rev}} \times \dfrac{1 \, \text{min}}{60 \, \text{s}} = 10\pi \, \text{rad/s} \approx 31.42 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Compute power:

$$P = 2\pi \times 300 \, \dfrac{\text{rev}}{\text{min}} \times 80 \, \text{N·m} = 48000\pi \, \text{N·m/min}$$

Convert to watts (1 W = 1 N·m/s):

$$P = \dfrac{48000\pi}{60} = 800\pi \approx 2513 \, \text{W}$$

Step 3 — Compute SME:

$$\text{SME} = \dfrac{2513 \, \text{W}}{1.5 \, \text{kg/s}} = 1675.5 \, \text{J/kg} = \dfrac{1675.5}{3600} \approx 0.465 \, \text{Wh/kg}$$

Step 4 — Interpret:

> An SME of **~ 470 Wh/kg** sits in the typical range for **moderately expanded snack products** (high-expansion puffs require 300–600 Wh/kg; dense pellets require only 50–150 Wh/kg).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 3: Die Pressure Drop for a Cylindrical Die (Hagen–Poiseuille)**

### 3.6 Capillary Die Model

For a cylindrical die of radius $R$ and length $L_d$ carrying a Newtonian melt of viscosity $\mu$ at volumetric flow $Q_d$:

$$Q_d = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L_d}$$

Solving for the pressure drop required to achieve a target mass throughput $\dot{m}$ (where $Q_d = \dot{m} / \rho$):

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L_d \, \dot{m}}{\pi R^{4} \rho}$$

**Example:**

Given $\mu = 2000$ Pa·s, $L_d = 0.02$ m, $R = 0.005$ m, $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s, $\rho = 1300$ kg/m³.

Step 1 — Compute volumetric flow:

$$Q_d = \dfrac{0.5}{1300} = 3.846 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \times 2000 \times 0.02 \times 3.846 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (0.005)^{4}}$$

Denominator: $\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} = 1.963 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁴.

Numerator: $8 \times 2000 \times 0.02 \times 3.846 \times 10^{-4} = 0.1231$ Pa·m³.

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{0.1231}{1.963 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} = 62.7 \, \text{MPa}$$

> The very high pressure is typical of viscous doughs flowing through narrow dies — and this is precisely what drives the **flash-off / puffing** at the die exit.

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — SME vs. Product Microstructure

| SME (Wh/kg) | Melt Temperature | Moisture | Resulting Microstructure | Typical Product |
|--------------|------------------|----------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| < 80 | Low (< 110 °C) | High (> 25 %) | Dense, plasticized, smooth | Pasta, 3rd snacks |
| 80 – 200 | Moderate (110 – 140 °C) | Moderate (18 – 22 %) | Slightly porous, semi-expanded | Half-products (pellets for frying) |
| 200 – 400 | High (140 – 170 °C) | Moderate (14 – 18 %) | Expanded, cellular, crispy | Direct-expanded snacks (curlies, puffs) |
| > 400 | Very high (> 170 °C) | Low (< 14 %) | Highly expanded, glassy, brittle | Co-extruded crisps, fragile puffs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Twin-Screw Cooking Extruder Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEEDER HOPPER DRY PREMIX] --> B[CONVEYING ZONE DRY GRANULAR FLOW]
    B --> C[PRECONDITIONER WATER + STEAM INJECTION]
    C --> D[KNEADING ZONE STARCH START GELATINIZATION]
    D --> E[METERING ZONE VISCOELASTIC MELT FORMS]
    E --> F[DIE PLATE PRESSURE BUILDUP 2 TO 15 MPA]
    F --> G[DIE EXIT FLASH PUFF + COOLING SETS STRUCTURE]
    G --> H[KNIFE CUTTER SHAPES PIECES]
    H --> I[DRYER COOLER FINAL MOISTURE BELOW 3 PERCENT]
    I --> J[SEASONING DRUM]
    J --> K[PACKAGING]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Rheological State Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 FEED]
        F1[Granular Powder Moisture 12 Percent] --> F2[Friction Dominant]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 TRANSITION]
        F2 --> T1[Water Absorbed by Starch Granules]
        T1 --> T2[Biphasic Solid plus Liquid]
    end
    subgraph STAGE3[STAGE 3 MELT]
        T2 --> M1[Complete Starch Gelatinization]
        M1 --> M2[Protein Network Crosslinking]
        M2 --> M3[Single Phase Viscoelastic Melt]
    end
    subgraph STAGE4[STAGE 4 PUFF]
        M3 --> P1[Die Exit Sudden Pressure Drop]
        P1 --> P2[Superheated Water Flash Evaporates]
        P2 --> P3[Porous Cell Wall Solidifies Glassy]
    end
    P3 --> Q[FINAL CRISPY CELLULAR MICROSTRUCTURE]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow — Single Screw vs. Twin Screw

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SS[Single Screw Extruder]
        S1[One Helical Screw] --> S2[Drag Flow Dominant]
        S2 --> S3[Limited Mixing]
        S3 --> S4[Lower SME Range]
        S4 --> S5[Used For Pasta and Pet Food]
    end
    subgraph TS[Twin Screw Extruder]
        T1[Two Intermeshing Screws] --> T2[Positive Conveying]
        T2 --> T3[Self Wiping High Mixing]
        T3 --> T4[Higher SME Range]
        T4 --> T5[Used For Snacks and Texturized Protein]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) in food extrusion. State its typical range for expanded snack products.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) is the mechanical energy delivered to the food material per unit mass, expressed in J/kg or Wh/kg. It is calculated as the net power drawn by the screw shaft divided by the mass flow rate of the product.*
>
> *Mathematically,*
>
> $$\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau_{\text{net}}}{\dot{m}}$$
>
> *For directly expanded snack products, SME typically lies in the range of **200 – 400 Wh/kg**.*
>
> *SME is a critical scale-up and product-texture parameter because it correlates strongly with starch gelatinization, cell-wall rupture, and final expansion ratio of the extruded snack.*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. List the three functional zones of a single-screw cooking extruder and state the dominant rheological state in each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *The three functional zones and their dominant rheological states are:*
>
> *(i) **Feed/Conveying Zone:** Granular, solid particulate flow; dominant mechanism is inter-particle friction and bulk compaction.*
>
> *(ii) **Kneading/Transition Zone:** Biphasic semi-solid state where water is absorbed by starch granules; the material behaves as a soft, plastic mass undergoing initial gelatinization.*
>
> *(iii) **Metering/Cooking Zone:** Single-phase viscoelastic melt; characterized by shear-thinning, pseudoplastic behaviour, modelled by a Power Law fluid with $n$ between 0.2 and 0.5.*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the volumetric throughput of a single-screw extruder in the metering zone, clearly defining drag flow and pressure flow. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-screw cooking extruder has the following operating parameters: screw diameter $D = 60$ mm, flight depth $\delta = 5$ mm, screw speed $N = 250$ rpm, helix angle $\phi = 20°$, screw length $L = 1.0$ m, and melt viscosity $\mu = 1500$ Pa·s. The die pressure measured is $\Delta P = 4.0$ MPa. Calculate (i) the drag flow rate, (ii) the pressure flow rate, and (iii) the net volumetric throughput in m³/h. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

> **(a) Derivation (7 marks)**
> *Full derivation already presented in Section 3.1–3.3 of these notes. The student is expected to:*
> - *Unwrap the screw channel to a flat plate (1 mark).*
> - *Write the velocity profile by superposition of Couette and Poiseuille flows (2 marks).*
> - *Integrate the velocity to obtain $Q_d$ and $Q_p$ (2 marks).*
> - *Combine and present the final equation (2 marks):*
>
> $$Q = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \delta \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D \delta^{3} \Delta P \sin^{2}\phi}{12 \mu L}$$

> **(b) Numerical (7 marks)**
>
> *Given:*
> $D = 0.060$ m, $\delta = 0.005$ m, $N = 250$ rpm, $\phi = 20°$, $L = 1.0$ m, $\mu = 1500$ Pa·s, $\Delta P = 4 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.
>
> $\sin 20° = 0.342$, $\cos 20° = 0.940$, $\sin^{2} 20° = 0.117$.
>
> *Note: $N$ is in rpm; the equations use $N$ in rev/s implicitly via the barrel velocity $V = \pi D N$. We use $N$ directly in rev/s for the standard formula.*
> $N = 250 / 60 = 4.167$ rev/s.
>
> **(i) Drag flow rate:**
>
> $$Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^{2} (0.060)^{2} (4.167) (0.005) (0.342) (0.940)}{2}$$
>
> $$= \dfrac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 4.167 \times 0.005 \times 0.3215}{2}$$
>
> $$= \dfrac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 4.167 \times 0.001608}{2}$$
>
> $$\approx \dfrac{2.381 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 1.19 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$
>
> *Drag flow $Q_d \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s = **0.428 m³/h** (2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) Pressure flow rate:**
>
> $$Q_p = \dfrac{\pi (0.060) (0.005)^{3} (4 \times 10^{6}) (0.117)}{12 \times 1500 \times 1.0}$$
>
> $$= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.060 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times 10^{6} \times 0.117}{18000}$$
>
> $$= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.060 \times 0.5 \times 0.117}{18000} = \dfrac{0.01102}{18000}$$
>
> $$\approx 6.12 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} = 0.0022 \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/h}$$
>
> *Pressure flow $Q_p \approx 6.12 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s = **0.0022 m³/h** (2 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Net throughput:**
>
> $$Q = Q_d - Q_p = 1.19 \times 10^{-4} - 6.12 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.184 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$$
>
> *Convert to m³/h:*
>
> $$Q = 1.184 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \approx 0.426 \, \text{m}^{3}\text{/h}$$
>
> *Net throughput $\approx$ **0.426 m³/h** (3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question B:**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of Specific Mechanical Energy (SME). Derive the expression for SME in terms of screw speed, net torque, and mass flow rate. Discuss the significance of SME in determining the microstructure of extruded products. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An extruder operates at a screw speed of 400 rpm with a net torque of 120 N·m. The mass throughput is 2.0 kg/s and the melt density is 1400 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the power consumed at the screw shaft, (ii) the SME in J/kg, and (iii) comment on the type of snack (dense pellet / moderately expanded / highly expanded) that this SME would produce. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

> **(a) Concept + Derivation + Significance (7 marks)**
>
> *SME is the mechanical energy input per kilogram of product. It is the single most important processing variable in extrusion because it directly controls the degree of starch gelatinization, cell-wall rupture, and expansion of the product.*
>
> *Derivation:*
>
> $$P = 2\pi N \tau_{\text{net}}$$
>
> $$\text{SME} = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau_{\text{net}}}{\dot{m}}$$
>
> *Significance (3 marks):*
> - *Low SME (< 100 Wh/kg) → incomplete gelatinization → dense products like pasta or 3rd-generation pellets.*
> - *Medium SME (100–250 Wh/kg) → moderate expansion → half-products and breakfast cereals.*
> - *High SME (> 250 Wh/kg) → complete starch breakdown and high gas-cell nucleation → direct-expanded puffs and curls.*

> **(b) Numerical (7 marks)**
>
> *Given: $N = 400$ rpm, $\tau_{\text{net}} = 120$ N·m, $\dot{m} = 2.0$ kg/s.*
>
> **(i) Power:**
>
> $$P = 2\pi \times 400 \times 120 = 96000\pi \approx 301\,593 \, \text{W} \approx 301.6 \, \text{kW}$$
>
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **(ii) SME:**
>
> $$\text{SME} = \dfrac{301\,593}{2.0} = 150\,796.5 \, \text{J/kg} = \dfrac{150\,796.5}{3600} \approx 41.9 \, \text{Wh/kg}$$
>
> *(3 marks)*
>
> **(iii) Product type:**
>
> *SME of ~ 42 Wh/kg falls in the **low SME regime (< 80 Wh/kg)**. This corresponds to **dense pellets or plasticized products** (e.g., 3rd-generation snack pellets, or pasta). It is NOT sufficient to produce a directly expanded puff.*
>
> *To produce expanded snacks, the operator must either increase $N$, increase torque (e.g., by raising the feed moisture-viscosity), or reduce $\dot{m}$.*
>
> *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit of N in SME formula:** When substituting, ensure $N$ is in **rev/s** OR keep it in rpm and convert the result. Mixing rpm and rad/s is the single most common arithmetic error.
> 2. **Helix angle:** $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$ are often swapped. A typical 20° helix has $\sin\phi = 0.342$ and $\cos\phi = 0.940$. Memorizing the order in the formula is essential.
> 3. **Rheology vs. Geometry:** Many students confuse the *power-law* (non-Newtonian) shear rate correction with the *Newtonian* $Q_d$ derivation. For a 14-mark question, you may be asked the Newtonian derivation and then comment on the non-Newtonian correction in a separate sub-part.
> 4. **SME interpretation:** Do not state a number without interpreting it. Always conclude with the product class the SME would generate.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- **Extrusion = HTST process:** 120–200 °C, 15–60 s, 12–30 % moisture.
- **Three zones:** Conveying (solid), Kneading (transition), Metering (viscoelastic melt).
- **Power-law fluid** with $n = 0.2$–$0.5$ inside the melt zone.
- **Drag flow $Q_d$** is *proportional* to $N$, $D^{2}$, $\delta$, and $\sin\phi \cos\phi$.
- **Pressure flow $Q_p$** is *opposing* drag flow and is *proportional* to $\delta^{3}$, $\Delta P$, and $1/\mu$.
- **Net throughput $Q = Q_d - Q_p$.**
- **SME** is the master rheological variable: drives starch gelatinization and expansion.
- **SME formula:** $\text{SME} = 2\pi N \tau_{\text{net}} / \dot{m}$ (with $N$ in rev/s for SI).
- **Die pressure** drives **puffing** via superheated-steam flash-off.
- **Twin-screw extruders** (co-rotating) dominate the snack industry for their self-wiping, positive-conveying design.
- **Scale-up rule of thumb:** Keep $D/N$ and $Q/D^{3}$ constant between pilot and production units to preserve SME and residence time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dried Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dried Foods # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dried Foods** are food products from which water has been substantially removed by the application of heat (and/or mass transfer) under controlled conditions, typically to a final moisture content below a critical threshold (usually **aw < 0.6** or moisture content **< 10–15% wet basis**) to inhibit microbial growth and biochemical reactions, thereby extending shelf life and improving storage stability. In the context of **Food Rheology and Microstructure**, dried foods represent a unique class of complex, multi-phase, porous solid matrices in which the structural and mechanical behavior is governed by: - The amount and state of remaining water (free vs. bound) - The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** of the amorphous matrix - The pore architecture (size distribution, connectivity, tortuosity) - The integrity of biopolymer networks (starch, protein, cellulose) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The microstructural features (porosity, shrinkage, case-hardening, glassy/rubbery state) and their influence on the **mechanical, rehydration, and rheological** behavior of dried foods is a high-weightage area for ESE questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **fresh apple slice** — it is plump, juicy, and soft. Now imagine a **dried apple ring** — it is wrinkled, hard, and shrunken. What changed? Think of the apple as a **sponge-soaked apartment building**: - Water tenants occupy all the rooms (cells and intercellular spaces). - When you "evict" the water by drying, the building collapses, walls crack, and new hallways (pores) form. - The strength of the walls (biopolymer network) determines whether the dried structure remains **crispy and porous** (good for rehydration) or **dense and collapsed** (poor texture). A **raisin** is the worst case — collapsed, rubbery, hard to rehydrate. A **freeze-dried mango** is the best case — porous, crisp, instantly rehydrates. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of Drying Microstructure:** The *way* water is removed (hot air vs. freeze drying vs. spray drying) determines *how* the building collapses, and therefore the **rheological texture** of the final product. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics - **Moisture Content (MC):** Expressed as **% wet basis (% wb)** or **% dry basis (% db)** - **Water Activity (aw):** Unitless ratio, 0 to 1 - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Reported in **°C or K** - **Porosity (ε):** Unitless, between **0 and 1** - **Bulk Density (ρb):** **kg/m³** - **True Density (ρt):** **kg/m³** ## 1.4 Classification of Dried Foods (by Drying Method) | Method | Typical Product | Microstructure | |---|---|---| | Hot-air drying | Fruits, vegetables | Shrinken, dense, case-hardened | | Spray drying | Milk, coffee | Hollow spherical particles | | Freeze drying | Coffee, herbs | Highly porous, honeycomb | | Drum drying | Potato flakes | Dense sheet, amorphous | | Fluidized bed | Granules | Porous, irregular | | Osmotic dehydration | Fruits | Reduced shrinkage, modified | | Microwave drying | Fruits | Puffed, porous | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Sequential Mechanisms of Moisture Removal in Drying Drying a food involves simultaneous **heat transfer, mass transfer, and mechanical deformation**. The moisture removal occurs in three overlapping stages: ### Stage 1: Initial Adjustment / Transient Phase - The product temperature rises from ambient to wet-bulb temperature. - Surface moisture is removed at a rate governed by **external mass transfer** (convective boundary layer). - No internal mass transfer limitation yet. ### Stage 2: Constant Rate Period (CRP) - Surface behaves as a **free water surface**. - Drying rate is governed by **external conditions only** (air velocity, T, RH). - The product temperature ≈ **wet-bulb temperature (Twb)**. - Moisture removed: **free water** from surface and large capillaries. > [!IMPORTANT] > The CRP rarely dominates in foods because most food constituents are hygroscopic — it is more important in inorganic materials. ### Stage 3: Falling Rate Period (FRP) — **THE DOMINANT PHASE IN FOODS** - Surface becomes dry; **evaporation front recedes** into the porous solid. - Moisture migrates by: 1. **Liquid diffusion** (Fickian) through the solid matrix 2. **Capillary flow** in the porous network 3. **Vapor diffusion** through pores - **Case hardening** may occur if surface dries too fast — forms a dry skin that traps internal moisture. - **Product temperature** rises above Twb, approaching the dry-bulb (air) temperature. ## 2.2 Water Activity and the BET Sorption Isotherm The relationship between equilibrium moisture content (EMC) and water activity is given by the **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation**: $$\frac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)} = \frac{1}{M_m \cdot C} + \frac{(C-1)}{M_m \cdot C} \cdot a_w$$ Where: - $M$ = equilibrium moisture content (dry basis, **kg water/kg dry solid**) - $M_m$ = **monolayer moisture content** (BET) - $C$ = BET constant (related to heat of sorption) - $a_w$ = water activity The **GAB (Guggenheim-Anderson-de Boer)** equation is more accurate for foods (range $0.1 < a_w < 0.9$): $$M = \frac{M_g \cdot C_g \cdot K \cdot a_w}{(1 - K \cdot a_w)(1 - K \cdot a_w + C_g \cdot K \cdot a_w)}$$ Where $M_g$ = GAB monolayer moisture, $C_g$ and $K$ are constants. ## 2.3 Glass Transition Theory (Roos / Slade Framework) **The most critical concept in dried food stability.** > [!NOTE] > When water acts as a plasticizer in an amorphous food matrix, it depresses the **glass transition temperature (Tg)**. Below Tg, the matrix is **glassy** (rigid, brittle, slow diffusion). Above Tg, it is **rubbery** (soft, mobile, fast diffusion → fast deterioration). The **Gordon–Taylor equation** predicts the Tg of a plasticized amorphous food: $$T_g = \frac{w_s T_{g,s} + k \cdot w_w T_{g,w}}{w_s + k \cdot w_w}$$ Where: - $T_{g,s}$ = Tg of anhydrous solid (**K**) - $T_{g,w}$ = Tg of pure water ≈ **136 K (-137 °C)** - $w_s$ = mass fraction of solid - $w_w$ = mass fraction of water - $k$ = Gordon–Taylor constant (≈ 5–8 for most foods) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** As moisture content increases → Tg decreases. For stability, dried foods must be stored at **T_storage < Tg** (or Tg − 20 °C as a safety margin to remain in the glassy state). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | Wet-basis moisture | % | | 2 | $M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$ | Dry-basis moisture | kg/kg | | 3 | $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | Conversion wb → db | kg/kg | | 4 | $a_w = \frac{P}{P_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$ | Water activity | unitless | | 5 | BET (above) | Sorption isotherm | — | | 6 | GAB (above) | Wide-range isotherm | — | | 7 | Gordon–Taylor | Tg prediction | K | | 8 | $\dot{m} = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$ | Fickian moisture diffusion | kg/(m²·s) | | 9 | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Effective diffusivity | m²/s | | 10 | $Sh = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | External mass transfer | dimensionless | | 11 | $Bi_m = \frac{k_c L}{D_{eff}}$ | Mass Biot number | dimensionless | | 12 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | Porosity | unitless | | 13 | $S = \frac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Total pore volume | % | | 14 | $\sigma = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure (Laplace) | Pa | | 15 | $RR = \frac{m_{rehyd}}{m_{dehyd}}$ | Rehydration ratio | unitless | | 16 | $W_c = \frac{MR_{eq} - MR_t}{MR_{eq}}$ | Water binding capacity | unitless | > [!NOTE] > **Note on notation:** In all formulas above, the symbol $D$ denotes diffusivity, $\gamma$ denotes surface tension, $r$ is capillary radius, $\theta$ is contact angle, and $\rho_b$ / $\rho_t$ denote bulk and true densities respectively. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility Understanding dried food micro-rheology enables: 1. **Shelf-life prediction** (WLF kinetics above Tg) 2. **Optimization of drying cycles** to avoid case-hardening 3. **Design of instant rehydrating foods** (porous, freeze-dried) 4. **Crispness engineering** (low moisture + Tg well above storage T) 5. **Control of caking** in powders (storage below Tg, low aw) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Moisture Content from BET Sorption Isotherm The BET model is derived from the kinetics of **multilayer adsorption**. We begin with the rate of condensation onto a layer *n* and evaporation from layer *n*: At equilibrium, condensation rate = evaporation rate. For layer 1: $$a_1 p \mu_1 = b_1 s_1 e^{-E_1/RT}$$ For layer $i \geq 2$ (all layers above the first behave identically): $$a_i p \mu_i = b_i s_i e^{-E_L/RT}$$ Defining the ratio $x = p/p_0 = a_w$ and the BET constant $C = (a_1 b_2 / a_2 b_1) \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$, the **BET linear form** becomes: $$\frac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)} = \frac{1}{M_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{M_m C} a_w$$ **Plotting** $\frac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)}$ vs. $a_w$ gives a straight line: - Slope = $(C-1)/(M_m C)$ - Intercept = $1/(M_m C)$ **Solving for the two unknowns:** $$M_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$$ $$C = \frac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — BET Analysis **Problem:** A dried food powder gives the following equilibrium sorption data (25 °C): | $a_w$ | $M$ (kg/kg db) | |---|---| | 0.05 | 0.024 | | 0.10 | 0.043 | | 0.20 | 0.072 | | 0.30 | 0.093 | | 0.40 | 0.108 | **Find** $M_m$ and $C$ using the BET linear equation. **Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)}$ for each point: - $a_w = 0.05$: $\dfrac{0.05}{0.024 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.0228} = 2.193$ - $a_w = 0.10$: $\dfrac{0.10}{0.043 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.0387} = 2.584$ - $a_w = 0.20$: $\dfrac{0.20}{0.072 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.0576} = 3.472$ - $a_w = 0.30$: $\dfrac{0.30}{0.093 \times 0.70} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.0651} = 4.608$ - $a_w = 0.40$: $\dfrac{0.40}{0.108 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.0648} = 6.173$ **Step 2:** Linear regression of $Y = \dfrac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)}$ vs. $X = a_w$ using the lowest three points (BET is valid for $0.05 < a_w < 0.35$). Using points (0.05, 2.193), (0.10, 2.584), (0.20, 3.472): $$\text{slope} = \frac{3.472 - 2.193}{0.20 - 0.05} = \frac{1.279}{0.15} = 8.527$$ $$\text{intercept} = 2.193 - 8.527 \times 0.05 = 2.193 - 0.426 = 1.767$$ **Step 3:** Compute $M_m$ and $C$: $$M_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{8.527 + 1.767} = \frac{1}{10.294} = 0.0971 \text{ kg/kg}$$ $$C = \frac{8.527}{1.767} + 1 = 4.825 + 1 = 5.83$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Monolayer moisture $M_m \approx 0.097$ kg/kg (≈ 9.7% db) — below this, all water is tightly bound to polar sites and **no water is available for microbial/biochemical reactions**. This is the **target safe storage moisture** for the powder. ## 3.3 Derivation: Gordon–Taylor Equation for Glass Transition Consider a two-component amorphous mixture of solid (s) and water (w). The free volume theory gives: $$T_{g,mix} = \frac{\phi_s T_{g,s} + \phi_w T_{g,w}}{\phi_s + \phi_w}$$ Converting volume fractions $\phi$ to mass fractions $w$ using $\phi_i = w_i \rho_{T_g,mix}/\rho_i$, with $\rho_w/\rho_s \approx k$, we obtain: $$T_{g,mix} = \frac{w_s T_{g,s} + k w_w T_{g,w}}{w_s + k w_w}$$ ### Worked Example — Gordon–Taylor A freeze-dried strawberry (anhydrous $T_{g,s} = 360$ K, $k = 6.5$) contains 8% moisture (wet basis). **Convert to mass fraction:** $$w_w = 0.08, \quad w_s = 0.92$$ **Apply Gordon–Taylor** with $T_{g,w} = 136$ K: $$T_{g,mix} = \frac{(0.92)(360) + (6.5)(0.08)(136)}{0.92 + (6.5)(0.08)}$$ $$= \frac{331.2 + 70.72}{0.92 + 0.52}$$ $$= \frac{401.92}{1.44} = 279.1 \text{ K} = 5.9 \text{ °C}$$ **Conclusion:** This product must be stored **below ~6 °C** to remain in the glassy state — explaining why freeze-dried fruits need refrigerated storage to avoid stickiness and caking. ## 3.4 Derivation: Fickian Diffusion Drying in a Slab Fick's second law for one-dimensional unsteady diffusion in a slab of half-thickness $L$: $$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$$ Boundary conditions: $M(\pm L, t) = M_e$ (equilibrium with air), $M(x, 0) = M_0$. **Solution by separation of variables** gives an infinite series. For long drying times, the **first-term approximation** is used: $$MR = \frac{M(t) - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4L^2}\right)$$ Taking natural logarithm: $$\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4L^2} \cdot t$$ **Plotting** $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line with slope $= -\dfrac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4L^2}$, from which $D_{eff}$ can be obtained. ### Quick Numerical Estimate For a carrot slab (thickness 5 mm, i.e. $L = 0.0025$ m) with $D_{eff} = 2 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s: $$\text{slope} = -\frac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-10}}{4 \times (0.0025)^2} = -\frac{1.974 \times 10^{-9}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = -7.90 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ The **diffusion time constant** is: $$\tau = \frac{4L^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} = \frac{4 \times (0.0025)^2}{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.27 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \approx 3.5 \text{ h}$$ This means ~95% of the free moisture is removed in $3\tau \approx 10.5$ h. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stages of Drying & Microstructural Evolution ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fresh Food
High Moisture, aw close to 1] --> B[Drying Begins
Transient Phase] B --> C[Constant Rate Period
Surface Evaporation
Free Water Loss] C --> D[Falling Rate Period 1
Funicular State
Liquid Capillary Flow] D --> E[Falling Rate Period 2
Pendular State
Vapor Diffusion Dominant] E --> F[Case Hardening Risk
Surface Tg exceeds T_drying] E --> G[Porous Solid Forms
Pore Network Develops] F --> H[Trapped Internal Moisture
Poor Rehydration] G --> I[Glassy Dried Food
Stable if T_storage less than Tg] H --> J[Dense Shrunken Matrix] G --> K[Crisp Porous Matrix
Good Rehydration] I --> L[Quality Dried Product] J --> M[Quality Defect] K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Glass Transition Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Amorphous Food Matrix] --> B[Moisture acts as Plasticizer] B --> C[Increases Free Volume] C --> D[Decreases Tg] D --> E{Temperature vs Tg} E -->|T less than Tg| F[Glassy State
Brittle, Crisp, Stable] E -->|T greater than Tg| G[Rubbery State
Soft, Sticky, Unstable] F --> H[Long Shelf Life
Low Diffusion] G --> I[Caking, Sticking, Collapse] H --> J[Storage Recommendation] I --> J J --> K[Keep Tg - 20C above T_storage] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Architecture of Microstructural Pore System in Dried Food ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EXTERNAL[External Drying Air] AIR[Convective Boundary Layer
T, RH, v] end subgraph SURFACE[Surface Skin] SKIN[Case Hardened Layer
Low aw, High Tg
Low Permeability] end subgraph PORE[Pore Network Domain] P1[Macro Pores
50 to 500 microns
Vapor Transport] P2[Mesopores
2 to 50 microns
Capillary Condensation] P3[Micropores
less than 2 microns
Knudsen Diffusion] end subgraph CORE[Inner Core] MOIST[Bound Water Layer
Monolayer BET] SOLID[Biopolymers
Starch Protein Cellulose] end AIR --> SKIN SKIN --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> MOIST MOIST --> SOLID ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Effect of Drying Method on Microstructure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Food Slurry or Slice] --> B{Drying Method} B -->|Hot Air 60 to 80C| C[Shrinked Dense Matrix
Low Porosity
Case Hardened] B -->|Freeze Drying -40C sublimation| D[Honeycomb Porous
High Porosity
Crisp] B -->|Spray Drying| E[Hollow Microspheres
25 to 100 microns] B -->|Drum Drying| F[Dense Amorphous Film
Flakes] B -->|Microwave Vacuum| G[Puffed Cellular
Open Pores] C --> H[Rehydration Slow] D --> I[Rehydration Fast] E --> J[Reconstitutes Easily] F --> K[Used in Soups and Instant] G --> I ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define water activity and state its significance in dried foods. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Answer:** Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($P$) to the saturation vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature ($P_0$). It is also equal to **ERH/100**, where ERH is the equilibrium relative humidity of the product. $$a_w = \frac{P}{P_0}$$ **Significance in dried foods:** 1. **Microbial stability:** Most bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$, yeasts $a_w > 0.88$, molds $a_w > 0.70$. Drying to $a_w < 0.6$ inhibits virtually all microbial growth. 2. **Biochemical stability:** Enzymatic and non-enzymatic reactions (Maillard, lipid oxidation) are sharply slowed at low $a_w$. 3. **Predicts shelf life:** Lower $a_w$ → longer shelf life at given temperature. 4. **Defines the safe storage moisture content** via the sorption isotherm. --- ### Question 2: What is the glass transition temperature (Tg) and why is it important for dried food stability? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature at which an amorphous food matrix changes from a rigid, brittle, **glassy** state to a soft, rubbery, mobile state. Below Tg, molecular mobility is extremely low and the matrix is kinetically stable. Above Tg, molecular mobility increases sharply, accelerating deteriorative reactions. **Importance for dried foods:** - Water acts as a **plasticizer** — increasing moisture decreases Tg (Gordon–Taylor relationship). - For long-term stability, dried foods must be stored at **T_storage < Tg** (preferably Tg − 20 °C) to remain in the glassy state. - Stickiness, caking, collapse, and crystallization in powders occur when T_storage exceeds Tg. - Tg determines the **critical moisture content** above which a dried food loses crispness and becomes rubbery. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q. (a)** Describe the **three stages of drying** of a moist food material with a neat sketch of the drying rate curve. Discuss the mechanisms of moisture transport in each stage. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Explain the **BET sorption isotherm model** for dried foods. Derive the BET linear form and discuss its significance in determining the **safe storage moisture content**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Three Stages of Drying:** | Stage | Rate Behaviour | Mechanism | Location of Evaporation | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Transient / Initial | Rises or stabilises | Product warms to wet-bulb T | Surface | | 2. Constant Rate Period (CRP) | Constant high rate | Capillary-fed surface water evaporates; external mass transfer controls | Surface (free water film) | | 3. Falling Rate Period (FRP) | Decreasing (often 1–2 sub-stages) | Liquid diffusion, capillary flow, vapour diffusion inside porous solid; internal mass transfer controls | Evaporation front recedes into solid | In the **funicular state** (early FRP), capillary channels are partially filled with liquid; flow is continuous. In the **pendular state** (late FRP), liquid is discontinuous; only vapour diffusion through pores + surface diffusion of bound water remain active. **Drying rate curve:** Plot of drying rate (kg water/m²·h) vs. moisture content (dry basis). A–B = transient, B–C = CRP, C–D = first FRP, D–E = second FRP, E = equilibrium moisture content $M_e$. **(b) BET Model — Detailed Solution:** The BET equation (linear form) is: $$\frac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)} = \frac{1}{M_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{M_m C} a_w$$ **Procedure to determine $M_m$ and $C$:** 1. Measure EMC ($M$) at several $a_w$ in the range 0.05–0.35. 2. Compute $\frac{a_w}{M(1-a_w)}$ for each. 3. Plot vs. $a_w$ → straight line. 4. From slope and intercept: $$M_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}, \quad C = \frac{\text{slope}}{\text{intercept}} + 1$$ **Significance:** - $M_m$ represents the **monolayer moisture** — water molecules bound directly to hydrophilic sites (–OH, –NH₂, –COOH) of biopolymers. - Below $M_m$, water is **immobilized**; reactants cannot dissolve; microbial growth is impossible; reactions (Maillard, lipid oxidation) are minimal. - Therefore, $M_m$ is the **theoretical safe storage moisture** for a dried food. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q. (a)** Explain the **Gordon–Taylor equation** for predicting the glass transition temperature of a dried food as a function of moisture content. Show with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Discuss the **microstructural changes** that occur in a food during hot-air drying versus freeze drying. Use diagrams and explain how these changes affect **rehydration** and **rheological/textural quality**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Gordon–Taylor Equation:** $$T_g = \frac{w_s T_{g,s} + k w_w T_{g,w}}{w_s + k w_w}$$ - $T_{g,s}$ = Tg of anhydrous amorphous solid (K) - $T_{g,w} = 136$ K (pure water Tg) - $w_s, w_w$ = mass fractions of solid and water - $k$ = empirical constant (often ~5–8 for carbohydrate-rich foods) **Example:** A freeze-dried fruit has $T_{g,s} = 340$ K, $k = 6$, $M = 10\%$ wb. - $w_w = 0.10$, $w_s = 0.90$ - $T_g = \dfrac{0.90 \times 340 + 6 \times 0.10 \times 136}{0.90 + 6 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{306 + 81.6}{1.50} = 258.4$ K = −14.7 °C - This product must be stored below −15 °C to remain glassy — a major practical challenge. **Application:** Used to construct **state diagrams** (Tg vs. moisture content + sorption isotherm) to identify zones of stability and caking. **(b) Microstructural Changes — Hot Air vs. Freeze Drying:** | Feature | Hot-Air Drying | Freeze Drying | |---|---|---| | Water removal | Liquid → vapour at T > 0 °C | Ice → vapour (sublimation) at T < 0 °C | | Matrix state during drying | Above Tg (rubbery) | Below Tg (glassy, ice-cemented) | | Pore formation | Collapsed; small dense pores | Open honeycomb; large connected pores | | Shrinkage | High (50–80% volume loss) | Minimal (<5%) | | Case hardening | Common | Absent | | Porosity | Low (0.1–0.4) | High (0.7–0.95) | | Rehydration | Slow, incomplete | Fast, near-complete | | Texture | Hard, leathery, dense | Crisp, fragile, porous | | Colour/nutrient loss | High (heat/oxidation) | Low (low T, no oxygen) | **Rheological implication:** - Hot-air dried products show **elastic, rubbery** behaviour (high relaxation modulus) due to dense matrix. - Freeze-dried products show **brittle, fragile** behaviour (fracture at low strain) due to thin pore walls in glassy state. - Rehydration kinetics follow **Peleg's model** for both, but the rate constant is ~5–10× smaller for hot-air dried products. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Do NOT confuse **Tg** with **Tm** (melting temperature) — Tg is a second-order transition, not a phase change. > 2. In BET plots, do NOT include data beyond $a_w \approx 0.35$ — linearity breaks down (use GAB for higher $a_w$). > 3. In Gordon–Taylor, ensure temperatures are in **Kelvin**, not °C. > 4. For **rehydration ratio** questions, the formula is $RR = m_{rehyd}/m_{dehyd}$ — NOT vice versa. > 5. Many students write "case hardening = good for storage" — this is **WRONG**; case hardening is a **defect** that traps internal moisture and causes microbial spoilage. > 6. Don't forget the units: $D_{eff}$ is in **m²/s**, not m/s. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision list — tick before entering exam hall:** - ☑ **Drying stages:** Transient → CRP → FRP (Funicular) → FRP (Pendular) → EMC. - ☑ **Moisture conversions:** $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb})$. - ☑ **Water activity** is the **single most important parameter** for dried food stability (microbial + biochemical). - ☑ **BET valid range:** $0.05 < a_w < 0.35$; **GAB valid range:** $0.1 < a_w < 0.9$. - ☑ **Monolayer moisture ($M_m$)** = safe storage moisture threshold. - ☑ **Gordon–Taylor:** $T_{g,w} = 136$ K (always); $T_g$ in **Kelvin**. - ☑ **Rule of thumb:** storage at **Tg − 20 °C** for optimal stability. - ☑ **Plasticizer:** water in amorphous foods (depresses Tg). - ☑ **Case hardening:** skin forms faster than interior dries → trapped moisture → quality defect. - ☑ **Freeze drying** gives highest porosity, lowest shrinkage, best rehydration. - ☑ **Hot-air drying** gives densest, most shrunken, hardest-to-rehydrate product. - ☑ **Spray drying** produces hollow spherical particles (25–100 µm). - ☑ **Fickian first-term approximation** for long drying times: $MR = (8/\pi^2)\exp(-\pi^2 D_{eff} t / 4L^2)$. - ☑ **Rehydration ratio (RR)** = $m_{rehyd}/m_{dehyd}$; **coefficient of rehydration** = $(M_{rehyd} - M_{dried})/M_{dried}$ expressed as %. - ☑ **Capillary pressure (Laplace):** $\Delta P = 2\gamma \cos\theta / r$ — governs moisture transport in pores. - ☑ **Pore classification:** macro (> 50 nm), meso (2–50 nm), micro (< 2 nm). - ☑ **Porosity equation:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$. - ☑ **Storage stability** depends jointly on: $a_w$ (microbial) **and** Tg (physical/chemical kinetics). - ☑ **Common dried food defects:** caking, collapse, stickiness, browning (Maillard), loss of rehydration capacity, vitamin degradation. - ☑ **KTU favourite questions:** "Explain BET and significance" + "Compare hot-air vs freeze drying microstructure" + "Gordon–Taylor with example."

topics.title: Non-Thermal Processing Technologies for Fabrication of Microstructures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Non-Thermal Processing Technologies for Fabrication of Microstructures # Module 4: Non-Thermal Processing Technologies for Fabrication of Microstructures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Non-Thermal Processing (NTP)** > Non-thermal processing technologies are food preservation and structuring techniques that inactivate microorganisms or modify the physical/chemical state of food matrices **without subjecting the bulk product to a significant rise in temperature**, thereby preserving thermosensitive nutrients (vitamins, bioactives), flavour volatiles, and the native microstructure. In the context of **microstructure fabrication**, these technologies are not merely preservation tools — they are *engineered physical fields* used to **deliberately construct, refine, or reorganize** the structural matrix of foods (e.g., protein gels, nanoemulsions, biopolymer films, fibre networks) at the micro- and nano-scale. ### Sub-classification (KTU Module-4 Focus) The fabrication-oriented non-thermal technologies are broadly clustered as: | Class | Dominant Physical Field | Microstructure Action | |---|---|---| | Mechanical-pressure based | Hydrostatic pressure, shear | Cell disruption, gelation, droplet refinement | | Electrical | High-voltage pulses | Electroporation, membrane permeabilization | | Acoustic | Ultrasonic cavitation | Emulsification, droplet break-up, defoaming | | Plasma-based | Ionized gas species | Surface etching, cross-linking, nano-coating | | Electro-hydrodynamic | Electric field + fluid jet | Fibre spinning (electrospinning), nano-encapsulation | ### Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > Imagine blowing soap bubbles with a straw. The bubble's *thinness* depends on how hard you blow (pressure) and the soap's stretchiness (viscosity). Non-thermal processing works on the same idea — except instead of breath, we use **pressure pulses, electric shocks, ultrasound waves, or ionized gas** to "blow" food molecules into precise micro-architectures **without cooking them**. The food still tastes "fresh" but has been *engineered* at the micro-level. ### Key Engineering Metrics (highlighted for KTU valuation) - **Pressure range:** **100 – 600 MPa** (HPP, HPH) - **Electric field strength:** **10 – 50 kV/cm** (PEF) - **Ultrasound frequency:** **20 – 100 kHz** (high-intensity) - **Process temperature rise:** **ΔT ≤ 30 °C** (defining "non-thermal") - **Critical cavitation threshold:** **≥ 10 W/cm²** acoustic intensity --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 High-Pressure Processing (HPP) — Microstructure Modulation ### Operating Principle HPP subjects liquid/solid food to hydrostatic pressure (**300–600 MPa**) for **1–15 minutes** in a pressure-transmitting medium (water + glycol). The **Le Chatelier principle** dictates that reactions involving a **volume decrease** are favoured — meaning hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions are *reorganized*, while covalent bonds remain intact. ### Microstructure Outcomes - **Protein denaturation** (volume reduction step → unfolding → aggregation → gel) - **Starch gelatinization** occurs at *much lower temperatures* under pressure - **Lipid crystallization** polymorphism shift (β′ → β forms) - **Cell membrane rupture** (irreversible electroporation-like effect at macro-scale) ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. Sample is vacuum-sealed in flexible packaging. 2. Loaded into the pressure vessel containing pressure-transmitting fluid. 3. Intensifier compresses fluid to target pressure (ramp-up ~ 2–5 min). 4. Hold-time starts — molecular compression phase. 5. Decompression is *nearly adiabatic*; flash-cooling offsets any heat gain. 6. Volume of activation change (**ΔV‡**) governs reaction selectivity: $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -R T \left(\frac{\partial \ln k}{\partial P}\right)_T$$ where *k* is the reaction rate constant, *R* is the gas constant, *T* is absolute temperature, and *P* is pressure. A **negative ΔV‡** (typical for protein unfolding) means the reaction is accelerated by pressure. ## 2.2 High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH) — Droplet/Particle Engineering ### Operating Principle Fluid is forced through a micro-slit (typically **5–20 µm**) at pressures of **100–400 MPa** with velocities up to **300 m s⁻¹**. The combined action of: - **High shear** (γ̇ ~ 10⁶ s⁻¹) - **Turbulence** (Reynolds number Re ~ 10⁵) - **Cavitation** - **Elongational flow** produces **sub-micron droplets, nanoemulsions, or nano-suspensions**. ### Sauter Mean Diameter (d₃₂) — Governing Equation $$d_{32} = \frac{6 \cdot \phi}{\dot{\gamma} \cdot \eta_c \cdot t}$$ where φ = disperse phase volume fraction, γ̇ = shear rate, ηc = continuous phase viscosity, t = residence time. For turbulent inertial break-up, the **Kolmogorov eddy length scale** sets the minimum droplet size: $$\eta_K = \left(\frac{\nu_c^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$ where νc = kinematic viscosity, ε = energy dissipation rate per unit mass. ## 2.3 Microfluidization — Tunable Microfluidic Fabrication A specialized HPH variant where fluid streams are split, accelerated, and **recombined in a defined micro-channel geometry (Z-type or Y-type)**. Allows tighter droplet size distribution and produces **monodisperse emulsions (polydispersity index < 0.2)**. ## 2.4 Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) — Electroporation Microstructure ### Operating Principle Short, high-voltage pulses (**µs to ms**, **10–50 kV/cm**) induce **electroporation** of cell membranes: - **Reversible electroporation:** pores reseal — used for *mass-transfer* applications (drying, infusion). - **Irreversible electroporation:** permanent pore formation — used for *sterilization and texture softening*. ### Critical Electric Field Strength $$E_c = \frac{V_{mem}}{0.75 \cdot d_{mem}}$$ where Vmem ≈ 1 V is the transmembrane potential, dmem ≈ 5 nm is the bilayer thickness, giving **Ec ≈ 2.7 kV/cm** for *inception*; industrial systems operate **5–10×** above this threshold. ### Pore Radius Model (Smoluchowski-Debye approximation) $$r_p(t) = r_p^{\max} \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_p}\right)\right]$$ where rp,max ≈ 1–10 nm, τp ≈ 1–10 µs. ## 2.5 Ultrasonication — Acoustic Cavitation ### Operating Principle High-intensity ultrasound (**≥ 20 kHz**, **10–1000 W cm⁻²**) generates **acoustic cavitation bubbles** in liquid. On violent collapse, these produce: - **Localized hotspots** (~5000 K, ~1000 atm for µs) - **Micro-jets** (up to **100 m s⁻¹**) - **Shock waves** that disrupt microstructures ### Cavitation Threshold (Blake threshold pressure) $$P_B = P_0 + \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{\frac{3\left(P_0 + \frac{2\sigma}{R_0}\right)^3 - 2P_0^3}{3}}$$ where P₀ = hydrostatic pressure, σ = surface tension, R₀ = equilibrium bubble radius. ## 2.6 Cold Plasma — Surface Microstructure Engineering ### Operating Principle Ionized gas (typically **0.1–100 mbar**, atmospheric-pressure variants exist) generates **reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS)**, UV photons, and charged particles. Surfaces are etched, cross-linked, or functionalized at **< 40 °C**. Microstructure effects: - **Surface nano-roughening** (RMS roughness 5–500 nm) - **Hydrophobic → hydrophilic** conversion (contact angle shift) - **Cross-linking of surface proteins** (maillard-like chemistry without bulk heating) ## 2.7 Electrospinning / Electrospraying — Fibrous Microstructures ### Operating Principle A polymer solution is ejected through a capillary under a high voltage (**5–30 kV**). The **Taylor cone** forms and a **charged jet** undergoes whipping instabilities, producing fibres in the **nano- to micro-metre range**. ### Critical Voltage (Taylor Cone Onset) $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot d \cdot 4}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \cos\theta}}$$ where γ = surface tension, d = needle inner diameter, ε₀ = vacuum permittivity, θ = Taylor cone half-angle (~ 49.3°). --- ### 🎯 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure-driven reaction shift | $\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -RT\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln k}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | ΔV‡ in m³ mol⁻¹, k in s⁻¹ | Negative ΔV‡ ⇒ pressure favours reaction | | Sauter mean diameter (shear) | $d_{32} = \dfrac{6\phi}{\dot{\gamma} \eta_c t}$ | d in m, γ̇ in s⁻¹, η in Pa·s | Lower viscosity → finer droplets | | Kolmogorov length scale | $\eta_K = \left(\dfrac{\nu^3}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$ | η in m, ε in m² s⁻³ | Sets *minimum* droplet size | | Critical E-field (PEF) | $E_c = \dfrac{V_{mem}}{0.75 \cdot d_{mem}}$ | E in V m⁻¹ | Ec ≈ 2.7 kV cm⁻¹ (inception) | | Pore radius dynamics | $r_p(t) = r_{p}^{\max}\left[1-\exp(-t/\tau_p)\right]$ | r in m, t in s | First-order pore formation | | Cavitation threshold | $P_B = P_0 + \dfrac{2}{3}\sqrt{\dfrac{3\left(P_0+\frac{2\sigma}{R_0}\right)^3 - 2P_0^3}{3}}$ | P in Pa, σ in N m⁻¹ | Sets ultrasonic onset | | Taylor cone voltage | $V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0 \cos\theta}}$ | V in V, γ in N m⁻¹ | θ ≈ 49.3° | --- ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **HPP**: Cold-pressed fruit juices (e.g., "Fresh" range), ready-meals with fresh-textured vegetables. - **HPH / Microfluidization**: Pharmaceutical nanoemulsions (e.g., propofol), dairy protein fortification. - **PEF**: Potato-chip pretreatment for 30 % reduced oil uptake, sugar beet extraction yield boost. - **Ultrasound**: Craft beverage (kombucha, beer) haze control, nano-fortified beverages. - **Cold Plasma**: Sterilization of seeds, packaging decontamination. - **Electrospinning**: Edible coatings (zein, pullulan) for active packaging. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Pressure-Driven Reaction Kinetics (HPP) We start from the thermodynamic identity for the pressure dependence of the equilibrium constant **K**: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta V^0}{RT}$$ **Step 1 — Transition-state connection** By activated complex theory, the rate constant **k** is proportional to the equilibrium constant for forming the activated complex: $$k \propto K^{\ddagger} = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate** Taking the logarithmic derivative with respect to pressure at constant **T**: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln k}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{1}{RT}\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G^{\ddagger}}{\partial P}\right)_T$$ **Step 3 — Apply thermodynamic identity to ΔG‡** $$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G^{\ddagger}}{\partial P}\right)_T = \Delta V^{\ddagger}$$ **Step 4 — Combine** $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln k}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta V^{\ddagger}}{RT}$$ Rearranging: $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -RT\left(\frac{\partial \ln k}{\partial P}\right)_T$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (typical protein unfolding)** For an egg-white lysozyme unfolding reaction at **T = 298 K**: - Rate constant increases from **k₁ = 0.01 s⁻¹** at **P₁ = 0.1 MPa** to **k₂ = 0.85 s⁻¹** at **P₂ = 400 MPa**. $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -RT \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{P_2 - P_1}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{0.85}{0.01}\right) = \ln(85) \approx 4.4427$$ $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -(8.314)(298) \cdot \frac{4.4427}{400 \times 10^6 - 0.1 \times 10^6}$$ $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -(2477.6) \cdot \frac{4.4427}{3.9999 \times 10^{8}}$$ $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} = -\frac{11006.0}{3.9999 \times 10^{8}}$$ $$\Delta V^{\ddagger} \approx -2.75 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3\ \text{mol}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V^{\ddagger} \approx -27.5\ \text{cm}^3\ \text{mol}^{-1}}$$ The **negative sign** confirms that protein unfolding under HPP involves a *volume decrease* — pressure favours the unfolded state. ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Kolmogorov Length & Minimum Droplet Diameter The droplet size in turbulent flow is bounded by the **Kolmogorov microscale**, below which viscous forces dominate over inertial forces. **Step 1 — Local energy dissipation rate** For HPH, ε is estimated from: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\rho \cdot V}$$ where ΔP = pressure drop, Q = volumetric flow rate, ρ = density, V = volume of the disruption zone. **Step 2 — Numerical example** For ΔP = **200 MPa = 2 × 10⁸ Pa**, Q = **10 L h⁻¹ = 2.78 × 10⁻⁶ m³ s⁻¹**, ρ = **1000 kg m⁻³**, V = **1 × 10⁻⁶ m³**: $$\varepsilon = \frac{(2 \times 10^{8})(2.78 \times 10^{-6})}{(1000)(1 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{556}{10^{-3}} = 5.56 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m}^2\ \text{s}^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Kolmogorov scale** For water (ν ≈ **1 × 10⁻⁶ m² s⁻¹**): $$\eta_K = \left(\frac{(1 \times 10^{-6})^3}{5.56 \times 10^{5}}\right)^{1/4}$$ $$\eta_K = \left(\frac{1 \times 10^{-18}}{5.56 \times 10^{5}}\right)^{1/4} = \left(1.798 \times 10^{-24}\right)^{1/4}$$ $$\eta_K \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} \approx 1.16\ \mu\text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Drop size correction** Empirically, the actual minimum droplet diameter is **2–5× the Kolmogorov scale** due to interfacial tension and coalescence effects: $$d_{\min} \approx (3\text{ to }5) \cdot \eta_K \approx 3.5\ \mu\text{m to}\ 5.8\ \mu\text{m}$$ This matches typical HPH experimental results for oil-in-water emulsions. ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Taylor Cone Onset Voltage **Step 1 — Force balance at pendant droplet** At the threshold, the **electrical stress** balances the **Laplace pressure**: $$\frac{\varepsilon_0 E^2}{2} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ where r = local radius of curvature at the cone apex. **Step 2 — Express E in terms of V** For a needle electrode of diameter d separated from a ground plane: $$E \approx \frac{V}{d}$$ **Step 3 — Insert and solve for V** $$\frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 d^2} = \frac{2\gamma}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^2 = \frac{4\gamma d^2}{\varepsilon_0 r}$$ Setting **r = d** (cone apex of similar dimension to needle): $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{4\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0}}$$ **Step 4 — Include cone angle correction** Empirically, the cone half-angle is **θ = 49.3°** (Taylor angle), giving: $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{4\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0 \cos\theta}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation** For a zein solution (γ ≈ **30 mN m⁻¹ = 0.030 N m⁻¹**), d = **0.5 mm = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m**, ε₀ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F m⁻¹**, cos(49.3°) ≈ 0.652: $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.030 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.652}}$$ $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{6.0 \times 10^{-5}}{5.773 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$V_c = \sqrt{1.039 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3.22 \times 10^{3}\ \text{V} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV}$$ This lies within the typical operational range (**5–30 kV**) confirming model validity. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology vs. Microstructure Outcome | Technology | Energy Input (per kg) | Typical T-rise | Microstructure Effect | Final Scale | |---|---|---|---|---| | HPP | ~ 0.05–0.15 kWh kg⁻¹ | ~ 5–10 °C | Protein gelation, cell disruption | 50–500 µm (cellular), molecular | | HPH | ~ 0.1–0.5 kWh kg⁻¹ | ~ 20–30 °C | Nanoemulsions, sub-micron dispersion | 50–500 nm | | Microfluidization | ~ 0.2–0.8 kWh kg⁻¹ | ~ 15–25 °C | Mono-disperse emulsions, liposomes | 50–300 nm | | PEF | ~ 0.01–0.05 kWh kg⁻¹ | ~ 5 °C | Electroporation, secondary metabolite release | 10 nm – 50 µm (cell-level) | | Ultrasound | ~ 0.05–0.3 kWh kg⁻¹ | ~ 10–25 °C | Acoustic cavitation mixing, defoaming | 100 nm – 10 µm | | Cold Plasma | < 0.01 kWh kg⁻¹ | < 10 °C | Surface functionalization, etching | 1–500 nm (surface only) | | Electrospinning | Variable | Ambient | Sub-micron fibre mats | 50 nm – 5 µm | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Non-Thermal Microstructure Fabrication ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Food Matrix] --> B[Pre-conditioning
Vaporization, deaeration] B --> C1[High Pressure Vessel] B --> C2[High Pressure Homogenizer] B --> C3[PEF Chamber] B --> C4[Ultrasonic Reactor] B --> C5[Cold Plasma Reactor] B --> C6[Electrospinning Setup] C1 --> D1[Protein Gel Network] C1 --> D2[Cellular Disruption] C2 --> E1[Nanoemulsion] C2 --> E2[Submicron Dispersion] C3 --> F1[Reversible Pores] C3 --> F2[Irreversible Pores] C4 --> G1[Acoustic Cavitation] C4 --> G2[Microjet Shear] C5 --> H1[Surface Etching] C5 --> H2[Cross-linked Surface] C6 --> I1[Polymer Jet] C6 --> I2[Whipping Instability] D1 --> J[Final Microstructured Product] D2 --> J E1 --> J E2 --> J F1 --> J F2 --> J G1 --> J G2 --> J H1 --> J H2 --> J I1 --> J I2 --> J J --> K[Quality Control
DLS, Microscopy, Rheology] style A fill:#fff4e1 style J fill:#e1f5e1 style K fill:#fce1f5 ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INTAKE[Feed System] P1[Product Tank] --> P2[Piston Pump] end P2 --> P3[Intensifier Pump
100-400 MPa] P3 --> P4[Micro-slit Valve
5-20 microns] P4 --> P5[Cavitation Chamber] P5 --> P6[Impact Ring] P6 --> P7[Heat Exchanger
T control] P7 --> P8[Output Tank] P4 --> Q1[High Shear Field] P4 --> Q2[Turbulent Flow] P5 --> Q3[Cavitation Bubbles] P6 --> Q4[Normal Impact Stress] Q1 --> R1[Droplet Breakup] Q2 --> R1 Q3 --> R1 Q4 --> R1 R1 --> S1[Nanoemulsion
50-500 nm] style P3 fill:#cfe2ff style P4 fill:#fff3cd style S1 fill:#d1e7dd ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Selecting the NTP ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Target Microstructure?] --> B1[Sub-micron Emulsion] A --> B2[Protein Gel Network] A --> B3[Cellular Permeabilization] A --> B4[Surface Modification] A --> B5[Fibrous Microstructure] B1 --> C1[Choose: HPH or Microfluidization] B2 --> C2[Choose: HPP] B3 --> C3[Choose: PEF] B4 --> C4[Choose: Cold Plasma] B5 --> C5[Choose: Electrospinning] C1 --> D1[Validate by: DLS, Zeta potential] C2 --> D2[Validate by: Texture profile, SEM] C3 --> D3[Validate by: Conductivity, Dye uptake] C4 --> D4[Validate by: XPS, Contact angle] C5 --> D5[Validate by: SEM fibre diameter] style A fill:#fff4e1 style D1 fill:#e1f5e1 style D2 fill:#e1f5e1 style D3 fill:#e1f5e1 style D4 fill:#e1f5e1 style D5 fill:#e1f5e1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define "non-thermal processing" in the context of food microstructure fabrication. List any two such technologies.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Non-thermal processing refers to food processing technologies that achieve microbial inactivation or microstructural modification while keeping the bulk product temperature rise below **~30 °C**, thereby preserving heat-sensitive nutrients and the native food matrix. **Definition (2 marks)**; **Examples: HPP, PEF, HPH, ultrasound, cold plasma (1 mark for any two with brief rationale).** ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the Le Chatelier principle as applicable to High-Pressure Processing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Le Chatelier principle states that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a pressure change, it adjusts itself to **counteract the change**. Under HPP, reactions or transitions involving a **decrease in volume (ΔV < 0)** are favoured at high pressure. This explains why protein denaturation (which involves a volume decrease due to release of void spaces in the folded state) is accelerated under high hydrostatic pressure, while reactions involving bond cleavage (no volume change) are unaffected. **(3 marks)** --- ## 📝 Part B — 14 Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the working principle and microstructural effects of High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH). Derive the relationship between Kolmogorov length scale and energy dissipation rate. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(Step 1) Working Principle (3 marks):** HPH forces a fluid mixture through a narrow micro-slit or nozzle at pressures of **100–400 MPa**. The fluid experiences intense shear (γ̇ ~ 10⁶ s⁻¹), turbulence (Re ~ 10⁵), and cavitation. These combined stresses disrupt droplets or particles, producing **sub-micron to nano-sized dispersions**. **(Step 2) Microstructural Effects (2 marks):** - Production of **nanoemulsions** (50–500 nm). - Disruption of cell clusters in plant tissue. - Reduction of fat globule size in dairy. - Improved encapsulation of bioactives. **(Step 3) Derivation (2 marks):** For turbulent flow, the smallest eddy size is governed by the balance of inertia and viscosity, leading to the Kolmogorov length scale: $$\eta_K = \left(\frac{\nu_c^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$$ This represents the smallest length scale at which kinetic energy is dissipated as heat via viscous stresses. Below this scale, the flow is laminar and further droplet break-up is suppressed. #### (b) Discuss the mechanism of electroporation under Pulsed Electric Field (PEF). Derive the critical electric field strength required for membrane pore inception. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(Step 1) Mechanism (3 marks):** When a transmembrane potential exceeds a threshold, lipid bilayer molecules reorient, forming aqueous pores. Reversible electroporation (low E) is used for *mass-transfer enhancement*; irreversible electroporation (high E) leads to cell death. **(Step 2) Derivation (3 marks):** The transmembrane potential Vmem for a spherical cell of radius *a* in an external field *E₀* is: $$V_{mem} = 1.5 \cdot a \cdot E_0 \cos\theta$$ Setting Vmem = 1 V (typical breakdown potential) and rearranging: $$E_0 = \frac{V_{mem}}{1.5 \cdot a \cos\theta}$$ For a cell with a ≈ 10 µm, θ = 0 (worst case): $$E_0 = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{4}\ \text{V m}^{-1} = 0.667\ \text{kV cm}^{-1}$$ For non-spherical/stressed membranes, lower thresholds (~0.3 kV/cm) suffice. **(Step 3) Application (1 mark):** Industrial PEF systems operate at **5–10× the critical E-field** to ensure reliable electroporation across heterogeneous cell populations. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the principle of acoustic cavitation in high-intensity ultrasound processing. Derive an expression for the cavitation threshold pressure. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(Step 1) Principle (3 marks):** High-intensity ultrasound (**≥ 20 kHz, ≥ 10 W cm⁻²**) causes alternating compression and rarefaction cycles in the liquid. In the rarefaction phase, the local pressure falls below the vapour pressure of the liquid, forming cavitation bubbles. On collapse during the compression phase, these bubbles release enormous localized energy. **(Step 2) Derivation (4 marks):** Consider a gas-filled bubble of radius **R(t)** in a liquid with hydrostatic pressure **P₀**. The Rayleigh–Plesset equation (neglecting viscosity and surface tension for simplicity) gives the dynamic pressure at collapse. The Blake threshold pressure is defined as the acoustic pressure amplitude **P_B** needed for the bubble to expand unstably: $$P_B = P_0 + \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{\frac{3\left(P_0 + \frac{2\sigma}{R_0}\right)^3 - 2P_0^3}{3}}$$ where σ is the surface tension and R₀ is the equilibrium bubble radius. Numerical example: P₀ = 101325 Pa, σ = 0.072 N/m, R₀ = 10 µm = 1 × 10⁻⁵ m: $$P_0 + \frac{2\sigma}{R_0} = 101325 + \frac{2 \times 0.072}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 101325 + 14400 = 115725\ \text{Pa}$$ $$3 \times (115725)^3 = 4.86 \times 10^{15}$$ $$2 \times (101325)^3 = 2.08 \times 10^{15}$$ Difference = 2.78 × 10¹⁵ $$P_B = 101325 + \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{\frac{2.78 \times 10^{15}}{3}} = 101325 + \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{9.27 \times 10^{14}}$$ $$P_B \approx 101325 + \frac{2}{3}(3.045 \times 10^{7}) \approx 101325 + 2.03 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} \approx 20.3\ \text{MPa}$$ This is the **minimum acoustic amplitude** required to induce inertial cavitation in water. #### (b) Describe the electrospinning process for fabrication of food-grade fibrous microstructures. Derive the Taylor cone onset voltage. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(Step 1) Process Description (3 marks):** A polymer solution (e.g., zein, pullulan, gelatin) is loaded in a syringe with a metallic needle. High voltage (**5–30 kV DC**) is applied between the needle and a grounded collector. The solution at the needle tip deforms into a conical shape (Taylor cone), from which a charged jet is ejected. The jet undergoes bending/whipping instabilities due to electrostatic repulsion, stretching the polymer into fine fibres (50–500 nm) that deposit as a non-woven mat. **(Step 2) Derivation (4 marks):** The Taylor cone condition is the balance of electrical stress and surface tension. At the cone apex (radius r), the electrostatic stress equals the Laplace pressure: $$\frac{\varepsilon_0 E^2}{2} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$ For a needle-plate geometry where the gap is **L** and needle diameter is **d**, the electric field is approximated as E ≈ V/d. Solving: $$V_c^2 = \frac{4\gamma d^2}{\varepsilon_0 r}$$ Assuming r ≈ d: $$V_c = \sqrt{\frac{4\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0}}$$ Correcting for the Taylor angle **θ = 49.3°**: $$\boxed{V_c = \sqrt{\frac{4\gamma d}{\varepsilon_0 \cos\theta}}}$$ **Numerical example:** γ = 30 mN m⁻¹, d = 0.5 mm, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F m⁻¹, cos(49.3°) ≈ 0.652: **V_c ≈ 3.2 kV** (consistent with experimental observation). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Conflating HPP and HPH**: HPP is *batch, low-shear* (whole food preservation); HPH is *continuous, high-shear* (droplet/particle reduction). Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged. > 2. **Missing units in derivations**: KTU evaluators **strictly** expect SI units in formula derivations. Always carry units to the final line. > 3. **Omitting numerical constants**: When deriving Taylor cone voltage, forgetting **cos(49.3°)** loses 1 mark. > 4. **No numerical example**: Part-B answers without a worked numerical example are penalized **2 marks** (out of 7) under KTU 2024 evaluation norms. > 5. **Writing "high temperature" for NTP**: A common error — NTP means **bulk temperature rise is minimal (< 30 °C)**; localized micro-hotspots in cavitation ≠ bulk heating. > 6. **Forgetting negative sign on ΔV‡**: Always state explicitly that protein unfolding under HPP has **ΔV‡ < 0**. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Non-thermal processing** keeps bulk food **< 30 °C** while modifying microstructure. - **HPP**: uses **hydrostatic pressure (100–600 MPa)**; ΔV‡ < 0 reactions are favoured. - **HPH / Microfluidization**: high-shear turbulence produces **nanoemulsions (50–500 nm)**; minimum droplet size scales with **Kolmogorov length η_K ∝ ε⁻¹/⁴**. - **PEF**: **electroporation** of cell membranes at **Ec ≈ 0.3–0.7 kV/cm**; reversible vs irreversible regimes. - **Ultrasound**: **acoustic cavitation** with Blake threshold ~ **20 MPa** for water. - **Cold Plasma**: surface-only modification; **RMS roughness 5–500 nm**. - **Electrospinning**: **Taylor cone voltage Vc = √(4γd / ε₀ cosθ)** produces fibres of **50–500 nm**. - **KTU must-know constants**: R₀ ≈ 1 µm, σ_water ≈ 0.072 N/m, ε₀ ≈ 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F m⁻¹, Taylor angle = 49.3°. - **Remember to validate** any fabricated microstructure using **DLS, SEM, TEM, or textural analysis**. - **Mark-winning tip**: Always provide a **numerical example with SI units** in 14-mark questions. ---

topics.title: Engineered Food Microstructure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engineered Food Microstructure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Engineered Food Microstructure** is the deliberate, scientific design, control, and manipulation of the spatial organization, composition, and interactions of food components at the micrometer ($10^{-6}$ m) and nanometer ($10^{-9}$ m) length scales to achieve specific functional, sensory, nutritional, and shelf-life properties in food products.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is the systematic application of processing principles and biopolymer physics to construct hierarchical structures — from molecular assemblies (1–100 nm) → supramolecular aggregates (100 nm – 1 µm) → microstructural networks (1–100 µm) — that govern macroscopic food quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus is on understanding *how* unit operations (homogenization, extrusion, gelation, high-pressure processing) create microstructures, and *how* these microstructures dictate rheology, texture, and stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a house. You don't just throw bricks, cement, and wood together randomly — an **architect designs** how the bricks should be laid (bond pattern), how the wood beams are arranged (load-bearing skeleton), and how the rooms connect. The house's strength, insulation, and appearance depend entirely on this design.

Similarly, in food:
- **Bricks & Beams =** Proteins, polysaccharides, lipids, water, air.
- **Architect =** Food engineer using processes like shearing, heating, acidification, enzymatic crosslinking.
- **Final House =** Engineered microstructure (gel network, foam, emulsion, foam-gel composite).

A custard, a marshmallow, a sausage, and a salad dressing all use the *same building blocks* (egg protein, sugar, water, fat) but have **radically different textures** because their microstructures are engineered differently. The "invisible architecture" is what makes food *feel* and *behave* the way it does.

## 1.3 Hierarchical Length Scales

Food structure exists across a hierarchy spanning **10 orders of magnitude**:

| Length Scale | Structure Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular (0.1–10 nm)** | Atoms, bonds, monomers, primary protein folding | Amino acid sequence, starch anhydroglucose units |
| **Supramolecular (10–100 nm)** | Secondary/tertiary protein folding, casein micelles, lipid bilayers | β-lactoglobulin globule, micelle |
| **Microstructural (0.1–100 µm)** | Network strands, gel pores, fat globules, air cells | Casein gel strands, foam bubbles |
| **Macrostructural (>100 µm)** | Visible product structure | Bread crumb cells, fruit pulp chunks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Engineered food microstructure primarily operates at the **supramolecular and microstructural scales**, where the "building blocks" are assembled into functional architectures.

## 1.4 Core Metrics of Engineered Microstructure

The following measurable parameters define a food microstructure quantitatively:

- **Particle / droplet size distribution** (e.g., $D_{3,2}$ — Sauter mean diameter, $D_{4,3}$ — De Brouckere mean diameter) with units of **µm**.
- **Specific surface area** ($a_{sp}$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$).
- **Interfacial tension / interfacial area** ($\gamma$ in $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{mN/m}$).
- **Gel network pore size** (typically **1–100 µm**).
- **Fractal dimension** $D_f$ (dimensionless, range 1–3).
- **Volume fraction** $\phi$ of dispersed phase (dimensionless, 0 to 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Building Block Categories

Engineered food microstructures are constructed from four primary colloidal / polymeric building blocks. Each has unique assembly behavior.

### A. Proteins
- Amphiphilic copolymers of amino acids.
- Assemble via **hydrophobic interactions**, **electrostatic forces**, **disulfide bonds**, and **hydrogen bonding**.
- Can form **fine-stranded** (transparent) or **coarse-stranded** (turbid) gels depending on pH relative to isoelectric point (pI) and ionic strength.

### B. Polysaccharides
- Long-chain carbohydrates (starch, pectin, alginate, xanthan, carrageenan, gellan).
- Assemble via **chain entanglement**, **junction zones** (e.g., egg-box for alginate), and **helix formation** (e.g., carrageenan).
- Mostly **hydrophilic**; provide viscosity and gel strength.

### C. Lipids
- Triglycerides, phospholipids, monoglycerides.
- Self-assemble into **emulsions, liquid crystals, bilayers, and crystalline fat networks** to minimize interfacial free energy.
- Critical in controlling mouthfeel and lubrication.

### D. Water + Air (Dispersed Phases)
- Water droplets in oil (W/O) or oil in water (O/W) — emulsions.
- Air bubbles in liquid/solid matrix — foams, aerated gels, baked goods.

## 2.2 The Five Primary Microstructure Types

### 1. **Emulsions** (Liquid-in-Liquid)
- Droplets of one immiscible liquid dispersed in another.
- Stabilized by **surfactants** or **amphiphilic biopolymers** (e.g., milk proteins).
- Example: milk, mayonnaise, salad cream.

### 2. **Foams** (Gas-in-Liquid/Solid)
- Air cells separated by a thin continuous liquid/solid lamella.
- Stabilized by surface-active proteins, low-molecular-weight surfactants (LMW), or Pickering particles.
- Example: meringue, mousses, bread, ice cream.

### 3. **Gels** (Solid-like Matrix)
- 3D polymer network trapping solvent.
- Two types:
  - **Particle gels** (e.g., acid-induced casein gels, heat-set whey gels).
  - **Biopolymer gels** (e.g., agar, alginate, gelatin).
- Example: tofu, jam, jelly, gelatin dessert.

### 4. **Suspensions / Dispersions** (Solid-in-Liquid)
- Insoluble solid particles dispersed in a liquid.
- Example: chocolate (cocoa solids + sugar in cocoa butter), fruit juice with pulp.

### 5. **Composites / Multi-phase**
- Combination of above: e.g., **emulsion gels** (mayonnaise is an emulsion; yogurt is an emulsion-filled gel).
- Most real foods are composites.

## 2.3 Why Engineer Microstructure? — The Functional Drivers

| Driver | Microstructural Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Texture / Mouthfeel** | Network density, gel firmness, particle size | Smoothness of ice cream vs. chunky ice cream |
| **Sensory perception (flavor release)** | Droplet/particle size controls diffusion and mass transfer | Small droplets → faster flavor release |
| **Shelf-life / Stability** | Stokes' law-driven creaming, Ostwald ripening, coalescence | Homogenized milk resists creaming |
| **Nutrient delivery** | Encapsulation of bioactives in micro/nano structures | Omega-3 in nanoemulsion |
| **Processability** | Viscosity, yield stress, thixotropy | Tomato ketchup must flow on shaking |
| **Appearance** | Light scattering by droplets (Tyndall / Mie regimes) | Whiteness of milk, opacity of mayonnaise |

## 2.4 The Engineering Toolbox — How Microstructures are Built

| Process | Microstructural Effect |
|---|---|
| **Homogenization** | Reduces droplet size from ~10 µm to ~0.5–1 µm; increases interfacial area by ~10–100× |
| **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** | Denatures proteins, inactivates microbes; creates fine-stranded gels |
| **Extrusion** | Shears + cooks biopolymers; creates porous, anisotropic structures (snacks, pasta) |
| **Acidification** | Lowers pH near protein pI → aggregation → particle gel |
| **Enzymatic crosslinking** | Transglutaminase forms ε-(γ-glutamyl)lysine bonds → stronger gel |
