# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (Part A)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define X-ray diffraction. State Bragg's law and explain the significance of the order of diffraction ($n$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a technique in which X-rays interact with the periodic atomic arrangement in a crystalline material, producing constructive interference at specific angles governed by Bragg's law.
>
> **Bragg's Law (1 mark):** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$, where $n$ = order of diffraction, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $d$ = inter-planar spacing, $\theta$ = angle of incidence.
>
> **Significance of $n$ (1 mark):** The order of diffraction $n$ represents the integer number of wavelengths by which the path difference between successive atomic planes must differ to produce constructive interference. $n=1$ is the first-order (strongest) diffraction; higher orders ($n=2,3,...$) occur at larger angles but with progressively weaker intensities.

---

### **Question 2 (Part A)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray micro-computed tomography (micro-CT). Mention any two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Feature | XRD | Micro-CT |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Information obtained** | Crystallographic phases, $d$-spacing, crystallinity | 3D density map, porosity, internal geometry |
> | **Sample state** | Powder, thin film, or bulk crystalline | Intact, non-destructive 3D imaging |
> | **Resolution** | Atomic / nm scale (crystal lattice) | µm scale (microstructure) |
> | **Physical principle** | Coherent scattering (Bragg) | Differential photoelectric attenuation |
> | **Output** | Diffractogram ($I$ vs. $2\theta$) | 3D voxel array |
>
> (Any two contrasting points = 3 marks; 1.5 each)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-internal choice is provided. Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model - Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the working principle of an X-ray powder diffractometer. Explain the role of the monochromator and detector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food-grade cocoa butter sample is analyzed by XRD using Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å). A strong diffraction peak is observed at $2\theta = 21.4°$. Calculate: **(i)** the inter-planar spacing $d$, and **(ii)** the crystallite size, given that the FWHM of this peak is $\beta = 0.42°$ and the Scherrer constant $K = 0.9$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of X-Ray Powder Diffractometer (7 marks):**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   [X-Ray Tube] → [Monochromator] → [Slits] → [Sample]
                                                  ↓
   [Computer/Software] ← [Detector] ← [Diffracted Beam]
```

*(Equivalent Mermaid allowed; students may draw a labeled schematic.)*

**Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **X-Ray Generation:** A heated tungsten filament emits electrons that are accelerated by a high voltage (30–40 kV) toward a Cu (or Mo) target. Characteristic Cu Kα X-rays ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å) are produced. **(1 mark)**

2. **Monochromator:** Isolates the desired Kα radiation by Bragg reflection from a graphite crystal or by absorption through a Ni filter (which absorbs Kβ). This ensures single-wavelength illumination. **(1 mark)**

3. **Collimation:** Divergence slits restrict the beam to a narrow, parallel stream. Soller slits reduce axial divergence. **(0.5 mark)**

4. **Sample Stage:** The powdered food sample is packed into a holder. The stage can rotate by angle $\theta$. **(0.5 mark)**

5. **Detection:** A scintillation counter or solid-state detector rotates by $2\theta$ on the goniometer arm, recording diffracted intensity as a function of $2\theta$. **(1 mark)**

6. **Data Processing:** The recorded diffractogram is matched against the ICDD database for phase identification. Crystallinity and crystallite size are computed. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å $= 0.15406$ nm
- $2\theta = 21.4° \Rightarrow \theta = 10.7°$
- FWHM $\beta = 0.42°$
- $K = 0.9$

**(i) Inter-planar spacing $d$ (3.5 marks):**

From Bragg's law (with $n=1$):

$$d = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}$$

$$d = \dfrac{1.5406}{2 \times \sin(10.7°)}$$

$$d = \dfrac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.1857}$$

$$d = \dfrac{1.5406}{0.3714}$$

$$\boxed{d = 4.148 \text{ Å} = 0.4148 \text{ nm}}$$

**(ii) Crystallite size $L$ (3.5 marks):**

Convert $\beta$ to radians:

$$\beta = 0.42° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 0.00733 \text{ rad}$$

Apply Scherrer equation:

$$L = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$$

$$L = \dfrac{0.9 \times 0.15406}{0.00733 \times \cos(10.7°)}$$

$$L = \dfrac{0.13865}{0.00733 \times 0.9827}$$

$$L = \dfrac{0.13865}{0.007204}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 19.25 \text{ nm}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model - Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle of X-ray micro-computed tomography (micro-CT). Discuss its applications in food microstructure analysis with at least three examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Bragg's law of X-ray diffraction starting from the path difference between two waves reflected from successive atomic planes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Micro-CT Principle & Food Applications (7 marks):**

**Principle (3.5 marks):**

X-ray micro-CT is based on the differential attenuation of X-rays as they pass through a rotating sample. The transmitted intensity $I$ at each angular projection follows the Beer-Lambert relation:

$$I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$$

A series of 2D projections are acquired as the sample rotates through 0°–180° (or 360°). These projections are mathematically combined using **filtered back-projection (FBP)** algorithms to reconstruct a 3D attenuation map. Each voxel in the reconstructed volume represents the local X-ray attenuation coefficient, which correlates with local density and effective atomic number.

**Food Applications (3.5 marks — at least 3):**

1. **Bread crumb porosity analysis:** Quantifies cell size distribution, wall thickness, and total porosity — directly correlating with perceived softness.
2. **Cheese eye (gas pocket) characterization:** Maps the size, distribution, and connectivity of gas holes in Swiss-type cheeses.
3. **Ice crystal distribution in frozen foods:** Non-destructively maps ice crystal morphology — critical for texture quality of ice cream and frozen meats.
4. **Foreign body detection in packaged foods:** Industrial use for identifying glass, metal, or stone contaminants.
5. **Encapsulation microstructure:** Visualizes the internal architecture of microcapsules used for probiotic or flavor delivery.

---

**(b) Derivation of Bragg's Law (7 marks):**

**Setup (2 marks):**
Consider two parallel atomic planes, Plane 1 (top) and Plane 2 (bottom), separated by inter-planar spacing $d$. An incident monochromatic X-ray beam of wavelength $\lambda$ strikes both planes at glancing angle $\theta$. Ray 1 reflects from Plane 1; Ray 2 penetrates and reflects from Plane 2.

**Path Difference Calculation (3 marks):**
The perpendicular distance from the point of incidence on Plane 2 to the wavefront of Ray 1 is $d\sin\theta$. The same perpendicular distance is again traversed upon reflection. Therefore, the total extra path of Ray 2 compared to Ray 1 is:

$$\Delta = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Constructive Interference Condition (1 mark):**
For constructive interference (a diffraction peak), the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$\Delta = n\lambda$$

**Final Equation (1 mark):**

Equating the two expressions:

$$\boxed{n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta}$$

This is **Bragg's Law**. It implies that diffraction occurs only at discrete angles, providing the basis for crystal structure determination.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in X-Ray Analysis Questions (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to halve $2\theta$:** Students often plug $2\theta$ directly into $\sin\theta$. Always compute $\theta = (2\theta)/2$ first.
> 2. **Degrees vs. radians:** Scherrer equation requires $\beta$ in **radians**. Convert using $\beta_{\text{rad}} = \beta_{\text{deg}} \times \pi/180$.
> 3. **Mixing up $d$ and $L$:** $d$ is the inter-planar spacing (from Bragg's law); $L$ is the crystallite size (from Scherrer). They are different quantities.
> 4. **Setting $n=2$ unnecessarily:** Unless the question explicitly says "second-order," assume $n=1$.
> 5. **Wrong units in Scherrer:** Ensure $\lambda$ and $L$ are in the same units (both nm or both Å).
> 6. **Confusing XRD with micro-CT:** XRD gives crystallographic information (atomic scale); micro-CT gives morphological information (micro-scale).
> 7. **Not drawing a labeled diagram:** KTU examiners allocate 1.5–2 marks for a neat labeled schematic in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — X-Ray Analysis (Module 4, PEFTT745)**

- ✅ **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — memorize and understand derivation.
- ✅ **Scherrer Equation:** $L = K\lambda / (\beta\cos\theta)$ — used for crystallite size; $\beta$ must be in **radians**.
- ✅ **Crystallinity Index:** Ratio of crystalline peak areas to total area; key for starch retrogradation studies.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — basis of X-ray imaging and micro-CT contrast.
- ✅ **Cu Kα wavelength:** $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å — standard for most food XRD.
- ✅ **XRD gives:** Phase identity, $d$-spacing, crystallinity, crystallite size, polymorphism.
- ✅ **Micro-CT gives:** 3D density map, porosity, pore size distribution, internal geometry.
- ✅ **SAXS / WAXS:** Used for nano-scale (1–100 nm) periodic structures (e.g., lipid bilayers, micellar structures).
- ✅ **Photoelectric absorption** (Z³ dependence) → why high-density regions appear bright in radiographs.
- ✅ **Coherent vs. Compton scattering:** Coherent = signal; Compton = background to be filtered.
- ✅ **Typical XRD operating conditions:** 30–40 kV, 15–40 mA, Cu target.
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** Chocolate tempering monitoring, bread staling, frozen food texture, foreign body detection.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfalls:** $2\theta$ vs. $\theta$ confusion, degrees vs. radians in Scherrer, mixing up $d$ and $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** Master the **derivation of Bragg's law** (almost always asked as a 7-mark question) and the **application of Scherrer's equation** (frequently tested as a 7-mark numerical). Together, they account for ~50% of the Module 4 X-ray marks in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is a non-optical, high-resolution electron-optical imaging technique that produces topographical, morphological, and compositional micrographs of a specimen by raster-scanning its surface with a finely focused, accelerated beam of electrons. The image is reconstructed pixel-by-pixel from the intensity of secondary electrons (SE), backscattered electrons (BSE), or other interaction signals generated when the primary beam interacts with atoms in the sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **KTU 2024 PEFTT745 syllabus**, SEM is positioned as a *direct visual tool* for food microstructure studies — starch granules, protein networks, fat globules, polysaccharide gels, emulsion interfaces, and crystalline phases. It is grouped alongside TEM, AFM, and Confocal Microscopy as the four pillars of food microstructural imaging.

### 1.2 Key Quantitative Specs (Bolded)

- **Resolution:** **1 – 10 nm** (high-end field-emission SEM can reach **0.5 nm**).
- **Magnification Range:** **10× to 1,000,000×** (optical microscopy caps at ≈2000×).
- **Accelerating Voltage Range:** **0.1 kV – 30 kV** (some FEG-SEM go to 100 kV).
- **Working Distance:** **1 mm – 50 mm**.
- **Depth of Field:** **~100× greater than optical microscopy** (typically several µm to mm).
- **Vacuum Required:** **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Pa** (high-vacuum); **10 – 100 Pa** for low-vac/ESEM mode.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fingertip Reader"

Imagine a **blind person reading a Braille page with their fingertips**. They do not look at the page; instead, they systematically drag one fingertip across every part of the surface, *feeling* the bumps and grooves. A computer in their brain converts each tactile signal into a mental map.

SEM does **the exact same thing**, but with **electrons instead of fingertips**:

| Fingertip Reader | SEM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Fingertip touching paper | Primary electron beam striking specimen |
| Bumps/grooves felt | Atomic surface features |
| Brain interpreting signals | Detector + processing electronics |
| Mental image | Grayscale micrograph on screen |

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike light microscopes, SEM does NOT use a lens to form a real image in one shot. It builds the image **sequentially, point by point**, like painting a picture with a single electron "brush" — this is called *raster scanning*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The SEM operates through a tightly orchestrated sequence:

1. **Electron Generation (Electron Gun):** A thermal (W/LaB₆) or field-emission source emits electrons, which are then accelerated through a potential difference **V** (typically **0.5 – 30 kV**).
2. **Beam Collimation (Condenser Lenses):** Two or more electromagnetic condenser lenses demagnify the electron source to produce a coherent, narrow beam (~**1 – 100 µm** diameter) at the final aperture.
3. **Final Focusing (Objective Lens):** The objective lens further demagnifies the beam to a **probe size of 1 – 10 nm** at the specimen surface.
4. **Raster Scanning (Scan Coils):** Electromagnetic scan coils deflect the beam in a *Cartesian raster pattern* across an (x, y) grid of pixels on the sample.
5. **Signal Generation (Beam–Specimen Interaction):** As electrons strike the sample, they generate multiple signals — SE, BSE, X-rays, Auger electrons, cathodoluminescence, and transmitted electrons.
6. **Signal Collection (Detectors):** Detectors (Everhart–Thornley for SE, semiconductor BSE detector, EDS for X-rays) convert each signal into an amplified electrical pulse.
7. **Image Reconstruction (Display CRT/Software):** The intensity of the signal at each (x, y) point modulates the brightness of a corresponding pixel on a synchronous display — building the final micrograph in real time.

### 2.2 The Why Behind Each Step

- **Why accelerate electrons?** Because accelerating voltage controls their **de Broglie wavelength** — the shorter the λ, the higher the resolution.
- **Why use lenses, not glass?** Glass is opaque to electrons. **Electromagnetic lenses** (current-carrying coils) generate Lorentz fields that bend electron trajectories like a convex lens bends light.
- **Why raster-scan?** Because the SEM uses only **one focused probe**, it must build a 2-D map by sweeping that probe. This is unlike TEM, which images the entire field in parallel.
- **Why high vacuum?** Electrons scatter heavily on gas molecules. A vacuum ensures the beam travels unimpeded to the specimen.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** set for KTU 2024 ESE — questions are frequently asked on resolution, wavelength, and magnification.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | De Broglie Wavelength (non-relativistic) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ | h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, mₑ = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C, V in Volts | Predicts resolution ceiling |
| 2 | De Broglie Wavelength (relativistic) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$ | c = 3×10⁸ m/s | Used at V > 5 kV (accurate) |
| 3 | Abbe Diffraction Limit | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$ | n = refractive index, α = half-angle, λ in nm | Maximum optical resolution |
| 4 | Magnification | $M = \dfrac{L}{H}$ | L = image length, H = object length | Image scale factor |
| 5 | Depth of Field (DOF) | $DOF = \dfrac{\delta}{M \cdot NA} + \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | δ = detector resolution (~0.1 mm), NA = numerical aperture | Critical for rough food surfaces |
| 6 | Interaction Volume (Kanaya–Okayama Range) | $R_{KO} = \dfrac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$ | A = atomic weight (g/mol), E = beam energy (keV), Z = atomic number, ρ = density (g/cm³) | Estimates electron penetration |
| 7 | Critical Excitation Energy (Moseley) | $E_c = \dfrac{1}{3} \left( U_K - 1.3 \right)$ | U_K = K-shell ionization energy | EDS peak threshold |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Food Engineering**, SEM is the gold standard for:

- Visualizing **starch granule morphology** (size 1 – 100 µm, lenticular/spherical).
- Mapping **casein micelle networks** in dairy gels.
- Studying **fat crystal networks** in chocolate, butter, and shortenings.
- Examining **air cell walls** in bread crumb structure.
- Characterizing **emulsion droplets** in mayonnaise, sauces, and dressings.
- Detecting **freeze–thaw damage** in frozen foods via cellular collapse.
- **Failure analysis** of food biopolymers, packaging laminates, and coating defects.
- **Process optimization** in extrusion, baking, fermentation, and spray-drying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relativistic De Broglie Wavelength of an Electron

**Starting assumption:** An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference V, gaining kinetic energy eV. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks for the **relativistic form**, so we must include the relativistic correction factor.

**Step 1 — Energy conservation (classical starting point):**
The non-relativistic kinetic energy is:
$$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

Solving for momentum p = mₑv:
$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the de Broglie relation:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the relativistic correction:**

At high accelerating voltages (V > 5 kV), the electron's velocity approaches a significant fraction of c. The total energy is:
$$E_{total} = m_e c^2 + eV$$

The relativistic momentum is:
$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V \left( 1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back into de Broglie's equation:**

\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left( 1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} \right)}} \\
\lambda &= \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}}}
\end{aligned}

This is the **complete relativistic wavelength formula** used in SEM physics.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantitative Wavelength at Typical SEM Voltages

**Compute λ at V = 30 kV** (standard SEM operating voltage for food samples).

**Given:** h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, mₑ = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, V = 30,000 V.

**Step 1 — Compute the non-relativistic term:**

\begin{aligned}
2 m_e e V &= 2 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (30000) \\
2 m_e e V &= 8.757 \times 10^{-45} \; \text{kg}^2 \text{m}^2 \text{s}^{-2}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Compute the relativistic correction factor:**

\begin{aligned}
\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} &= \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 30000}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2} \\
&= \frac{4.806 \times 10^{-15}}{1.6398 \times 10^{-13}} \\
&= 0.0293
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Apply the full formula:**

\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{8.757 \times 10^{-45} \times (1 + 0.0293)}} \\
\lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{8.757 \times 10^{-45} \times 1.0293}} \\
\lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{9.014 \times 10^{-45}}} \\
\lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{9.494 \times 10^{-23}} \\
\lambda &= 6.98 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m} \\
\lambda &= 0.00698 \; \text{nm} \quad (\text{or } 6.98 \; \text{pm})
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Visible light has λ ≈ 550 nm. Therefore, **electrons at 30 kV have a wavelength ~80,000× shorter than visible light**, which is why SEM achieves nanometer-level resolution compared to the ~200 nm diffraction limit of optical microscopes.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Kanaya–Okayama Electron Penetration Depth

**Why this matters in food SEM:** Overvoltage causes the beam to penetrate **below the actual surface**, contaminating the image with subsurface information. The K-O range predicts how deep the beam reaches.

**Starting formula:**
$$R_{KO} = \frac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 1 — For a starch granule (C₆H₁₀O₅), compute effective parameters:**
- Mean atomic weight A ≈ 12 (organic) for biological tissue.
- Mean atomic number Z ≈ 7 (organic).
- Density ρ ≈ 1.5 g/cm³.
- Beam energy E = 20 keV.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

\begin{aligned}
R_{KO} &= \frac{0.0276 \times 12 \times (20)^{1.67}}{7^{0.89} \times 1.5}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Compute each component:**

\begin{aligned}
(20)^{1.67} &= 20^{1} \times 20^{0.67} \approx 20 \times 7.49 = 149.8 \\
7^{0.89} &\approx 5.85
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

\begin{aligned}
R_{KO} &= \frac{0.0276 \times 12 \times 149.8}{5.85 \times 1.5} \\
R_{KO} &= \frac{49.61}{8.775} \\
R_{KO} &= 5.65 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**
For 20 keV electrons on a typical food matrix, the interaction volume penetrates ~5.6 µm below the surface. This means **fine submicron features on the very top surface are blurred by subsurface signal** — a critical consideration when choosing accelerating voltage for food imaging.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (SEM vs. Optical vs. TEM)

| Parameter | Optical Microscope | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation Source | Visible light photons | Accelerated electrons | Accelerated electrons |
| Wavelength | 400 – 700 nm | 0.001 – 0.01 nm | 0.001 – 0.01 nm |
| Max Useful Magnification | ~2000× | ~1,000,000× | ~50,000,000× |
| Resolution (Practical) | ~200 nm | 1 – 10 nm | 0.05 – 0.2 nm |
| Depth of Field | Low (0.1 µm) | High (1 – 100 µm) | Very Low |
| Sample State | Live/fixed/stained | Solid, dry, conductive (or coated) | Ultra-thin (<100 nm) |
| Vacuum Required | None | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ Pa | 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁷ Pa |
| Cost Range | ₹ 50k – ₹ 5L | ₹ 50L – ₹ 10Cr | ₹ 5Cr – ₹ 30Cr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SEM Internal Architecture & Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 0.5 to 30 kV] --> B[Electron Source W or LaB6 or FEG]
    B --> C[Wehnelt Cylinder and Anode]
    C --> D[First Condenser Lens C1]
    D --> E[Second Condenser Lens C2]
    E --> F[Objective Aperture]
    F --> G[Objective Lens Final Focusing]
    G --> H[Scanning Coils Raster XY]
    H --> I[Specimen Stage XY Z Tilt Rotation]
    I --> J[Sample Surface Interaction Volume]
    J --> K1[Secondary Electrons SE 50 eV]
    J --> K2[Backscattered Electrons BSE High Energy]
    J --> K3[Characteristic X Rays EDS]
    J --> K4[Auger Electrons]
    J --> K5[Cathodoluminescence CL]
    K1 --> L1[Everhart Thornley Detector]
    K2 --> L2[Semiconductor BSE Detector]
    K3 --> L3[Energy Dispersive Spectrometer]
    L1 --> M[Signal Amplifier]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> N[Analog to Digital Converter]
    N --> O[Image Processor and Sync Generator]
    O --> P[Display Monitor Micrograph]
    H -.->|Synchronization Signal| O
    Q[Vacuum System Rotary plus Turbo plus Ion Pump 10 to the minus 4 Pa] --> B
    Q --> C
    Q --> I
    R[Cooling System Water Chiller] --> D
    R --> E
    R --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Beam–Specimen Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Primary Electron Beam] --> B[Specimen Atoms]
    B --> C1[Elastic Scattering] 
    B --> C2[Inelastic Scattering]
    C1 --> D1[Backscattered Electrons]
    C1 --> D2[Diffracted Electrons]
    C2 --> D3[Secondary Electrons]
    C2 --> D4[Characteristic X Rays]
    C2 --> D5[Auger Electrons]
    C2 --> D6[Cathodoluminescence]
    C2 --> D7[Specimen Current Absorbed Electrons]
    B --> E[Interaction Volume Tear Drop Shape]
    E --> F[Penetration Depth RKO]
    E --> G[Lateral Spread]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Food Sample Preparation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Food Sample] --> B[Fixation Glutaraldehyde 2.5 percent]
    B --> C[Dehydration Ethanol Gradient]
    C --> D[Critical Point Drying or Freeze Drying]
    D --> E[Mounting on Aluminum Stub]
    E --> F[Sputter Coating Gold or Platinum 5 to 20 nm]
    F --> G[Sample Insertion in SEM Chamber]
    G --> H[High Vacuum Stabilization]
    H --> I[Beam Alignment and Focusing]
    I --> J[Image Acquisition SE Mode]
    I --> K[Compositional Mapping BSE or EDS]
    J --> L[Micrograph Analysis]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Food-specific prep note:** Unlike metals, food samples are non-conductive and contain water. Without (a) dehydration, (b) conductive coating, and (c) high vacuum, the sample will *charge up*, *outgas*, and *collapse* — destroying the image and contaminating the column. This is the single most important practical lesson for KTU 2024 food microstructural imaging.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Module 4]**
**Define Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). List any two advantages of SEM over optical microscopy in food microstructure analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Scanning Electron Microscopy is a type of electron microscopy that produces high-resolution images of a sample's surface by scanning it with a focused beam of electrons. The electrons interact with atoms in the sample, generating secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, and X-rays, which are detected to form a topographical or compositional image.
- **Any two advantages (1 mark):**
  1. Much higher resolution (1–10 nm vs. 200 nm for optical).
  2. Much greater depth of field (hundreds of times that of optical), enabling 3-D-like visualization of rough food surfaces.
  3. Magnification up to 1,000,000×, far beyond the 2000× limit of optical systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper, Module 4]**
**What is the role of sputter coating in SEM sample preparation? Why is it essential for food samples?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (2 marks):** Sputter coating deposits a thin (5–20 nm) conductive layer of gold, platinum, or palladium onto the specimen surface. This layer conducts away the excess electrons deposited by the primary beam, preventing **charge build-up (charging artifacts)** that would distort the image and damage the specimen.
- **Why essential for food (1 mark):** Food materials are **non-conductive** (low electrical conductivity) due to high water, lipid, and biopolymer content. Without a conductive coating, the sample acts as an electron trap, leading to bright streaks, image drift, and ultimately sample destruction. Therefore, sputter coating is **mandatory** for virtually all food SEM samples.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the working principle and major components of a Scanning Electron Microscope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 25 kV. Compare it with the wavelength of green light (λ = 550 nm) and comment on the resolution implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle & Components (7 marks)**

The SEM works on the principle of **electron–specimen interaction** in a high-vacuum environment. Major components are:

1. **Electron Gun** (1 mark): Generates electrons via thermionic emission (W filament, LaB₆ crystal) or field emission (FEG). The electrons are accelerated through a high potential difference (0.5 – 30 kV).

2. **Electromagnetic Lens System** (2 marks): Two condenser lenses (C1, C2) and an objective lens demagnify the electron source to a fine probe of diameter 1 – 10 nm. Electromagnetic lenses use Lorentz-force focusing rather than optical refraction.

3. **Scanning Coils** (1 mark): Electromagnetic deflection coils sweep the focused beam across the specimen in a *raster pattern* (X-Y grid) for synchronous image formation.

4. **Specimen Stage** (0.5 mark): A high-precision mechanical stage (X, Y, Z, tilt, rotation) holds the sample and allows positioning and orientation.

5. **Detectors** (1.5 marks):
   - **Everhart–Thornley detector** for secondary electrons (SE) — gives topographic contrast.
   - **Solid-state BSE detector** for backscattered electrons — gives compositional (Z-contrast).
   - **EDS detector** for characteristic X-rays — gives elemental analysis.
   - **CL detector** for cathodoluminescence — gives electronic band-gap info.

6. **Vacuum System & Display Electronics** (1 mark): Rotary + turbo + ion pumps maintain 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ Pa. The amplified detector signal modulates a synchronously scanned display, building the final micrograph pixel-by-pixel.

---

**(b) Relativistic De Broglie Wavelength Derivation (7 marks)**

**Starting equation (1 mark):**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left( 1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} \right)}}$$

**Substitute values at V = 25 kV:** h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, mₑ = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C, c = 3×10⁸ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute 2mₑeV (1 mark):**

\begin{aligned}
2 m_e e V &= 2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25000 \\
&= 7.298 \times 10^{-45} \; \text{kg}^2 \text{m}^2 \text{s}^{-2}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Compute the relativistic correction factor (2 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} &= \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25000}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2} \\
&= \frac{4.005 \times 10^{-15}}{1.6398 \times 10^{-13}} \\
&= 0.0244
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Apply the formula (1 mark):**

\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{7.298 \times 10^{-45} \times 1.0244}} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{7.475 \times 10^{-45}}} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{8.646 \times 10^{-23}} \\
&= 7.66 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m} \\
&\approx 0.00766 \; \text{nm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Comparison with green light (1 mark):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{\lambda_{light}}{\lambda_{electron}} = \frac{550 \text{ nm}}{0.00766 \text{ nm}} \\
&\approx 71,800
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Resolution comment (1 mark):**
The electron wavelength at 25 kV is ~**71,800× shorter** than green light. By the Abbe diffraction limit, this translates to a **theoretical resolution** that is ~71,800× better than the optical diffraction limit of ~200 nm, giving **sub-nanometer practical resolution**. This is why SEM can resolve starch granule lamellae, casein sub-micelles, and protein fibrils that are completely invisible under optical microscopy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of signals generated when the primary electron beam interacts with a specimen in SEM. Discuss the imaging modes based on secondary electrons (SE) and backscattered electrons (BSE) in the context of food microstructure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Scanning Electron Microscope is used to image a baked bread crumb at 15 kV. The bread crumb has density 0.25 g/cm³, effective atomic number 6, and effective atomic weight 12. Using the Kanaya–Okayama range formula, calculate the electron penetration depth. Comment on the imaging implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electron–Specimen Interaction Signals (7 marks)**

When the primary electron beam strikes the specimen, the interaction generates **two broad classes** of signals:

**A. Elastic Scattering Signals (1.5 marks):** High-energy primary electrons are deflected by the Coulomb field of the nucleus without significant energy loss.
- **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** Electrons with E > 50 eV that are reflected back. Yield depends on **atomic number (Z)** — high Z = bright, low Z = dark → *compositional (Z-contrast) imaging*.
- **Diffracted Electrons:** Used in Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) for crystal orientation mapping.

**B. Inelastic Scattering Signals (2 marks):** Electrons transfer energy to atomic electrons, causing excitation/ionization.
- **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low-energy (< 50 eV) electrons ejected from outer shells. Yield is high but **topographic in origin** — they are most abundant at edges, protrusions, and surface features → *3-D topographic imaging*.
- **Characteristic X-rays:** Emitted when outer-shell electrons fill the inner-shell vacancy. Energy is *element-specific* → used in **EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy)** for elemental mapping (C, N, O, Na, K, Ca in food).
- **Auger Electrons:** Emitted during the de-excitation of the ionized atom (alternative to X-ray emission). Surface-sensitive → used for thin-film analysis.
- **Cathodoluminescence (CL):** Light emitted as the excited atom returns to ground state → used for electronic band-gap and crystalline-defect studies.

**Imaging modes in food microstructure (3.5 marks):**

- **SE Mode (Secondary Electron Imaging):** Provides the *classic* SEM image of food — porous structure of bread, granular surfaces of cheese, fracture surfaces of cookies, droplet morphology of mayonnaise. SE is sensitive to *topography*, so rough food surfaces appear naturally 3-D.
- **BSE Mode (Backscattered Electron Imaging):** Provides *compositional contrast* — useful for distinguishing fat crystals from protein networks, or for visualizing the distribution of mineral-rich regions (e.g., calcium-rich casein micelles in cheese).
- **EDS Mode:** Maps the spatial distribution of C, O, N, Na, K, Ca, P, etc. in heterogeneous foods — used in salt distribution in dough, calcium migration in cheese ripening, and mineral fortification studies.

---

**(b) Kanaya–Okayama Range Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given formula (1 mark):**
$$R_{KO} = \frac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$$

**Given data:** A = 12, E = 15 keV, Z = 6, ρ = 0.25 g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator (1.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
E^{1.67} &= 15^{1.67} \\
&= 15^1 \times 15^{0.67} \\
&\approx 15 \times 6.0 \\
&\approx 90
\end{aligned}

(Note: 15^0.67 = e^(0.67 × ln 15) = e^(0.67 × 2.708) = e^1.814 ≈ 6.13)

\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.0276 \times 12 \times 90 \\
&= 29.81
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator (1.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
Z^{0.89} &= 6^{0.89} = e^{0.89 \times \ln 6} = e^{0.89 \times 1.7918} = e^{1.595} \approx 4.93 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 4.93 \times 0.25 = 1.2325
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Final computation (1 mark):**

\begin{aligned}
R_{KO} &= \frac{29.81}{1.2325} \\
&\approx 24.2 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Imaging implications (2 marks):**

The electron beam at 15 kV penetrates ~**24.2 µm** into the bread crumb. This is a relatively large depth — much greater than the size of individual starch granules (5 – 20 µm) and protein strands (1 – 5 µm). The implications are:

1. **Loss of surface detail:** The SE signal will be contaminated by subsurface features, reducing sharpness for the topmost cell-wall structure.
2. **Reduced resolution at high voltage:** For bread crumb, the *optimal voltage* is **5 – 10 kV**, not 15 kV. Lowering the voltage reduces penetration to ~5 – 8 µm, which is ideal for visualizing the cell wall (1 – 3 µm thick) and starch granules.
3. **Edge blooming:** BSE and SE yield will be enhanced at pore edges, but the broad interaction volume blurs fine details.
4. **Recommendation:** For porous, low-density food like bread, a *low kV (3 – 10 kV)* is preferred, often combined with a **field-emission SEM (FE-SEM)** for sub-100 nm resolution on cell-wall biopolymers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the relativistic correction** at V ≥ 5 kV → loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing SE and BSE contrast mechanism** — SE is *topographic*, BSE is *compositional (Z-contrast)*. Writing them backwards is a guaranteed -2.
> 3. **Mixing up penetration depth vs. escape depth** — Penetration is total depth reached; escape is the shallower layer from which SE can actually leave the sample.
> 4. **Skipping units in derivation** — KTU examiners often dock 1 mark per missing unit in the final answer.
> 5. **Writing "SEM uses light" or "SEM uses glass lenses"** — this is a fatal conceptual error worth zero in definition questions.
> 6. **Not mentioning food-specific sample prep** (dehydration, sputter coating) when asked about *food* microstructural imaging — partial marking of -2 is common.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **SEM = Electron-based, surface-imaging, raster-scanning, high-vacuum technique.**
- **Electron wavelength at 30 kV ≈ 0.007 nm** (relativistic). At 25 kV ≈ 0.0077 nm.
- **Wavelength equation:** $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V (1 + eV/2m_e c^2)}$ — always use the relativistic form in KTU answers.
- **Resolution = 1 – 10 nm**; **Magnification = 10× to 10⁶×**; **DOF ≫ optical**.
- **5 Signals to remember:** SE (topographic), BSE (Z-contrast), X-rays (EDS), Auger (surface), CL (electronic).
- **SE = 50 eV (low E)**, **BSE = high E** (> 50 eV, can equal primary E).
- **4 Critical Components:** Electron gun → Lenses → Scan coils → Detectors.
- **Vacuum: 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Pa** is mandatory for high-vac SEM (10 – 100 Pa in low-vac/ESEM).
- **Sample prep for food =** Fix → Dehydrate → Critical-point-dry → Sputter coat (Au/Pt) → Mount.
- **Kanaya–Okayama range =** the depth to which electrons penetrate — directly affects image sharpness.
- **High kV = more penetration = more subsurface blur = less surface detail** → for food, prefer *low kV (3 – 10 kV)*.
- **SEM vs. TEM:** SEM = surface imaging, bulk samples, reflects electrons. TEM = internal imaging, ultra-thin (<100 nm) sections, transmits electrons.
- **Magnification M = L/H** — purely a *ratio*, not a power.
- **Abbe diffraction limit** applies to *optical* systems, but the *same physical principle* limits SEM resolution; the difference is the λ of the probe.
- **Common food applications:** Starch granules, casein micelles, fat crystals, air cells in bread, emulsion droplets, freeze-thaw damage, biopolymer networks.
- **Remember the Fingertip Analogy** for definitions — examiners love intuitive explanations in KTU 2024 OBE-based grading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT745 - FOOD RHEOLOGY AND MICROSTRUCTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** is a high-resolution electron-optical characterization technique in which a finely collimated, monochromatic beam of high-energy electrons (typically accelerated through **60–300 kV**) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (≤ **100 nm** thickness). The interaction of the transmitted electrons with the atomic nuclei and electron cloud of the specimen produces elastic and inelastic scattering events. A series of electromagnetic lenses projects the scattered electron distribution onto a fluorescent screen, photographic plate, or CCD camera to form a two-dimensional magnified image that maps the internal mass-density and crystallographic orientation of the specimen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Food Rheology and Microstructure*, TEM is the technique of choice for resolving **sub-micron to nanometer scale internal structural features** of food biopolymers (proteins, polysaccharides), emulsion droplets, micelles, starch granules, and lipid bilayers — features that dictate macroscopic rheological behavior.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "X-Ray Vision Slide Projector"

Imagine you have a super-thin slice of an orange (so thin that light passes through it). If you placed this slice inside a normal projector, you would see orange-colored light projected on the wall — but with limited detail because visible light has a long wavelength (~550 nm) and diffracts around tiny structures.

Now imagine replacing the light bulb with an extremely bright **"anti-light"** (electrons) that has a wavelength **100,000 times smaller** than visible light. Because the wavelength is so tiny, the electrons do not bend around small features — they faithfully reproduce the structure on the screen. The orange slice is the specimen, the projector lens is the electromagnetic lens, and the screen is the fluorescent phosphor/CCD. This is precisely what TEM does — it is a **"transparency projector"** that uses electrons instead of photons to see through matter at the nanoscale.

### 1.3 Why TEM Matters in Food Microstructure

| Food System | Microstructural Feature Resolved by TEM | Rheological Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Milk/cheese | Casein micelles (50–500 nm) | Gel firmness, curd syneresis |
| Starch | Lamellar alternation of amylose/amylopectin | Gel retrogradation, pasting |
| Meat | Myofibrillar lattice (sarcomere Z-lines) | Tenderness, water-holding |
| Emulsions | Surfactant bilayers, droplet interfaces | Emulsion stability, creaming |
| Bread dough | Gluten network strands | Dough elasticity, gas retention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for TEM:**
> - Electron rest mass: **mₑ = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
> - Elementary charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Relativistic correction becomes significant above **100 kV** acceleration voltage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Electron Generation:** A high-voltage field emits electrons from a cathode filament (tungsten hairpin, LaB₆ crystal, or field-emission gun).
2. **Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated by an applied potential difference (typically 40–300 kV) inside a vacuum column (≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Pa) to avoid gas-molecule scattering.
3. **Condensation:** The **condenser lens** system focuses the electron beam into a coherent, narrow probe (~1 µm diameter) illuminating the specimen.
4. **Specimen Interaction:** Electrons pass through the thin specimen and undergo:
   - **Elastic scattering** (no energy loss, change of direction) → carries diffraction and mass-thickness contrast information.
   - **Inelastic scattering** (energy loss to specimen atoms) → carries compositional information (used in EELS/EFTEM).
5. **Objective Lens Imaging:** The primary objective lens forms a first intermediate image, with an **objective aperture** selecting only the directly transmitted (or selected Bragg-diffracted) beam to form **bright-field** or **dark-field** images.
6. **Intermediate & Projector Lenses:** A cascade of intermediate and projector lenses magnifies the image (typically 1,000× to 1,000,000×) and projects it onto the viewing screen.

### 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind Each Component

- **Why vacuum?** Electrons are charged particles; they would scatter off any gas molecules and never reach the detector. Also, the filament would oxidize instantly.
- **Why electromagnetic lenses?** Glass lenses cannot focus electrons. Coils of wire with a current passing through them generate a magnetic field gradient that bends electron trajectories (Lorentz force).
- **Why ultra-thin specimen?** If the specimen is too thick, multiple scattering blurs the image and prevents meaningful interpretation. The mean free path for 100 keV electrons in biological matter is only ~50 nm.
- **Why heavy-metal staining?** Biological food materials are made of light atoms (C, H, O, N) that scatter electrons weakly. Stains like **uranyl acetate, osmium tetroxide, and phosphotungstic acid** introduce heavy-metal atoms that enhance elastic scattering contrast.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | de Broglie wavelength of electron | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V (1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2})}}$ | Determines achievable resolution |
| 2 | Non-relativistic approximation (low V) | $\lambda \approx \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}$ (nm, with V in volts) | Quick estimate at low kV |
| 3 | Relativistic wavelength at 100 kV | $\lambda \approx 0.0037$ **nm = 3.7 pm** | Actual operating wavelength in modern TEM |
| 4 | Relativistic wavelength at 200 kV | $\lambda \approx 0.0025$ **nm = 2.5 pm** | Used for high-resolution TEM |
| 5 | Abbe diffraction limit | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mu \, \sin \alpha}$ | Theoretical point-resolution limit |
| 6 | Relativistic electron mass | $m = \dfrac{m_0}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$ | Necessary above 100 kV |
| 7 | Accelerating voltage to wavelength (relativistic) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$ | Master equation for TEM |
| 8 | Lorentz force on electron in lens | $\vec{F} = -e \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ | Basis of electromagnetic lens action |
| 9 | Spherical aberration coefficient (Cs) | Typical 0.5–5 mm | Limits practical resolution |
| 10 | Scherzer defocus (optimum defocus) | $\Delta f_{Sch} = -1.2 \sqrt{C_s \lambda}$ | Maximizes information transfer |
| 11 | Information limit | $d_{info} \approx \sqrt{C_s \lambda^3}^{1/4}$ | Modern practical resolution |
| 12 | Mass-thickness contrast (Beer-Lambert analog) | $I = I_0 \exp(-S \, \rho \, t)$ | Image intensity vs specimen density & thickness |
| 13 | Effective electron wavelength at 60 kV | $\lambda \approx 0.00487$ **nm** | Typical for food/biological TEM |
| 14 | Magnetic field of condenser lens | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{d}$ | Used to focus electron beam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of TEM in Food Science:**
> - Verifying micelle size distribution in **reconstituted infant formula**.
> - Imaging **amylose–lipid complexes** in starch granules after enzymatic hydrolysis.
> - Studying **emulsifier-stabilized interface** morphology in mayonnaise.
> - Confirming **protein–polysaccharide complex coacervation** in microencapsulation systems.
> - Investigating **ice crystal morphology** in frozen foods via cryo-TEM.

### 2.4 Comparison Snapshot (Useful for KTU Short Notes)

| Parameter | TEM | SEM |
|---|---|---|
| Signal detected | Transmitted electrons | Secondary/backscattered electrons |
| Specimen type | Ultra-thin section (≤ 100 nm) | Bulk, surface |
| Image type | 2-D projection (internal) | 3-D-like (topographical) |
| Resolution | **~0.1–0.2 nm** (atomic) | ~1–10 nm |
| Vacuum requirement | Ultra-high | High |
| Typical application | Internal microstructure (food biopolymers) | Surface microstructure (crust, freeze-dried) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electron Wavelength from de Broglie's Hypothesis (Non-Relativistic)

We start from the de Broglie hypothesis that every moving particle has an associated wavelength:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p}$$

where $p$ is the relativistic momentum of the electron.

For a non-relativistic electron accelerated through a potential $V$, the work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy:

$$eV = \dfrac{1}{2} m_0 v^2$$

Therefore, the velocity is:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m_0}}$$

The non-relativistic momentum is:

$$p = m_0 v = \sqrt{2 m_0 e V}$$

Substituting back into the de Broglie equation:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V}}$$

Substituting the numerical values of $h$, $m_0$, and $e$:

$$\lambda \, (\text{m}) = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}}$$

$$\lambda \, (\text{m}) = \dfrac{1.227 \times 10^{-9}}{\sqrt{V}} \quad \text{with } V \text{ in volts}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \, (\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relativistic Correction for TEM Voltages

At typical TEM accelerating voltages (60–300 kV), electrons reach velocities that are a substantial fraction of the speed of light, so the relativistic mass correction becomes essential. The total energy (rest + kinetic) from Einstein's special relativity is:

$$E_{total} = m c^2 = m_0 c^2 + eV$$

Hence the relativistic mass is:

$$m = m_0 + \dfrac{eV}{c^2}$$

The relativistic momentum becomes:

$$p = m v = m_0 \, \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{1 - (v/c)^2}}$$

A more elegant derivation uses the relativistic energy-momentum relation:

$$(pc)^2 = (E_{total})^2 - (m_0 c^2)^2 = (m_0 c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_0 c^2)^2$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$(pc)^2 = 2 m_0 c^2 (eV) + (eV)^2$$

Dividing by $c^2$:

$$p^2 = 2 m_0 (eV) + \dfrac{(eV)^2}{c^2} = 2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)$$

Therefore the momentum magnitude is:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}$$

The de Broglie wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

Substituting the numerical constants:

$$\boxed{\lambda \, (\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V\right)}}}$$

where the term $0.978 \times 10^{-6} V$ accounts for the relativistic correction at high voltages.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Wavelength at 100 kV

Plug in $V = 100{,}000$ volts:

$$V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V\right) = 100{,}000 \times (1 + 0.0978) = 100{,}000 \times 1.0978 = 109{,}780$$

$$\sqrt{109{,}780} \approx 331.3$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{331.3} \approx 0.00370 \, \text{nm} = 3.70 \, \text{pm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3.7 pm wavelength is **~150,000 times smaller** than visible light (~550 nm). This is precisely why TEM achieves sub-nanometer resolution while optical microscopes are limited by diffraction to ~200 nm.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Abbe Resolution Limit for Electrons

The Abbe diffraction-limited resolution of an optical/electron-optical instrument is:

$$d = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mu \sin \alpha} = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

For an electron microscope, the refractive index $\mu = 1$ (electrons travel in vacuum), and the numerical aperture NA is small (typically 0.001–0.01 rad). Using $\lambda = 3.7$ pm at 100 kV and $\alpha \approx 0.01$ rad:

$$d = \dfrac{0.61 \times 3.7 \times 10^{-12}}{0.01} \approx 0.226 \, \text{nm}$$

This is the theoretical resolution, but practical resolution is dominated by **spherical aberration** $C_s$:

$$d_{min} \approx 0.66 (C_s \lambda^3)^{1/4}$$

With $C_s = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m and $\lambda = 3.7$ pm:

$$d_{min} = 0.66 \times (10^{-3} \times (3.7 \times 10^{-12})^3)^{1/4}$$

$$(3.7 \times 10^{-12})^3 = 5.07 \times 10^{-35}$$

$$10^{-3} \times 5.07 \times 10^{-35} = 5.07 \times 10^{-38}$$

$$(5.07 \times 10^{-38})^{1/4} = 1.50 \times 10^{-9.5} = 4.73 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} = 0.473 \, \text{nm}$$

$$d_{min} \approx 0.66 \times 0.473 = 0.31 \, \text{nm}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Specimen Preparation Trade-offs in Food TEM

| Technique | Specimen State | Resolution Range | Damage Risk | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ultramicrotomy + heavy-metal stain | Fixed, dehydrated, resin-embedded | 2–5 nm | Moderate | Casein micelles, muscle |
| Negative staining (uranyl acetate) | Aqueous suspension dried on grid | 1–3 nm | Low (artefact risk) | Isolated biopolymers |
| Freeze-fracture / freeze-etch | Frozen, fractured, replicated | 5–20 nm | Low | Emulsion droplets, lipid bilayers |
| Cryo-TEM (vitrified) | Plunge-frozen, hydrated | 1–2 nm | Very low | Native emulsions, micelles |
| Immunogold labeling | Antibody-conjugated gold particles | 10–30 nm | Moderate | Locating specific proteins |
| High-pressure freezing | Vitreous ice, native state | 1–5 nm | Very low | Hydrated food matrices |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
